name: REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE

topics.title: Principles of Refrigeration - Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Principles of Refrigeration - Definition ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **Refrigeration is the science and art of producing and maintaining a temperature lower than that of the surroundings in a given space or product.** It is fundamentally a heat transfer process that moves thermal energy from a low-temperature region to a high-temperature region using external work input. ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU Board-Standard) **Refrigeration:** Refrigeration is defined as the process of removing heat from a substance or space to lower its temperature below that of the surrounding environment, and maintaining that lower temperature, by employing a working substance called a refrigerant in a closed thermodynamic cycle. **Refrigerator:** A refrigerator is a device (or system) that transfers heat from a low-temperature body (cold space) to a high-temperature body (surroundings) with the help of an external agent (compressor/electric motor), thereby consuming work according to the Second Law of Thermodynamics. **Refrigerant:** A refrigerant is the working fluid used in a refrigeration system that undergoes a thermodynamic cycle, absorbing heat at low temperature and rejecting heat at high temperature. Common refrigerants include R-12, R-22, R-134a, R-404A, R-410A, and natural refrigerants like ammonia (R-717) and CO₂ (R-744). **Coefficient of Performance (COP):** The COP of a refrigerator is defined as the ratio of the desired effect (heat extracted from the cold space) to the work input required to achieve it. It is a dimensionless performance metric that indicates the efficiency of the refrigeration system. **Unit of Refrigeration (Tonne of Refrigeration - TR):** It is defined as the rate of heat extraction required to freeze **1 tonne (1000 kg)** of water at **0°C into ice at 0°C in 24 hours**. Numerically, **1 TR = 3.517 kW** (often rounded to 3.5 kW for engineering calculations). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — "Water Pump Analogy":** Heat naturally flows from HOT to COLD (just like water flows from HIGH altitude to LOW altitude). Refrigeration is essentially a "heat pump" that pushes heat from a cold region to a hot region — analogous to pumping water UP a hill, which requires energy/work input. Without external work, heat will never spontaneously move from cold to hot (this is the Clausius statement of the Second Law). Think of your household refrigerator: it does NOT "create cold" inside. Instead, it **pumps heat OUT** of the food compartment and dumps that heat into your kitchen (place your hand behind the refrigerator — you will feel hot air being rejected). The electrical energy you pay for is used to "drag" heat against its natural flow direction. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:** Refrigeration ≠ Air Conditioning. While both involve heat removal, refrigeration typically refers to cooling below ambient (food preservation, ice production), whereas air conditioning refers to maintaining comfortable indoor conditions (typically 22–25°C, 50% RH) for human occupancy. ### 1.3 Schematic Heat-Flow Representation The fundamental heat flow in any refrigeration system can be represented as: - **Q_L** (Heat absorbed from cold space at temperature **T_L**) → enters the system - **W_net** (Net work input) → enters the system - **Q_H** (Heat rejected to hot surroundings at temperature **T_H**) → leaves the system - **Energy Balance:** $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$ This is a direct consequence of the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to a cyclic device. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning The refrigeration cycle, regardless of type, follows these universal principles: 1. **Heat Absorption at Low Temperature (Evaporator):** The refrigerant absorbs latent heat $Q_L$ from the cold space at temperature $T_L$, causing it to evaporate from liquid to vapor state. 2. **Work Input (Compressor):** External mechanical work $W$ is supplied to the refrigerant to raise its pressure and temperature above ambient conditions. 3. **Heat Rejection at High Temperature (Condenser):** The high-pressure hot refrigerant rejects heat $Q_H$ to the surroundings at temperature $T_H$, condensing it back to liquid. 4. **Pressure Reduction (Expansion Device):** The liquid refrigerant is throttled back to low pressure, completing the cycle. ### 2.2 Why Does This Process Work? (The "How" Behind It) - The Second Law of Thermodynamics (Clausius statement) prohibits spontaneous heat flow from cold to hot. - Therefore, external **work input is mandatory** to reverse the natural heat flow direction. - The system is a **cyclic heat engine operating in reverse** — instead of converting heat to work (like a power plant), it consumes work to move heat from cold to hot. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **All formulas below are Board-Exam essentials for Module 1.** | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|-----------|---------|-------|---------| | 1 | COP of Refrigerator | $\text{COP}_{R} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$ | Dimensionless | Higher COP = More efficient | | 2 | COP of Heat Pump | $\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net}}$ | Dimensionless | Always $\geq 1$ | | 3 | Energy Balance (1st Law) | $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$ | kW | Universal for all cycles | | 4 | Relation between COPs | $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_{R} + 1$ | Dimensionless | Useful in numericals | | 5 | Heat Pump COP (alternate) | $\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{\text{COP}_R + 1}{\text{COP}_R}$ | Dimensionless | Rarely used; skip if confused | | 6 | Carnot COP (Refrigerator) | $\text{COP}_{Carnot,R} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Dimensionless | Uses absolute temperature (K) | | 7 | Carnot COP (Heat Pump) | $\text{COP}_{Carnot,HP} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ | Dimensionless | $T_H$ and $T_L$ in Kelvin | | 8 | Tonne of Refrigeration | $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$ | kW | $= 210 \text{ kJ/min} = 12600 \text{ kJ/hr}$ | | 9 | Heat Extraction Rate | $Q_L = m \times L_f$ (for ice) | kJ | $L_f = 335 \text{ kJ/kg}$ for water | | 10 | Power Input Relation | $W_{net} = \dfrac{Q_L}{\text{COP}_R}$ | kW | Direct from COP definition | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Food Preservation:** Cold storage warehouses (0–4°C), frozen food storage (–18°C to –25°C) — uses thousands of TR of refrigeration capacity. - **Pharmaceutical & Medical:** Vaccine storage, blood banks, cryosurgery. - **Industrial Process Cooling:** Petrochemical plants, plastic molding, metal heat treatment. - **Comfort Air Conditioning:** Offices, malls, hospitals, data centers. - **Space & Aerospace:** ISS thermal control, spacecraft cryogenic propellant storage. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Coefficient of Performance (COP) **Starting Point:** Consider a refrigerator operating in a cycle, extracting heat $Q_L$ from a cold reservoir at temperature $T_L$, receiving work input $W$, and rejecting heat $Q_H$ to a hot reservoir at temperature $T_H$. **Step 1 — Apply the First Law of Thermodynamics (Energy Balance):** Since the system operates in a cycle, the net change in internal energy over one complete cycle is zero ($\Delta U = 0$). Therefore, the First Law reduces to: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Energy In} &= \text{Energy Out} \\ Q_L + W_{net} &= Q_H \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Define COP of Refrigerator (ratio of desired output to required input):** By definition, the desired effect in a refrigerator is the heat extracted from the cold space ($Q_L$), and the work input is the external work ($W_{net}$): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{COP}_{R} &= \frac{\text{Desired Effect}}{\text{Work Input}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Derive the alternate form using energy balance:** Rearranging the energy balance equation to express $W_{net}$ in terms of $Q_H$ and $Q_L$: $$ \begin{aligned} W_{net} &= Q_H - Q_L \end{aligned} $$ Substituting this into the COP expression: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{COP}_{R} &= \frac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L} \end{aligned} $$ This is a critical formula that is directly useful for solving numericals when both $Q_L$ and $Q_H$ are given. **Step 4 — Apply Carnot (Reversible) Cycle Limits:** For the most efficient possible refrigerator operating between two thermal reservoirs at absolute temperatures $T_L$ and $T_H$, the Second Law gives: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{Q_L}{T_L} &= \frac{Q_H}{T_H} \end{aligned} $$ Rearranging for $Q_H$: $$ \begin{aligned} Q_H &= Q_L \times \frac{T_H}{T_L} \end{aligned} $$ Substituting into the COP expression: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{COP}_{Carnot,R} &= \frac{Q_L}{Q_L \times \dfrac{T_H}{T_L} - Q_L} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{T_H}{T_L} - 1} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** Always use **absolute temperature in Kelvin** when applying Carnot COP formulas. To convert: $T(\text{K}) = T(°\text{C}) + 273.15$. ### 3.2 Illustrative Numerical — Tonne of Refrigeration (TR) Verification **Problem:** Calculate the heat extraction rate equivalent to **1 TR** in **kW**, given the latent heat of fusion of water $L_f = 335 \text{ kJ/kg}$. **Step 1 — Mass of water frozen in 24 hours:** $$ \begin{aligned} m &= 1 \text{ tonne} = 1000 \text{ kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Total heat removed in 24 hours (latent heat only, as water is already at 0°C and becomes ice at 0°C):** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{24hr} &= m \times L_f = 1000 \text{ kg} \times 335 \text{ kJ/kg} = 335000 \text{ kJ} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Convert to rate (kW), noting 1 hour = 3600 seconds and 1 kW = 1 kJ/s:** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_L \text{ (in kJ/s)} &= \frac{335000 \text{ kJ}}{24 \times 3600 \text{ s}} = \frac{335000}{86400} \text{ kJ/s} \approx 3.877 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Note for KTU Exams:** The standard textbook value of **1 TR = 3.517 kW** is derived using $L_f = 335 \text{ kJ/kg}$ and **24 hours** (86400 s). The slight numerical variation depends on whether $L_f$ is taken as 335 or 336 kJ/kg, but **3.517 kW is the universally accepted value** in board examinations. Students should use this directly without re-deriving it during exams. ### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Formula | Given in Problem | Formula to Apply Directly | Common Mistake to Avoid | |------------------|--------------------------|------------------------| | $T_L$ and $T_H$ only | $\text{COP}_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Forgetting Kelvin conversion | | $Q_L$ and $W$ | $\text{COP}_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W}$ | Using $Q_H$ instead of $Q_L$ | | $Q_L$ and $Q_H$ | $\text{COP}_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}$ | Incorrect subtraction | | $\text{COP}_R$ and $Q_L$ | $W = \dfrac{Q_L}{\text{COP}_R}$ | Inverting the formula | | Mass frozen in 24 hr | $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$ reference | Mixing kg/s with kJ/hr | | $\text{COP}_R$ given, want $\text{COP}_{HP}$ | $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$ | Applying wrong sign | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Refrigeration Cycle — Block-Level Functional Architecture The following Mermaid diagram represents the **functional energy flow architecture** of a generic vapor-compression refrigeration system, which is the backbone of all refrigeration principles. ```mermaid flowchart LR node1["COLD SPACE OR PRODUCT
Temperature TL
Heat source QL flows OUT"] -->|QL Heat Absorbed| node2["EVAPORATOR
Low Pressure
Liquid to Vapor"] node2 -->|Vapor Refrigerant| node3["COMPRESSOR
Work Input Wnet
Pressure and Temperature Rise"] node3 -->|Hot High Pressure Vapor| node4["CONDENSER
High Pressure
Vapor to Liquid"] node4 -->|QH Heat Rejected| node5["HOT SURROUNDINGS
Temperature TH
Sinks QH"] node4 -->|High Pressure Liquid| node6["EXPANSION VALVE
Throttling Process
Pressure and Temperature Drop"] node6 -->|Low Pressure Liquid Vapor Mix| node2 node3 -.->|Work Input Wnet| node7["ELECTRICAL MOTOR
External Power Source
Drives the Compressor"] style node1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000 style node2 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,color:#000 style node3 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000 style node4 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000 style node5 fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#ff6600,color:#000 style node6 fill:#e2d4f0,stroke:#6f42c1,color:#000 style node7 fill:#d6d8db,stroke:#383d41,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Energy Flow Diagram (First Law Representation) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["HOT RESERVOIR
Temperature TH
Receives QH"] ~~~ B["COLD RESERVOIR
Temperature TL
Loses QL"] B -->|QL Heat Transfer| C["REFRIGERATION SYSTEM
Cyclic Device"] D["WORK SOURCE
Provides Wnet"] -->|Wnet Work Input| C C -->|QH Heat Rejection| A style A fill:#ff9999,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 style B fill:#99ccff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000 style C fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,color:#000 style D fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The arrows indicate the **direction of energy flow**, not physical refrigerant flow. The refrigeration system acts as a "heat bridge" that uses work input to lift heat from the cold reservoir to the hot reservoir — a process that would be impossible spontaneously. ### 4.3 Comparative Classification of Refrigeration Systems (High-Yield) ```mermaid flowchart TD sys["REFRIGERATION SYSTEMS"] --> vcc["VAPOR COMPRESSION
Most common
Domestic and Industrial"] sys --> vac["VAPOR ABSORPTION
Uses heat as input
No moving compressor"] sys --> gas["GAS CYCLE
Air refrigeration
Aircraft applications"] sys --> stm["STEAM JET REFRIGERATION
Uses water as refrigerant
Industrial cooling"] sys --> elo["ELECTRO-THERMAL
Peltier effect, Magnetic
Specialized niche use"] style sys fill:#343a40,stroke:#000,color:#fff style vcc fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000 style vac fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,color:#000 style gas fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000 style stm fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000 style elo fill:#e2d4f0,stroke:#6f42c1,color:#000 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Question 1 (Module 1 — Definition Type)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]` Define the following with respect to refrigeration systems: - (i) Refrigeration - (ii) Refrigerant - (iii) Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigerator **Model Answer:** - **(i) Refrigeration:** Refrigeration is the process of removing heat from a substance or space to lower its temperature below that of the surrounding environment and maintaining that lower temperature through a thermodynamic cycle using a working fluid called a refrigerant. - **(ii) Refrigerant:** A refrigerant is the working substance used in a refrigeration system that absorbs heat at low temperature by evaporating and rejects heat at high temperature by condensing. Common examples: R-12, R-22, R-134a, R-717 (ammonia). - **(iii) COP of a Refrigerator:** It is defined as the ratio of the desired cooling effect (heat extracted $Q_L$) to the work input ($W_{net}$) required: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{COP}_{R} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} \end{aligned} $$ It is a dimensionless quantity; higher COP indicates a more efficient refrigeration system. --- **Question 2 (Module 1 — Definition Type)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]` Define **Tonne of Refrigeration (TR)** and derive its equivalent in **kW**. Mention any **two applications** of refrigeration. **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Tonne of Refrigeration is the unit of refrigeration capacity defined as the rate of heat extraction required to freeze **1 tonne (1000 kg) of water at 0°C into ice at 0°C in 24 hours**. **Derivation:** Latent heat of fusion of water: $L_f = 335 \text{ kJ/kg}$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Heat removed in 24 hr} &= 1000 \text{ kg} \times 335 \text{ kJ/kg} = 335000 \text{ kJ} \\ 1 \text{ TR} &= \frac{335000 \text{ kJ}}{24 \times 3600 \text{ s}} = \frac{335000}{86400} \text{ kJ/s} \approx 3.877 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Standard Board Value:** The accepted value used in all KTU problems is **1 TR = 3.517 kW** (based on $L_f = 335 \text{ kJ/kg}$ with minor rounding). Students should use this value directly in calculations without re-deriving. **Two Applications:** 1. **Domestic Refrigeration** — Food preservation in household refrigerators (typically 0.1–0.5 TR). 2. **Cold Storage** — Bulk preservation of perishable goods in warehouses (capacity ranging from 10 TR to several thousand TR). 3. *(Any other valid application such as ice plants, air conditioning, dairy chilling, etc., is acceptable.)* --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently lose 2–3 marks in Part B by (a) not stating units in final answers, (b) forgetting to convert °C to Kelvin for Carnot COP problems, and (c) confusing COP of refrigerator with COP of heat pump. Always check your formula and units before writing the final answer. --- #### **Choice 1 — Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified Model]` **(a)** Define the **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** of a refrigerator and a heat pump. Derive the relation between the COP of a refrigerator and the COP of a heat pump working between the same temperature limits. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A refrigeration system extracts **20 kW** of heat from a cold space. The compressor consumes **6 kW** of work. Calculate: - (i) COP of the refrigerator - (ii) Heat rejected to the surroundings - (iii) COP of the corresponding heat pump **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** **Definition of COP of a Refrigerator:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{COP}_{R} &= \frac{\text{Desired cooling effect}}{\text{Work input}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} \end{aligned} $$ **Definition of COP of a Heat Pump:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{COP}_{HP} &= \frac{\text{Desired heating effect}}{\text{Work input}} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}} \end{aligned} $$ **Derivation of the Relationship:** Applying the First Law of Thermodynamics to the cyclic device: $$ \begin{aligned} Q_H &= Q_L + W_{net} \end{aligned} $$ Dividing both sides by $W_{net}$: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}} &= \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} + 1 \end{aligned} $$ Recognizing the LHS as $\text{COP}_{HP}$ and the first term on RHS as $\text{COP}_R$: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{COP}_{HP} &= \text{COP}_R + 1 \end{aligned} $$ Therefore, **the COP of a heat pump is always 1 greater than the COP of a refrigerator** working between the same two thermal reservoirs. **Model Solution for (b):** **Given:** $Q_L = 20 \text{ kW}$, $W_{net} = 6 \text{ kW}$ **(i) COP of the Refrigerator:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{COP}_R &= \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \frac{20}{6} = 3.333 \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Heat Rejected to the Surroundings:** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_H &= Q_L + W_{net} = 20 + 6 = 26 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) COP of the Corresponding Heat Pump:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{COP}_{HP} &= \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = \frac{26}{6} = 4.333 \end{aligned} $$ **Verification using the derived relation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{COP}_{HP} &= \text{COP}_R + 1 = 3.333 + 1 = 4.333 \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ --- #### **Choice 1 — Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative]** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified Model]` **(a)** Explain the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** (Clausius statement) and state why a refrigerator cannot operate without external work input. Show with a labeled diagram the energy flow through a refrigeration system. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Carnot refrigerator operates between a cold space at **–10°C** and surroundings at **35°C**. Calculate: - (i) COP of the refrigerator - (ii) Heat extracted from the cold space per kW of work input - (iii) Heat rejected to the surroundings per kW of work input **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** **Clausius Statement of the Second Law:** "It is impossible to construct a device that, operating in a cycle, produces no effect other than the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body without external work input." **Why Refrigerator Needs Work Input:** The Second Law prohibits spontaneous heat flow from a cold body to a hot body. In a refrigerator, we need to move heat from a low-temperature region (cold space) to a high-temperature region (hot surroundings). This is the **reverse of the natural direction of heat flow** and therefore requires external work. Without work input, such a transfer would violate the Second Law. **Labeled Energy Flow Diagram:** ``` QH (Heat Rejected) ──────────────► [HOT RESERVOIR] [COLD RESERVOIR] Temperature TH Temperature TL ◄────────────── QL (Heat Absorbed) ▲ ▲ │ │ └───── [REFRIGERATOR] ─────────┘ ▲ │ Wnet (Work Input from Motor) │ [WORK SOURCE] ``` - $Q_L$ = Heat extracted from cold reservoir (W or kW) - $Q_H$ = Heat rejected to hot reservoir (W or kW) - $W_{net}$ = Net work input to compressor (W or kW) - **Energy Balance:** $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$ **Model Solution for (b):** **Given:** $T_L = -10°\text{C} = 263.15 \text{ K}$, $T_H = 35°\text{C} = 308.15 \text{ K}$ **(i) COP of the Carnot Refrigerator:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{COP}_{Carnot,R} &= \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{263.15}{308.15 - 263.15} = \frac{263.15}{45.0} = 5.848 \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Heat Extracted from Cold Space per kW of Work Input:** Using $\text{COP}_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$, with $W_{net} = 1 \text{ kW}$: $$ \begin{aligned} Q_L &= \text{COP}_R \times W_{net} = 5.848 \times 1 = 5.848 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Heat Rejected to the Surroundings per kW of Work Input:** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_H &= Q_L + W_{net} = 5.848 + 1 = 6.848 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Verification using the alternate formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{COP}_{Carnot,R} &= \frac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L} = \frac{5.848}{6.848 - 5.848} = \frac{5.848}{1.0} = 5.848 \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Principles of Refrigeration** - **Refrigeration = Heat removal from a cold space** (not creation of cold). It is a heat transfer process in the unnatural direction. - **Refrigerator** = device that transfers heat from cold to hot reservoir using external work (Second Law compliance). - **Refrigerant** = working fluid undergoing a thermodynamic cycle (examples: R-12, R-22, R-134a, R-717, R-744). - **First Law for cyclic device:** $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$ — universal and must be applied to **all** refrigeration problems. - **COP of Refrigerator:** $\text{COP}_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$ — dimensionless, higher = better. - **COP of Heat Pump:** $\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net}}$ — always greater than or equal to 1. - **Golden Relation:** $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$ — a very frequently tested conversion in board exams. - **Carnot COP Formula:** $\text{COP}_{Carnot,R} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ — uses **absolute temperature in Kelvin** (always add 273 or 273.15). - **1 TR = 3.517 kW** — universally accepted board value; do not derive unless asked. - **Typical Temperatures (K):** Household fridge cold side ≈ 263 K; AC evaporator ≈ 280 K; surroundings ≈ 305 K. - **Real COP is always less than Carnot COP** — because real processes are irreversible; Carnot is the theoretical maximum. - **COP of Heat Pump ≥ 1 always**; **COP of Refrigerator can be greater than or less than 1** depending on application. - **Most common refrigeration cycle:** Vapor Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRC) — covered in depth in Module 2. - **Common student pitfalls to avoid:** (1) Using °C instead of K in Carnot formula → 4-mark deduction risk; (2) Forgetting units in numericals → 1-mark deduction; (3) Using $\text{COP}_R$ formula for a heat pump problem → full marks lost. - **High-yield exam topics:** Unit of refrigeration, definition of COP, Carnot COP derivation, First Law application, relationship between refrigerator and heat pump COPs. > [!WARNING] > **Final KTU Examiner Warning:** Numerical problems on this topic almost always test **(a) unit conversions (°C to K), (b) TR to kW conversion, and (c) the COP-relation trick.** Practice at least 5 numericals involving Carnot cycles before the exam. Marks are awarded for **clean, step-by-step working** — not just the final answer.

topics.title: Background with Second Law of Thermodynamics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Background with Second Law of Thermodynamics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Refrigeration and Air Conditioning

**Refrigeration** is the branch of engineering science which deals with the process of **removing heat from a low-temperature body/system and transferring it to a high-temperature body/system** with the help of an external agent (work input or heat input from a higher temperature source).

In simple terms, refrigeration is the process of **cooling a substance below the temperature of its surroundings**.

**Air Conditioning** is the process of **simultaneously controlling the temperature, humidity, air motion, cleanliness (purity), and distribution of air** in an enclosed space to maintain a specified level of comfort.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:**
> - **Refrigeration** = Primarily focuses on *temperature reduction* and heat removal.
> - **Air Conditioning** = Encompasses *temperature + humidity + air quality + distribution*.

## 1.2 Historical Background of Refrigeration

| Era | Development |
|-----|-------------|
| **Ancient Times** (Pre-1000 BC) | Egyptians, Chinese, and Indians used natural ice harvested from lakes and mountains for cooling. |
| **17th Century** | Sir William Cullen (1748) demonstrated artificial cooling using evaporative principles. |
| **18th Century** | Oliver Evans (1805) designed the first closed-cycle vapor compression refrigeration system. |
| **19th Century** | Jacob Perkins (1834) built the first practical vapor compression refrigeration machine. |
| **20th Century** | Development of ammonia, Freon (CFCs) refrigerants; later environmental concerns led to HFCs and natural refrigerants. |
| **21st Century** | Focus on eco-friendly refrigerants (CO₂, ammonia, hydrocarbons), magnetic refrigeration, and energy-efficient systems. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Modern refrigeration is dominated by the **Vapor Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS)** and the **Vapor Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS)**.

## 1.3 Second Law of Thermodynamics — The Foundation

The **Second Law of Thermodynamics** is the cornerstone of refrigeration and air conditioning engineering. While the First Law establishes *conservation of energy*, the Second Law dictates the **direction of heat flow** and the **feasibility of a process**.

### Kelvin-Planck Statement
> "It is impossible to construct a device operating in a cycle that produces no other effect than the extraction of heat from a single reservoir and the performance of an equivalent amount of work."

In simpler terms: **You cannot convert heat completely into work in a cyclic process.**

### Clausius Statement
> "It is impossible to construct a device operating in a cycle that produces no other effect than the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body without any external work or energy input."

In simpler terms: **Heat cannot spontaneously flow from a cold body to a hot body.** This is why refrigerators *need work input*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Clausius Statement is the **direct basis for all refrigeration systems**. Without work input, heat cannot be moved from a cold region to a hot region.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The Refrigeration "Ladder" Analogy

Imagine you are at the bottom of a hill with a bucket of water. The cold water is at the bottom (cold reservoir), and you need to pour it onto the top of a wall (hot reservoir). The Second Law says **water cannot flow uphill by itself** — you need a pump (work input) to lift it.

- The **bucket of water** = Heat extracted from the cold space.
- The **pump** = Compressor (which requires work input).
- The **wall top** = The hot reservoir (ambient surroundings).
- The **flow of water** = Heat energy.

This is exactly what a refrigerator does — it **"pumps" heat from cold to hot**, but only by consuming external work.

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Universal Gas Constant (R):** **R = 8.314 kJ/kmol·K**
- **1 Ton of Refrigeration (TR):** **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 210 kJ/min**
- **1 kCal:** **1 kCal = 4.186 kJ**
- **Specific Heat of Water (c_p):** **c_p = 4.186 kJ/kg·K**
- **Latent Heat of Fusion of Ice:** **h_f = 335 kJ/kg**
- **Latent Heat of Vaporization of Water:** **h_fg = 2260 kJ/kg at 100°C**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Need for Refrigeration

Refrigeration is essential across multiple engineering and societal domains:

- **Food Preservation:** Slowing bacterial growth and enzymatic reactions in perishable food items.
- **Comfort Cooling:** Air conditioning of residential, commercial, and industrial spaces.
- **Industrial Processes:** Liquefaction of gases (O₂, N₂, H₂), petroleum refining, chemical processing.
- **Medical Applications:** Preservation of vaccines, blood, organs, and pharmaceutical products.
- **Cryogenics:** Achieving ultra-low temperatures for superconducting magnets, space technology, and scientific research.
- **Cold Storage:** Storage of agricultural produce, dairy products, and frozen foods at controlled temperatures.

## 2.2 Unit of Refrigeration — Ton of Refrigeration (TR)

The **standard unit of refrigeration capacity** is the **Ton of Refrigeration (TR)**.

**Definition:** One ton of refrigeration is the amount of heat required to be removed to freeze **1 ton (1000 kg) of water at 0°C into ice at 0°C in 24 hours**.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the most commonly used practical unit in KTU exam problems. Always convert TR to kW when using SI units.

## 2.3 Coefficient of Performance (COP) — The Heart of Refrigeration

The **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** is the figure of merit for a refrigeration/heat pump system. It is defined as the **ratio of desired effect to the work input**.

### 2.3.1 COP of a Refrigerator (COP_R)

$$COP_{R} = \frac{\text{Desired Effect (Heat Extracted from Cold Space)}}{\text{Work Input to Compressor}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$$

where:
- $Q_L$ = Heat extracted from the cold space (refrigerating effect), in **kW** or **kJ/s**.
- $W_{net}$ = Net work input to the cycle, in **kW** or **kJ/s**.

### 2.3.2 COP of a Heat Pump (COP_HP)

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{\text{Desired Effect (Heat Delivered to Hot Space)}}{\text{Work Input}} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}}$$

### 2.3.3 Relationship Between COP_R and COP_HP

By the First Law of Thermodynamics: $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$

Therefore:

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_L + W_{net}}{W_{net}} = COP_R + 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **COP is always greater than 1** for any practical refrigeration system. A higher COP means a more efficient system.

## 2.4 The Carnot Refrigeration Cycle — Theoretical Benchmark

The **Reversed Carnot Cycle** is the **most efficient refrigeration cycle** operating between two given temperature limits. It serves as the **theoretical benchmark** against which all real refrigeration cycles are compared.

### 2.4.1 Carnot COP (Refrigerator)

$$COP_{Carnot,R} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$

where:
- $T_L$ = Absolute temperature of the cold space, in **K**.
- $T_H$ = Absolute temperature of the hot space (sink), in **K**.

### 2.4.2 Carnot COP (Heat Pump)

$$COP_{Carnot,HP} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$$

### 2.4.3 Significance of Carnot COP

- **No real refrigeration cycle can have a COP greater than the Carnot COP** operating between the same temperature limits.
- The Carnot COP depends **only on the temperature limits** ($T_L$ and $T_H$), not on the working fluid.
- As $T_H - T_L$ increases, the COP decreases, meaning **larger temperature differences require more work input**.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$ | Ton of refrigeration to kW | kW |
| 2 | $1 \text{ TR} = 210 \text{ kJ/min}$ | Ton of refrigeration to kJ/min | kJ/min |
| 3 | $1 \text{ TR} = \dfrac{14000 \text{ kJ}}{3600 \text{ s}} \approx 3.89 \text{ kW}$ (US definition) | US Ton of refrigeration | kW |
| 4 | $COP_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$ | COP of a refrigerator | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net}}$ | COP of a heat pump | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ | Relation between HP and Ref. COP | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $COP_{Carnot,R} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Carnot COP (Refrigerator) | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $COP_{Carnot,HP} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ | Carnot COP (Heat Pump) | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$ | Energy balance (First Law) | kW |
| 10 | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Carnot Heat Engine Efficiency | Dimensionless |
| 11 | $COP_{Carnot,R} \cdot \eta_{Carnot} = 1$ | Product relation (Refrigerator) | Dimensionless |
| 12 | $\text{HP} = \dfrac{3}{4} \cdot \text{TR}$ (1 HP ≈ 0.75 TR) | Horsepower to TR conversion | HP |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Conversion:** When a problem gives temperatures in °C, **always convert to Kelvin** (K = °C + 273.15) before computing Carnot COP. This is a frequent mistake point in KTU valuations.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HVAC Industry:** COP is used to rate the energy efficiency of air conditioners (EER, SEER in US; EER in India — 1 EER = COP/0.293).
- **Refrigerant Selection:** Lower temperature applications require refrigerants with lower boiling points (e.g., R-23 for -80°C).
- **Cold Chain Logistics:** COP directly determines the operating cost of cold storage facilities.
- **Energy Policy:** Indian Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) uses star ratings based on Indian Seasonal Energy Efficiency Ratio (ISEER), which is directly related to COP.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of 1 Ton of Refrigeration in kW

**Given:**
- 1 Ton = 1000 kg of water at 0°C converted to ice at 0°C in 24 hours.
- Latent heat of fusion of ice, $h_{if}$ = **335 kJ/kg** (standard value at 0°C).
- Time = 24 hours = 24 × 3600 s = 86400 s.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total heat to be removed } (Q) &= m \times h_{if} \\
Q &= 1000 \text{ kg} \times 335 \text{ kJ/kg} \\
Q &= 335000 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rate of heat removal (in kJ/s)} &= \frac{Q}{\text{Time in seconds}} \\
&= \frac{335000 \text{ kJ}}{86400 \text{ s}} \\
&= 3.877 \text{ kJ/s} \\
&= 3.877 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standardly, **1 TR ≈ 3.517 kW** is often used in KTU problems (based on the older definition using 1390 BTU/min or 200 BTU/lb). However, the modern SI definition using 335 kJ/kg gives **3.877 kW**. **Always check the problem's stated conversion factor.**

## 3.2 Derivation of the Relationship: COP_HP = COP_R + 1

**Starting Point — First Law Applied to Refrigeration Cycle:**

For a cyclic refrigeration system, energy balance gives:

$$Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$$

This is because all the heat extracted from the cold space ($Q_L$) plus the work input ($W_{net}$) is rejected to the hot space ($Q_H$).

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
COP_{HP} &= \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}} \quad \text{[Definition]} \\
&= \frac{Q_L + W_{net}}{W_{net}} \quad \text{[Substituting } Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}\text{]} \\
&= \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} + \frac{W_{net}}{W_{net}} \\
&= \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} + 1 \\
&= COP_R + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Explanation:** The heat pump delivers *more* useful heat ($Q_H$) than the work input ($W_{net}$) because it also "delivers" the heat that was originally extracted from the cold space. Hence, COP_HP is always exactly 1 unit higher than COP_R.

## 3.3 Derivation of Carnot COP (Refrigerator)

**Setup:** Consider a Carnot cycle operating in *reverse* between a cold reservoir at $T_L$ (in Kelvin) and a hot reservoir at $T_H$ (in Kelvin).

The Carnot heat engine efficiency is:

$$\eta_{Carnot} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{net} &= Q_H - Q_L \quad \text{[First Law]} \\
COP_R &= \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \frac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $Q_L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
COP_R &= \frac{1}{\dfrac{Q_H}{Q_L} - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a Carnot cycle, the ratio of heat transfer to absolute temperature is constant:

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_L}{T_L} \implies \frac{Q_H}{Q_L} = \frac{T_H}{T_L}$$

Substituting this into the COP expression:

$$
\begin{aligned}
COP_R &= \frac{1}{\dfrac{T_H}{T_L} - 1} \\
&= \frac{1}{\dfrac{T_H - T_L}{T_L}} \\
&= \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{COP_{Carnot,R} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}}$$

## 3.4 Verification: COP_Carnot,R × η_Carnot = 1

$$
\begin{aligned}
COP_{Carnot,R} \times \eta_{Carnot} &= \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} \times \left(1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}\right) \\
&= \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} \times \left(\frac{T_H - T_L}{T_H}\right) \\
&= \frac{T_L}{T_H}
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — this gives $\frac{T_L}{T_H}$, not 1. The correct relation is:

**For a Heat Engine + Refrigerator combination operating as a compound device:**

$$COP_{Carnot,R} = \frac{1}{\eta_{Carnot}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} \quad \text{and} \quad \eta_{Carnot} = \frac{T_H - T_L}{T_H}$$

These two are reciprocals of each other. The product $COP_{Carnot,R} \times \eta_{Carnot}$ does **NOT** equal 1 in general.

**Correct Note:** The relationship $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ is the *additive* one. The *multiplicative* relation:

$$COP_{Carnot,R} = \frac{1}{\eta_{Carnot}}$$

is only true if we redefine the heat engine's reservoir. The standard KTU formula to remember is:

$$\boxed{COP_{Carnot,R} = \frac{1}{\eta_{Carnot}} \quad \text{when the heat engine's cold reservoir is the refrigerator's hot space, etc.}}$$

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — Typical KTU 2-Mark Conversion

**Problem:** A refrigerator operates between -10°C and 35°C. Calculate the Carnot COP.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_L &= -10 + 273 = 263 \text{ K} \\
T_H &= 35 + 273 = 308 \text{ K} \\
COP_{Carnot,R} &= \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} \\
&= \frac{263}{308 - 263} \\
&= \frac{263}{45} \\
&= 5.844
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $COP_{Carnot,R} \approx 5.84$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Temperature on COP

| Scenario | Change | Effect on COP | Engineering Implication |
|----------|--------|---------------|-------------------------|
| Cold space temperature ($T_L$) **decreases** | $T_L \downarrow$ | COP **decreases** sharply | More work needed for deeper cooling |
| Hot space temperature ($T_H$) **increases** | $T_H \uparrow$ | COP **decreases** | Hotter ambient = less efficient AC |
| Both $T_L$ and $T_H$ are closer | $T_H - T_L \downarrow$ | COP **increases** | Less temperature lift = easier work |
| Both $T_L$ and $T_H$ are far apart | $T_H - T_L \uparrow$ | COP **decreases** | Cryogenic applications need very high work input |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Schematic — Basic Refrigeration Cycle Concept

The following Mermaid block diagrams the fundamental heat flow in a refrigeration cycle as a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["COLD SPACE QL"] --> nodeB["EVAPORATOR Absorbs Heat QL"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["COMPRESSOR Work Input Wnet"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["CONDENSER Rejects Heat QH"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["EXPANSION VALVE Pressure Drop"]
    nodeE --> nodeB
    nodeD --> nodeF["HOT SINK Surroundings QH Rejected"]

    style nodeA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000000
    style nodeB fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000000
    style nodeC fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000000
    style nodeD fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000000
    style nodeE fill:#e6b8b7,stroke:#990000,color:#000000
    style nodeF fill:#c9daf8,stroke:#1155cc,color:#000000
```

**Interpretation:**
- The **Cold Space** provides the heat $Q_L$ that is absorbed by the evaporator.
- The **Compressor** consumes work $W_{net}$ to raise the refrigerant pressure/temperature.
- The **Condenser** rejects the combined heat $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$ to the hot sink.
- The **Expansion Valve** drops the pressure to complete the cycle.

## 4.2 Second Law Schematic — Heat Flow Direction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["HOT RESERVOIR TH"] -->|"QH Rejected"| node2["REFRIGERATION SYSTEM"]
    node2 -->|"QL Absorbed"| node3["COLD RESERVOIR TL"]
    node2 -->|"Wnet Work Input"| node4["EXTERNAL SOURCE Compressor"]

    style node1 fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000000
    style node2 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000000
    style node3 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000000
    style node4 fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#674ea7,color:#000000
```

**Interpretation:**
- The Clausius Statement is illustrated: **Heat cannot flow from Cold to Hot spontaneously**; external work is mandatory.
- $T_H > T_L$ always; the refrigeration system is a "heat pump" operating against the natural direction.

## 4.3 Refrigeration Capacity Hierarchy (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["REFRIGERATION SYSTEMS"] --> nodeB["VAPOR COMPRESSION VCRS"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["VAPOR ABSORPTION VARS"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["GAS CYCLE REFRIGERATION"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["STEAM JET REFRIGERATION"]
    nodeA --> nodeF["CRYOGENIC LIQUEFACTION"]

    nodeB --> nodeG["MOST COMMON Domestic AC Fridges"]
    nodeC --> nodeH["HEAT DRIVEN No Compressor"]
    nodeD --> nodeI["BELL COLEMAN Aircraft AC"]
    nodeE --> nodeJ["INDUSTRIAL Large Tonnage"]

    style nodeA fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000000
    style nodeB fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000000
    style nodeC fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000000
    style nodeD fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000000
    style nodeE fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#674ea7,color:#000000
    style nodeF fill:#e6b8b7,stroke:#990000,color:#000000
    style nodeG fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#38761d,color:#000000
    style nodeH fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#1155cc,color:#000000
    style nodeI fill:#ea9999,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000000
    style nodeJ fill:#b4a7d6,stroke:#674ea7,color:#000000
```

**Interpretation:**
- This block architecture map shows the **major families of refrigeration systems** that KTU Module 1 will progressively explore.
- **VCRS** (Vapor Compression) is the most widely used in domestic and commercial applications.
- **VARS** (Vapor Absorption) is preferred where waste heat is available.
- **Gas Cycle** is used in aircraft air conditioning (Bell Coleman cycle).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**State and explain the Clausius statement of the Second Law of Thermodynamics. How is it relevant to refrigeration systems?**

**Model Answer:**

The Clausius statement of the Second Law of Thermodynamics states: *"It is impossible to construct a device operating in a cycle that produces no other effect than the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body without any external work or energy input."*

**Relevance to Refrigeration:**
- A refrigeration system essentially transfers heat from a low-temperature region (cold space) to a high-temperature region (surroundings).
- According to the Clausius statement, this heat transfer **cannot occur spontaneously**; it requires an **external work input** (typically through a compressor).
- Therefore, the Clausius statement provides the **fundamental thermodynamic justification** for why all refrigeration systems must consume work to function.
- The **COP of any refrigerator** is derived directly from this law's constraints.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define Ton of Refrigeration (TR). Convert 1 TR to kW.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** One Ton of Refrigeration (TR) is defined as the amount of heat required to be removed to freeze **1 metric ton (1000 kg) of water at 0°C into ice at 0°C in 24 hours**.

**Conversion to kW:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Heat to be removed} &= m \times L_f \\
&= 1000 \text{ kg} \times 335 \text{ kJ/kg} \\
&= 335000 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 \text{ TR} &= \frac{335000 \text{ kJ}}{24 \times 3600 \text{ s}} \\
&= \frac{335000}{86400} \text{ kW} \\
&\approx 3.877 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** The standard textbook value used in most KTU problems is **1 TR ≈ 3.517 kW** (based on short ton of 2000 lb and 144 BTU/lb). Always verify with the problem's given conversion.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**A refrigerator operates between the temperature limits of -10°C and 35°C. The refrigerator maintains the cold space at -10°C by removing heat at the rate of 5 kW. Calculate:**
**(a)** The Carnot COP of the refrigerator and the COP of the heat pump.
**(b)** The power required by the compressor in kW, and the rate of heat rejection to the surroundings.

#### Part (a) — Model Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Convert temperatures to Kelvin:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_L &= -10 + 273 = 263 \text{ K} \\
T_H &= 35 + 273 = 308 \text{ K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Carnot COP of the refrigerator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
COP_{Carnot,R} &= \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} \\
&= \frac{263}{308 - 263} \\
&= \frac{263}{45} \\
&= 5.844
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Carnot COP of the heat pump:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
COP_{Carnot,HP} &= COP_{Carnot,R} + 1 \\
&= 5.844 + 1 \\
&= 6.844
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b) — Model Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Calculate power required (assuming Carnot/reversible operation):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{net} &= \frac{Q_L}{COP_{Carnot,R}} \\
&= \frac{5 \text{ kW}}{5.844} \\
&= 0.8555 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate rate of heat rejection:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_H &= Q_L + W_{net} \\
&= 5 + 0.8555 \\
&= 5.8555 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $COP_{Carnot,R} = 5.844$
- $COP_{Carnot,HP} = 6.844$
- $W_{net} = 0.856 \text{ kW}$
- $Q_H = 5.856 \text{ kW}$

---

### Question B (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**A refrigeration system has a COP of 3.2 and a refrigerating capacity of 10 TR. Calculate:**
**(a)** The power consumption of the compressor in kW and the heat rejected to the surroundings per hour.
**(b)** If the system operates between -5°C and 40°C, determine the second-law efficiency (ratio of actual COP to Carnot COP) of the system.

#### Part (a) — Model Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Convert 10 TR to kW:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_L &= 10 \times 3.517 \\
&= 35.17 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate power consumed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{net} &= \frac{Q_L}{COP_R} \\
&= \frac{35.17}{3.2} \\
&= 10.99 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate heat rejected per second and per hour:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_H &= Q_L + W_{net} \\
&= 35.17 + 10.99 \\
&= 46.16 \text{ kW} \\
Q_{H,\text{per hour}} &= 46.16 \times 3600 \\
&= 166176 \text{ kJ/hr} \\
&= 46.16 \text{ MJ/hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b) — Model Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Convert temperatures to Kelvin:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_L &= -5 + 273 = 268 \text{ K} \\
T_H &= 40 + 273 = 313 \text{ K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Carnot COP:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
COP_{Carnot,R} &= \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} \\
&= \frac{268}{313 - 268} \\
&= \frac{268}{45} \\
&= 5.956
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Second-Law (Exergy) Efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{II} &= \frac{COP_{actual}}{COP_{Carnot}} \\
&= \frac{3.2}{5.956} \\
&= 0.5373 \\
&= 53.73\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $W_{net} = 10.99 \text{ kW}$
- $Q_H = 46.16 \text{ kW}$ (or 166.18 MJ/hr)
- Second-Law Efficiency = **53.73%**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Temperature Unit Error:** Forgetting to convert °C to Kelvin. This is the **#1 reason** for wrong answers in Carnot COP problems. *Always use absolute temperatures.*
> 2. **TR Conversion Confusion:** Using 3.517 kW vs 3.877 kW inconsistently within the same problem. *Stick to one conversion as per problem statement.*
> 3. **Sign Convention:** Heat rejected $Q_H$ is always **positive** when written in absolute terms. Work input is **positive** in refrigeration cycle conventions.
> 4. **COP vs Efficiency Confusion:** COP is **not an efficiency**; it can be greater than 1. Don't say "COP is 320% efficient" — say "COP is 3.2".
> 5. **Formula Misuse:** Using $COP_{Carnot,HP}$ when the problem asks for refrigerator COP. *Always re-read the problem statement carefully.*
> 6. **Missing Units in Final Answer:** KTU examiners **deduct 0.5 to 1 mark** for missing units in the final boxed answer.
> 7. **Not Drawing P-V or T-S Diagrams:** For Part B questions worth 14 marks, **always include a schematic diagram** of the refrigeration cycle to fetch full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Refrigeration** = Heat removal from a cold space using external work.
- **Air Conditioning** = Temperature + Humidity + Air Quality + Air Distribution control.
- **1 TR** = 3.517 kW (standard KTU value) = 210 kJ/min.
- **Clausius Statement** = Heat cannot flow from cold to hot without work input.
- **Kelvin-Planck Statement** = Heat cannot be fully converted to work in a cycle.
- **COP_R = Q_L / W_net** (always > 1 for real systems).
- **COP_HP = Q_H / W_net = COP_R + 1**.
- **Carnot COP (Refrigerator) = T_L / (T_H - T_L)** — temperatures **must be in Kelvin**.
- **Carnot COP is the maximum possible COP** between two given temperature limits.
- **Higher temperature difference → Lower COP → More work required.**
- **Lower cold space temperature → Lower COP → More work required for deep cooling.**
- **COP is dimensionless but always carries a numerical value > 1 for refrigeration systems.**
- **Second-Law Efficiency = (Actual COP) / (Carnot COP) × 100%** — a measure of how close a real system is to ideal.
- **Energy Balance:** $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$ (First Law applied to refrigeration cycle).
- **Modern refrigerants trend:** Moving from CFCs/HCFCs to HFCs and natural refrigerants (CO₂, ammonia, hydrocarbons) due to ozone depletion and global warming concerns.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Unit of Refrigerating Capacity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Refrigerating Capacity** is defined as the quantity of heat energy that a refrigeration system is capable of removing from a refrigerated space (or substance) per unit of time, under specified operating conditions. It is the most fundamental performance rating parameter for any refrigeration, air conditioning, or cold storage equipment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Definition:**
> *Refrigerating Capacity (R) = Rate of heat extraction from the cold body in unit time.*
> 
> $$\dot{Q}_L = \frac{Q_L}{t} \quad \text{[W or kW]}$$

where:
- $\dot{Q}_L$ = Rate of heat removal (Refrigerating Effect)
- $Q_L$ = Total heat removed
- $t$ = Time

The **SI Unit** of refrigerating capacity is the **Watt (W)** or **Kilowatt (kW)**, but in engineering practice, especially in India, the **Ton of Refrigeration (TR)** is the most widely used commercial unit.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Ice-Melting" Analogy:**
> 
> Imagine a block of pure ice weighing exactly **1 metric ton (1000 kg)** at **0 °C**. To melt this ice completely into water at 0 °C, a specific quantity of heat energy must be supplied — this is known as the *latent heat of fusion* of ice (**335 kJ/kg** or **80 kcal/kg**).
> 
> If you run a refrigeration system that is powerful enough to melt this entire 1-ton block of ice in exactly **24 hours**, then the rate at which it extracts heat equals **1 Ton of Refrigeration (1 TR)**.
> 
> In essence: **1 TR = The cooling power needed to freeze 1 ton of water at 0 °C into ice at 0 °C in 24 hours.**

This historical, practical origin is why the refrigeration industry retains "Tons" as a unit even today, despite the SI system preferring kilowatts.

## 1.3 Key Reference Constants

| Constant | Value | Significance |
|----------|-------|--------------|
| **Latent heat of fusion of ice** | **335 kJ/kg** (or 80 kcal/kg) | Heat needed to melt 1 kg of ice at 0 °C |
| **1 Metric Ton** | **1000 kg** | Base mass reference for TR |
| **Standard reference temperature** | **0 °C (32 °F)** | Evaporator and ice temperature baseline |
| **Time period** | **24 hours (1440 minutes)** | Standard rating duration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 scheme specifically expects students to master the conversion between **TR, kW, kcal/hr, and BTU/hr** because real-world HVAC equipment is often dual-rated in multiple units.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Ton of Refrigeration (TR) — Origin and Definition

The "Ton of Refrigeration" is a unit of refrigerating capacity that emerged during the early days of commercial refrigeration in the late 19th century, when ice was the primary cooling medium. Engineers wanted a way to express the cooling capacity of mechanical refrigeration machines in terms that could be directly compared to natural ice blocks.

### 2.1.1 Mathematical Derivation Logic of 1 TR

To compute the refrigerating effect equivalent to melting 1 ton of ice in 24 hours:

**Step 1 — Latent heat available in 1 ton of ice:**
$$Q_L = m \times L_f = 1000 \text{ kg} \times 335 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Convert time to seconds or minutes:**
$$t = 24 \text{ hr} = 24 \times 3600 \text{ s} = 86400 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute rate of heat extraction:**
$$\dot{Q}_L = \frac{1000 \times 335}{86400} \text{ kW}$$

This yields the standard value of **1 TR ≈ 3.517 kW**.

## 2.2 Other Common Units of Refrigerating Capacity

| Unit | Symbol | Common Use Case |
|------|--------|-----------------|
| Ton of Refrigeration | TR | Commercial HVAC, Cold Storage (India) |
| Kilowatt | kW | SI Unit, technical specifications |
| Kilo-calorie per hour | kcal/hr | European, Indian process industries |
| British Thermal Unit per hour | BTU/hr | US, UK HVAC industry |
| Horsepower (Refrigeration) | HP | Older compressor ratings (1 HP ≈ 0.985 TR) |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the table below — these conversion factors are tested every year in KTU exams.**

| # | Conversion Identity | Numerical Value | Reverse Conversion |
|---|---------------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **1 TR = 3.517 kW** | (≈ 3.51685 kW) | **1 kW = 0.2843 TR** |
| 2 | **1 TR = 3024 kcal/hr** | (≈ 50.4 kcal/min) | **1 kcal/hr = 3.307 × 10⁻⁴ TR** |
| 3 | **1 TR = 12,000 BTU/hr** | (200 BTU/min) | **1 BTU/hr = 8.333 × 10⁻⁵ TR** |
| 4 | **1 TR = 50 kcal/min** | Exact (approx.) | **1 kcal/min = 0.02 TR** |
| 5 | **1 kW = 859.85 kcal/hr** | Thermal equivalent of work | **1 kcal/hr = 1.163 × 10⁻³ kW** |
| 6 | **1 BTU = 1055.056 J** | Imperial-SI conversion | **1 kJ = 0.9478 BTU** |
| 7 | **1 kcal = 4186.8 J** | SI equivalent of calorie | **1 kJ = 0.2388 kcal** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Shortcut for Exams:**
> $$\text{TR} \to \text{kW}: \text{Multiply by } 3.517$$
> $$\text{kW} \to \text{TR}: \text{Divide by } 3.517 \text{ (or multiply by } 0.2843)$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cold Storage Sizing:** Designers calculate the total cooling load of a cold room (transmission load, product load, respiration load) and select compressors rated in **TR**.
- **Chiller Selection:** HVAC consultants rate chillers in **TR** (e.g., 100 TR chiller) for building AC systems.
- **Refrigerant Selection:** The capacity in **kW** is required to match the evaporator enthalpy drop $\Delta h$ with the mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ of refrigerant:

$$\dot{Q}_L = \dot{m} \times (h_g - h_f) = \dot{m} \times \Delta h_{evap} \quad \text{[kW]}$$

- **Energy Auditing:** Heat extraction in **BTU/hr** is often used in US-standard Energy Performance Index (EPI) calculations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: 1 TR in kW

### Step 1 — Establish the mass of ice

We are given **1 metric ton** of ice:
$$m = 1 \text{ ton} = 1000 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 2 — Establish the latent heat of fusion of ice

The latent heat of fusion of pure ice at 0 °C is:
$$L_f = 335 \text{ kJ/kg} = 335{,}000 \text{ J/kg}$$

> **Conversion logic:** The Joule equivalent of 1 kcal is 4.1868 kJ, so 80 kcal/kg × 4.1868 ≈ 334.94 kJ/kg, rounded to **335 kJ/kg**.

### Step 3 — Compute the total heat absorbed in melting 1 ton of ice

$$Q_L = m \times L_f$$

Substituting values:
$$Q_L = 1000 \text{ kg} \times 335 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$Q_L = 335{,}000 \text{ kJ} = 3.35 \times 10^5 \text{ kJ}$$

### Step 4 — Convert the time period of 24 hours to seconds

$$t = 24 \text{ hr} = 24 \times 60 \text{ min} = 1440 \text{ min}$$

$$t = 1440 \times 60 \text{ s} = 86{,}400 \text{ s} = 8.64 \times 10^4 \text{ s}$$

### Step 5 — Compute the rate of heat extraction (Refrigerating Capacity)

$$\dot{Q}_L = \frac{Q_L}{t}$$

$$\dot{Q}_L = \frac{3.35 \times 10^5 \text{ kJ}}{8.64 \times 10^4 \text{ s}}$$

$$\dot{Q}_L = 3.8767... \text{ kJ/s}$$

### Step 6 — Convert kJ/s to kW

Since $1 \text{ kJ/s} = 1 \text{ kW}$:

$$\boxed{\dot{Q}_L = 3.877 \text{ kW} \approx 3.517 \text{ kW}}$$

> **Note on the slight numerical discrepancy:** The exact textbook value uses **Latent heat = 335 kJ/kg** for pure ice or **latent heat = 80 kcal/kg**. The factor **3.517 kW** is the universally accepted standard rounded value. Some references use **3.51685 kW** for higher precision.

## 3.2 Derivation: 1 TR in kcal/hr

Using the same mass and time:

### Step 1 — Total heat in kcal
$$Q_L = 1000 \text{ kg} \times 80 \text{ kcal/kg} = 80{,}000 \text{ kcal}$$

### Step 2 — Time in hours
$$t = 24 \text{ hr}$$

### Step 3 — Rate of heat extraction
$$\dot{Q}_L = \frac{80{,}000 \text{ kcal}}{24 \text{ hr}} = 3333.33 \text{ kcal/hr}$$

### Step 4 — Using latent heat 79.7 kcal/kg (Refrigeration engineering standard)
$$Q_L = 1000 \times 79.7 = 79{,}700 \text{ kcal}$$

$$\dot{Q}_L = \frac{79{,}700}{24} = 3320.8 \text{ kcal/hr}$$

This yields the standard approximation:
$$\boxed{1 \text{ TR} \approx 3024 \text{ kcal/hr} \text{ to } 3320 \text{ kcal/hr}}$$

> **Engineering convention:** In Indian engineering practice, **1 TR = 3024 kcal/hr (or 3.024 kcal/s × 1000 = 50.4 kcal/min)** is the most commonly used value.

## 3.3 Derivation: 1 TR in BTU/hr

### Step 1 — Convert total heat to BTU

Using 1 kcal = 3.968 BTU:
$$Q_L = 80{,}000 \text{ kcal} \times 3.968 \text{ BTU/kcal}$$

$$Q_L = 317{,}440 \text{ BTU}$$

### Step 2 — Time conversion (24 hours)
$$t = 24 \text{ hr}$$

### Step 3 — Rate of heat extraction
$$\dot{Q}_L = \frac{317{,}440 \text{ BTU}}{24 \text{ hr}}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{Q}_L = 13{,}226.67 \text{ BTU/hr} \approx 12{,}000 \text{ BTU/hr}}$$

The standard engineering rounded value is **1 TR = 12,000 BTU/hr**.

## 3.4 Analytical Conversion Matrix

The matrix below allows direct cross-conversion between units (read the row to convert from, the column to convert to):

| From ↓ / To → | **TR** | **kW** | **kcal/hr** | **BTU/hr** |
|---------------|--------|--------|-------------|------------|
| **1 TR** | 1 | 3.517 | 3024 | 12,000 |
| **1 kW** | 0.2843 | 1 | 859.85 | 3412.14 |
| **1 kcal/hr** | 3.307 × 10⁻⁴ | 1.163 × 10⁻³ | 1 | 3.968 |
| **1 BTU/hr** | 8.333 × 10⁻⁵ | 2.931 × 10⁻⁴ | 0.252 | 1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU paper setters frequently give values in **kW** and ask students to express in **TR** (or vice-versa). Memorize: **1 kW ≈ 0.2843 TR** and **1 TR ≈ 3.517 kW**.

## 3.5 Sample Problem Demonstrating Mastery

**Problem:** A cold storage requires a refrigerating capacity of **250 kW**. Express this in:
- (a) TR
- (b) kcal/hr
- (c) BTU/hr

### Solution:

**Part (a):** Convert kW → TR
$$R = 250 \text{ kW} \times \frac{1 \text{ TR}}{3.517 \text{ kW}} = 71.08 \text{ TR}$$

**Part (b):** Convert kW → kcal/hr (using 1 kW = 859.85 kcal/hr)
$$R = 250 \times 859.85 = 214{,}962.5 \text{ kcal/hr}$$

**Part (c):** Convert kW → BTU/hr (using 1 kW = 3412.14 BTU/hr)
$$R = 250 \times 3412.14 = 853{,}035 \text{ BTU/hr}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concept Map: Units of Refrigerating Capacity

The following block-level architecture flow visualizes the relationships between the major units and their underlying physical definitions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Refrigerating Capacity\n(Heat extracted per unit time)"] --> B["Historical Base:\n1 Ton of Ice in 24 hrs"]
    A --> C["SI Unit:\nKilowatt (kW)"]
    A --> D["Commercial Unit:\nTon of Refrigeration (TR)"]
    A --> E["Imperial Unit:\nBTU per hour"]
    A --> F["CGS Unit:\nKcal per hour"]

    B --> B1["Latent Heat of Fusion\n335 kJ/kg or 80 kcal/kg"]
    B --> B2["Mass = 1000 kg\nTime = 24 hrs = 86400 s"]
    B1 --> B3["Total Heat = 3.35e5 kJ"]
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4["Q/t = 3.517 kW"]
    B4 --> D

    C --> G["Conversion Bridge\n1 kW = 0.2843 TR"]
    D --> G
    E --> H["Conversion Bridge\n1 TR = 12000 BTU/hr"]
    F --> I["Conversion Bridge\n1 TR = 3024 kcal/hr"]
    G --> J["Unified Rating\nof HVAC Equipment"]
    H --> J
    I --> J
```

## 4.2 Process Diagram: Derivation Flow of 1 TR

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Start: 1 Metric Ton Ice\nat 0 degree C"] --> S2["Multiply by Latent Heat\n335 kJ/kg"]
    S2 --> S3["Total Heat Q = 3.35e5 kJ"]
    S3 --> S4["Divide by Time\nt = 24 hr = 86400 s"]
    S4 --> S5["Q dot = 3.877 kJ/s"]
    S5 --> S6["Convert to kW\n1 kJ/s = 1 kW"]
    S6 --> S7["Final Result:\n1 TR = 3.517 kW"]

    S1 --> P1["Parallel Path:\n80 kcal/kg used"]
    P1 --> P2["Total Heat = 80000 kcal"]
    P2 --> P3["Divide by 24 hr"]
    P3 --> P4["Q dot = 3333 kcal/hr"]
    P4 --> S7
```

## 4.3 Application Block Diagram: Where Units are Used

```mermaid

    APP --> CS["Cold Storage Plants\nRated in TR"]

    APP --> EXP["Export Equipment\nRated in BTU/hr"]
    APP --> LAB["Laboratory Calorimeters\nRated in Watts"]

    AC --> AC1["Window AC: 1 to 2 TR\nSplit AC: 1 to 5 TR"]

```

## 4.4 Schematic: Conversion Architecture

```mermaid
    TR <-->|3024| KCAL

```

---
