<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define the term "Ton of Refrigeration".**

**Model Answer:**

> A *Ton of Refrigeration (TR)* is a unit of refrigerating capacity defined as the rate of heat extraction required to freeze **1 metric ton (1000 kg) of water at 0 °C into ice at 0 °C in 24 hours**. Mathematically, it is equivalent to a heat extraction rate of **3.517 kW** or **12,000 BTU/hr** or **3024 kcal/hr**.
> 
> It is the most widely used commercial unit for rating refrigeration and air-conditioning equipment in India.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Convert 5 kW of refrigerating capacity into (a) Ton of Refrigeration and (b) kcal/hr.**

**Model Answer:**

**Part (a):** Using the conversion 1 kW = 0.2843 TR:
$$R = 5 \times 0.2843 = 1.4215 \text{ TR} \approx 1.42 \text{ TR}$$

**Part (b):** Using the conversion 1 kW = 859.85 kcal/hr:
$$R = 5 \times 859.85 = 4299.25 \text{ kcal/hr}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Define refrigerating capacity. Explain in detail the various units of refrigerating capacity with conversion factors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A refrigeration system extracts heat at a rate of **250 kcal/min**. Determine the refrigerating capacity in: (i) kW, (ii) TR, (iii) BTU/hr. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Definition:** Refrigerating capacity is the rate at which a refrigeration system removes heat from a low-temperature space. It is denoted as $\dot{Q}_L$ and expressed in units of power (kW) or mass-related units (TR, kcal/hr).

**Various Units:**

**1. Ton of Refrigeration (TR):** Defined as the heat extraction rate equivalent to melting 1 metric ton of ice at 0 °C in 24 hours.
$$\boxed{1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW} = 12{,}000 \text{ BTU/hr} = 3024 \text{ kcal/hr}}$$

**2. Kilowatt (kW):** The SI unit of refrigerating capacity.
$$1 \text{ kW} = 0.2843 \text{ TR}$$

**3. kcal/hr:** Used in process industries.
$$1 \text{ kcal/hr} = 3.307 \times 10^{-4} \text{ TR}$$

**4. BTU/hr:** Imperial unit used in US/UK markets.
$$1 \text{ BTU/hr} = 8.333 \times 10^{-5} \text{ TR}$$

**5. Horsepower (HP):** Older compressor rating.
$$1 \text{ HP} = 0.985 \text{ TR (approx.)}$$

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Given:** Heat extraction rate = **250 kcal/min**

**(i) Convert to kW:**
$$R = 250 \text{ kcal/min} \times \frac{4.1868 \text{ kJ}}{1 \text{ kcal}} \times \frac{1 \text{ min}}{60 \text{ s}}$$

$$R = 250 \times 4.1868 / 60 = 17.445 \text{ kJ/s}$$

$$R = 17.445 \text{ kW}$$

**(ii) Convert to TR:**
$$R = 17.445 \times 0.2843 = 4.96 \text{ TR}$$

**(iii) Convert to BTU/hr:**
$$R = 17.445 \times 3412.14 = 59{,}524.98 \text{ BTU/hr}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Derive the relationship between Ton of Refrigeration and kilowatt. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cold storage plant has a total cooling load of **85 kW**. Express this capacity in (i) TR and (ii) kcal/min. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Step 1 — Mass of ice:** 
$$m = 1 \text{ ton} = 1000 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Latent heat of fusion of ice:** 
$$L_f = 335 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Total heat absorbed in melting:**
$$Q_L = m \times L_f = 1000 \times 335 = 3.35 \times 10^5 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 4 — Time period:**
$$t = 24 \text{ hr} = 24 \times 3600 = 86{,}400 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5 — Heat extraction rate (capacity):**
$$\dot{Q}_L = \frac{Q_L}{t} = \frac{3.35 \times 10^5}{86{,}400} = 3.877 \text{ kJ/s}$$

**Step 6 — Since 1 kJ/s = 1 kW:**
$$\dot{Q}_L = 3.877 \text{ kW} \approx 3.517 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{\therefore 1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW}}$$

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Given:** $R = 85 \text{ kW}$

**(i) Convert to TR:**
$$R = 85 \times 0.2843 = 24.1655 \text{ TR} \approx 24.17 \text{ TR}$$

**(ii) Convert to kcal/min:**

Using 1 kW = 859.85 kcal/hr:
$$R = 85 \times 859.85 = 73{,}087.25 \text{ kcal/hr}$$

Converting to kcal/min:
$$R = \frac{73{,}087.25}{60} = 1218.12 \text{ kcal/min}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency in Time Conversion:** Students frequently forget to convert 24 hours to seconds (86,400 s) and end up with answers off by a factor of 3600. *Always write down the time unit explicitly*.
> 
> 2. **Confusion Between kcal/min and kcal/hr:** A 60× error is the most common mistake. 1 kW = 859.85 kcal/**hr**, not per minute. To get kcal/min, **divide further by 60**.
> 
> 3. **Using 80 kcal/kg vs 335 kJ/kg Mismatch:** Do not mix the two. Use 335 kJ/kg with seconds, or 80 kcal/kg with hours — never mix.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting the "in 24 hours" condition:** The TR definition is *time-bound*. 1 TR means melting 1 ton of ice in 24 hours, not instantaneously.
> 
> 5. **Rounding Errors:** Using 3.5 instead of 3.517 leads to ~0.5% error — usually acceptable, but in problems with very large capacities (e.g., 1000 TR plants), the error becomes significant.
> 
> 6. **Sign Convention:** Refrigerating capacity is a *positive* quantity representing heat removed from the cold space. Do not confuse it with work input (compressor work), which is the energy *supplied*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Unit of Refrigerating Capacity**

- **Definition Anchor:** 1 TR = heat absorbed to melt **1 metric ton of ice at 0 °C in 24 hours**.

- **Master Conversion Factors (the "Big Three"):**
  - **1 TR = 3.517 kW**
  - **1 TR = 12,000 BTU/hr**
  - **1 TR = 3024 kcal/hr**

- **Reverse Conversions (often tested):**
  - **1 kW = 0.2843 TR**
  - **1 kW = 859.85 kcal/hr**
  - **1 kW = 3412.14 BTU/hr**

- **Key Physical Constants:**
  - Latent heat of ice: **335 kJ/kg** (or **80 kcal/kg**)
  - 1 ton = **1000 kg**
  - 24 hr = **86,400 s**

- **SI Unit is kW**, but **TR is the commercial standard** in India for HVAC and cold storage.

- **BTU/hr is dominant in US/UK markets** — important for export equipment.

- **kcal/hr is still used in chemical and process refrigeration** sectors.

- **A 1 TR air conditioner** has a heat extraction capacity of ~3.5 kW — enough to cool a small room (~100–150 sq. ft.).

- **For Vapour Compression Cycle:** The capacity is related to refrigerant mass flow rate and evaporator enthalpy change:
$$\dot{Q}_L = \dot{m} \times (h_1 - h_4) \quad \text{[kW]}$$

- **For Vapour Absorption Cycle:** Capacity is determined by the heat input at the generator minus rejected heat.

- **Common exam traps:**
  - Time conversion errors (hr ↔ s)
  - Mixing kJ/kg with kcal/kg
  - Forgetting the 24-hour condition
  - Wrong sign convention

- **Practical Thumb Rule:** **1 TR ≈ cooling a 1000 ft³ enclosed space** in tropical conditions (rough estimate only — actual load calculation requires detailed heat load analysis).

- **Real-World Reference Points:**
  - Window AC: 1–2 TR
  - Split AC: 1–5 TR
  - Cold Storage: 5–500 TR
  - Industrial Chiller: 50–2000 TR

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coefficient of Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Coefficient of Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

The **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** is a dimensionless figure of merit that quantifies the *thermodynamic efficiency* of a refrigerator, heat pump, or any refrigeration/cooling device. Unlike thermal efficiency (which is bounded by 100%), the COP can, and frequently does, exceed unity (1).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Examiner Approved):**
> The Coefficient of Performance is defined as the ratio of the *Desired Effect* (useful heating or cooling delivered) to the *Work Input* (or total energy input) required to produce that effect.

Mathematically:

$$\text{COP} = \frac{\text{Desired Effect}}{\text{Energy Input}}$$

There are two principal forms used throughout the Refrigeration & Air Conditioning (RAC) industry:

| Device | Desired Effect | Energy Input | COP Expression |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Refrigerator (Cooling)** | Refrigerating Effect (RE) | Compressor Work ($W_{in}$) | $COP_R = \dfrac{RE}{W_{in}} = \dfrac{Q_2}{W}$ |
| **Heat Pump (Heating)** | Heating Effect (HE) | Compressor Work ($W_{in}$) | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{HE}{W_{in}} = \dfrac{Q_1}{W}$ |

Where:
- $Q_1$ = Heat rejected at the high-temperature reservoir (Condenser) in **kJ/kg** or **kW**
- $Q_2$ = Heat extracted from the low-temperature reservoir (Evaporator) in **kJ/kg** or **kW**
- $W$ = Net work input to the cycle in **kJ/kg** or **kW**

> [!NOTE]
> **First Law Application:** By the principle of energy conservation, $Q_1 = Q_2 + W$. This single equation is the foundation of nearly every KTU 2-mark and 14-mark COP problem you will encounter.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

Imagine you are a student preparing for the KTU exam.

- **You** are the **refrigeration system**.
- **Cool air in your room** is the *Desired Effect* (the cooling you want).
- **The electricity you consume while studying** is the *Work Input* (energy you "spend").

If you study for **1 hour using a fan** and your room feels pleasantly cool, you have achieved a **high COP** — you got a *lot of cooling for a little energy*. If you instead had to run a giant air conditioner at full blast just to stay cool, your **COP would be low** — you spent a *lot of energy for the same cooling effect*.

> **Key Intuition:** A *higher COP = more cooling (or heating) per unit of energy consumed = more economical operation*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction (Frequently tested in KTU):** A COP of 3 does **NOT** mean 300% efficiency. It simply means the system delivers **3 units of desired effect for every 1 unit of energy input**. Thermal efficiency and COP are different metrics on different scales.

---

### 1.3 Why COP is the Preferred Metric in Refrigeration

The **Carnot Efficiency** of a heat engine is bounded between 0 and 1 (0%–100%). However, refrigeration systems are *heat pumps operating in reverse* — they *move* heat rather than *convert* it to work. Since the desired effect ($Q_2$ or $Q_1$) is typically *larger* than the work input ($W$), the ratio $\dfrac{Q}{W}$ is naturally greater than 1.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical KTU Industrial Benchmark Values:**
> - Domestic Refrigerator (VCR): COP = **1.5 to 2.5**
> - Window/Split Air Conditioner: COP = **2.5 to 3.5**
> - Industrial Cold Storage (Ammonia VCR): COP = **3.0 to 4.5**
> - Geothermal Heat Pump: COP = **4.0 to 5.5**

---

### 1.4 Higher COP vs. Higher Efficiency — The Subtle Truth

Although COP and thermal efficiency are not directly comparable, there is a **monotonic relationship**: *any system with a higher COP is, by definition, a more thermodynamically efficient system for its purpose*. The COP simply makes the comparison numerically clearer when values exceed 1.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — From First Law to COP

To derive the governing equations of COP, we consider a refrigeration cycle operating between two thermal reservoirs:

- **High-Temperature Reservoir (Condenser side)** at absolute temperature $T_1$ (in Kelvin)
- **Low-Temperature Reservoir (Evaporator side)** at absolute temperature $T_2$ (in Kelvin)

**Step 1: Apply the First Law of Thermodynamics (Steady-Flow Energy Equation)**

For a cyclic device, the net change in internal energy is zero over a complete cycle. Therefore:

$$Q_{net} = W_{net}$$

This is written for refrigeration as:

$$Q_1 - Q_2 = W$$

Where:
- $Q_1$ = Heat rejected at the condenser (output to hot reservoir)
- $Q_2$ = Heat absorbed at the evaporator (input from cold reservoir — this is the *refrigerating effect*)
- $W$ = Net work input by the compressor

**Step 2: Define the COP for a Refrigerator**

$$\boxed{COP_R = \frac{Q_2}{W} = \frac{Q_2}{Q_1 - Q_2}}$$

**Step 3: Define the COP for a Heat Pump**

$$\boxed{COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_1}{W} = \frac{Q_1}{Q_1 - Q_2}}$$

**Step 4: Establish the Master Relationship**

$$\boxed{COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1}$$

This is a **highly-tested KTU result** — typically a guaranteed 2 to 3 marks in the ESE (End Semester Examination).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the "+1" appears:** A heat pump delivers the heat $Q_1$, which is the *sum* of the refrigerant's absorbed heat $Q_2$ *plus* the work converted to heat $W$. The refrigerator only delivers $Q_2$. Hence $COP_{HP}$ is always exactly one unit greater than $COP_R$ for the same cycle.

---

### 2.2 COP of a Reversed Carnot Cycle (The Theoretical Maximum)

The **Reversed Carnot Cycle** is the *most efficient* refrigeration cycle possible between two given temperature limits. It serves as the universal benchmark against which all real cycles are compared.

**Step 1: Carnot Cycle Heat Ratios**

For a Carnot cycle, the ratio of heat transfer to absolute temperature is constant:

$$\frac{Q_1}{T_1} = \frac{Q_2}{T_2}$$

**Step 2: Solve for Heat in Terms of Temperature**

$$Q_1 = Q_2 \cdot \frac{T_1}{T_2}$$

**Step 3: Substitute into the COP Equation**

$$COP_{R,\text{Carnot}} = \frac{Q_2}{Q_1 - Q_2} = \frac{Q_2}{Q_2 \cdot \frac{T_1}{T_2} - Q_2} = \frac{Q_2}{Q_2 \left(\frac{T_1 - T_2}{T_2}\right)}$$

Simplifying:

$$\boxed{COP_{R,\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_2}{T_1 - T_2}}$$

For a heat pump operating on the reversed Carnot cycle:

$$\boxed{COP_{HP,\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_1}{T_1 - T_2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Numerical Problems:** Since $T_1 > T_2$, the denominator $(T_1 - T_2)$ is *smaller* than $T_1$ or $T_2$ individually, ensuring $COP > 1$ always (for a refrigerator). The smaller the temperature difference $(T_1 - T_2)$, the higher the COP.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Utility of COP in Engineering

The COP is the **single most important specification** engineers use to:

1. **Select refrigerants** for a given application (e.g., low-GWP, high-COP refrigerants like R-290, R-32).
2. **Compare cycles** (VCR vs. VARS) for cold storage and industrial applications.
3. **Estimate Seasonal Energy Efficiency Ratio (SEER)** and **Energy Efficiency Ratio (EER)** for HVAC equipment — both are essentially *weighted* forms of COP multiplied by a constant.
4. **Comply with energy codes** such as the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) star rating in India.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Quick Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MANDATORY REVISION BLOCK — Memorize This Table Before Any KTU Exam**

| # | Formula | Symbol Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $COP_R = \dfrac{Q_2}{W}$ | Refrigerator COP | Direct definition |
| 2 | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_1}{W}$ | Heat Pump COP | Direct definition |
| 3 | $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ | Master relationship | Universal link |
| 4 | $Q_1 = Q_2 + W$ | First Law (energy balance) | All cycle problems |
| 5 | $COP_{R,\text{Carnot}} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_1 - T_2}$ | Carnot refrigerator COP | Upper-bound benchmark |
| 6 | $COP_{HP,\text{Carnot}} = \dfrac{T_1}{T_1 - T_2}$ | Carnot heat pump COP | Upper-bound benchmark |
| 7 | $COP_{R,\text{VCR}} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | VCR (dry compression) | Standard vapor compression |
| 8 | $COP_{R,\text{VCR}} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{(h_2 - h_1) + (h_3 - h_4)}$ | VCR (subcooled liquid) | With condenser subcooling |
| 9 | $COP_{R,\text{VCR}} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4'}{h_2' - h_1}$ | VCR (with superheat) | With evaporator superheat |
| 10 | $COP_{R,\text{VARS}} = \dfrac{(h_1 - h_4)}{(h_6 - h_5) + (h_3 - h_4)}$ | Ideal Vapor Absorption System | With pump work neglected |
| 11 | $COP_{R,\text{VARS}} = \dfrac{Q_E}{Q_G + W_P}$ | Real VARS | With pump work $W_P$ included |
| 12 | $\text{EER} = 3.412 \times COP$ | Energy Efficiency Ratio | U.S./BEE Indian rating (BTU/hr per W) |
| 13 | $\text{RE} = h_1 - h_4$ | Refrigerating Effect | Unit: kJ/kg |
| 14 | $W_{comp} = h_2 - h_1$ | Compressor work | Unit: kJ/kg |

*All temperatures $T_1, T_2$ **must** be in Kelvin (K) when using the Carnot equations. Convert from °C using $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$.*

---

### 2.5 Factors That Improve COP (Engineering Levers)

1. **Lower condenser temperature** $T_1$ — improves COP dramatically.
2. **Higher evaporator temperature** $T_2$ — improves COP.
3. **Smaller temperature lift** $(T_1 - T_2)$ — the single most influential variable.
4. **Subcooling** the liquid before expansion — increases RE without adding work.
5. **Superheating** the vapor before compression (with caution — dry compression is preferred over wet).
6. **Multi-stage compression with intercooling** — for high lift applications (industrial cold storage).
7. **Use of advanced refrigerants** with favorable thermodynamic properties.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: General Relationship Between $COP_{HP}$ and $COP_R$

**Given:** A refrigeration device operating in a cycle absorbs heat $Q_2$ from a cold body, rejects heat $Q_1$ to a hot body, and consumes net work $W$.

**To Prove:** $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$

---

**Step 1: Write the First Law energy balance for the cyclic device.**

By conservation of energy for a closed cycle, the net work input equals the net heat input:

$$W = Q_1 - Q_2$$

*Logic:* All heat energy entering the system ($Q_2$ from the cold reservoir) plus the work input ($W$) must leave the system as $Q_1$ to the hot reservoir.

---

**Step 2: Write the expression for the COP of a refrigerator.**

The refrigerator's *desired effect* is the heat extracted from the cold body:

$$COP_R = \frac{Q_2}{W} = \frac{Q_2}{Q_1 - Q_2}$$

---

**Step 3: Write the expression for the COP of a heat pump.**

The heat pump's *desired effect* is the heat delivered to the hot body:

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_1}{W} = \frac{Q_1}{Q_1 - Q_2}$$

---

**Step 4: Express $Q_1$ in terms of $Q_2$ and $W$.**

From the First Law, $Q_1 = Q_2 + W$. Substitute this into the $COP_{HP}$ expression:

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_2 + W}{W}$$

---

**Step 5: Separate the fraction and apply the definition of $COP_R$.**

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_2}{W} + \frac{W}{W} = \frac{Q_2}{W} + 1$$

Since $\dfrac{Q_2}{W} = COP_R$, we obtain:

$$\begin{aligned}
COP_{HP} &= COP_R + 1 \\
\therefore COP_{HP} - COP_R &= 1 \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}$$

---

**Analytical Insight (KTU Board Perspective):**
This result holds for *all* refrigeration cycles — Carnot, VCR, VARS, cascade — without exception. In a 14-mark problem, you can use it as a sanity check: if your answer violates this relationship, a numerical error has occurred.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Carnot COP for Refrigerator and Heat Pump

**Given:** A reversible Carnot cycle operating between temperature limits $T_1$ (hot) and $T_2$ (cold), both in Kelvin.

**To Find:** The maximum possible $COP_R$ and $COP_{HP}$ between these limits.

---

**Step 1: Apply the Second Law for a reversible cycle.**

For a completely reversible cycle, the Clausius equality holds:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = 0$$

For the Carnot cycle with two isothermal and two isentropic processes, this reduces to:

$$\frac{Q_1}{T_1} = \frac{Q_2}{T_2}$$

*Logic:* In a reversible cycle, the entropy change over a complete cycle is zero. Heat $Q_1$ is rejected isothermally at $T_1$, and heat $Q_2$ is absorbed isothermally at $T_2$.

---

**Step 2: Solve the equality for $Q_1$ in terms of $Q_2$.**

$$Q_1 = Q_2 \cdot \frac{T_1}{T_2}$$

---

**Step 3: Write the work input from the First Law.**

$$W = Q_1 - Q_2 = Q_2 \cdot \frac{T_1}{T_2} - Q_2 = Q_2 \left( \frac{T_1 - T_2}{T_2} \right)$$

---

**Step 4: Compute the Carnot COP of the refrigerator.**

$$COP_{R,\text{Carnot}} = \frac{Q_2}{W} = \frac{Q_2}{Q_2 \left( \frac{T_1 - T_2}{T_2} \right)}$$

Cancel $Q_2$ from numerator and denominator:

$$\begin{aligned}
COP_{R,\text{Carnot}} &= \frac{T_2}{T_1 - T_2} \\
\therefore COP_{R,\text{Carnot}} &= \frac{T_2}{T_1 - T_2} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}$$

---

**Step 5: Compute the Carnot COP of the heat pump.**

$$COP_{HP,\text{Carnot}} = \frac{Q_1}{W} = \frac{Q_2 \cdot \frac{T_1}{T_2}}{Q_2 \left( \frac{T_1 - T_2}{T_2} \right)} = \frac{T_1}{T_1 - T_2}$$

$$\therefore COP_{HP,\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_1}{T_1 - T_2} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

**Step 6: Verify the master relationship.**

$$\begin{aligned}
COP_{HP,\text{Carnot}} - COP_{R,\text{Carnot}} &= \frac{T_1}{T_1 - T_2} - \frac{T_2}{T_1 - T_2} \\
&= \frac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1 - T_2} = 1
\end{aligned}$$

The "+1 rule" is verified for the Carnot case.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: COP of an Ideal Vapor Compression Refrigeration (VCR) Cycle

**Given:** A standard single-stage VCR cycle with the following state points on a Pressure-Enthalpy (P-h) or Temperature-Entropy (T-s) diagram:

- **State 1:** Saturated vapor leaving the evaporator (entry to compressor)
- **State 2:** Superheated vapor leaving the compressor (entry to condenser)
- **State 3:** Saturated liquid leaving the condenser (entry to expansion valve)
- **State 4:** Liquid-vapor mixture leaving the expansion valve (entry to evaporator)

**To Find:** The COP in terms of specific enthalpies $h_1, h_2, h_3, h_4$.

---

**Step 1: Identify the energy transfers at each component.**

| Component | Energy Transfer | Magnitude (per kg) |
|---|---|---|
| Evaporator | Heat absorbed (RE) | $q_E = h_1 - h_4$ |
| Compressor | Work input | $w_{comp} = h_2 - h_1$ |
| Condenser | Heat rejected | $q_C = h_2 - h_3$ |
| Expansion Valve | Isenthalpic throttling | $h_3 = h_4$ |

---

**Step 2: Write the COP using its definition.**

$$COP_R = \frac{\text{Refrigerating Effect (RE)}}{\text{Compressor Work Input}} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$

---

**Step 3: Confirm the First Law energy balance.**

$$\begin{aligned}
q_C &= q_E + w_{comp} \\
(h_2 - h_3) &= (h_1 - h_4) + (h_2 - h_1) \\
h_2 - h_3 &= h_2 - h_4 \\
\therefore h_3 &= h_4 \quad \text{(is enthalpic expansion confirmed)}
\end{aligned}$$

---

**Step 4: Final COP expression for the ideal VCR cycle (dry compression).**

$$\boxed{COP_{R,\text{VCR}} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Enhancement — Effect of Subcooling:** If the liquid leaving the condenser is subcooled from $T_3$ to $T_3'$, then $h_3 < h_3'$, and the new state after expansion is $h_4 = h_3'$. The enhanced COP becomes:
>
> $$COP_{R,\text{subcooled}} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{(h_2 - h_1) + (h_3 - h_4)}$$
>
> The added term $(h_3 - h_4)$ in the denominator is *zero* for dry compression but *positive* when subcooling is applied. However, the numerator also increases (since $h_4$ decreases), giving a *net improvement* in COP.

---

### 3.4 Derivations 4: COP of an Ideal Vapor Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS)

**Given:** A simple VARS using a refrigerant-absorbent pair (e.g., ammonia-water). The cycle components are the **evaporator, absorber, pump, generator, condenser, and expansion valve**.

State points (on a P-h diagram, mass flow rates are involved — typically 1 kg of refrigerant circulates in the evaporator side, while the strong/weak solution mass flow rates differ).

- **State 1:** Refrigerant vapor leaving the evaporator
- **State 4:** Refrigerant liquid after expansion valve (entering evaporator)
- **State 5:** Weak (dilute) solution leaving the generator
- **State 6:** Strong (rich) solution entering the generator
- **State 7:** Strong solution after the pump (entering the generator)

**To Find:** The COP of the ideal VARS in terms of enthalpies.

---

**Step 1: Energy balance at the evaporator (per kg of refrigerant).**

$$Q_E = h_1 - h_4$$

---

**Step 2: Energy balance at the generator (per kg of refrigerant).**

Heat input at the generator per kg of refrigerant:

$$Q_G = (h_6 - h_5) + (h_2 - h_1) \cdot m_{weak}$$

For a *simplified* ideal VARS (neglecting pump work and assuming pure refrigerant at the condenser exit):

$$Q_G = h_6 - h_5$$

*(This simplification is acceptable when mass flow rate adjustments are absorbed into the enthalpy difference.)*

---

**Step 3: COP expression for the ideal VARS.**

$$\boxed{COP_{R,\text{VARS}} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_6 - h_5}}$$

With pump work $W_P$ included:

$$COP_{R,\text{VARS, real}} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{(h_6 - h_5) + W_P}$$

Where $W_P = v_{abs}(P_G - P_A)$ for an incompressible absorbent solution.

---

**Step 4: Compare with VCR.**

A VARS has a *lower COP* than a VCR for the same temperature limits, but it offers critical advantages:
- Uses **heat input** instead of mechanical/electrical work
- Can operate on **waste heat** or **solar thermal energy**
- Ideal for **large-scale cold storage** in remote areas

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:**
A refrigeration cycle operates between a cold reservoir at $-10°C$ and a hot reservoir at $40°C$. The system absorbs 50 kW of heat from the cold space. Calculate:
(a) The COP of the refrigerator if it operates on a Carnot cycle.
(b) The COP of the heat pump operating between the same reservoirs.
(c) The work input required.

---

**Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $T_2 = -10°C = -10 + 273 = 263$ K
- $T_1 = 40°C = 40 + 273 = 313$ K
- $Q_2 = 50$ kW

---

**Part (a): COP of Carnot Refrigerator**

$$COP_{R,\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_2}{T_1 - T_2} = \frac{263}{313 - 263} = \frac{263}{50}$$

$$COP_{R,\text{Carnot}} = 5.26$$

---

**Part (b): COP of Carnot Heat Pump**

$$COP_{HP,\text{Carnot}} = COP_R + 1 = 5.26 + 1 = 6.26$$

*Verification using direct formula:*

$$COP_{HP,\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_1}{T_1 - T_2} = \frac{313}{50} = 6.26 \quad \checkmark$$

---

**Part (c): Work Input Required**

Since $COP_R = \dfrac{Q_2}{W}$, we solve for $W$:

$$W = \frac{Q_2}{COP_R} = \frac{50 \text{ kW}}{5.26}$$

$$W = 9.506 \text{ kW}$$

*Verification via First Law:*

$$Q_1 = Q_2 + W = 50 + 9.506 = 59.506 \text{ kW}$$

Check $\dfrac{Q_1}{W} = \dfrac{59.506}{9.506} = 6.26 = COP_{HP}$ ✓

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Answer-Writing Strategy:** Always show (a) the formula, (b) the substitution with units, (c) the numerical result, and (d) a unit check. Examiners award **partial marks** generously when method is shown, even if arithmetic slips occur.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of COP Determination

The diagram below maps the logical flow of how the COP is determined for any refrigeration/heat-pump cycle — from identifying the *desired effect* to applying the appropriate governing equation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Refrigeration or Heat Pump Cycle] --> B{Identify Application}
    B -->|Cooling| C[Refrigerator Mode]
    B -->|Heating| D[Heat Pump Mode]
    C --> E[Desired Effect = Q2 Refrigerating Effect]
    D --> F[Desired Effect = Q1 Heating Effect]
    E --> G[Energy Input = W Compressor Work]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Identify Cycle Type}
    H -->|Reversible Carnot| I[Use T1 and T2 in Kelvin]
    H -->|VCR with P-h data| J[Use h1 h2 h3 h4]
    H -->|VARS with enthalpy| K[Use h1 h4 h5 h6]
    I --> L[Compute COPR Carnot = T2 divided by T1 minus T2]
    J --> M[Compute COPR VCR = h1 minus h4 divided by h2 minus h1]
    K --> N[Compute COPR VARS = h1 minus h4 divided by h6 minus h5]
    L --> O[Final COP Result]
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P{Validate Master Relationship}
    P --> Q[Check COP HP equals COP R plus 1]
    Q --> R[Verified Final Answer]
```

---

### 4.2 Influence Map of Parameters on COP

This flowchart illustrates how each thermodynamic parameter affects the overall COP of a refrigeration cycle — a frequently tested conceptual point in KTU viva voce and short-answer questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[COP Performance] --> B[Temperature Lift T1 minus T2]
    A --> C[Evaporator Temperature T2]
    A --> D[Condenser Temperature T1]
    A --> E[Subcooling Effect]
    A --> F[Superheat at Compressor Inlet]
    A --> G[Refrigerant Properties]
    B --> B1[Smaller lift Higher COP]
    C --> C1[Higher T2 Higher COP]
    D --> D1[Lower T1 Higher COP]
    E --> E1[More subcooling Higher COP]
    F --> F1[Dry compression preferred]
    G --> G1[Low viscosity and high latent heat]
```

---

### 4.3 VCR Cycle on a P-h Diagram (Schematic Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Cycle[Ideal VCR Cycle on P-h Diagram]
        direction LR
        P1[State 1 Saturated Vapor at Evaporator Pressure]
        P2[State 2 Superheated Vapor at Condenser Pressure after Isentropic Compression]
        P3[State 3 Saturated Liquid at Condenser Pressure]
        P4[State 4 Wet Mixture at Evaporator Pressure after Throttling]
    end
    P1 -->|Isentropic Compression in Compressor| P2
    P2 -->|Heat Rejection in Condenser| P3
    P3 -->|Isenthalpic Throttling in Expansion Valve| P4
    P4 -->|Evaporation in Evaporator| P1
```

**Annotations on the P-h Diagram:**

- **Process 1 → 2 (Compressor):** Isentropic compression. Work input $= h_2 - h_1$
- **Process 2 → 3 (Condenser):** Heat rejection at constant pressure. Heat out $= h_2 - h_3$
- **Process 3 → 4 (Expansion Valve):** Isenthalpic throttling. $h_3 = h_4$
- **Process 4 → 1 (Evaporator):** Heat absorption at constant pressure. Refrigerating Effect $= h_1 - h_4$

---

```mermaid
    B -->|Mechanical or Electrical| C[Choose VCR Vapor Compression]

    C --> E[Typical COP Range 2.5 to 4.5]

```
