## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A1: Define Coefficient of Performance. Why is COP preferred over efficiency in refrigeration systems?** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
The Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigeration system is defined as the ratio of the desired effect (refrigerating effect or heating effect) to the work input required to achieve that effect.

$$COP_R = \frac{Q_2}{W} \quad \text{and} \quad COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_1}{W}$$

**Why COP is preferred over thermal efficiency:**

1. **Numerical clarity:** In a refrigeration system, the desired effect ($Q_2$) is *larger* than the work input ($W$). Therefore, the ratio $Q_2 / W$ exceeds unity. A traditional efficiency metric (bounded between 0 and 1) would obscure the practical performance.

2. **Comparative ease:** Two refrigerators can be compared directly by their COP values — higher COP means a more efficient system for the given purpose.

3. **Industry standardization:** COP is the international standard metric for rating refrigeration and air-conditioning equipment (along with EER and SEER).

4. **Direct economic relevance:** COP directly indicates operating cost — a system with twice the COP consumes half the energy for the same cooling.

**(3 marks – Board Key: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for justification with any 2 points)**

---

#### **Question A2: Show that $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ for any refrigeration cycle.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

By the First Law of Thermodynamics applied to a cyclic refrigeration device:

$$Q_1 = Q_2 + W$$

By definition:

$$COP_R = \frac{Q_2}{W} \quad \text{and} \quad COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_1}{W}$$

Substituting $Q_1 = Q_2 + W$ into the $COP_{HP}$ expression:

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_2 + W}{W} = \frac{Q_2}{W} + \frac{W}{W} = COP_R + 1$$

Hence proved. $\blacksquare$

**(3 marks – Board Key: 1 mark First Law + 1 mark substitution + 1 mark final result)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**Derive the expression for the COP of a Carnot refrigeration cycle and a heat pump operating between two temperature reservoirs. A Carnot refrigerator operates between $-15°C$ and $35°C$. If the heat extracted from the cold space is 60 kJ per cycle, calculate the work input and the COP of the corresponding heat pump.**

---

##### **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)**

**Step 1: State the Second Law for a reversible cycle.**

For a reversible Carnot cycle:

$$\frac{Q_1}{T_1} = \frac{Q_2}{T_2}$$

**Step 2: Apply the First Law (energy balance).**

$$W = Q_1 - Q_2$$

**Step 3: Derive the COP of the Carnot refrigerator.**

$$\begin{aligned}
COP_{R,\text{Carnot}} &= \frac{Q_2}{W} = \frac{Q_2}{Q_1 - Q_2}
\end{aligned}$$

Substitute $Q_1 = Q_2 \cdot \dfrac{T_1}{T_2}$:

$$COP_{R,\text{Carnot}} = \frac{Q_2}{Q_2 \cdot \frac{T_1}{T_2} - Q_2} = \frac{T_2}{T_1 - T_2}$$

**Step 4: Derive the COP of the Carnot heat pump.**

$$COP_{HP,\text{Carnot}} = \frac{Q_1}{W} = \frac{T_1}{T_1 - T_2}$$

**Step 5: Establish the master relationship.**

$$COP_{HP,\text{Carnot}} = COP_{R,\text{Carnot}} + 1$$

---

##### **Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $T_2 = -15°C = -15 + 273 = 258$ K
- $T_1 = 35°C = 35 + 273 = 308$ K
- $Q_2 = 60$ kJ per cycle

**Step 1: Calculate the COP of the Carnot refrigerator.**

$$COP_{R,\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_2}{T_1 - T_2} = \frac{258}{308 - 258} = \frac{258}{50} = 5.16$$

**Step 2: Calculate the work input.**

$$W = \frac{Q_2}{COP_R} = \frac{60}{5.16} = 11.63 \text{ kJ per cycle}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the COP of the heat pump.**

$$COP_{HP,\text{Carnot}} = COP_R + 1 = 5.16 + 1 = 6.16$$

*Verification using direct formula:*

$$COP_{HP,\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_1}{T_1 - T_2} = \frac{308}{50} = 6.16 \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Answers:**
- Work input $W = 11.63$ kJ/cycle
- $COP_{HP} = 6.16$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**An ideal vapor compression refrigeration cycle uses R-134a as the refrigerant. The enthalpy values at the four key state points are as follows: $h_1 = 240$ kJ/kg (saturated vapor at evaporator exit), $h_2 = 270$ kJ/kg (superheated vapor at compressor exit), $h_3 = 115$ kJ/kg (saturated liquid at condenser exit), and $h_4 = 115$ kJ/kg (after throttling). Determine the (a) refrigerating effect, (b) compressor work input, (c) heat rejected at the condenser, (d) COP of the refrigerator, and (e) COP of the corresponding heat pump.**

---

##### **Part (a) and (b) — Refrigerating Effect and Compressor Work (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Calculate the refrigerating effect (RE) per kg of refrigerant.**

$$RE = h_1 - h_4 = 240 - 115 = 125 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the compressor work input per kg of refrigerant.**

$$W_{comp} = h_2 - h_1 = 270 - 240 = 30 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3: State the energy balance observation.**

Since $h_3 = h_4 = 115$ kJ/kg, the throttling process is verified as isenthalpic. ✓

---

##### **Part (c), (d), and (e) — Heat Rejected, COP of Refrigerator, and COP of Heat Pump (7 marks)**

**Step 4: Calculate the heat rejected at the condenser per kg.**

$$Q_C = h_2 - h_3 = 270 - 115 = 155 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

*Verification using First Law:*

$$Q_C = RE + W_{comp} = 125 + 30 = 155 \text{ kJ/kg} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5: Calculate the COP of the refrigerator.**

$$COP_R = \frac{RE}{W_{comp}} = \frac{125}{30} = 4.167$$

**Step 6: Calculate the COP of the heat pump.**

$$COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1 = 4.167 + 1 = 5.167$$

*Verification using direct formula:*

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_C}{W_{comp}} = \frac{155}{30} = 5.167 \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Answers:**
- RE = 125 kJ/kg
- $W_{comp}$ = 30 kJ/kg
- $Q_C$ = 155 kJ/kg
- $COP_R = 4.167$
- $COP_{HP} = 5.167$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion:** The most common error is using °C instead of K in the Carnot COP formula. KTU examiners *deduct 1 mark immediately* for this. Always convert: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273$.
>
> 2. **Confusing $Q_1$ and $Q_2$:** Students frequently swap the condenser and evaporator heat values. Remember: $Q_1$ = rejected to hot reservoir (larger magnitude), $Q_2$ = absorbed from cold reservoir (smaller magnitude, this is the RE).
>
> 3. **Assuming COP is bounded by 1:** Writing "$COP = 4.167$ is impossible" is a fatal error. A COP > 1 is *normal* and *desirable* in refrigeration. Thermal efficiency, not COP, is bounded by 1.
>
> 4. **Mixing the heat pump and refrigerator definitions:** In a heat pump, the desired effect is the heat *delivered* ($Q_1$), not the heat extracted ($Q_2$). Read the question carefully.
>
> 5. **Omitting the P-h diagram:** KTU examiners award **at least 1 mark** for a properly labeled P-h or T-s diagram. A neat sketch with state points 1, 2, 3, 4 is essential.
>
> 6. **Skipping the First Law verification:** Always confirm $Q_1 = Q_2 + W$ as a sanity check. Examiners reward students who demonstrate this cross-verification.
>
> 7. **Confusing subcooling and superheat effects on COP:** Subcooling the liquid *always* increases COP. Superheating the vapor *before* compression may *slightly decrease* COP (if it adds to compressor work disproportionately), but it is needed to prevent liquid slugging at the compressor.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this list — read it the night before the KTU exam!**

- ✅ **COP Definition:** Ratio of Desired Effect to Energy Input.
- ✅ **Two Forms:** $COP_R = \dfrac{Q_2}{W}$ and $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_1}{W}$.
- ✅ **Master Relationship:** $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ — *always true, for any cycle.*
- ✅ **First Law:** $Q_1 = Q_2 + W$ — sanity-check every numerical problem with this.
- ✅ **Carnot COP (Maximum):** $COP_{R,\text{Carnot}} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_1 - T_2}$ and $COP_{HP,\text{Carnot}} = \dfrac{T_1}{T_1 - T_2}$.
- ✅ **Always use Kelvin** for $T_1$ and $T_2$ in Carnot equations: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$.
- ✅ **VCR Cycle COP:** $COP_R = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ — *dry compression formula.*
- ✅ **VARS Cycle COP:** $COP_R = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_6 - h_5}$ (ideal, no pump work).
- ✅ **Higher COP is better** — it means more cooling/heating per unit energy consumed.
- ✅ **Lower temperature lift** $(T_1 - T_2)$ → **Higher COP** — the single most important design lever.
- ✅ **Subcooling** the liquid before the expansion valve increases COP.
- ✅ **COP is not thermal efficiency** — it can exceed 1, often does, and *that is the whole point.*
- ✅ **EER** $= 3.412 \times COP$ — a useful conversion for industry-rating questions.
- ✅ **Real cycles have COP < Carnot COP** — irreversibilities (friction, pressure drops, non-isentropic compression) reduce real performance.
- ✅ **COP of VCR > COP of VARS** for the same temperature limits — VARS uses heat, not work, hence the "lower" numerical COP.
- ✅ **Heat pump + Refrigerator = Same hardware** — the difference is whether you extract heat from the cold space (refrigerator) or deliver it to the conditioned space (heat pump).

> **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Coefficient of Performance. Best of luck for your KTU ESE! 🎓**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Production of Low Temperatures - Expansion of a Liquid with Flashing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Production of Low Temperatures - Expansion of a Liquid with Flashing # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Production of Low Temperatures — Formal Definition In the context of refrigeration and cryogenics, **Production of Low Temperatures** refers to the systematic engineering processes employed to achieve and sustain temperatures below that of the ambient surroundings. The most widely adopted commercial method is the **vapour-compression refrigeration cycle (VCRC)**, in which a working fluid (refrigerant) absorbs heat at a low temperature and rejects it at a higher temperature, enabling the generation of useful cooling. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 1):** The two principal methods for producing low temperatures are: > 1. **Expansion of a liquid with flashing** (Throttling/Joule–Thomson process) > 2. **Non-fluids system** — Vapor Compression, Vapor Absorption, Steam Jet, and Air Refrigeration systems. ## 1.2 Expansion of a Liquid with Flashing — Rigorous Definition **Expansion of a liquid with flashing** is the process in which a high-pressure, sub-cooled (or saturated) liquid refrigerant is suddenly throttled (expanded) to a lower pressure, causing a portion of the liquid to **vaporise (flash) instantly** into vapour due to the pressure drop. This adiabatic, irreversible throttling process drops the refrigerant temperature well below ambient, producing the **cold** required for refrigeration. **Key Terminology:** | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Throttling** | Irreversible adiabatic expansion of a fluid through a restriction (e.g., expansion valve). | | **Flashing** | Sudden partial vaporisation of liquid caused by a pressure drop. | | **Sub-cooling** | Cooling of liquid refrigerant below its saturation temperature at the given pressure. | | **Flash Gas** | Vapour formed during the flashing process. | | **Quality (x)** | Mass fraction of vapour in a liquid–vapour mixture. | ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Pressure Cooker Whistle" > [!NOTE] > **Real-world analogy:** Imagine a pressure cooker where steam (high-pressure liquid+vapour) is suddenly released through the whistle. As the high-pressure fluid escapes to atmospheric pressure, you observe a **cold, white fog** at the nozzle — this is **flash evaporation** in action. The fluid that was hot and pressurised cools rapidly because the energy used to push the molecules apart (latent heat) is taken from the fluid itself, dropping its temperature. This is exactly what happens inside a refrigeration system's expansion valve. ## 1.4 Why This Process is Used in Refrigeration - **No moving parts** — Highly reliable, simple construction (just a restriction/orifice). - **Constant enthalpy process** ($h_3 = h_4$) — Makes energy calculations simple. - **Provides the Joule–Thomson cooling effect** for nearly all real refrigerants (except hydrogen, helium at NTP). - **Converts high-pressure liquid into a low-temperature two-phase mixture** ready to absorb heat in the evaporator. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Constant Used:** At standard conditions, the **Joule–Thomson coefficient** ($\mu_{JT}$) is positive for most practical refrigerants, meaning temperature **decreases** upon throttling — this is the *cooling effect we exploit*. $$\mu_{JT} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_h > 0 \quad \text{(for cooling)}$$ # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic The **expansion with flashing** process is the *expansion stroke* of a Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRC). Let us break it down: 1. **Entry Condition (State 3):** Saturated (or sub-cooled) liquid refrigerant at high pressure $P_{cond}$ and temperature $T_{cond}$. 2. **Process:** The liquid passes through an **expansion valve** — a deliberate throttling device that creates a large pressure drop. 3. **Exit Condition (State 4):** Low-pressure liquid + vapour mixture at evaporator pressure $P_{evap}$ and low temperature $T_{evap}$. 4. **Flashing Mechanism:** A fraction $x_4$ of the liquid vaporises; the **latent heat for this vaporisation is drawn from the liquid itself**, causing the temperature to plummet. 5. **Result:** A cold, two-phase mixture enters the evaporator, ready to absorb heat from the refrigerated space. > [!NOTE] > **Why does temperature drop?** Energy cannot be created or destroyed. The total enthalpy remains constant, but the *internal energy is redistributed* into the **latent heat of vaporisation** of the flashed vapour. This "consumes" the sensible heat of the liquid, dropping its temperature to the saturation value corresponding to the lower pressure. ## 2.2 The Throttling (Isenthalpic) Process A throttling process is governed by the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** with the following assumptions: - **Adiabatic** ($Q = 0$) — no heat transfer - **No work** ($W = 0$) — no shaft work - **Negligible kinetic and potential energy changes** ($\Delta KE \approx 0$, $\Delta PE \approx 0$) Applying SFEE: $$h_3 = h_4 \quad \text{(Constant Enthalpy Process)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **most important equation** for this topic. Always remember: **Throttling = Isenthalpic = Adiabatic + No Work**. ## 2.3 Quality of Refrigerant After Flashing ($x_4$) The fraction of refrigerant that flashes into vapour after the expansion is called the **quality** $x_4$. Using the constant-enthalpy condition $h_3 = h_4$: $$h_3 = h_{f4} + x_4 \cdot h_{fg4}$$ Rearranging: $$x_4 = \frac{h_3 - h_{f4}}{h_{fg4}}$$ Where: - $h_3$ = Enthalpy of liquid entering expansion valve (kJ/kg) - $h_{f4}$ = Saturated liquid enthalpy at evaporator pressure (kJ/kg) - $h_{fg4}$ = Latent heat of vaporisation at evaporator pressure (kJ/kg) - $x_4$ = Quality of mixture leaving expansion valve ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $h_3 = h_4$ | Isenthalpic (throttling) condition | kJ/kg | | 2 | $x_4 = \dfrac{h_3 - h_{f4}}{h_{fg4}}$ | Quality after flashing | dimensionless | | 3 | $\dot{m}_{flash} = x_4 \cdot \dot{m}_{total}$ | Flash gas mass flow rate | kg/s | | 4 | $RE = h_1 - h_4 = h_1 - h_3$ | Refrigerating Effect per kg | kJ/kg | | 5 | $\text{Flash \%} = \dfrac{x_4}{1} \times 100$ | Percentage of refrigerant flashing | % | | 6 | $\mu_{JT} = \left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_h$ | Joule–Thomson coefficient | K/Pa | | 7 | $T_4 = T_{sat}(P_{evap})$ | Temperature after flashing | °C or K | | 8 | $W_{comp} = h_2 - h_1$ | Compressor work per kg | kJ/kg | | 9 | $COP = \dfrac{RE}{W_{comp}} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | Coefficient of Performance | dimensionless | | 10 | $h_4 = h_3 = h_{f3} + (h_{g3} - h_{f3})\cdot 0 = h_{f3}$ | If $h_3$ is saturated liquid at $P_{cond}$ | kJ/kg | > [!CAUTION] > **Equation Pitfall:** When the liquid entering the expansion valve is **saturated** (not sub-cooled), $h_3 = h_{f3}$ at the condenser pressure. When **sub-cooled**, $h_3 = h_{f3} - c_p \cdot \Delta T_{sub}$ or you read directly from tables. Always identify the entry state correctly. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Domestic refrigerators & ACs:** Capillary tube/thermostatic expansion valve (TXV/TEV) exploits this principle. - **Cryogenic liquefaction** of gases (LNG, oxygen, nitrogen). - **Industrial chillers** for process cooling. - **Cold storage plants** for food preservation. - The **flash chamber** in multi-stage systems removes flash gas before it reaches the evaporator, **improving COP** by 5–15%. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Constant Enthalpy Condition (SFEE Proof) **Statement:** Prove that throttling is an isenthalpic (constant-enthalpy) process. **Starting Equation:** Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) for a control volume (expansion valve): $$Q - W = \dot{m}\left[(h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)\right]$$ **Assumptions for throttling:** - Adiabatic: $Q = 0$ (no heat exchange — process is rapid) - No work: $W = 0$ (no shaft or electrical work) - $\Delta KE \approx 0$ (small flow area, velocities similar) - $\Delta PE \approx 0$ (negligible height difference) **Substituting** $Q = 0$, $W = 0$, $\Delta KE = 0$, $\Delta PE = 0$: $$0 - 0 = \dot{m}\left[(h_2 - h_1) + 0 + 0\right]$$ $$0 = h_2 - h_1$$ $$\boxed{h_1 = h_2 \quad \text{or} \quad h_3 = h_4 \text{ (for VCRC notation)}}$$ **Conclusion:** The throttling process is **isenthalpic** (constant enthalpy). --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Quality of Refrigerant After Flashing ($x_4$) **Given:** A saturated liquid refrigerant at state 3 ($P_3$, $T_3 = T_{sat}(P_3)$) is throttled to evaporator pressure $P_4$. **Step 1 — Write enthalpy at state 3 (saturated liquid):** $$h_3 = h_{f3}$$ **Step 2 — Write enthalpy at state 4 (liquid–vapour mixture at $P_4$):** $$h_4 = h_{f4} + x_4 \cdot h_{fg4}$$ **Step 3 — Apply isenthalpic condition** $h_3 = h_4$: $$h_{f3} = h_{f4} + x_4 \cdot h_{fg4}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $x_4$:** $$x_4 \cdot h_{fg4} = h_{f3} - h_{f4}$$ $$\boxed{x_4 = \frac{h_{f3} - h_{f4}}{h_{fg4}}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Refrigerating Effect (RE) Per kg of Refrigerant **Refrigerating Effect (RE)** is the heat absorbed per kg of refrigerant in the evaporator. **Step 1 — Definition:** $$RE = h_1 - h_4$$ Where $h_1$ is the enthalpy of vapour leaving the evaporator (saturated/superheated vapour) and $h_4$ is the enthalpy of the mixture entering the evaporator. **Step 2 — Using the isenthalpic condition** $h_4 = h_3$: $$RE = h_1 - h_3$$ **Step 3 — Expressing RE in terms of temperature and latent heat** (when $h_1 = h_{g4}$): $$RE = h_{g4} - h_{f3} = h_{fg4} + (h_{f4} - h_{f3})$$ This is interpreted as: $$RE = \underbrace{h_{fg4}}_{\text{latent heat absorbed}} - \underbrace{(h_{f3} - h_{f4})}_{\text{cooling of liquid to } T_{evap}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical meaning:** The **flash loss** $(h_{f3} - h_{f4})$ represents the cooling capacity that was *wasted* in vaporising some liquid during flashing. This is why **sub-cooling** (lowering $h_3$ below $h_{f3}$) improves the refrigerating effect. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix) **Problem:** A refrigerant (R-134a) leaves the condenser as a saturated liquid at **40 °C (pressure ≈ 1.017 MPa)** and is throttled to an evaporator pressure of **0.18 MPa (≈ –12 °C saturation)**. Find: (a) The quality $x_4$ after throttling (b) The refrigerating effect per kg (c) The percentage of refrigerant that flashes **Property Data (R-134a):** | State | Pressure (MPa) | Temperature (°C) | $h_f$ (kJ/kg) | $h_{fg}$ (kJ/kg) | $h_g$ (kJ/kg) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 3 (Sat. liquid, condenser exit) | 1.017 | 40 | 108.26 | 171.45 | 279.71 | | 4 (Sat. liquid, evaporator pressure) | 0.18 | –12.46 | 35.92 | 198.18 | 234.10 | | 1 (Sat. vapour, evaporator exit) | 0.18 | –12.46 | 35.92 | 198.18 | 234.10 | ### Part (a): Quality $x_4$ **Step 1:** Apply isenthalpic condition. $$h_3 = h_{f3} = 108.26 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 2:** Use mixture enthalpy at state 4. $$h_4 = h_{f4} + x_4 \cdot h_{fg4} = 35.92 + x_4 \cdot 198.18$$ **Step 3:** Set $h_3 = h_4$. $$108.26 = 35.92 + x_4 \cdot 198.18$$ **Step 4:** Solve. $$x_4 \cdot 198.18 = 108.26 - 35.92 = 72.34$$ $$x_4 = \frac{72.34}{198.18}$$ $$\boxed{x_4 = 0.3650 \quad (\text{or } 36.50\%)}$$ ### Part (b): Refrigerating Effect **Step 1:** Enthalpy at evaporator exit (saturated vapour at $P_{evap}$). $$h_1 = h_{g4} = 234.10 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 2:** Apply RE formula. $$RE = h_1 - h_4 = 234.10 - 108.26$$ $$\boxed{RE = 125.84 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$ ### Part (c): Percentage of Refrigerant Flashing $$\text{Flash \%} = x_4 \times 100 = 0.3650 \times 100$$ $$\boxed{\text{Flash \%} = 36.50\%}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Nearly **36.5% of the refrigerant flashes** before reaching the evaporator. This is the *flash loss* — a major reason why sub-cooling, multi-stage compression, and flash chambers are used in advanced systems to improve COP. --- ## 3.5 Effect of Sub-Cooling on Quality If the liquid is sub-cooled by $\Delta T_{sub}$ before throttling: $$h_3 = h_{f3} - c_{p,l} \cdot \Delta T_{sub}$$ The new quality: $$x_4' = \frac{h_3 - h_{f4}}{h_{fg4}} < x_4$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sub-cooling decreases $x_4$** (less flash gas) and **increases RE** — this is why sub-coolers are essential in industrial refrigeration plants. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Throttling Process on P–h and T–s Diagrams The expansion of a liquid with flashing is best visualised on a **Pressure–Enthalpy (P–h) diagram**, which is the standard working tool for VCRC analysis. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["State 3: Saturated Liquid
at P_cond, T_cond
h3 = hf3 (high h)"] -->|Throttling Process
h3 = h4 constant| B["State 4: Liquid + Vapour Mixture
at P_evap, T_evap
h4 = h3 (low P, same h)"] B -->|Heat Absorption RE| C["State 1: Saturated Vapour
at P_evap
h1 = hg4"] C -->|Compression| D["State 2: Superheated Vapour
at P_cond, T2
h2 = h1 + W_comp"] D -->|Heat Rejection| A ``` **Description of the cycle flow:** - **3 → 4:** Throttling (isenthalpic) — moves vertically downward on P–h chart. - **4 → 1:** Evaporation — horizontal line at $P_{evap}$ from $x_4$ to $x=1$. - **1 → 2:** Compression — moves upward and rightward. - **2 → 3:** Condensation — moves leftward at high pressure, ending at saturated liquid. ## 4.2 Block Diagram of Components ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph HP["HIGH PRESSURE SIDE"] COMP["Compressor
W in"] end subgraph COND["CONDENSER"] COOL["Refrigerant Rejects Heat
to Environment
h2 to h3"] end subgraph LP["LOW PRESSURE SIDE"] EXP["Expansion Valve
Throttling
h3 = h4"] EVAP["Evaporator
Refrigerant Absorbs Heat
h4 to h1"] end subgraph CYCLE["CIRCULATION"] FLOW1["Superheated Vapour
h2, P_high"] -.-> COMP COMP -.-> FLOW1 COOL --> EXP EXP --> EVAP EVAP -.->|"Saturated Vapour
h1, P_low"| COMP end ``` **Component Functionality:** - **Compressor:** Raises refrigerant pressure and temperature. - **Condenser:** Rejects latent heat to surroundings; refrigerant condenses to saturated liquid. - **Expansion Valve:** Performs throttling — pressure drops, flash evaporation occurs. - **Evaporator:** Cold low-pressure refrigerant absorbs heat from the refrigerated space. ## 4.3 P–h Diagram Schematic Representation ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph PH["PRESSURE ENTHALPY P-h DIAGRAM"] Y["Pressure P (log scale)"] -->|vertical axis| Y X["Enthalpy h (kJ/kg)"] -->|horizontal axis| X BUB["Saturated Liquid Line"] -->|left boundary| BUB DEW["Saturated Vapour Line"] -->|right boundary| DEW CRIT["Critical Point"] -->|top of dome| CRIT S3["State 3
Saturated Liquid
P_cond, hf3"] -->|vertical drop h3=h4| S4 S4["State 4
Mixture Quality x4
P_evap, h4=h3"] -->|horizontal line RE| S1 S1["State 1
Saturated Vapour
P_evap, hg4"] -->|compression line| S2 S2["State 2
Superheated Vapour
P_cond, h2"] -->|condensation line| S3 end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway from Diagrams:** On a P–h chart, **throttling is a vertical line** (constant h). The **refrigerating effect** is the horizontal distance between state 4 and state 1 at evaporator pressure. The **flash loss** is the horizontal distance from $h_{f4}$ to $h_3$ at $P_{evap}$ — representing the enthalpy "lost" to flash evaporation. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper] **Define the throttling process. Why is it considered isenthalpic?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Throttling is an **irreversible adiabatic expansion** of a fluid through a restriction (e.g., expansion valve, porous plug, or capillary tube), where the fluid pressure drops significantly without any external heat transfer or work interaction. **Isenthalpic Explanation (2 marks):** Applying the Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) to the throttling device with the assumptions: - Adiabatic: $Q = 0$ - No work: $W = 0$ - Negligible changes in kinetic and potential energy We obtain: $h_1 = h_2$ (or $h_3 = h_4$ in VCRC notation). This constant-enthalpy condition is the defining feature of the throttling process, and the process is called **isenthalpic**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse throttling with **adiabatic expansion** in a turbine. In throttling, **no work is extracted**; in a turbine, work is produced. Both are adiabatic, but only throttling is isenthalpic. --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper] **What is meant by "flashing" in a refrigeration system? State the consequences of flashing.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Meaning (1.5 marks):** Flashing is the **sudden vaporisation of a portion of the high-pressure liquid refrigerant** as it passes through the expansion valve, due to the abrupt pressure drop from condenser pressure to evaporator pressure. The latent heat required for this vaporisation is drawn from the liquid itself, causing a temperature drop. **Consequences (1.5 marks):** 1. The **refrigerating effect decreases** because some refrigerant vaporises *before* entering the evaporator. 2. The **mass flow of refrigerant through the evaporator is reduced** (effective liquid flow = $(1 - x_4)\dot{m}$). 3. A **flash loss** $(h_{f3} - h_{f4})$ is incurred, reducing the overall COP of the system. 4. Flash gas entering the evaporator must be **re-vaporised**, slightly reducing evaporator efficiency. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A common mistake is to say "flashing increases refrigerating effect." The opposite is true — flashing *reduces* effective RE. Sub-cooling *reduces* flashing and thus *increases* RE. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** For each 14-mark question, students must answer **both sub-parts (a) and (b)** unless an internal choice is offered. Model solutions are provided below. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]** **(a) Derive the expression for the quality of refrigerant after throttling in a vapour-compression refrigeration system. (7 marks)** **(b) In a vapour-compression refrigeration cycle using R-134a, the saturated liquid refrigerant leaves the condenser at 38 °C and is throttled to an evaporator pressure of 0.14 MPa. Determine: (i) the quality of refrigerant after throttling, (ii) the refrigerating effect per kg, and (iii) the mass of refrigerant to be circulated per ton of refrigeration. (7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question A: **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Consider a throttling device (expansion valve) with refrigerant entering at state 3 (high pressure, saturated liquid) and leaving at state 4 (low pressure, two-phase mixture). **Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply the Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) to the control volume of the expansion valve. $$h_3 = h_4 + \frac{V_4^2 - V_3^2}{2} + g(z_4 - z_3) + q - w$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply the throttling assumptions: - Adiabatic: $q = 0$ - No work: $w = 0$ - $\Delta KE \approx 0$, $\Delta PE \approx 0$ This reduces the SFEE to: $$h_3 = h_4 \quad \text{(Isenthalpic condition)}$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** At state 3, refrigerant is saturated liquid: $$h_3 = h_{f3}$$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** At state 4, refrigerant is a liquid–vapour mixture at evaporator pressure: $$h_4 = h_{f4} + x_4 \cdot h_{fg4}$$ **Step 6 (1 mark):** Equate $h_3 = h_4$: $$h_{f3} = h_{f4} + x_4 \cdot h_{fg4}$$ **Step 7 (1 mark):** Rearrange for $x_4$: $$\boxed{x_4 = \frac{h_{f3} - h_{f4}}{h_{fg4}}}$$ This is the desired expression. --- **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** - R-134a saturated liquid at $T_{cond} = 38$ °C - Evaporator pressure: $P_{evap} = 0.14$ MPa **Property Data (R-134a):** | State | Pressure | Temperature | $h_f$ (kJ/kg) | $h_{fg}$ (kJ/kg) | $h_g$ (kJ/kg) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 3 | 0.962 MPa (at 38 °C) | 38 °C | 105.29 | 173.42 | 278.71 | | 4 | 0.14 MPa | –18.8 °C | 30.18 | 200.27 | 230.45 | **(i) Quality $x_4$ (3 marks):** **Step 1:** Apply the isenthalpic condition: $h_3 = h_{f3} = 105.29$ kJ/kg. **Step 2:** Use mixture enthalpy: $h_4 = 30.18 + x_4 \cdot 200.27 = h_3$. **Step 3:** Solve: $$x_4 = \frac{105.29 - 30.18}{200.27} = \frac{75.11}{200.27}$$ $$\boxed{x_4 = 0.3751 \quad (\approx 37.51\%)}$$ **(ii) Refrigerating Effect (2 marks):** $$RE = h_1 - h_4 = h_{g4} - h_3 = 230.45 - 105.29$$ $$\boxed{RE = 125.16 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$ **(iii) Mass Flow Rate per TR (2 marks):** **Conversion:** 1 TR = 3.517 kW = 3.517 kJ/s. $$\dot{m} = \frac{3.517 \text{ kJ/s}}{RE} = \frac{3.517}{125.16}$$ $$\boxed{\dot{m} = 0.02810 \text{ kg/s} = 101.16 \text{ kg/hr}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often **forget to convert 1 TR to kJ/s (3.517)** and directly divide 3.517/RE. While this is correct numerically, **explicitly stating the unit conversion** earns full marks. Some textbooks use 1 TR = 210 kJ/min — confirm consistency. Also ensure you use the **correct $h_3$ and $h_4$** values from the property table for the given refrigerant. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a) Explain with a neat P–h diagram the production of low temperatures by expansion of a liquid with flashing. (7 marks)** **(b) A refrigerant R-12 enters the expansion valve as a saturated liquid at 30 °C and leaves at –10 °C. Determine: (i) the quality after throttling, (ii) the refrigerating effect, and (iii) the percentage of flash gas formed. (7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question B: **Part (a) — Conceptual Explanation (7 marks):** **Step 1 (1 mark):** The production of low temperatures by expansion of a liquid with flashing is the basis of all vapour-compression refrigeration cycles. The principle is to thottle a high-pressure liquid to a low pressure, causing partial vaporisation (flashing) and a temperature drop. **Step 2 (2 marks):** State 3: Saturated liquid refrigerant leaves the condenser at $P_3 = P_{cond}$, $T_3 = T_{sat}(P_3)$, $h_3 = h_{f3}$. This is the **entry** to the expansion valve. **Step 3 (2 marks):** The refrigerant passes through the expansion valve (isenthalpic, $h_3 = h_4$). The pressure drops from $P_{cond}$ to $P_{evap}$. State 4 is a two-phase mixture with quality $x_4$ and temperature $T_4 = T_{sat}(P_{evap})$. **Step 4 (2 marks):** On a P–h diagram (must be drawn), the throttling appears as a **vertical line** from state 3 to state 4. The **refrigerating effect** RE is the **horizontal distance** between state 4 and state 1 (saturated vapour at $P_{evap}$). The **flash loss** is the distance from $h_{f4}$ to $h_3$ at $P_{evap}$. **P–h Diagram (Neat Sketch Required):** ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph DIAG["P-h DIAGRAM: EXPANSION WITH FLASHING"] Y["Pressure P (log)"] -.->|Y-axis| Y X["Enthalpy h"] -.->|X-axis| X S3["State 3
Sat. Liquid
P_cond, hf3"] -->|VERTICAL DROP
h3 = h4| S4 S4["State 4
Two-Phase
P_evap, h4 = h3, x4"] -->|RE = h1 - h4
Evaporation| S1 S1["State 1
Sat. Vapour
P_evap, hg4"] S3 -.->|hfg4| SATL["Saturated
Liquid Line"] S1 -.-> SATV["Saturated
Vapour Line"] end ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Marking Tip:** Always draw a **neat, labelled P–h diagram** in the answer sheet. Even if the rest of the answer is partial, a clear diagram can fetch 2–3 marks. Label all four states, the saturation dome, and the constant-enthalpy line. --- **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** R-12 enters as saturated liquid at $T_3 = 30$ °C; leaves at $T_4 = -10$ °C. **Property Data (R-12):** | State | Temperature | $h_f$ (kJ/kg) | $h_{fg}$ (kJ/kg) | $h_g$ (kJ/kg) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 3 (Sat. liquid) | 30 °C | 64.62 | 154.78 | 219.40 | | 4 (Sat. liquid) | –10 °C | 26.87 | 168.74 | 195.61 | **(i) Quality $x_4$ (3 marks):** **Step 1:** $h_3 = h_{f3} = 64.62$ kJ/kg (saturated liquid at 30 °C). **Step 2:** Mixture enthalpy at state 4: $h_4 = 26.87 + x_4 \cdot 168.74$. **Step 3:** Set $h_3 = h_4$: $$64.62 = 26.87 + x_4 \cdot 168.74$$ $$x_4 = \frac{64.62 - 26.87}{168.74} = \frac{37.75}{168.74}$$ $$\boxed{x_4 = 0.2237 \quad (\approx 22.37\%)}$$ **(ii) Refrigerating Effect (2 marks):** $$RE = h_1 - h_4 = h_{g4} - h_3 = 195.61 - 64.62$$ $$\boxed{RE = 130.99 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$ **(iii) Percentage of Flash Gas (2 marks):** $$\text{Flash \%} = x_4 \times 100 = 0.2237 \times 100$$ $$\boxed{\text{Flash \%} = 22.37\%}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** > 1. For R-12 properties, students often confuse **pressure** and **temperature** lookups. Always confirm the saturation state before reading $h_f$ and $h_{fg}$. > 2. Don't forget to **state the assumption** that the refrigerant is saturated liquid at the condenser exit (unless told otherwise). > 3. If the problem says "sub-cooled liquid," use $h_3 = h_{f3} - c_{p,l} \cdot \Delta T_{sub}$ — this is a frequent exam trap. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Production of Low Temperatures: Expansion of a Liquid with Flashing** - **Core Concept:** Throttling a high-pressure liquid through an expansion valve produces a low-temperature, two-phase mixture via **flash evaporation**. - **Governing Equation:** $h_3 = h_4$ (Isenthalpic process) — derived from SFEE with $Q=0$, $W=0$, $\Delta KE \approx 0$, $\Delta PE \approx 0$. - **Quality After Flashing:** $x_4 = \dfrac{h_{f3} - h_{f4}}{h_{fg4}}$ — this is the *most frequently tested formula* in KTU exams. - **Refrigerating Effect:** $RE = h_1 - h_4 = h_1 - h_3$ (using isenthalpic condition). - **COP:** $\text{COP} = \dfrac{RE}{W_{comp}} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$. - **Flash Loss:** The enthalpy difference $(h_{f3} - h_{f4})$ at evaporator pressure represents *lost refrigerating effect* due to flashing. - **Effect of Sub-cooling:** Lowers $h_3$ → reduces $x_4$ → increases RE → improves COP. - **Joule–Thomson Coefficient:** $\mu_{JT} = \left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_h > 0$ for most refrigerants — temperature drops upon throttling. - **Real Gases Cool, Ideal Gases Don't:** Throttling of an ideal gas produces *no temperature change*; the cooling effect is unique to real gases. - **P–h Diagram Signature:** Throttling is a **vertical line** (constant $h$) on the P–h diagram; RE is the **horizontal distance** between state 4 and state 1. - **Common Refrigerants in KTU Syllabus:** R-12, R-22, R-134a, R-404A, R-410A, NH₃ (ammonia) — always use correct property tables. - **KTU 2024 Exam Weightage:** Expect 1 Part-A (3 marks) and 1 sub-part of Part-B (7 marks) on this topic in every end-semester exam. - **Examiner Loves:** Neat P–h diagrams, clear derivation of $h_3 = h_4$, and explicit property table references. - **Examiner Penalises:** Skipping SFEE assumptions, confusing throttling with turbine expansion, using wrong refrigerant properties, and not labelling the P–h diagram.

topics.title: Reversible/Irreversible Adiabatic Expansion of a Gas/Real Gas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reversible/Irreversible Adiabatic Expansion of a Gas/Real Gas

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adiabatic Expansion** is a thermodynamic process in which a gas expands against a resistive force (or into a vacuum) **without any heat transfer** across the system boundary (i.e., $Q = 0$). When this expansion proceeds through a continuous sequence of equilibrium states, it is termed **Reversible Adiabatic Expansion** (also called an **Isentropic process** for an ideal gas, since entropy remains constant). Conversely, **Irreversible Adiabatic Expansion** proceeds spontaneously through non-equilibrium states, with the most common example being the **free expansion of a gas** (also known as the **Joule Expansion**).

### Key Terminology — Syllabus Vocabulary

| Term | Formal Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Adiabatic** | A process where no heat is exchanged with the surroundings: $\delta Q = 0$ |
| **Isentropic** | A reversible adiabatic process where entropy change is zero: $\Delta S = 0$ |
| **Free Expansion** | Gas expands into a vacuum; external work done $W = 0$ |
| **Joule Expansion** | A specific free expansion experiment in an insulated rigid chamber |
| **Polytropic Process** | A general expansion described by $PV^n = \text{constant}$, where $n = \gamma$ gives the reversible adiabatic case |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Controlled Balloon vs. The Burst Balloon**
>
> Imagine two identical sealed balloons filled with hot air, both placed in a cold room:
>
> 1. **Reversible Adiabatic Expansion (Controlled Balloon):** A researcher slowly pulls a pin, letting air escape through a tiny nozzle. Because the release is *infinitesimally slow*, the gas inside is always in equilibrium with its surroundings. The temperature drops *predictably* (we can calculate the exact final temperature).
>
> 2. **Irreversible Adiabatic Expansion (Burst Balloon):** The balloon is popped violently. Air rushes out chaotically, creating turbulence and eddies. There is *no defined path* on a P-V diagram. The internal gas molecules smash into one another randomly, and some kinetic energy is lost to internal disorder (entropy increases).
>
> **Refrigeration connection:** Real expansion valves in your refrigerator are *closer* to the irreversible throttling (Joule-Thomson) process, which is why the gas cools down — this is the principle behind every domestic fridge!

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Specific heat ratio for air:** $\gamma = 1.4$
- **Universal gas constant:** $R_u = 8.314$ **kJ/kmol·K**
- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325$ **kPa**
- **For a diatomic gas (e.g., N₂, O₂):** $\gamma = 1.4$
- **For a monoatomic gas (e.g., He, Ar):** $\gamma = 1.667$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Comparative Theory — Reversible vs. Irreversible

### A. Reversible Adiabatic Expansion of an Ideal Gas

- The gas expands **quasi-statically** through a series of equilibrium states.
- The path follows the **Poisson equations**.
- Work is done **on the surroundings** (positive work output).
- Entropy change $\Delta S = 0$ (isentropic).
- Temperature **decreases** significantly as the gas does work using its internal energy.

### B. Irreversible Adiabatic (Free) Expansion of an Ideal Gas

- The gas expands into a **vacuum** or through a throttling device.
- The path **cannot be plotted** on a P-V diagram because intermediate states are non-equilibrium.
- **External work** done, $W_{ext} = 0$.
- For an ideal gas, internal energy depends only on temperature: $U = f(T)$ → thus $\Delta T = 0$ for free expansion.
- Entropy **increases**: $\Delta S > 0$.

### C. Real Gas Behaviour

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a **Real Gas**, internal energy is a function of *both* temperature and volume: $U = f(T, V)$. Hence, even in free expansion ($\Delta U = 0$ since $W = 0$ and $Q = 0$), the temperature **may change** due to the work done *against* the intermolecular forces.

## 2.2 Why and How — The Underlying Physics

- **Why reversible is "frictionless":** In a reversible process, there are no dissipative effects (no turbulence, no friction, no sudden pressure gradients). The system and surroundings can be restored to their initial state without net change.
- **Why irreversible creates entropy:** The chaotic motion of molecules in a free expansion is *irrecoverable* work — energy is conserved, but the *quality* of energy is degraded (this is the basis of the **Gouy-Stodola theorem** and **exergy destruction**).
- **How real gases differ:** Intermolecular forces (van der Waals) cause molecules to either attract or repel each other. During expansion, work is done *against* these forces, leading to either a temperature drop (attractive forces dominant — the Joule-Thomson effect used in refrigeration) or a temperature rise (repulsive forces dominant).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the single most important reference for Module 1 examination problems.**

| # | Process Type | Key Equation | Work Done (W) | Heat (Q) | $\Delta T$ (Ideal Gas) | $\Delta S$ |
|:---:|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Reversible Adiabatic (Ideal Gas)** | $PV^{\gamma} = C$ | $W = \dfrac{P_1V_1 - P_2V_2}{\gamma - 1} = \dfrac{mR(T_1 - T_2)}{\gamma - 1}$ | $0$ | $T_2 = T_1 \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | $0$ |
| 2 | **Irreversible Free Expansion (Ideal Gas)** | No defined path | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ (since $\Delta U = 0$) | $\Delta S = nR \ln \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} > 0$ |
| 3 | **Polytropic Process (General)** | $PV^n = C$ | $W = \dfrac{P_1V_1 - P_2V_2}{n - 1}$ | $Q = m \cdot c_n (T_2 - T_1)$ | Depends on $n$ | $\Delta S = m \cdot c_v \ln \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} + mR \ln \dfrac{V_2}{V_1}$ |
| 4 | **Real Gas — Joule-Thomson** | $\mu_{JT} = \left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_H$ | $W = -\Delta(PV)$ | $0$ | $T_2 - T_1 = \mu_{JT} (P_2 - P_1)$ | $\Delta S > 0$ |

> **Special Relations:**
>
> - $c_p - c_v = R$
> - $\gamma = c_p / c_v$
> - For air: $c_p = 1.005$ **kJ/kg·K**, $c_v = 0.718$ **kJ/kg·K**, $R = 0.287$ **kJ/kg·K**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Domestic Refrigerators:** The expansion valve performs irreversible throttling, exploiting the **Joule-Thomson cooling** of real refrigerant gases.
- **Gas Liquefaction Plants (Linde/Hampson Process):** Multi-stage reversible + irreversible expansion is used to liquefy air, oxygen, and nitrogen.
- **Cryogenic Engineering:** Adiabatic expansion of helium is fundamental to achieving near-absolute-zero temperatures in superconducting MRI machines.
- **Nozzle and Turbine Design:** In steam/gas turbines, the expansion through the nozzle is modeled as reversible adiabatic (isentropic), with isentropic efficiency used to quantify deviation from ideality.
- **Gas Dynamics:** Rocket and jet engine nozzles operate on the principle of reversible adiabatic expansion to convert thermal energy into kinetic energy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Reversible Adiabatic Relation $PV^\gamma = \text{constant}$ for an Ideal Gas

### Starting Point — The First Law of Thermodynamics

The first law for a closed system undergoing a quasi-static process is:

$$dQ = dU + dW$$

For a **reversible** process: $dW = P \cdot dV$.

For an **adiabatic** process: $dQ = 0$.

For an **ideal gas**: $dU = m c_v \, dT$.

Substituting all three conditions:

$$0 = m c_v \, dT + P \, dV$$

For an ideal gas, $PV = mRT$, so differentiating using the product rule:

$$P \, dV + V \, dP = mR \, dT \implies dT = \dfrac{P \, dV + V \, dP}{mR}$$

Substituting $dT$ into the first-law equation:

$$0 = m c_v \left(\dfrac{P \, dV + V \, dP}{mR}\right) + P \, dV$$

Multiplying through by $R$:

$$0 = c_v (P \, dV + V \, dP) + RP \, dV$$

$$0 = (c_v + R) P \, dV + c_v V \, dP$$

Since $c_p = c_v + R$, we have:

$$0 = c_p P \, dV + c_v V \, dP$$

Dividing both sides by $c_v PV$:

$$0 = \gamma \dfrac{dV}{V} + \dfrac{dP}{P}$$

Integrating between states 1 and 2:

$$\int_1^2 \gamma \dfrac{dV}{V} + \int_1^2 \dfrac{dP}{P} = 0$$

$$\gamma \ln \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} + \ln \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = 0$$

$$\ln \left[ \left(\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^{\gamma} \cdot \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right] = 0$$

$$\boxed{P_1 V_1^{\gamma} = P_2 V_2^{\gamma} = \text{constant}}$$

> **Physical interpretation:** This single equation is the cornerstone of all adiabatic flow analysis. It tells us that as the gas expands (volume increases), its pressure must drop *faster* than for an isothermal process, because the gas is also cooling.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Temperature-Pressure Relation in Reversible Adiabatic Process

Starting from $PV^\gamma = \text{constant}$, substitute $V = \dfrac{mRT}{P}$:

$$P \left(\dfrac{mRT}{P}\right)^{\gamma} = \text{constant}$$

$$P^{1-\gamma} \cdot T^{\gamma} = \text{constant}$$

This can be equivalently written as:

$$\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$

> **Derivation logic:** The pressure term is raised to $(1-\gamma)$, so when pressure *decreases* in expansion, the temperature *must decrease* to keep the product constant.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Work Done in a Reversible Adiabatic Process

For a reversible process: $W = \int_1^2 P \, dV$.

Using the identity $P_1 V_1^{\gamma} = P V^{\gamma}$, we have $P = P_1 V_1^{\gamma} V^{-\gamma}$.

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P_1 V_1^{\gamma} V^{-\gamma} \, dV$$

$$W = P_1 V_1^{\gamma} \left[ \dfrac{V^{1-\gamma}}{1-\gamma} \right]_{V_1}^{V_2}$$

$$W = \dfrac{P_1 V_1^{\gamma}}{1-\gamma} \left(V_2^{1-\gamma} - V_1^{1-\gamma}\right)$$

$$W = \dfrac{P_1 V_1^{\gamma} V_2^{1-\gamma} - P_1 V_1^{\gamma} V_1^{1-\gamma}}{1-\gamma}$$

$$W = \dfrac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1-\gamma} = \dfrac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{\gamma - 1}$$

Using the ideal gas law, $PV = mRT$:

$$\boxed{W = \dfrac{mR(T_1 - T_2)}{\gamma - 1}}$$

> **Examination tip:** This is the most-tested work formula in Module 1.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Entropy Change in Irreversible Free Expansion

Consider an ideal gas expanding freely from volume $V_1$ to $V_2$ in an insulated chamber. Although the *actual* process is irreversible, entropy is a **state function**, so we can calculate $\Delta S$ using a hypothetical *reversible path* between the same two end states.

For a reversible isothermal path (since $\Delta T = 0$ for ideal gas free expansion):

$$dQ_{rev} = P \, dV = \dfrac{mRT}{V} dV$$

$$\Delta S = \int_1^2 \dfrac{dQ_{rev}}{T} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \dfrac{mR}{V} dV$$

$$\boxed{\Delta S = mR \ln \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} > 0}$$

> **Why this is critical:** The increase in entropy quantifies the *irreversibility* of the process. It is a direct measure of the energy that has been "degraded" and is no longer available to do useful work.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Joule-Thomson Coefficient for a Real Gas

For a real gas undergoing a throttling (irreversible adiabatic) process, the **enthalpy** remains constant ($H_1 = H_2$) because $Q = 0$ and $W = -\Delta(PV)$, so $\Delta U + \Delta(PV) = 0$, giving $\Delta H = 0$.

The Joule-Thomson coefficient is defined as:

$$\mu_{JT} = \left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_H$$

For a van der Waals gas, after a lengthy thermodynamic manipulation (using the Maxwell relations), one obtains:

$$\mu_{JT} = \dfrac{1}{c_p} \left[ \dfrac{2a}{RT} - b \right]$$

where $a$ and $b$ are the van der Waals constants representing intermolecular attraction and finite molecular volume, respectively.

> **Physical interpretation:** The $\dfrac{2a}{RT}$ term represents *attractive* intermolecular forces (causes cooling during expansion), and $b$ represents *repulsive* forces (causes heating). At the **inversion temperature**, $T_i = \dfrac{2a}{Rb}$, and $\mu_{JT} = 0$.

## 3.6 Summary Matrix — Final State Predictions

| Process | Path on P-V Diagram | Final Temperature | Entropy Change | Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Rev. Adiabatic (Ideal) | Smooth curve $PV^\gamma = C$ | $T_2 < T_1$ | $0$ | Gas turbine nozzle |
| Irrev. Free Expansion | Cannot be plotted | $T_2 = T_1$ (ideal) | $\Delta S > 0$ | Joule expansion experiment |
| Irrev. Throttling (Real) | Cannot be plotted | $T_2 < T_1$ (below $T_i$) | $\Delta S > 0$ | Refrigerant expansion valve |
| Reversible Isothermal | Hyperbola $PV = C$ | $T_2 = T_1$ | $\Delta S > 0$ (heat absorbed) | Slow piston-cylinder |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 P-V Diagram — Reversible vs. Irreversible Adiabatic Expansion

The following Mermaid block renders a schematic functional architecture comparing the two expansion processes, including P-V curves and the physical setup.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PVDIAG["P-V DIAGRAM COMPARISON"]
        direction TB
        A1["Initial State 1: P1 high, V1 small"] -->|Reversible Adiabatic Path PV^gamma = C| B1["Final State 2: P2 low, V2 large"]
        A1 -.->|Irreversible Free Expansion: Dashed Line| B1
        B1["Final State 2: P2 low, V2 large"] --> C1["Isothermal Reference: PV = Constant"]
    end

    subgraph REVCASE["REVERSIBLE ADIABATIC SETUP"]
        direction LR
        D1["Insulated Piston Cylinder"] --> D2["Gas Pushes Piston Slowly"]
        D2 --> D3["External Work W positive"]
        D3 --> D4["Final Temperature T2 less than T1"]
    end

    subgraph IRREVCASE["IRREVERSIBLE FREE EXPANSION SETUP"]
        direction LR
        E1["Insulated Rigid Chamber"] --> E2["Partition Ruptures Suddenly"]
        E2 --> E3["Gas Rushes into Vacuum"]
        E3 --> E4["External Work W equals zero"]
        E4 --> E5["T2 equals T1 for ideal gas"]
    end

    PVDIAG --- REVCASE
    PVDIAG --- IRREVCASE
```

## 4.2 Process Decision Flow — Identifying Expansion Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Given: A gas expansion process"] --> B0{"Is heat transfer zero? Q equals zero?"}
    B0 -- "No" --> C0["Process is NOT adiabatic — use general first law"]
    B0 -- "Yes" --> D0["Process is ADIABATIC — proceed"]
    D0 --> E0{"Is the process quasi-static and frictionless?"}
    E0 -- "Yes" --> F0["REVERSIBLE ADIABATIC: PV^gamma = Constant, Delta S equals 0"]
    E0 -- "No" --> G0["IRREVERSIBLE ADIABATIC: W not equal to integral P dV"]
    G0 --> G1{"Is the gas ideal or real?"}
    G1 -- "Ideal" --> G2["Free Expansion: Delta T equals 0, Delta S greater than 0"]
    G1 -- "Real" --> G3["Throttling: Delta H equals 0, Joule-Thomson cooling or heating"]
    F0 --> H0["Engineering Use: Turbine Nozzle, Compressor Analysis"]
    G3 --> H0
    H0 --> I0["Outcome: Final state predicted using KTU Formula Sheet"]
```

## 4.3 Real Gas Behaviour Map — Joule-Thomson Effect

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HIGHP["HIGH PRESSURE REGION"]
        HP1["Molecules tightly packed"] --> HP2["Repulsive forces DOMINATE"]
        HP2 --> HP3["Expansion causes HEATING: T2 greater than T1"]
    end
    subgraph INV["INVERSION TEMPERATURE"]
        INV1["T equals T_i equals 2a over Rb"] --> INV2["Mu JT equals zero"]
        INV2 --> INV3["No temperature change on expansion"]
    end
    subgraph LOWP["LOW PRESSURE REGION BELOW T_i"]
        LP1["Molecules moderately spaced"] --> LP2["Attractive forces DOMINATE"]
        LP2 --> LP3["Expansion causes COOLING: T2 less than T1"]
        LP3 --> LP4["Used in REFRIGERATION: Joule-Thomson valve"]
    end
    HIGHP --> INV
    INV --> LOWP
```

## 4.4 System Architecture — Application in a Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["1. COMPRESSOR\nReversible Adiabatic Compression\nPressure rises, Temperature rises"] --> B["2. CONDENSER\nHeat Rejected Q out\nIsothermal or Isobaric"]
    B --> C["3. EXPANSION VALVE\nIRREVERSIBLE THROTTLING\nAdiabatic Q equals zero, W equals zero, Delta H equals zero"]
    C --> D["4. EVAPORATOR\nHeat Absorbed Q in\nRefrigeration Effect Produced"]
    D --> A
    style A fill:#f9c,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#9cf,stroke:#333
    style C stroke-width:3px
    style D fill:#fc9,stroke:#333
```

> **Note on this diagram:** The expansion valve (highlighted in blue) is the *exact* real-world application of irreversible adiabatic expansion of a *real* refrigerant gas. This is the cooling stage of every domestic refrigerator.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1, CO1]`

**Q: Differentiate between reversible and irreversible adiabatic expansion of an ideal gas, with a neat P-V sketch.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Distribution shown):**

| Component | Marks |
|---|---|
| Reversible definition + W expression | 1 |
| Irreversible definition + W expression | 1 |
| P-V diagram description | 1 |

**Model Solution:**

A **reversible adiabatic expansion** is a quasi-static process in which the gas expands infinitesimally slowly through a continuous series of equilibrium states, with no friction, turbulence, or dissipative effects. For an ideal gas, it follows the relation $PV^\gamma = \text{constant}$, and the work done is:

$$W_{rev} = \dfrac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{\gamma - 1}$$

An **irreversible adiabatic expansion** (e.g., free expansion into a vacuum) is a spontaneous, non-equilibrium process in which the gas has no defined intermediate states. The external work done is zero ($W = 0$), and no path can be plotted on the P-V diagram.

**P-V Diagram Description:** The reversible path is a smooth curve from state 1 to state 2, lying *below* the isothermal curve and *above* the irreversible free expansion (which is shown as a vertical dashed line at constant internal pressure — or, more rigorously, no defined path).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1, CO1]`

**Q: State the first law of thermodynamics applied to an adiabatic process. Why does an ideal gas free expansion result in zero temperature change, while a real gas may show cooling?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

For an adiabatic process, $Q = 0$, so the first law reduces to:

$$dU = -dW$$

or, for a finite change:

$$\Delta U = -W$$

**For an ideal gas:** Internal energy is a function of temperature alone: $U = U(T)$. During free expansion into a vacuum, $W = 0$ (no work is done against external pressure) and $Q = 0$ (insulated). Therefore, $\Delta U = 0$, which implies $\Delta T = 0$. The temperature remains unchanged.

**For a real gas:** Internal energy is a function of *both* temperature and volume: $U = U(T, V)$. During expansion, intermolecular forces perform internal "work" — attractive forces do positive work on the molecules, reducing their kinetic energy, which manifests as a **drop in temperature** (the Joule-Thomson effect exploited in refrigeration).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 1, CO2 — 14 Marks]`

**A. (a)** Derive the relation $PV^\gamma = \text{constant}$ for the reversible adiabatic expansion of an ideal gas. **(7 Marks)**

**A. (b)** A quantity of air ($\gamma = 1.4$) at $27^\circ\text{C}$ and $200$ kPa is expanded reversibly and adiabatically to **one-fourth** of its original volume. Determine the final pressure, final temperature, and work done per kg of air. Take $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** State the first law for a reversible adiabatic process.

For a closed system: $dQ = dU + dW$.
Reversible: $dW = P \, dV$.
Adiabatic: $dQ = 0$.
Ideal gas: $dU = m c_v \, dT$.

Combining:

$$0 = m c_v \, dT + P \, dV$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Express $dT$ using the ideal gas law $PV = mRT$.

$$P \, dV + V \, dP = mR \, dT$$

$$dT = \dfrac{P \, dV + V \, dP}{mR}$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Substitute back and simplify.

$$0 = m c_v \cdot \dfrac{P \, dV + V \, dP}{mR} + P \, dV$$

$$0 = c_v (P \, dV + V \, dP) + R P \, dV$$

$$0 = (c_v + R) P \, dV + c_v V \, dP = c_p P \, dV + c_v V \, dP$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Integrate to obtain the final relation.

Dividing by $c_v PV$:

$$\gamma \dfrac{dV}{V} + \dfrac{dP}{P} = 0$$

Integrating:

$$\gamma \ln \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} + \ln \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = 0$$

$$\boxed{P_1 V_1^\gamma = P_2 V_2^\gamma}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data (1 Mark):**
- $T_1 = 27 + 273 = 300$ K
- $P_1 = 200$ kPa
- $V_2 = V_1 / 4$ → $V_2 / V_1 = 1/4$
- $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K

**Step 1: Find Final Volume Ratio and Use Adiabatic Relation (1 Mark)**

$$P_1 V_1^\gamma = P_2 V_2^\gamma$$

$$P_2 = P_1 \left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^\gamma = 200 \times (4)^{1.4}$$

Computing $(4)^{1.4}$:

$$\ln(4^{1.4}) = 1.4 \times \ln 4 = 1.4 \times 1.3863 = 1.9408$$

$$4^{1.4} = e^{1.9408} = 6.964$$

$$P_2 = 200 \times 6.964 = 1392.8 \text{ kPa}$$

**Final Pressure: $P_2 = 1392.8$ kPa** ✓

**Step 2: Find Final Temperature (2 Marks)**

Using the temperature-volume relation:

$$T_2 = T_1 \left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = 300 \times (4)^{0.4}$$

$$\ln(4^{0.4}) = 0.4 \times 1.3863 = 0.5545$$

$$4^{0.4} = e^{0.5545} = 1.741$$

$$T_2 = 300 \times 1.741 = 522.3 \text{ K} = 249.3^\circ\text{C}$$

**Final Temperature: $T_2 = 522.3$ K (≈ 249.3 °C)**

**Step 3: Calculate Work Done (3 Marks)**

$$W = \dfrac{mR(T_1 - T_2)}{\gamma - 1} = \dfrac{1 \times 0.287 \times (300 - 522.3)}{1.4 - 1}$$

$$W = \dfrac{0.287 \times (-222.3)}{0.4} = \dfrac{-63.80}{0.4} = -159.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

The negative sign indicates work is done **by** the system (expansion work output).

**Work Done: $W = -159.5$ kJ/kg (or 159.5 kJ/kg done by the gas)**

---

### QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 1, CO2 — 14 Marks]`

**B. (a)** Explain the free expansion of a real gas. How does it differ from the free expansion of an ideal gas? Use the Joule-Thomson coefficient concept. **(7 Marks)**

**B. (b)** A real gas undergoes an irreversible adiabatic (throttling) expansion from $10$ bar, $300$ K to $2$ bar. The Joule-Thomson coefficient is $0.15$ K/bar. Determine the final temperature and the change in entropy per kmol. Take $c_p = 30$ kJ/kmol·K and $R = 8.314$ kJ/kmol·K. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (2 Marks):**

**Free expansion of an ideal gas:** When an ideal gas expands into a vacuum in an insulated rigid chamber, $Q = 0$ and $W = 0$, so $\Delta U = 0$. Since $U$ depends only on $T$ for an ideal gas, $\Delta T = 0$. The gas temperature does not change.

**Free expansion of a real gas:** For a real gas, $U$ is a function of both $T$ and $V$, i.e., $U = U(T, V)$. Therefore, even when $\Delta U = 0$, the temperature may change because the gas performs internal work against intermolecular forces.

**Joule-Thomson Coefficient Explanation (3 Marks):**

The Joule-Thomson coefficient is defined as:

$$\mu_{JT} = \left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_H$$

For a van der Waals gas:

$$\mu_{JT} = \dfrac{1}{c_p}\left(\dfrac{2a}{RT} - b\right)$$

- **If $\mu_{JT} > 0$ (below inversion temperature):** Expansion causes **cooling**. This is the regime used in refrigeration.
- **If $\mu_{JT} < 0$ (above inversion temperature):** Expansion causes **heating**.
- **If $\mu_{JT} = 0$ (at inversion temperature):** No temperature change.

**Comparison Table (2 Marks):**

| Property | Ideal Gas Free Expansion | Real Gas Free Expansion |
|---|---|---|
| $\Delta U$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| $\Delta T$ | $0$ | Generally ≠ 0 (cooling or heating) |
| Reason | $U = U(T)$ only | $U = U(T, V)$ due to intermolecular forces |
| Joule-Thomson coefficient | $\mu_{JT} = 0$ always | $\mu_{JT} \neq 0$ in general |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data (1 Mark):**
- $P_1 = 10$ bar, $T_1 = 300$ K
- $P_2 = 2$ bar
- $\mu_{JT} = 0.15$ K/bar
- $c_p = 30$ kJ/kmol·K
- $R = 8.314$ kJ/kmol·K

**Step 1: Calculate Final Temperature (2 Marks)**

Using the definition of the Joule-Thomson coefficient:

$$T_2 - T_1 = \mu_{JT} (P_2 - P_1)$$

$$T_2 = 300 + 0.15 \times (2 - 10) = 300 + 0.15 \times (-8)$$

$$T_2 = 300 - 1.2 = 298.8 \text{ K}$$

**Final Temperature: $T_2 = 298.8$ K** ✓

**Step 2: Calculate Enthalpy Change (1 Mark)**

For a throttling process, $H_1 = H_2$, so $\Delta H = 0$.

**Step 3: Calculate Entropy Change Using Maxwell Relation (3 Marks)**

The entropy change of a real gas in a throttling process can be calculated as:

$$\Delta S = - \int_{P_1}^{P_2} \mu_{JT} \, c_p \, dP$$

(approximation valid when $c_p$ and $\mu_{JT}$ are roughly constant over the pressure range)

$$\Delta S = -\mu_{JT} \cdot c_p \cdot (P_2 - P_1)$$

$$\Delta S = -0.15 \times 30 \times (2 - 10) = -0.15 \times 30 \times (-8)$$

$$\Delta S = +36 \text{ kJ/kmol·K}$$

**Entropy Change: $\Delta S = +36$ kJ/kmol·K** (positive, confirming irreversibility)

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic — Read Before Writing the Exam!**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention for Work (–2 to –3 Marks):** Forgetting that *work done BY the gas is negative* by the IUPAC / KTU convention. Many students write $W = +159.5$ kJ/kg and lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing up $\gamma$ with $R$ in Formulas (–1 to –2 Marks):** Using $R = 8.314$ instead of $R = 0.287$ for per-kg air calculations is a frequent blunder. Always check whether the answer is per kg, per kmol, or total.
> 3. **Confusing Free Expansion with Throttling (–1 Mark):** Free expansion → $W = 0$ into vacuum. Throttling → $H = \text{constant}$ through a porous plug or valve. They are *different* irreversible processes.
> 4. **Forgetting $\Delta S$ for Reversible Adiabatic (–1 Mark):** Many students write $\Delta S = 0$ for *all* adiabatic processes. WRONG! Only *reversible* adiabatic is isentropic. Irreversible adiabatic has $\Delta S > 0$.
> 5. **Unit Inconsistency in Joule-Thomson (–1 Mark):** $\mu_{JT}$ is often given in K/bar or K/atm. Make sure the pressure difference uses the *same unit* before plugging in.
> 6. **Missing Path Justification in Derivations (–2 Marks):** The examiner expects to see *every* algebraic step in the $PV^\gamma$ derivation. Skipping the integration step or "logarithm both sides" step costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.5 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Most Important Things to Remember — Final 60-Second Revision**
>
> - **Reversible Adiabatic = Isentropic = $PV^\gamma = C$** for an ideal gas. Work done: $W = \dfrac{mR(T_1 - T_2)}{\gamma - 1}$.
> - **Irreversible Free Expansion:** $W = 0$, $Q = 0$, $\Delta U = 0$, $\Delta T = 0$ (ideal gas only).
> - **Entropy change in free expansion:** $\Delta S = mR \ln(V_2 / V_1) > 0$ — this is the canonical proof of irreversibility.
> - **Real Gas Free Expansion:** $\Delta T \neq 0$ because $U = f(T, V)$; Joule-Thomson cooling occurs below inversion temperature.
> - **Joule-Thomson Coefficient:** $\mu_{JT} = (1/c_p)[(2a/RT) - b]$. Positive value → cooling on expansion.
> - **Polytropic Process (general case):** $PV^n = C$, where $n = 0$ → isobaric, $n = 1$ → isothermal, $n = \gamma$ → adiabatic, $n = \infty$ → isochoric.
> - **Refrigeration Link:** The expansion valve in a vapour compression cycle is the engineering application of irreversible adiabatic expansion of a real refrigerant.
> - **Three formulas to memorize cold:** (i) $T_2/T_1 = (P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$, (ii) $W = (P_1V_1 - P_2V_2)/(\gamma - 1)$, (iii) $\Delta S = mR \ln(V_2/V_1)$.
> - **Always convert temperatures to Kelvin** before using in any gas-law equation.
> - **Always include units** in the final answer — KTU examiners explicitly award 1 mark for correct units in numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
