topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thermoelectric Cooling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ⚡ MODULE 1 — THERMOELECTRIC COOLING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermoelectric Cooling (Peltier Effect Cooling)** is a solid-state refrigeration method that utilizes the **Peltier effect** to transfer heat from one junction of a dissimilar conductor pair to another when a direct electric current is passed through the circuit, thereby producing a cooling (heat-absorbing) effect at one junction and a heating (heat-rejecting) effect at the other junction without any moving parts, refrigerants, or compressors.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, thermoelectric refrigeration falls under **non-conventional / unconventional refrigeration systems** studied in Module 1 of the PEAGT411 syllabus. The basic unit is called a **Thermoelectric Module (TEM)** or **Peltier Couple**, typically constructed using heavily doped **bismuth telluride (Bi₂Te₃)** semiconductors — one leg doped **p-type** and the other **n-type**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Person Sponge" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a busy railway platform** with two porters standing side by side. Porter A (p-type leg) and Porter B (n-type leg) are connected by a small wooden plank (the copper bridge/junction).
>
> 1. When the Station Master (DC power supply) orders them to start walking in a loop carrying water buckets:
>    - At the **inner junction** (cold side), they *pick up* water (heat) from the surrounding air → the platform feels **cool**.
>    - At the **outer junction** (hot side), they *drop off* all that water (heat) → the platform feels **hot**.
> 2. The "sponge" quality of each porter is governed by the **Seebeck Coefficient (α)**, and the friction of carrying the buckets is the **Electrical Resistance (R)** and **Thermal Conductance (K)**.

So, in one sentence: **Push electrons across a junction, and they physically drag heat along with them.** No compressor, no refrigerant — just electrons doing the heavy lifting!

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard constants and reference metrics for Bi₂Te₃ thermoelectric couples used in KTU problems:
>
> - **Seebeck Coefficient (α)** ≈ **400 μV/°C** per couple (typical n–p pair combined)
> - **Electrical Resistivity (ρ)** ≈ **10⁻⁵ Ω·m**
> - **Thermal Conductivity (k)** ≈ **1.5 W/m·K**
> - **Maximum Figure of Merit (ZT)** ≈ **0.7 – 1.0** at room temperature
> - **Operating current density** ≈ **5–10 A/mm²**
> - **Practical temperature drop** per stage ≈ **60–70 °C**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Thermoelectric Effects

A thermoelectric system rests on **three interrelated phenomena** discovered in the 19th century:

#### 🔹 2.1.1 Seebeck Effect (1821)
- A **temperature difference** between two junctions of dissimilar conductors generates an **electromotive force (EMF)**.
- Mathematically: $V = \alpha \, (T_h - T_c)$
- This is the **reverse** of Peltier — it is the principle behind **thermocouples and TEGs (Thermoelectric Generators)**.

#### 🔹 2.1.2 Peltier Effect (1834)
- When a **DC current** passes through the junction of two dissimilar conductors, heat is either **absorbed or evolved** at the junction depending on the direction of current flow.
- Rate of heat absorption: $q_{peltier} = \alpha \, T \, I$
- **This is the foundation of thermoelectric cooling.**

#### 🔹 2.1.3 Thomson Effect (1856)
- A single homogeneous conductor carrying current, while simultaneously subjected to a **temperature gradient**, absorbs or releases heat along its length.
- Thomson heat: $q_{thomson} = \tau \, I \, \dfrac{dT}{dx}$
- Where $\tau$ is the **Thomson coefficient**.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of a Peltier Couple

A single-stage Peltier module consists of an **n-type** and a **p-type** semiconductor pellet joined by a metallic bridge (usually copper). The pellets are sandwiched between two ceramic plates (electrical insulators but thermal conductors).

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-step energy flow (read this carefully — KTU board questions test this):**
>
> 1. **DC Current Entry**: Electrons flow from the n-type leg → through the copper bridge → into the p-type leg at the cold side junction.
> 2. **Peltier Heat Absorption**: At the cold junction, electrons move from a higher to a lower energy state (n → p type), absorbing **Peltier heat** equal to $\alpha T_c I$.
> 3. **Joule Heating Loss**: Along the length of each leg, resistive (I²R) heating occurs — half of it conducts back to the cold side and reduces net cooling.
> 4. **Fourier Conduction Loss**: Heat naturally flows back from hot side to cold side by conduction through the legs: $K(T_h - T_c)$.
> 5. **Peltier Heat Rejection**: At the hot junction, electrons jump from low to high energy state (p → n), releasing **Peltier heat** $\alpha T_h I$.
> 6. **Net Cooling Effect**: The useful cooling rate is therefore: **(Peltier heat at cold side) − (½ Joule heat) − (Fourier back-conduction)**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Meaning of Each Symbol** | **Units** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | Heat absorbed at cold junction | $Q_c = \alpha T_c I - \tfrac{1}{2} I^{2}R - K(T_h - T_c)$ | $\alpha$ = Seebeck coefficient; $T_c$ = cold junction temp; $I$ = current; $R$ = resistance; $K$ = thermal conductance; $T_h$ = hot junction temp | W |
| 2 | Heat rejected at hot junction | $Q_h = \alpha T_h I + \tfrac{1}{2} I^{2}R - K(T_h - T_c)$ | Same as above | W |
| 3 | Power input to the couple | $W = Q_h - Q_c = \alpha (T_h - T_c) I + I^{2}R$ | $W$ = electrical input power | W |
| 4 | Coefficient of Performance | $COP = \dfrac{Q_c}{W} = \dfrac{\alpha T_c I - \tfrac{1}{2} I^{2}R - K(T_h - T_c)}{\alpha (T_h - T_c) I + I^{2}R}$ | Dimensionless performance index | — |
| 5 | Figure of Merit | $Z = \dfrac{\alpha^{2}}{R \cdot K}$ | Material property determining performance | 1/K |
| 6 | Maximum COP (derived) | $COP_{\max} = \left(\dfrac{T_c}{T_h - T_c}\right) \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{1 + Z \bar{T}} - \dfrac{T_h}{T_c}}{\sqrt{1 + Z \bar{T}} + 1}$ | $\bar{T} = (T_h+T_c)/2$ | — |
| 7 | Maximum cooling rate | $Q_{c,\max} = \dfrac{\alpha^{2} T_c^{2}}{2R} - K (T_h - T_c)$ | Achieved at optimal current | W |
| 8 | Optimum current for max COP | $I_{COP,\max} = \dfrac{\alpha (T_h - T_c)}{R\left(\sqrt{1+Z \bar{T}} - 1\right)}$ | Gives best efficiency | A |
| 9 | Optimum current for max cooling | $I_{Q,\max} = \dfrac{\alpha T_c}{R}$ | Gives largest cooling capacity | A |
| 10 | Carnot COP (upper limit) | $COP_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_c}{T_h - T_c}$ | Theoretical upper bound | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Reality Check**: $COP_{\max}$ of a thermoelectric refrigerator is always **less than Carnot COP** because of the irreversible Joule and Fourier losses. This is a frequent KTU board question!

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| **Application Sector** | **Use Case** |
|:---|:---|
| Electronics | CPU/GPU spot coolers, laser diode temperature stabilization |
| Medical | Portable vaccine cold boxes, PCR thermal cycling |
| Aerospace | Spacecraft instrument cooling (RTGs' reverse mode) |
| Military | Field-deployable refrigeration for rations & blood |
| Automotive | Electric car battery thermal management |
| Domestic | Mini wine coolers, camping coolers |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Net Cooling Rate at the Cold Junction

#### **Starting Physical Model**

Consider a single p-n thermocouple with:
- Electrical resistance $R$ (sum of both legs)
- Thermal conductance $K$ (sum of both legs)
- Seebeck coefficient $\alpha$ (algebraic sum of the two legs)
- Cold and hot junction temperatures $T_c$ and $T_h$ in **Kelvin**.

#### **Step 1 — Peltier Heat Absorbed at the Cold Junction**

When current $I$ flows from the n-type to the p-type material at the cold junction, the electrons absorb energy equal to the Peltier coefficient times the absolute temperature.

$$
q_{\text{Peltier, c}} = \alpha \, T_c \, I
$$

This is the **useful cooling** generated at the cold side.

#### **Step 2 — Joule (Ohmic) Heat Generation**

Current flowing through the resistance $R$ of the couple produces I²R heat uniformly along the legs. By symmetry, exactly **half** of this heat flows toward the cold junction and the other half toward the hot junction.

$$
q_{\text{Joule, to cold}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \, I^{2} \, R
$$

This is a **parasitic loss** — it opposes the cooling effect.

#### **Step 3 — Fourier Back-Conduction**

Due to the temperature difference $(T_h - T_c)$, heat conducts back through the legs from hot to cold by Fourier's law.

$$
q_{\text{Fourier}} = K \, (T_h - T_c)
$$

This is **another parasitic loss**.

#### **Step 4 — Net Cooling Rate**

The **useful cooling rate at the cold junction** is therefore:

$$
Q_c = \alpha T_c I - \tfrac{1}{2} I^{2} R - K (T_h - T_c)
$$

> **Equation (A)** — *Net heat absorbed at cold junction (Watts).*

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Power Input to the Couple

#### **Step 5 — Heat Rejected at the Hot Junction**

By the same energy-balance logic, at the hot junction:

$$
q_{\text{Peltier, h}} = \alpha \, T_h \, I
$$

Plus half the Joule heat also arrives here (the other half):

$$
q_{\text{Joule, to hot}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \, I^{2} \, R
$$

Minus the Fourier loss that left toward the cold side:

$$
q_{\text{Fourier out}} = K(T_h - T_c)
$$

So:

$$
Q_h = \alpha T_h I + \tfrac{1}{2} I^{2} R - K(T_h - T_c)
$$

> **Equation (B)** — *Net heat rejected at hot junction.*

#### **Step 6 — Energy Conservation ⇒ Power Input**

Applying the **First Law of Thermodynamics** (steady state, no accumulation):

$$
W = Q_h - Q_c
$$

$$
W = \bigl[\alpha T_h I + \tfrac{1}{2} I^{2} R - K (T_h - T_c)\bigr] - \bigl[\alpha T_c I - \tfrac{1}{2} I^{2} R - K (T_h - T_c)\bigr]
$$

Notice the $K(T_h - T_c)$ terms cancel cleanly:

$$
W = \alpha T_h I + \tfrac{1}{2} I^{2} R - \alpha T_c I + \tfrac{1}{2} I^{2} R
$$

Grouping:

$$
W = \alpha (T_h - T_c) \, I + I^{2} R
$$

> **Equation (C)** — *Electrical power input to the couple.*

---

### 3.3 Derivation of COP and Its Maximum

#### **Step 7 — Coefficient of Performance**

$$
COP = \dfrac{Q_c}{W} = \dfrac{\alpha T_c I - \tfrac{1}{2} I^{2} R - K(T_h - T_c)}{\alpha (T_h - T_c) I + I^{2} R}
$$

#### **Step 8 — Condition for Maximum COP**

To find the current $I$ that gives maximum COP, set $\dfrac{d(COP)}{dI} = 0$.

Let numerator $= N = \alpha T_c I - \tfrac{1}{2} I^{2}R - K(T_h - T_c)$ and denominator $= D = \alpha (T_h - T_c) I + I^{2}R$.

Then:

$$
\dfrac{d(COP)}{dI} = \dfrac{N' D - N D'}{D^{2}} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N' D = N D'
$$

Compute derivatives:

$$
N' = \alpha T_c - I R
$$

$$
D' = \alpha (T_h - T_c) + 2 I R
$$

Substituting and simplifying (extensive algebra omitted for space but **must be shown in exam**), one obtains:

$$
COP_{\max} = \left( \dfrac{T_c}{T_h - T_c} \right) \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{1 + Z \bar{T}} - \dfrac{T_h}{T_c}}{\sqrt{1 + Z \bar{T}} + 1}
$$

where $\bar{T} = \dfrac{T_h + T_c}{2}$ and $Z = \dfrac{\alpha^{2}}{R K}$ is the **figure of merit**.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem**: A thermoelectric refrigerator uses a couple with $\alpha = 400 \,\mu V/°C$, $R = 0.005 \,\Omega$, $K = 0.05 \, W/K$. Cold side is at $T_c = 270\,K$, hot side at $T_h = 300\,K$. If the current is $I = 10\,A$, calculate the cooling rate, power input, and COP.

#### **Solution**

**Given:**
- $\alpha = 400 \times 10^{-6} = 4 \times 10^{-4}$ V/K
- $R = 0.005 \,\Omega$
- $K = 0.05$ W/K
- $T_c = 270$ K, $T_h = 300$ K
- $I = 10$ A

**Step 1 — Cooling rate:**

$$
\alpha T_c I = (4 \times 10^{-4})(270)(10) = 1.08 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\tfrac{1}{2} I^{2} R = \tfrac{1}{2}(100)(0.005) = 0.25 \text{ W}
$$

$$
K(T_h - T_c) = (0.05)(30) = 1.50 \text{ W}
$$

$$
Q_c = 1.08 - 0.25 - 1.50 = -0.67 \text{ W}
$$

> The negative sign indicates **no net cooling** at 10 A — the Joule + Fourier losses dominate! In KTU exams, always comment on this.

**Step 2 — Power input:**

$$
W = \alpha (T_h - T_c) I + I^{2} R
$$

$$
W = (4 \times 10^{-4})(30)(10) + (100)(0.005)
$$

$$
W = 0.12 + 0.50 = 0.62 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 3 — COP:**

Since $Q_c < 0$, COP is negative — the device is **not cooling**, only heating. We must reduce the current or increase the figure of merit $Z$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Architecture of a Single-Stage Peltier Module

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DC[DC Power Supply V volts] --> Pos[Positive Terminal]
    DC --> Neg[Negative Terminal]
    Pos --> TopCu1[Top Copper Strip - HOT side]
    Neg --> TopCu2[Top Copper Strip - HOT side]
    TopCu1 --> N1[n-type Pellet Bi2Te3]
    N1 --> MidCu1[Middle Copper Bridge - COLD side]
    MidCu1 --> P1[p-type Pellet Bi2Te3]
    P1 --> TopCu3[Top Copper Strip - HOT side]
    TopCu3 --> Neg
    MidCu1 -. absorbs .-> Heat1[Qc Heat absorbed here]
    TopCu1 -. rejects .-> Heat2[Qh Heat rejected here]
    TopCu2 -. rejects .-> Heat2
    TopCu3 -. rejects .-> Heat2
```

> **Figure 4.1** — Single-stage Peltier module: current enters the n-type leg at the hot side, crosses the cold-side copper bridge to the p-type leg, and exits at the hot side. Heat $Q_c$ is absorbed at the bridge and $Q_h$ is dumped at the top.

### 4.2 Energy Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[Electrical Input]
        P[DC Power W = alpha delta T I plus I squared R]
    end
    subgraph COLD[Cold Side Reservoir at Tc]
        QC[Useful Cooling Qc absorbed by load]
    end
    subgraph HOT[Hot Side Reservoir at Th]
        QH[Total Heat Qh rejected including I squared R]
    end
    subgraph LOSSES[Internal Irreversibilities]
        JL[Half Joule Heat 0.5 I squared R]
        FC[Fourier Back Conduction K delta T]
    end
    P --> COLD
    P --> HOT
    LOSSES -. reduces .-> QC
    LOSSES -. adds to .-> QH
```

> **Figure 4.2** — Energy accounting: useful cooling $Q_c$ is diminished by **Joule heating** and **Fourier back-conduction**, while total rejection $Q_h$ is increased by these losses.

### 4.3 Functional Operating Principle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1 DC current injected into p n couple] --> B[Step 2 Electrons travel from n to p across cold junction]
    B --> C[Step 3 At cold junction electrons absorb Peltier heat alpha Tc I]
    C --> D[Step 4 Simultaneously Joule heat I squared R generated along legs]
    D --> E[Step 5 Fourier conduction K times delta T flows from hot to cold]
    E --> F[Step 6 Net cooling Qc equals alpha Tc I minus 0.5 I squared R minus K delta T]
    F --> G[Step 7 At hot junction Peltier heat alpha Th I released]
    G --> H[Step 8 Plus half Joule heat arrives here]
    H --> I[Step 9 Net rejection Qh equals alpha Th I plus 0.5 I squared R minus K delta T]
    I --> J[Step 10 Power W equals Qh minus Qc equals alpha delta T I plus I squared R]
```

> **Figure 4.3** — Step-by-step operating principle (10 sequential energy transformations).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State and explain the Peltier effect. How is it utilized in thermoelectric refrigeration?*

#### **Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
> When a direct electric current is passed through the junction of two dissimilar conductors, heat is either absorbed or liberated at the junction, depending on the direction of current. The rate of heat absorbed (or released) is given by $q = \alpha T I$, where $\alpha$ is the Seebeck coefficient, $T$ is the absolute junction temperature, and $I$ is the current.

**Application in TEC (1.5 marks):**
> In a thermoelectric refrigerator (TEC), the couple is made of **p-type and n-type bismuth telluride (Bi₂Te₃)** semiconductors joined by a copper bridge. When DC current flows from the n-leg to the p-leg across the cold junction, **Peltier heat is absorbed** at the cold junction ($\alpha T_c I$), cooling the load. At the hot junction, the reverse transition releases heat $\alpha T_h I$, which is removed by a heat sink.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the figure of merit (Z) for a thermoelectric material and state its significance.*

#### **Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
> The figure of merit of a thermoelectric material is defined as:
> $$
> Z = \dfrac{\alpha^{2}}{R \cdot K} = \dfrac{\alpha^{2}}{\rho \cdot k}
> $$
> where $\alpha$ = Seebeck coefficient, $R$ = electrical resistance, $K$ = thermal conductance, $\rho$ = electrical resistivity, and $k$ = thermal conductivity.

**Significance (1 mark):**
> The dimensionless product $Z \bar{T}$ (where $\bar{T}$ is average temperature) determines how closely the **COP of a thermoelectric refrigerator approaches the Carnot COP**. Higher $Z$ ⇒ better performance. For practical Bi₂Te₃ modules at room temperature, $ZT \approx 0.7$–$1.0$.

---

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024]*

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **net rate of heat absorption at the cold junction** of a single-stage thermoelectric refrigerator. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A thermoelectric couple has the following specifications: $\alpha = 4.2 \times 10^{-4}$ V/K, $R = 0.004 \,\Omega$, $K = 0.04$ W/K. Cold junction temperature $T_c = 268$ K and hot junction temperature $T_h = 308$ K. Determine: **(i)** the current for maximum cooling, **(ii)** the maximum cooling rate, **(iii)** the COP at this current. **(7 marks)**

#### **MODEL SOLUTION**

##### **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Steady-state, one-dimensional heat flow.
2. Thomson effect is negligible (small compared to Peltier & Joule).
3. Constant material properties (α, R, K independent of T).
4. No heat loss to surroundings except through heat sinks.
5. Couple is thermally and electrically isolated except at junctions.

**Derivation Steps (6 marks):**

**Step 1:** Peltier heat absorbed at cold junction:
$$
q_{P,c} = \alpha T_c I
$$

**Step 2:** Joule heat produced in legs = $I^2 R$, of which half (½ $I^2 R$) flows toward cold side as parasitic loss.

**Step 3:** Fourier back-conduction from hot to cold = $K(T_h - T_c)$.

**Step 4:** Applying energy balance at cold junction (heat in = heat out at steady state):
$$
Q_c = \alpha T_c I - \tfrac{1}{2} I^2 R - K(T_h - T_c)
$$

> ✔ This is the **net cooling rate**.

##### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
$\alpha = 4.2 \times 10^{-4}$ V/K; $R = 0.004 \,\Omega$; $K = 0.04$ W/K; $T_c = 268$ K; $T_h = 308$ K.

**(i) Current for maximum cooling rate (2 marks):**

For maximum $Q_c$, set $\dfrac{dQ_c}{dI} = 0$:

$$
\dfrac{dQ_c}{dI} = \alpha T_c - I R = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_{Q,\max} = \dfrac{\alpha T_c}{R}
$$

$$
I_{Q,\max} = \dfrac{(4.2 \times 10^{-4})(268)}{0.004} = \dfrac{0.11256}{0.004} = 28.14 \text{ A}
$$

**(ii) Maximum cooling rate (2.5 marks):**

Substituting $I_{Q,\max} = \alpha T_c / R$ into $Q_c$:

$$
Q_{c,\max} = \alpha T_c \cdot \dfrac{\alpha T_c}{R} - \tfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{\alpha T_c}{R}\right)^{2} R - K(T_h - T_c)
$$

$$
Q_{c,\max} = \dfrac{\alpha^{2} T_c^{2}}{R} - \dfrac{\alpha^{2} T_c^{2}}{2R} - K(T_h - T_c) = \dfrac{\alpha^{2} T_c^{2}}{2R} - K(T_h - T_c)
$$

Numerically:

$$
\dfrac{\alpha^{2} T_c^{2}}{2R} = \dfrac{(4.2 \times 10^{-4})^{2} (268)^{2}}{2 \times 0.004} = \dfrac{(1.764 \times 10^{-7})(71824)}{0.008}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{0.01267}{0.008} = 1.584 \text{ W}
$$

$$
K(T_h - T_c) = (0.04)(40) = 1.60 \text{ W}
$$

$$
Q_{c,\max} = 1.584 - 1.60 = -0.016 \text{ W}
$$

> ⚠️ **The maximum cooling is slightly negative**, meaning the couple cannot produce useful cooling for this temperature span. The **maximum allowable temperature differential** is required:

$$
\Delta T_{\max} = \dfrac{\alpha^{2} T_c^{2}}{2RK} = \dfrac{1.584}{0.04} = 39.6 \text{ K}
$$

Since the actual $\Delta T = 40$ K > 39.6 K, cooling is infeasible.

**(iii) COP at $I = I_{Q,\max}$ (2.5 marks):**

$$
W = \alpha(T_h - T_c) I + I^2 R
$$

$$
W = (4.2 \times 10^{-4})(40)(28.14) + (28.14)^{2}(0.004)
$$

$$
W = 0.4727 + 3.168 = 3.641 \text{ W}
$$

$$
COP = \dfrac{Q_c}{W} = \dfrac{-0.016}{3.641} \approx -0.0044
$$

> Negative COP again confirms no net cooling. A **better material** (higher $Z$) is needed.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **construction and working of a thermoelectric refrigeration system**. Also state the materials preferred for the p- and n-type legs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **maximum coefficient of performance (COP)** of a thermoelectric refrigerator in terms of the figure of merit $Z$ and the average temperature $\bar{T}$. **(7 marks)**

#### **MODEL SOLUTION**

##### **Part (a) — Construction & Working (7 marks)**

**Construction (3.5 marks):**
A thermoelectric refrigeration system consists of:

1. **P-N Thermocouple**: Two heavily doped semiconductor pellets — one **p-type (Bi₂Te₃ + Sb)** and one **n-type (Bi₂Te₃ + Se)** — connected in series electrically but in parallel thermally by a small copper bridge (cold junction).
2. **Ceramic Plates**: Top and bottom **alumina (Al₂O₃)** plates act as electrical insulators but excellent thermal conductors, providing mechanical rigidity.
3. **DC Power Supply**: A regulated low-voltage, high-current DC source drives current through the couple.
4. **Heat Sink**: A finned aluminium/copper heat sink with forced-air cooling on the hot side rejects $Q_h$ to the ambience.
5. **Cold Plate**: The cold-side ceramic plate is in contact with the load to be cooled.

**Material Preferences (1 mark):**
- **p-type leg**: Bismuth telluride doped with antimony — Bi₂SbTe₃.
- **n-type leg**: Bismuth telluride doped with selenium — Bi₂Te₃Se.
- These give $\alpha \approx 200 \,\mu V/K$ per leg (combined $\approx 400 \,\mu V/K$).

**Working (2.5 marks):**
> When DC current passes such that electrons travel from n-leg to p-leg at the cold junction, electrons drop in energy (from high to low Fermi level) and **absorb heat** $\alpha T_c I$ — cooling the load. They travel through the p-leg to the hot junction, where they climb back to high energy and **release heat** $\alpha T_h I$ to the heat sink. Useful net cooling = $Q_c = \alpha T_c I - \tfrac{1}{2}I^2R - K\Delta T$.

##### **Part (b) — Derivation of Maximum COP (7 marks)**

Starting from:

$$
COP = \dfrac{\alpha T_c I - \tfrac{1}{2} I^{2} R - K (T_h - T_c)}{\alpha (T_h - T_c) I + I^{2} R}
$$

Let $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$ and $\bar{T} = (T_h + T_c)/2$. Define $\beta = \dfrac{1}{2} = $ (assumed for derivation simplicity — full derivation uses half-Joule factor).

**Step 1:** Divide numerator and denominator by $\alpha \Delta T \cdot I$:

After algebraic manipulation and using $Z = \alpha^{2}/(RK)$, express $I$ in terms of $Z$ and $\bar{T}$.

**Step 2:** Set $\dfrac{d(COP)}{dI} = 0$ to find the optimum current. After differentiating and simplifying, the optimal current satisfies:

$$
I_{\text{opt}} = \dfrac{\alpha \Delta T}{R \left( \sqrt{1 + Z\bar{T}} - 1 \right)}
$$

**Step 3:** Substituting this back into the COP expression and simplifying using $Z\bar{T}$:

$$
COP_{\max} = \left( \dfrac{T_c}{T_h - T_c} \right) \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{1 + Z\bar{T}} - \dfrac{T_h}{T_c}}{\sqrt{1 + Z\bar{T}} + 1}
$$

> This is the final expression (2 marks).

**Step 4:** As $Z \to \infty$, $COP_{\max} \to T_c/(T_h - T_c)$ = **Carnot COP**, confirming thermodynamic consistency (1 mark).

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### 🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls!
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder**: Students frequently forget to convert $\mu V/°C$ to **V/K**. Always use **α in V/K** and **temperatures in Kelvin**. Losing 1–2 marks here.
> 2. **Sign of $Q_c$**: If $Q_c$ comes out negative, **never assume it's an error** — it means the device is not producing net cooling. Comment on it and identify $\Delta T_{\max}$.
> 3. **Forgetting the half-factor of Joule heating**: By symmetry, only **½ I²R** reaches the cold junction. Students often write the full $I^2 R$ — instant 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Confusing $I_{COP,max}$ with $I_{Q,max}$**: These are **two different currents**. $I_{Q,max} = \alpha T_c/R$ (for maximum cooling), while $I_{COP,max}$ uses $\sqrt{1+Z\bar{T}}$ (for best efficiency).
> 5. **Omitting the figure of merit**: When asked about "best performance," always introduce $Z = \alpha^{2}/(RK)$ explicitly.

---

## ✅ TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> **Important Things to Remember (Quick-Fire Revision)**

- 🔹 **Thermoelectric cooling** = solid-state refrigeration using the **Peltier effect** — no refrigerant, no compressor.
- 🔹 **Peltier Effect**: $q = \alpha T I$ — heat absorbed/released at junction when current flows.
- 🔹 **Seebeck Effect**: $V = \alpha \Delta T$ — reverse principle (used in thermocouples).
- 🔹 **Net cooling at cold side**: $Q_c = \alpha T_c I - \tfrac{1}{2}I^{2}R - K(T_h - T_c)$.
- 🔹 **Power input**: $W = \alpha(T_h - T_c)I + I^{2}R$.
- 🔹 **Heat rejected at hot side**: $Q_h = \alpha T_h I + \tfrac{1}{2}I^{2}R - K(T_h - T_c)$.
- 🔹 **COP**: $COP = Q_c / W$ — always less than Carnot.
- 🔹 **Figure of merit**: $Z = \alpha^{2}/(RK)$ — higher Z = better performance.
- 🔹 **$COP_{\max}$** depends on $Z\bar{T}$; as $Z\bar{T} \to \infty$, COP → Carnot.
- 🔹 **$I_{Q,\max} = \alpha T_c / R$** — current for maximum cooling capacity.
- 🔹 **Best material**: Bi₂Te₃ doped with Sb (p-type) and Se (n-type).
- 🔹 **Applications**: CPU coolers, portable vaccine boxes, laser diode stabilization, spacecraft instruments.
- 🔹 **Advantages**: No moving parts, silent, compact, precise temperature control, long life.
- 🔹 **Disadvantages**: Low COP (~0.5–1.0), expensive, limited $\Delta T$ per stage (~60–70 °C).
- 🔹 **Unit discipline**: α in **V/K**, T in **Kelvin**, I in **Amps** — final answers in **Watts**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adiabatic Demagnetization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Adiabatic Demagnetization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Adiabatic Demagnetization Refrigeration (ADR)** is a cryogenic cooling technique that exploits the **magnetocaloric effect** in paramagnetic salts to attain temperatures approaching **absolute zero (0 K)**. The system uses a two-stage thermodynamic cycle: **(i)** *isothermal magnetization* of a paramagnetic material inside a strong magnetic field while rejecting heat to a thermal bath, followed by **(ii)** *adiabatic demagnetization* — thermally isolating the salt and switching off the field — causing the lattice (vibrational) temperature to plummet because total entropy must remain conserved.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This topic appears under *Module 1 – Miscellaneous / Special Refrigeration Systems* as one of the unconventional methods used to obtain sub-Kelvin temperatures, alongside cascade refrigeration, thermoelectric cooling, and vortex tubes.

## 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| Curie Constant | $C$ | Material-specific constant relating magnetization to $B/T$ |
| Magnetic Field | $B$ | Applied field strength (Tesla) |
| Magnetic Entropy | $S_M$ | Disorder of magnetic dipoles |
| Lattice Entropy | $S_L$ | Vibrational disorder of the crystal lattice |
| Initial Temperature | $T_i$ | Starting bath temperature (typically **1–4 K**) |
| Final Temperature | $T_f$ | Sub-Kelvin temperature achieved after demagnetization |
| Magnetic Moment | $\mu$ | Dipole strength of each atom/ion |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Spinning Coins on a Frictionless Table"

Imagine a hundred spinning coins scattered randomly on a perfectly frictionless table — heads and tails pointing every which way. This is **disorder** (high entropy). Now sweep a magnet (your hand) over them: all coins align in one direction — **order** (low entropy). Because energy was injected by the magnet, the coins vibrate faster — the table warms up.

- **Isothermal magnetization** = you sweep the magnet while a fan (the bath) blows the heat away, leaving the coins perfectly aligned at the original temperature.
- **Adiabatic demagnetization** = you yank the magnet away instantly. To keep total "disorder" (entropy) constant, the coins must now spin chaotically again — but since no fan is blowing and the surrounding is vacuum-insulated, the spinning energy is stolen from the table itself. **The table cools dramatically.**

> [!NOTE]
> This is exactly how ADR works at the atomic scale with paramagnetic ions like $\text{Ce}^{3+}$, $\text{Gd}^{3+}$, or $\text{Pr}^{3+}$ whose electron spins behave like tiny magnetic dipoles.

## 1.4 Working Substances Used

| Material | Curie Constant $C$ | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Cerium Magnesium Nitrate (CMN) | Low | First used; reaches ~**2 mK** |
| Gadolinium Gallium Garnet (GGG) | Moderate | Used in modern space cryocoolers |
| Gadolinium Sulphate Octahydrate | Moderate | Common lab ADR salt |
| Dysprosium Compounds | High | Used in millikelvin–microkelvin regimes |
| Ferromagnetic materials (near $T_C$) | Very High | Used in *room-temperature* magnetocaloric prototypes |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Magnetocaloric Effect — The Heart of ADR

When a paramagnetic material is placed in an external magnetic field $B$, the magnetic dipoles (electron spins) tend to align with the field. This alignment **reduces magnetic entropy** $S_M$. Since total entropy is the sum of lattice and magnetic entropies:

$$S_{total} = S_{L} + S_{M}$$

when $S_M$ drops, $S_L$ must rise if the process is adiabatic — meaning the material heats up. Conversely, when the field is removed adiabatically, $S_M$ rises and $S_L$ drops, producing refrigeration.

> [!NOTE]
> The **magnetocaloric coefficient** quantifies this: $\left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial B}\right)_S = -\dfrac{T}{C_B}\left(\dfrac{\partial M}{\partial T}\right)_B$, where $C_B$ is the heat capacity at constant field.

## 2.2 The Two Operational Stages of an ADR Cycle

### **Stage A — Isothermal Magnetization** (Heat-Rejection Stage)

1. The paramagnetic salt is placed inside a solenoid generating field $B_i$ (typically **1–2 Tesla**).
2. The salt is in **thermal contact** with a liquid-helium bath at temperature $T_i$ (typically **4.2 K** or **1.5 K** under reduced vapor pressure).
3. As $B$ ramps up, magnetic dipoles align and $S_M$ decreases.
4. The lattice entropy $S_L$ increases — heat $Q = T \cdot \Delta S_M$ is dumped into the helium bath.
5. The salt remains at $T_i$; the system is now "magnetized at constant temperature."

### **Stage B — Adiabatic Demagnetization** (Cooling Stage)

1. The salt is **thermally isolated** from the bath (a mechanical heat switch or a gas-filled chamber is evacuated).
2. The magnetic field is **slowly reduced** to $B_f$ (often near zero).
3. As $B$ decreases, dipoles disorder; $S_M$ tries to rise.
4. **Because the process is adiabatic**, $dS_{total} = 0$. Hence $S_L$ must fall.
5. With $S_L$ falling, the **lattice temperature $T$ drops dramatically** from $T_i$ down to $T_f$ — often by **two or more orders of magnitude**.

## 2.3 Why Paramagnets? — Curie's Law

Paramagnetic salts obey **Curie's Law** at temperatures well above any ordering transition:

$$M = \frac{C \cdot B}{T} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \chi = \frac{M}{H} = \frac{C}{T}$$

where $\chi$ is the **magnetic susceptibility** and $C$ is the **Curie constant**. As $T \to 0$, $\chi \to \infty$, meaning a tiny field can magnetize the salt enormously — the perfect requirement for ADR.

## 2.4 Total Entropy as a Function of B and T

The total entropy $S$ of the paramagnetic salt at low temperatures can be expressed as:

$$S(B, T) = S_{L}(T) + S_{M}(B, T)$$

For an **ideal paramagnet in the low-field, low-temperature limit** ($x = \mu B / k_B T \ll 1$):

$$S_{M}(B, T) \approx N k_B \ln 2 - \frac{C B^{2}}{2 T^{2}}$$

The first term is the saturation disorder (all spins random); the second term is the field-induced order. The **lattice entropy** at low $T$ follows Debye's $T^{3}$ law:

$$S_{L}(T) = \frac{12 \pi^{4}}{5}\, N k_B \left(\frac{T}{\Theta_D}\right)^{3} = a T^{3}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** At very low temperatures (e.g., $T < 1$ K), the magnetic entropy contribution dominates over the lattice term because $S_L \propto T^{3}$ collapses rapidly while $S_M$ remains significant. This is precisely the regime where ADR becomes thermodynamically powerful.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S_{total} = S_{L} + S_{M}$ | Total entropy decomposition |
| 2 | $dS_{total} = 0$ | Adiabatic condition (Stage B) |
| 3 | $T_f = T_i \cdot (B_f / B_i)$ | Final temperature relation (ideal case) |
| 4 | $\chi = C / T$ | Curie's Law (paramagnetic susceptibility) |
| 5 | $S_{M} \approx N k_B \ln 2 - C B^{2} / (2 T^{2})$ | Low-field magnetic entropy |
| 6 | $S_{L} = a T^{3}$ | Debye lattice entropy at low $T$ |
| 7 | $C_{V} \propto T^{3}$ | Debye lattice specific heat |
| 8 | $\mu = g \sqrt{J(J+1)} \, \mu_B$ | Effective magnetic moment |
| 9 | $\left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial B}\right)_S = -\dfrac{T}{C_B}\left(\dfrac{\partial M}{\partial T}\right)_B$ | Magnetocaloric coefficient |
| 10 | $Q_{removed} = T \cdot \Delta S_{M}$ | Heat dumped to bath during Stage A |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoided:** Do **NOT** confuse $\chi = C/T$ (paramagnet — valid at all $T$ above ordering) with the **Curie–Weiss law** $\chi = C / (T - T_C)$ used for ferromagnets near their Curie temperature $T_C$.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Space Science** | NASA's **ADR** on the *Chandra X-ray Observatory* and the *Hitomi* satellite — cools X-ray detectors to **60 mK** |
| **Quantum Computing** | Pre-cooling stage for dilution refrigerators housing superconducting qubits |
| **Fundamental Physics** | Reaching microkelvin/nanokelvin for studying superfluid He-3, superconductivity |
| **Medical Imaging** | Sub-K pre-cooling of MRI magnet pick-up loops |
| **Materials Research** | Study of quantum phase transitions, nuclear demagnetization seed stage |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Master ADR Relation: $T_f / T_i = B_f / B_i$

We derive this result from first principles, treating the paramagnetic salt as a system of $N$ non-interacting spin-1/2 dipoles.

### Step 1 — Energy Levels of a Single Spin in a Field $B$

Each magnetic dipole of moment $\mu$ in a field $B$ has two energy eigenstates:

$$E_{\uparrow} = -\mu B, \qquad E_{\downarrow} = +\mu B$$

### Step 2 — Single-Spin Partition Function

The canonical partition function for a single spin is:

$$Z_1 = e^{\mu B / k_B T} + e^{-\mu B / k_B T} = 2 \cosh\!\left(\frac{\mu B}{k_B T}\right)$$

Let $x \equiv \dfrac{\mu B}{k_B T}$ (a dimensionless field parameter). Then:

$$Z_1 = 2 \cosh(x)$$

### Step 3 — Helmholtz Free Energy per Mole

For $N$ independent spins:

$$F = -N k_B T \ln Z_1 = -N k_B T \ln\!\left[2 \cosh(x)\right]$$

### Step 4 — Magnetic Entropy

Using $S_M = -\partial F / \partial T$ at constant $B$:

$$S_M = N k_B \!\left[\ln\!\left(2 \cosh(x)\right) - x \tanh(x)\right]$$

### Step 5 — Low-Field / High-Temperature Expansion ($x \ll 1$)

Using the Taylor series $\cosh(x) \approx 1 + x^{2}/2$ and $\tanh(x) \approx x - x^{3}/3$, and $\ln(1+x^{2}/2) \approx x^{2}/2$:

$$\ln\!\left(2 \cosh(x)\right) \approx \ln 2 + \frac{x^{2}}{2}$$

$$x \tanh(x) \approx x^{2} - \frac{x^{4}}{3} \approx x^{2} \quad \text{(to leading order)}$$

Substituting back:

$$S_M \approx N k_B \!\left[\ln 2 + \frac{x^{2}}{2} - x^{2}\right] = N k_B \ln 2 - N k_B \frac{x^{2}}{2}$$

Now substitute $x = \mu B / (k_B T)$:

$$S_M \approx N k_B \ln 2 - \frac{N \mu^{2} B^{2}}{2 k_B T^{2}}$$

Defining the **Curie constant** as $C \equiv N \mu^{2} / (2 k_B)$:

$$\boxed{\,S_M(B,T) \approx N k_B \ln 2 - \frac{C B^{2}}{T^{2}}\,}$$

### Step 6 — Apply the Adiabatic Constraint

The adiabatic demagnetization process demands $S_{total} = \text{constant}$. If we work in the regime where the **lattice contribution $S_L$ is negligible** compared to $S_M$ (valid at sub-Kelvin temperatures before any warming), then $S_M$ alone must be conserved:

$$S_M(B_f, T_f) = S_M(B_i, T_i)$$

$$N k_B \ln 2 - \frac{C B_f^{2}}{T_f^{2}} = N k_B \ln 2 - \frac{C B_i^{2}}{T_i^{2}}$$

The $N k_B \ln 2$ terms cancel:

$$\frac{B_f^{2}}{T_f^{2}} = \frac{B_i^{2}}{T_i^{2}}$$

Taking the square root of both sides:

$$\boxed{\,\frac{T_f}{T_i} = \frac{B_f}{B_i}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result Significance:** This single equation tells us the *entire engineering promise* of ADR. Halving the field halves the temperature. Going from 1 Tesla to 0.001 Tesla reduces temperature by a factor of **1000**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Numerical Estimate of Final Temperature

**Given:**
- Initial bath temperature: $T_i = 4.2$ K (liquid helium at 1 atm)
- Initial magnetic field: $B_i = 1.0$ T
- Final magnetic field: $B_f = 0.01$ T (after demagnetization)

**Find:** Final temperature $T_f$

**Solution:**

$$T_f = T_i \cdot \frac{B_f}{B_i} = 4.2 \times \frac{0.01}{1.0}$$

$$T_f = 4.2 \times 0.01 = 0.042 \text{ K} = 42 \text{ mK}$$

**Conversion check:** $0.042 \text{ K} = 42 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K} = 42 \text{ mK}$. ✓

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** This is the *ideal* theoretical limit. Practical ADRs achieve $T_f \approx 20$–$100$ mK because (a) lattice entropy is not truly negligible, (b) non-ideal spin–spin interactions occur, and (c) thermal contact resistance reduces efficiency.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Heat Rejected During Isothermal Magnetization

During Stage A, the lattice entropy rises by exactly the amount by which magnetic entropy falls:

$$\Delta S_L = -\Delta S_M = \frac{C B_i^{2}}{T_i^{2}} - 0 = \frac{C B_i^{2}}{T_i^{2}}$$

The heat rejected to the bath at constant $T_i$:

$$Q_{rejected} = T_i \cdot \Delta S_L = T_i \cdot \frac{C B_i^{2}}{T_i^{2}} = \frac{C B_i^{2}}{T_i}$$

For a single mole of CMN ($C \approx 0.011$ K·emu/mol, but treating it in SI-style Curie constant), plugging in $B_i = 1$ T, $T_i = 4.2$ K:

$$Q_{rejected} = \frac{C \cdot (1)^{2}}{4.2} = \frac{C}{4.2}$$

This is a **very small** quantity, justifying why only tiny salt pills are needed in laboratory ADRs.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Regime Comparison

| Regime | $T$ Range | Dominant Entropy | Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| **High-T paramagnetic** | $T \gg \Theta_D$, $T \gg \mu B / k_B$ | $S_L \gg S_M$ | ADR ineffective; use conventional cryocoolers |
| **Classical ADR** | $1 \text{ K} > T > 0.05 \text{ K}$ | $S_M$ dominates | $T_f = T_i (B_f / B_i)$ valid |
| **Nuclear ADR (Spin-1/2 nuclei)** | $T < 0.01$ K | $S_{nuclear}$ dominates | Pushing into $\mu$K regime |
| **Dilution refrigerator regime** | $T < 0.3$ K | He-3/He-4 phase entropy | Continuous cooling, complements ADR |

## 3.5 Derivations — Stage-by-Stage Entropy Tracking (Tabular Form)

| Stage | Process | $S_L$ | $S_M$ | $S_{total}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Initial (no field)** | Salt at $T_i$, $B = 0$ | Moderate ($aT_i^{3}$) | Maximum ($Nk_B \ln 2$) | High |
| **After Isothermal Magnetization** | $B = B_i$, $T = T_i$ | Higher (heat dumped) | Lower (dipoles aligned) | **Same** (heat removed) |
| **After Adiabatic Demagnetization** | $B = B_f$, $T = T_f \ll T_i$ | **Very low** ($T^{3}$ collapsed) | Higher (dipoles disorder) | **Same** (adiabatic) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an ADR System

The physical apparatus is intricate (cryostat, superconducting magnet, vacuum jackets, heat switches), but the functional flow of energy and entropy can be represented as follows:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Paramagnetic Salt Pill] --> B[Superconducting Magnet Solenoid]
    B --> C{B-Field Control}
    C --> D[Isothermal Magnetization Stage]
    C --> E[Adiabatic Demagnetization Stage]
    D --> F[Liquid Helium Bath at 4.2 K]
    F --> G[Heat Rejection Q out]
    E --> H[Thermal Isolation Vacuum]
    H --> I[Cold Finger / Load to be Cooled]
    A --> J[Curie Law M = CB slash T]
    A --> K[Magnetic Entropy S_M]
    A --> L[Lattice Entropy S_L]
    K --> M[Total Entropy S_total constant]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Final Temperature T_f equals T_i times B_f over B_i]
    I --> O[Reaches Sub-Kelvin T]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — Two-Stage ADR Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1 Salt at Ti Bi greater than 0 Thermal contact ON] --> P2[Step 2 Apply field Bi isothermally Dipoles align S_M drops]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 Heat Q rejected to Helium bath Salt remains at Ti]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 Thermal contact OFF Vacuum isolation Adiabatic]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5 Field reduced from Bi to Bf Dipoles disorder S_M rises]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6 S_L must fall to conserve S_total Temperature drops to Tf]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7 Sub-Kelvin load cooling achieved]
```

## 4.3 Entropy–Temperature (S–T) Schematic Mapping

Although rendering a precise S–T diagram requires numerical Brillouin-function calculations, the topology is captured below:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph High_Field_Bi
        HF1[S_M High Field Curve at Bi]
    end
    subgraph Low_Field_Bf
        LF1[S_M Low Field Curve at Bf near 0]
    end
    HF1 --> X[Point A at Ti Bi isothermal magnetization]
    X --> Y[Point B at Tf Bf adiabatic demagnetization]
    LF1 --> Y
    Y --> Z[Temperature drops along constant S_total line]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the S–T Diagram:** In the real S–T plane, the curve at $B = B_i$ lies **below** the curve at $B = 0$ (because alignment reduces $S_M$). Starting at $T_i$, $B_i$ (Point A) and moving along the curve $B = B_i$ to the same temperature, then tracking horizontally to the $B = 0$ curve gives the final temperature $T_f$.

## 4.4 Subsystem Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Magnet Power Supply] --> M2[Superconducting Coil]
    M2 --> M3[Paramagnetic Salt Pill]
    M3 --> M4[Thermal Switch]
    M4 --> M5[Helium Bath 4.2 K]
    M4 --> M6[Vacuum Isolated Space]
    M6 --> M7[Cold Load X-ray Detector or Qubit]
    M3 --> M8[Temperature Sensor calibrated RuO2 or Cernox]
    M8 --> M9[Control Electronics]
    M9 --> M1
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Define the magnetocaloric effect. How is it exploited in adiabatic demagnetization refrigeration?**

**Model Answer:**

The **magnetocaloric effect** is the phenomenon in which a magnetic material undergoes a reversible temperature change when subjected to a changing external magnetic field under adiabatic (or isothermal) conditions. It arises because the magnetic dipoles store/释放 magnetic entropy depending on their degree of alignment with the field.

In **Adiabatic Demagnetization Refrigeration (ADR)**, this effect is exploited in two stages:
1. **Isothermal magnetization** — the salt is magnetized at temperature $T_i$ while rejecting heat $Q = T_i \Delta S_M$ to a helium bath, lowering the magnetic entropy.
2. **Adiabatic demagnetization** — the field is reduced under thermal isolation; magnetic entropy rises, lattice entropy must fall to keep $S_{total}$ constant, producing a temperature drop to $T_f = T_i \cdot (B_f / B_i)$.

(3 marks)

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**State and explain Curie's Law as applied to paramagnetic salts used in ADR.**

**Model Answer:**

**Curie's Law** states that for a paramagnetic material well above any magnetic ordering temperature, the **magnetic susceptibility** $\chi$ is inversely proportional to the absolute temperature $T$:

$$\chi = \frac{M}{H} = \frac{C}{T}$$

where $C$ is the **Curie constant**, $M$ is the magnetization, and $H$ is the magnetic field strength.

**Significance for ADR:**
- As $T \to 0$, $\chi \to \infty$, allowing even a small field to produce strong magnetization.
- The magnetic entropy becomes $S_M \approx N k_B \ln 2 - C B^{2} / T^{2}$, making ADR increasingly effective at lower temperatures.
- Paramagnetic salts such as Cerium Magnesium Nitrate (CMN) and Gadolinium Sulphate obey Curie's Law down to millikelvin temperatures, justifying their use as ADR working substances.

(3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **forget to state the adiabatic condition** $dS = 0$ explicitly before deriving $T_f = T_i (B_f / B_i)$. This alone costs **2 marks**.
> - Many confuse **Curie's Law** ($\chi = C/T$, valid for paramagnets) with the **Curie–Weiss Law** ($\chi = C/(T-T_C)$, valid near ferromagnetic transition). Read the question wording carefully.
> - Always **carry units** through your derivation — examiners deduct **1 mark** for bare numerical answers without units like "K" or "T".
> - The condition "**ideal paramagnet, low-field limit, lattice entropy negligible**" must be stated to validate the $T_f/B_f = T_i/B_i$ formula. Missing this caveat costs **2 marks**.
> - For numerical problems, write the **given data, the formula, the substitution, and the boxed answer**. Skipping substitution steps loses **partial marks** even if the final number is correct.

---

### **Question B (A)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Derive the relation $T_f = T_i (B_f / B_i)$ for an ideal paramagnetic salt undergoing adiabatic demagnetization, clearly stating all assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A paramagnetic salt with Curie constant $C = 0.020 \text{ K}$ is magnetized isothermally at $T_i = 4.2$ K in a field $B_i = 1.5$ T and then adiabatically demagnetized to $B_f = 0.05$ T. Calculate: (i) the final temperature $T_f$, and (ii) the heat rejected to the helium bath per mole during isothermal magnetization. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — State the assumptions clearly:**
- The salt is an **ideal paramagnet** (non-interacting dipoles).
- Working in the **low-field, low-temperature limit** so that $\mu B / k_B T \ll 1$.
- **Lattice entropy $S_L$ is negligible** compared to $S_M$ (valid at sub-Kelvin temperatures).
- Process is **reversible and adiabatic** in Stage B.

**Step 2 — Single-spin partition function:**

$$Z_1 = 2 \cosh\!\left(\frac{\mu B}{k_B T}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Total magnetic entropy for $N$ spins:**

$$S_M = N k_B \!\left[\ln\!\left(2 \cosh(x)\right) - x \tanh(x)\right], \quad x = \frac{\mu B}{k_B T}$$

**Step 4 — Taylor expansion for $x \ll 1$:**

$$S_M \approx N k_B \ln 2 - \frac{N \mu^{2} B^{2}}{2 k_B T^{2}} = N k_B \ln 2 - \frac{C B^{2}}{T^{2}}$$

where $C \equiv N\mu^{2} / (2 k_B)$ is the Curie constant.

**Step 5 — Apply adiabatic condition $S_M = \text{constant}$:**

$$N k_B \ln 2 - \frac{C B_f^{2}}{T_f^{2}} = N k_B \ln 2 - \frac{C B_i^{2}}{T_i^{2}}$$

$$\frac{B_f^{2}}{T_f^{2}} = \frac{B_i^{2}}{T_i^{2}}$$

**Step 6 — Take the square root:**

$$\boxed{\frac{T_f}{T_i} = \frac{B_f}{B_i}} \qquad \text{(QED)}$$

**(7 marks: 1 for assumptions, 2 for partition function and entropy, 2 for Taylor expansion, 2 for final derivation)**

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- $C = 0.020 \text{ K}$
- $T_i = 4.2 \text{ K}$
- $B_i = 1.5 \text{ T}$
- $B_f = 0.05 \text{ T}$

**Part (i) — Final Temperature:**

$$T_f = T_i \cdot \frac{B_f}{B_i} = 4.2 \times \frac{0.05}{1.5}$$

$$T_f = 4.2 \times 0.0333\ldots = 0.140 \text{ K} = 140 \text{ mK}$$

$$\boxed{T_f \approx 140 \text{ mK}}$$

**Part (ii) — Heat Rejected During Isothermal Magnetization:**

The heat rejected equals $T_i \cdot \Delta S_M$:

$$\Delta S_M = \frac{C B_i^{2}}{T_i^{2}} = \frac{0.020 \times (1.5)^{2}}{(4.2)^{2}} = \frac{0.020 \times 2.25}{17.64}$$

$$\Delta S_M = \frac{0.045}{17.64} = 2.55 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J/K per mole}$$

Heat rejected:

$$Q_{rejected} = T_i \cdot \Delta S_M = 4.2 \times 2.55 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{rejected} \approx 1.07 \times 10^{-2} \text{ J} = 10.7 \text{ mJ per mole}}$$

**(7 marks: 2 for part (i) formula and substitution, 3 for part (ii) formula, 2 for arithmetic and units)**

---

### **Question B (B)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the working principle of an adiabatic demagnetization refrigeration system. Discuss the role of (i) the paramagnetic salt, (ii) the superconducting magnet, and (iii) the thermal isolation switch. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ADR system operates between an initial bath at $1.5$ K and uses a field swing from $B_i = 2.0$ T down to $B_f = 0.02$ T. Calculate the final temperature. If the paramagnetic salt has Curie constant $C = 0.015$ K, estimate the cooling capacity (heat absorbed from the load) per mole when warmed back from $T_f$ to $T_i$ at zero field. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

The ADR system consists of three primary components:

**(i) Paramagnetic Salt Pill:** Acts as the **working substance**. It must satisfy Curie's Law $\chi = C/T$ over the operating range, possess low lattice entropy (i.e., high Debye temperature), and exhibit minimal spin–spin interactions. Common choices: CMN, GGG, Gd₂(SO₄)₃·8H₂O.

**(ii) Superconducting Magnet:** Generates the high field $B_i$ (typically 1–7 T) needed to align dipoles. Superconducting operation ensures **zero ohmic dissipation** and sustained high current. The magnet must be ramped slowly to avoid eddy-current heating.

**(iii) Thermal Isolation Switch (Heat Switch):** A controlled thermal link — typically a gas-filled chamber that, when filled with He exchange gas, connects the salt to the bath (Stage A), and when evacuated, isolates it for adiabatic operation (Stage B).

**Working Principle (Sketch Description):**

```
       [Superconducting Coil]
              |
        [Salt Pill] ===(heat switch)==> [Helium Bath at 1.5 K]
              |
         [Cold Load]
```

1. Salt + bath in thermal contact → apply field → dipoles align → heat dumped to bath.
2. Open heat switch → evacuate chamber → isolate salt.
3. Reduce field → dipoles disorder → lattice cools → cold finger absorbs heat from load.

**(7 marks: 2 for sketch description, 3 for role of three components, 2 for working principle)**

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- $T_i = 1.5$ K
- $B_i = 2.0$ T
- $B_f = 0.02$ T
- $C = 0.015$ K

**Part (i) — Final Temperature:**

$$T_f = T_i \cdot \frac{B_f}{B_i} = 1.5 \times \frac{0.02}{2.0}$$

$$T_f = 1.5 \times 0.01 = 0.015 \text{ K} = 15 \text{ mK}$$

$$\boxed{T_f = 15 \text{ mK}}$$

**Part (ii) — Cooling Capacity (Heat Absorbed When Warming from $T_f$ to $T_i$ at $B = 0$):**

At zero field, the magnetic entropy per mole is:

$$S_M(B=0, T) = N k_B \ln 2 \quad \text{(essentially temperature-independent for ideal paramagnet)}$$

However, the **lattice entropy** changes from $S_L(T_f) = a T_f^{3}$ to $S_L(T_i) = a T_i^{3}$, and this increase equals the cooling capacity absorbed from the load.

But the question asks for the cooling at zero field — at zero field the magnetic contribution is constant, so we focus on the **lattice term**. The heat absorbed to warm the salt back to $T_i$ is approximately the integral of lattice specific heat $C_L = \beta T^{3}$ from $T_f$ to $T_i$:

$$Q_{cooling} = \int_{T_f}^{T_i} C_L \, dT = \int_{T_f}^{T_i} \beta T^{3} \, dT = \frac{\beta}{4} \left[T_i^{4} - T_f^{4}\right]$$

However, using the **magnetic-entropy-driven cooling approximation** as a direct estimate:

$$Q_{cooling} \approx T_f \cdot \Delta S_M \approx T_f \cdot \frac{C B_i^{2}}{T_i^{2}}$$

Plugging values:

$$Q_{cooling} \approx 0.015 \times \frac{0.015 \times (2.0)^{2}}{(1.5)^{2}}$$

$$Q_{cooling} \approx 0.015 \times \frac{0.015 \times 4}{2.25} = 0.015 \times \frac{0.060}{2.25}$$

$$Q_{cooling} \approx 0.015 \times 0.0267 \approx 4.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J per mole}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{cooling} \approx 0.40 \text{ mJ per mole}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **first-order estimate** assuming ideal paramagnet behavior. Real ADR cooling capacity is somewhat lower due to non-idealities, but the order of magnitude (sub-millijoule per mole) is accurate for $C \sim 0.015$ K and field swings of order Tesla.

**(7 marks: 2 for part (i) formula and arithmetic, 2 for setting up part (ii), 3 for evaluation and final boxed answer with units)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **ADR = Adiabatic Demagnetization Refrigeration** — a cryogenic technique exploiting the **magnetocaloric effect** to reach sub-Kelvin temperatures.
- **Two stages:** (1) Isothermal magnetization at $T_i$ in field $B_i$, with heat rejection to bath. (2) Adiabatic demagnetization from $B_i$ to $B_f$ under thermal isolation, producing cooling.
- **Master Equation:** $T_f = T_i \cdot (B_f / B_i)$ — valid for an ideal paramagnet in the low-field, low-temperature limit where **lattice entropy is negligible**.
- **Curie's Law:** $\chi = C / T$ — fundamental governing paramagnet behavior; allows strong magnetization as $T \to 0$.
- **Magnetic Entropy (low-field limit):** $S_M \approx N k_B \ln 2 - (C B^{2}) / T^{2}$.
- **Lattice Entropy (Debye, low $T$):** $S_L \approx a T^{3}$.
- **Total Entropy:** $S_{total} = S_L + S_M$ — conserved in the adiabatic stage.
- **Working Substances:** CMN, GGG, Gd₂(SO₄)₃·8H₂O, dysprosium compounds; chosen for high Curie constant and low lattice heat capacity.
- **Engineering Components:** Superconducting magnet (field source), thermal switch (gas-filled chamber), paramagnetic salt pill, helium bath, vacuum isolation jacket, calibrated low-$T$ thermometer (RuO₂ or Cernox).
- **Refrigeration Capacity:** Sub-millijoule per mole per cycle — limited but sufficient for ultra-low-$T$ experiments.
- **Real-World Use:** Space cryocoolers (NASA Chandra, Hitomi), quantum computing pre-cooling, sub-K physics research.
- **KTU Pitfall:** Don't confuse **Curie's Law** (paramagnet) with **Curie–Weiss Law** (ferromagnet near $T_C$); always state assumptions before using the master equation.
- **Practical limitation:** Cannot operate continuously — it is a **single-shot** process. The salt must be re-magnetized with the bath contact restored.
- **Comparison:** ADR is generally capable of reaching lower temperatures than dilution refrigerators in a single-shot mode but is **not continuous**; dilution refrigerators are continuous but have a lower minimum temperature in practice.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Tip:** Memorize (a) the master equation $T_f = T_i (B_f / B_i)$, (b) the magnetic-entropy formula $S_M \approx N k_B \ln 2 - C B^{2}/T^{2}$, (c) Curie's Law $\chi = C/T$, and (d) the two-stage process description. These four items alone secure **80% of marks** in any ADR question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 1
