<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Engine:** A heat engine is a thermodynamic device that continuously converts heat energy supplied from a high-temperature source (hot reservoir) into mechanical work (shaft work or useful work), while rejecting the remaining unconverted portion of heat to a low-temperature sink (cold reservoir), operating in a cyclic manner.

Mathematically, the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to a cyclic heat engine gives the energy balance:

$$Q_H = W_{net} + Q_L$$

Where:
- $Q_H$ = Heat supplied from the high-temperature source (kJ)
- $W_{net}$ = Net work output (kJ)
- $Q_L$ = Heat rejected to the low-temperature sink (kJ)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Note:** Since the working fluid returns to its initial state after one complete cycle, the change in internal energy $\Delta U = 0$. Therefore, by the First Law, all the net heat transfer equals the net work done — this is the foundational principle for every cycle-based derivation you will encounter in RAC.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **water wheel on a flowing river**:
- The **river at the top (high level)** = Hot Reservoir (provides energy, $Q_H$)
- The **river at the bottom (low level)** = Cold Reservoir (where water finally goes, $Q_L$)
- The **rotating wheel** = Heat Engine (converts potential energy of falling water into rotational work)
- The **difference in water levels** = Temperature difference ($T_H - T_L$)

Just as the water wheel **cannot extract 100% of the water's energy** because some water must flow downstream, a heat engine **cannot convert 100% of the heat input into work** — a portion must always be rejected to the cold reservoir. This is dictated by the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard SI Units & Constants:**
> - Heat ($Q$): **kJ** or **Joules (J)**
> - Work ($W$): **kJ** or **Joules (J)**
> - Temperature ($T$): **Kelvin (K)** — *Always use absolute scale in thermodynamic efficiency equations*
> - Thermal Efficiency ($\eta_{th}$): **Dimensionless** (0 to 1) or expressed as **percentage (%)**

## 1.3 Classification of Heat Engines

Heat engines are broadly classified into two families based on **where combustion occurs**:

| Type | Combustion Location | Examples | Working Fluid |
|------|---------------------|----------|---------------|
| **External Combustion Engine (ECE)** | Outside the engine | Steam Engine, Stirling Engine | Steam, Air |
| **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** | Inside the engine cylinder | Petrol Engine, Diesel Engine, Gas Turbine | Combustion Gases |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How a Heat Engine Works

A heat engine operates on a **cyclic thermodynamic process**. Let us break down the operational logic step-by-step:

- **Step 1 — Heat Absorption:** The working fluid receives heat $Q_H$ from a high-temperature source at $T_H$.
- **Step 2 — Expansion / Work Production:** The fluid expands (gas phase or phase-change expansion), producing useful mechanical work $W$.
- **Step 3 — Heat Rejection:** The fluid releases the remaining unused heat $Q_L$ to a low-temperature sink at $T_L$.
- **Step 4 — Compression / Reset:** The fluid is compressed back to its original state, ready for the next cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the cycle crucial?** Because the working fluid must return to its original thermodynamic state (pressure, temperature, volume, internal energy) after each cycle — this ensures *continuous* operation, unlike a one-time nozzle or throttle process.

## 2.2 Thermal Efficiency — The Performance Metric

The single most important metric for any heat engine is its **Thermal Efficiency** $\eta_{th}$, defined as the ratio of useful work output to heat input:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = \frac{Q_H - Q_L}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H}$$

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Parameter / Formula | Mathematical Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---------------------|-------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | First Law (Cycle) | $Q_H - Q_L = W_{net}$ | Energy balance, $\Delta U = 0$ |
| 2 | Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H}$ | Always $\eta_{th} < 1$ |
| 3 | Carnot Efficiency (Max possible) | $\eta_{carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Temperatures in **Kelvin** |
| 4 | Carnot Heat Engine Work | $W_{carnot} = Q_H \left(1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}\right)$ | Reversible ideal engine |
| 5 | Heat Rejected (Carnot) | $Q_L = Q_H \cdot \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | For reversible cycles only |
| 6 | COP of Heat Engine | Not defined (COP is for Refrigerators/HP) | Heat engines use $\eta$ |
| 7 | PMM-2 Relation | $\eta_{th} \leq \eta_{carnot}$ | Kelvin-Planck statement |

## 2.3 The Carnot Heat Engine — The Ideal Benchmark

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** The **Carnot Cycle** is the most frequently asked topic in Module 1. It is a *theoretical reversible cycle* operating between two thermal reservoirs, consisting of two isothermal and two adiabatic (isentropic) processes.

### The Four Carnot Processes:

1. **Process 1→2: Isothermal Heat Addition** at $T_H$ — Heat $Q_H$ is added, gas expands, work is produced.
2. **Process 2→3: Isentropic (Reversible Adiabatic) Expansion** — Temperature drops from $T_H$ to $T_L$.
3. **Process 3→4: Isothermal Heat Rejection** at $T_L$ — Heat $Q_L$ is rejected, gas is compressed.
4. **Process 4→1: Isentropic Compression** — Temperature rises from $T_L$ back to $T_H$.

### Real-World Engineering Utility:

Heat engine theory underpins the design and analysis of:
- **Power Plants** (Steam Rankine Cycle, Brayton Cycle for Gas Turbines)
- **Internal Combustion Engines** (Otto Cycle for petrol, Diesel Cycle for diesel)
- **Jet Propulsion Systems** (aircraft engines, rocket nozzles)
- **Combined Cycle Plants** (maximizing efficiency by stacking cycles)

## 2.4 Second Law Statements Applicable to Heat Engines

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kelvin-Planck Statement:** *"It is impossible to construct a device operating in a cycle that produces no other effect than the absorption of heat from a single reservoir and the performance of an equivalent amount of work."*

This means **100% conversion of heat to work in a cycle is impossible** — some heat $Q_L$ must be rejected. This is why $\eta_{th} < 1$ always.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Expression for Thermal Efficiency from First Law

We will derive the thermal efficiency formula from the **First Law of Thermodynamics applied to a cyclic device**.

### Given:
- A heat engine operates in a thermodynamic cycle.
- Heat $Q_H$ is supplied from a hot reservoir at temperature $T_H$.
- Heat $Q_L$ is rejected to a cold reservoir at temperature $T_L$.
- Net work output is $W_{net}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Apply the First Law of Thermodynamics to the cyclic process.

For any cyclic process, the working fluid returns to its initial state, hence the net change in internal energy is zero:

$$\Delta U_{cycle} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Write the First Law energy balance.

The First Law states that net heat transfer into the system equals the net work done by the system plus the change in internal energy:

$$Q_{net} = W_{net} + \Delta U$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $\Delta U = 0$ for a cyclic process.

Since the engine operates in a cycle, the change in internal energy over one complete cycle is zero. Substituting this into the First Law equation:

$$Q_{net} = W_{net} + 0$$

$$Q_{net} = W_{net}$$

**Step 4:** Express the net heat transfer as the algebraic sum of all heat interactions.

Heat $Q_H$ is supplied to the engine (taken as positive input), while heat $Q_L$ is rejected by the engine (taken as negative output or simply subtracted). Therefore, the net heat input to the engine is:

$$Q_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$$

**Step 5:** Equate the two expressions for $Q_{net}$ to obtain the work output.

From Step 3 and Step 4, both expressions equal $W_{net}$. Setting them equal gives the fundamental heat engine energy equation:

$$Q_H - Q_L = W_{net}$$

**Step 6:** Define thermal efficiency as the ratio of desired output to required input.

Thermal efficiency is defined as the ratio of net work output (the useful output) to the total heat supplied from the hot reservoir (the required input). Mathematically:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H}$$

**Step 7:** Substitute the expression for $W_{net}$ from Step 5 into the efficiency definition.

Replacing $W_{net}$ with the energy balance $Q_H - Q_L$:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{Q_H - Q_L}{Q_H}$$

**Step 8:** Simplify the fraction by separating the numerator.

Distributing the denominator to both terms in the numerator:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{Q_H}{Q_H} - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H}$$

**Step 9:** Final simplification of the first term.

Since $\dfrac{Q_H}{Q_H} = 1$, we obtain the final canonical form of thermal efficiency:

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** This is the universal expression for the thermal efficiency of any heat engine operating between two reservoirs. It shows that to maximize $\eta_{th}$, we must minimize the ratio $\dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Carnot (Maximum) Thermal Efficiency

### Given:
- A reversible (Carnot) heat engine operating between two thermal reservoirs.
- Hot reservoir absolute temperature = $T_H$ (Kelvin).
- Cold reservoir absolute temperature = $T_L$ (Kelvin).

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Use the property of the Carnot cycle — it is fully reversible.

For a reversible isothermal process, the heat transfer is related to temperature by the **Clausius Equality**:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Expand the cyclic integral for the Carnot cycle.

The Carnot cycle consists of two isothermal processes (heat addition at $T_H$ and heat rejection at $T_L$) and two adiabatic processes (no heat transfer). Hence the integral becomes:

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} - \frac{Q_L}{T_L} = 0$$

**Step 3:** Isolate the heat terms.

Rearranging the equation to bring $Q_L$ to the right-hand side:

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_L}{T_L}$$

**Step 4:** Express the ratio $\dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H}$.

Cross-multiplying to obtain the ratio:

$$\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute into the thermal efficiency expression derived earlier.

Using $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H}$ and replacing $\dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H}$ with $\dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$:

$$\eta_{carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result:** The Carnot efficiency depends **only on the reservoir temperatures** (in Kelvin). It is the *upper bound* for any real engine operating between the same two temperatures.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table for KTU Exams

| Feature | Heat Engine | Refrigerator | Heat Pump |
|---------|-------------|--------------|-----------|
| **Purpose** | Produce work | Produce cooling | Produce heating |
| **Direction of $W$** | Input (drives cycle from outside) | Input | Input |
| **Direction of $Q_H$** | Output (to sink) | Output | Output |
| **Direction of $Q_L$** | Input (from source) | Input | Input |
| **Performance Metric** | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W}{Q_H}$ | $COP_{ref} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W}$ | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W}$ |
| **Carnot Value** | $\eta_{carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | $COP_{ref,carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | $COP_{HP,carnot} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The refrigerator, heat pump, and heat engine are *the same physical device* running in *different directions*. Examiners frequently ask students to derive the relationship: $COP_{HP} = COP_{ref} + 1$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Generalized Heat Engine

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the functional architecture of a generic heat engine, showing the energy flow paths between the two reservoirs and the engine block.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS1[("HOT RESERVOIR T_H")]
    nodeS2["HEAT ENGINE BLOCK"]
    nodeS3[("COLD RESERVOIR T_L")]
    nodeS4["MECHANICAL WORK OUTPUT W_net"]

    nodeS1 -- "Q_H supplied" --> nodeS2
    nodeS2 -- "Q_L rejected" --> nodeS3
    nodeS2 -- "Shaft work delivered" --> nodeS4

    style nodeS1 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF
    style nodeS3 fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#0E5E5A,color:#FFFFFF
    style nodeS2 fill:#FFE66D,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000000
    style nodeS4 fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#0E5E5A,color:#000000
```

### Interpretation of the Diagram:
- **Red node (Hot Reservoir):** The high-temperature energy source, e.g., a furnace, boiler, or combustion chamber.
- **Yellow node (Engine Block):** The thermodynamic working substance (gas, steam) inside the cylinder/turbine.
- **Teal node (Cold Reservoir):** The low-temperature heat sink, e.g., atmosphere, cooling tower, river.
- **Green node (Work Output):** The useful mechanical energy delivered, e.g., rotating shaft of a turbine.

---

## 4.2 P-V and T-S Diagram of the Carnot Cycle

The Carnot cycle on a **Pressure-Volume (P-V) diagram** appears as a closed curve with two isotherms (curves) and two isentropes (steeper curves). On a **Temperature-Entropy (T-S) diagram**, it appears as a **perfect rectangle**, which makes it the easiest cycle to analyze.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CarnotTS["T-S Diagram Representation"]
        direction LR
        state1["State 1: T_H, S1"]
        state2["State 2: T_H, S2"]
        state3["State 3: T_L, S2"]
        state4["State 4: T_L, S1"]

        state1 -- "Isothermal heat addition Q_H" --> state2
        state2 -- "Isentropic expansion T drops" --> state3
        state3 -- "Isothermal heat rejection Q_L" --> state4
        state4 -- "Isentropic compression T rises" --> state1
    end

    style state1 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF
    style state2 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF
    style state3 fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#0E5E5A,color:#FFFFFF
    style state4 fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#0E5E5A,color:#FFFFFF
```

### Key Visual Insight for Students:

> [!NOTE]
> On the **T-S diagram**, the area enclosed by the rectangular Carnot cycle equals the **net work output** of the engine: $W_{net} = \oint T \, dS = \text{Area of rectangle}$.

---

## 4.3 Energy Flow Architecture — Sankey-Style Logic Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Total Heat Input Q_H from Source"]
    B["Useful Work Output W_net = eta_th times Q_H"]
    C["Wasted Heat Q_L rejected to Sink"]

    A --> B
    A --> C

    style A fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF
    style B fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#0E5E5A,color:#000000
    style C fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#0E5E5A,color:#FFFFFF
```

This representation reinforces the energy conservation principle: **what comes in as heat must leave as either work or waste heat**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Heat Engine. State the Kelvin-Planck statement of the Second Law.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A heat engine is a cyclic device that converts heat energy supplied from a high-temperature source into mechanical work, rejecting the remaining heat to a low-temperature sink.

**Kelvin-Planck Statement:** *"It is impossible to construct a device which operates in a cycle and produces no effect other than extracting heat from a single reservoir and performing an equivalent amount of work."*

In simpler terms, **no heat engine can convert all the heat input into work** — a portion must always be rejected to a cold reservoir.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between a Heat Engine, Refrigerator, and Heat Pump.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Device | Objective | Useful Output | Performance Metric |
|--------|-----------|---------------|--------------------|
| Heat Engine | Produce work | Work $W$ | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W}{Q_H}$ |
| Refrigerator | Produce cooling | Heat extracted from cold space $Q_L$ | $COP_{ref} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W}$ |
| Heat Pump | Produce heating | Heat delivered to hot space $Q_H$ | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W}$ |

The three are thermodynamically the same device, but the *desired output* differs.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: Carnot Heat Engine Numerical and Theory

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q:** A Carnot heat engine operates between a hot reservoir at temperature **600 K** and a cold reservoir at temperature **300 K**. If the engine receives **1000 kJ** of heat from the hot reservoir, calculate:
(i) The thermal efficiency of the engine.
(ii) The net work output per cycle.
(iii) The heat rejected to the cold reservoir.

**Complete Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Hot reservoir temperature, $T_H = 600 \, K$
- Cold reservoir temperature, $T_L = 300 \, K$
- Heat supplied from hot reservoir, $Q_H = 1000 \, kJ$
- Cycle: Reversible Carnot Cycle

---

**(i) Thermal Efficiency of the Engine**

**Step 1:** Apply the Carnot efficiency formula for a reversible heat engine operating between two thermal reservoirs:

$$\eta_{carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the given temperature values into the formula. The cold reservoir temperature $T_L$ is 300 K and the hot reservoir temperature $T_H$ is 600 K:

$$\eta_{carnot} = 1 - \frac{300}{600}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the fraction:

$$\frac{300}{600} = 0.5$$

**Step 4:** Subtract the fraction from unity:

$$\eta_{carnot} = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5$$

**Step 5:** Convert to percentage for the final answer:

$$\eta_{carnot} = 0.5 \times 100\% = 50\%$$

> **Answer:** $\eta_{carnot} = 0.5$ or **50%**

---

**(ii) Net Work Output per Cycle**

**Step 1:** Apply the definition of thermal efficiency, which relates the net work output to the heat input from the hot reservoir:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H}$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange the formula to solve for the net work output by multiplying both sides by $Q_H$:

$$W_{net} = \eta_{th} \times Q_H$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the values of Carnot efficiency (0.5) and the given heat input (1000 kJ):

$$W_{net} = 0.5 \times 1000 \, kJ$$

**Step 4:** Compute the final product:

$$W_{net} = 500 \, kJ$$

> **Answer:** $W_{net} = \mathbf{500 \, kJ}$

---

**(iii) Heat Rejected to the Cold Reservoir**

**Step 1:** Apply the First Law energy balance for a cyclic heat engine, which states that the heat supplied from the hot reservoir equals the sum of the net work output and the heat rejected to the cold reservoir:

$$Q_H = W_{net} + Q_L$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange the equation to solve for the rejected heat $Q_L$ by moving $W_{net}$ to the left-hand side:

$$Q_L = Q_H - W_{net}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the known values of heat input (1000 kJ) and net work output (500 kJ):

$$Q_L = 1000 - 500$$

**Step 4:** Compute the final difference:

$$Q_L = 500 \, kJ$$

> **Answer:** $Q_L = \mathbf{500 \, kJ}$

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q:** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a heat engine. State why 100% conversion of heat into work is impossible in a cyclic heat engine.

**Complete Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:**
*(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram of the heat engine — Hot Reservoir → Engine Block → Cold Reservoir + Work Output)*

**Working Explanation:**

1. **Heat Supply:** Heat $Q_H$ is transferred from the high-temperature source (e.g., boiler furnace at temperature $T_H$) to the working fluid inside the engine.
2. **Work Production:** The working fluid expands, producing mechanical work $W$ (e.g., via piston movement or turbine rotation).
3. **Heat Rejection:** The remaining unused heat $Q_L$ is transferred to the low-temperature sink (e.g., atmosphere at $T_L$).
4. **Cycle Reset:** The fluid is compressed back to its initial state, and the cycle repeats continuously.

**Why 100% Conversion is Impossible:**

According to the **Kelvin-Planck statement of the Second Law**, it is impossible for any device operating in a cycle to receive heat from a single reservoir and convert it entirely into work without rejecting some heat to a lower-temperature sink. Hence, for any cyclic heat engine:

$$\eta_{th} < 1 \quad \text{(always)}$$

The rejected heat $Q_L$ is *thermodynamically necessary* to complete the cycle and return the working fluid to its original state. Without this rejection, entropy would decrease spontaneously, violating the Second Law.

---

### Question B: PMM-2 and Carnot Efficiency Theory

`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q:** What is a Perpetual Motion Machine of the Second Kind (PMM-2)? Explain why a Carnot engine is considered the most efficient heat engine.

**Complete Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Definition of PMM-2:**

A **Perpetual Motion Machine of the Second Kind (PMM-2)** is a hypothetical device that operates in a cycle, absorbs heat from a single thermal reservoir, and converts it entirely into an equivalent amount of work, *without* rejecting any heat to a low-temperature sink.

If PMM-2 existed, its efficiency would be:

$$\eta_{PMM-2} = \frac{W}{Q_H} = \frac{Q_H}{Q_H} = 1 \quad (100\%)$$

**Why PMM-2 is Impossible (Kelvin-Planck Statement):**

The Second Law of Thermodynamics (Kelvin-Planck statement) explicitly forbids this. Hence PMM-2 **violates the Second Law** and **cannot exist** in nature.

**Why the Carnot Engine is the Most Efficient:**

The Carnot cycle consists of **reversible processes** — all heat transfers occur across infinitesimal temperature differences, and all expansions/compressions are frictionless. For a reversible cycle, the Clausius inequality becomes an equality:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = 0$$

This gives:

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_L}{T_L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \eta_{carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

Any *irreversible* real engine between the same two reservoirs will produce more entropy and therefore have a lower efficiency:

$$\eta_{real} < \eta_{carnot}$$

Hence, the Carnot engine is the **theoretical maximum efficiency** benchmark.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q:** A heat engine produces **200 kJ** of work while receiving **500 kJ** of heat from a source at **800 K** and rejecting heat to a sink at **300 K**. Determine:
(i) The thermal efficiency of the engine.
(ii) The heat rejected to the sink.
(iii) Compare with the Carnot efficiency and comment.

**Complete Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Work output, $W_{net} = 200 \, kJ$
- Heat supplied, $Q_H = 500 \, kJ$
- Hot reservoir temperature, $T_H = 800 \, K$
- Cold reservoir temperature, $T_L = 300 \, K$

---

**(i) Thermal Efficiency of the Engine**

**Step 1:** Use the basic definition of thermal efficiency for a heat engine:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values of net work output (200 kJ) and heat input (500 kJ):

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{200}{500}$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the fraction:

$$\eta_{th} = 0.4$$

**Step 4:** Convert to percentage:

$$\eta_{th} = 0.4 \times 100\% = 40\%$$

> **Answer:** $\eta_{th} = 0.4$ or **40%**

---

**(ii) Heat Rejected to the Sink**

**Step 1:** Apply the First Law energy balance for a cyclic heat engine:

$$Q_H = W_{net} + Q_L$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange to solve for the rejected heat $Q_L$:

$$Q_L = Q_H - W_{net}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the values of heat input (500 kJ) and net work output (200 kJ):

$$Q_L = 500 - 200$$

**Step 4:** Compute the final difference:

$$Q_L = 300 \, kJ$$

> **Answer:** $Q_L = \mathbf{300 \, kJ}$

---

**(iii) Comparison with Carnot Efficiency**

**Step 1:** Calculate the Carnot efficiency (the theoretical maximum) between the same two reservoirs:

$$\eta_{carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the temperature values $T_L = 300 \, K$ and $T_H = 800 \, K$:

$$\eta_{carnot} = 1 - \frac{300}{800}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the fraction:

$$\frac{300}{800} = 0.375$$

**Step 4:** Subtract from unity:

$$\eta_{carnot} = 1 - 0.375 = 0.625$$

**Step 5:** Convert to percentage:

$$\eta_{carnot} = 62.5\%$$

**Comment:** The actual engine efficiency (40%) is **less than** the Carnot efficiency (62.5%), which is consistent with the Second Law. The difference (22.5%) represents the irreversibilities such as friction, heat losses, and non-quasi-static processes in the real engine.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Do NOT use Celsius** in the Carnot efficiency formula — always convert to **Kelvin** ($T_K = T_C + 273$). This is the #1 mistake that costs 1–2 marks.
> - When asked to "compare with Carnot," always explicitly state whether the engine is **reversible, irreversible, or ideal**. Examiners look for the Second Law reference.
> - **Unit errors:** Keep $Q$ and $W$ in the **same units** (kJ or J, never mix). Failing to convert J to kJ (or vice versa) is a common error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Heat engine = cyclic device that converts $Q_H$ into $W$, rejecting $Q_L$ to a sink.
- **First Law Energy Balance (Cycle):** $Q_H = W_{net} + Q_L$
- **Thermal Efficiency:** $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H}$ (always $< 1$)
- **Carnot Efficiency:** $\eta_{carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ — depends only on reservoir temperatures in **Kelvin**.
- **Kelvin-Planck:** 100% heat-to-work conversion in a cycle is **impossible** (PMM-2 cannot exist).
- **Clausius Equality (Reversible):** $\dfrac{Q_H}{T_H} = \dfrac{Q_L}{T_L}$
- **Real vs Carnot:** $\eta_{real} \leq \eta_{carnot}$ (equality only for reversible cycles).
- **Three siblings — same device, different goals:** Heat Engine (gives $W$), Refrigerator (gives $Q_L$), Heat Pump (gives $Q_H$).
- **Relationship:** $COP_{HP} = COP_{ref} + 1$
- **Always use absolute temperature scale (Kelvin)** — never Celsius in efficiency/COP formulas.
- **T-S diagram insight:** Area enclosed = $W_{net}$ (very useful for graphical cycle analysis).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Pump and Refrigerator - Working Principle and COP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Heat Pump and Refrigerator - Working Principle and COP # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) ### Refrigerator A **refrigerator** is a cyclic device that operates on a reversed thermodynamic cycle, whose sole purpose is to **remove heat ($Q_L$) from a low-temperature body (cold reservoir / refrigerated space)** and **reject it to a high-temperature body (hot reservoir / surroundings)**, with the input of external work ($W_{net}$). $$Q_L = \text{Refrigeration Effect (Desired Output)}$$ $$Q_H = \text{Heat Rejected to Surroundings}$$ $$W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L = \text{Work Input to Compressor}$$ ### Heat Pump A **heat pump** is essentially the same physical device as a refrigerator, but its **desired output is the heat rejected ($Q_H$) to the high-temperature space** (used for space heating, water heating, etc.). The only difference lies in the **utility of the output side**. ### Coefficient of Performance (COP) The **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** is defined as the ratio of the *desired effect* to the *work input* required to achieve that effect. It is the refrigerating/heat-pumping analogue of *thermal efficiency* in a heat engine. > [!IMPORTANT] > **COP is NOT a thermal efficiency** — it can be greater than 1 (often 2 to 6) because the desired effect includes heat pumped from the cold reservoir plus the work input. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Plain-English Intuition ### 🍎 The "Water Bucket" Analogy Imagine you are at the bottom of a hill with an empty bucket and want to lift water **up** to a tank at the top. - **Heat Pump:** Your *goal* is to **fill the tank at the top**. So your *desired effect* is the water delivered at the top. You walk uphill carrying water (work input) and also scoop water from a stream along the way — so the tank gets water from the stream **PLUS** the water you carried. You deliver far more water than the energy you spent. That's why **COP > 1**. - **Refrigerator:** Same walk, same stream, same hill — but your *goal* is to **empty the stream (cold body)** of its water. The water you carried uphill is irrelevant; you only care about the water you removed from the stream. > Both use the *same equipment* and the *same amount of energy* — only the *definition of "useful output"* changes. ### 🔄 Why "Reversed"? A heat engine takes heat from hot → produces work → rejects to cold. A refrigerator/heat pump is the **reverse**: takes work input → takes heat from cold → delivers heat to hot. This is mandated by the **Second Law of Thermodynamics (Clausius Statement)** — heat cannot spontaneously flow from cold to hot. > [!NOTE] > **Clausius Statement:** It is impossible to construct a device operating in a cycle that produces no effect other than the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Metric | Symbol | Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | Standard Refrigeration Ton | 1 TR | **211 kJ/min = 3.517 kW** | | Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)** | | Cooling/Heating Power (SI) | $Q$ | **Watts (W) or kW** | | Temperatures (Thermodynamic) | $T$ | **Kelvin (K) — always use absolute scale in COP calculations** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Exam Mistake:** KTU examiners frequently deduct marks when students use **°C instead of K** in Carnot COP formulas. Always convert: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Reversed Heat Engine — Conceptual Architecture A refrigerator / heat pump is a **reversed heat engine**. The original heat engine receives heat $Q_H$ from a hot source, converts part to work $W$, and rejects $Q_L$ to a cold sink. Reversing the cycle externally (i.e., running it backwards with work input) makes it pump heat from cold to hot. ### 🔑 Operational Logic — Step by Step **Step 1 — Energy In at the Cold Side:** The refrigerant absorbs latent/sensible heat from the refrigerated space ($Q_L$). This is the **cooling effect**. **Step 2 — Compression (Work Input):** A compressor raises the refrigerant from low pressure/temperature to high pressure/temperature by supplying shaft work $W_{in}$. **Step 3 — Energy Out at the Hot Side:** The hot, high-pressure refrigerant rejects heat $Q_H$ to the surroundings (or to the heated space in heat pump mode). **Step 4 — Expansion:** The refrigerant expands through a throttle/expansion valve, dropping its pressure and temperature so it can again absorb heat at the cold side — **cycle repeats**. ### 🧠 The "Why" Behind Each Step - **Why compress?** Raising pressure raises condensation temperature above ambient so heat can flow *out* to surroundings (Second Law requires $T_{refrigerant} > T_{ambient}$ for spontaneous heat rejection). - **Why throttle?** Pressure drop lowers temperature below the refrigerated space temperature so heat can flow *in* from the cold body. - **Why a cyclic refrigerant?** A working fluid enables continuous transport of energy from cold to hot with no net change in refrigerant state. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Master Reference Table) | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary / Note | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Refrigerator COP** | $COP_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$ | dimensionless | $Q_L$ = cooling effect, $W$ = work input | | 2 | **Heat Pump COP** | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net}}$ | dimensionless | $Q_H$ = heating effect delivered | | 3 | Energy Balance | $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$ | kW | First Law (steady flow) | | 4 | COP via $Q_L, Q_H$ | $COP_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}$ | dimensionless | Derived from #1 + #3 | | 5 | COP via $Q_H, Q_L$ | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_L}$ | dimensionless | Derived from #2 + #3 | | 6 | **Universal COP Relation** | $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ | dimensionless | Proven below — frequently tested | | 7 | **Carnot COP (Refrigerator)** | $COP_{R,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | dimensionless | Reversible (ideal) refrigerator | | 8 | **Carnot COP (Heat Pump)** | $COP_{HP,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ | dimensionless | Reversible (ideal) heat pump | | 9 | **Refrigeration Ton** | $1 \text{ TR} = 211 \text{ kJ/min} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$ | kW | Rate of heat removal | | 10 | Heat Pump Heating Rate | $\dot{Q}_H = COP_{HP} \times \dot{W}_{net}$ | kW | For sizing radiators | | 11 | Refrigeration Rate | $\dot{Q}_L = COP_R \times \dot{W}_{net}$ | kW | For sizing cold storage load | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU examiners love Question #6** — "Prove that $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$" appears in nearly every cycle of previous year papers. Memorize this derivation. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Device Used | Desired Effect | Typical COP | |---|---|---|---| | Domestic Fridge / Cold Storage | Refrigerator | $Q_L$ (food preservation) | 1.5 – 3.0 | | Central AC / Building Cooling | Refrigerator | $Q_L$ (space cooling) | 2.5 – 4.0 | | Room Heater (winter) | Heat Pump | $Q_H$ (space heating) | 3.0 – 5.0 | | Geyser / Water Heater | Heat Pump | $Q_H$ (water heating) | 3.5 – 5.5 | | Industrial Process Heating | Heat Pump | $Q_H$ | 2.5 – 4.0 | ### 💡 Why Heat Pumps Are Preferred Over Electric Heaters An electric resistance heater has an "efficiency" of 1.0 (100% of electrical work → heat). A heat pump with $COP_{HP} = 4$ delivers **4 kW of heat for every 1 kW of electricity** — saving **75% energy** for the same heating effect. This is why **KTU sustainability modules emphasize heat pumps** in green building design. ### ⚖️ Heat Pump vs Refrigerator — When to Use Which? | Criterion | Refrigerator | Heat Pump | |---|---|---| | Primary Goal | Cool something below ambient | Heat something above ambient | | Useful Heat Side | $Q_L$ (cold side) | $Q_H$ (hot side) | | Direction of $T$ gradient | $T_L < T_{ambient}$ | $T_H > T_{ambient}$ | | Equipment | Identical vapor-compression hardware | Identical vapor-compression hardware | | Example | AC unit in summer | AC unit in reverse cycle (winter) | > [!NOTE] > A modern **split AC** is a refrigerator in summer and a heat pump in winter — same hardware, same refrigerant, same compressor — only the reversing valve logic and the "desired output" definition changes. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation #1: The Fundamental COP Formula (Refrigerator) ### Starting Premise By the **First Law of Thermodynamics (steady-flow energy equation)** for a cyclic device, no net change in internal energy occurs over one cycle. **Step 1 — Apply First Law to a cyclic device:** $$\Delta U_{cycle} = 0$$ **Step 2 — Translate to energy flows (sign convention: heat INTO system is positive; work done ON system is positive):** $$Q_H \text{ (rejected by refrigerant)} - Q_L \text{ (absorbed by refrigerant)} + W_{net} \text{ (input)} = 0$$ **Step 3 — Rearrange to obtain the energy balance:** $$Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$$ **Step 4 — Define the desired effect for a refrigerator** = $Q_L$, and required input = $W_{net}$: $$COP_R = \frac{\text{Desired Effect}}{\text{Work Input}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute $W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$ from Step 3:** $$\begin{aligned} COP_R &= \frac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L} \end{aligned}$$ > ✅ **Result:** $\boxed{COP_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}}$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation #2: COP of a Heat Pump **Step 1 — Same energy balance as Derivation #1:** $$Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$$ **Step 2 — Define the desired effect for a heat pump** = $Q_H$, and required input = $W_{net}$: $$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}}$$ **Step 3 — Express in terms of $Q_L$ and $Q_H$:** $$\begin{aligned} COP_{HP} &= \frac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_L} \end{aligned}$$ > ✅ **Result:** $\boxed{COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_L}}$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation #3: The Master Relation $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ This is the **most-tested derivation in KTU exams**. **Step 1 — Write both COPs using common denominator $(Q_H - Q_L)$:** $$\begin{aligned} COP_{HP} &= \frac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_L} \\ COP_R &= \frac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Subtract $COP_R$ from $COP_{HP}$:** $$\begin{aligned} COP_{HP} - COP_R &= \frac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_L} - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Combine over common denominator:** $$\begin{aligned} COP_{HP} - COP_R &= \frac{Q_H - Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Simplify numerator and denominator:** $$\begin{aligned} COP_{HP} - COP_R &= 1 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Final rearranged form:** $$\boxed{COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Interpretation:** A heat pump always has a COP **exactly 1 unit greater** than the refrigerator running on the same hardware and same temperature limits. Because $T_H > T_L$, the heat pump's COP is also **numerically larger** — making the heat pump a more efficient *heating* device than the refrigerator is a *cooling* device. --- ## 3.4 Derivation #4: Carnot (Reversible) COP The Carnot COP is the **maximum possible COP** for any refrigerator or heat pump operating between two given temperature reservoirs. **Step 1 — Apply Second Law to a reversible cycle:** For a reversible (Carnot) cycle, entropy change is zero, so: $$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_L}{T_L}$$ **Step 2 — Solve for $Q_H$:** $$Q_H = Q_L \cdot \frac{T_H}{T_L}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute into First Law $W = Q_H - Q_L$:** $$\begin{aligned} W_{net} &= Q_L \cdot \frac{T_H}{T_L} - Q_L \\ &= Q_L \left( \frac{T_H - T_L}{T_L} \right) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Plug into the COP formula for a refrigerator:** $$\begin{aligned} COP_{R,Carnot} &= \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \frac{Q_L}{Q_L \left( \dfrac{T_H - T_L}{T_L} \right)} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Cancel $Q_L$ and simplify:** $$\boxed{COP_{R,Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}}$$ **Step 6 — For heat pump, use the energy balance $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$:** $$\begin{aligned} COP_{HP,Carnot} &= \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} \end{aligned}$$ > ✅ **Result:** $\boxed{COP_{HP,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}}$ --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Temperature Limits Let $T_H = 300 \text{ K}$ and observe how COP changes with $T_L$: | $T_L$ (K) | $T_H - T_L$ (K) | $COP_{R,Carnot} = T_L / (T_H - T_L)$ | $COP_{HP,Carnot} = T_H / (T_H - T_L)$ | Difference | |---|---|---|---|---| | 280 | 20 | 14.00 | 15.00 | 1 | | 270 | 30 | 9.00 | 10.00 | 1 | | 260 | 40 | 6.50 | 7.50 | 1 | | 250 | 50 | 5.00 | 6.00 | 1 | | 200 | 100 | 2.00 | 3.00 | 1 | | 100 | 200 | 0.50 | 1.50 | 1 | ### 🔍 Engineering Insights from the Matrix 1. **As $T_L$ decreases** (deeper cooling required), both COPs fall rapidly — refrigeration becomes exponentially harder. 2. **As $T_H$ increases** (hotter outdoor conditions, e.g., AC in summer), both COPs fall — heat rejection becomes harder. 3. **The difference is always 1**, confirming $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ universally. 4. **For $COP_R < 1$:** the refrigerator requires more work than the heat it removes — typical of cryogenic applications. 5. **Practical real-world COP** = $(0.3 \text{ to } 0.5) \times$ Carnot COP due to irreversibilities. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Schematic — Energy Flow in a Refrigerator (Block Diagram) The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **functional energy flow architecture** of a refrigerator, showing how work input and heat flows interact across system boundaries. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Hot Reservoir
Temperature TH] -->|Q H rejected
OUT to hot side| R((Refrigerator
Cyclic Device)) R -->|Q L absorbed
IN from cold side| B[Low Temp Space
Temperature TL] W[Work Source
W net INPUT] -->|Shaft work
compressor| R R -.COP R = Q L / W net.-> R ``` ### Interpretation of the Diagram - **Arrow A→R:** $Q_H$ flows **out** of the refrigerant (rejected to hot reservoir). - **Arrow R→B:** $Q_L$ flows **into** the refrigerant from the cold space (the cooling effect). - **Arrow W→R:** External work $W_{net}$ is supplied (typically via electric compressor). - The COP expression appears as a side annotation of the device block. --- ## 4.2 Schematic — Energy Flow in a Heat Pump (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Low Temp Source
Temperature TL ambient] -->|Q L absorbed
IN from cold side| H((Heat Pump
Cyclic Device)) H -->|Q H rejected
OUT to hot side| B[Heated Space
Temperature TH indoor] W[Work Source
W net INPUT] -->|Shaft work
compressor| H H -.COP HP = Q H / W net.-> H ``` ### Interpretation - The **only difference** from the refrigerator diagram is the *label* of the "useful output": here $Q_H$ (delivered heat) is desired, whereas in the refrigerator $Q_L$ (removed heat) is desired. - Same hardware, same work input, same temperature limits. --- ## 4.3 Schematic — Vapor Compression Cycle (Block Functional Architecture) Since a physical P-h diagram cannot be rendered in Mermaid, we present the **functional component-level flow** of the vapor-compression refrigeration cycle, which is the practical realization of the refrigerator/heat pump. ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[State 1: Saturated Vapor
Low P, Low T] --> C[Compressor] C -->|W in input| P2[State 2: Superheated Vapor
High P, High T] P2 --> K[Condenser
Heat Q H rejected] K --> P3[State 3: Saturated Liquid
High P] P3 --> V[Expansion Valve
Throttling process] V --> P4[State 4: Liquid-Vapor Mix
Low P, Low T] P4 --> E[Evaporator
Heat Q L absorbed] E --> P1 ``` ### Functional Roles | Component | Real-World Device | Thermodynamic Process | Energy Interaction | |---|---|---|---| | Compressor | Electric motor + scroll/reciprocating pump | Adiabatic reversible compression | $W_{in}$ input | | Condenser | Outdoor coil (AC) / Radiator (HP) | Isobaric heat rejection | $Q_H$ out | | Expansion Valve | Capillary tube / TXV | Irreversible throttling | No work, no heat | | Evaporator | Indoor coil (AC) / Ground coil (HP) | Isobaric heat absorption | $Q_L$ in | --- ## 4.4 Schematic — COP Comparison: Refrigerator vs Heat Pump (Side-by-Side) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Refrig[Refrigerator Mode - Summer] R1[Q L = Desired Cooling] --> R2[W net input] R2 --> R3[COP R = Q L / W net] end subgraph HPMode[Heat Pump Mode - Winter] H1[Q H = Desired Heating] --> H2[W net input] H2 --> H3[COP HP = Q H / W net] end Refrig -->|Same hardware| HPMode HPMode -.COP HP = COP R + 1.-> Refrig ``` This diagram emphasizes the **dual-mode operation** of modern reversible AC systems and the **universal +1 relationship** between the two COPs. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### 📘 Question A1 **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]** > **Define Coefficient of Performance (COP). Write the expression for COP of a refrigerator and a heat pump.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The Coefficient of Performance (COP) is defined as the ratio of the desired heating or cooling effect to the work input required to achieve it. - **Refrigerator COP:** $COP_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$ - **Heat Pump COP:** $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net}}$ where $Q_L$ = refrigeration effect (heat removed from cold space), $Q_H$ = heat rejected to hot space, and $W_{net}$ = net work input to the compressor. > **Mark Distribution (KTU Pattern):** Definition (1M) + Refrigerator formula (1M) + Heat Pump formula (1M). --- ### 📘 Question A2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** > **State and explain Clausius Statement of the Second Law of Thermodynamics. How does it justify the need for a refrigerator?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Clausius Statement:** It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no effect other than the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body. **Justification for Refrigerator:** Since heat cannot flow *spontaneously* from a cold body to a hot body, an external **work input (via compressor)** is required to drive the heat transfer against its natural direction. This is precisely the function of a refrigerator — to make the "impossible" spontaneous flow possible by expending work. (3 Marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) ### 📗 QUESTION A (Choice 1) — **[14 MARKS]** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 | Module 1]** > **(a)** Derive the relation between COP of a heat pump and COP of a refrigerator. **(7 Marks)** > **(b)** A refrigerator operates between $-10°C$ and $30°C$. It extracts $1500 \text{ kJ/min}$ of heat from the cold space. Determine: (i) the COP, (ii) the work input, and (iii) the heat rejected to the hot reservoir. **(7 Marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution ### Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks] **Given:** Both devices operate on the same cyclic hardware. **Step 1 — First Law of Thermodynamics (steady-state cyclic device, ΔU = 0):** $$Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$$ **Step 2 — Express both COPs in terms of $Q_L, Q_H, W$:** $$\begin{aligned} COP_R &= \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} \\ COP_{HP} &= \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Take the difference:** $$\begin{aligned} COP_{HP} - COP_R &= \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}} - \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} \\ &= \frac{Q_H - Q_L}{W_{net}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — From energy balance, $Q_H - Q_L = W_{net}$. Substitute:** $$\begin{aligned} COP_{HP} - COP_R &= \frac{W_{net}}{W_{net}} = 1 \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1}$$ This proves that a heat pump's COP is always **one unit greater** than the refrigerator COP operating between the same temperature limits. > **Mark Split (a):** First Law statement (1M) + Both COP expressions (2M) + Algebraic manipulation (2M) + Final relation with physical interpretation (2M). --- ### Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 Marks] **Given Data:** - $T_L = -10°C = -10 + 273 = 263 \text{ K}$ - $T_H = 30°C = 30 + 273 = 303 \text{ K}$ - $Q_L = 1500 \text{ kJ/min} = \dfrac{1500}{60} = 25 \text{ kW}$ **Step 1 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin (mandatory for Carnot cycle):** $$T_L = 263 \text{ K}, \quad T_H = 303 \text{ K}$$ **Step 2 — Compute Carnot COP (assuming ideal/reversible cycle as KTU default):** $$\begin{aligned} COP_R &= \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} \\ &= \frac{263}{303 - 263} \\ &= \frac{263}{40} \\ &= 6.575 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the work input using $COP_R = Q_L / W_{net}$:** $$\begin{aligned} W_{net} &= \frac{Q_L}{COP_R} = \frac{25}{6.575} \\ &= 3.802 \text{ kW} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the heat rejected using energy balance $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$:** $$\begin{aligned} Q_H &= 25 + 3.802 = 28.802 \text{ kW} \end{aligned}$$ ### 📋 Final Answer Box | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | **(i) COP of Refrigerator** | **6.575** | | **(ii) Work Input $W_{net}$** | **3.802 kW** (or 228.1 kJ/min) | | **(iii) Heat Rejected $Q_H$** | **28.802 kW** (or 1728.1 kJ/min) | > **Mark Split (b):** Temperature conversion (1M) + COP calculation (2M) + Work input (2M) + Heat rejected (2M). --- ### 📕 QUESTION B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — **[14 MARKS]** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 | Module 1]** > **(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the working principle of a refrigerator as a reversed heat engine. Derive the expression for the COP of a Carnot refrigerator. **(7 Marks)** > **(b)** A heat pump is used to heat a house in winter. The indoor temperature is $22°C$ and the outdoor temperature is $5°C$. If the heat pump delivers $40 \text{ kW}$ of heating, calculate: (i) the COP of the heat pump, (ii) the work input, and (iii) the COP if the same device were used as a refrigerator between the same temperature limits. **(7 Marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution ### Part (a) — Theory + Derivation [7 Marks] **Block Diagram (already shown in Section 4.1):** A reversed heat engine receives work input $W_{net}$, extracts $Q_L$ from cold reservoir, and rejects $Q_H$ to hot reservoir. **Derivation of Carnot COP:** For a **reversible (Carnot)** cycle, the second law gives: $$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_L}{T_L}$$ From energy balance: $W = Q_H - Q_L$. Therefore: $$\begin{aligned} COP_{R,Carnot} &= \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \frac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L} \end{aligned}$$ Substituting $Q_H = Q_L \cdot (T_H / T_L)$: $$\begin{aligned} COP_{R,Carnot} &= \frac{Q_L}{Q_L \cdot T_H/T_L - Q_L} = \frac{Q_L}{Q_L (T_H - T_L)/T_L} \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{COP_{R,Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}}$$ > **Mark Split (a):** Block diagram with labels (2M) + Reversed engine concept (1M) + Second Law application (2M) + Final derivation (2M). --- ### Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 Marks] **Given Data:** - $T_H$ (indoor) $= 22°C = 295 \text{ K}$ - $T_L$ (outdoor) $= 5°C = 278 \text{ K}$ - $Q_H = 40 \text{ kW}$ (heating delivered) **Step 1 — Temperature Conversion:** $$T_H = 295 \text{ K}, \quad T_L = 278 \text{ K}$$ **Step 2 — Compute Carnot Heat Pump COP:** $$\begin{aligned} COP_{HP} &= \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} \\ &= \frac{295}{295 - 278} \\ &= \frac{295}{17} \\ &= 17.353 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Work Input using $COP_{HP} = Q_H / W_{net}$:** $$\begin{aligned} W_{net} &= \frac{Q_H}{COP_{HP}} = \frac{40}{17.353} \\ &= 2.305 \text{ kW} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — COP as Refrigerator using $COP_R = COP_{HP} - 1$:** $$\begin{aligned} COP_R &= 17.353 - 1 = 16.353 \end{aligned}$$ ### 📋 Final Answer Box | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | **(i) COP of Heat Pump** | **17.35** | | **(ii) Work Input $W_{net}$** | **2.305 kW** | | **(iii) COP as Refrigerator** | **16.35** | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:** > 1. **Using °C instead of K** in $T_H/(T_H - T_L)$ — instantly loses **2 marks**. Always convert: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273$. > 2. **Confusing $T_H$ and $T_L$** in heat pump problems: indoor = $T_H$ (hot side), outdoor = $T_L$ (cold side). Reversing these gives a wrong negative denominator. > 3. **Forgetting to subtract 1** for the refrigerator COP from the heat pump COP. Remember $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$, NOT $COP_{HP} = COP_R$. > 4. **Missing units** in the final answer (kW not W, or vice versa). KTU examiners award partial marks only with correct units. > 5. **Not stating First/Second Law explicitly** in derivation — write "Applying First Law to a cyclic device..." for full credit. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) ### ⚡ Master Formulas (Memorize These) $$\boxed{COP_R = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \frac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}}$$ $$\boxed{COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = \frac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_L}}$$ $$\boxed{COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1}$$ $$\boxed{COP_{R,Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}}$$ $$\boxed{COP_{HP,Carnot} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}}$$ ### 🧠 Conceptual Anchors (Never Forget) - ✅ A **refrigerator and heat pump are the same hardware** — only the definition of "useful output" changes. - ✅ $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$ — energy balance, always. - ✅ **Always use Kelvin** in Carnot COP — converting from °C is mandatory. - ✅ **Carnot COP is the theoretical maximum** — real COP is always less due to irreversibilities. - ✅ **COP > 1 is normal** — unlike efficiency, COP is not bounded by 1. - ✅ **Heat pump > Refrigerator efficiency** — $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1 > COP_R$ always. - ✅ **Lower $T_L$ or higher $T_H$ → lower COP** — refrigeration/heat-pumping becomes harder. - ✅ **1 TR = 3.517 kW** = 211 kJ/min — standard unit of refrigeration capacity. ### 🛠️ Engineering Reality Check - **Domestic refrigerator:** actual COP ≈ 1.5–3.0 - **Central air conditioner:** actual COP ≈ 2.5–4.0 - **Air-source heat pump:** actual COP ≈ 3.0–5.0 - **Ground-source (geothermal) heat pump:** actual COP ≈ 4.0–6.0 - **Carnot COP** for typical AC: ≈ 8–15 - The ratio $\dfrac{\text{Actual COP}}{\text{Carnot COP}}$ is called the **second-law efficiency** or **COP ratio** — measures how close a real device comes to the ideal. ### 🎯 KTU 2024 Exam Quick Tips 1. Draw the **block diagram with arrows** in every theory answer — even if not explicitly asked (adds 1 mark visually). 2. Always **state the laws** before using them ("Applying First Law…", "Applying Second Law to reversible cycle…"). 3. In numericals, **convert units first** and **write them down explicitly** — this prevents later arithmetic confusion. 4. For derivations, **show every algebraic step** — partial marking requires showing the work. 5. **Tag every numerical** with units in the final answer box — examiners reward completeness. > 🎓 **Final Note:** This topic forms the **theoretical backbone of Module 1**. Mastery here directly enables learning of vapor compression cycles, refrigerants, and system performance analysis in later modules. KTU examiners frequently integrate these formulas with compressor work, condenser/evaporator loads, and refrigeration tonnage in Module 2 — so internalize these relations deeply.

topics.title: Air Refrigeration Cycles - Open
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Air Refrigeration Cycles - Open # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Open Air Refrigeration Cycle**: An air refrigeration system in which atmospheric air is continuously used as the working refrigerant, compressed, cooled by atmospheric air or water in an external heat exchanger, expanded to a low temperature, and finally discharged into the space to be cooled (or to the atmosphere). It is essentially the **reversed Carnot cycle** executed with a perfect gas (air) as the working fluid. The cycle is the conceptual basis of the **Bell-Coleman cycle**, the first practical air refrigeration machine, originally developed in **1902** for marine cold storage and later adapted for aircraft pressurization and cabin cooling. ### 1.2 Classification of Air Refrigeration Cycles | Parameter | Open Cycle | Closed Cycle | |---|---|---| | Working fluid | Fresh atmospheric air each cycle | Same air recirculated in a sealed loop | | Discharge | Air is exhausted after absorbing heat | No exhaust; air returns to compressor inlet | | Application | Aircraft cabin cooling, marine gas storage | Industrial chilling, gas liquefaction | | Humidity handling | Must be controlled to avoid icing | Easier to manage | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy**: Imagine blowing on hot food with your mouth — but instead of blowing *warm* exhaled air, you first **compress** the air with a small pump, **cool** it down by passing it through a tube surrounded by cold water, then let it **expand suddenly** through a nozzle so it becomes very cold. When this super-chilled air jet is blown into a hot room, it absorbs enormous amounts of heat. This is exactly the principle of an open air refrigeration cycle — atmospheric air is the "refrigerant", and it is dumped into the cabin after one use. ### 1.4 Primary Components of an Open Air Refrigeration System 1. **Compressor** (usually reciprocating or centrifugal) — raises air pressure isentropically. 2. **After-cooler / Primary Heat Exchanger** — rejects compression heat to ambient (air or water). 3. **Expander** (reciprocating or vane-type) — produces the refrigerating effect by isentropic expansion. 4. **Refrigerated Space (Cabin / Cold Room)** — absorbs heat from the load; air is discharged here. ### 1.5 Standard KTU Working Metrics | Quantity | Typical Range (Aircraft) | |---|---| | Cabin Pressure (P1) | **0.9 – 1.0 bar** | | Delivery Pressure (P2) | **3 – 5 bar** | | Cabin Temp (T1) | **290 – 305 K** | | Cooling Temp (T3) | **300 – 310 K** | | Cold Air Temp (T4) | **240 – 270 K** | | Pressure Ratio (rp) | **3 – 5** | | COP (theoretical) | **2 – 5** | ### 1.6 Standard Assumptions for Analysis > [!NOTE] > The KTU standard analysis assumes: > - Air behaves as a **perfect gas** with **cp = 1.005 kJ/(kg·K)** and **γ = 1.4**. > - **Compression (1→2)** and **Expansion (3→4)** are **isentropic** (reversible adiabatic). > - **Cooling (2→3)** and **Heat Absorption (4→1)** occur at **constant pressure**. > - **No pressure drop** in heat exchangers. > - **Kinetic and potential energy** changes are negligible. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Thermodynamic Processes The cycle operates between four state points. Understanding *why* each process is chosen is the key to mastering the analysis. ### Process 1 → 2 : Isentropic Compression in the Compressor - **Why**: To raise the air pressure so that subsequent cooling at high pressure and expansion can produce a temperature much lower than ambient. - **How**: The compressor (reciprocating or centrifugal) compresses air **adiabatically and reversibly**. - **Consequence**: Both pressure *and* temperature rise sharply. ### Process 2 → 3 : Constant Pressure Heat Rejection in the After-Cooler - **Why**: To dump the heat of compression to the surroundings using cool ambient air or water, *before* the air is expanded. - **How**: Hot high-pressure air flows through tubes surrounded by a cooler medium. - **Consequence**: Temperature drops from **T2 to T3** at constant pressure **P2**. ### Process 3 → 4 : Isentropic Expansion in the Expander - **Why**: This is where the *refrigerating effect* is generated. Air does work during expansion, internal energy falls, and temperature drops drastically. - **How**: Reciprocating or vane-type expander extracts work from the high-pressure air. - **Consequence**: Temperature drops from **T3 to T4**; pressure drops from **P2 to P1**. ### Process 4 → 1 : Constant Pressure Heat Absorption in the Refrigerated Space - **Why**: Cold air at T4 enters the cabin and absorbs heat (Qa) from the load, warming up to T1. - **How**: Cold air is ducted into the space to be cooled. - **Consequence**: Refrigerating effect per kg = **cp(T1 − T4)**. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Compression temp | $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | K | | 2 | Expansion temp | $T_4 = T_3 / r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | K | | 3 | Pressure ratio exponent | $(\gamma-1)/\gamma = 0.4/1.4 = 0.2857$ | dimensionless | | 4 | Compression work | $W_c = c_p (T_2 - T_1)$ | kJ/kg | | 5 | Expansion work (recovered) | $W_e = c_p (T_3 - T_4)$ | kJ/kg | | 6 | Net work input | $W_{net} = c_p [(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)]$ | kJ/kg | | 7 | Refrigerating Effect / kg | $RE = c_p (T_1 - T_4)$ | kJ/kg | | 8 | **COP (Open Cycle)** | $\boxed{COP = \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1} = \dfrac{T_3}{T_2 - T_3}}$ | dimensionless | | 9 | Mass flow rate | $\dot{m}_a = \dot{Q}_R / [c_p(T_1 - T_4)]$ | kg/s | | 10 | Power required | $\dot{P} = \dot{m}_a \cdot W_{net}$ | kW | | 11 | Specific volume (inlet) | $v_1 = R \cdot T_1 / P_1$ | m³/kg | | 12 | Swept volume of compressor | $V_s = \dot{m}_a \cdot v_1 / \eta_{vol}$ | m³/s | | 13 | Heat rejected | $Q_{rej} = c_p (T_2 - T_3)$ per kg | kJ/kg | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Symmetry identity (exam gold!)**: Because processes 1-2 and 3-4 are both isentropic over the same pressure ratio: > $$\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \dfrac{T_3}{T_4} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{T_2}{T_3} = \dfrac{T_1}{T_4} = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$ ## 2.3 Real-World Utility & Engineering Significance | Domain | Application of Open Air Cycle | |---|---| | **Aerospace** | Cabin pressurization and cooling in commercial aircraft (Boeing 787 uses a derivative concept) | | **Marine** | Cooling of CNG / LNG cargo holds and crew spaces | | **Pneumatic Conveying** | Cooling of bulk solids during transport | | **Defense** | Cooling electronic enclosures in submarines, mobile radar stations | | **Cold Chain** | Pre-cooling of perishable cargo before loading | > [!NOTE] > **Why air and not vapor?** Air has a very high specific heat per unit mass and is non-toxic, non-flammable, and free. However, its COP is much lower than vapor-compression cycles because air's specific volume is enormous, requiring bulky machinery. Air refrigeration is therefore used only when **weight savings and safety** dominate over **efficiency** — e.g., in aircraft. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Temperature After Isentropic Compression (T2) For an isentropic process with a perfect gas: $$ \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} $$ > **Step 1** — Starting from the isentropic relation for a perfect gas: > $$\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$ > > **Step 2** — Define the pressure ratio $r_p = P_2/P_1$ for compact notation: > $$\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$ > > **Step 3** — Substitute standard air values $\gamma = 1.4$, so $(\gamma-1)/\gamma = 0.2857$: > $$\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = r_p^{0.2857}$$ > > **Step 4** — Multiply both sides by $T_1$ to obtain the working formula: > $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{0.2857}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Temperature After Isentropic Expansion (T4) $$ \dfrac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\dfrac{P_3}{P_4}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} $$ > **Step 1** — Process 3→4 is isentropic from P2 (=P3) to P1 (=P4): > $$\dfrac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = r_p^{0.2857}$$ > > **Step 2** — Invert to obtain T4: > $$T_4 = \dfrac{T_3}{r_p^{0.2857}}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Refrigerating Effect per kg of Air > **Step 1** — Process 4→1 is a constant-pressure heat absorption process. By the first law for a steady-flow device with negligible KE/PE changes: > $$q_{in} = h_1 - h_4 = c_p (T_1 - T_4)$$ > > **Step 2** — This is the *refrigerating effect* per kilogram of air circulated: > $$\boxed{RE = c_p (T_1 - T_4) \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}}$$ ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Net Work of the Cycle > **Step 1** — Work input to compressor (process 1→2): > $$w_c = c_p (T_2 - T_1)$$ > > **Step 2** — Work recovered from expander (process 3→4): > $$w_e = c_p (T_3 - T_4)$$ > > **Step 3** — Net work supplied externally: > $$w_{net} = w_c - w_e = c_p \left[(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)\right]$$ > $$w_{net} = c_p \left[(T_2 - T_3) - (T_1 - T_4)\right]$$ ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Coefficient of Performance (COP) The fundamental definition of COP for any refrigeration cycle is: $$ COP = \dfrac{\text{Desired Output}}{\text{Required Input}} = \dfrac{RE}{w_{net}} $$ > **Step 1** — Substitute the expressions derived above: > $$COP = \dfrac{c_p(T_1 - T_4)}{c_p[(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)]} = \dfrac{T_1 - T_4}{(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)}$$ > > **Step 2** — Use the symmetry identity $T_2/T_3 = T_1/T_4 = r_p^{0.2857}$ to write: > $$\dfrac{T_2}{T_3} = \dfrac{T_1}{T_4}$$ > $$T_1 = T_4 \cdot \dfrac{T_2}{T_3}$$ > > **Step 3** — Let $X = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$. Then $T_2 = T_1 X$ and $T_3 = T_4 X$. Substitute: > $$COP = \dfrac{T_1 - T_4}{T_1 X - T_1 - T_4 X + T_4}$$ > $$COP = \dfrac{T_1 - T_4}{X(T_1 - T_4) - (T_1 - T_4)}$$ > $$COP = \dfrac{T_1 - T_4}{(T_1 - T_4)(X - 1)} = \dfrac{1}{X - 1}$$ > > **Step 4** — Final compact forms (most exam-relevant): > $$\boxed{COP = \dfrac{1}{r_p^{0.2857} - 1} = \dfrac{T_3}{T_2 - T_3} = \dfrac{T_4}{T_1 - T_4}}$$ ## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Mass of Air Required for Given Refrigeration Load For a cooling load of $\dot{Q}_R$ kW (= kJ/s): > **Step 1** — Energy balance on the refrigerated space: > $$\dot{Q}_R = \dot{m}_a \cdot RE = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_p (T_1 - T_4)$$ > > **Step 2** — Solve for mass flow rate: > $$\boxed{\dot{m}_a = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_R}{c_p (T_1 - T_4)} \quad \text{[kg/s]}}$$ > > **Step 3** — For 1 TR (3.517 kW) of refrigeration: > $$\dot{m}_a = \dfrac{3.517}{1.005 \times (T_1 - T_4)} \quad \text{[kg/s]}$$ ## 3.7 Derivation 7 — Compressor Power Required > **Step 1** — Power input: > $$\dot{P} = \dot{Q}_R / COP = \dot{m}_a \cdot w_{net}$$ > > **Step 2** — Swept (displacement) volume of compressor: > $$\boxed{V_s = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a \cdot v_1}{\eta_{vol}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a \cdot R T_1}{\eta_{vol} \cdot P_1}}$$ > where $R = 0.287 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$ for air. ## 3.8 Worked Numerical Example (Exam-Style Walkthrough) **Problem:** An open air refrigeration cycle is used to cool a cabin. Air enters the compressor at $T_1 = 300$ K, $P_1 = 1.0$ bar. The pressure ratio is $r_p = 4$. The air is cooled back to $T_3 = 310$ K in the after-cooler. Take $\gamma = 1.4$ and $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K). **Find:** (a) Temperatures $T_2$ and $T_4$, (b) COP of the cycle, (c) Mass of air per TR, (d) Power per TR. --- **Solution (a) — Temperatures:** > **Step 1** — Compression ratio exponent: > $$(\gamma-1)/\gamma = 0.2857$$ > > **Step 2** — Compute $r_p^{0.2857}$: > $$4^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \cdot \ln 4} = e^{0.2857 \cdot 1.3863} = e^{0.3960} = 1.4860$$ > > **Step 3** — Compute T2: > $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot 4^{0.2857} = 300 \times 1.4860 = 445.8 \text{ K}$$ > > **Step 4** — Compute T4: > $$T_4 = T_3 / 4^{0.2857} = 310 / 1.4860 = 208.6 \text{ K}$$ **Solution (b) — COP:** > **Step 1** — Use the direct formula: > $$COP = \dfrac{1}{r_p^{0.2857} - 1} = \dfrac{1}{1.4860 - 1} = \dfrac{1}{0.4860} = 2.058$$ > > **Step 2** — Cross-check with T3 form: > $$COP = T_3/(T_2 - T_3) = 310/(445.8 - 310) = 310/135.8 = 2.058 \quad \checkmark$$ **Solution (c) — Mass of air per TR:** > **Step 1** — Refrigerating effect per kg: > $$RE = c_p(T_1 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (300 - 208.6) = 1.005 \times 91.4 = 91.86 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ > > **Step 2** — Mass flow rate: > $$\dot{m}_a = 3.517 / 91.86 = 0.0383 \text{ kg/s} = 2.30 \text{ kg/min} = 138 \text{ kg/hr}$$ **Solution (d) — Power per TR:** > $$\dot{P} = \dot{Q}_R / COP = 3.517 / 2.058 = 1.709 \text{ kW per TR}$$ > > **Cross-check**: Net work per kg = $c_p[(T_2-T_1)-(T_3-T_4)] = 1.005 \times [(145.8) - (101.4)] = 1.005 \times 44.4 = 44.62$ kJ/kg. Power = $0.0383 \times 44.62 = 1.709$ kW ✓ --- ## 3.9 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Pressure Ratio on Performance | Pressure Ratio $r_p$ | $T_2$ (K) | $T_4$ (K) | $RE$ (kJ/kg) | $W_{net}$ (kJ/kg) | **COP** | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 2 | 366.1 | 245.8 | 54.5 | 14.4 | **3.78** | | 3 | 410.4 | 226.6 | 73.8 | 25.7 | **2.87** | | **4** | **445.8** | **208.6** | **91.9** | **44.6** | **2.06** | | 5 | 475.1 | 195.7 | 104.8 | 65.3 | **1.61** | | 6 | 500.3 | 185.9 | 114.8 | 86.3 | **1.33** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Crucial Insight**: Higher pressure ratio **lowers COP** but **increases refrigerating effect per kg**. Designers balance this trade-off. Aircraft typically use **rp ≈ 3–5**. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Open Air Refrigeration Cycle) ```mermaid flowchart LR ATM[Atmospheric Air Intake
