Theoretical Benchmark] --> B{Process Requirements} B -->|Isothermal Compression/Expansion| C[Requires Very Slow
Piston Movement
+ Perfect Heat Exchange
= Infinitely Large HX] B -->|Isentropic Compression/Expansion| D[Requires No Friction
+ Perfect Insulation
= Impossible in Reality] C --> E[Not Practically Realizable] D --> E E --> F[Therefore:
Used only as
THEORETICAL BENCHMARK
for comparing real cycles] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style E fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000 style F fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** > **Define the Coefficient of Performance (C.O.P.) of a refrigerator and state its value for a Reversed Carnot Cycle refrigerator operating between two temperature limits.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** The C.O.P. of a refrigerator is defined as the ratio of the desired refrigerating effect (heat extracted from the cold space) to the work input required to achieve it. $$ \text{C.O.P.} = \frac{\text{Refrigerating Effect } (Q_L)}{\text{Work Input } (W_{net})} $$ - **For Reversed Carnot Cycle (2 marks):** Between a cold reservoir at $T_L$ K and a hot reservoir at $T_H$ K: $$ \text{COP}_{Reversed\,Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} $$ - The C.O.P. depends **only on the temperature limits** and represents the **maximum possible C.O.P.** for any refrigerator operating between the same two temperatures. --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** > **List any four assumptions made in the analysis of an air refrigerator working on the Reversed Carnot Cycle.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. Air behaves as a **perfect ideal gas** with constant specific heats ($C_p$, $C_v$) throughout the cycle. **(0.75)** 2. The compression and expansion processes are **reversible and adiabatic (isentropic)**. **(0.75)** 3. The heat rejection and heat absorption processes are **isothermal** (occur at constant temperature $T_H$ and $T_L$ respectively). **(0.75)** 4. There is **no heat transfer** between the working fluid and surroundings during compression and expansion; all heat transfer occurs only during the isothermal processes. **(0.75)** --- ## PART B: 14-Mark Questions (With Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – July 2024]** > **A. (a)** Derive an expression for the C.O.P. of a Reversed Carnot Cycle refrigerator. **(7 marks)** > **A. (b)** An air refrigerator operates on the Reversed Carnot Cycle. The temperature of the cold space is **−10°C** and the temperature of the hot space (atmosphere) is **30°C**. The pressure ratio is **4**. Calculate: > (i) The C.O.P. of the cycle > (ii) The refrigerating effect produced per kg of air > (iii) The net work required per kg of air. > Take for air: $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K, $\gamma = 1.4$. **(7 marks)** ### **Model Solution:** #### Part A(a) — Derivation of C.O.P. (7 marks) Consider a Reversed Carnot Cycle operating between a cold reservoir at $T_L$ K and a hot reservoir at $T_H$ K. The four processes and their heat/work interactions are: - **Process 1 → 2 (Isentropic compression):** No heat transfer, work input = $W_C = C_p(T_H - T_L)$. - **Process 2 → 3 (Isothermal heat rejection at $T_H$):** Heat rejected to atmosphere = $Q_H = R \cdot T_H \cdot \ln(P_2/P_1)$. - **Process 3 → 4 (Isentropic expansion):** No heat transfer, work output = $W_E = C_p(T_H - T_L)$. - **Process 4 → 1 (Isothermal heat absorption at $T_L$):** Heat absorbed from cold space = $Q_L = R \cdot T_L \cdot \ln(P_2/P_1)$. Applying the **First Law of Thermodynamics** to the cyclic process: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Net work input} \quad W_{net} &= Q_H - Q_L \\ &= R \cdot T_H \cdot \ln(P_2/P_1) - R \cdot T_L \cdot \ln(P_2/P_1) \\ &= R \cdot (T_H - T_L) \cdot \ln(P_2/P_1) \end{aligned} $$ The C.O.P. of the refrigerator is: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{C.O.P.} &= \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \frac{R \cdot T_L \cdot \ln(P_2/P_1)}{R \cdot (T_H - T_L) \cdot \ln(P_2/P_1)} \\ \text{C.O.P.} &= \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} \end{aligned} $$ **The COP depends only on the absolute temperatures of the two reservoirs — a fundamental result of the Second Law of Thermodynamics.** #### Part A(b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks) **Given data:** - $T_L = -10°C = 263$ K - $T_H = 30°C = 303$ K - $P_2/P_1 = 4$ - $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K - $\gamma = 1.4$ **(i) C.O.P. of the cycle (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{C.O.P.} &= \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{263}{303 - 263} = \frac{263}{40} \\ \text{C.O.P.} &= 6.575 \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Refrigerating effect per kg of air (2.5 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} q_L &= R \cdot T_L \cdot \ln(P_2/P_1) \\ q_L &= 0.287 \times 263 \times \ln(4) \\ q_L &= 0.287 \times 263 \times 1.3863 \\ q_L &= 104.61 \text{ kJ/kg} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Net work required per kg of air (2.5 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} w_{net} &= R \cdot (T_H - T_L) \cdot \ln(P_2/P_1) \\ w_{net} &= 0.287 \times 40 \times 1.3863 \\ w_{net} &= 15.91 \text{ kJ/kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Verification:** $$ \text{C.O.P.} = \frac{q_L}{w_{net}} = \frac{104.61}{15.91} = 6.575 \quad \checkmark $$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > **B. (a)** With the help of neat P-V and T-S diagrams, explain the working of an air refrigerator on the Reversed Carnot Cycle. List any two practical limitations of this cycle. **(7 marks)** > **B. (b)** A Reversed Carnot Cycle air refrigerator is used to maintain a space at **−5°C**. The ambient temperature is **35°C**. If the refrigerating capacity required is **5 tonnes of refrigeration (1 TR = 3.517 kW)**, determine: (i) C.O.P., (ii) Power required to drive the compressor, and (iii) Heat rejected to the atmosphere per hour. **(7 marks)** ### **Model Solution:** #### Part B(a) — Working of Air Refrigerator on Reversed Carnot Cycle (7 marks) **P-V and T-S diagrams:** (Refer to Section 4 of these notes for schematic representations.) **(3 marks)** **Working Principle (3 marks):** The cycle consists of four reversible processes: 1. **Process 1–2:** Air at $T_L$ is isentropically compressed to $T_H$ in the compressor. Work is done on the air, raising its temperature. 2. **Process 2–3:** The hot, high-pressure air rejects heat $Q_H$ to the atmosphere at constant temperature $T_H$ in the cooled heat exchanger. 3. **Process 3–4:** The high-pressure air expands isentropically in the expander, dropping its temperature back to $T_L$. 4. **Process 4–1:** The cold, low-pressure air absorbs heat $Q_L$ from the refrigerated space at constant temperature $T_L$, providing the cooling effect. **Practical Limitations (1 mark):** 1. The isothermal processes require the air to be compressed/expanded at **infinitely slow rates** with **perfect heat exchange** — impossible in practice. 2. The isentropic processes require **no friction and perfect insulation** — also impractical; real compressors have mechanical friction and heat leaks. #### Part B(b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks) **Given data:** - $T_L = -5°C = 268$ K - $T_H = 35°C = 308$ K - Refrigerating capacity $Q_L = 5$ TR = $5 \times 3.517 = 17.585$ kW **(i) C.O.P. (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{C.O.P.} &= \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{268}{308 - 268} = \frac{268}{40} \\ \text{C.O.P.} &= 6.70 \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Power required to drive the compressor (2.5 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} W_{in} &= \frac{Q_L}{\text{C.O.P.}} = \frac{17.585}{6.70} \\ W_{in} &= 2.625 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Heat rejected to atmosphere per hour (2.5 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_H &= Q_L + W_{in} = 17.585 + 2.625 = 20.21 \text{ kW} \\ Q_{H,\,per\,hour} &= 20.21 \times 3600 = 72,756 \text{ kJ/h} \\ Q_{H,\,per\,hour} &= 72.756 \text{ MJ/h} \end{aligned} $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to K.** A very common 1-mark deduction. $T(K) = T(°C) + 273$. Always work in Kelvin. > 2. **Mixing up $C_p$ and $R$ formulas.** $C_p$ is used for isentropic work; $R$ is used for isothermal work. > 3. **Assuming compressor work = expander work = net work.** This is a classic trap. The net work is the difference in the **isothermal** heats, not the isentropic works. > 4. **Using $\ln(P_2/P_1)$ and forgetting the value of $\ln(4) = 1.3863$.** Memorize this value. > 5. **Saying "Reversed Carnot Cycle is impossible, so it's useless."** It is the **theoretical benchmark** — extremely important for comparison. --- ## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **Before stepping into the KTU exam hall, ensure you can confidently tick every box below:** - [ ] **Reversed Carnot Cycle** has 2 isothermal + 2 isentropic processes, executed in reverse. - [ ] **COP formula** (must be memorized): $\text{COP} = T_L / (T_H - T_L)$ — **temperatures in KELVIN!** - [ ] **Refrigerating effect per kg:** $q_L = R \cdot T_L \cdot \ln(P_2/P_1)$ - [ ] **Net work per kg:** $w_{net} = R \cdot (T_H - T_L) \cdot \ln(P_2/P_1)$ - [ ] **Isentropic relation:** $P_2/P_1 = (T_H/T_L)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$ - [ ] **Air properties:** $C_p = 1.005$, $C_v = 0.718$, $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K. - [ ] **Practical limitations:** Isothermal processes need infinite time + perfect HX; isentropic processes need no friction + perfect insulation. - [ ] **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 210 kJ/min** (conversion to remember). - [ ] **Why air?** Non-toxic, non-flammable, freely available, low boiling point, no condensation issues. - [ ] **Applications:** Aircraft cooling, gas liquefaction, cold storage, cryogenics. - [ ] The Reversed Carnot Cycle is the **theoretical maximum** — no real cycle can exceed its COP. - [ ] The T-S diagram is a **perfect rectangle**; the P-V diagram is a curved quadrilateral. - [ ] **First Law application:** For a cyclic device, $W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$. - [ ] **Second Law insight:** A larger temperature lift $(T_H - T_L)$ ⇒ smaller COP ⇒ more work for same cooling.

topics.title: Air Refrigerators Working on Bell Coleman Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Air Refrigerators Working on Bell Coleman Cycle # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Bell-Coleman Cycle (Reverse Brayton / Joule Cycle)**: A gas refrigeration cycle in which **air** is used as the refrigerant. The working fluid (air) undergoes a **reverse thermodynamic cycle** consisting of four internally reversible processes: **isentropic compression**, **isobaric (constant pressure) cooling**, **isentropic expansion**, and **isobaric heat absorption**. The cycle is named after **Lt. Col. John Bell-Coleman** (1876) who proposed its use for shipboard refrigeration and for cooling air supplied to submarines to allow deeper diving. The Bell-Coleman cycle is the **gas counterpart** of the vapor compression refrigeration cycle. Whereas VCRS uses a phase-changing refrigerant, Bell-Coleman uses a **permanent gas (air)** throughout — meaning the working fluid never condenses or evaporates; it only changes temperature and pressure. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: This topic is a **2-mark / short-answer favorite** and also appears as a **14-mark derivation problem** asking for COP expression, work required, and refrigerating effect. Master the four process definitions and the standard assumptions (Air Standard Cycle). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "How Does It Actually Work?" > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Bicycle Pump Principle** > Have you ever felt a bicycle pump get **hot** when you push the handle down, and **cold** when you release it? That is exactly the Bell-Coleman principle! > - **Compressing air** → air heats up (compression process) > - **Heat is removed** by passing through a cooler (cooling process) > - **Expanding air** → air cools down dramatically (expansion process) > - **Cold air absorbs heat** from the space to be refrigerated (refrigeration process) In a Bell-Coleman refrigerator, the air is compressed, its heat is dumped to the outside (or a coolant), then it is expanded through an engine to make it very cold. This cold air is then blown into the cold room where it **picks up heat** from the stored goods before returning to the compressor — completing the cycle. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Constants & Units (KTU Standard):** > - Specific heat of air at constant pressure, **$c_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$** > - Specific heat of air at constant volume, **$c_v = 0.718 \text{ kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$** > - Ratio of specific heats, **$\gamma = c_p/c_v = 1.4$** (for air) > - Gas constant for air, **$R = 0.287 \text{ kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$** ## 1.3 Open vs. Closed Bell-Coleman System > [!NOTE] > **Open System**: The air being cooled in the cold room **exhausts to the atmosphere** after absorbing heat. Fresh air is continuously drawn in, compressed, and recirculated. Used typically in **aircraft air-conditioning**. > **Closed System**: The same air is **re-circulated** continuously in a sealed loop using a separate expansion cylinder. Used in **ship refrigeration** and packaged chillers. Both systems follow the **same thermodynamic cycle** on a P-V and T-S diagram; the only difference is the **physical arrangement** of components. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Processes of the Bell-Coleman Cycle The Bell-Coleman cycle operates on the **Air Standard Assumptions**: 1. Air is the working fluid and behaves as an **ideal gas**. 2. All processes are **internally reversible**. 3. **Specific heats** $c_p$ and $c_v$ are **constant** during each process. 4. The compression and expansion processes are **isentropic** (adiabatic + reversible). 5. The heat rejection and heat addition processes occur at **constant pressure**. ### Process 1 → 2 : Isentropic Compression - Air is compressed from **low pressure $P_1$** to **high pressure $P_2$** in a compressor. - Temperature rises from $T_1$ to $T_2$. - Work is done **on the system** (work input). - For ideal gas isentropic process: $$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$$ ### Process 2 → 3 : Isobaric (Constant Pressure) Cooling - Hot, high-pressure air passes through a **cooler/heat exchanger**. - Heat $Q_R$ is rejected to the cooling medium (atmosphere/cold water). - Temperature drops from $T_2$ to $T_3$ at constant pressure $P_2 = P_3$. - No work is done in this process. ### Process 3 → 4 : Isentropic Expansion - Cooled, high-pressure air expands through an **expansion cylinder / engine**. - Pressure drops from $P_3$ to $P_4$ and temperature falls from $T_3$ to $T_4$. - Work is done **by the system** (work output, often used to drive the compressor). - For isentropic expansion: $$\frac{T_4}{T_3} = \left(\frac{P_4}{P_3}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$$ ### Process 4 → 1 : Isobaric (Constant Pressure) Heat Absorption - Cold, low-pressure air enters the **cold room / refrigerated space**. - It absorbs heat $Q_A$ from the space, raising its temperature from $T_4$ back to $T_1$. - This is the **useful refrigerating effect (RE)**. - Pressure remains constant: $P_4 = P_1$. ## 2.2 Why These Specific Processes? (The "Why" Behind the Design) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Logic Behind Each Process:** > - **Why isentropic compression/expansion?** → Maximises pressure/temperature change with minimum work loss. Real compressors/expanders approximate isentropic behaviour. > - **Why constant pressure heat exchange?** → Easier and cheaper to design heat exchangers (radiators) at constant pressure than at constant volume. > - **Why isentropic expansion (not throttling)?** → In VCRS, a throttling valve is used (irreversible, ~wasted work). In Bell-Coleman, the expansion cylinder **recovers expansion work**, giving a **higher COP** than a purely throttling-based cycle. This is one of its biggest advantages. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Bell-Coleman Cycle — Master Formula Table** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|----------|---------|-------|---------| | 1 | Isentropic Relation (Comp.) | $T_2/T_1 = (P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | K, Pa | $\gamma = 1.4$ for air | | 2 | Isentropic Relation (Exp.) | $T_3/T_4 = (P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | K, Pa | Same pressure ratio $r_p$ | | 3 | Refrigerating Effect (RE) | $RE = c_p(T_1 - T_4)$ | kJ/kg | Heat absorbed in cold room | | 4 | Heat Rejected | $Q_R = c_p(T_2 - T_3)$ | kJ/kg | Heat given to cooling water | | 5 | Net Work of Cycle | $W_{net} = c_p[(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)]$ | kJ/kg | Compressor work − Expander work | | 6 | Net Work (Alternate) | $W_{net} = c_p[(T_2 - T_3) - (T_1 - T_4)]$ | kJ/kg | Rearranged form | | 7 | COP of Bell-Coleman | $COP = \dfrac{RE}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{T_1 - T_4}{(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)}$ | — | Dimensionless | | 8 | COP in terms of $T_1$ & $r_p$ | $COP = \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1}$ | — | Most-used KTU formula | | 9 | Mass of air circulated | $m = Q_E / RE$ | kg/s | $Q_E$ = total cooling load in kW | | 10 | Compressor work | $W_C = m \cdot c_p(T_2 - T_1)$ | kW | Positive input | | 11 | Expander work | $W_E = m \cdot c_p(T_3 - T_4)$ | kW | Recovered output | | 12 | Pressure ratio | $r_p = P_2/P_1 = P_3/P_4$ | — | $r_p > 1$ always | | 13 | Air standard efficiency | $\eta = 1 - 1/r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | — | Similar to Otto cycle | > [!NOTE] > **Formula Tip for KTU**: The most-favoured derivation is **COP $= 1 / (r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1)$** — memorise this. Note that **higher pressure ratio** = **higher temperature after compression** = **more work input** = **LOWER COP**. This is a standard KTU conceptual question. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > **Where is the Bell-Coleman cycle used today?** > 1. **Aircraft air-conditioning systems** — pressurised cabin air is bled from engine compressors, cooled, and expanded to provide cabin cooling. > 2. **Submarine air supply cooling** — historical use; allows the submarine to dive deeper because the air in the boat is cooled before crew consumption. > 3. **Gas turbine inlet cooling** — inlet air is refrigerated using Bell-Coleman to boost power output in hot climates. > 4. **Very low temperature applications** (e.g., **gas liquefaction**) — cascade versions of reversed Brayton cycles are used to reach cryogenic temperatures. > 5. **Mine ventilation cooling** — large-scale underground air cooling in deep mines. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Refrigerating Effect (RE) per kg of Air The refrigerating effect is the heat absorbed by the air in the cold room during the constant-pressure process **4 → 1**. For a constant-pressure process in an ideal gas: $$dQ = c_p \cdot dT$$ Integrating from state 4 to state 1: $$RE = \int_{T_4}^{T_1} c_p \cdot dT = c_p(T_1 - T_4) \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}$$ **Why is this positive?** Because in the cold room the air picks up heat, so $T_1 > T_4$, making $RE > 0$. This is the **useful cooling output** of the cycle. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Heat Rejected in the Cooler (2 → 3) Similarly, in the cooler, the air rejects heat at constant pressure: $$Q_R = c_p(T_2 - T_3) \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}$$ Since the air enters the cooler hot and leaves cooler, $T_2 > T_3$, so $Q_R > 0$ (heat flows out of the air). ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Net Work of the Cycle Applying the **First Law of Thermodynamics** over the complete cycle (steady-flow energy equation): $$\oint dQ = \oint dW$$ The net work is the **algebraic sum** of the two shaft-work processes (compressor and expander), since the two constant-pressure processes involve **no work** ($W = \int V \, dP = 0$ at constant $P$). **Compressor work input (process 1 → 2, isentropic):** $$W_C = c_p(T_2 - T_1) \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}$$ **Expander work output (process 3 → 4, isentropic):** $$W_E = c_p(T_3 - T_4) \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}$$ **Net work done ON the system (i.e. external work that must be supplied):** $$\begin{aligned} W_{net} &= W_C - W_E \\ &= c_p(T_2 - T_1) - c_p(T_3 - T_4) \\ &= c_p\big[(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)\big] \quad \text{[kJ/kg]} \end{aligned}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Interpretation**: $W_C$ is the work required to drive the compressor; $W_E$ is recovered from the expansion cylinder. In a well-designed system, the expander is **coupled to the compressor** to reduce external power consumption. The **net external work** is what is supplied from the prime mover (electric motor or engine). ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — COP in Terms of Pressure Ratio ($r_p$) This is the **most important derivation** in the KTU syllabus and is asked almost every year. **Step 1:** Start from the basic COP definition. $$COP = \frac{RE}{W_{net}} = \frac{c_p(T_1 - T_4)}{c_p\big[(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)\big]}$$ $$COP = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)} \quad \cdots (i)$$ **Step 2:** Apply the isentropic relations. Define the **pressure ratio**: $$r_p = \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{P_3}{P_4}$$ From isentropic compression (1 → 2): $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} \quad \cdots (ii)$$ From isentropic expansion (3 → 4): $$T_4 = T_3 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{r_p}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = \frac{T_3}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}} \quad \cdots (iii)$$ **Step 3:** Use the constant-pressure processes to relate $T_3$ to $T_1$. Since cooling is isobaric from 2 to 3, and the expansion drops temperature, but to obtain a **fully pressure-ratio-only expression**, we make the **classical KTU assumption** that the **compressor inlet temperature equals the cold room temperature**, i.e., $T_1$ is the cold air temperature entering the cold room, and we re-define the cycle such that the **expander outlet temperature $T_4$ is reached by expanding from $T_1$** (i.e. $T_3 = T_1$). This assumption gives a clean expression. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard KTU Convention**: In the simplified cycle used in the KTU textbook derivation, $T_3$ is assumed equal to $T_1$ (i.e., the cooler brings the compressed air back to the same temperature as the cold room). This is a textbook idealisation; the air is then expanded isentropically from $T_1$ to $T_4$, giving $T_4 < T_1$. With this assumption $T_3 = T_1$, and from isentropic expansion: $$T_4 = T_1 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{r_p}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = \frac{T_1}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}}$$ **Step 4:** Substitute into Equation (i). **Numerator:** $$T_1 - T_4 = T_1 - \frac{T_1}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}} = T_1 \left[1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1)}{\gamma}}}\right]$$ **Denominator:** With $T_3 = T_1$, and using (ii) and (iii): $$\begin{aligned} (T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4) &= T_1 r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - T_1 - T_1 + \frac{T_1}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}} \\ &= T_1 \left[r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} + \frac{1}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}} - 2\right] \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5:** Now use the **algebraic identity**: $$a + \frac{1}{a} - 2 = \frac{(a-1)^2}{a}, \quad \text{where } a = r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$$ So the denominator becomes: $$T_1 \cdot \frac{\left(r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right)^2}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}}$$ **Step 6:** Form the COP ratio. $$COP = \frac{T_1 \left[1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}}\right]}{T_1 \cdot \dfrac{\left(r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right)^2}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}}}$$ The numerator factorises as: $$1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}} = \frac{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}}$$ **Step 7:** Therefore: $$COP = \frac{\dfrac{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}}}{\dfrac{\left(r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right)^2}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}}} = \frac{1}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1}$$ $$\boxed{\;COP_{\text{Bell-Coleman}} = \frac{1}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1}\;}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Observation**: This COP expression is **independent of temperature**! It depends only on the **pressure ratio** $r_p$ and the working fluid (via $\gamma$). Therefore, **increasing $r_p$ decreases COP** — the opposite of intuition, but true because higher $r_p$ demands disproportionately more compression work. ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Work Required for Given Cooling Load **Given:** Total cooling load in the cold room $= \dot{Q}_E$ (kW), with cold room air entering at $T_1$ and leaving at $T_4$. **Step 1:** Find the mass flow rate of air. $$\dot{m} = \frac{\dot{Q}_E}{RE} = \frac{\dot{Q}_E}{c_p(T_1 - T_4)} \quad \text{[kg/s]}$$ **Step 2:** Find the compressor work. $$\dot{W}_C = \dot{m} \cdot c_p(T_2 - T_1) \quad \text{[kW]}$$ **Step 3:** Find the expander work (recovered). $$\dot{W}_E = \dot{m} \cdot c_p(T_3 - T_4) \quad \text{[kW]}$$ **Step 4:** Net external work. $$\dot{W}_{net} = \dot{W}_C - \dot{W}_E \quad \text{[kW]}$$ ## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern) > [!NOTE] > **Sample Problem (Internal Assessment Pattern):** > A Bell-Coleman refrigerator operates between pressure limits of **1 bar** and **4 bar**. The temperature of air entering the compressor is **17°C (290 K)**. The air is cooled to **27°C (300 K)** in the cooler. Assuming isentropic compression and expansion with $\gamma = 1.4$, calculate: > (a) The temperature after compression and expansion > (b) The COP of the cycle > (c) The work required per tonne of refrigeration **Given:** $P_1 = 1 \text{ bar}, \; P_2 = 4 \text{ bar}, \; T_1 = 290 \text{ K}, \; T_3 = 300 \text{ K}, \; r_p = 4, \; \gamma = 1.4$ ### (a) Temperatures Pressure ratio exponent: $$\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma} = \frac{0.4}{1.4} = 0.2857$$ Compression ratio factor: $$r_p^{0.2857} = 4^{0.2857} = 1.486$$ Temperature after compression: $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{0.2857} = 290 \times 1.486 = 430.97 \text{ K} \approx 431 \text{ K}$$ Temperature after expansion: $$T_4 = T_3 / r_p^{0.2857} = 300 / 1.486 = 201.88 \text{ K} \approx 202 \text{ K}$$ ### (b) COP Using the standard formula: $$COP = \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1} = \frac{1}{1.486 - 1} = \frac{1}{0.486} = 2.058$$ ### (c) Work per Tonne of Refrigeration 1 TR = 3.517 kW of cooling. Refrigerating effect per kg: $$RE = c_p(T_1 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (290 - 201.88) = 1.005 \times 88.12 = 88.56 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Mass flow rate for 1 TR: $$\dot{m} = \frac{3.517}{88.56} = 0.0397 \text{ kg/s}$$ Net work per kg (using COP): $$W_{net} = \frac{RE}{COP} = \frac{88.56}{2.058} = 43.03 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Power required for 1 TR: $$\dot{W}_{net} = 0.0397 \times 43.03 = 1.708 \text{ kW per TR}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Sanity Check**: COP ≈ 2.0 is typical for Bell-Coleman cycles at moderate pressure ratios. Note that **vapor compression refrigerators can achieve COP of 3–6** in the same temperature range — this is why VCRS dominates domestic refrigeration while Bell-Coleman is reserved for special applications (aircraft, gas liquefaction). # 4. Compresses air from $P_1$ to $P_2$. > 2. **Cooler (Intercooler/Aftercooler)**: Shell-and-tube or finned heat exchanger using cooling water or atmospheric air to cool compressed air. > 3. **Expander**: Either a **reciprocating expansion cylinder** (work recovered) or a **turbine** for large systems. Could be replaced by a throttling valve in cheaper systems (but this lowers COP). > 4. **Cold Room / Refrigerated Space**: Insulated enclosure where cold expanded air absorbs heat from the goods. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Explain the four processes of a Bell-Coleman cycle on a P-V and T-S diagram.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Bell-Coleman cycle consists of four internally reversible processes using air as the working fluid: 1. **Process 1 → 2 (Isentropic Compression)**: Air is compressed from low pressure $P_1$ to high pressure $P_2$ in a compressor. Temperature rises from $T_1$ to $T_2$. On P-V diagram, this is a steep curve; on T-S diagram, it is a **vertical line** (entropy constant). 2. **Process 2 → 3 (Isobaric Cooling)**: Compressed hot air is cooled in a heat exchanger at constant pressure $P_2 = P_3$. Temperature drops from $T_2$ to $T_3$. On P-V diagram, this is a **horizontal line** (constant P); on T-S diagram, it is a falling curve (heat rejection). 3. **Process 3 → 4 (Isentropic Expansion)**: Cooled high-pressure air expands in an expander. Pressure falls from $P_3$ to $P_4$; temperature drops from $T_3$ to $T_4$. Vertical line on T-S diagram. 4. **Process 4 → 1 (Isobaric Heat Absorption)**: Cold low-pressure air absorbs heat from the refrigerated space at constant pressure $P_4 = P_1$. Temperature rises from $T_4$ to $T_1$, completing the cycle. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **State the assumptions made in the air-standard analysis of a Bell-Coleman cycle.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The air-standard assumptions for a Bell-Coleman cycle are: 1. The working fluid is **air**, which behaves as an **ideal gas** with constant specific heats $c_p$ and $c_v$. 2. The **compression (1→2) and expansion (3→4) processes are isentropic** (reversible adiabatic). 3. The **heat addition (4→1) and heat rejection (2→3) processes occur at constant pressure**. 4. The cycle is **internally reversible** and there are no pressure drops in the heat exchangers. 5. Kinetic and potential energy changes of the air are **negligible**. 6. The system is **closed** (mass of air is constant), unless specifically an open system. --- ## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` ### Question A **(a)** Derive the expression for the **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** of a Bell-Coleman cycle in terms of the **pressure ratio** $r_p$ and the ratio of specific heats $\gamma$. (7 marks) **(b)** A Bell-Coleman refrigerator operates between **1.0 bar** and **5.0 bar**. The air enters the compressor at **5°C**. After compression, it is cooled in the cooler to **35°C**. Assuming isentropic compression and expansion with $\gamma = 1.4$ for air, calculate the **COP of the cycle** and the **work required per kg of air circulated**. (7 marks) ### Solution to Part (a) **Step 1 — COP Definition:** $$COP = \frac{RE}{W_{net}} = \frac{c_p(T_1 - T_4)}{c_p(T_2 - T_1) - c_p(T_3 - T_4)} = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)}$$ **Step 2 — Isentropic Relations:** With $r_p = P_2/P_1 = P_3/P_4$: - $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ - $T_4 = T_3 \cdot (1/r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ **Step 3 — Simplification:** Assuming $T_3 = T_1$ (idealised cooling to cold room temperature): - $T_4 = T_1 / r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ - $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ **Step 4 — Substitution and simplification** (as shown in Section 3.4): $$\boxed{COP = \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1}}$$ ### Solution to Part (b) **Given:** - $P_1 = 1.0$ bar, $P_2 = 5.0$ bar → $r_p = 5$ - $T_1 = 5 + 273 = 278$ K - $T_3 = 35 + 273 = 308$ K - $\gamma = 1.4$ **Calculate pressure ratio exponent:** $$\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma} = 0.2857, \quad r_p^{0.2857} = 5^{0.2857} = 1.5838$$ **Temperature after compression:** $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{0.2857} = 278 \times 1.5838 = 440.3 \text{ K}$$ **Temperature after expansion (using $T_3$):** $$T_4 = T_3 / r_p^{0.2857} = 308 / 1.5838 = 194.5 \text{ K}$$ **Refrigerating Effect:** $$RE = c_p(T_1 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (278 - 194.5) = 1.005 \times 83.5 = 83.92 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Net Work per kg:** $$W_{net} = c_p(T_2 - T_1) - c_p(T_3 - T_4)$$ $$W_{net} = 1.005 \times (440.3 - 278) - 1.005 \times (308 - 194.5)$$ $$W_{net} = 1.005 \times 162.3 - 1.005 \times 113.5 = 163.11 - 114.07 = 49.04 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **COP:** $$COP = \frac{RE}{W_{net}} = \frac{83.92}{49.04} = 1.711$$ **Cross-verification with formula:** $$COP = \frac{1}{r_p^{0.2857} - 1} = \frac{1}{1.5838 - 1} = \frac{1}{0.5838} = 1.713 \checkmark$$ The slight difference (1.711 vs 1.713) is due to rounding. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to Kelvin** — losing 1 mark instantly. > 2. **Using $c_v$ instead of $c_p$** for the constant-pressure processes — the heat transfer in 2→3 and 4→1 occurs at constant pressure, so $c_p$ must be used. > 3. **Confusing expansion direction** — students write $T_4 = T_3 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ (wrong!). Correct relation is $T_4 = T_3 \cdot (1/r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ because pressure **drops** during expansion. > 4. **Not specifying the working fluid / assumptions** — in 14-mark problems, the examiner expects you to **state the air-standard assumptions** before deriving. Missing this loses 1–2 marks. --- ## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` ### Question B **(a)** With the help of a **schematic diagram** and **P-V & T-S diagrams**, explain the working of a **closed Bell-Coleman air refrigeration system**. (7 marks) **(b)** An aircraft using a Bell-Coleman refrigeration cycle takes in air at **0.9 bar** and **−5°C**. The air is compressed to **4 bar** and then cooled to **25°C** in the cooler. The expanded air leaves the cold cabin at **20°C**. Calculate: (i) the **refrigerating effect per kg** of air, (ii) the **compressor work** per kg, (iii) the **expander work** per kg, and (iv) the **net COP** of the cycle. Take $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K and $\gamma = 1.4$. (7 marks) ### Solution to Part (a) **Working Principle:** In a closed Bell-Coleman refrigeration system, a fixed mass of air continuously circulates through four components. The schematic is: 1. **Compressor**: Air is drawn in from the cold room (state 1: $P_1, T_1$) and compressed isentropically to high pressure (state 2: $P_2, T_2$). 2. **Cooler**: The hot compressed air passes through a heat exchanger where it rejects heat to the atmosphere or cooling water, cooling from $T_2$ to $T_3$ at constant pressure. 3. **Expander**: The cooled high-pressure air is expanded isentropically in an expansion engine, dropping both pressure and temperature to state 4 ($P_4, T_4$). 4. **Cold Room**: The very cold air enters the refrigerated space, absorbs heat from the goods at constant pressure $P_4$, and returns to state 1. **Diagrams:** (As shown in Section 4 — P-V curve is a closed loop with two isentropics and two isobars; T-S diagram is rectangular-ish, with two verticals for the isentropics and two horizontal-ish lines for the isobars). **Key Advantage**: The expander recovers work, which is fed back to the compressor shaft, reducing the net external power required. ### Solution to Part (b) **Given:** - $P_1 = 0.9$ bar, $P_2 = 4$ bar → $r_p = 4/0.9 = 4.444$ - $T_1 = -5 + 273 = 268$ K - $T_3 = 25 + 273 = 298$ K - $T_1' = 20 + 273 = 293$ K (air leaving cold cabin — this is the new $T_1$ entering compressor) - $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $\gamma = 1.4$ **Step 1 — Calculate pressure ratio exponent:** $$\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma} = 0.2857, \quad r_p^{0.2857} = 4.444^{0.2857} = 1.546$$ **Step 2 — Temperatures:** - $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{0.2857} = 268 \times 1.546 = 414.3$ K - $T_4 = T_3 / r_p^{0.2857} = 298 / 1.546 = 192.8$ K **Step 3 — Refrigerating Effect per kg:** $$RE = c_p(T_1 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (268 - 192.8) = 1.005 \times 75.2 = 75.58 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 4 — Compressor Work per kg:** $$W_C = c_p(T_2 - T_1) = 1.005 \times (414.3 - 268) = 1.005 \times 146.3 = 147.03 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 5 — Expander Work per kg:** $$W_E = c_p(T_3 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (298 - 192.8) = 1.005 \times 105.2 = 105.73 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 6 — Net Work:** $$W_{net} = W_C - W_E = 147.03 - 105.73 = 41.30 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 7 — COP:** $$COP = \frac{RE}{W_{net}} = \frac{75.58}{41.30} = 1.830$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall for Part (b)**: > 1. The problem says "leaves the cold cabin at 20°C" — this is the air **going back to the compressor** (i.e., the actual $T_1$ entering the compressor is 293 K, not 268 K). Students often confuse this and use the wrong inlet temperature. **Read the problem statement carefully!** > 2. Some students compute $W_{net}$ as $W_C + W_E$ (added) instead of subtracted. Remember: expander gives work **out**, compressor takes work **in**, so we **subtract**. > 3. **Don't forget units** in the final answer — kJ/kg, dimensionless for COP. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Bell-Coleman Cycle — Last-Minute Revision Checklist** > > **🔑 Must-Know Definitions:** > - Bell-Coleman = Reverse Brayton cycle using **air** as refrigerant > - Four processes: isentropic compression → isobaric cooling → isentropic expansion → isobaric heating > - Closed vs. Open system distinction (re-circulation vs. exhaust) > > **🔑 Must-Know Formulas (memorise these!):** > - Isentropic relation: $T_2/T_1 = (P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ > - RE per kg: $c_p(T_1 - T_4)$ > - Compressor work: $c_p(T_2 - T_1)$ > - Expander work: $c_p(T_3 - T_4)$ > - Net work: $c_p[(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)]$ > - **COP: $1/[r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1]$** ⭐ (most important!) > > **🔑 Key Numerical Values:** > - $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K > - 1 TR = 3.517 kW > > **🔑 Conceptual High-Yield Points:** > - COP **decreases** as pressure ratio **increases** (non-intuitive but true) > - COP is **independent of temperature** (depends only on $r_p$ and $\gamma$) > - COP of Bell-Coleman (≈ 1–2) is **lower** than VCRS (≈ 3–6) → so Bell-Coleman is used only in special applications > - **Expansion engine** is a key advantage (work recovery) over simple throttling > - Air never changes phase → no evaporator/condenser in classical sense > > **🔑 Common Applications:** > - Aircraft cabin air-conditioning > - Submarine air cooling > - Gas liquefaction (cascade reversed Brayton) > - Mine ventilation cooling > > **🔑 Examiner's Favourite Questions:** > - "Derive COP of Bell-Coleman cycle in terms of $r_p$" (7 marks — most-asked!) > - "Sketch P-V and T-S diagrams and label all four states" (3–7 marks) > - "State air-standard assumptions" (3 marks) > - "Calculate work required for X TR cooling load" (7 marks) > - "Compare Bell-Coleman with Vapour Compression Cycle" (7 marks)

topics.title: Reversed Brayton or Joule or Bell Coleman Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reversed Brayton or Joule or Bell Coleman Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Reversed Brayton Cycle** (also universally known as the **Joule Cycle** or the **Bell-Coleman Cycle**) is the fundamental thermodynamic cycle upon which **gas (air) refrigeration systems** operate. It is the thermodynamic inverse of the Brayton power cycle. In this closed/regenerative loop, a working fluid — almost always **air** — is compressed, cooled, expanded, and finally allowed to absorb heat from the cold space to be refrigerated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The Reversed Brayton Cycle is a **core derivation topic**. Students must be able to draw the P-v and T-s diagrams, identify all four processes, and derive the **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** using standard air-standard assumptions (typically $C_p$ = **1.005 kJ/kg·K** and $\gamma$ = **1.4**).

### Cycle Identification & Nomenclature

| Cycle Name | Common Usage | Working Fluid |
|---|---|---|
| **Reversed Brayton Cycle** | Engineering Thermodynamics (universal) | Any ideal gas |
| **Reversed Joule Cycle** | European / British textbooks | Any ideal gas |
| **Bell-Coleman Cycle** | Original patent (1857, Dr. Alexander C. Kirk) — used in aircraft refrigeration | Atmospheric Air |

The Bell-Coleman cycle was the **first practical air refrigeration machine**, originally proposed for preserving food on long sea voyages. Today, it is the foundational cycle for **aircraft air-conditioning and pressurization systems**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bellows and the Breeze"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy — Think of a Bicycle Pump Working Backwards**

Imagine you are cooling a small room using a mechanical bellows:

1. **Squeezing (Compression):** You rapidly compress air in a cylinder. The air gets **hot** (this is exactly what happens when you pump a bicycle tire and the pump barrel feels warm).
2. **Cooling:** You pass this hot, compressed air through a radiator (heat exchanger) so it loses heat to the atmosphere and returns to room temperature.
3. **Expansion (Reverse Squeezing):** You let this cooled, compressed air expand in another cylinder. As it expands, it becomes **very cold** — colder than the room you want to chill.
4. **Cold Absorption:** This chilled air is blown into the room. It absorbs heat from the room, providing refrigeration. The warmed air returns to the compressor to repeat the cycle.

This is precisely the **Reversed Brayton Cycle** — air is the *refrigerant*, and the cold produced by expansion is what chills the space.

### Why Air and Not a Refrigerant Liquid?

In Vapor Compression Refrigeration (VCR), the refrigerant **changes phase** (liquid → vapor) to absorb latent heat. In the Reversed Brayton Cycle, the working fluid (air) **remains in the gaseous phase** throughout the entire cycle. It relies on the **sensible cooling effect** of expanding gas rather than phase change.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** Because air has a very low specific heat capacity and no latent heat absorption in the cycle, the **mass flow rate of air required is enormous** (often 20–50 times the mass flow rate of refrigerant in a VCR plant of equal capacity). This is why Bell-Coleman plants are bulky and used mainly in **aircraft**, where weight per kW cooling is less critical than the elimination of condensers/evaporators.

---

## 1.3 Schematic Block Diagram of an Air Refrigeration Plant

The actual hardware realization consists of **four essential components**:

1. **Compressor** — raises the pressure of air (input: work $W_C$).
2. **Cooling / After-Cooler (R-H, Rejector Heat Exchanger)** — rejects heat to the atmosphere.
3. **Expander** — produces the cooling effect (output: work $W_E$).
4. **Refrigerator / Cold Chamber (Load)** — the cold air absorbs heat $Q_A$ from the space to be cooled.

> [!NOTE]
> **Schematic Flow:** Cold Space → (warm air returned at low P) → **Compressor** → (high P, hot) → **After-Cooler** → (high P, cool) → **Expander** → (low P, very cold) → **Cold Space** (refrigerating effect produced)

The **net work input** is the difference between compressor work and expander work, since the expander recovers a portion of the compression energy.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Processes of the Ideal Reversed Brayton Cycle

The cycle consists of **four internally reversible processes** when ideal:

| Process | Path | Thermodynamic Type | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1 → 2** | Compression | **Isentropic (Reversible Adiabatic)** | Air compressed in compressor from low pressure $P_1$ to high pressure $P_2$. Temperature rises from $T_1$ to $T_2$. |
| **2 → 3** | Cooling | **Isobaric (Constant Pressure)** | Hot, high-pressure air passes through the after-cooler / heat exchanger, rejecting heat $Q_{2-3}$ to the surroundings at constant pressure $P_2 = P_3$. |
| **3 → 4** | Expansion | **Isentropic (Reversible Adiabatic)** | Cool, high-pressure air expands in the expander from $P_3$ to $P_1$. Temperature drops from $T_3$ to $T_4$. |
| **4 → 1** | Heating / Refrigeration | **Isobaric (Constant Pressure)** | Cold, low-pressure air passes through the cold space, **absorbing heat $Q_{4-1}$ (Refrigerating Effect)** at constant pressure $P_4 = P_1$. |

### Process-by-Process Logic Explanation

- **Process 1→2 (Isentropic Compression):** Energy is added as work. Both temperature and pressure rise. The compression is rapid enough that there is no time for heat transfer with surroundings.
  - **Why isentropic?** The compressor is modeled as ideal (no friction, no clearance, perfect insulation).
- **Process 2→3 (Isobaric Heat Rejection):** The hot compressed air flows through fins/tubes exposed to ambient air or water. Heat flows out, temperature drops, but pressure remains constant (open flow system assumption, or frictionless duct).
- **Process 3→4 (Isentropic Expansion):** The cooled, high-pressure air expands in an expander (could be a turbine or piston-expander). Work is extracted, temperature plummets.
  - **Why is this where the cooling happens?** Expansion drops the temperature *below* the cold space temperature, so when this air enters the cold space, it can absorb heat.
- **Process 4→1 (Isobaric Heat Absorption):** Cold air circulates through the cold chamber, picks up heat, and exits warmer, ready to be re-compressed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Sign Convention:** In a refrigeration cycle, we treat heat *absorbed* by the refrigerant as **positive** (Refrigerating Effect, $RE$), and heat *rejected* as **negative** (or shown as heat out). Work *in* (compressor) is positive, work *out* (expander) is negative.

---

## 2.2 Air-Standard Assumptions

For KTU derivations, we adopt the **Air-Standard Cycle** assumptions:

1. Air behaves as an **ideal gas** throughout the cycle: $P v = R T$.
2. **Specific heats** $C_p$ and $C_v$ are **constant** during the cycle (cold air-standard analysis). Standard values:
   - $C_p$ = **1.005 kJ/kg·K**
   - $C_v$ = **0.718 kJ/kg·K**
   - $R$ = **0.287 kJ/kg·K**
   - $\gamma = C_p / C_v$ = **1.4**
3. All processes are **internally reversible**; compression and expansion are isentropic.
4. Pressure drops in the heat exchangers (after-cooler and refrigerator) are **neglected** (ideal case).
5. Kinetic and potential energy changes of the air are **negligible** (steady-flow, low velocity).
6. The cycle is a **closed loop** (air recirculates); there is no phase change.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master this table — it covers ~80% of all numerical questions in the KTU University Exam on this topic.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Isentropic relation (1→2) | $\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$ | – | Temperature rise in compressor |
| 2 | Isentropic relation (3→4) | $\dfrac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\dfrac{P_3}{P_4}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$ | – | Temperature drop in expander |
| 3 | Pressure ratio | $r_p = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{P_3}{P_4}$ | – | Common ratio in cycle |
| 4 | Compressor work (per kg) | $W_C = C_p (T_2 - T_1)$ | kJ/kg | Work input to compressor |
| 5 | Expander work (per kg) | $W_E = C_p (T_3 - T_4)$ | kJ/kg | Work recovered from expander |
| 6 | Net work input (per kg) | $W_{net} = W_C - W_E = C_p[(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)]$ | kJ/kg | Actual work supplied |
| 7 | Refrigerating Effect (per kg) | $RE = C_p (T_1 - T_4)$ | kJ/kg | Heat absorbed in cold space |
| 8 | Heat rejected (per kg) | $Q_{rej} = C_p (T_2 - T_3)$ | kJ/kg | Heat given to after-cooler |
| 9 | **COP (Ideal Reversed Brayton)** | $\text{COP} = \dfrac{RE}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{T_1 - T_4}{(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)}$ | – | Primary exam quantity |
| 10 | COP in terms of $T_1, T_3, r_p$ | $\text{COP} = \dfrac{1}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1}$ | – | The famous closed form |
| 11 | COP of ideal Reversed Carnot (reference) | $\text{COP}_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_1}{T_2 - T_1}$ | – | Theoretical maximum |
| 12 | Mass flow rate of air | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\text{Cooling Load (kW)}}{RE \text{ (kJ/kg)}}$ | kg/s | Required air flow |
| 13 | Power input to plant | $P = \dot{m} \times W_{net}$ | kW | Actual plant input |
| 14 | Volumetric flow rate | $\dot{V} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot R \cdot T_1}{P_1}$ | m³/s | Air displaced per second |

### The Most Important Formula — Derivation Highlight

The celebrated result

$$\text{COP} = \frac{1}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1}$$

shows that the COP of the reversed Brayton cycle **depends only on the pressure ratio** $r_p$ — *not* on the absolute temperature. This is a stunning result that the KTU examiner often tests.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Relationship:** As $r_p \to 1$, COP $\to \infty$ (no compression, no cooling effect — degenerate). As $r_p$ increases, COP **decreases**. There is a *trade-off* between high pressure ratio (low temperatures achievable in the cold space) and high COP (efficient operation). The KTU exam frequently asks: *"For a given cold space temperature $T_1$ and ambient $T_3$, find the optimum pressure ratio that maximizes COP."* — The answer is $r_p = (T_3/T_1)^{\gamma/2(\gamma-1)}$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aviation/Aircraft Refrigeration:** Modern jet aircraft use this cycle (or its bootstrap variant) for cabin air conditioning. The compressed bleed air from the jet engine is the compressor output, and a turbine expander provides the cold air.
- **Gas Liquefaction:** Modified versions (with regenerative cooling) are used to liquefy gases like nitrogen, oxygen, and natural gas (Linde-Hampson process, Claude cycle).
- **High-Temperature Refrigeration:** Wherever very low temperatures are needed and refrigerants are unsuitable (cryogenics).
- **Pressurized Cabins:** Aircraft cabin pressurization is a direct byproduct of compression in this cycle.
- **Food Preservation (Historical):** Bell & Coleman's original 1857 patent was for ship-board refrigeration to transport frozen meat from Australia/New Zealand to Britain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not use this cycle for home AC?** The required mass flow rate of air is huge (since $C_p$ for air is small compared to latent heat of vaporization of refrigerants). A typical 1 TR (3.516 kW) domestic AC would need over **2000 m³ of air per minute** — impractical for a home. Hence, VCR dominates household and commercial refrigeration.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 P-v and T-s Diagrams of the Ideal Reversed Brayton Cycle

Before deriving, let us anchor the four states on the two fundamental property diagrams.

### P-v Diagram
- **Process 1→2:** Isentropic compression — curve rises steeply (from right to left, lower v).
- **Process 2→3:** Isobaric cooling — horizontal line moving left (v decreases as T drops at constant P).
- **Process 3→4:** Isentropic expansion — curve drops (from left to right, v increases).
- **Process 4→1:** Isobaric heating (refrigerating effect) — horizontal line moving right (v increases as T rises at constant P).

### T-s Diagram
- **Process 1→2:** Isentropic compression — vertical line going **up** (entropy constant, T rises).
- **Process 2→3:** Isobaric cooling — line sloping **down-right** (heat rejected).
- **Process 3→4:** Isentropic expansion — vertical line going **down** (entropy constant, T falls).
- **Process 4→1:** Isobaric heating — line sloping **up-right** (heat absorbed; this is the **refrigerating effect**).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Compressor Work ($W_C$)

Applying the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** to an adiabatic, reversible (isentropic) compressor with negligible KE and PE changes:

$$h_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + gz_1 + q = h_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + gz_2 + w$$

Neglecting KE, PE, and heat transfer ($q = 0$):

$$w_C = h_1 - h_2 = -(h_2 - h_1)$$

The **work done ON the air** is the negative of work done BY the air. Per unit mass:

$$w_C = h_2 - h_1 = C_p (T_2 - T_1) \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}$$

Using the isentropic relation for an ideal gas:

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$$

Therefore:

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$$

And the compressor work simplifies to:

$$w_C = C_p T_1 \left( r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right)$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Expander Work ($W_E$)

The expander (turbine or reciprocating expansion engine) extracts work from the high-pressure, low-temperature air. Applying SFEE similarly:

$$w_E = h_3 - h_4 = C_p (T_3 - T_4)$$

Using the isentropic relation:

$$T_4 = \frac{T_3}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}}$$

So:

$$w_E = C_p T_3 \left( 1 - r_p^{-\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} \right) = C_p (T_3 - T_4)$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Net Work ($W_{net}$)

By **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to the cyclic closed loop (state function: net change in internal energy = 0):

$$W_{net} = W_C - W_E = C_p \left[ (T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4) \right]$$

Substituting the isentropic relations $T_2 = T_1 r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ and $T_4 = T_3 / r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$:

Let $x = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$. Then $T_2 = T_1 x$ and $T_4 = T_3 / x$.

$$W_{net} = C_p \left[ (T_1 x - T_1) - \left(T_3 - \frac{T_3}{x}\right) \right]$$

$$W_{net} = C_p \left[ T_1(x - 1) - T_3 \cdot \frac{x - 1}{x} \right]$$

$$W_{net} = C_p (x - 1) \left[ T_1 - \frac{T_3}{x} \right]$$

$$W_{net} = C_p (x - 1) \left[ \frac{T_1 x - T_3}{x} \right]$$

Since $x = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ and $\frac{x - 1}{x} = 1 - \frac{1}{x}$:

$$W_{net} = C_p \left[ T_1 \left(r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right) - T_3 \left(1 - r_p^{-\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}\right) \right]$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation of Refrigerating Effect (RE)

The refrigerating effect is the heat absorbed by the air in the cold space (process 4→1) at constant pressure $P_1$:

$$RE = h_1 - h_4 = C_p (T_1 - T_4)$$

Since $T_4 = T_3 / r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$:

$$RE = C_p \left( T_1 - \frac{T_3}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}} \right)$$

---

## 3.6 Derivation of COP

The **Coefficient of Performance** is defined as:

$$\text{COP} = \frac{\text{Desired Effect (RE)}}{\text{Work Input (}W_{net}\text{)}}$$

$$\text{COP} = \frac{C_p (T_1 - T_4)}{C_p \left[ (T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4) \right]}$$

$$\text{COP} = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)}$$

**Substituting the closed-form expressions** in terms of $T_1, T_3, r_p$:

$$\text{COP} = \frac{T_1 - T_3 / r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}}{T_1 \left( r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right) - T_3 \left( 1 - r_p^{-\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} \right)}$$

Factoring $r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ in the numerator:

$$\text{COP} = \frac{\frac{T_1 r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - T_3}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}}}{\frac{T_1 \left( r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right) r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - T_3 \left( r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right)}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}}}$$

The denominators simplify, and remarkably:

$$\boxed{\text{COP}_{RB} = \frac{1}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1}}$$

This is the **single most important KTU derivation outcome** for this topic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special Case Analysis (KTU 2024):**
> If we also set $T_1 = T_3$ (cold space and ambient at the same temperature, e.g., $T_1 = T_3 = T$), the same formula holds and represents the *maximum possible COP* for that pressure ratio. This special case is often asked to compare with Carnot COP.

---

## 3.7 Optimum Pressure Ratio for Maximum COP

For a **given** cold space temperature $T_1$ and ambient (heat rejection) temperature $T_3$, the COP is:

$$\text{COP} = \frac{T_1 - T_3 / r_p^{n}}{T_1 (r_p^n - 1) - T_3 (1 - r_p^{-n})}, \quad \text{where } n = \frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}$$

Differentiating COP with respect to $r_p$ and setting $\dfrac{d(\text{COP})}{d r_p} = 0$ yields:

$$r_{p,\text{opt}} = \left(\frac{T_3}{T_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{2(\gamma-1)}}$$

At this optimum:

$$\text{COP}_{\max} = \frac{T_1}{2 \left[ \sqrt{T_1 T_3} - T_1 \right]} = \frac{1}{2 \left[ \sqrt{T_3/T_1} - 1 \right]}$$

This elegant result is the **geometric-mean** relationship, often appearing in KTU 14-mark problems.

---

## 3.8 Comparison: Reversed Brayton vs Reversed Carnot

| Parameter | Reversed Carnot | Reversed Brayton |
|---|---|---|
| Cycle | Rectangular on T-s | Quadrilateral on T-s |
| Working fluid | Any (phase-changing) | Ideal gas (air) |
| Processes | 2 isothermal + 2 isentropic | 2 isentropic + 2 isobaric |
| COP (max) | $T_1 / (T_2 - T_1)$ | $1/(r_p^{n} - 1)$ |
| Practical? | No (isothermal compression not possible) | Yes (used in aircraft) |
| Phase change | Yes (VCR variant) | No (purely sensible) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** Reversed Carnot is the **theoretical benchmark** — no real cycle can exceed it. Reversed Brayton is *practical but always less efficient* than Carnot operating between the same two temperature limits. Students should be ready to compute both COPs and comment on the ratio.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Component-Level Block Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional flow of air** through the four essential components of a Bell-Coleman / Reversed Brayton refrigeration plant.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CS[COLD SPACE / REFRIGERATED CHAMBER] -->|Warm low-P air returns| COMP
    COMP[COMPRESSOR - Isentropic Compression 1 to 2] -->|Hot high-P air leaves| AC
    AC[AFTER COOLER - Isobaric Heat Rejection 2 to 3] -->|Cool high-P air leaves| EXP
    EXP[EXPANDER - Isentropic Expansion 3 to 4] -->|Very cold low-P air leaves| CS
    AC -.->|Heat Q reject to atmosphere| AMB[AMBIENT ENVIRONMENT]
    COMP -.->|Work input W net from motor| M[MOTOR SHAFT]
    EXP -.->|Work output W E recovered| M
```

### Node Identifier Reference
- `CS`: Cold Space (heat load)
- `COMP`: Compressor (input: $T_1, P_1$ → output: $T_2, P_2$)
- `AC`: After Cooler (rejects $Q_{2-3}$)
- `EXP`: Expander (input: $T_3, P_3$ → output: $T_4, P_4$)
- `AMB`: Ambient (heat sink)
- `M`: Motor shaft (net work source/sink)

---

## 4.2 State-Point Topology on P-v Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph P_v [P-v DIAGRAM LOGIC]
        s1[STATE 1: Low P, High v, Low T] -->|ISENTROPIC COMPRESSION - P rises, v falls, T rises| s2[STATE 2: High P, Lower v, High T]
        s2 -->|ISOBARIC COOLING - P constant, v falls, T falls| s3[STATE 3: High P, Even lower v, Medium T]
        s3 -->|ISENTROPIC EXPANSION - P falls, v rises, T falls| s4[STATE 4: Low P, High v, Lowest T]
        s4 -->|ISOBARIC HEATING RE - P constant, v rises, T rises| s1
    end
```

---

## 4.3 State-Point Topology on T-s Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph T_s [T-s DIAGRAM LOGIC]
        a1[STATE 1: T1, s] -->|VERTICAL UP - Isentropic Compression| a2[STATE 2: T2 greater, same s]
        a2 -->|DOWNWARD SLOPING - Isobaric Heat Rejection Q reject| a3[STATE 3: T3 less, s higher]
        a3 -->|VERTICAL DOWN - Isentropic Expansion| a4[STATE 4: T4 lowest, same s as 3]
        a4 -->|UPWARD SLOPING - Isobaric Heat Absorption RE| a1
    end
```

---

## 4.4 Energy Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q_RE[REFRIGERATING EFFECT Q4 to 1 - Desired Output] --> SYS[AIR REFRIGERATION SYSTEM]
    SYS --> Q_RJ[HEAT REJECTED Q2 to 3 - Waste to Ambient]
    W_NET[NET WORK INPUT W net to Compressor minus W E from Expander] --> SYS
    SYS --> DEL[TEMPERATURE REDUCTION IN COLD SPACE]
```

---

## 4.5 Functional Architecture — Real Aircraft Bootstrap System

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Context:** In modern aircraft, the Reversed Brayton cycle is realized as the **"Air Cycle Machine" (ACM)**. The compressor is a turbo-compressor, the expander is a turbine, and a fan drives both. Heat exchangers (Primary + Secondary) are used. The diagram below shows the high-level architecture for understanding the practical implementation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BLEED[ENGINE BLEED AIR - Hot High P] --> PC[PRIMARY HEAT EXCHANGER - Reject heat to ram air]
    PC --> COMP[TURBO COMPRESSOR - Raises pressure further]
    COMP --> SC[SECONDARY HEAT EXCHANGER - Further cooling]
    SC --> TURB[EXPANSION TURBINE - Produces cold air]
    TURB --> CAB[AIRCRAFT CABIN - Absorbs heat load]
    CAB --> FAN[FAN / MIXER - Returns air to compressor inlet]
    FAN --> COMP
```
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"List the four processes of the Reversed Brayton Cycle and state the energy interaction in each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four processes of the Reversed Brayton cycle are:

1. **Process 1→2: Isentropic (Reversible Adiabatic) Compression** — Air is compressed in the compressor. **Energy interaction: Work input** $W_C = C_p(T_2 - T_1)$. No heat transfer.
2. **Process 2→3: Isobaric (Constant Pressure) Cooling** — Hot compressed air is cooled in the after-cooler. **Energy interaction: Heat rejection** $Q_{rej} = C_p(T_2 - T_3)$ to the surroundings. No work done.
3. **Process 3→4: Isentropic Expansion** — Cool high-pressure air expands in the expander. **Energy interaction: Work output** $W_E = C_p(T_3 - T_4)$ is recovered. No heat transfer.
4. **Process 4→1: Isobaric Heating (Refrigerating Effect)** — Cold air absorbs heat from the cold space. **Energy interaction: Heat absorption** $RE = C_p(T_1 - T_4)$ from the refrigerated space. This is the *desired output* of the cycle.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Why is the COP of an ideal Reversed Brayton cycle dependent only on the pressure ratio?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The COP of the ideal Reversed Brayton cycle is given by:

$$\text{COP} = \frac{T_1 - T_3/r_p^n}{T_1(r_p^n - 1) - T_3(1 - r_p^{-n})}, \quad n = \frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}$$

This expression contains **two temperature parameters** ($T_1$ and $T_3$) and one pressure parameter ($r_p$). However, when we substitute the isentropic relations and simplify algebraically, the temperature terms *cancel out* (the numerator and denominator both scale identically with $T_1$ when $T_1 = T_3$ as in many textbook formulations), leaving:

$$\text{COP} = \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1}$$

This shows that for a given working fluid (fixed $\gamma$), the COP depends **exclusively on the pressure ratio** $r_p$. As $r_p$ increases, the denominator increases, and COP decreases monotonically. Therefore, lower pressure ratios yield higher COP, but this comes at the cost of a smaller temperature drop in the cold space.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates **internal choice** in Part B. Both alternatives must be solved during preparation. The two questions below are independent — solve *any one*.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**
**"An air refrigeration plant works on the Reversed Brayton cycle. The air enters the compressor at $1$ bar and $20°C$ and is compressed to $5$ bar. The cooled air leaves the after-cooler at $30°C$. Assuming isentropic compression and expansion, calculate:**
**(i) The temperature of air at the exit of the compressor and at the inlet to the expander.**
**(ii) The temperature of air at the exit of the expander.**
**(iii) The refrigerating effect per kg of air."**

**Given Data:**
- $P_1 = 1$ bar, $T_1 = 20 + 273 = 293$ K
- $P_2 = 5$ bar, $T_3 = 30 + 273 = 303$ K
- $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $\gamma = 1.4$, $n = (\gamma-1)/\gamma = 0.2857$

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Temperature at compressor exit ($T_2$) and expander inlet ($T_3$ is given):**

For isentropic compression 1→2:

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$

$$T_2 = 293 \times (5/1)^{0.2857}$$

$$T_2 = 293 \times 5^{0.2857}$$

Computing $5^{0.2857}$:

$$5^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \cdot \ln 5} = e^{0.2857 \times 1.6094} = e^{0.4598} = 1.5838$$

$$\boxed{T_2 = 293 \times 1.5838 = 464.05 \text{ K} \approx 191.05°C}$$

The expander inlet temperature $T_3 = 303$ K (given, after cooling).

**(ii) Temperature at expander exit ($T_4$):**

For isentropic expansion 3→4 (with $P_3 = P_2 = 5$ bar and $P_4 = P_1 = 1$ bar):

$$T_4 = T_3 / (P_3/P_4)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = T_3 / 5^{0.2857}$$

$$T_4 = 303 / 1.5838 = 191.30 \text{ K}$$

$$\boxed{T_4 = 191.30 \text{ K} \approx -81.70°C}$$

**(iii) Refrigerating Effect per kg of air:**

$$RE = C_p (T_1 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (293 - 191.30)$$

$$RE = 1.005 \times 101.70 = 102.21 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\boxed{RE \approx 102.21 \text{ kJ/kg of air}}$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**"Continuing from part (a), calculate:**
**(i) Compressor and expander work per kg of air.**
**(ii) Net work input per kg of air.**
**(iii) COP of the plant.**
**(iv) Mass flow rate of air required for a 5 TR (Tonnes of Refrigeration) cooling load.**
**(v) Compare the COP with that of a Reversed Carnot cycle operating between the same temperature limits."**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Compressor and expander work:**

$$W_C = C_p (T_2 - T_1) = 1.005 \times (464.05 - 293) = 1.005 \times 171.05$$

$$\boxed{W_C = 171.91 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

$$W_E = C_p (T_3 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (303 - 191.30) = 1.005 \times 111.70$$

$$\boxed{W_E = 112.26 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

**(ii) Net work input:**

$$W_{net} = W_C - W_E = 171.91 - 112.26 = 59.65 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\boxed{W_{net} = 59.65 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

**(iii) Coefficient of Performance:**

$$\text{COP} = \frac{RE}{W_{net}} = \frac{102.21}{59.65} = 1.714$$

$$\boxed{\text{COP} \approx 1.714}$$

**Verification using closed-form formula:**

$$\text{COP} = \frac{1}{5^{0.2857} - 1} = \frac{1}{1.5838 - 1} = \frac{1}{0.5838} = 1.713 \checkmark$$

**(iv) Mass flow rate for 5 TR:**

1 TR = 3.516 kW, so 5 TR = $5 \times 3.516 = 17.58$ kW of cooling.

$$\dot{m} = \frac{\text{Cooling Load}}{RE} = \frac{17.58}{102.21} = 0.1720 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m} = 0.172 \text{ kg/s} \approx 619 \text{ kg/hr}}$$

**(v) Comparison with Reversed Carnot:**

The Reversed Carnot cycle operates between $T_1 = 293$ K and $T_2 = 464.05$ K (highest temperature in the cycle, which is $T_2$):

$$\text{COP}_{Carnot} = \frac{T_1}{T_2 - T_1} = \frac{293}{464.05 - 293} = \frac{293}{171.05} = 1.713$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interesting observation:** In this problem, the COP of the Reversed Brayton cycle happens to *coincide* with that of the Reversed Carnot cycle. This is because $T_1 = T_3$ was implicitly not assumed — the actual ratio yields nearly identical values due to numerical coincidence. In general, $\text{COP}_{Carnot} > \text{COP}_{Brayton}$ strictly.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**"With the help of a neat P-v and T-s diagram, derive an expression for the COP of an ideal Reversed Brayton cycle in terms of the pressure ratio. State all assumptions clearly."**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Air is the working fluid and behaves as an ideal gas.
2. Specific heats $C_p$ and $C_v$ are constant (cold air-standard cycle).
3. Compression and expansion are isentropic (reversible adiabatic).
4. No pressure drop in the heat exchangers.
5. Kinetic and potential energy changes are negligible.
6. The cycle is a closed loop — no mass loss.

**Diagrams (2 marks):** [Draw P-v and T-s diagrams as in Section 4.2 and 4.3 above — four states with the four processes labeled.]

**Derivation (3 marks):**

Starting from the isentropic relations:

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = r_p^{n}, \quad n = \frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}$$

$$T_2 = T_1 r_p^{n}, \qquad T_4 = T_3 / r_p^{n}$$

$$RE = C_p(T_1 - T_4) = C_p\left(T_1 - \frac{T_3}{r_p^{n}}\right)$$

$$W_{net} = C_p[(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)] = C_p\left[T_1(r_p^{n} - 1) - T_3\left(1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{n}}\right)\right]$$

$$\text{COP} = \frac{RE}{W_{net}} = \frac{T_1 - T_3/r_p^{n}}{T_1(r_p^{n} - 1) - T_3(1 - 1/r_p^{n})}$$

For the special case $T_1 = T_3 = T$:

$$\text{COP} = \frac{T(1 - 1/r_p^{n})}{T(r_p^{n} - 1) - T(1 - 1/r_p^{n})} = \frac{T(1 - 1/r_p^{n})}{T[(r_p^{n} - 1) - (1 - 1/r_p^{n})]}$$

$$= \frac{1 - 1/r_p^{n}}{(r_p^{n} - 1) - (1 - 1/r_p^{n})} = \frac{1 - 1/r_p^{n}}{r_p^{n} - 2 + 1/r_p^{n}}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $r_p^{n}$:

$$= \frac{r_p^{n} - 1}{(r_p^{n})^2 - 2 r_p^{n} + 1} = \frac{r_p^{n} - 1}{(r_p^{n} - 1)^2} = \frac{1}{r_p^{n} - 1}$$

$$\boxed{\text{COP}_{RB} = \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1}}$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**"An air refrigerator using the Reversed Brayton cycle is required to produce $5$ TR of cooling. The air enters the compressor at $0.9$ bar, $-7°C$, and leaves at $4.5$ bar. The index of compression and expansion is $1.35$. Calculate:**
**(i) Work of compression and expansion per kg of air.**
**(ii) COP of the cycle.**
**(iii) Mass of air circulated per minute.**
**(iv) Piston displacement of the compressor per minute, if the volumetric efficiency is $80\%$ and the clearance is negligible."**

**Given:**
- $P_1 = 0.9$ bar, $T_1 = -7 + 273 = 266$ K
- $P_2 = 4.5$ bar, $r_p = 4.5/0.9 = 5$
- $n_{\text{actual}} = 1.35$ (note: this is **NOT** the isentropic index — use given $n$ directly)
- $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K
- Cooling load = 5 TR = $5 \times 3.516 = 17.58$ kW

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Work of compression and expansion:**

The compressor work with the given polytropic index $n$:

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(n-1)/n} = 266 \times 5^{(1.35-1)/1.35} = 266 \times 5^{0.2593}$$

Computing $5^{0.2593}$:

$$5^{0.2593} = e^{0.2593 \times 1.6094} = e^{0.4173} = 1.5179$$

$$T_2 = 266 \times 1.5179 = 403.76 \text{ K}$$

$$W_C = C_p (T_2 - T_1) = 1.005 \times (403.76 - 266) = 1.005 \times 137.76$$

$$\boxed{W_C = 138.45 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

For the expander, we need $T_3$. For ideal case after cooling to ambient $T_3 = T_1 = 266$ K (assuming after-cooler brings air back to inlet temperature):

$$T_4 = T_3 / r_p^{(n-1)/n} = 266 / 1.5179 = 175.24 \text{ K}$$

$$W_E = C_p (T_3 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (266 - 175.24) = 1.005 \times 90.76$$

$$\boxed{W_E = 91.21 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

**(ii) COP:**

$$RE = C_p (T_1 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (266 - 175.24) = 1.005 \times 90.76 = 91.21 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$W_{net} = W_C - W_E = 138.45 - 91.21 = 47.24 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\text{COP} = \frac{RE}{W_{net}} = \frac{91.21}{47.24} = 1.931$$

$$\boxed{\text{COP} \approx 1.931}$$

**(iii) Mass flow rate per minute:**

$$\dot{m} = \frac{17.58 \text{ kW}}{91.21 \text{ kJ/kg}} = 0.1927 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\dot{m} = 0.1927 \times 60 = 11.56 \text{ kg/min}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m} \approx 11.56 \text{ kg/min}}$$

**(iv) Piston displacement of compressor:**

The specific volume at compressor inlet:

$$v_1 = \frac{R T_1}{P_1} = \frac{0.287 \times 266}{0.9 \times 100} = \frac{76.342}{90} = 0.8482 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

(Using $P_1 = 0.9$ bar $= 90$ kPa)

Volume flow rate of air at compressor inlet:

$$\dot{V}_1 = \dot{m} \times v_1 = 0.1927 \times 0.8482 = 0.1635 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

With volumetric efficiency $\eta_v = 0.80$:

$$\text{Swept Volume} = \frac{\dot{V}_1}{\eta_v} = \frac{0.1635}{0.80} = 0.2044 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Per minute:

$$\boxed{V_{\text{swept}} = 0.2044 \times 60 = 12.26 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in the KTU University Exam:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Work Equations:** Students often write $W_{net} = W_E - W_C$ instead of $W_C - W_E$. **Remember:** Net work *input* = Compressor work (in) − Expander work (recovered out).
> 2. **Wrong $T_4$ Calculation:** The isentropic relation for the expander is $T_4 = T_3 / r_p^n$, **not** $T_4 = T_3 \times r_p^n$. The expander temperature *decreases*, not increases.
> 3. **Confusing Carnot and Brayton COP Formulae:** Carnot COP = $T_1/(T_2 - T_1)$ (depends on absolute temperatures). Brayton COP = $1/(r_p^n - 1)$ (depends on pressure ratio). Do not mix them.
> 4. **Units Mismatch:** Pressure in formula $v = RT/P$ must be in **kPa**, not bar. $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K = 287 J/kg·K. Convert carefully.
> 5. **Missing $\gamma$ value in COP formula:** Always state $\gamma = 1.4$ for air. If the question gives a different gas, use that $\gamma$.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert TR to kW:** 1 TR = 3.516 kW. Many students lose 1–2 marks here.
> 7. **Wrong direction in T-s diagram:** In a refrigeration cycle, the *refrigerating effect* (process 4→1) is the **bottom horizontal curve** going *rightward and upward*. The compressor process (1→2) is a *vertical line going upward*. Misdrawing these is an instant -2 mark deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Master These Before the KTU Exam:**

- ☐ **Reversed Brayton = Reversed Joule = Bell-Coleman** (all three names refer to the **same** cycle).
- ☐ The cycle uses **air (ideal gas)** as the working fluid; **no phase change** occurs.
- ☐ **Four processes:** Isentropic compression → Isobaric cooling → Isentropic expansion → Isobaric heating (refrigeration).
- ☐ **Compressor** does work *in*; **Expander** recovers work *out*; **Net work** = $W_C - W_E$.
- ☐ **Refrigerating Effect** (RE) = $C_p (T_1 - T_4)$ — heat absorbed in the cold space.
- ☐ **Closed-form COP:** $\text{COP} = \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1}$
- ☐ **COP depends only on pressure ratio** for ideal cycle (when $T_1 = T_3$).
- ☐ **Higher pressure ratio → Lower COP** (trade-off with cooling capacity).
- ☐ **Optimum pressure ratio for max COP:** $r_{p,\text{opt}} = (T_3/T_1)^{\gamma / 2(\gamma-1)}$
- ☐ **Standard air properties:** $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K, $\gamma = 1.4$.
- ☐ **T-s diagram** is a *skewed quadrilateral* with two vertical (isentropic) sides and two slanted (isobaric) sides.
- ☐ **P-v diagram** is a *curvilinear quadrilateral* with two curved (isentropic) sides and two horizontal (isobaric) sides.
- ☐ **Unit conversions:** 1 TR = 3.516 kW; 1 bar = 100 kPa = $10^5$ Pa; °C + 273 = K.
- ☐ **Reversed Brayton is always less efficient than Reversed Carnot** between the same $T_{\max}, T_{\min}$.
- ☐ **Main application:** Aircraft air-conditioning and pressurization (Air Cycle Machine).
- ☐ **Limitation:** Requires **very large mass flow rate** of air → bulky plant → unsuitable for domestic AC.
- ☐ The **expander can be a turbine** (rotary) or **reciprocating expansion engine** (piston-type).
- ☐ The **after-cooler** (R-H, Rejector-Heat exchanger) is a critical component — without it, the cycle would have *no net cooling*.
- ☐ **Historical note:** Bell & Coleman's 1857 patent was the first practical refrigeration machine.
- ☐ The cycle is a **closed loop** in theory, but in aircraft, fresh bleed air is continuously taken from the engine (open-loop variant).

> **End of Module 1 — Reversed Brayton / Joule / Bell Coleman Cycle Notes**
> **Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme | Course: PEAGT411 | Outcome-Based Education (OBE) Aligned**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Gas Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Analysis of Gas Cycle # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Gas Refrigeration Cycle (KTU 2024 Module 1 Definition):** > A *gas refrigeration cycle* is a reversed thermodynamic cycle in which a **gaseous working fluid** (typically air) is compressed, cooled, expanded, and used to absorb heat from a low-temperature space, producing a refrigeration effect. The most fundamental reference is the **Reverse Carnot Cycle**, while the practical gas cycle used in aviation and marine applications is the **Bell-Coleman (Air Refrigeration) Cycle**. The governing thermodynamic principle is the **Coefficient of Performance (COP)**, which is the ratio of the desired refrigeration effect (RE) to the net work input (W_net): $$COP_{ref} = \frac{Desired\ Output}{Required\ Input} = \frac{Q_{absorbed}}{W_{net}}$$ ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: "The Bellows and the Chimney"** > Imagine you are blowing air into a room through a long pipe. If you **squeeze the bellows (compress)** the air, it gets hot. If you pass this hot air over a **cold water shower (cooled heat exchanger)**, it loses its heat to the water. Now, if you suddenly **release the bellows (expand)** this cool air, it becomes colder than it was originally. When this chilled air enters a **cold room**, it absorbs heat from the food and drinks inside, keeping them cool. This, in essence, is what a **gas refrigeration cycle** does — compress, cool, expand, and absorb. The working fluid remains in the **gaseous phase** throughout the entire cycle. This is the key distinction from vapor compression cycles where the refrigerant changes phase (condenses and evaporates). ## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics The standard parameters and constants used in the analysis of gas cycles: - **Working fluid:** Air (ideal gas, R = **0.287 kJ/kg·K**) - **Specific heat at constant pressure:** Cp = **1.005 kJ/kg·K** - **Ratio of specific heats (γ):** **1.4** (for air) - **Typical temperature limits:** - **T1 (cold space):** 263 K to 273 K (-10 °C to 0 °C) - **T2 (after compression):** up to 350–400 K - **T3 (after cooling):** ~300 K (ambient) - **T4 (after expansion):** below T1 > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always start by clearly defining the four state points (1, 2, 3, 4) and the corresponding processes before writing any formula. Examiners allocate the first 1–2 marks specifically for **clear process identification**. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Reverse Carnot Cycle (Theoretical Benchmark) The **Reverse Carnot Cycle** is the ideal, fully reversible refrigeration cycle operating between two fixed temperature reservoirs **T1 (low)** and **T2 (high)**. It serves as the **theoretical upper limit** for any refrigeration cycle's COP. ### 2.1.1 Constituent Processes 1. **Process 1–2: Isentropic Compression** - The gas is compressed reversibly and adiabatically from T1 to T2. - No heat transfer, entropy remains constant. 2. **Process 2–3: Isothermal Heat Rejection** - The gas is cooled at constant high temperature T2. - Heat Q2 is rejected to the high-temperature reservoir. 3. **Process 3–4: Isentropic Expansion** - The gas expands reversibly and adiabatically from T2 to T1. - No heat transfer. 4. **Process 4–1: Isothermal Heat Absorption** - The gas expands at constant low temperature T1. - Heat Q1 is absorbed from the cold space (this is the refrigeration effect). ### 2.1.2 Why It Is the Benchmark > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The Reverse Carnot Cycle achieves the **highest possible COP** for any cycle operating between T1 and T2. No real cycle can exceed this. It is **physically unrealizable** in practice because: > - It requires an isothermal heat transfer process with a **continuously variable volume** in a piston-cylinder device. > - The isentropic processes are practically impossible to achieve in a finite-time real engine. > - It is, however, the **gold standard reference** used to compare actual cycles. ### 2.1.3 COP of Reverse Carnot Cycle $$COP_{Carnot} = \frac{T_1}{T_2 - T_1}$$ The **net work input** is the area enclosed by the cycle on a p-V diagram. $$W_{net} = Q_2 - Q_1 = T_2 \cdot \Delta S - T_1 \cdot \Delta S = (T_2 - T_1) \cdot \Delta S$$ ## 2.2 The Bell-Coleman (Air Refrigeration) Cycle The **Bell-Coleman Cycle** is the practical realization of gas refrigeration using air as the working fluid.
