It circulates the refrigerant through the cycle. 2. **Condenser:** The high-pressure, high-temperature superheated vapour from the compressor enters the condenser, where it rejects heat $Q_{cond}$ to the surroundings (atmosphere or cooling water) at constant pressure, condensing into a saturated liquid. 3. **Expansion Device (Throttling Valve):** The high-pressure saturated liquid is throttled to the low evaporator pressure. The throttling process is **isenthalpic** ($h_3 = h_4$) and produces a low-quality two-phase mixture, ready for evaporation. 4. **Evaporator:** The cold liquid-vapour mixture absorbs heat $Q_E$ (refrigerating effect, RE) from the refrigerated space at constant low pressure, completely evaporating into saturated vapour, which returns to the compressor. --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Why is the throttling process in a VCRS considered isenthalpic? Justify.** **Model Answer:** The throttling (expansion) process is considered **isenthalpic** ($h_3 = h_4$) because of the following reasons: - **Adiabatic:** The expansion valve is well-insulated and the process occurs too rapidly for any significant heat transfer to occur ($Q = 0$). - **No work transfer:** The expansion valve has no moving parts and does no shaft work on the refrigerant ($W = 0$). - **Negligible KE/PE changes:** The kinetic and potential energy changes across the valve are small compared to the enthalpy change. Applying the Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE): $$ h_3 + \dfrac{V_3^2}{2} + g z_3 = h_4 + \dfrac{V_4^2}{2} + g z_4 $$ With the above three simplifying assumptions: $$ h_3 = h_4 $$ Hence, throttling is an **isenthalpic** process. The drop in temperature and the partial vaporization ("flashing") occur at the expense of the refrigerant's own internal energy. --- ## 5.2 PART B: Long-Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) ### **Question Choice A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` > **Q: With the help of a neat P-h diagram, explain the working of a simple Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS). Derive the expression for its Coefficient of Performance (COP).** **Model Solution Outline:** #### (a) Description of Components and Working [7 Marks] 1. **State the objective:** The VCRS operates on the reverse-Rankine cycle to produce refrigeration by absorbing heat from a low-temperature region and rejecting it to a high-temperature region using mechanical work. 2. **Describe each component** (refer to Section 2 of this note for detailed descriptions of compressor, condenser, expansion valve, evaporator). 3. **Explain the cyclic operation:** - Process 1-2: Compression — saturated vapour compressed isentropically. - Process 2-3: Condensation — high-pressure vapour rejects heat, condenses to saturated liquid. - Process 3-4: Throttling — saturated liquid expanded to two-phase mixture. - Process 4-1: Evaporation — mixture absorbs heat, fully vaporizes to saturated vapour. 4. **Draw a neat P-h diagram** showing all four states and processes with appropriate labels ($P_{evap}$, $P_{cond}$, saturation dome, isentropic line). #### (b) Derivation of COP [7 Marks] > Apply SFEE to each component as shown in **Section 3.1** of this note (Steps 1 through 5). Final result to be boxed: $$ \boxed{COP = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}} $$ Mention that the **Carnot COP** for the same temperature limits is: $$ COP_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} $$ and conclude that $COP_{VCRS}$ is generally **less than** $COP_{Carnot}$ in absolute terms when both are calculated on equivalent bases. --- ### **Question Choice B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` > **Q: A refrigeration system using R-134a operates between an evaporator temperature of −15 °C and a condenser temperature of 35 °C. The refrigerant is dry saturated at the compressor inlet and saturated liquid at the condenser exit. Calculate: (a) Refrigerating effect per kg, (b) Compressor work per kg, (c) COP of the cycle, and (d) The Carnot COP operating between the refrigerated space at −5 °C and surroundings at 40 °C.** **Given Data (R-134a Property Values):** $$ \text{At } T_{evap} = -15\,^\circ\text{C}: \quad h_1 = h_g = 389.0 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad s_1 = 1.7380 \text{ kJ/kg·K} $$ $$ \text{At } T_{cond} = 35\,^\circ\text{C}: \quad h_{f3} = 100.87 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad s_{g2s} = 1.7145 \text{ kJ/kg·K} $$ Reading from R-134a superheated tables at $35\,^\circ\text{C}$ and $s = 1.7380 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$: $$ h_2 = 425.4 \text{ kJ/kg} \quad \text{(isentropic compression)} $$ #### (a) Refrigerating Effect (RE) [3 Marks] Since $h_3 = h_4 = 100.87 \text{ kJ/kg}$: $$ RE = h_1 - h_4 = 389.0 - 100.87 $$ $$ \boxed{RE = 288.13 \text{ kJ/kg}} $$ #### (b) Compressor Work ($W_c$) [3 Marks] $$ W_c = h_2 - h_1 = 425.4 - 389.0 $$ $$ \boxed{W_c = 36.4 \text{ kJ/kg}} $$ #### (c) COP of the Vapour Compression Cycle [4 Marks] $$ COP_{VCRS} = \dfrac{RE}{W_c} = \dfrac{288.13}{36.4} $$ $$ \boxed{COP_{VCRS} = 7.92} $$ #### (d) Carnot COP [4 Marks] $$ T_L = -5 + 273.15 = 268.15 \text{ K} $$ $$ T_H = 40 + 273.15 = 313.15 \text{ K} $$ $$ COP_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \dfrac{268.15}{313.15 - 268.15} = \dfrac{268.15}{45.0} $$ $$ \boxed{COP_{Carnot} = 5.96} $$ #### Comparison and Conclusion: The VCRS COP (7.92) is higher than Carnot COP (5.96) computed for *refrigerator and sink temperatures*. This indicates that the actual refrigerant saturation temperatures (−15 °C and 35 °C) are **inside** the actual temperature limits of the cold space (−5 °C) and surroundings (40 °C), giving the cycle a thermal advantage — but the **second-law efficiency** (relative efficiency) is: $$ \eta_{rel} = \dfrac{COP_{Carnot\,(refrig-sink)}}{COP_{Carnot}} \times 100 $$ For board purposes, **mention the comparison** and state that the **actual cycle efficiency is always less than the Carnot limit** when compared on identical temperature bases. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks in VCRS-Mechanism questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting to write the unit of enthalpy** ($\text{kJ/kg}$) in each step — KTU examiners deduct **0.5 mark** per missing unit. > > 2. **Confusing State 1 and State 2 locations** on the P-h diagram. State 1 is on the **saturated vapour line** (right of dome); State 2 is in the **superheated region** (right of dome, above saturated vapour line). Mixing these up destroys the entire COP derivation. > > 3. **Not stating assumptions** explicitly in derivations: students must explicitly mention "neglecting KE/PE changes," "steady-state operation," and "adiabatic compressor" for full marks in 14-mark questions. > > 4. **Writing $|h_2 - h_1|$ with vertical bars** in the answer sheet — use parentheses $(h_2 - h_1)$ to avoid ambiguity (since both $h_2 > h_1$ in compression). > > 5. **Confusing the cycle direction on T-s vs P-h diagrams:** VCRS is a **clockwise cycle on P-h** but a **counter-clockwise cycle on T-s**. Writing it clockwise on T-s is a guaranteed 2-mark penalty. > > 6. **Skipping the energy-balance verification step** — adding $W_c + RE = Q_{cond}$ at the end of the derivation earns an extra **0.5 to 1 mark** as a "check step." > > 7. **Using °C directly in Carnot COP formula** — always convert to **Kelvin**. This is the single most common mistake and costs full marks in Part (d). > > 8. **Forgetting to draw the saturation dome** in the P-h diagram — even a rough dome earns full marks; a missing dome loses 1–2 marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Revision Bullet Points for VCRS-Mechanism:** > > - **Cycle Type:** VCRS is the **reverse-Rankine cycle**, work-operated, with phase change of refrigerant. > > - **Four Components (Mnemonic: "C-C-E-E" — Compressor, Condenser, Expansion valve, Evaporator):** never forget this order in clockwise flow. > > - **Four State Points:** **1** = saturated vapour (compressor inlet); **2** = superheated vapour (compressor outlet); **3** = saturated liquid (condenser outlet); **4** = liquid-vapour mixture (evaporator inlet). > > - **Throttling is isenthalpic:** $h_3 = h_4$ — *never* write $T_3 = T_4$. > > - **Compression is isentropic (ideal):** $s_1 = s_2$. > > - **Condensation and evaporation are isobaric** (constant pressure): $P_2 = P_3 = P_{cond}$; $P_4 = P_1 = P_{evap}$. > > - **Key formulas (write 5 times before exam):** > > $$ > RE = h_1 - h_4, \quad W_c = h_2 - h_1, \quad COP = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} > $$ > > $$ > COP_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} \quad (\text{Use Kelvin!}) > $$ > > - **Energy balance check:** $W_c + RE = Q_{cond} = h_2 - h_3$. > > - **P-h diagram shapes to remember:** > - 1 → 2: **vertical line up** (isentropic compression) > - 2 → 3: **horizontal line left** (condensation at $P_{cond}$) > - 3 → 4: **vertical line down** (isenthalpic expansion) > - 4 → 1: **horizontal line right** (evaporation at $P_{evap}$) > > - **Effect of parameters on COP:** > - ↓ $T_{evap}$ → COP **decreases sharply** > - ↑ $T_{cond}$ → COP **decreases** > - ↑ Subcooling → COP **increases** > - ↑ Superheating → COP **decreases** > > - **Always write units** in every line of your solution. > > - **Convert °C to K** for Carnot COP — non-negotiable. > > - **Quality at State 4** formula: $x_4 = \dfrac{h_4 - h_{f4}}{h_{fg4}}$ — typically between 0.15 and 0.25. > > - **Real-world auxiliary components** (not in ideal cycle): liquid receiver, filter-drier, accumulator, solenoid valve, thermostatic expansion valve (TXV), sight glass. ---

topics.title: P-H Diagrams and Types of Vapour Compression Cycles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** P-H Diagrams and Types of Vapour Compression Cycles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Pressure-Enthalpy (P-H) Diagram — Formal Definition

The **Pressure-Enthalpy (P-H) Diagram**, also called the **Mollier Diagram** for refrigerants, is the most essential graphical tool used in the analysis of vapour compression refrigeration systems. It is a thermodynamic chart in which:

- **Y-axis (Ordinate):** Absolute Pressure (in kPa or bar), plotted on a **logarithmic scale**.
- **X-axis (Abscissa):** Enthalpy (in kJ/kg) of the refrigerant, plotted on a **linear scale**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The P-H diagram is a *high-yield* topic. Nearly every numerical problem in Module 2 involves locating state points on this chart, or using the cycle path to determine refrigerating effect, compressor work, and COP. Mastering the chart means mastering the subject.

### Key Features of the P-H Chart

1. **Saturation Dome (Bell-Shaped Curve):** Encloses the two-phase region (liquid + vapour mixture).
   - **Left limb:** Saturated liquid line (x = 0).
   - **Right limb:** Saturated vapour line (x = 1).
   - Inside the dome: **Wet region** (subcooled liquid + saturated vapour mixture).
2. **Critical Point (C):** The apex of the dome. Beyond this point, liquid and vapour phases become indistinguishable (supercritical region).
3. **Subcooled Liquid Region:** Left of the saturated liquid line.
4. **Superheated Vapour Region:** Right of the saturated vapour line and above critical pressure.
5. **Constant Temperature Lines:**
   - In the wet region: constant temperature = constant pressure (horizontal lines).
   - In the superheated region: constant temperature lines slope upward and to the right.
6. **Constant Entropy Lines (Isentropes):** Slope slightly to the right in the superheated region.
7. **Constant Volume Lines (Isometrics):** Slope steeply upward in the superheated region.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Mountain Map of Refrigeration"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of the P-H diagram as a *hiking trail map* of a mountain. The **mountain dome** represents the saturation region (clouds of misty two-phase fluid). The **valley on the left** is the cool, calm subcooled liquid (like a lake at the base of the mountain). The **open plains on the right** are the superheated vapour (hot, dry air above the mountain). A **refrigeration cycle** is the round-trip journey a refrigerant takes — climbing up (compression), traversing across the top (condensation), sliding down the slopes (expansion), and returning to the starting base (evaporation). The *horizontal distance* (enthalpy change) tells us how much "thermal energy" was moved, while the *vertical position* (pressure) tells us the "altitude" of compression.

## 1.3 Types of Vapour Compression Cycles — Overview

A vapour compression refrigeration system (VCRS) circulates refrigerant through a closed loop. The **ideal theoretical cycle** is called the **Simple Saturation Cycle** (or Standard Vapour Compression Cycle), but **practical cycles** differ due to real-world constraints:

| Cycle Type | Description | Compressor Inlet Condition |
|---|---|---|
| **Simple Saturation cycle** | Theoretical; compression from saturated vapour | Saturated vapour (x = 1) |
| **Wet compression cycle** | Compression starts in wet region | Wet vapour (x < 1) |
| **Dry (Superheated) Compression cycle** | Compression from superheated state | Superheated vapour |
| **Cycle with subcooling** | Condensed liquid cooled below saturation | Subcooled liquid |
| **Cycle with superheat and subcooling** | Most practical real-world cycle | Superheated vapour |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Convention:** For the simple saturation cycle, the **suction line** to the compressor carries **dry saturated vapour** (state 1 lies exactly on the saturated vapour line). The **liquid line** leaving the condenser carries **saturated liquid** (state 3 lies on the saturated liquid line). The **expansion valve** is treated as an **isenthalpic** (throttling) process, so $h_3 = h_4$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Simple (Standard) Saturation Vapour Compression Cycle

The cycle consists of **four reversible processes** connecting **four state points** (1, 2, 3, 4):

### Process-by-Process Breakdown

**Process 1 → 2 : Isentropic Compression (Compressor)**
- The refrigerant enters the compressor as **dry saturated vapour** at low pressure $P_1$ and enthalpy $h_1$.
- The compressor raises the pressure from $P_1$ (evaporator pressure) to $P_2$ (condenser pressure).
- Compression is modelled as **isentropic** (reversible adiabatic): $s_1 = s_2$.
- Work input is required; refrigerant leaves at high temperature superheated state.
- **Work of compression per kg:** $w_c = h_2 - h_1$ (kJ/kg)

**Process 2 → 3 : Constant Pressure Heat Rejection (Condenser)**
- Hot, superheated refrigerant vapour enters the condenser at pressure $P_2 = P_3$.
- It first **desuperheats** (cools down to saturated vapour), then **condenses** to saturated liquid at constant pressure.
- Heat is rejected to the surroundings (cooling medium: air or water).
- **Heat rejected per kg:** $q_{out} = h_2 - h_3$ (kJ/kg)

**Process 3 → 4 : Isenthalpic Expansion (Throttling Valve)**
- Saturated liquid passes through the **expansion device** (capillary tube / thermostatic expansion valve).
- This is a **throttling process**: enthalpy remains constant, $h_3 = h_4$.
- Pressure drops from $P_3$ to $P_1$, and a portion of liquid **flashes** into vapour (low quality mixture).
- No work done, no heat transfer (idealized).

**Process 4 → 1 : Constant Pressure Heat Absorption (Evaporator)**
- The low-quality two-phase mixture absorbs heat from the refrigerated space.
- The liquid portion evaporates completely, becoming **dry saturated vapour** at state 1.
- **Refrigerating Effect per kg:** $RE = h_1 - h_4 = h_1 - h_3$ (kJ/kg)

## 2.2 Why These Idealizations?

- **Isentropic compression:** Real compressors are close to isentropic (90–95% efficient). Modeling as ideal is the first step; deviations are handled later via **isentropic efficiency**.
- **Constant pressure heat exchange:** Flow friction in heat exchangers is small, so pressure drop is often neglected.
- **Throttling as isenthalpic:** A justified idealization for small orifices and valves.
- **Dry compression at state 1:** Prevents liquid droplets from entering the compressor, which would cause **slugging** and mechanical damage. Hence the real cycle aims for **dry compression**.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Cycle Improvements

| Modification | Reason / Motivation |
|---|---|
| **Superheating at compressor inlet** | Ensures dry compression (safety), increases refrigerating effect slightly, but increases work. Net COP effect depends on refrigerant. |
| **Subcooling at condenser outlet** | Increases refrigerating effect *without* increasing compressor work → **COP always improves**. |
| **Use of multi-stage compression with intercooling** | Reduces discharge temperature, improves COP for large pressure ratios. |
| **Expansion with work recovery (expander)** | Replaces throttling, recovers some work, but mechanical complexity limits use. |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the *master cheat sheet* for this topic. Memorize these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Refrigerating Effect (RE)** | $RE = h_1 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | $h_4 = h_3$ for throttle |
| 2 | **Compressor Work (Wc)** | $W_c = h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | Isentropic compression |
| 3 | **Heat Rejected in Condenser** | $Q_{out} = h_2 - h_3$ | kJ/kg | — |
| 4 | **Coefficient of Performance (Refrigerator)** | $COP_{ref} = \dfrac{RE}{W_c} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | dimensionless | — |
| 5 | **Coefficient of Performance (Heat Pump)** | $COP_{hp} = \dfrac{Q_{out}}{W_c} = \dfrac{h_2 - h_3}{h_2 - h_1}$ | dimensionless | $COP_{hp} = COP_{ref} + 1$ |
| 6 | **Mass Flow Rate of Refrigerant** | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_L}{RE} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_L}{h_1 - h_4}$ | kg/s | $\dot{Q}_L$ = cooling load in kW |
| 7 | **Compressor Power Input** | $\dot{W}_c = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_1)$ | kW | — |
| 8 | **Heat Rejection Rate (Condenser)** | \dot{Q}_{H} = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_3)$ | kW | — |
| 9 | **Volumetric Refrigerating Effect** | $VRE = \dfrac{RE}{v_1}$ | kJ/m³ | $v_1$ = specific volume at suction |
| 10 | **Compressor Displacement Volume** | $V_d = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot v_1}{\eta_v}$ | m³/s | $\eta_v$ = volumetric efficiency |
| 11 | **Isentropic Efficiency of Compressor** | $\eta_{isen} = \dfrac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_{2a} - h_1}$ | — | $h_{2s}$: ideal, $h_{2a}$: actual |
| 12 | **Power with Actual Compressor** | $\dot{W}_{act} = \dfrac{\dot{m}(h_{2s} - h_1)}{\eta_{isen}}$ | kW | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Convention:** Always plot or visualize the cycle on the P-H diagram first — this guides which enthalpies are higher and which are lower. For example, $h_2 > h_1$ and $h_3 > h_4$ always hold.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HVAC Industry:** Engineers use P-H charts to select refrigerants, size compressors, and predict system performance at various operating conditions.
- **Cold Storage Design:** Subcooling margins are directly read off the chart to prevent flash gas at the expansion valve.
- **Energy Audits:** COP degradation due to suction-line losses and condenser fouling can be diagnosed by comparing actual state points to the design cycle on the P-H chart.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Balance of Each Component

We apply the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** to each component, neglecting kinetic and potential energy changes.

### Component 1: Compressor (Process 1 → 2)

For an adiabatic compressor with no significant heat transfer (ideal case):

$$q - w = (h_2 - h_1)$$

Since the process is adiabatic, $q = 0$, and $w$ is the work input (negative on the system):

$$-w_c = h_2 - h_1$$

Rearranging (work done **on** the system is positive):

$$w_c = h_2 - h_1$$

**Units check:** $h$ is in kJ/kg, so $w_c$ is in kJ/kg. ✅

### Component 2: Condenser (Process 2 → 3)

For a constant-pressure heat exchanger with no work and negligible KE/PE changes:

$$q_{out} = h_2 - h_3$$

**Sign convention:** Heat leaving the refrigerant is positive $q_{out}$. This is the **heat rejected to the environment**.

### Component 3: Expansion Valve (Process 3 → 4)

Throttling is a **purely irreversible** process. The SFEE reduces to:

$$h_3 = h_4$$

This is the **isenthalpic** condition. No work, no heat transfer (ideal), no change in enthalpy.

### Component 4: Evaporator (Process 4 → 1)

The refrigerant absorbs heat at constant low pressure:

$$q_{in} = h_1 - h_4 = RE$$

This is the **refrigerating effect** per unit mass of refrigerant.

## 3.2 Derivation: Coefficient of Performance (COP)

The COP of a refrigerator is defined as the ratio of desired effect (cooling) to work input:

$$COP_{ref} = \frac{\text{Desired Effect}}{\text{Work Input}} = \frac{q_{in}}{w_c} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$

For a heat pump, the desired effect is heat delivered at high temperature:

$$COP_{hp} = \frac{q_{out}}{w_c} = \frac{h_2 - h_3}{h_2 - h_1}$$

**Useful algebraic relationship:**

$$COP_{hp} = \frac{q_{out}}{w_c} = \frac{q_{in} + w_c}{w_c} = COP_{ref} + 1$$

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Standard Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A vapour compression refrigeration cycle using R-134a operates between evaporator temperature $T_{evap} = -10°C$ and condenser temperature $T_{cond} = 40°C$. The refrigerant is **dry saturated vapour** at compressor inlet and **saturated liquid** at condenser exit. Determine: (a) Refrigerating Effect, (b) Compressor Work, (c) COP, (d) Mass flow rate for a 5 TR cooling load. Take $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$.

**Step 1: Read state points from R-134a P-H chart (or saturation tables).**

At $T_{evap} = -10°C$ (saturated):
- $P_1 = P_4 = 200.6 \text{ kPa}$
- $h_1 = h_g = 392.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (saturated vapour enthalpy)
- $h_4 = h_f = 187.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$ at $-10°C$ (note: $h_4 = h_3$)

At $T_{cond} = 40°C$ (saturated):
- $P_2 = P_3 = 1017 \text{ kPa}$
- $h_3 = h_f = 256.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (saturated liquid)
- $h_2 = ?$ (superheated vapour, to be found)

**Step 2: Find $h_2$ using isentropic compression.**

At state 1, saturated vapour at $-10°C$: $s_1 = s_g = 1.733 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ (approx).

At $P_2 = 1017 \text{ kPa}$ and $s_2 = s_1 = 1.733 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, from superheated R-134a tables:
- $h_2 \approx 430.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (interpolated)

**Step 3: Calculate Refrigerating Effect (RE).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RE &= h_1 - h_4 = h_1 - h_3 \\
&= 392.3 - 256.4 \\
&= 135.9 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Compressor Work (Wc).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_c &= h_2 - h_1 \\
&= 430.5 - 392.3 \\
&= 38.2 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Calculate COP.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
COP_{ref} &= \frac{RE}{W_c} \\
&= \frac{135.9}{38.2} \\
&= 3.558
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Calculate Mass Flow Rate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_L &= 5 \text{ TR} \times 3.517 \text{ kW/TR} = 17.585 \text{ kW} \\
\dot{m} &= \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{RE} \\
&= \frac{17.585}{135.9} \\
&= 0.1294 \text{ kg/s} \\
&= 7.76 \text{ kg/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Subcooling and Superheating

Let $T_{evap} = -10°C$, $T_{cond} = 40°C$, and we now introduce **subcooling** $\Delta T_{sub} = 5°C$ and **superheat** $\Delta T_{sh} = 5°C$.

| Cycle Variant | $h_1$ (kJ/kg) | $h_3$ (kJ/kg) | $RE$ (kJ/kg) | $W_c$ (kJ/kg) | $COP$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Simple Saturation | 392.3 | 256.4 | 135.9 | 38.2 | 3.56 |
| + Subcooling 5°C | 392.3 | 247.0 | 145.3 | 38.2 | 3.80 |
| + Superheat 5°C | 404.7 | 256.4 | 148.3 | 50.6 | 2.93 |
| + Subcool + Superheat | 404.7 | 247.0 | 157.7 | 50.6 | 3.12 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion from Analytical Matrix:**
> - **Subcooling always improves COP** (increases $RE$ without increasing $W_c$).
> - **Superheating *reduces COP* for R-134a** in this case, because the additional work input outweighs the extra refrigerating effect. However, superheating is still used in practice for **compressor safety** (dry compression).
> - **Best practice:** Use **effective subcooling + minimal superheat** for optimum real-world performance.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: P-H Chart State Point Mapping

The following Mermaid block is a *block-level functional architecture flow* representing the P-H diagram regions and the cycle path. (Since Mermaid cannot draw a true thermodynamic chart, this is an architectural mapping of state transitions.)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["REGION A: Subcooled Liquid - High P, Low h"] --> B["REGION B: Saturated Liquid Line - Left limb of dome"]
    B --> C["REGION C: Wet Region - Two phase mixture"]
    C --> D["REGION D: Saturated Vapour Line - Right limb of dome"]
    D --> E["REGION E: Superheated Vapour - Low P, High h"]
    E --> F["REGION F: Critical Point C - Apex of dome"]

    P1["STATE 1: Compressor Inlet - Saturated Vapour"] --> P2["STATE 2: Compressor Outlet - Superheated Vapour"]
    P2 --> P3["STATE 3: Condenser Outlet - Saturated Liquid"]
    P3 --> P4["STATE 4: After Expansion Valve - Wet mixture"]
    P4 --> P1

    P1 -.-> D
    P2 -.-> E
    P3 -.-> B
    P4 -.-> C
```

## 4.2 Mermaid System Schematic: Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EVAP["EVAPORATOR\nLow Pressure\nHeat Absorbed from cold space"] --> COMP["COMPRESSOR\nIsentropic Compression\nWork Input: Wc"]
    COMP --> COND["CONDENSER\nConstant Pressure\nHeat Rejected to ambient"]
    COND --> EXV["EXPANSION VALVE\nIsenthalpic Throttling\nPressure Drop"]
    EXV --> EVAP

    QL["QL = h1 minus h4\nRefrigerating Effect"] -.-> EVAP
    WC["WC = h2 minus h1\nCompressor Work"] -.-> COMP
    QH["QH = h2 minus h3\nHeat Rejected"] -.-> COND
```

## 4.3 P-H Chart Visual Representation (Block-Level Description)

Since a literal P-H chart cannot be rendered in Mermaid, here is a **structured textual description** of the cycle positions:

```
                    PRESSURE (P)
                         |
       (P2 = P3) ----------|------------------- (High P: Condenser)
                         |
              2 [Superheated Vapour] ← 3 [Sat. Liquid]
                         |
                         |
                         |
                         |
                         |
                         |
                         |
                         |          _______ Critical Point C
                         |         /    \    ← Apex of dome
                         |        /      \
                         |       /  WET   \
                         |      /  REGION  \
       (P1 = P4) ---------|-----4----1------- (Low P: Evaporator)
                         |       \           /
                         |        \         /
                         |         \_______/
                         |
                    (0 kPa)

   <------ h4 = h3  h1 ------ h2  h ---------->
                     ENTHALPY (h)
```

**State Point Location Summary:**

| State | Location on P-H Chart | Condition |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | On saturated vapour line (right limb of dome) | Dry saturated vapour |
| **2** | In superheated region, right of dome at $P_2$ | Superheated vapour |
| **3** | On saturated liquid line (left limb of dome) at $P_2$ | Saturated liquid |
| **4** | Inside wet region, at $P_1$ | Liquid + vapour mixture |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cycle Direction:** On the P-H chart, the cycle proceeds **1 → 2 (up and right) → 3 (horizontal left) → 4 (down and left) → 1 (horizontal right)** — forming a closed loop. The **clockwise traversal** of this loop on the P-H diagram is the visual signature of a refrigeration cycle.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the Pressure-Enthalpy (P-H) diagram. List its important features.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Pressure-Enthalpy (P-H) diagram** is a thermodynamic chart used to represent the state of a refrigerant during the vapour compression cycle, with **absolute pressure** (log scale) on the Y-axis and **enthalpy** (linear scale) on the X-axis.

**Important features (1 mark each):**
1. **Saturation dome** (bell-shaped curve) with critical point at apex, enclosing the wet (two-phase) region.
2. **Saturated liquid line** (left limb) and **saturated vapour line** (right limb) bound the wet region.
3. **Constant temperature, pressure, entropy, and volume lines** plotted within the chart for thermodynamic analysis.
4. **Subcooled liquid region** (left of saturated liquid line) and **superheated vapour region** (right of saturated vapour line).
5. The chart is **wide in the wet region** (horizontal constant-pressure lines) and shows **curved isotherms** in the superheated region.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the difference between a simple saturation cycle and a dry (superheated) compression cycle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Simple Saturation Cycle | Dry (Superheated) Compression Cycle |
|---|---|---|
| **Compressor inlet state (1)** | Saturated vapour ($x = 1$) | Superheated vapour ($T_1 > T_{sat}$ at $P_1$) |
| **Purpose** | Theoretical/ideal benchmark | Practical, prevents liquid slugging in compressor |
| **Refrigerating Effect** | $RE = h_1 - h_3$ (smaller) | $RE = h_1' - h_3$ (larger, since $h_1' > h_1$) |
| **Compressor Work** | $W_c = h_2 - h_1$ (smaller) | $W_c' = h_2' - h_1'$ (larger) |
| **COP** | Higher theoretical COP | COP may decrease (depends on refrigerant) |

**Conclusion:** Superheating improves compressor safety but may reduce COP — hence real systems aim for **just enough superheat** (≈ 5°C) to prevent liquid carryover.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**A vapour compression refrigeration cycle using R-12 operates between an evaporator pressure of 1.2 bar and a condenser pressure of 9 bar. The refrigerant is dry saturated at the compressor inlet and saturated liquid at the condenser exit.**

**(a) [7 marks]** Represent the cycle on a P-H diagram and find the refrigerating effect, compressor work, and COP.

**(b) [7 marks)** If the saturated liquid from the condenser is subcooled by 5°C before throttling, determine the new refrigerating effect, compressor work, COP, and comment on the improvement.

---

### Question A — Model Solution

**Given:**
- $P_1 = 1.2 \text{ bar}$ (evaporator), $P_2 = 9 \text{ bar}$ (condenser)
- State 1: Dry saturated vapour; State 3: Saturated liquid
- Refrigerant: R-12 (freon-12)

**From R-12 saturation tables:**

At $P_1 = 1.2 \text{ bar}$ (≈ $T_{sat} = -22°C$):
- $h_1 = h_g = 346.7 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $s_1 = s_g = 1.557 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$
- $v_1 = 0.160 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$ (not needed here)

At $P_2 = P_3 = 9 \text{ bar}$ (≈ $T_{sat} = 38°C$):
- $h_3 = h_f = 238.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $s_3 = 1.0 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ (not needed for isenthalpic throttle)

**At $P_2 = 9 \text{ bar}$ and $s_2 = s_1 = 1.557 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$:**
- From R-12 superheated tables: $h_2 = 382.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (interpolated)

#### Part (a) Solution

**Refrigerating Effect (RE):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_4 &= h_3 = 238.5 \text{ kJ/kg} \quad \text{(isenthalpic throttle)} \\
RE &= h_1 - h_4 = 346.7 - 238.5 \\
RE &= 108.2 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compressor Work (Wc):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_c &= h_2 - h_1 = 382.5 - 346.7 \\
W_c &= 35.8 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**COP:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
COP_{ref} &= \frac{RE}{W_c} = \frac{108.2}{35.8} \\
COP_{ref} &= 3.02
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b) Solution — With 5°C Subcooling

Subcooling of 5°C below $T_{sat} = 38°C$ means the liquid is cooled to $T = 33°C$ at $P = 9 \text{ bar}$.

For an incompressible liquid approximation, subcooling reduces enthalpy by $c_p \cdot \Delta T$:

$$h_3' = h_3 - c_{p,liq} \cdot \Delta T_{sub}$$

For R-12 liquid, $c_{p,liq} \approx 0.93 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_3' &= 238.5 - (0.93)(5) \\
&= 238.5 - 4.65 \\
&= 233.85 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

After throttle: $h_4' = h_3' = 233.85 \text{ kJ/kg}$

**New Refrigerating Effect:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RE' &= h_1 - h_4' = 346.7 - 233.85 \\
RE' &= 112.85 \text{ kJ/kg} \quad \text{(increased by 4.65 kJ/kg)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compressor Work:** Unchanged ($W_c$ depends only on $h_1$ and $h_2$):

$$
W_c = 35.8 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**New COP:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
COP_{ref}' &= \frac{RE'}{W_c} = \frac{112.85}{35.8} \\
COP_{ref}' &= 3.152
\end{aligned}
$$

**Improvement Analysis:**
- $RE$ increased by ≈ **4.3%** (from 108.2 to 112.85 kJ/kg).
- $COP$ increased by ≈ **4.4%** (from 3.02 to 3.152).
- $W_c$ unchanged → **subcooling always improves COP** because it increases the refrigerating effect without additional work input.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Common mistakes students make on this type of question:
> 1. **Forgetting that $h_4 = h_3$** when using a throttle valve — this is the #1 error. Always state this assumption explicitly in your answer.
> 2. **Using $c_p$ of vapour instead of liquid** for subcooling enthalpy correction. Subcooling happens in the **liquid** state, so use $c_{p,liq} \approx 0.9$ to $1.0 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, not the vapour $c_p \approx 0.6$ to $0.8$.
> 3. **Not commenting on the qualitative result** — KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for the *engineering insight* (e.g., "subcooling improves COP without extra work"). Always include a brief conclusion.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency** — ensure enthalpies are in kJ/kg consistently. Mixing kJ/kg and kcal/kg is a common losing-mark mistake.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**An actual vapour compression refrigeration cycle using R-134a operates with the following state points:**
- **State 1:** Superheated vapour at $-5°C$, 2.5 bar (suction line after evaporator)
- **State 2:** Superheated vapour at 12 bar (compressor discharge)
- **State 3:** Saturated liquid at 12 bar (condenser exit)
- **State 4:** Two-phase mixture at 2.5 bar (after expansion)

**(a) [7 marks]** Determine the refrigerating effect, compressor work, COP, and heat rejected per kg of refrigerant. Comment on whether the compression is dry or wet.

**(b) [7 marks]** A cooling load of 20 TR is to be met. Calculate the mass flow rate, compressor power input, and the rate of heat rejection in the condenser. Take 1 TR = 3.517 kW.

---

### Question B — Model Solution (Outline)

**Given R-134a Properties:**

| State | $T$ (°C) | $P$ (bar) | $h$ (kJ/kg) | $s$ (kJ/kg·K) | Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | -5 | 2.5 | 399.4 | 1.738 | Superheated (5 K above $T_{sat}$) |
| 2 | ~50 | 12 | 430.0 | — | Superheated |
| 3 | ~46 | 12 | 263.0 | — | Saturated liquid |
| 4 | -5 | 2.5 | 263.0 | — | Wet (h4 = h3) |

**At $P_1 = 2.5 \text{ bar}$**, $T_{sat} \approx -10°C$. Since the refrigerant is at $-5°C$ (5 K above saturation), it is **superheated by 5°C** → **dry compression** ✅.

#### Part (a) — Performance Parameters

$$
\begin{aligned}
RE &= h_1 - h_4 = 399.4 - 263.0 = 136.4 \text{ kJ/kg} \\
W_c &= h_2 - h_1 = 430.0 - 399.4 = 30.6 \text{ kJ/kg} \\
COP_{ref} &= \frac{136.4}{30.6} = 4.46 \\
Q_H &= h_2 - h_3 = 430.0 - 263.0 = 167.0 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Dry Compression:** Since state 1 is in the **superheated region** (above the saturated vapour line), the compression is **dry** — no liquid droplets enter the compressor. ✅

#### Part (b) — Capacity Calculations

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_L &= 20 \times 3.517 = 70.34 \text{ kW} \\
\dot{m} &= \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{RE} = \frac{70.34}{136.4} = 0.5158 \text{ kg/s} \\
\dot{W}_c &= \dot{m} \cdot W_c = 0.5158 \times 30.6 = 15.78 \text{ kW} \\
\dot{Q}_H &= \dot{m} \cdot Q_H = 0.5158 \times 167.0 = 86.13 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Question B:**
> 1. **Skipping the dry/wet verification:** Many students skip explicitly stating whether compression is dry or wet. KTU awards marks for this conclusion. Always check $T_1$ against $T_{sat}$ at $P_1$.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by mass flow rate:** Part (b) requires $\dot{m}$, $\dot{W}_c$, $\dot{Q}_H$ — these are *rate* quantities. Many students report per-kg values, losing 4–5 marks.
> 3. **Unit conversion errors:** $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$ is a *must-remember* constant in refrigeration. Some textbooks use 3.516 or 3.5 — check the KTU convention.
> 4. **Sign of heat rejected:** $\dot{Q}_H$ is always greater than $\dot{Q}_L$ (due to compressor work addition). If your answer shows the opposite, recheck.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — P-H Diagrams & Vapour Compression Cycles**

### Core Concepts
- [ ] **P-H diagram axes:** Pressure (log, Y-axis) vs. Enthalpy (linear, X-axis).
- [ ] **Saturation dome:** Bell-shaped curve with critical point at the apex; bounded by saturated liquid and vapour lines.
- [ ] **Four cycle processes:** Isentropic compression, constant-pressure condensation, isenthalpic throttling, constant-pressure evaporation.
- [ ] **Four state points:** 1 (saturated/superheated vapour) → 2 (superheated vapour) → 3 (saturated/subcooled liquid) → 4 (wet mixture).

### Critical Formulas (Memorize)
- [ ] $RE = h_1 - h_4 = h_1 - h_3$
- [ ] $W_c = h_2 - h_1$
- [ ] $COP_{ref} = \dfrac{RE}{W_c} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$
- [ ] $COP_{hp} = COP_{ref} + 1$
- [ ] $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_L}{RE}$
- [ ] $\dot{W}_c = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_1)$
- [ ] $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$

### Engineering Insights
- [ ] **Subcooling always improves COP** (increases RE without increasing Wc).
- [ ] **Superheating may reduce COP** (increases both RE and Wc, but Wc effect dominates for most refrigerants).
- [ ] **Dry compression** is essential for compressor safety — never let liquid enter the compressor.
- [ ] **Isentropic efficiency** correction makes actual work input *larger* than the ideal $h_2 - h_1$.
- [ ] **Throttling is irreversible** — no work output, no heat transfer, enthalpy conserved.
- [ ] **The cycle on P-H chart is a closed loop traversed clockwise.**

### Common KTU Mistakes to Avoid
- [ ] Confusing $h_3$ and $h_4$ — they are equal ONLY across the throttle.
- [ ] Using vapour $c_p$ instead of liquid $c_p$ for subcooling enthalpy.
- [ ] Forgetting to convert TR to kW when computing $\dot{m}$ or $\dot{W}_c$.
- [ ] Not stating the dry/wet condition of compression explicitly.
- [ ] Reading incorrect values from the P-H chart (always cross-check with saturation tables).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Components of Mechanical Refrigerator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Components of Mechanical Refrigerator ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Mechanical Refrigerator** is a thermodynamic device that uses mechanical work input (typically through an electric motor-driven compressor) to extract heat from a low-temperature region (refrigerated space) and reject it to a high-temperature region (surroundings/ambient), thereby producing a cooling effect. The most widely employed mechanical refrigeration system is the **Vapor Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS)**, which circulates a refrigerant through a closed loop undergoing phase change. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 2):** The mechanical refrigeration system consists of **four essential components** that form the primary refrigeration loop, supplemented by several **auxiliary components** that ensure safe, efficient, and reliable operation. ### 1.2 The Four Essential Components (Primary Loop) | # | Component | Function | State of Refrigerant | |---|-----------|----------|----------------------| | 1 | **Compressor** | Raises pressure and temperature of refrigerant vapor | Suction → Discharge (Low P → High P vapor) | | 2 | **Condenser** | Rejects heat from hot vapor to surroundings | High P vapor → High P liquid | | 3 | **Expansion Device** | Reduces pressure of liquid refrigerant | High P liquid → Low P liquid-vapor mix | | 4 | **Evaporator** | Absorbs heat from refrigerated space | Low P liquid → Low P vapor | ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Water Pump of Cold" Imagine you have a sponge soaked in cold water. To make the sponge **colder** so it can absorb more heat from a room, you would need to: 1. **Squeeze the sponge** (Compressor) — applying force to push the "cold" to a higher pressure, 2. **Place it on a sunny window** (Condenser) — letting it release heat to surroundings, 3. **Release the squeeze through a tiny nozzle** (Expansion Valve) — sudden pressure drop makes it colder, 4. **Place it in the room to absorb heat** (Evaporator) — the now-cold sponge soaks up heat from the room. > [!NOTE] > The refrigerant in a VCRS acts exactly like this "sponge of cold" — it circulates, absorbing heat at the evaporator and rejecting it at the condenser, with the compressor providing the necessary energy to maintain this continuous flow. ### 1.4 System Schematic Overview ``` ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ VAPOR COMPRESSION REFRIGERATION LOOP │ │ │ │ COMPRESSOR → CONDENSER → EXPANSION → EVAP │ │ ↑ │ │ └────────────── (RETURN) ──────────────────┘ │ └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > The standard refrigeration **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** for a mechanical refrigerator is defined as: > > $$\text{COP} = \frac{\text{Desired Effect (Refrigeration Effect)}}{\text{Work Input}} = \frac{Q_E}{W_{net}}$$ ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Detailed Component Analysis #### 2.1.1 Compressor (The Heart of the System) **Purpose:** The compressor is the only power-consuming component in the primary loop. It: - Draws low-pressure refrigerant vapor from the evaporator (suction) - Compresses it to a high-pressure, high-temperature vapor - Delivers it to the condenser (discharge) **Why is compression needed?** - To raise the saturation temperature of the refrigerant **above the ambient temperature**, so heat can be rejected naturally to surroundings. - To maintain continuous refrigerant circulation against pressure drops. **Types of Compressors (KTU High-Yield):** | Type | Mechanism | Typical Application | |------|-----------|---------------------| | **Reciprocating** | Piston-cylinder | Small to medium capacity (domestic, commercial) | | **Rotary (Vane)** | Rotating eccentric vane | Small capacity (window AC, refrigerators) | | **Scroll** | Orbiting & fixed scrolls | Medium capacity (split ACs) | | **Screw (Helical)** | Rotating helical rotors | Large capacity (industrial chillers) | | **Centrifugal** | Impeller with high-speed rotation | Very large capacity (district cooling) | **Key Compressor Performance Parameters:** - **Displacement Volume** $V_d = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L \cdot n \cdot N$ where D = bore, L = stroke, n = number of cylinders, N = RPM - **Volumetric Efficiency** $\eta_v = \frac{\text{Actual Mass Flow}}{\text{Displacement Mass Flow}}$ - **Isentropic / Adiabatic Efficiency** $\eta_{isen} = \frac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_{2a} - h_1}$ --- #### 2.1.2 Condenser (Heat Rejection Unit) **Purpose:** Reject latent heat of condensation plus superheat from the high-pressure vapor to the cooling medium (air or water). **Operating Cycle Inside Condenser:** 1. **Desuperheating zone** — vapor cools from discharge temperature to saturation temperature (sensible heat rejected) 2. **Condensation zone** — vapor condenses to saturated liquid at constant temperature/pressure (latent heat rejected) 3. **Subcooling zone** — liquid cools below saturation temperature (sensible heat rejected) **Types of Condensers:** | Type | Cooling Medium | Application | |------|----------------|-------------| | **Air-cooled** | Ambient air | Small capacity, domestic refrigerators | | **Water-cooled (shell & tube)** | Water | Medium to large capacity | | **Evaporative** | Air + Water spray | Large capacity, hot climates | --- #### 2.1.3 Expansion Device (Throttling Element) **Purpose:** Performs **throttling process** (isenthalpic expansion) which: - Drops pressure from condenser pressure to evaporator pressure - Causes partial flashing of liquid → vapor (flash gas) - Produces cold liquid-vapor mixture entering evaporator **Types of Expansion Devices:** | Type | Mechanism | Application | |------|-----------|-------------| | **Capillary Tube** | Fixed bore length tube | Small hermetic systems (domestic fridges) | | **Automatic Expansion Valve (AXV)** | Constant evaporator pressure | Older systems | | **Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TEV/TXV)** | Controls superheat | Most commercial systems | | **Float Valve** | Maintains liquid level | Flooded evaporators | | **Electronic Expansion Valve (EEV)** | Microprocessor controlled | Modern precision systems | > [!NOTE] > **Throttling process is irreversible and isenthalpic:** $h_3 = h_4$. This means **no work is done and no heat transfer** occurs during expansion — only pressure drop with temperature reduction. --- #### 2.1.4 Evaporator (Heat Absorption Unit) **Purpose:** Absorb heat from the refrigerated space as the refrigerant evaporates at low pressure/temperature. **Operating Zones:** 1. **Vapor-liquid zone** (if expansion produces flash gas) — wet mixture evaporates 2. **Evaporation zone** — saturated liquid → saturated vapor at constant T, P (latent heat absorbed) 3. **Superheating zone** — saturated vapor heated above saturation temperature (sensible heat absorbed) **Types of Evaporators:** | Type | Configuration | Application | |------|---------------|-------------| | **Bare / Finned tube** | Copper tubes with fins | Forced air cooling | | **Plate type** | Refrigerant in plates | Milk chillers, cold storage | | **Shell & coil** | Coil inside shell | Water coolers | | **Shell & tube** | Tubes inside shell | Chilled water production | | **Flooded** | Liquid refrigerant fills shell | Large industrial systems | --- ### 2.2 Auxiliary Components of VCRS > [!IMPORTANT] > Beyond the four primary components, several auxiliary components are essential for safe and efficient operation. These are **frequently asked in KTU exams**: | Auxiliary Component | Function | |--------------------|----------| | **Receiver** | Stores excess liquid refrigerant; ensures liquid-only supply to expansion device | | **Filter-Drier** | Removes moisture and contaminants from refrigerant | | **Sight Glass** | Visual indicator of refrigerant state and moisture | | **Accumulator** | Prevents liquid slugging at compressor suction | | **Oil Separator** | Removes lubricating oil from discharge vapor | | **Liquid Line Solenoid Valve** | Shut-off control during off-cycles | | **High/Low Pressure Cut-outs** | Safety devices for abnormal pressures | | **Thermostat** | Temperature-based on/off control | | **Reversing Valve** | Switches flow direction (heat pump operation) | --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $COP = \dfrac{Q_E}{W_{net}}$ | Coefficient of Performance | dimensionless | | 2 | $COP_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_1}{T_2 - T_1}$ | Carnot COP (ideal limit) | dimensionless | | 3 | $COP_{ideal} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | Ideal VCRS COP | dimensionless | | 4 | $Q_E = m (h_1 - h_4)$ | Refrigeration Effect | kW or kJ/s | | 5 | $W_c = m (h_2 - h_1)$ | Compressor Work | kW | | 6 | $Q_C = m (h_2 - h_3)$ | Heat Rejected at Condenser | kW | | 7 | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{V_d \cdot \eta_v}{v_1}$ | Mass flow rate of refrigerant | kg/s | | 8 | $V_d = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L \cdot n \cdot N$ | Compressor Displacement Volume | m³/s | | 9 | $\eta_{isen} = \dfrac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_{2a} - h_1}$ | Isentropic Efficiency | dimensionless | | 10 | $\eta_{carnot} = \dfrac{COP_{actual}}{COP_{carnot}}$ | Relative / Carnot Efficiency | dimensionless | | 11 | $\dot{V}_{swept} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L \cdot N \cdot \frac{n}{60}$ | Swept volume per second | m³/s | **Where:** - $T_1$ = Evaporator absolute temperature (K) - $T_2$ = Condenser absolute temperature (K) - State points: **1** = Compressor inlet, **2** = Compressor outlet, **3** = Condenser outlet, **4** = Evaporator inlet - $h$ = Enthalpy (kJ/kg), $v_1$ = Specific volume at suction (m³/kg) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** The refrigerant **mass flow rate $\dot{m}$** is found by dividing the required refrigeration effect $Q_E$ by the specific refrigeration effect $(h_1 - h_4)$: > > $$\dot{m} = \frac{Q_E}{h_1 - h_4} \quad [\text{kg/s}]$$ ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Domestic Refrigerators:** Reciprocating/rotary compressor + capillary tube + finned tube evaporator - **Cold Storage Plants:** Screw compressor + shell-and-tube condenser + TEV + plate evaporators - **Ice Plants:** Reciprocating compressors + water-cooled condensers + flooded evaporators - **Supermarket Display:** Multi-evaporator systems with EEV and scroll compressors - **Pharmaceutical Cold Chain:** Precise TEV/EEV + redundant compressors for reliability ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical COP of Vapor Compression Refrigeration System **Starting Premises:** - Steady-state, steady-flow operation - No pressure drop in connecting pipelines (ideal) - Throttling process: $h_3 = h_4$ (isenthalpic) - Compression: ideal isentropic, $s_1 = s_2$ - Refrigerant leaves condenser as saturated liquid (state 3) - Refrigerant enters compressor as saturated vapor (state 1) **Step 1 — Apply Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) to each component:** For **Evaporator** (control volume, no work): $$ q_E = h_1 - h_4 $$ For **Compressor** (control volume, adiabatic, no heat transfer): $$ w_c = h_2 - h_1 $$ For **Condenser** (control volume, no work): $$ q_C = h_2 - h_3 $$ For **Expansion Valve** (throttling — adiabatic, no work): $$ h_3 = h_4 $$ **Step 2 — Apply the definition of COP:** By definition, the coefficient of performance of a refrigerator is the ratio of the desired effect (heat extracted in evaporator) to the work input (compressor work): $$ \text{COP} = \frac{\text{Refrigeration Effect}}{\text{Compressor Work}} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute the enthalpy terms:** $$ \text{COP}_{ideal} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} $$ **Step 4 — Introduce subcooling (state 3 becomes subcooled liquid below saturation):** If the refrigerant leaves the condenser at temperature $T_3$ lower than saturation temperature at $P_2$, then: $$ q_E = h_1 - h_4 = h_1 - h_3 \quad \text{(since } h_3 = h_4 \text{)} $$ $$ \text{COP}_{subcooled} = \frac{h_1 - h_3}{h_2 - h_1} $$ **Step 5 — Introduce superheating (state 1 becomes superheated vapor above saturation):** If the refrigerant leaves the evaporator at temperature $T_1$ higher than saturation temperature at $P_1$: $$ q_E = h_{1'} - h_4 $$ $$ w_c = h_2 - h_{1'} $$ $$ \text{COP}_{actual} = \frac{h_{1'} - h_4}{h_2 - h_{1'}} $$ **Step 6 — Final General Form:** $$ \boxed{\text{COP} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}} $$ > This is the **most frequently tested formula** in KTU Module 2 problems. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Compressor Mass Flow Rate from Volumetric Efficiency **Given:** A reciprocating compressor with bore $D$, stroke $L$, number of cylinders $n$, running at $N$ RPM, and refrigerant specific volume at suction $v_1$. **Step 1 — Volume swept per cylinder per stroke:** $$ V_{cyl} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L $$ **Step 2 — Total swept volume per minute:** $$ V_{swept/min} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L \cdot n \cdot N \quad \text{[m}^3\text{/min]} $$ **Step 3 — Total swept volume per second:** $$ V_d = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L \cdot n \cdot \frac{N}{60} \quad \text{[m}^3\text{/s]} $$ **Step 4 — Actual mass flow rate (accounting for volumetric losses):** $$ \dot{m} = \frac{V_d \cdot \eta_v}{v_1} \quad \text{[kg/s]} $$ **Step 5 — Refrigeration Effect produced:** $$ Q_E = \dot{m} \cdot (h_1 - h_4) \quad \text{[kW]} $$ --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) **Problem:** A refrigerator using R-134a operates between $-10°C$ (evaporator) and $40°C$ (condenser). Given: - $h_1 = 241.3$ kJ/kg (saturated vapor at $-10°C$) - $h_2 = 271.7$ kJ/kg (superheated vapor at 40°C, isentropic compression) - $h_3 = 111.2$ kJ/kg (saturated liquid at 40°C) - Required cooling load: $Q_E = 5$ kW **Find:** (a) COP, (b) Compressor work, (c) Mass flow rate, (d) Heat rejected in condenser. **Solution:** **Step (a) — COP:** Since throttling: $h_4 = h_3 = 111.2$ kJ/kg $$ \text{COP} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} = \frac{241.3 - 111.2}{271.7 - 241.3} $$ $$ \text{COP} = \frac{130.1}{30.4} = 4.28 $$ **Step (b) — Compressor Work per kg:** $$ w_c = h_2 - h_1 = 271.7 - 241.3 = 30.4 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **Step (c) — Mass Flow Rate:** $$ \dot{m} = \frac{Q_E}{h_1 - h_4} = \frac{5}{130.1} $$ $$ \dot{m} = 0.0384 \text{ kg/s} = 138.4 \text{ kg/hr} $$ **Step (d) — Heat Rejected in Condenser:** $$ Q_C = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_3) = 0.0384 \times (271.7 - 111.2) $$ $$ Q_C = 0.0384 \times 160.5 = 6.165 \text{ kW} $$ **Verification (Energy Balance):** $$ Q_E + W_c = Q_C \quad \Rightarrow \quad 5 + 1.167 = 6.167 \text{ kW} \checkmark $$ --- ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Component Selection Guide | Selection Criterion | Compressor | Condenser | Expansion Device | Evaporator | |---------------------|-----------|-----------|------------------|-----------| | **Capacity Range** | Reciprocating: <100 kW, Centrifugal: >500 kW | Air-cooled <50 kW; Water-cooled >50 kW | Capillary <2 kW; TEV for all others | Finned: air cooling; Shell-coil: liquids | | **Cost** | Rotary/Capillary cheapest | Air-cooled most expensive to operate | Capillary cheapest | Plate type most expensive | | **Maintenance** | Reciprocating high | Water-cooled high (scaling) | TEV moderately high | Flooded high | | **Efficiency** | Centrifugal highest at full load | Evaporative highest | EEV highest | DX vs Flooded depends on application | | **Control** | Capacity control by unloaders | Variable fans | TEV/EEV auto | TXV superheat control | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Vapor Compression Refrigeration System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Compressor
Low P Vapor In
High P Vapor Out] -->|Discharge
Hot Vapor| B[Condenser
Heat Rejected
to Surroundings] B -->|Subcooled
Liquid| C[Receiver
Stores Liquid
Refrigerant] C -->|Liquid Line| D[Filter Drier
Removes Moisture
and Dirt] D -->|Clean Liquid| E[Sight Glass
Moisture and
Flow Indicator] E -->|Liquid| F[Expansion Valve
TEV/Capillary
Throttling] F -->|Low P
Two Phase| G[Evaporator
Heat Absorbed
from Load] G -->|Low P Vapor| H[Accumulator
Prevents Liquid
Slugging] H -->|Dry Vapor| A style A fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#ffffff style B fill:#ffa94d,stroke:#e8590c,color:#ffffff style C fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#fab005,color:#000000 style D fill:#74c0fc,stroke:#1971c2,color:#ffffff style E fill:#a9e34b,stroke:#5c940d,color:#000000 style F fill:#e599f7,stroke:#862e9c,color:#ffffff style G fill:#63e6be,stroke:#099268,color:#000000 style H fill:#f783ac,stroke:#c2255c,color:#ffffff ``` ### 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Refrigeration Cycle States ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CycleRefrig["VCRS THERMODYNAMIC CYCLE"] direction TB State1["STATE 1
Saturated Vapor
Low Pressure

h1, s1, P1"] State2["STATE 2
Superheated Vapor

Saturated Liquid

Two-Phase Mixture

COMPRESSION

W = h2 - h1"] --> State1 State1 --> CompProc State2 --> CondProc CondProc["HEAT REJECTION

Qc = h2 - h3"] --> State3 State3 --> ExpProc ExpProc["THROTTLING
