h3 = h4"] --> State4 State4 --> EvapProc EvapProc["HEAT ABSORPTION
4 to 1
QE = h1 - h4"] --> State1 style State1 fill:#ff8787,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#ffffff style State2 fill:#ffa94d,stroke:#e8590c,color:#ffffff style State3 fill:#4dabf7,stroke:#1864ab,color:#ffffff style State4 fill:#69db7c,stroke:#2b8a3e,color:#ffffff style CompProc fill:#ffe066,stroke:#fab005,color:#000000 style CondProc fill:#ffe066,stroke:#fab005,color:#000000 style ExpProc fill:#ffe066,stroke:#fab005,color:#000000 style EvapProc fill:#ffe066,stroke:#fab005,color:#000000 ``` ### 4.3 Component Classification Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB MainComp["PRIMARY COMPONENTS
of Mechanical Refrigerator"] MainComp --> CompGroup["COMPRESSOR"] CompGroup --> C1["Reciprocating
Piston-Cylinder"] CompGroup --> C2["Rotary
Vane Type"] CompGroup --> C3["Scroll
Orbiting Scroll"] CompGroup --> C4["Screw
Helical Rotor"] CompGroup --> C5["Centrifugal
Impeller"] MainComp --> CondGroup["CONDENSER"] CondGroup --> CD1["Air Cooled
Finned Tube"] CondGroup --> CD2["Water Cooled
Shell and Tube"] CondGroup --> CD3["Evaporative
Spray Type"] MainComp --> ExpGroup["EXPANSION DEVICE"] ExpGroup --> EX1["Capillary Tube
Fixed Restriction"] ExpGroup --> EX2["TEV / TXV
Thermostatic"] ExpGroup --> EX3["AXV
Automatic"] ExpGroup --> EX4["Float Valve
Level Controlled"] ExpGroup --> EX5["EEV
Electronic"] MainComp --> EvapGroup["EVAPORATOR"] EvapGroup --> EV1["DX Direct Expansion
Finned Coil"] EvapGroup --> EV2["Flooded
Shell and Coil"] EvapGroup --> EV3["Plate Type
Surface Contact"] EvapGroup --> EV4["Shell and Tube
Liquid Chilling"] MainComp --> AuxGroup["AUXILIARY COMPONENTS"] AuxGroup --> AU1["Receiver"] AuxGroup --> AU2["Filter Drier"] AuxGroup --> AU3["Sight Glass"] AuxGroup --> AU4["Accumulator"] AuxGroup --> AU5["Oil Separator"] AuxGroup --> AU6["Solenoid Valve"] AuxGroup --> AU7["Pressure Cutout"] style MainComp fill:#495057,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff style CompGroup fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#ffffff style CondGroup fill:#ffa94d,stroke:#e8590c,color:#ffffff style ExpGroup fill:#74c0fc,stroke:#1971c2,color:#ffffff style EvapGroup fill:#69db7c,stroke:#2b8a3e,color:#ffffff style AuxGroup fill:#b197fc,stroke:#5f3dc4,color:#ffffff ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *List the four essential components of a mechanical vapor compression refrigeration system. State the function of each in one line.* **Model Answer:** | # | Component | Function | |---|-----------|----------| | 1 | **Compressor** | Compresses low-pressure refrigerant vapor to high-pressure, high-temperature vapor (raises pressure and temperature). | | 2 | **Condenser** | Rejects heat from hot, high-pressure refrigerant vapor to the surroundings, condensing it to liquid. | | 3 | **Expansion Device** | Reduces pressure of liquid refrigerant suddenly (throttling), causing partial vaporization and temperature drop. | | 4 | **Evaporator** | Absorbs heat from the refrigerated space as refrigerant evaporates at low pressure and temperature. | **Marks Distribution:** Component listing = 2 marks; Functions = 1 mark. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *Differentiate between TEV (Thermostatic Expansion Valve) and Capillary Tube as expansion devices in refrigeration systems.* **Model Answer:** | Feature | TEV (Thermostatic Expansion Valve) | Capillary Tube | |---------|-----------------------------------|----------------| | **Operation** | Modulates refrigerant flow based on evaporator superheat | Fixed flow based on tube dimensions | | **Adjustability** | Adjustable — responds to load variations | Non-adjustable once selected | | **Capacity Range** | Suitable for wide capacity range (small to large) | Suitable for small, fixed-load systems | | **Complexity** | Has moving parts, sensing bulb, diaphragm | Simple fixed-bore copper tube | | **Application** | Commercial refrigeration, AC systems | Domestic refrigerators, window ACs | | **Cost** | Higher cost | Lowest cost | **Marks Distribution:** 6 distinct valid differences = 3 marks. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full Length Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] **(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the working of a vapor compression refrigeration system. List the various types of compressors and condensers used in VCRS. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A vapor compression refrigeration cycle uses R-12 as refrigerant. The operating temperatures are $-5°C$ in the evaporator and $40°C$ in the condenser. The refrigerant is dry saturated at the compressor inlet and saturated liquid at the condenser outlet. The refrigeration load is 10 kW. For R-12: $h_1 = 183.2$ kJ/kg, $h_2 = 209.6$ kJ/kg, $h_3 = 74.5$ kJ/kg. Calculate: (i) Mass flow rate of refrigerant (ii) Compressor work per kg and total compressor power (iii) COP of the system (iv) Heat rejected in the condenser **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q.A(a):** **Block Diagram:** ``` COMPRESSOR → CONDENSER → EXPANSION → EVAPORATOR → (back to COMPRESSOR) (1→2) (2→3) (3→4) (4→1) ``` **Working Principle:** - State 1: Low-pressure, low-temperature saturated vapor enters compressor from evaporator - State 1→2: Compressor compresses vapor isentropically, raising pressure and temperature - State 2→3: Hot vapor passes through condenser, rejecting heat to surroundings and condensing to liquid - State 3→4: Liquid refrigerant passes through expansion device — pressure drops, partial flashing occurs, temperature drops sharply - State 4→1: Cold two-phase mixture enters evaporator, absorbs heat from refrigerated space and evaporates, returning to state 1 **Types of Compressors:** 1. Reciprocating (piston type) 2. Rotary (vane type) 3. Centrifugal (turbo type) 4. Scroll (orbital scroll) 5. Screw (helical rotary) **Types of Condensers:** 1. Air-cooled condensers (natural or forced convection) 2. Water-cooled condensers (shell & coil, shell & tube, double tube) 3. Evaporative condensers --- **Model Solution for Q.A(b):** **Given Data:** - $h_1 = 183.2$ kJ/kg, $h_2 = 209.6$ kJ/kg, $h_3 = 74.5$ kJ/kg - Throttling: $h_4 = h_3 = 74.5$ kJ/kg - Refrigeration load: $Q_E = 10$ kW **(i) Mass flow rate:** $$ \dot{m} = \frac{Q_E}{h_1 - h_4} = \frac{10}{183.2 - 74.5} $$ $$ \dot{m} = \frac{10}{108.7} = 0.092 \text{ kg/s} $$ **(ii) Compressor work per kg and total power:** $$ w_c = h_2 - h_1 = 209.6 - 183.2 = 26.4 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ $$ W_c = \dot{m} \cdot w_c = 0.092 \times 26.4 $$ $$ W_c = 2.43 \text{ kW} $$ **(iii) COP of the system:** $$ \text{COP} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} = \frac{108.7}{26.4} = 4.12 $$ **(iv) Heat rejected in the condenser:** $$ Q_C = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_3) = 0.092 \times (209.6 - 74.5) $$ $$ Q_C = 0.092 \times 135.1 = 12.43 \text{ kW} $$ **Verification:** $Q_E + W_c = 10 + 2.43 = 12.43$ kW $= Q_C$ ✓ --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative** [KTU University Exam - May 2023] **(a)** Describe in detail the construction and working of a hermetically sealed reciprocating compressor used in domestic refrigerators. Explain why such compressors are preferred over open-type compressors for small capacity systems. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A refrigeration system has a cooling load of 50 kW. The compressor has 4 cylinders, each of bore 60 mm and stroke 50 mm, running at 900 RPM. The volumetric efficiency is 80% and refrigerant specific volume at suction is 0.10 m³/kg. Calculate: (i) Displacement volume per second (ii) Mass flow rate of refrigerant (iii) Refrigeration effect per kg if COP = 4.0 (iv) Compressor power required **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q.B(a):** **Construction of Hermetically Sealed Reciprocating Compressor:** A hermetic compressor houses the motor and compressor in a single welded steel shell, making the unit completely sealed from the atmosphere. Major components include: 1. **Hermetic Shell** — Welded steel casing containing motor and compressor; refrigerant surrounds the motor providing cooling. 2. **Electric Motor** — Single-phase or three-phase induction motor mounted inside the shell. 3. **Crankshaft** — Connected directly to motor rotor via eccentric shaft. 4. **Piston and Cylinder** — Reciprocating piston in a sealed cylinder bore. 5. **Suction and Discharge Valves** — Spring-loaded reed or disc valves. 6. **Internal Muffler** — Reduces suction pulsations. 7. **Terminal Connector** — Electrical supply terminals sealed on shell. 8. **Oil Sump** — Lubricating oil at bottom of shell. **Working:** - Motor receives electrical power and rotates crankshaft - Piston moves down creating low pressure → suction valve opens → low-pressure vapor drawn from evaporator - Piston moves up compressing vapor → discharge valve opens → high-pressure vapor pushed into condenser - Continuous reciprocation produces pulsating flow **Advantages of Hermetic Compressor over Open-Type:** 1. **No leakage:** Completely sealed — refrigerant cannot leak out 2. **No shaft seal:** Eliminates the weakest point in open compressors 3. **Motor cooling:** Suction vapor cools the motor → better efficiency 4. **Lower maintenance:** No external seals to replace 5. **Quieter operation:** Muffled by shell 6. **Compact and lightweight:** Suitable for domestic refrigerators --- **Model Solution for Q.B(b):** **Given:** - $Q_E = 50$ kW, $D = 60$ mm = 0.06 m, $L = 50$ mm = 0.05 m - $n = 4$ cylinders, $N = 900$ RPM - $\eta_v = 0.80$, $v_1 = 0.10$ m³/kg, COP = 4.0 **(i) Displacement volume per second:** $$ V_d = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L \cdot n \cdot \frac{N}{60} $$ $$ V_d = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.06)^2 \times 0.05 \times 4 \times \frac{900}{60} $$ $$ V_d = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0036 \times 0.05 \times 4 \times 15 $$ $$ V_d = 0.7854 \times 0.0036 \times 0.05 \times 60 $$ $$ V_d = 0.7854 \times 0.0108 = 0.00848 \text{ m}^3\text{/s} $$ **(ii) Mass flow rate:** $$ \dot{m} = \frac{V_d \cdot \eta_v}{v_1} = \frac{0.00848 \times 0.80}{0.10} $$ $$ \dot{m} = \frac{0.006784}{0.10} = 0.06784 \text{ kg/s} $$ **(iii) Refrigeration effect per kg:** $$ \text{COP} = \frac{Q_E}{W_c} = \frac{q_E}{w_c} = 4.0 $$ Also: $$ Q_E = \dot{m} \cdot q_E \quad \Rightarrow \quad q_E = \frac{Q_E}{\dot{m}} = \frac{50}{0.06784} $$ $$ q_E = 737.0 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **(iv) Compressor power required:** $$ W_c = \frac{Q_E}{\text{COP}} = \frac{50}{4.0} $$ $$ W_c = 12.5 \text{ kW} $$ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Exams (Module 2):** > > 1. **Forgetting $h_4 = h_3$** — Students often compute COP using $h_1 - h_3$ for numerator AND $h_2 - h_1$ for denominator, but the **refrigeration effect per kg is $(h_1 - h_4)$ where $h_4 = h_3$ due to throttling**. LOSE 1 MARK if confused. > > 2. **Wrong state identification** — In actual problems with subcooling/superheating, $h_1$ is **superheated vapor at compressor inlet** and $h_3$ is **subcooled liquid at condenser exit**, not saturated values. > > 3. **Mass flow rate unit error** — $\dot{m}$ must be in **kg/s** for kW; convert to kg/hr only after computing in SI base. > > 4. **Energy balance sign convention** — Heat rejected by condenser: $Q_C = Q_E + W_c$. Forgetting to add compressor work loses 1 mark. > > 5. **Displacement volume formula error** — Students forget the factor $\frac{N}{60}$ for converting RPM to revolutions per second. Total LOSE = 2 marks. > > 6. **Not stating units** — Always write units like kJ/kg, kW, kg/s — partial marks are deducted otherwise. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Components of Mechanical Refrigerator** ✅ **Four Primary Components:** Compressor → Condenser → Expansion Valve → Evaporator (in cyclic order) ✅ **Compressor Types:** Reciprocating, Rotary, Scroll, Screw, Centrifugal (in increasing capacity order) ✅ **Condenser Types:** Air-cooled (small), Water-cooled (medium), Evaporative (large) ✅ **Expansion Devices:** Capillary tube (small/fixed load), TEV (most common commercial), AXV (obsolete), Float valve (flooded), EEV (precision) ✅ **Evaporator Types:** DX (Direct Expansion), Flooded, Plate, Shell & Coil, Shell & Tube ✅ **Auxiliary Components:** Receiver, Filter-Drier, Sight Glass, Accumulator, Oil Separator, Solenoid Valve, Pressure Cutouts ✅ **Key Thermodynamic States:** - State 1: Compressor inlet (saturated/superheated vapor, low P) - State 2: Compressor outlet (superheated vapor, high P) - State 3: Condenser outlet (saturated/subcooled liquid, high P) - State 4: Evaporator inlet (two-phase mixture, low P) ✅ **Critical Equations to Memorize:** - $\text{COP} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ - $\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q_E}{h_1 - h_4}$ - $V_d = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L \cdot n \cdot \dfrac{N}{60}$ - $Q_C = Q_E + W_c$ (Energy balance) - $h_3 = h_4$ (Throttling — isenthalpic) ✅ **Important Constants / Conventions:** - COP is **dimensionless** and **always greater than 1** for practical refrigerators - All enthalpy values in **kJ/kg**, work/heat in **kW** (= kJ/s) - Compressor is the **only work-consuming** component in the primary loop - Expansion device does **NO work** and has **NO heat transfer** — pure throttling ✅ **KTU Exam-Favourite Topics from this Module:** - State-point identification on P-h and T-s diagrams - COP calculation under various operating conditions - Mass flow rate and compressor power calculations - Compressor displacement calculations - Comparison between TEV and capillary tube (3-mark favorite) ✅ **Working Substance:** Refrigerant undergoes **phase change** (evaporation + condensation) — distinguishes VCRS from gas refrigeration cycles.

topics.title: Study of Compressors
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Study of Compressors - Refrigeration and Air Conditioning

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Study of Compressors

---

## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 What is a Refrigerant Compressor?

A **refrigerant compressor** is the prime mover of the **Vapor Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRC)**. It is a mechanical device that draws low-pressure, low-temperature **refrigerant vapor** from the evaporator and compresses it to a high-pressure, high-temperature **superheated vapor**, which is then discharged into the condenser. The compressor essentially pumps the refrigerant around the closed thermodynamic loop and elevates its energy level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standard Terminology):**
> A compressor is a work-consuming device that increases the pressure of the refrigerant vapour (from evaporator pressure $P_e$ to condenser pressure $P_c$) by reducing its specific volume. It is the only component in the basic VCRC that requires external **work input ($W_{in}$)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy - "The Heart of the Refrigeration System"

Imagine the refrigeration system as the **human circulatory system**:

- **The Compressor** acts as the **Heart** — it pumps the working fluid (blood/refrigerant) around the body (the cycle).
- The **Evaporator** is like the **Lungs** (where the fluid absorbs heat from the surroundings it serves).
- The **Condenser** is like the **Body's cooling system / sweating mechanism** (where unwanted heat is rejected).
- The **Expansion Device (Throttle)** is like a **narrow capillary passage** (which drops the pressure suddenly).

Just as the heart must work against back-pressure to keep blood flowing, the compressor must work against the high condenser pressure to push the refrigerant through. If the heart fails, the body fails. Similarly, **if the compressor fails, refrigeration stops**.

### 1.3 Classification of Refrigerant Compressors

Compressors used in refrigeration and air conditioning are broadly classified into two main families:

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification of Compressors Used in RAC**

| **Positive Displacement Compressors** | **Dynamic (Centrifugal) Compressors** |
|---|---|
| A fixed mass of refrigerant is trapped and mechanically compressed by reducing its volume. | Kinetic energy is imparted to the refrigerant by a high-speed impeller, then converted to pressure energy. |
| **Sub-Types:** | **Sub-Types:** |
| 1. **Reciprocating** (Piston-Cylinder) | 1. Centrifugal |
| 2. **Rotary** (Vane / Rolling Piston) | 2. Axial Flow |
| 3. **Screw** (Twin / Single Helical Rotor) | (Rare in small/medium RAC) |
| 4. **Scroll** (Orbiting + Fixed Spiral) | |

### 1.4 Physical Standards and Typical Metrics

- **Domestic refrigerators**: Use hermetically sealed **reciprocating** or **rotary** compressors, sizes from **1/20 HP to 1 HP**.
- **Window/split ACs**: Use **reciprocating**, **rotary**, or **scroll** compressors (**1 HP to 5 HP** typical).
- **Large chillers**: Use **centrifugal** or **screw** compressors (**10 TR to 1500+ TR**).
- **Standard SI Units**: Pressure in **kPa** or **bar**, Work in **kJ/kg**, Mass flow rate in **kg/s**, Cooling capacity in **Watts (W)** or **Tons of Refrigeration (TR)** where **1 TR = 3.517 kW**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Reciprocating Compressor (Most Exam-Relevant in KTU)

The **reciprocating compressor** is the most frequently examined compressor in KTU papers. It uses a piston moving inside a cylinder, with suction and discharge valves (both acting as **check valves**). It works on the principle of **positive displacement**.

### 2.1.1 Cycle of Operation (Four Stroke Phases)

A single-acting reciprocating compressor completes one compression cycle in **one full revolution (360°)** of the crankshaft, while a double-acting version completes it in **half a revolution (180°)**.

The four phases are:

1. **Suction Stroke (0° → 180°)**: The piston moves from Top Dead Center (TDC) to Bottom Dead Center (BDC). The suction valve opens due to the lower cylinder pressure (below $P_e$), and refrigerant at evaporator pressure $P_e$ is drawn in.
2. **Compression Stroke (180° → 360°)**: The piston moves from BDC back to TDC. Both valves remain closed. The trapped refrigerant is compressed from $P_e$ to condenser pressure $P_c$ following an approximate **adiabatic/polytropic process**.
3. **Discharge Stroke (continues 180° → 360° overlap)**: When cylinder pressure exceeds $P_c$, the discharge valve opens, and high-pressure vapor is pushed into the condenser.
4. **Re-expansion (Clearance Volume Re-expansion)**: When the piston reaches TDC, a small amount of vapor remains trapped in the **clearance volume ($V_c$)**. As the piston descends for the next suction stroke, this residual vapor **re-expands** from $P_c$ back to $P_e$ before fresh suction can begin. This is the **clearance effect**.

### 2.1.2 The Polytropic Process Assumption

The actual compression of a refrigerant vapor in a reciprocating compressor is neither perfectly isothermal nor perfectly isothermal. It closely follows the **polytropic process**:

$$P V^n = \text{constant}$$

Where $n$ is the **polytropic index** ($1 < n < k$, where $k$ is the specific heat ratio). For a perfect **adiabatic** (reversible, no heat transfer) compression, $n = k = 1.4$ for air, but for refrigerants, $n$ is typically between **1.25 and 1.35** due to small heat losses to the cool cylinder walls.

### 2.2 Theoretical vs. Actual Volumetric Efficiency

### 2.2.1 Swept Volume vs. Clearance Volume

- **Swept Volume ($V_s$)**: Volume displaced by the piston per stroke = $\frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \times L$
- **Clearance Volume ($V_c$)**: The unavoidable small volume above the piston at TDC required for mechanical safety of valves. Typically $V_c = 0.02$ to $0.05$ of $V_s$.
- **Clearance Ratio ($C$)**: $C = \frac{V_c}{V_s}$

### 2.2.2 Volumetric Efficiency ($\eta_v$)

The **volumetric efficiency** is defined as the ratio of **actual volume of refrigerant drawn in (at suction conditions)** to the **swept volume of the piston**.

$$\eta_v = \frac{V_a}{V_s} = 1 + C - C \left(\frac{P_c}{P_e}\right)^{1/n}$$

This is the **most critical equation** in the entire module, and KTU regularly asks derivations and numerical applications based on it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is clearance volume needed?**
> - To prevent the piston from physically hitting the cylinder head.
> - To provide space for valve mechanisms.
> - To absorb any moisture/hydraulic lock effects.
> - **The trade-off**: A small clearance is mechanically essential, but it directly *reduces* the volumetric efficiency.

### 2.3 Actual Work of Compression (With Clearance)

The **actual indicated work per cycle** for a compressor with clearance volume, considering the re-expansion stroke, is calculated using a P-V diagram. KTU expects students to know that the work done in re-expansion (from $V_c$ to $V_4$) is a **negative work** that reduces the net work output, but in practice, the net **work of compression is HIGHER** than the work of a clearance-free compressor for the same mass flow — but the compressor is **less efficient per unit volume displaced**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Application** | **Compressor Type Used** | **Reason** |
|---|---|---|
| Domestic Refrigerator | Reciprocating (hermetic) | Low cost, suitable for small capacities |
| Window Air Conditioner | Reciprocating / Rotary | Compact, good part-load efficiency |
| Split AC (1-2 TR) | Rotary / Scroll | Smooth, quiet, fewer moving parts |
| Central AC Chillers | Screw / Centrifugal | High capacity (50-1500 TR), continuous duty |
| Cold Storage (Industrial) | Screw compressors | Reliable for long-running, large tonnage |

---

## KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA CHEAT SHEET (Memorize This!)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All critical formulas for the "Study of Compressors" module — tested every year in KTU exams.**

| # | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = \frac{V_c}{V_s}$ | Clearance Ratio | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $W_{iso} = P_e V_1 \ln\left(\frac{P_c}{P_e}\right)$ | Work of Isothermal Compression (per kg) | kJ/kg |
| 3 | $W_{adia} = \frac{n}{n-1} P_e V_1 \left[\left(\frac{P_c}{P_e}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$ | Work of Polytropic Compression (per cycle) | kJ |
| 4 | $\eta_v = 1 + C - C \left(\frac{P_c}{P_e}\right)^{1/n}$ | Volumetric Efficiency with Clearance | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $\eta_v = 1 - C \left[\left(\frac{P_c}{P_e}\right)^{1/n} - 1\right]$ | Alternate form of Volumetric Efficiency | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $V_a = \eta_v \times V_s$ | Actual Volume of Gas Drawn In | m³ |
| 7 | $m = \frac{P_a V_a}{R T_a}$ | Mass of Refrigerant Compressed (per cycle) | kg |
| 8 | $N = \frac{V_{des}}{V_a \times n_{cyl} \times N_r}$ | Compressor Speed (for given displacement) | rpm |
| 9 | $\text{COP} = \frac{Q_e}{W_{comp}}$ | Coefficient of Performance | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $\text{Isothermal Eff.} = \frac{W_{iso}}{W_{actual}}$ | Compressor Isothermal Efficiency | Dimensionless |
| 11 | $\text{Adiabatic Eff.} = \frac{W_{adia}}{W_{actual}}$ | Compressor Adiabatic Efficiency | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Boundary Conditions for KTU Numericals:**
> - For $C = 0$: $\eta_v = 1$ (theoretical maximum)
> - For $C = 0.05$, $P_c / P_e = 4$, $n = 1.3$: $\eta_v \approx 0.879$
> - As $P_c / P_e$ increases, $\eta_v$ decreases.
> - As $C$ increases, $\eta_v$ decreases.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 DERIVATION: Volumetric Efficiency with Clearance Volume

This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU Module 2. Every student must be able to reproduce this in the exam.

### 3.1.1 Setting Up the P-V Diagram

Consider a single-acting reciprocating compressor. The actual cycle on a P-V diagram has the following points:

- **Point 1**: End of re-expansion (start of suction). Pressure = $P_e$, Volume = $V_4$
- **Point 2**: End of suction. Pressure = $P_e$, Volume = $V_1$ (= $V_4 + V_a$)
- **Point 3**: End of compression / start of discharge. Pressure = $P_c$, Volume = $V_3$
- **Point 4**: End of discharge (TDC). Pressure = $P_c$, Volume = $V_c$

Where:
- $V_s = V_1 - V_4$ = Swept Volume
- $V_c$ = Clearance Volume
- $V_a = V_1 - V_4$ = Actual Volume of Refrigerant Drawn In (at suction conditions)

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — The Re-Expansion Process (4 → 1)

The trapped gas at $V_c$ and $P_c$ re-expands polytropically until pressure drops to $P_e$. Applying the polytropic relation:

$$P_c V_c^{n} = P_e V_4^{n}$$

Solving for $V_4$:

$$V_4 = V_c \left(\frac{P_c}{P_e}\right)^{1/n}$$

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Express the Actual Volume Inducted

The actual volume of fresh refrigerant drawn in per cycle is the difference between $V_1$ and $V_4$:

$$V_a = V_1 - V_4 = V_s + V_c - V_c \left(\frac{P_c}{P_e}\right)^{1/n}$$

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Factor Out and Form the Volumetric Efficiency

Divide both sides by the swept volume $V_s$, and define $C = V_c / V_s$:

$$\frac{V_a}{V_s} = 1 + C - C \left(\frac{P_c}{P_e}\right)^{1/n}$$

Since $\eta_v = \frac{V_a}{V_s}$, the final expression is:

$$\boxed{\eta_v = 1 + C - C \left(\frac{P_c}{P_e}\right)^{1/n}}$$

### 3.1.5 Limiting Cases (for KTU 2-mark conceptual questions)

**Case 1**: When $C = 0$ (no clearance) → $\eta_v = 1$ (100% — the theoretical maximum)

**Case 2**: When $P_c = P_e$ (no compression) → $\eta_v = 1$ (no work done, fills fully)

**Case 3**: When $P_c / P_e$ becomes very large → $\eta_v$ approaches $1 - C$, but in reality the compressor becomes inoperable (called "short-circuiting" condition).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:**
> The alternate form $\eta_v = 1 - C\left[\left(\frac{P_c}{P_e}\right)^{1/n} - 1\right]$ is **mathematically identical** and is sometimes expected. Know both forms to be safe.

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION: Work of Polytropic Compression Per Cycle

### 3.2.1 Polytropic Work Integral

The work done by the compressor on the gas during the polytropic compression process is the area under curve 2-3 on the P-V diagram, which is given by:

$$W_{comp} = \int_{V_1}^{V_3} P \, dV$$

For a polytropic process, $P V^n = P_1 V_1^n = P_3 V_3^n = $ constant, so $P = \frac{P_1 V_1^n}{V^n}$.

### 3.2.2 Performing the Integration

$$W_{comp} = \int_{V_1}^{V_3} \frac{P_1 V_1^n}{V^n} \, dV = P_1 V_1^n \int_{V_1}^{V_3} V^{-n} \, dV$$

$$W_{comp} = P_1 V_1^n \left[ \frac{V^{1-n}}{1-n} \right]_{V_1}^{V_3} = P_1 V_1^n \left( \frac{V_3^{1-n} - V_1^{1-n}}{1-n} \right)$$

$$W_{comp} = \frac{P_1 V_1^n}{n-1} \left( V_1^{1-n} - V_3^{1-n} \right) = \frac{P_1 V_1}{n-1} \left[ 1 - \left(\frac{V_1}{V_3}\right)^{n-1} \right]$$

Since $P_1 V_1^n = P_3 V_3^n$:

$$\left(\frac{V_1}{V_3}\right)^{n-1} = \left(\frac{P_3}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}}$$

### 3.2.3 Final Polytropic Work Expression

$$\boxed{W_{comp} = \frac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right]}$$

Where $P_1 = P_e$, $P_2 = P_c$, and $V_1 = V_s$ (assuming clearance-free).

---

## 3.3 ANALYTICAL MATRIX: Effect of Various Parameters on Compressor Performance

This is a **high-yield KTU 2-mark question** — frequently asked in Part A.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How does each parameter affect $\eta_v$?**

| **Parameter** | **Change** | **Effect on $\eta_v$** | **Physical Reason** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Clearance Ratio $C$** | Increases ↑ | **Decreases** ↓ | More residual gas re-expands, less fresh gas enters |
| **Pressure Ratio $P_c / P_e$** | Increases ↑ | **Decreases** ↓ | Re-expansion to $P_e$ takes longer, more volume lost |
| **Polytropic Index $n$** | Increases ↑ (towards adiabatic) | **Decreases** ↓ | Steeper P-V curve, more work, more re-expansion volume |
| **Suction Temperature $T_s$** | Increases ↑ | **Decreases slightly** ↓ | Higher temp → lower density → less mass per stroke |
| **Discharge Pressure $P_c$** | Increases ↑ | **Decreases** ↓ | Higher back-pressure resists suction |
| **Compressor Speed (RPM)** | Increases ↑ | **Slightly decreases** ↓ | Valve throttling losses become significant at high speed |

---

## 3.4 SAMPLE NUMERICAL FOR KTU BOARD-STYLE PRACTICE

**Problem:** A reciprocating compressor has a swept volume of $0.005 \text{ m}^3$ and a clearance volume of $0.0001 \text{ m}^3$. The compressor takes in refrigerant at 1.2 bar and compresses it polytropically with $n = 1.3$ to 9 bar. Calculate:
(a) The volumetric efficiency
(b) The actual volume of refrigerant drawn in per cycle

**Solution:**

Given: $V_s = 0.005 \text{ m}^3$, $V_c = 0.0001 \text{ m}^3$, $P_e = 1.2 \text{ bar}$, $P_c = 9 \text{ bar}$, $n = 1.3$

**Step 1 — Calculate Clearance Ratio:**

$$C = \frac{V_c}{V_s} = \frac{0.0001}{0.005} = 0.02$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Pressure Ratio:**

$$\frac{P_c}{P_e} = \frac{9}{1.2} = 7.5$$

**Step 3 — Apply Volumetric Efficiency Formula:**

$$\left(\frac{P_c}{P_e}\right)^{1/n} = 7.5^{1/1.3} = 7.5^{0.7692}$$

$$\ln(7.5^{0.7692}) = 0.7692 \times \ln(7.5) = 0.7692 \times 2.0149 = 1.5499$$

$$7.5^{0.7692} = e^{1.5499} = 4.711$$

$$\eta_v = 1 + 0.02 - 0.02 \times 4.711 = 1.02 - 0.0942 = 0.9258$$

**Step 4 — Actual Volume Drawn In:**

$$V_a = \eta_v \times V_s = 0.9258 \times 0.005 = 0.004629 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\boxed{\eta_v = 0.9258 \text{ or } 92.58\%, \quad V_a = 0.004629 \text{ m}^3}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Compressor Working Cycle in VCRC

This diagram traces the working of a reciprocating compressor in a complete VCRC system — a frequently asked KTU diagram question (often 5-7 marks for a labeled sketch + explanation).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Evaporator Outlet: Low P, Low T Vapor] --> B[Suction Manifold]
    B --> C[Suction Filter and Strainer]
    C --> D[Suction Valve of Compressor]
    D --> E[Piston Descends in Cylinder]
    E --> F[Refrigerant at P_e Trapped in Clearance]
    F --> G[Piston Ascends, Compresses Gas Polytropically]
    G --> H{Pressure Exceeds P_c?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Discharge Valve Opens]
    H -- No --> G
    I --> J[High P, High T Superheated Vapor]
    J --> K[Discharge Manifold]
    K --> L[Oil Separator]
    L --> M[Condenser: Heat Rejected]
    M --> N[Receiver]
    N --> O[Expansion Valve: Pressure Drop]
    O --> P[Evaporator: Heat Absorbed]
    P --> A
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: P-V Diagram of Reciprocating Compressor (Schematic)

This is a schematic representation of the four processes of a reciprocating compressor with clearance, equivalent to the P-V diagram (since physical drawings aren't supported in Mermaid, we use a block-level functional flow):

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Point 4: P_c, V_c at TDC] -->|Re-expansion 4 to 1, n=poly| P2[Point 1: P_e, V_4 at start of suction]
    P2 -->|Suction at constant P_e, V_4 to V_1| P3[Point 2: P_e, V_1 at BDC]
    P3 -->|Polytropic Compression 2 to 3| P4[Point 3: P_c, V_3 at discharge start]
    P4 -->|Discharge at constant P_c, V_3 to V_c| P1
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Hermetic Reciprocating Compressor

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Low Pressure Suction Vapor] --> B[Suction Muffler]
    B --> C[Suction Reed Valve Assembly]
    C --> D[Cylinder Head with Suction and Discharge Valves]
    D --> E[Cylinder Bore]
    E --> F[Piston with Connecting Rod]
    F --> G[Crankshaft Driven by Electric Motor]
    G --> H[Discharge Reed Valve Assembly]
    H --> I[Discharge Plenum Chamber]
    I --> J[High Pressure Superheated Vapor to Condenser]
    K[Cooling Oil Sump] --> L[Oil Pickup Tube]
    L --> M[Crankshaft Oil Gallery]
    M --> N[Bearing Surfaces]
    N --> E
```

## 4.4 Comparison Schematic: Types of Compressors in RAC

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Refrigerant Compressors] --> B[Positive Displacement]
    A --> C[Dynamic]
    B --> B1[Reciprocating]
    B --> B2[Rotary - Rolling Piston]
    B --> B3[Rotary - Vane]
    B --> B4[Screw - Twin Helical]
    B --> B5[Scroll - Orbiting]
    C --> C1[Centrifugal]
    C --> C2[Axial Flow]
    B1 --> D1[Hermetic: Domestic Fridge, Window AC]
    B2 --> D2[Split AC, Small Chillers]
    B3 --> D3[Automotive AC, Some Refrigerators]
    B4 --> D4[Large Chillers, Cold Storage]
    B5 --> D5[Modern Split AC, VRF Systems]
    C1 --> D6[Large Commercial Chillers 50 to 1500 TR]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students often forget to convert $P_c / P_e$ into absolute pressures when the problem states them as gauge. **Always use absolute pressures** ($P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$).
> 2. The exponent $1/n$ (not $n$) is used in the volumetric efficiency formula. Mixing up $n$ and $1/n$ is a **very common 2-mark blunder**.
> 3. Work of compression and work of re-expansion must be calculated **per cycle**, not per second, then multiplied by the RPM. Don't confuse the units.
> 4. For double-acting compressors, multiply the swept volume by 2 before calculating $\eta_v$.
> 5. Polytropic index $n = 1$ gives the isothermal result; $n = k$ gives the adiabatic result. KTU often asks this trick question.

---

## PART A: SHORT-ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define volumetric efficiency of a reciprocating compressor. State the factors affecting it.**

**Model Answer:**

The **volumetric efficiency** of a reciprocating compressor is defined as the ratio of the **actual volume of refrigerant vapor drawn in at suction conditions** to the **swept (displaced) volume of the piston**, both expressed per cycle.

$$\eta_v = \frac{V_{actual}}{V_{swept}}$$

The factors affecting volumetric efficiency are:

1. **Clearance Ratio ($C$)** — Higher clearance leads to lower $\eta_v$ due to greater re-expansion of residual gas.
2. **Pressure Ratio ($P_c / P_e$)** — Higher pressure ratio reduces $\eta_v$ because re-expansion occupies a larger fraction of the stroke.
3. **Polytropic Index ($n$)** — A higher index (more adiabatic) lowers $\eta_v$.
4. **Throttling effect of suction and discharge valves** — at high RPMs, valves restrict flow.
5. **Heat exchange with cylinder walls** — the refrigerant heats up, reducing density.
6. **Leakage past piston rings and valves** — reduces actual mass inducted.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Explain why clearance volume is necessary in a reciprocating compressor. What is its disadvantage?**

**Model Answer:**

**Necessity of Clearance Volume:**

- The clearance volume is the small space left between the piston (at TDC) and the cylinder head. It is **mechanically essential** for the following reasons:
  1. It prevents the piston from physically **striking the cylinder head**, which would cause catastrophic mechanical failure.
  2. It provides space for the **discharge and suction valve mechanisms** to operate.
  3. It accommodates **thermal expansion** of the piston and the refrigerant.
  4. It provides a small reservoir to absorb any **liquid slugging** (if liquid refrigerant enters the compressor).

**Disadvantage:**

- The residual high-pressure vapor trapped in the clearance volume **re-expands** when the piston begins its downward stroke, until cylinder pressure drops to $P_e$. This re-expansion **reduces the volume available for fresh refrigerant**, thus directly reducing the **volumetric efficiency** of the compressor.

$$\eta_v = 1 + C - C \left(\frac{P_c}{P_e}\right)^{1/n}$$

Hence, a small clearance is desirable mechanically, but it always hurts compressor performance.

---

## PART B: LONG-ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 MARKS WITH INTERNAL CHOICE)

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the volumetric efficiency of a reciprocating compressor with clearance volume. Explain how it varies with clearance ratio and pressure ratio.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A single-acting reciprocating compressor has a cylinder bore of 100 mm and a stroke of 80 mm. The clearance volume is 5% of the swept volume. It compresses R-134a from 1.4 bar to 10 bar polytropically with $n = 1.3$. Calculate: (i) the volumetric efficiency, (ii) the actual mass of refrigerant compressed per cycle at a suction temperature of $-10°C$ (use $R_{R134a} = 0.0815 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$).

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Setup:** Consider a single-acting reciprocating compressor with clearance volume. The actual cycle on a P-V diagram consists of four processes:

1. **Re-expansion (4→1):** Trapped gas expands from $V_c, P_c$ to $V_4, P_e$ polytropically.
2. **Suction (1→2):** Fresh refrigerant at $P_e$ enters as piston moves from $V_4$ to $V_1$.
3. **Compression (2→3):** Piston compresses gas from $P_e$ to $P_c$ polytropically.
4. **Discharge (3→4):** High-pressure gas pushed out from $V_3$ to $V_c$.

**Step 1 — Apply the polytropic relation to the re-expansion process (4→1):**

$$P_c V_c^{n} = P_e V_4^{n}$$

Solving for $V_4$:

$$V_4 = V_c \left(\frac{P_c}{P_e}\right)^{1/n}$$

**Step 2 — Express the actual inducted volume:**

The fresh refrigerant volume per cycle is the difference between $V_1$ and $V_4$:

$$V_a = V_1 - V_4 = (V_4 + V_s) - V_c \left(\frac{P_c}{P_e}\right)^{1/n}$$

Since $V_1 = V_4 + V_s$ and $V_4 = V_c \left(\frac{P_c}{P_e}\right)^{1/n}$:

$$V_a = V_s + V_c - V_c \left(\frac{P_c}{P_e}\right)^{1/n}$$

**Step 3 — Divide by $V_s$ to get $\eta_v$:**

$$\frac{V_a}{V_s} = 1 + \frac{V_c}{V_s} - \frac{V_c}{V_s} \left(\frac{P_c}{P_e}\right)^{1/n}$$

Let $C = \frac{V_c}{V_s}$ (clearance ratio). Therefore:

$$\boxed{\eta_v = 1 + C - C \left(\frac{P_c}{P_e}\right)^{1/n}}$$

**Variation with Clearance Ratio ($C$):** $\eta_v$ **decreases** as $C$ increases. A larger clearance volume means more residual gas re-expands, leaving less space for fresh refrigerant. This is a **linear** decrease per unit increase in $C$ when the pressure ratio is constant.

**Variation with Pressure Ratio ($P_c / P_e$):** $\eta_v$ **decreases** as $P_c / P_e$ increases. The term $\left(\frac{P_c}{P_e}\right)^{1/n}$ grows non-linearly, meaning the re-expanded volume $V_4$ becomes much larger, drastically reducing $V_a$. In a multi-stage compressor (where each stage has a smaller pressure ratio), $\eta_v$ of each individual stage is higher, which is why **multi-stage compression is preferred for high overall pressure ratios**.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given Data:**
- Bore $D = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
- Stroke $L = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.08 \text{ m}$
- Clearance ratio $C = 0.05$
- $P_e = 1.4 \text{ bar} = 140 \text{ kPa}$, $P_c = 10 \text{ bar} = 1000 \text{ kPa}$
- $n = 1.3$
- $T_{suction} = -10°C = 263 \text{ K}$
- $R = 0.0815 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$

**Step 1 — Calculate Swept Volume:**

$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \times L = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.1)^2 (0.08) = 6.283 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Pressure Ratio:**

$$\frac{P_c}{P_e} = \frac{10}{1.4} = 7.1429$$

**Step 3 — Calculate $\eta_v$:**

$$\left(\frac{P_c}{P_e}\right)^{1/n} = 7.1429^{1/1.3} = 7.1429^{0.7692}$$

$$\ln(7.1429^{0.7692}) = 0.7692 \times \ln(7.1429) = 0.7692 \times 1.9661 = 1.5124$$

$$7.1429^{0.7692} = e^{1.5124} = 4.538$$

$$\eta_v = 1 + 0.05 - 0.05 \times 4.538 = 1.05 - 0.2269 = 0.8231$$

$$\boxed{\eta_v = 82.31\%}$$

**Step 4 — Actual Volume Drawn In:**

$$V_a = \eta_v \times V_s = 0.8231 \times 6.283 \times 10^{-4} = 5.171 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Mass of Refrigerant Per Cycle (using ideal gas law):**

$$m = \frac{P_e V_a}{R T_e} = \frac{140 \times 5.171 \times 10^{-4}}{0.0815 \times 263}$$

$$m = \frac{0.07239}{21.4345} = 3.378 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg}$$

$$\boxed{m = 3.378 \text{ g per cycle}}$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2023] — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a P-V diagram, explain the working of a single-acting reciprocating compressor. Discuss the effect of clearance on the work of compression.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A two-stage reciprocating compressor compresses air from 1 bar to 25 bar. The clearance ratio of each stage is 0.04, and the compression follows the law $PV^{1.3} = $ constant. Find the optimum intermediate pressure for minimum work of compression.

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Working of a Single-Acting Reciprocating Compressor (with P-V Diagram Description):**

The P-V diagram consists of four processes per cycle. The diagram is plotted with **pressure (P) on the Y-axis** and **volume (V) on the X-axis**. The four key points are:

- **Point 4 → Point 1: Re-expansion (4-1):** At TDC, residual refrigerant at $V_c$ and $P_c$ is trapped. As the piston moves down, this gas re-expands polytropically from $P_c$ to $P_e$ until the cylinder pressure equals suction pressure. The volume at point 1 is $V_4$.

- **Point 1 → Point 2: Suction (1-2):** When cylinder pressure drops to $P_e$, the suction valve opens. Refrigerant at $P_e$ is drawn in as the piston moves to BDC, and the volume increases from $V_4$ to $V_1$ ($V_s = V_1 - V_4$).

- **Point 2 → Point 3: Compression (2-3):** Both valves are closed. The trapped gas is compressed polytropically from $P_e$ to $P_c$, with volume reducing from $V_1$ to $V_3$.

- **Point 3 → Point 4: Discharge (3-4):** When cylinder pressure reaches $P_c$, the discharge valve opens. High-pressure vapor is expelled as the piston moves to TDC, and volume reduces from $V_3$ to $V_c$.

**Effect of Clearance on Work of Compression:**

The presence of clearance volume does **not** change the work per cycle of the *ideal* polytropic compression process (work depends on the polytropic curve from 2 to 3, which is determined by $P_e$, $P_c$, and $V_1$). However, the work per unit mass of refrigerant **increases** because:

1. The actual mass of refrigerant per cycle is **less** (lower $\eta_v$).
2. To deliver the same mass flow, more cycles are needed, meaning the compressor must run longer (or larger) for the same duty.
3. The compressor's **indicated power** must increase because the back-pressure work done during re-expansion (negative work from 4 to 1) is partially recovered, but the loss in mass flow is not.

In summary: **Clearance increases the specific work of compression** (kJ/kg of refrigerant) and reduces the **volumetric efficiency**, but it does not change the shape of the work integral for a single cycle.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Concept of Optimum Intermediate Pressure:**

In a two-stage compressor with **complete intercooling**, the refrigerant is cooled back to the initial temperature $T_1$ between the two stages. The total work of compression is the sum of work done in the low-pressure (LP) stage and the high-pressure (HP) stage. To find the minimum total work, we differentiate the total work with respect to the intermediate pressure $P_i$ and equate to zero.

**Given:**
- $P_1 = 1 \text{ bar}$, $P_2 = 25 \text{ bar}$
- $C_1 = C_2 = 0.04$ (clearance ratio of each stage)
- $n = 1.3$

**Step 1 — Optimum Pressure Ratio Condition:**

For a two-stage compressor with intercooling, the **optimum pressure ratio per stage** is the **square root** of the overall pressure ratio:

$$\frac{P_i}{P_1} = \frac{P_2}{P_i} = \sqrt{\frac{P_2}{P_1}}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Overall Pressure Ratio:**

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{25}{1} = 25$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Optimum Intermediate Pressure:**

$$\sqrt{\frac{P_2}{P_1}} = \sqrt{25} = 5$$

$$P_i = P_1 \times 5 = 1 \times 5 = 5 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 4 — Verification of the Optimum Condition:**

$$\frac{P_i}{P_1} = \frac{5}{1} = 5, \quad \frac{P_2}{P_i} = \frac{25}{5} = 5$$

Both are equal, confirming the optimum condition is satisfied.

$$\boxed{P_{i,optimum} = 5 \text{ bar}, \quad \text{with } \frac{P_i}{P_1} = \frac{P_2}{P_i} = 5}$$

**Note on the Effect of Clearance:** Since both stages have the same clearance ratio and polytropic index, the condition for minimum total work still yields the same optimum intermediate pressure. The clearance primarily affects the **mass flow rate** (and hence the size of the compressor), not the **optimum pressure ratio** for minimum specific work.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — "Study of Compressors"**

### ✅ Core Concepts
- A compressor is the **work-consuming device** in VCRC, raising refrigerant pressure from $P_e$ to $P_c$.
- **Two main families:** Positive Displacement (reciprocating, rotary, screw, scroll) and Dynamic (centrifugal).
- Reciprocating compressors are the **most exam-relevant** type in KTU.

### ✅ Key Definitions to Memorize
- **Swept Volume ($V_s$):** $\frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L$
- **Clearance Volume ($V_c$):** Volume above piston at TDC (mechanically necessary, 2-5% of $V_s$).
- **Clearance Ratio ($C$):** $V_c / V_s$
- **Volumetric Efficiency ($\eta_v$):** $V_{actual} / V_{swept}$

### ✅ Must-Know Formulas
- $\eta_v = 1 + C - C \left(\frac{P_c}{P_e}\right)^{1/n}$ ⭐ (Most Important)
- $W_{comp} = \frac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right]$
- $P_{i,opt} = \sqrt{P_1 \times P_2}$ (for two-stage with intercooling)
- $m = \frac{PV}{RT}$ (for mass of refrigerant per cycle)

### ✅ Parameter Effects (Quick Reference)
- $\eta_v \downarrow$ when $C \uparrow$, $P_c/P_e \uparrow$, or $n \uparrow$
- $W_{comp} \uparrow$ when pressure ratio $\uparrow$ or $n \uparrow$ (towards adiabatic)

### ✅ Numerical Solving Strategy
1. **Always** convert gauge pressures to **absolute**.
2. **Always** use **SI units** (Pa for pressure, m³ for volume, K for temperature).
3. Identify whether the question asks for **per cycle** or **per second** (multiply by RPM/60 if needed).
4. For two-stage problems, **check intercooling assumption** — without intercooling, the optimum is different.

### ✅ Common Confusions to Avoid
- Don't confuse $n$ (polytropic index) with $k$ (specific heat ratio) — they are different unless compression is perfectly adiabatic.
- $1/n$ is used in $\eta_v$ formula; $(n-1)/n$ is used in work formula.
- "Volumetric efficiency" is dimensionless (a ratio), not a percentage — though it's often expressed as %.
- Clearance volume does NOT reduce the polytropic work per cycle, but it **increases the specific work per kg of refrigerant**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Condensers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Condensers # 🧊 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Condensers ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard) A **Condenser** is a heat exchanger in a **Vapor Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS)** in which the superheated refrigerant vapor discharged from the compressor is condensed (de-superheated) and then sub-cooled by rejecting heat to a surrounding cooling medium (air, water, or a combination of both). The refrigerant enters as a high-pressure, high-temperature superheated vapor and leaves as a high-pressure saturated or sub-cooled liquid, releasing its **latent heat of condensation** and **sensible heat** to the external sink. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Verbatim):** > "A condenser is a heat-rejecting component of a refrigeration system in which the refrigerant vapor, after compression, is liquefied by extracting its latent heat to a cooling medium at a lower temperature, while remaining at essentially constant pressure." Mathematically, the heat rejected by the condenser (per unit mass of refrigerant) is: $$q_c = h_{2} - h_{3}$$ where $h_2$ = enthalpy of superheated vapor at compressor discharge, and $h_3$ = enthalpy of liquid leaving the condenser. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Steam Radiator" Intuition Imagine you have a hot cup of tea (the compressed refrigerant vapor). You want to cool it down quickly. The simplest way is to pour it into a metal cup placed inside a bucket of cold water. - The **hot tea** is the high-pressure refrigerant vapor. - The **metal cup** is the condenser tubes (heat-conducting surface). - The **cold water in the bucket** is the cooling medium (air or water). - As the tea loses heat, it condenses from vapor → liquid. > [!NOTE] > **Just like a tea cup gives its heat to surrounding water**, a condenser transfers heat from hot refrigerant vapor to a cooler sink (air or water), causing the refrigerant to change phase from gas to liquid. The **driving force** is the **temperature difference** between the refrigerant (hot) and the cooling medium (cold). ## 1.3 Why is the Condenser the "Heat-Rejecting Twin" of the Evaporator? In any refrigeration cycle, **Heat rejected by condenser = Heat absorbed by evaporator + Compressor work**. This follows directly from the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to the cyclic VCRS: $$Q_c = Q_e + W_{comp}$$ where $Q_c$ is condenser heat rejection, $Q_e$ is evaporator heat absorption, and $W_{comp}$ is compressor work input. > [!TIP] > **Memory Trick for KTU Exams:** *"Hot to Cold, Vapor to Liquid, Work In, Heat Out"* — that's the Condenser's job description in VCRS. ## 1.4 Classification of Condensers (Syllabus Mapping) KTU 2024 Module 2 broadly classifies condensers into three families based on the **cooling medium** used: | S.No. | Type of Condenser | Cooling Medium | Common Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Air-Cooled Condenser (ACC)** | Atmospheric Air | Domestic refrigerators, split ACs, window ACs | | 2 | **Water-Cooled Condenser (WCC)** | Cooling Water | Industrial chillers, large commercial plants | | 3 | **Evaporative Condenser (EC)** | Air + Water Spray | Cold storage, large refrigeration plants | Each type will be analyzed in detail in **SECTION 2**. # 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of Condensation The condensation of refrigerant inside a condenser is a **three-stage thermal process** occurring at essentially constant pressure. Understanding these stages is critical for both conceptual and numerical questions in KTU exams. ### Stage 1: De-Superheating - The refrigerant enters as a **superheated vapor** at temperature $T_2$ (typically 70–90 °C). - It is cooled from $T_2$ down to the **saturation temperature** $T_{sat}$ corresponding to condenser pressure. - **Heat transfer mode:** Sensible heat removal (vapor cools without phase change). ### Stage 2: Condensation (Latent Heat Removal) - At saturation temperature, the vapor begins to **condense** into saturated liquid. - A large amount of **latent heat** ($h_{fg}$) is rejected at constant temperature. - This stage dominates the heat load — typically **60–70% of total condenser duty**. ### Stage 3: Sub-Cooling - The saturated liquid is further cooled below $T_{sat}$ to a **sub-cooled liquid** at $T_3$. - **Heat transfer mode:** Sensible heat removal from liquid. > [!NOTE] > **Why Sub-cool the Liquid?** > Sub-cooling increases the **refrigerating effect (RE)** by reducing the enthalpy of liquid entering the expansion valve, thereby improving the **COP** of the system. ## 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Each Condenser Type ### 2.2.1 Air-Cooled Condenser (ACC) - **Construction:** A bank of finned copper tubes with a fan to force air over them. - **Heat Transfer:** $Q_c = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_{pa} \cdot (T_{a,out} - T_{a,in})$ - **Pros:** No water required, low maintenance, suitable for remote locations. - **Cons:** Low heat transfer coefficient (~30 W/m²K), bulky, performance affected by ambient air temperature. **Working Principle (Why Forced Convection?):** Natural convection alone gives a very low heat transfer coefficient. A fan increases air velocity, raising the convective heat transfer coefficient and reducing the required surface area. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Favorite Question (Frequent):** *"Why is finning used in air-cooled condensers but not in water-cooled condensers?"* > **Answer:** Air has a much lower heat transfer coefficient than water. To compensate, fins **increase the air-side surface area** dramatically, balancing the lower $h_a$ with a larger area $A$. Water does not need fins because $h_w$ is inherently high (~1500–10000 W/m²K). ### 2.2.2 Water-Cooled Condenser (WCC) Subtypes commonly examined in KTU: | Subtype | Construction | Heat Transfer Coefficient | |---|---|---| | **Shell-and-Coil** | Water coil inside a refrigerant shell | Moderate | | **Shell-and-Tube** | Tubes carrying water inside a refrigerant shell | High | | **Tube-in-Tube (Double Tube)** | Water in inner tube, refrigerant in annular space | High | | **Brazed Plate Heat Exchanger (BPHE)** | Stacked brazed plates | Very High | - **Heat Transfer:** $Q_c = \dot{m}_w \cdot c_{pw} \cdot (T_{w,out} - T_{w,in})$ - **Pros:** Compact, high $U$-value, less affected by ambient conditions. - **Cons:** Requires cooling tower, water treatment, pump, and water piping. ### 2.2.3 Evaporative Condenser (EC) - **Construction:** A water-sprayed coil with a fan drawing air through it. Some of the spray water evaporates, absorbing heat from the refrigerant. - **Hybrid Operation:** Combines features of ACC and WCC. - **Heat Transfer:** Combination of sensible cooling by air and latent cooling by water evaporation. - **Best Suited For:** Ammonia-based industrial refrigeration, cold storage plants. > [!TIP] > **Evaporative condensers achieve the lowest condensing temperature** among the three types, which directly translates into the **highest COP** — a favorite KTU numerical trick. ## 2.3 The Heat Rejection Factor (HRF) — A Key KTU Concept The **Heat Rejection Factor** is the ratio of condenser heat rejection to evaporator heat absorption: $$\text{HRF} = \frac{Q_c}{Q_e} = \frac{h_2 - h_3}{h_1 - h_4}$$ Using the energy balance of VCRS, this can also be expressed as: $$\text{HRF} = 1 + \frac{1}{\text{COP}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** For every 1 kW of refrigeration effect, the condenser must reject $\text{HRF} \times Q_e$ kW of heat. A higher HRF means a less efficient cycle. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $q_c = h_2 - h_3$ | Heat rejected per kg of refrigerant | kJ/kg | | 2 | $Q_c = \dot{m}_r \cdot (h_2 - h_3)$ | Total condenser heat rejection | kW | | 3 | $Q_c = Q_e + W_{comp}$ | Energy balance of VCRS | kW | | 4 | $Q_c = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_{pa} \cdot \Delta T_a$ | Heat rejected to air (ACC) | kW | | 5 | $Q_c = \dot{m}_w \cdot c_{pw} \cdot \Delta T_w$ | Heat rejected to water (WCC) | kW | | 6 | $A_c = \dfrac{Q_c}{U_c \cdot \text{LMTD}}$ | Required condenser surface area | m² | | 7 | $\text{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log Mean Temperature Difference | K or °C | | 8 | $\dot{m}_r = \dfrac{Q_e}{h_1 - h_4}$ | Mass flow rate of refrigerant | kg/s | | 9 | $\text{HRF} = 1 + \dfrac{1}{\text{COP}}$ | Heat Rejection Factor | dimensionless | | 10 | $\text{COP} = \dfrac{T_e}{T_c - T_e}$ | Carnot COP (ideal reference) | dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Caution on LMTD formula:** For counter-flow water-cooled condensers, $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$ and $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$ (use hot = refrigerant side, cold = water side). ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Condensers are the **backbone of every refrigeration and air-conditioning installation**: - **Domestic Refrigerators:** Use natural convection air-cooled condensers (mounted on the back wall). - **Centralized AC Plants:** Use brazed plate or shell-and-tube water-cooled condensers. - **Cold Storage Warehouses (a focus of this entire course):** Use **evaporative condensers** for ammonia systems to maintain low condensing temperatures and high reliability. - **Industrial Process Cooling:** Multi-pressure condensers are used to serve multiple evaporators at different temperatures. # 📐 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Heat Rejection in a Condenser from Energy Balance Consider the VCRS with the following state points: - **State 1:** Saturated vapor at evaporator exit - **State 2:** Superheated vapor at compressor exit - **State 3:** Saturated or sub-cooled liquid at condenser exit - **State 4:** Liquid-vapor mixture after expansion valve **Step 1:** Apply the steady-flow energy equation (SFEE) to the **condenser control volume**, neglecting kinetic and potential energy changes and assuming no work interaction: $$Q_c = \dot{m}_r \cdot (h_2 - h_3)$$ **Step 2:** Apply SFEE to the **compressor**: $$W_{comp} = \dot{m}_r \cdot (h_2 - h_1)$$ **Step 3:** Apply SFEE to the **evaporator**: $$Q_e = \dot{m}_r \cdot (h_1 - h_4)$$ **Step 4:** For a **cyclic steady-state process**, the net change in enthalpy over one complete cycle is zero. Therefore, summing the energy interactions: $$\begin{aligned} Q_{net} &= W_{comp} \\ Q_c &= Q_e + W_{comp} \\ \dot{m}_r (h_2 - h_3) &= \dot{m}_r (h_1 - h_4) + \dot{m}_r (h_2 - h_1) \\ h_2 - h_3 &= h_1 - h_4 + h_2 - h_1 \\ h_2 - h_3 &= h_2 - h_4 \\ \therefore Q_c &= \dot{m}_r (h_2 - h_3) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 (Important — Heat Rejection Factor Derivation):** Divide both sides by $Q_e$: $$\frac{Q_c}{Q_e} = \frac{\dot{m}_r (h_2 - h_3)}{\dot{m}_r (h_1 - h_4)} = 1 + \frac{W_{comp}}{Q_e} = 1 + \frac{1}{\text{COP}}$$ $$\boxed{\text{HRF} = 1 + \frac{1}{\text{COP}}}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Required Condenser Surface Area from LMTD **Step 1:** The basic rate equation for a heat exchanger is: $$Q_c = U_c \cdot A_c \cdot \text{LMTD}$$ where $U_c$ is the **overall heat transfer coefficient** (W/m²K) and $A_c$ is the heat transfer area (m²). **Step 2:** For a **counter-flow** water-cooled condenser, the LMTD is defined as: $$\text{LMTD} = \frac{(T_{r,in} - T_{w,out}) - (T_{r,out} - T_{w,in})}{\ln\left(\dfrac{T_{r,in} - T_{w,out}}{T_{r,out} - T_{w,in}}\right)}$$ **Step 3:** In a condenser, the refrigerant temperature remains nearly constant at $T_{sat}$ during the latent heat phase, simplifying the LMTD calculation. **Step 4:** Solving for the required area: $$\boxed{A_c = \frac{Q_c}{U_c \cdot \text{LMTD}}}$$ ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU 2024 Style) ### Example 1: Heat Rejection Calculation **Problem (KTU Pattern):** A refrigeration system using R-134a has: - Enthalpy at compressor exit: $h_2 = 280$ kJ/kg - Enthalpy at condenser exit: $h_3 = 100$ kJ/kg - Refrigeration effect: $q_e = 150$ kJ/kg - Required cooling capacity: $Q_e = 30$ kW **Find:** (a) Heat rejected by the condenser per kg. (b) Total condenser heat rejection. (c) Heat Rejection Factor. **Solution:** **(a) Heat rejected per kg:** $$q_c = h_2 - h_3 = 280 - 100 = 180 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **(b) Mass flow rate of refrigerant:** $$\dot{m}_r = \frac{Q_e}{q_e} = \frac{30}{150} = 0.2 \text{ kg/s}$$ **Total condenser heat rejection:** $$Q_c = \dot{m}_r \cdot q_c = 0.2 \times 180 = 36 \text{ kW}$$ **(c) Heat Rejection Factor:** $$\text{HRF} = \frac{Q_c}{Q_e} = \frac{36}{30} = 1.2$$ **Verification using COP:** $$\text{COP} = \frac{q_e}{q_c - q_e} = \frac{150}{180 - 150} = \frac{150}{30} = 5$$ $$\text{HRF} = 1 + \frac{1}{\text{COP}} = 1 + \frac{1}{5} = 1.2 \quad \checkmark$$ ### Example 2: LMTD and Surface Area of a Water-Cooled Condenser **Problem:** A counter-flow shell-and-tube water-cooled condenser operates with the following data: - Refrigerant (R-134a) enters at $T_{r,in} = 60$ °C (superheated) and leaves at $T_{r,out} = 35$ °C (sub-cooled) - Cooling water enters at $T_{w,in} = 25$ °C and leaves at $T_{w,out} = 32$ °C - Overall heat transfer coefficient: $U_c = 1500$ W/m²K - Heat to be rejected: $Q_c = 50$ kW **Find:** (a) LMTD. (b) Required heat transfer area. **Solution:** **(a) Calculate LMTD:** $$\Delta T_1 = T_{r,in} - T_{w,out} = 60 - 32 = 28 \text{ °C}$$ $$\Delta T_2 = T_{r,out} - T_{w,in} = 35 - 25 = 10 \text{ °C}$$ $$\text{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \frac{28 - 10}{\ln(28 / 10)} = \frac{18}{\ln(2.8)} = \frac{18}{1.0296}$$ $$\boxed{\text{LMTD} \approx 17.48 \text{ °C}}$$ **(b) Required area:** $$A_c = \frac{Q_c}{U_c \cdot \text{LMTD}} = \frac{50{,}000}{1500 \times 17.48} = \frac{50{,}000}{26{,}220}$$ $$\boxed{A_c \approx 1.907 \text{ m}^2}$$ > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Insight:** Always verify the temperature cross is within limits. Since $\Delta T_2 = 10$ °C > 0, the LMTD is valid and physically realizable. ### Example 3: Cooling Water Flow Rate for a Water-Cooled Condenser **Problem:** A condenser rejects 60 kW of heat. Cooling water enters at 24 °C and leaves at 30 °C. Take $c_{pw} = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K. **Find:** Required water mass flow rate. **Solution:** $$\dot{m}_w = \frac{Q_c}{c_{pw} \cdot \Delta T_w} = \frac{60}{4.18 \times (30 - 24)} = \frac{60}{25.08}$$ $$\boxed{\dot{m}_w \approx 2.39 \text{ kg/s}}$$ In volumetric terms (at $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³): $$\dot{V}_w = \frac{\dot{m}_w}{\rho_w} = \frac{2.39}{1000} \times 3600 \approx 8.6 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Condenser Types | Parameter | Air-Cooled (ACC) | Water-Cooled (WCC) | Evaporative (EC) | |---|---|---|---| | $U$-value (W/m²K) | 30–60 | 1500–3000 | 600–1200 | | Condensing Temp. (°C) | 50–60 | 35–45 | 30–40 | | COP Impact | Lowest | Highest | Highest | | Water Consumption | None | High | Moderate (blow-down) | | Capital Cost | Low | High | Moderate | | Maintenance | Low (clean fins) | High (water treatment) | Moderate | | Footprint | Large | Compact | Moderate | | Best For | Small/domestic | Industrial chillers | Cold storage | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Memory Hook:** *"Air = Big & Bulky, Water = Compact & Costly, Evaporative = Best of Both Worlds"* # 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Position of Condenser in VCRS ```mermaid flowchart LR compNode["Compressor"] -->|"State 2: Superheated Vapour

"Refrigerant Path" .-> B1 B1 -. Always trace state numbers in VCRS diagrams to avoid confusion in exam answers. # 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1: [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **"Define a condenser. List the three types of condensers used in vapor compression refrigeration systems with one application each."** **Model Answer (Verbatim for Board Valuation):** A condenser is a **heat-rejecting heat exchanger** in a VCRS in which the high-pressure, high-temperature refrigerant vapor from the compressor is condensed by rejecting its latent heat to a cooling medium (air or water) maintained at a lower temperature, while the pressure remains essentially constant. **Three types of condensers:** | Type | Cooling Medium | Application | |---|---|---| | 1. Air-Cooled Condenser (ACC) | Atmospheric air | Domestic refrigerators, window ACs | | 2. Water-Cooled Condenser (WCC) | Cooling water | Industrial chillers, large plants | | 3. Evaporative Condenser (EC) | Air + water spray | Cold storage, ammonia refrigeration plants | > [!NOTE] > **Valuation Tip (3 marks):** Definition = 1 mark, List with applications = 2 marks (½ mark × 4). ### Question A2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **"Define Heat Rejection Factor (HRF) of a condenser. Derive its relation with COP."** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Heat Rejection Factor is defined as the ratio of the rate of heat rejected in the condenser to the rate of heat absorbed in the evaporator of a refrigeration system. $$\text{HRF} = \frac{Q_c}{Q_e}$$ **Derivation:** From the energy balance of VCRS, $Q_c = Q_e + W_{comp}$ $$\begin{aligned} \text{HRF} &= \frac{Q_e + W_{comp}}{Q_e} = 1 + \frac{W_{comp}}{Q_e} \\ \text{Since COP} &= \frac{Q_e}{W_{comp}} \Rightarrow \frac{W_{comp}}{Q_e} = \frac{1}{\text{COP}} \\ \therefore \text{HRF} &= 1 + \frac{1}{\text{COP}} \end{aligned}$$ > [!TIP] > **Valuation Tip:** State the definition first (1 mark), then the algebraic derivation (2 marks). Do not skip steps. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks, typically split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each: (a) descriptive/conceptual and (b) numerical/derivation. --- ### 📌 Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme Pattern]** **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working of an **evaporative condenser**. State its advantages and disadvantages over a water-cooled condenser. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A refrigeration system using R-12 has a refrigerating effect of 200 kJ/kg and a heat rejected in the condenser of 250 kJ/kg. The mass flow rate of refrigerant is 0.15 kg/s. The condenser uses cooling water entering at 22 °C and leaving at 30 °C. The overall heat transfer coefficient is 2000 W/m²K. Calculate: (i) COP of the system (ii) Heat rejected per second in the condenser (iii) Mass flow rate of cooling water required (take $c_p = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K) (iv) LMTD and required heat transfer area, if the refrigerant condenses at 40 °C **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** An **evaporative condenser** is a hybrid heat-rejecting device that combines the principles of air-cooled and water-cooled condensers. It consists of a coil carrying the refrigerant over which water is sprayed continuously, while a fan draws ambient air through the coil. **Working:** - Hot refrigerant vapor flows through the coil. - Water sprayed on the coil partially **evaporates**, absorbing both the sensible heat of the refrigerant and the latent heat of vaporization of water. - The induced-draft fan forces air over the coil, removing the moist air and replacing it with fresh dry air. - The refrigerant condenses and may even get sub-cooled. **Diagram (Schematic Description):** A rectangular box with a finned coil at the bottom, water spray nozzles above the coil, an axial fan at the top (induced draft), eliminator plates to remove water droplets from the exhaust air. **Advantages over Water-Cooled Condenser:** 1. Lower condensing temperature → higher COP and lower compressor work. 2. Reduced water consumption (only for makeup, not circulation through cooling tower). 3. No need for a separate cooling tower and circulation pump → lower capital and operating cost. 4. Better suited for areas with scarce water. **Disadvantages:** 1. Performance depends on ambient wet-bulb temperature. 2. Requires water treatment to prevent scaling and algae growth. 3. Higher initial cost compared to ACC. **Part (b) — 7 Marks (Numerical):** **Given:** - $q_e = 200$ kJ/kg, $q_c = 250$ kJ/kg - $\dot{m}_r = 0.15$ kg/s - $T_{w,in} = 22$ °C, $T_{w,out} = 30$ °C - $U = 2000$ W/m²K - $T_{sat} = 40$ °C (condensing temperature) **(i) COP of the system:** $$\text{COP} = \frac{q_e}{q_c - q_e} = \frac{200}{250 - 200} = \frac{200}{50} = 4.0$$ **(ii) Total heat rejected in condenser:** $$Q_c = \dot{m}_r \cdot q_c = 0.15 \times 250 = 37.5 \text{ kW}$$ **(iii) Mass flow rate of cooling water:** $$\dot{m}_w = \frac{Q_c}{c_p \cdot (T_{w,out} - T_{w,in})} = \frac{37.5}{4.18 \times (30 - 22)} = \frac{37.5}{33.44} = 1.121 \text{ kg/s}$$ **(iv) LMTD and required area:** For counter-flow condenser (refrigerant condenses at constant $T_{sat} = 40$ °C, water heats from 22 °C to 30 °C): $$\Delta T_1 = 40 - 30 = 10 \text{ °C}$$ $$\Delta T_2 = 40 - 22 = 18 \text{ °C}$$ $$\text{LMTD} = \frac{18 - 10}{\ln(18 / 10)} = \frac{8}{\ln(1.8)} = \frac{8}{0.5878} \approx 13.61 \text{ °C}$$ $$\boxed{A_c = \frac{Q_c}{U \cdot \text{LMTD}} = \frac{37{,}500}{2000 \times 13.61} = \frac{37{,}500}{27{,}220} \approx 1.378 \text{ m}^2}$$ --- ### 📌 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme Pattern]** **(a)** Compare **air-cooled, water-cooled, and evaporative condensers** on the basis of: cooling medium, heat transfer coefficient, condensing temperature, COP achieved, water consumption, and typical applications. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A water-cooled condenser of a refrigeration plant rejects 80 kW of heat. The condensing temperature is 40 °C. The cooling water enters at 25 °C and leaves at 32 °C. The overall heat transfer coefficient based on the outer (water-side) area is 1500 W/m²K. Calculate: (i) The LMTD of the condenser (counter-flow assumption) (ii) The required heat transfer surface area (iii) The mass flow rate of cooling water (iv) The capacity of the cooling tower required, if the range and approach are 7 °C and 4 °C respectively. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 Marks (Comparison Table):** | Parameter | Air-Cooled | Water-Cooled | Evaporative | |---|---|---|---| | Cooling medium | Atmospheric air | Cooling water (recirculated) | Air + water spray | | $U$-value (W/m²K) | 30–60 | 1500–3000 | 600–1200 | | Condensing T (°C) | 50–60 (high) | 35–45 (moderate) | 30–40 (lowest) | | COP achieved | Lowest | Moderate to High | Highest | | Water consumption | None | Very high | Low (blow-down only) | | Capital cost | Low | High | Moderate | | Maintenance | Clean fins | Water treatment, scaling | Moderate (spray nozzles) | | Typical application | Domestic fridge, window AC | Chillers, industrial | Cold storage, ammonia plants | **Part (b) — 7 Marks (Numerical):** **Given:** $Q_c = 80$ kW, $T_c = 40$ °C, $T_{w,in} = 25$ °C, $T_{w,out} = 32$ °C, $U = 1500$ W/m²K, Range = 7 °C, Approach = 4 °C. **(i) LMTD (Counter-flow):** $$\Delta T_1 = 40 - 32 = 8 \text{ °C}$$ $$\Delta T_2 = 40 - 25 = 15 \text{ °C}$$ $$\text{LMTD} = \frac{15 - 8}{\ln(15 / 8)} = \frac{7}{\ln(1.875)} = \frac{7}{0.6286} \approx 11.135 \text{ °C}$$ **(ii) Heat transfer area:** $$A_c = \frac{80{,}000}{1500 \times 11.135} = \frac{80{,}000}{16{,}702.5} \approx 4.79 \text{ m}^2$$ **(iii) Mass flow rate of cooling water:** $$\dot{m}_w = \frac{80}{4.18 \times (32 - 25)} = \frac{80}{29.26} = 2.734 \text{ kg/s}$$ $$\dot{V}_w = 2.734 \times 3.6 = 9.84 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$$ **(iv) Cooling tower capacity:** Wet-bulb temperature of inlet air = $T_{c,out}$ - Approach = $32 - 4 = 28$ °C Range = 7 °C (already given) Cooling tower capacity = $\dot{m}_w \times c_p \times \text{Range} = 2.734 \times 4.18 \times 7 = 80.0$ kW (matches $Q_c$) > [!NOTE] > **Verification:** The cooling tower must reject the same heat as the condenser, i.e., 80 kW. Hence, the result is consistent. **The numerical value confirms the energy balance is satisfied.** --- > [!WARNING] > **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Wrong sign convention in LMTD:** Always take the **larger** temperature difference as $\Delta T_1$ and the **smaller** as $\Delta T_2$. Reversing them gives a negative LMTD — an instant 2-mark deduction. > 2. **Units mismatch:** Converting kW to W (multiply by 1000) is the most common error. $Q$ in W, $U$ in W/m²K, LMTD in K — all must be consistent. > 3. **Forgetting to use $c_p$ for water:** Some students use 1 kJ/kg·K (which is only correct at 0 °C). At 25–30 °C, $c_p \approx 4.18$ kJ/kg·K is the KTU-accepted value. > 4. **Confusing Q_c with Q_e:** Condenser rejects MORE heat than evaporator absorbs. If a student gets $Q_c < Q_e$, energy conservation has been violated — full marks denied. > 5. **Missing the cooling tower approach/range in part (b):** Several students calculate Range but ignore Approach, losing 2 marks. > 6. **Mixing up ACC finning logic:** Saying "fins increase the water-side area" is incorrect. Fins are always on the **air side** in ACC. > 7. **Forgetting the energy balance check:** $Q_c = Q_e + W_{comp}$ should always hold. Use this as a verification step in numerical problems. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Definition:** Condenser = constant-pressure, constant-temperature (during latent phase) heat exchanger that rejects heat from refrigerant to a sink. - ✅ **Three Types:** Air-Cooled, Water-Cooled, Evaporative. - ✅ **Three Internal Stages:** De-superheating → Condensation → Sub-cooling. - ✅ **Core Formula:** $Q_c = \dot{m}_r (h_2 - h_3)$ — must always be positive and greater than $Q_e$. - ✅ **Energy Balance:** $Q_c = Q_e + W_{comp}$ (Cyclic First Law). - ✅ **HRF Relation:** $\text{HRF} = 1 + \dfrac{1}{\text{COP}}$ — memorize this verbatim. - ✅ **COP Formula:** $\text{COP} = \dfrac{q_e}{w_{comp}} = \dfrac{q_e}{q_c - q_e}$ for VCRS. - ✅ **LMTD Formula:** $\text{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ — verify both $\Delta T$'s are positive. - ✅ **Surface Area:** $A_c = \dfrac{Q_c}{U_c \cdot \text{LMTD}}$ — keep units in SI. - ✅ **Fins in ACC:** Always on the air side (low $h$ side) — never on refrigerant side. - ✅ **Cooling Tower Design Range:** Range = $T_{w,out} - T_{w,in}$; Approach = $T_{w,out} - T_{wb}$. - ✅ **ACC vs WCC vs EC — Memory Hook:** *"Air = Big & Bulky, Water = Compact & Costly, Evaporative = Best of Both Worlds."* - ✅ **Always verify:** $Q_c \geq Q_e$ (condenser always rejects more heat than evaporator absorbs). - ✅ **Most common numerical pattern in KTU:** Given $Q_e$, refrigerant enthalpies → find $Q_c$, $\dot{m}_r$, $\dot{m}_w$, $\text{LMTD}$, $A_c$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Examination Mantra:** *"Read the question, identify the condenser type, write the energy balance, apply LMTD correctly, watch your units, and verify $Q_c = Q_e + W$."* — If you do these five things in order, **full marks** are virtually guaranteed.

topics.title: Expansion Devices and Evaporators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Expansion Devices and Evaporators # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is an Expansion Device? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** > An **Expansion Device** (also called **Throttling Device** or **Expansion Valve**) is a mechanical component of a vapour-compression refrigeration system whose primary function is to **drop the high-pressure, high-temperature liquid refrigerant coming from the condenser down to the low-pressure, low-temperature two-phase mixture required at the inlet of the evaporator**. It simultaneously performs **three critical jobs**: (1) reduces refrigerant pressure, (2) controls the **mass flow rate** of refrigerant into the evaporator, and (3) creates the **pressure differential** between the high-side (condenser side) and low-side (evaporator side) of the system. ### Conceptual Analogy — "The Garden Tap on a Pressurised Pipe" Imagine a municipal water pipeline that delivers water at high pressure to your house. If you open the tap fully, water gushes out because the high pressure pushes it through the small opening. The tap is **restricting the flow** and **dropping the pressure** at the same time. In a refrigeration system, the expansion device plays the exact same role: - The **high-pressure liquid** from the condenser is like pressurised water in the pipe. - The **expansion device** is the tap — it restricts flow, drops pressure, and meters how much refrigerant enters the evaporator. - The **evaporator** is the spray nozzle where the cold two-phase mixture absorbs heat and boils. Without this device, the refrigerant would remain at high pressure, no evaporation could occur, and **no refrigeration effect would be produced**. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The expansion process is **isenthalpic** (constant enthalpy) because the device is essentially a small orifice with no work transfer and negligible heat transfer due to the very short residence time of the fluid. --- ## 1.2 What is an Evaporator? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Academic Definition:** > An **Evaporator** is the **heat-absorbing heat exchanger** of a vapour-compression refrigeration system. Its job is to **extract heat ($Q_e$) from the space or substance being refrigerated** by allowing the low-pressure liquid refrigerant to **boil and evaporate** at a controlled low temperature. The refrigerant enters as a low-quality two-phase mixture and leaves as a **saturated (or slightly superheated) vapour**, absorbing the **latent heat of vaporisation** in the process. ### Conceptual Analogy — "The Sponge Inside a Cold Box" Think of a sponge that has been soaked in a very cold liquid. When you place it inside a warm box, the liquid in the sponge absorbs heat from the air and evaporates, leaving the air cold. The evaporator is exactly this sponge: - The **liquid refrigerant** inside the tubes/coils is the "cold liquid". - The **air or water** to be cooled is the warm surroundings. - As refrigerant boils, it **sucks heat** out of its surroundings — this is the **refrigeration effect**. ### 1.3 Classification — At a Glance | Component | Primary Function | Key Output | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Expansion Device | Pressure drop + flow metering | Throttling enthalpy $h_3 = h_4$ | | Evaporator | Heat absorption + phase change | Refrigeration effect $RE = h_1 - h_4$ | | Combined System | Complete low-pressure loop | Cools the desired space | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric:** The most common pair in domestic refrigerators and window ACs is **Capillary Tube + Plate-Type/Bare-Tube Evaporator**, while central air-conditioning plants use **Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TEV) + DX Shell-and-Tube Evaporator**. --- ## 1.4 Why Are These Two Studied Together? In the KTU 2024 scheme, Module 2 groups **Expansion Devices** and **Evaporators** because they are the **two lowest-pressure components** of the refrigeration cycle. Their **sizing, selection, and interaction** directly determine: 1. **Refrigerating Capacity** ($\text{kW}$ or $\text{TR}$) 2. **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** of the system 3. **Stability of the cycle** under varying load conditions 4. **Reliability and defrosting behaviour** in low-temperature applications # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Classification of Expansion Devices ### A. **Capillary Tube** > [!IMPORTANT] > A **Capillary Tube** is a long, small-bore copper tube (typically **inner diameter 0.5 mm to 2.5 mm**, length **1.5 m to 6 m**) that acts as a **fixed-orifice** expansion device. It has **no moving parts** and meters flow by **friction losses** along its length. **Operating Principle:** - High-pressure sub-cooled or saturated liquid refrigerant enters. - As it flows through the narrow tube, **frictional pressure drop** occurs continuously. - At the exit, the refrigerant is at the **evaporator pressure** with a quality $x_4$ (typically $0.15$ to $0.25$). **Advantages:** - Simple, cheap, no maintenance. - No moving parts to wear out. - Provides near-constant pressure differential at steady state. **Disadvantages:** - **Cannot adjust** to changing load conditions automatically. - Sensitive to **particulate contamination** — chokes easily. - Pressure drop is highly sensitive to **sub-cooling** (higher sub-cool → lower mass flow → risk of starved evaporator). **Applications:** Domestic refrigerators, freezers, window ACs, small water coolers. --- ### B. **Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TEV / TXV)** > [!IMPORTANT] > A **TEV** is an **automatic, modulating** expansion device that maintains a **constant superheat** at the evaporator outlet by throttling refrigerant flow in response to the **thermodynamic state at the evaporator exit**. **Key Components of a TEV:** 1. **Sensing Bulb** — clamped at the evaporator outlet, filled with the **same refrigerant** (or a charge-matched fluid) as the system. 2. **Diaphragm / Power Head** — converts bulb pressure into a mechanical force. 3. **Spring** — provides opposing force, sets the superheat setpoint. 4. **Needle/Pin & Seat** — throttles refrigerant flow. **Operating Principle (Step-by-Step):** 1. As evaporator load **increases** → more refrigerant evaporates → superheat at outlet **decreases** → bulb pressure **decreases** → diaphragm moves **up** → pin **opens** → more refrigerant flows. 2.
