As load **decreases** → superheat **increases** → bulb pressure **rises** → diaphragm moves **down** → pin **closes** → less refrigerant flows. **Advantage:** Maintains **optimum refrigerant charge** in the evaporator under all load conditions → maximises COP. **Applications:** Central AC plants, commercial refrigeration, supermarket cold rooms, chillers. --- ### C. **Float Valves** > [!IMPORTANT] > A **Float Valve** is an expansion device that maintains a **constant liquid level inside a flooded evaporator** by acting like the ball-cock in a toilet tank. **Two Variants:** - **High-Side Float Valve:** Float is located in a chamber between condenser and evaporator — controls flow based on **condenser-side liquid level**. - **Low-Side Float Valve:** Float is located **inside the flooded evaporator** itself — controls flow directly based on evaporator liquid level. **Applications:** Large industrial ammonia refrigeration plants, ice plants, cold storage facilities with flooded evaporators. --- ### D. **Other Expansion Devices (Briefly)** | Device | Key Feature | Application | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Automatic Expansion Valve (AXV)** | Maintains **constant evaporator pressure** (not superheat) | Older systems, variable load | | **Electric Expansion Valve (EEV)** | Electronically controlled via solenoid + stepper motor | Modern precision AC, VRF systems | | **Solenoid Valve** | ON/OFF control, electrically operated | Load matching, pump-down cycles | --- ## 2.2 Detailed Classification of Evaporators ### **Based on Construction** | Type | Description | Heat Transfer Augmentation | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Bare-Tube Coil** | Smooth copper tubes | Low (relies on natural convection) | | **Plate-Type (Surface)** | Tubes bonded between metal plates | Medium (large flat surface) | | **Finned-Tube** | Aluminium fins mechanically bonded to copper tubes | High (forced convection air-side) | | **Shell-and-Tube** | Tubes inside a shell with water/brine on shell side | High (water-side convection) | ### **Based on Refrigerant Flow Pattern** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Dry Expansion (DX) Evaporator** — Only a portion of the refrigerant evaporates inside; outlet is **slightly superheated vapour**. Always paired with **TEV** to prevent liquid slugging at the compressor. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Flooded Evaporator** — Tubes are **completely filled with liquid refrigerant** at all times; evaporation occurs as bubbles rising through the liquid. Always paired with **float valve** or **low-pressure receiver**. ### **Based on Secondary Fluid** - **Air-Cooling Evaporator** (finned, forced convection): Used in ACs, cold rooms. - **Liquid-Cooling Evaporator** (shell-and-tube, chiller): Used for chilled water / brine production. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table is your one-stop cheat-sheet for every numerical problem the KTU board can ask on this topic.** | # | Formula / Equation | Symbols | Engineering Use | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $h_3 = h_4$ (Throttling) | $h_3$ = condenser exit enthalpy, $h_4$ = evaporator inlet enthalpy | Isenthalpic expansion | | 2 | $x_4 = \dfrac{h_4 - h_{f4}}{h_{fg4}}$ | $h_{f4}$, $h_{fg4}$ at evaporator pressure | Quality after throttling | | 3 | $RE = h_1 - h_4$ | $RE$ = Refrigeration Effect (kJ/kg) | Useful cooling per kg | | 4 | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q_e}{h_1 - h_4}$ | $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate (kg/s), $Q_e$ = cooling load (kW) | Mass flow rate calculation | | 5 | $\text{COP} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | — | System performance | | 6 | $Q_e = U \cdot A \cdot \text{LMTD}$ | $U$ = overall HTC, $A$ = area, LMTD = log mean temp diff | Evaporator sizing | | 7 | $\text{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | $\Delta T_1$, $\Delta T_2$ = terminal temperature differences | Heat exchanger design | | 8 | $1 \ \text{TR} = 3.517 \ \text{kW} = 211 \ \text{kJ/min}$ | — | Capacity unit conversion | | 9 | $\Delta P_{\text{cap}} = \dfrac{32 f L \dot{m}^2}{\rho \cdot \pi^2 D^5}$ | $f$ = friction factor, $L$ = length, $D$ = diameter, $\rho$ = density | Capillary tube design | | 10 | $\dot{m}_{\text{cap}} = C_D A_{\text{cap}} \sqrt{2 \rho (P_c - P_e)}$ | $C_D$ = discharge coefficient, $A_{\text{cap}}$ = area, $P_c$, $P_e$ = pressures | Capillary tube flow | | 11 | Superheat = $T_1 - T_{\text{sat}}(P_e)$ | $T_1$ = compressor inlet temp | TEV setpoint | | 12 | $h_a - h_b = U A (T_a - T_b) / 2$ | $h_a, h_b$ = enthalpies, $T_a, T_b$ = temps | Approximate evaporator | > [!WARNING] > **Pipe character `|` has been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` in the formulas above to prevent markdown table breakage — this is critical for the renderer.** --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility In industry, the choice of expansion device and evaporator type is **never arbitrary**: - **Domestic fridge manufacturers** choose **capillary tube + plate evaporator** because cost is paramount and the load is nearly constant. - **HVAC contractors** in Kerala's humid climate choose **TEV + DX finned coil** because summer/winter load varies by 30–50 %. - **Industrial ice plants** choose **low-side float valve + flooded shell-and-tube evaporator** because they need very high heat transfer rates for rapid ice formation. - **Supermarket cold storage** uses **EEV + DX evaporators with electric defrost** because precise temperature control minimises food spoilage. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Refrigeration Effect from First Principles > [!NOTE] > **Goal:** Prove that $RE = h_1 - h_4$ using steady-flow energy equation (SFEE) on the evaporator control volume. ### Step 1: Define the Control Volume The evaporator is treated as a **steady-flow, open system** (control volume) with: - **Inlet:** State 4 (low-quality two-phase mixture) - **Outlet:** State 1 (saturated or superheated vapour) ### Step 2: Apply the Steady-Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) For a control volume with **one inlet and one outlet**, **no work transfer**, and **negligible KE/PE changes**: $$ \dot{Q}_e = \dot{m} (h_1 - h_4) $$ ### Step 3: Define Refrigeration Effect per unit mass Dividing both sides by $\dot{m}$: $$ q_e = h_1 - h_4 \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{kg}}\right] $$ This is the **specific refrigeration effect** — the heat absorbed per kilogram of refrigerant circulated. ### Step 4: Verify Energy Balance The energy entering via refrigerant + heat absorbed = energy leaving via refrigerant: $$ \dot{m} h_4 + \dot{Q}_e = \dot{m} h_1 $$ Rearranging: $$ \dot{Q}_e = \dot{m}(h_1 - h_4) $$ **Interpretation:** The evaporator removes heat from the refrigerated space at a rate equal to the mass flow rate times the enthalpy rise of the refrigerant. > [!NOTE] > **Marks Tip:** In KTU valuation, you **must draw the control volume** and write the SFEE assumption list (steady-state, negligible KE, no work, negligible heat loss to surroundings). Missing these will cost **2 marks**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Capillary Tube Length (Basic Model) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Goal:** Derive an expression for capillary tube length $L$ as a function of pressure drop, flow rate, and geometry. ### Step 1: Force Balance Inside the Tube For steady, incompressible flow through a horizontal tube with friction: $$ \Delta P_{\text{friction}} = \dfrac{4 f L \dot{m}^2}{2 \rho D \cdot A^2} $$ Using $A = \pi D^2 / 4$, this simplifies to: $$ \Delta P = \dfrac{32 f L \dot{m}^2}{\rho \pi^2 D^5} $$ ### Step 2: Set Frictional Drop Equal to Available Pressure Difference For the capillary tube, the available pressure drop is $P_c - P_e$ (condenser to evaporator): $$ P_c - P_e = \dfrac{32 f L \dot{m}^2}{\rho \pi^2 D^5} $$ ### Step 3: Solve for Tube Length $$ L = \dfrac{(P_c - P_e) \rho \pi^2 D^5}{32 f \dot{m}^2} $$ ### Step 4: Incorporate Sub-Cooling Correction Because refrigerant sub-cooling increases density, the **effective** mass flow is: $$ \dot{m}_{\text{actual}} = K \cdot \dot{m}_{\text{saturated}} $$ where $K = \sqrt{\rho_{\text{actual}} / \rho_{\text{saturated}}}$. The corrected length: $$ L = \dfrac{(P_c - P_e) \rho \pi^2 D^5}{32 f \dot{m}_{\text{actual}}^2} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Exam Trap:** Many students forget to convert **mass flow rate** into **kg/s** consistently when $L$ is asked in metres and $P_c - P_e$ is in Pa. Always check units: $[Pa] = [N/m^2] = [kg/(m \cdot s^2)]$. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — LMTD for Evaporator Heat Transfer ### Step 1: Set Up the Heat Exchanger Configuration Consider a **counter-flow** evaporator (most efficient configuration): - **Hot fluid (refrigerant):** enters at $T_{r,\text{in}}$, leaves at $T_{r,\text{out}}$. - **Cold fluid (water/air):** enters at $T_{c,\text{in}}$, leaves at $T_{c,\text{out}}$. ### Step 2: Identify Terminal Temperature Differences At one end of the evaporator: $$ \Delta T_1 = T_{r,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}} $$ At the other end: $$ \Delta T_2 = T_{r,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{out}} $$ ### Step 3: Apply the LMTD Definition $$ \text{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} $$ This equation is **exact for constant specific heats and constant overall heat transfer coefficient $U$**. ### Step 4: Apply to Refrigerant Side (Phase Change) For the refrigerant, $T_{r,\text{in}} = T_{r,\text{out}} = T_{\text{sat}}$ (constant during evaporation). This special case gives: $$ \Delta T_1 = T_{\text{sat}} - T_{c,\text{in}} $$ $$ \Delta T_2 = T_{\text{sat}} - T_{c,\text{out}} $$ ### Step 5: Substitution Substituting into the LMTD formula: $$ \text{LMTD} = \dfrac{(T_{\text{sat}} - T_{c,\text{in}}) - (T_{\text{sat}} - T_{c,\text{out}})}{\ln \left[ \dfrac{T_{\text{sat}} - T_{c,\text{in}}}{T_{\text{sat}} - T_{c,\text{out}}} \right]} $$ Simplifying numerator: $$ \text{LMTD} = \dfrac{T_{c,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}}}{\ln \left[ \dfrac{T_{\text{sat}} - T_{c,\text{in}}}{T_{\text{sat}} - T_{c,\text{out}}} \right]} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Special Note:** In many KTU textbook problems, the LMTD reduces to the **Arithmetic Mean Temperature Difference (AMTD)** when the temperature difference is small — but the board **prefers LMTD** for full marks. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Expansion Devices | Parameter | Capillary Tube | TEV | Float Valve (Low-Side) | EEV | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Moving parts** | None | Yes (pin/diaphragm) | Yes (float) | Yes (motorised) | | **Control parameter** | None (fixed) | Superheat | Liquid level | Superheat (programmable) | | **Response to load change** | None | Automatic | Automatic | Automatic + fast | | **Initial cost** | Very low | Medium | High | High | | **Maintenance** | Replace if choked | Spring/diaphragm replacement | Float inspection | Electronic calibration | | **Best for** | Small fixed loads | Variable loads | Flooded systems | Precision systems | | **Evaporator partner** | DX, small | DX, medium–large | Flooded | DX, modern | --- ## 3.5 Numerical Example — Solved in KTU Exam Style > **Question:** A refrigeration system uses R-134a. The refrigerant enters the compressor as saturated vapour at $-10 \ ^\circ\text{C}$ and leaves the condenser as saturated liquid at $30 \ ^\circ\text{C}$. Calculate (a) the refrigeration effect, (b) the COP, and (c) the refrigerant mass flow rate for a **2 TR** cooling load. ### Solution: **Given:** - $T_e = -10 \ ^\circ\text{C}$ → from R-134a tables: $h_1 = h_g \approx 392.3 \ \text{kJ/kg}$ - $T_c = 30 \ ^\circ\text{C}$ → $h_3 = h_f \approx 241.3 \ \text{kJ/kg}$ - Capacity: $Q_e = 2 \ \text{TR} = 2 \times 3.517 = 7.034 \ \text{kW}$ - Throttling: $h_4 = h_3 = 241.3 \ \text{kJ/kg}$ **Step (a): Refrigeration Effect** $$ RE = h_1 - h_4 = 392.3 - 241.3 = 151.0 \ \text{kJ/kg} $$ **Step (b): COP** — We need compressor work. Assume isentropic compression to condenser pressure: From R-134a superheated tables, $h_2 \approx 415.0 \ \text{kJ/kg}$ (this depends on assumed isentropic efficiency). $$ w_c = h_2 - h_1 = 415.0 - 392.3 = 22.7 \ \text{kJ/kg} $$ $$ \text{COP} = \dfrac{RE}{w_c} = \dfrac{151.0}{22.7} \approx 6.65 $$ **Step (c): Mass Flow Rate** $$ \dot{m} = \dfrac{Q_e}{RE} = \dfrac{7.034}{151.0} \times 1000 \approx 46.6 \ \text{kg/h} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conversion tip:** $1 \ \text{kW} = 1 \ \text{kJ/s}$, so $\dot{m} [\text{kg/s}] = Q_e [\text{kW}] / RE [\text{kJ/kg}]$. Convert to kg/h by multiplying by **3600**. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Vapour-Compression Cycle — Highlighting Expansion Device & Evaporator The following block diagram shows the **complete low-pressure side** of a vapour-compression refrigeration system, focusing on the interaction between the expansion device and the evaporator. ```mermaid flowchart LR compOut[Compressor Outlet
State 2 Superheated Vapour] --> cond[CONDENSER
State 2 to State 3
Heat Rejected Qc] cond --> condOut[Condenser Exit
State 3 Saturated Liquid
High Pressure Pc] condOut --> expDev{EXPANSION DEVICE} expOut[Evaporator Inlet
State 4 Low Quality Mixture
Low Pressure Pe] --> evap[EVAPORATOR
State 4 to State 1
Heat Absorbed Qe] evap --> compIn[Compressor Inlet
State 1 Saturated or Slightly Superheated Vapour] compIn --> comp[COMPRESSOR
State 1 to State 2] comp --> compOut qeNote[Refrigeration Effect RE equals h1 minus h4] -.-> evap throtNote[Throttling Isenthalpic h3 equals h4] -.-> expDev ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Follow the loop counter-clockwise from the compressor — high-pressure vapour is condensed, throttled to low pressure, evaporated (refrigeration produced), then recompressed. --- ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TEV) The TEV is a complex assembly of mechanical + thermodynamic components. The following diagram shows how the **sensing signal** from the evaporator outlet drives the **flow control action** at the valve inlet. ```mermaid flowchart TB bulb[Sensing Bulb at Evaporator Outlet
Senses Temperature T1] -->|Bulb Pressure Pb| diaphragm[Power Head Diaphragm Chamber] spring[Adjustable Spring
Setpoint Superheat] -->|Spring Force Fs| diaphragm evapLoad[Refrigerant Load Demand] -->|Superheat Change| bulb diaphragm -->|Net Force Differential| pin[Needle Pin and Seat Assembly] pin -->|Opens or Closes| orifice[Throttling Orifice] condIn[High Pressure Liquid from Condenser] --> orifice orifice -->|Low Pressure Two Phase| evapOut[To Evaporator Inlet] pin -.->|Feedback Loop| bulb ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Operational Logic:** This is a **closed-loop negative feedback control system**. If load increases, superheat falls, bulb pressure drops, diaphragm lifts, pin opens, more refrigerant flows — restoring superheat to setpoint. --- ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Capillary Tube Flow Process ```mermaid flowchart LR hpIn[High Pressure Subcooled Liquid
Pc Tc] --> subcool[Sensible Cooling Region
Pressure Drop Small] subcool --> sat[Saturated Liquid Region
Two Phase Begins] sat --> twoPhase[Two Phase Flow Region
Major Pressure Drop] twoPhase --> lpOut[Low Pressure Quality Mixture
Pe Quality around 0.2] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters:** Most of the pressure drop in a capillary tube occurs in the **two-phase region** — not in the liquid region. This is why capillary tube length is sensitive to the quality at the exit. --- ## 4.4 Classification Tree — Evaporators ```mermaid flowchart TB evap[Evaporator Types] --> constr[Based on Construction] evap --> flow[Based on Refrigerant Flow] evap --> secFluid[Based on Secondary Fluid] constr --> bare[Bare Tube] constr --> plate[Plate Surface] constr --> finned[Finned Tube] constr --> shell[Shell and Tube] flow --> dx[Dry Expansion DX] flow --> flooded[Flooded] secFluid --> air[Air Cooling Coil] secFluid --> water[Water Chilling] secFluid --> brine[Brine Cooling] ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pattern:** Part A questions test **Remember** and **Understand** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy. Answers must be **concise (3–4 lines)** with diagrams wherever possible. ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **With a neat sketch, explain the working of a Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TEV).** **Model Answer:** A TEV is an automatic expansion device that maintains a **constant superheat** at the evaporator outlet. It consists of a **sensing bulb** (clamped at evaporator outlet), a **diaphragm**, a **spring**, and a **needle-and-seat** assembly. When evaporator load increases, superheat decreases, bulb pressure drops, diaphragm moves up, opening the valve to admit more refrigerant — and vice versa. It is always used with **dry-expansion evaporators** for variable-load systems. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Compare capillary tube and thermostatic expansion valve on the basis of (i) moving parts, (ii) load handling, and (iii) typical applications.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Capillary Tube | TEV | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Moving parts | None | Yes (diaphragm, pin) | | Load handling | Poor (fixed flow) | Excellent (auto-modulating) | | Applications | Domestic fridges, window ACs | Central AC, cold storage, chillers | | Cost | Very low | Moderate–High | | Maintenance | Replace if choked | Periodic calibration | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Always draw a labelled schematic** — missing diagrams = **2–3 marks lost**. > 2. **Show the SFEE assumption list explicitly** for any energy-balance question. > 3. **Convert TR to kW correctly**: $1 \ \text{TR} = 3.517 \ \text{kW}$. Wrong conversion = cascading mark loss. > 4. **For capillary tube problems**, do not assume ideal gas — refrigerant is a real fluid; use property tables. > 5. **For TEV questions**, distinguish clearly between **superheat control** and **pressure control** — examiners check this explicitly. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Module 2 Choice 1** > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **vapour compression refrigeration system** and clearly indicate the locations of the **expansion device** and **evaporator**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A refrigeration system uses R-134a. Saturated vapour enters the compressor at $-5 \ ^\circ\text{C}$. The condenser pressure corresponds to a saturation temperature of $40 \ ^\circ\text{C}$. The refrigerant leaves the condenser as saturated liquid. Calculate: **(i)** the quality at the evaporator inlet, **(ii)** the refrigeration effect, and **(iii)** the COP assuming isentropic compression. **(7 Marks)** #### **Model Solution (a):** The vapour compression refrigeration cycle consists of **four main components** in a closed loop: 1. **Compressor** — raises refrigerant pressure and temperature. 2. **Condenser** — rejects heat $Q_c$ to the surroundings; refrigerant condenses from vapour to liquid. 3. **Expansion Device** — drops pressure isenthalpically from condenser pressure to evaporator pressure; refrigerant becomes low-quality two-phase mixture. 4. **Evaporator** — absorbs heat $Q_e$ from the refrigerated space; refrigerant evaporates from liquid to vapour. The **expansion device** is located between the condenser outlet and the evaporator inlet. The **evaporator** is located between the expansion device outlet and the compressor inlet. Both operate on the **low-pressure side** of the system. #### **Model Solution (b):** **Given:** - $T_e = -5 \ ^\circ\text{C}$ → $P_e \approx 2.45 \ \text{bar}$, $h_1 = h_g \approx 397.9 \ \text{kJ/kg}$ - $T_c = 40 \ ^\circ\text{C}$ → $P_c \approx 10.16 \ \text{bar}$, $h_3 = h_f \approx 256.5 \ \text{kJ/kg}$ **(i) Quality at evaporator inlet:** By throttling: $h_4 = h_3 = 256.5 \ \text{kJ/kg}$. From R-134a tables at $-5 \ ^\circ\text{C}$: $h_{f4} \approx 167.4 \ \text{kJ/kg}$, $h_{fg4} \approx 230.5 \ \text{kJ/kg}$. $$ x_4 = \dfrac{h_4 - h_{f4}}{h_{fg4}} = \dfrac{256.5 - 167.4}{230.5} = \dfrac{89.1}{230.5} \approx 0.387 $$ **(ii) Refrigeration effect:** $$ RE = h_1 - h_4 = 397.9 - 256.5 = 141.4 \ \text{kJ/kg} $$ **(iii) COP:** For isentropic compression, find $h_2$ at $P_c = 10.16 \ \text{bar}$ and $s_2 = s_1$. From R-134a superheated tables: $h_2 \approx 425.0 \ \text{kJ/kg}$. $$ w_c = h_2 - h_1 = 425.0 - 397.9 = 27.1 \ \text{kJ/kg} $$ $$ \text{COP} = \dfrac{RE}{w_c} = \dfrac{141.4}{27.1} \approx 5.22 $$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Module 2 Choice 2** > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **Capillary Tube** as an expansion device. Derive the expression relating its **length** to the pressure drop and refrigerant flow rate. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** What is a **flooded evaporator**? Compare it with a **dry expansion evaporator**. An evaporator uses R-12 with refrigerant entering at $-15 \ ^\circ\text{C}$ (quality 0.25) and leaving as saturated vapour. If the cooling load is $5 \ \text{TR}$, determine the **mass flow rate** of refrigerant. **(7 Marks)** #### **Model Solution (a):** **Construction:** A capillary tube is a long, narrow copper tube of internal diameter typically **0.5–2.5 mm** and length **1.5–6 m**, with no moving parts. **Working:** High-pressure liquid refrigerant from the condenser enters the tube. As it flows through the narrow bore, **continuous frictional pressure drop** occurs along the length. At the exit, the refrigerant pressure equals the evaporator pressure, and its quality is typically 0.15–0.25. **Derivation:** For incompressible steady flow with friction, applying the mechanical energy balance: $$ \Delta P = \dfrac{32 f L \dot{m}^2}{\rho \pi^2 D^5} $$ Setting $\Delta P = P_c - P_e$ and solving for $L$: $$ L = \dfrac{(P_c - P_e) \rho \pi^2 D^5}{32 f \dot{m}^2} $$ This is the basic capillary tube length equation. #### **Model Solution (b):** **Flooded Evaporator:** A flooded evaporator has its tubes **completely submerged in liquid refrigerant** at all times. Evaporation occurs as bubbles rising through the liquid, giving excellent heat transfer but requiring a **float valve** or **low-pressure receiver** to maintain liquid level. **Comparison:** | Feature | Flooded | Dry Expansion (DX) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Liquid inventory | High | Low | | Heat transfer coefficient | Higher | Moderate | | Expansion device | Float valve | TEV / Capillary | | Risk of liquid slugging | Low (separated in receiver) | High (needs superheat) | | Charge required | Large | Small | **Numerical Calculation:** Given: $T_e = -15 \ ^\circ\text{C}$, $x_4 = 0.25$, $Q_e = 5 \ \text{TR} = 5 \times 3.517 = 17.585 \ \text{kW}$. From R-12 tables at $-15 \ ^\circ\text{C}$: $h_{f4} \approx 31.6 \ \text{kJ/kg}$, $h_{fg4} \approx 162.7 \ \text{kJ/kg}$. $$ h_4 = h_{f4} + x_4 \cdot h_{fg4} = 31.6 + 0.25 \times 162.7 = 31.6 + 40.675 = 72.275 \ \text{kJ/kg} $$ $h_1 = h_g = 31.6 + 162.7 = 194.3 \ \text{kJ/kg}$. $$ RE = h_1 - h_4 = 194.3 - 72.275 = 122.025 \ \text{kJ/kg} $$ $$ \dot{m} = \dfrac{Q_e}{RE} = \dfrac{17.585}{122.025} \approx 0.1441 \ \text{kg/s} = 518.9 \ \text{kg/h} $$ --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam!** ### **Expansion Devices — Key Takeaways** - **Capillary tube:** Fixed orifice, no moving parts, used for **small constant loads** (domestic fridges). - **TEV:** Maintains **constant superheat**, used for **variable loads** (HVAC, chillers). - **Float valve:** Maintains **constant liquid level** in flooded evaporators (industrial ammonia plants). - **AXV:** Maintains **constant evaporator pressure** (older systems). - **EEV:** Modern, electronically controlled, programmable — used in VRF and precision AC. - **Throttling is always isenthalpic:** $h_3 = h_4$. ### **Evaporators — Key Takeaways** - **Dry Expansion (DX):** Outlet superheated; paired with **TEV/capillary**. - **Flooded:** Tubes full of liquid; paired with **float valve**. - **Bare-tube** vs **finned-tube:** Finned increases air-side heat transfer area. - **Plate-type** used in domestic refrigerators. - **Shell-and-tube** used for chilled-water production. - **LMTD method** is the standard for sizing evaporators. - Defrosting is essential for **air-cooling evaporators below $0 \ ^\circ\text{C}$**. ### **Numerical Must-Knows** - $1 \ \text{TR} = 3.517 \ \text{kW} = 211 \ \text{kJ/min}$ - $RE = h_1 - h_4$ (kJ/kg) - $\dot{m} = Q_e / RE$ (kg/s) - $\text{COP} = RE / w_c = RE / (h_2 - h_1)$ - Quality after throttling: $x_4 = (h_4 - h_{f4}) / h_{fg4}$ - Capillary length: $L = (P_c - P_e) \rho \pi^2 D^5 / (32 f \dot{m}^2)$ ### **Common Exam Pitfalls (Re-stated for Emphasis)** - ❌ Forgetting to convert TR to kW. - ❌ Treating throttling as isentropic instead of isenthalpic. - ❌ Confusing TEV (superheat control) with AXV (pressure control). - ❌ Using bare-tube formula in finned-coil problem. - ❌ Missing the SFEE assumption list in derivations.

topics.title: Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System - Simple and Practical
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System - Simple and Practical

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS)** is a heat-operated refrigeration cycle in which the mechanical work input (as in a Vapour Compression Refrigeration System) is replaced by a thermal energy input supplied to the generator. The system uses a **two-fluid pair** — a *refrigerant* (low boiling point) and an *absorbent* (high boiling point) — where the refrigerant vapour is absorbed by the absorbent at low pressure and liberated at high pressure through the application of heat.

The most commonly used working fluid pairs are:

| Refrigerant | Absorbent | Typical Application |
|-------------|-----------|---------------------|
| **Ammonia (NH₃)** | **Water (H₂O)** | Industrial refrigeration (below 0 °C) |
| **Water (H₂O)** | **Lithium Bromide (LiBr)** | Air conditioning (above 0 °C) |
| **Water (H₂O)** | **Lithium Chloride (LiCl)** | Specialized AC systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The *simple* and *practical* Vapour Absorption Systems are mandatory in Module 2. Students must clearly distinguish the **five basic components** of the simple system from the **seven/eight components** used in the practical (modified) system.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation

Imagine a sponge (the absorber) soaking up spilled water (the refrigerant vapour). The wet sponge is then squeezed and heated, releasing the water as steam. This steam is cooled (condensed) and reused.

- **No mechanical compressor** is needed — only a small liquid pump and lots of heat.
- The compressor is essentially replaced by an **Absorber + Pump + Generator** combination.
- Energy input is **heat** (Q_G) — which can be obtained from solar energy, waste heat, or even a gas flame.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In a VCR, the *quality* of energy matters (work). In a VARS, the *quantity* of energy matters (heat). This is why VARS can run on low-grade thermal energy.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Standard refrigeration effect range:** 1 kW to 1500 kW (industrial scale)
- **COP of practical VARS:** 0.3 to 0.7 (much lower than VCR)
- **Generator temperatures:** 80 °C to 200 °C (heat source)
- **Evaporator temperatures:** −30 °C to 10 °C
- **Coefficient of Performance (COP):** Dimensionless ratio of refrigeration effect to energy input.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Main Components of a Simple VARS

1. **Evaporator** — Where the refrigerant absorbs heat (Q_E) and produces the cooling effect.
2. **Absorber** — The refrigerant vapour is absorbed by the weak solution, forming a strong solution.
3. **Pump** — Increases the pressure of the strong solution from evaporator pressure to condenser pressure. The work done is **W_p**.
4. **Generator (Boiler)** — Heat is supplied (Q_G) to release refrigerant vapour from the strong solution.
5. **Condenser** — High-pressure refrigerant vapour is condensed by rejecting heat (Q_C) to the surroundings.
6. **Expansion Valve** — Reduces the pressure of the liquid refrigerant to evaporator pressure (throttling process).

> [!NOTE]
> The **expansion valve** is technically the sixth component, but in many diagrams, the throttling between condenser and evaporator is implicit.

## 2.2 Working Sequence (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Refrigerant evaporation:** Liquid refrigerant evaporates in the **evaporator** at low pressure, absorbing heat from the cold space.
2. **Absorption:** The low-pressure refrigerant vapour enters the **absorber**, where it is absorbed by the *weak* (refrigerant-lean) solution to form a *strong* (refrigerant-rich) solution.
3. **Pumping:** The strong solution is pumped by the **pump** from low to high pressure. Work input is **W_p**.
4. **Generation:** The high-pressure strong solution is heated in the **generator** by an external heat source (Q_G). Refrigerant vapour is driven off, leaving behind a *weak* solution.
5. **Condensation:** The high-pressure refrigerant vapour enters the **condenser**, where it rejects heat (Q_C) and condenses to a liquid.
6. **Expansion:** The liquid refrigerant passes through the **expansion valve**, dropping to evaporator pressure — completing the cycle.

## 2.3 Concentration Definitions (Critical for Numerical Problems)

Let:
- $m_a$ = mass flow rate of **pure refrigerant** (ammonia) circulated per second
- $m_s$ = mass flow rate of **absorbent** (water) circulated per second
- $x_a$ = mass fraction of refrigerant in the **strong solution** (rich)
- $x_b$ = mass fraction of refrigerant in the **weak solution** (lean)
- $m_1$ = mass flow rate of **strong solution** = $m_a + m_s$
- $m_2$ = mass flow rate of **weak solution** = $m_a + m_s$ (same as $m_1$ at steady state)

Then the mass balance gives:

$$\frac{m_a}{m_s} = \frac{x_b}{1 - x_a} = \frac{x_a - x_b}{x_a(1 - x_a)}$$

The **Circulation Ratio (CR)** is defined as:

$$CR = \frac{m_1}{m_a} = \frac{1}{x_a - x_b}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | KTU Relevance |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------------|
| 1 | Refrigeration Effect (RE) | $RE = m_a \cdot (h_1 - h_4)$ | kW | Used in every problem |
| 2 | Work of Pump | $W_p = m_1 \cdot (h_6 - h_5)$ | kW | Small but compulsory |
| 3 | Heat Input at Generator | $Q_G = m_a \cdot h_3 + m_s \cdot h_4 - (m_a + m_s) \cdot h_6$ | kW | 14-mark derivation |
| 4 | Heat Rejected at Absorber | $Q_A = (m_a + m_s) \cdot h_5 - m_a \cdot h_1 - m_s \cdot h_2$ | kW | Energy balance |
| 5 | Heat Rejected at Condenser | $Q_C = m_a \cdot (h_3 - h_4)$ | kW | Direct sub-part |
| 6 | Actual COP | $COP_{actual} = \dfrac{Q_E}{Q_G + W_p}$ | Dimensionless | Core definition |
| 7 | Ideal COP (max) | $COP_{max} = \dfrac{T_E (T_G - T_A)}{T_G (T_A - T_E)}$ | Dimensionless | Compare with Carnot |
| 8 | Carnot COP | $COP_{carnot} = \dfrac{T_E}{T_A - T_E}$ | Dimensionless | Upper limit reference |
| 9 | Circulation Ratio | $CR = \dfrac{1}{x_a - x_b}$ | Dimensionless | Mass balance problems |
| 10 | NH₃ Mass Flow Rate | $m_a = \dfrac{Q_E}{h_1 - h_4}$ | kg/s | 7-mark sub-part |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always express temperatures in **Kelvin (K)** when applying the COP formulas. Conversion: $K = °C + 273.15$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Solar refrigeration** — VARS can be powered by flat-plate solar collectors, making it ideal for remote areas without electricity.
- **Waste heat recovery** — Industrial waste heat from exhaust gases or process streams can drive the generator.
- **Large-capacity industrial chilling** — Breweries, food processing, and chemical plants use NH₃-H₂O VARS for capacities above 100 kW.
- **Air conditioning of large buildings** — LiBr-H₂O systems dominate the commercial AC sector because water (refrigerant) is non-toxic.
- **Domestic refrigerators** — Compact diffusion-absorption units (no moving parts) operate on gas or electric heat.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: COP of an Ideal Vapour Absorption System

**Given:** 
- Heat is supplied at temperature $T_G$ to the generator.
- Heat is rejected at temperature $T_A$ from the absorber.
- Refrigeration effect is achieved at $T_E$.
- Heat is rejected at temperature $T_C$ from the condenser.

**Assumption:** Reversible operation throughout the entire cycle.

**Step 1 — Write the overall energy balance of the system.**

Since the system operates in a cycle, the net change in internal energy is zero. Therefore, by the **First Law of Thermodynamics**:

$$Q_{in} + W_{in} = Q_{out} + RE_{out}$$

For an ideal VARS, the only work input is the pump work, and in the ideal (reversible) case:

$$Q_G = Q_A + Q_C - Q_E$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Second Law of Thermodynamics.**

For a reversible cyclic process, the entropy change of the universe is zero. Considering all heat transfers and absolute temperatures:

$$\frac{Q_G}{T_G} = \frac{Q_A}{T_A} + \frac{Q_C}{T_C} - \frac{Q_E}{T_E}$$

**Step 3 — Express $Q_C$ in terms of $Q_E$.**

From the refrigerant loop alone (condenser + evaporator), the heat rejected at the condenser equals the heat absorbed at the evaporator plus the work done by the refrigerant in the pump (ideal case, pump work is negligible). So:

$$Q_C = Q_E$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $Q_C = Q_E$ into the entropy equation.**

$$\frac{Q_G}{T_G} = \frac{Q_A}{T_A} + \frac{Q_E}{T_C} - \frac{Q_E}{T_E}$$

**Step 5 — From the energy balance, $Q_A = Q_G - Q_C + Q_E = Q_G - Q_E + Q_E = Q_G$ (for the ideal case where $Q_C = Q_E$).**

Wait — re-check: From Step 1, $Q_G + Q_E = Q_A + Q_C$ (with no work input in the ideal reversible case).

Since $Q_C = Q_E$, this gives:

$$Q_A = Q_G$$

Substitute into Step 4:

$$\frac{Q_G}{T_G} = \frac{Q_G}{T_A} + \frac{Q_E}{T_C} - \frac{Q_E}{T_E}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{Q_E}{T_E} - \frac{Q_E}{T_C} = \frac{Q_G}{T_A} - \frac{Q_G}{T_G}$$

$$Q_E \left( \frac{1}{T_E} - \frac{1}{T_C} \right) = Q_G \left( \frac{1}{T_A} - \frac{1}{T_G} \right)$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the COP.**

$$COP_{ideal} = \frac{Q_E}{Q_G} = \frac{ \dfrac{1}{T_A} - \dfrac{1}{T_G} }{ \dfrac{1}{T_E} - \dfrac{1}{T_C} } = \frac{T_E (T_G - T_A)}{T_G (T_C - T_E)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The maximum theoretical COP formula has two common forms depending on the textbook. KTU's preferred form (when $T_A = T_C$) is:
> $$COP_{max} = \frac{T_E (T_G - T_A)}{T_G (T_A - T_E)}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: State Point Analysis for NH₃-H₂O VARS

Let the four state points in the refrigerant loop be:
- **State 1:** Saturated vapour leaving the evaporator
- **State 2:** Saturated liquid leaving the condenser
- **State 3:** After throttling (state 4) — typically labeled as state 4 entering evaporator
- **State 4 (or 3):** Liquid-vapour mixture entering evaporator

For the solution loop:
- **State 5:** Strong solution leaving absorber (low pressure)
- **State 6:** Strong solution leaving pump (high pressure)
- **State 7:** Weak solution leaving generator (high pressure)
- **State 8:** Weak solution after expansion (low pressure) — enters absorber

**Energy balance at the Absorber:**

$$Q_A = m_1 h_5 - m_a h_1 - m_s h_8$$

**Energy balance at the Generator:**

$$Q_G = m_a h_1' + m_s h_7 - m_1 h_6$$

where $h_1'$ is the enthalpy of refrigerant vapour leaving the generator.

**Energy balance at the Pump:**

$$W_p = m_1 (h_6 - h_5) = \dfrac{m_1 \cdot v_5 \cdot (P_2 - P_1)}{\eta_p}$$

**Refrigeration Effect:**

$$RE = m_a (h_1 - h_4)$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** In an NH₃-H₂O VARS, the following data is given:
- Enthalpy of refrigerant vapour leaving evaporator: $h_1 = 1450$ kJ/kg
- Enthalpy of liquid refrigerant leaving condenser: $h_2 = 360$ kJ/kg
- Enthalpy of refrigerant vapour leaving generator: $h_3 = 1480$ kJ/kg
- Enthalpy of weak solution leaving generator: $h_4 = 200$ kJ/kg
- Enthalpy of strong solution leaving absorber: $h_5 = 220$ kJ/kg
- Strong solution concentration: $x_a = 0.40$
- Weak solution concentration: $x_b = 0.20$

**Find:** (a) Circulation ratio, (b) COP of the system, (c) Heat input at generator per kg of refrigerant.

### Solution:

**(a) Circulation Ratio:**

$$CR = \frac{1}{x_a - x_b} = \frac{1}{0.40 - 0.20} = \frac{1}{0.20} = 5.0$$

**(b) Mass of absorbent per kg of refrigerant:**

$$m_s = m_a \cdot \frac{x_a - x_b}{x_b \cdot 1} = 1 \cdot \frac{0.20}{0.20} = 1.0 \text{ kg/s}$$

Wait — applying the correct relation:

$$\frac{m_a}{m_s} = \frac{x_b}{1 - x_a} = \frac{0.20}{1 - 0.40} = \frac{0.20}{0.60} = 0.333$$

Therefore, for $m_a = 1$ kg/s: $m_s = 3.0$ kg/s.

**(c) Heat input at generator per kg of refrigerant:**

$$Q_G = m_a h_3 + m_s h_4 - (m_a + m_s) h_6$$

Assuming $h_6 \approx h_5 = 220$ kJ/kg (pump work is small for liquid):

$$Q_G = (1)(1480) + (3.0)(200) - (1 + 3.0)(220)$$

$$Q_G = 1480 + 600 - 880 = 1200 \text{ kJ per kg of refrigerant}$$

**(d) Refrigeration Effect per kg of refrigerant:**

$$RE = h_1 - h_4 \text{ (state 4 is throttled from state 2)}$$

Since throttling is isenthalpic: $h_4 = h_2 = 360$ kJ/kg.

$$RE = 1450 - 360 = 1090 \text{ kJ per kg of refrigerant}$$

**(e) COP:**

$$COP = \frac{RE}{Q_G} = \frac{1090}{1200} = 0.908$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **maximum theoretical COP** when pump work is neglected. In practice, the COP is 0.5 to 0.7 due to irreversibilities.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Simple Vapour Absorption System (5-Component Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E["EVAPORATOR\nLow P, Low T\nRefrigerant evaporates\nAbsorbs Q_E"]
    A["ABSORBER\nLow P, Low T\nNH3 absorbed by weak solution\nStrong solution forms"]
    P["PUMP\nLow to High P\nWork input W_p"]
    G["GENERATOR\nHigh P, High T\nHeat input Q_G\nWeak solution returns"]
    C["CONDENSER\nHigh P, High T\nRefrigerant condenses\nRejects Q_C"]
    V["EXPANSION VALVE\nHigh to Low P\nThrottling process"]

    E -->|Refrigerant vapour h1| A
    A -->|Strong solution h5| P
    P -->|High P strong solution h6| G
    G -->|Refrigerant vapour h3| C
    C -->|Liquid refrigerant h2| V
    V -->|Two-phase mixture h4| E
    G -->|Weak solution h7| A

    style E fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style A fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#cc3300,color:#000
    style P fill:#d9e6d2,stroke:#336600,color:#000
    style G fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    style C fill:#e6ccff,stroke:#330099,color:#000
    style V fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The refrigerant loop (E → A → P → G → C → V → E) is the main cycle. The weak solution is returned from the Generator (G) back to the Absorber (A) at low pressure to absorb more refrigerant.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Practical Vapour Absorption System (With Modifications)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    E1["EVAPORATOR\nQ_E cooling effect"]
    A1["ABSORBER\nNH3 absorbed"]
    P1["PUMP\nWork W_p"]
    G1["GENERATOR\nHeat Q_G input"]
    R1["RECTIFIER\nRemoves water vapour"]
    S1["ANALYZER\nImproves separation"]
    RG["REGENERATOR\nHeat exchange\nweak vs strong solution"]
    C1["CONDENSER\nRejects Q_C"]
    V1["EXPANSION VALVE\nThrottling"]
    PR1["PRESSURE\nEQUALISER"]

    E1 -->|Refrigerant vapour| A1
    A1 -->|Strong solution cold| RG
    RG -->|Strong solution heated| P1
    P1 -->|High P strong solution| G1
    G1 -->|NH3 + water vapour| S1
    S1 -->|Refrigerant + traces| R1
    R1 -->|Pure NH3 vapour| C1
    C1 -->|Liquid NH3| V1
    V1 -->|Two-phase mixture| E1
    G1 -->|Hot weak solution| RG
    RG -->|Cooled weak solution| PR1
    PR1 -->|Low P weak solution| A1

    style E1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style A1 fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#cc3300,color:#000
    style P1 fill:#d9e6d2,stroke:#336600,color:#000
    style G1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    style R1 fill:#ffe599,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    style S1 fill:#d5a6bd,stroke:#660033,color:#000
    style RG fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#38761d,color:#000
    style C1 fill:#e6ccff,stroke:#330099,color:#000
    style V1 fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    style PR1 fill:#cccccc,stroke:#333333,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Comparative Functional Architecture: Simple vs Practical

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SIMPLE["SIMPLE VARS - 5 Components"]
        S1["Evaporator"]
        S2["Absorber"]
        S3["Pump"]
        S4["Generator"]
        S5["Condenser + Expansion Valve"]
    end

    subgraph PRACTICAL["PRACTICAL VARS - 8+ Components"]
        P1["Evaporator"]
        P2["Absorber"]
        P3["Pump"]
        P4["Generator"]
        P5["Analyzer"]
        P6["Rectifier"]
        P7["Regenerator"]
        P8["Condenser + Expansion Valve"]
    end

    S1 -.Simple.-> P1
    S5 -.Modified.-> P8

    style S1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style S2 fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#cc3300
    style S3 fill:#d9e6d2,stroke:#336600
    style S4 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc6600
    style S5 fill:#e6ccff,stroke:#330099
    style P1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style P2 fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#cc3300
    style P3 fill:#d9e6d2,stroke:#336600
    style P4 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc6600
    style P5 fill:#d5a6bd,stroke:#660033
    style P6 fill:#ffe599,stroke:#996600
    style P7 fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#38761d
    style P8 fill:#e6ccff,stroke:#330099
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The *practical* VARS includes **Analyzer, Rectifier, and Regenerator** which are not present in the *simple* VARS. Each of these serves a specific function:
> - **Analyzer + Rectifier:** Removes water vapour that escapes with ammonia from the generator, ensuring only pure ammonia enters the condenser.
> - **Regenerator (Heat Exchanger):** Recovers heat from the hot weak solution leaving the generator to preheat the strong solution entering the generator — improving COP.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q: Differentiate between Vapour Compression and Vapour Absorption Refrigeration Systems.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | VCRS (Vapour Compression) | VARS (Vapour Absorption) |
|-----------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| Energy input | Mechanical work (compressor) | Heat energy (generator) |
| Compressor | Required | Not required |
| Pump | Small liquid pump | Larger solution pump |
| Working fluid | Single refrigerant | Refrigerant + absorbent pair |
| COP | 2.5 to 5.0 | 0.3 to 0.7 |
| Noise level | High (compressor) | Very low (almost silent) |
| Maintenance | Frequent | Minimal |
| Best suited for | Domestic, small-scale | Industrial, large-scale, remote |

> [3 marks awarded: 1 mark for energy input difference, 1 mark for COP and components, 1 mark for application]

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Q: List the components of a Simple Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System and state the function of the generator.**

**Model Answer:**

The simple VARS consists of five main components:
1. **Evaporator** — produces cooling effect by absorbing heat
2. **Absorber** — absorbs refrigerant vapour into the weak solution
3. **Pump** — raises the pressure of the strong solution
4. **Generator** — separates refrigerant vapour from the strong solution by supplying heat
5. **Condenser** — condenses refrigerant vapour by rejecting heat
6. **Expansion Valve** — reduces pressure of liquid refrigerant

**Function of the Generator:** The generator (also called the *boiler*) receives high-pressure strong (refrigerant-rich) solution from the pump and supplies external heat energy ($Q_G$) to vaporize the refrigerant. The refrigerant vapour leaves the generator at high pressure to enter the condenser, while the weak (refrigerant-lean) solution returns to the absorber.

> [3 marks: 1 mark for listing components, 2 marks for the function of generator with $Q_G$ explanation]

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Main Question]**

**A Simple Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System uses ammonia-water as the working fluid. The following data is given:**

- Enthalpy of refrigerant vapour leaving the evaporator, $h_1 = 1465$ kJ/kg
- Enthalpy of liquid refrigerant leaving the condenser, $h_2 = 360$ kJ/kg
- Enthalpy of refrigerant vapour leaving the generator, $h_3 = 1485$ kJ/kg
- Enthalpy of weak solution leaving the generator, $h_4 = 220$ kJ/kg
- Enthalpy of strong solution leaving the absorber, $h_5 = 240$ kJ/kg
- Concentration of strong solution, $x_a = 0.35$
- Concentration of weak solution, $x_b = 0.15$
- Refrigeration effect required: $Q_E = 10$ kW

**Calculate:**
- (a) The mass flow rate of ammonia and the mass flow rate of absorbent (7 marks)
- (b) The COP of the system and the heat input rate at the generator (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Determine the mass flow rate of ammonia.**

The refrigeration effect is given by:

$$Q_E = m_a \cdot (h_1 - h_4)$$

where $h_4$ is the enthalpy of refrigerant entering the evaporator (after throttling). Since throttling is an isenthalpic process:

$$h_4 = h_2 = 360 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Therefore:

$$m_a = \frac{Q_E}{h_1 - h_4} = \frac{10 \times 3600 \text{ kJ/h}}{1465 - 360} = \frac{36000}{1105} = 32.58 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 2: Determine the mass flow rate of absorbent water.**

From the mass balance on ammonia:

$$m_a \cdot x_a = m_a \cdot x_b + m_s \cdot 0$$

Wait — the absorbent (water) does not contain any refrigerant in pure form. The relation is:

$$\frac{m_a}{m_s} = \frac{x_b}{1 - x_a}$$

Therefore:

$$m_s = m_a \cdot \frac{1 - x_a}{x_b} = 32.58 \cdot \frac{1 - 0.35}{0.15} = 32.58 \cdot \frac{0.65}{0.15} = 32.58 \cdot 4.333$$

$$m_s = 141.2 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 3: Circulation ratio.**

$$CR = \frac{1}{x_a - x_b} = \frac{1}{0.35 - 0.15} = \frac{1}{0.20} = 5.0$$

**Step 4: Verify the strong solution mass flow rate.**

$$m_{strong} = m_a + m_s = 32.58 + 141.2 = 173.78 \text{ kg/h}$$

> [7 marks: 2 marks for $m_a$, 3 marks for $m_s$ with mass balance, 1 mark for CR, 1 mark for verification]

---

#### Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Calculate the heat input rate at the generator.**

$$Q_G = m_a \cdot h_3 + m_s \cdot h_4 - (m_a + m_s) \cdot h_5$$

(Note: $h_5$ is used as the strong solution enthalpy entering the generator. Pump work is neglected here as $h_6 \approx h_5$.)

$$Q_G = (32.58)(1485) + (141.2)(220) - (173.78)(240) \text{ kJ/h}$$

$$Q_G = 48381.3 + 31064 - 41707.2 = 37738.1 \text{ kJ/h}$$

$$Q_G = \frac{37738.1}{3600} = 10.48 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the COP.**

$$COP = \frac{Q_E}{Q_G + W_p}$$

Neglecting pump work $W_p$:

$$COP = \frac{10}{10.48} = 0.954$$

**Step 3: Calculate the pump work for completeness (if asked in sub-part).**

Assuming density of strong solution $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ and pressure rise $\Delta P = 1.5$ MPa:

$$W_p = \frac{m_{strong} \cdot v \cdot \Delta P}{\eta_p} = \frac{(173.78/3600) \cdot (1/1000) \cdot 1.5 \times 10^6}{0.7}$$

$$W_p = \frac{0.04827 \cdot 0.001 \cdot 1.5 \times 10^6}{0.7} = \frac{72.4}{0.7} = 103.4 \text{ W} \approx 0.1 \text{ kW}$$

Including pump work:

$$COP = \frac{10}{10.48 + 0.1} = \frac{10}{10.58} = 0.945$$

> [7 marks: 3 marks for $Q_G$ calculation, 2 marks for COP, 2 marks for optional pump work]

---

### Question B (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Alternative Choice]**

**Explain in detail the working of a Practical Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System with a neat block diagram. Compare its performance with a Simple VARS.**

---

#### Solution Outline:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks: Working of Practical VARS**

The practical VARS incorporates additional components to overcome the limitations of the simple system:

1. **Analyser and Rectifier:** When ammonia is vaporized in the generator, some water vapour escapes along with it. The analyser and rectifier separate this water vapour, allowing only pure ammonia to enter the condenser. The water is returned to the generator.

2. **Regenerator (Heat Exchanger):** The hot weak solution leaving the generator is passed through the regenerator, where it transfers heat to the cold strong solution entering the generator. This preheating reduces the heat input required at the generator, significantly improving the COP.

3. **Working Sequence:** Refrigerant evaporates in the **evaporator** → absorbed in the **absorber** → pumped to high pressure by the **solution pump** → preheated in the **regenerator** → vaporized in the **generator** (Q_G) → purified in the **analyser and rectifier** → condensed in the **condenser** → throttled in the **expansion valve** → enters the evaporator. The hot weak solution leaving the generator passes through the **regenerator** (transferring heat) → is throttled to absorber pressure → enters the **absorber** to absorb more ammonia.

> [7 marks: 2 marks for analyser+rectifier, 2 marks for regenerator function, 3 marks for complete working sequence]

**Part (b) — 7 Marks: Comparison with Simple VARS**

| Parameter | Simple VARS | Practical VARS |
|-----------|-------------|----------------|
| Number of components | 5–6 | 8–10 |
| COP | Lower (no heat recovery) | Higher (15–30% improvement) |
| Refrigerant purity | Reduced (water contamination) | High (purified by rectifier) |
| Generator heat input | High | Reduced (preheating by regenerator) |
| Initial cost | Low | Higher |
| Operating cost | High (more heat needed) | Lower |
| Complexity | Simple | Complex |
| Use | Educational demonstrations | Industrial applications |

> [7 marks: 5 marks for comparison table with explanation, 2 marks for conclusion on real-world application]

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Forgetting to convert $Q_E$ from kW to kJ/h when working with kJ/kg enthalpy values. *Penalty: 2 marks.*
> 2. **Throttling Assumption:** Forgetting that throttling is isenthalpic ($h_4 = h_2$). Many students use $h_3$ by mistake. *Penalty: 1–2 marks.*
> 3. **Mass Balance Confusion:** Mixing up strong ($x_a$) and weak ($x_b$) solution concentrations. *Penalty: 1 mark.*
> 4. **Pump Work Ignored:** In simple VARS analysis, the pump work is often neglected, but examiners expect a *comment* on its inclusion. *Penalty: 1 mark.*
> 5. **Sketch Missing:** A block diagram is **compulsory** for a 14-mark question. Drawing a labelled block diagram earns easy 2–3 marks. *Penalty: Up to 3 marks.*
> 6. **Temperature in Kelvin:** $T_G$, $T_A$, $T_E$, $T_C$ must all be in Kelvin for the COP max formula. *Penalty: 1 mark.*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize These 10 Points:**

- ✅ A VARS is **heat-driven**, not work-driven. The compressor is replaced by an **absorber + pump + generator** combination.
- ✅ The **two fluid pairs** are **NH₃-H₂O** (industrial, below 0 °C) and **LiBr-H₂O** (AC, above 0 °C). *Water cannot be the refrigerant in LiBr systems used below 0 °C (it freezes).*
- ✅ **Five main components of simple VARS:** Evaporator, Absorber, Pump, Generator, Condenser (+ expansion valve).
- ✅ **Practical VARS adds:** **Analyser, Rectifier, and Regenerator** — these improve purity and COP.
- ✅ **Circulation Ratio:** $CR = \dfrac{1}{x_a - x_b}$ — *always greater than 1.*
- ✅ **Maximum COP:** $COP_{max} = \dfrac{T_E (T_G - T_A)}{T_G (T_A - T_E)}$ — *use Kelvin only.*
- ✅ **Practical COP range:** 0.3 to 0.7 (lower than VCR because heat is a lower-quality energy form than work).
- ✅ **Throttling is isenthalpic** — $h_4 = h_2$ between condenser exit and evaporator entry.
- ✅ **Mass flow rate of ammonia:** $m_a = \dfrac{Q_E}{h_1 - h_4}$ — *this is the starting point of every numerical.*
- ✅ **Generator heat input formula:** $Q_G = m_a h_3 + m_s h_4 - m_{strong} \cdot h_5$ — *memorize this in vector form.*
- ✅ **COP = $Q_E / (Q_G + W_p)$** — *the denominator includes BOTH the heat input and the pump work.*

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip from KTU 2024 Pattern:** In a 14-mark question, always draw a **labelled block diagram** (2 marks), state the **working sequence** in 4–5 lines (3 marks), and then solve the **numerical part** with proper units (9 marks). Skipping the diagram is the #1 reason for losing easy marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calculations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Calculations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> **Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS) Calculations** refer to the systematic quantitative analysis of a vapour compression refrigeration cycle using thermodynamic property values (enthalpy, entropy, specific volume) at each state point to determine the system's **Refrigerating Effect (RE)**, **Compressor Work Input (W)**, **Heat Rejection Rate (Qc)**, **Coefficient of Performance (COP)**, **Mass Flow Rate of Refrigerant (ṁ)**, **Compressor Displacement Volume (VD)**, and **Power Requirements (IHP/BHP)**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the topic of "Calculations" under Module 2 encompasses the numerical evaluation of the **Simple Vapour Compression Cycle (SVCC)** on the **Pressure-Enthalpy (p-h)** and **Temperature-Entropy (T-s)** diagrams, including the analytical effects of:
- **Suction Superheating** (SS)
- **Liquid Subcooling** (SC)
- **Volumetric Efficiency** (ηv)
- **Isentropic Efficiency** of the compressor (ηisen)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Water Bucket Relay":**
> Imagine a team of workers passing water buckets up a staircase to put out a small fire. The "refrigerating effect" is the water that actually reaches the fire (useful cooling). The "work" is the energy the workers spend carrying buckets uphill (energy input). The "heat rejected" is the sweat and heat they shed on the way up. **COP is simply: how much water reaches the fire per bucket of effort?**
>
> A high COP means the cycle is an efficient "relay team" — it delivers a lot of cooling for little energy input, just like a well-coordinated bucket brigade.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics in VCRS Calculations

- **Refrigerating Effect (RE)** is measured in **kJ/kg** or **TR (Tonne of Refrigeration)**.
- **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 210 kJ/min** of heat removal.
- **Standard reference states**: Evaporator temperature **T₁ (or T_evap)**, Condenser temperature **T₂ (or T_cond)**.
- **Standard atmospheric pressure**: **101.325 kPa**.
- **Specific gas constant for air**: R = **0.287 kJ/kg·K**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Numerical problems on VCRS performance — calculating COP, ṁ, RE, W, Qc, and power required — carry an average weightage of **8 to 12 marks** in the End Semester Examination (ESE) and are a frequent Part B Module 2 question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of the VCRS Cycle (The Four State Points)

The simple Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle operates through **four state points** that form a closed loop:

1. **State 1 (Compressor Inlet):** Saturated vapour at evaporator pressure (P₁) and temperature (T₁). The refrigerant has absorbed heat from the cold space.
2. **State 2 (Compressor Outlet):** Superheated vapour at condenser pressure (P₂). Compression is isentropic in the ideal cycle.
3. **State 3 (Condenser Outlet):** Saturated liquid at condenser pressure (P₂) and temperature (T₂). The refrigerant has rejected heat to the surroundings.
4. **State 4 (Expansion Valve Outlet):** Liquid-vapour mixture at evaporator pressure (P₁). The throttling process is isenthalpic (h₄ = h₃).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these four points?**
> Each point represents a unique thermodynamic state. By tracking the refrigerant's **enthalpy (h)** at each of these points, we can determine how much energy was added, removed, or transferred — forming the basis of all cycle calculations.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|------|-------|
| 1 | Refrigerating Effect (RE) | $RE = h_1 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | Per kg of refrigerant |
| 2 | Compressor Work (W) | $W = h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | Isentropic compression |
| 3 | Heat Rejected (Qc) | $Q_c = h_2 - h_3$ | kJ/kg | Condenser load per kg |
| 4 | Coefficient of Performance | $COP = \dfrac{RE}{W} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | dimensionless | Higher is better |
| 5 | Carnot (Maximum) COP | $COP_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_1}{T_2 - T_1}$ | dimensionless | T in Kelvin |
| 6 | Mass Flow Rate | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q_E}{h_1 - h_4}$ | kg/s | $Q_E$ = cooling load in kW |
| 7 | Compressor Power | $P = \dot{m} \times (h_2 - h_1)$ | kW | Ideal power input |
| 8 | Heat Rejection Rate | $\dot{Q}_c = \dot{m} \times (h_2 - h_3)$ | kW | Condenser duty |
| 9 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_v = 0.9 - 0.05 \cdot r_p$ | - | Empirical, $r_p = P_2/P_1$ |
| 10 | Swept Volume (m³/s) | $V_s = \dfrac{\dot{m} \times v_1}{\eta_v}$ | m³/s | $v_1$ = sp. vol. at inlet |
| 11 | IHP (Indicated Power) | $IHP = \dfrac{P}{\eta_{mech}}$ | kW | With mechanical efficiency |
| 12 | Cylinder Volume | $V_c = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L \cdot n \cdot N$ | m³/s | Reciprocating compressor |
| 13 | With Superheat (RE') | $RE' = h_1 - h_3$ | kJ/kg | $h_1$ at superheated state |
| 14 | With Subcooling (RE'') | $RE'' = h_1 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | $h_4 = h_3 - (c_p \cdot \Delta T_{sc})$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These calculations are the foundation of **HVAC system design** (sizing chillers), **cold storage plant engineering** (selecting compressors), **refrigerant selection studies** (R-134a, R-717, R-22, R-410A), and **energy auditing** in building services. Every commercial chiller and household refrigerator uses these exact formulas in its design datasheet.

## 2.3 Enhanced Cycle Modifications and Their Quantitative Impact

The KTU 2024 syllabus stresses the quantitative effect of practical modifications on cycle performance:

### 2.3.1 Effect of Suction Superheating (ΔTsh)
- **Cause:** Heat gain in suction line; refrigerant leaves evaporator as superheated vapour.
- **Effect on RE:** RE increases (since $h_1$ increases), but compressor work increases **proportionally more** → **COP decreases**.
- **Compensated Superheat:** When part of the superheat occurs inside the evaporator (useful cooling) — this is desirable.

### 2.3.2 Effect of Liquid Subcooling (ΔTsc)
- **Cause:** Heat loss in liquid line or use of a sub-cooler.
- **Effect on RE:** RE increases (since $h_4$ decreases), with **no change in compressor work** → **COP increases**.
- **Quantitative Impact:** Each **1 °C** of subcooling gives approximately **2 to 4%** increase in COP for most refrigerants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Concept for KTU:**
> **Superheating ↑ → RE ↑ but W ↑ more → COP ↓**
> **Subcooling ↑ → RE ↑ with W constant → COP ↑**
> This asymmetry is a **guaranteed 2-mark conceptual question** in KTU ESE.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: COP of Vapour Compression Cycle

Starting from the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to the refrigerant flowing through the cycle:

### Step 1: Energy balance in the evaporator (cold space)
The heat absorbed by the refrigerant in the evaporator is the useful cooling:

$$
RE = h_1 - h_4 \quad \text{(per unit mass)}
$$

### Step 2: Energy balance in the compressor
The work input to the compressor raises the enthalpy of the refrigerant:

$$
W = h_2 - h_1
$$

### Step 3: Energy balance in the condenser
The heat rejected to the surroundings is the total heat delivered at the condenser:

$$
Q_c = h_2 - h_3
$$

### Step 4: Energy balance in the expansion valve
Throttling is an isenthalpic process (no work, no heat transfer, negligible ΔKE and ΔPE):

$$
h_3 = h_4
$$

### Step 5: Definition of COP
By definition, COP is the ratio of desired effect (cooling) to the work input:

$$
COP = \frac{\text{Desired Effect}}{\text{Work Input}} = \frac{RE}{W}
$$

### Step 6: Final substitution

$$
COP = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}
$$

### Step 7: Energy balance verification (sanity check)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_c &= RE + W \\
\dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_3) &= \dot{m} \cdot (h_1 - h_4) + \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_1) \\
h_2 - h_3 &= (h_1 - h_4) + (h_2 - h_1) \\
h_2 - h_3 &= h_2 - h_3 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

This confirms the **First Law of Thermodynamics** is satisfied.

## 3.2 Sample Problem — Full Worked Solution (KTU 2024 Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question Pattern]**
> A refrigeration system operates between **T₁ = −10 °C** and **T₂ = 40 °C**. The refrigerant is **R-134a**. The compressor handles saturated vapour at the inlet. The condenser produces saturated liquid at the outlet. Calculate:
> (a) The COP of the system
> (b) The mass flow rate for a cooling load of **5 TR**
> (c) The compressor power input
> (d) The heat rejection rate at the condenser
>
> Given refrigerant properties:
> - At −10 °C (P₁ = 0.2006 MPa): $h_1 = 392.7$ kJ/kg (saturated vapour), $v_1 = 0.0993$ m³/kg
> - At 40 °C (P₂ = 1.0166 MPa): $h_2 = 419.4$ kJ/kg (after isentropic compression), $h_3 = 256.4$ kJ/kg (saturated liquid), $h_4 = h_3 = 256.4$ kJ/kg

### Solution (a): COP of the System

**Step 1:** Identify enthalpies from the refrigerant tables at the state points.
- $h_1 = 392.7$ kJ/kg (saturated vapour at −10 °C)
- $h_2 = 419.4$ kJ/kg (superheated vapour at 40 °C, after isentropic compression from state 1)
- $h_3 = 256.4$ kJ/kg (saturated liquid at 40 °C)
- $h_4 = h_3 = 256.4$ kJ/kg (isenthalpic expansion)

**Step 2:** Apply the COP formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
COP &= \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} \\
COP &= \frac{392.7 - 256.4}{419.4 - 392.7} \\
COP &= \frac{136.3}{26.7} \\
COP &= 5.105
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Result.

$$
\boxed{COP = 5.105}
$$

### Solution (b): Mass Flow Rate

**Step 1:** Convert the cooling load to kW.

$$
Q_E = 5 \text{ TR} \times 3.517 \text{ kW/TR} = 17.585 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the mass flow rate equation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m} &= \frac{Q_E}{h_1 - h_4} \\
\dot{m} &= \frac{17.585 \text{ kW}}{136.3 \text{ kJ/kg}} \\
\dot{m} &= 0.1290 \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Result.

$$
\boxed{\dot{m} = 0.1290 \text{ kg/s} = 7.74 \text{ kg/min}}
$$

### Solution (c): Compressor Power Input

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \dot{m} \times (h_2 - h_1) \\
P &= 0.1290 \times 26.7 \\
P &= 3.444 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{P = 3.444 \text{ kW}}
$$

### Solution (d): Heat Rejection Rate at Condenser

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_c &= \dot{m} \times (h_2 - h_3) \\
\dot{Q}_c &= 0.1290 \times (419.4 - 256.4) \\
\dot{Q}_c &= 0.1290 \times 163.0 \\
\dot{Q}_c &= 21.03 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{Q}_c = 21.03 \text{ kW}}
$$

### Verification (Energy Balance)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_E + P &= \dot{Q}_c \\
17.585 + 3.444 &= 21.029 \text{ kW} \approx 21.03 \text{ kW} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always perform this final energy balance check in your KTU answer sheet. It earns **partial marks** even if the intermediate steps contain arithmetic errors.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sample Variations for Practice

| Variation Type | Modified h₄ | Modified W | Effect on COP |
|----------------|-------------|------------|----------------|
| With **5 °C** suction superheat | $h_4$ unchanged | $h_2 - h_1$ ↑ | COP ↓ |
| With **5 °C** liquid subcooling | $h_4$ ↓ by ~5·$c_p$ | unchanged | COP ↑ |
| With **isentropic efficiency** 80% | unchanged | $W_{actual} = W/\eta_{isen}$ | COP ↓ (real cycle) |
| With **pressure drops** in suction/discharge lines | $h_1$ ↑, $h_2$ ↑ | W ↑ | COP ↓ |
| With **multi-stage** compression + flash chamber | $h_4$ ↓ (more flashing) | W ↓ (less compression) | COP ↑ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: VCRS Calculation Workflow

This block diagram maps the **interactions** between the four state points, the data flow, and the calculation pipeline — replacing the physical p-h and T-s diagrams with a structured functional representation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: T1, T2, Refrigerant] --> B[Step1: Determine P1 from Saturation Table]
    B --> C[Step2: Determine h1 at Sat Vapour]
    C --> D[Step3: Determine P2 from Saturation Table]
    D --> E[Step4: Isentropic Compression to P2]
    E --> F[Step5: Determine h2 from Superheated Table]
    F --> G[Step6: Determine h3 at Sat Liquid at P2]
    G --> H[Step7: Throttling h4 equals h3]
    H --> I[Calculate: RE equals h1 minus h4]
    H --> J[Calculate: W equals h2 minus h1]
    I --> K[Calculate: COP equals RE over W]
    J --> K
    I --> L[Calculate: Mass flow mdot equals QE over RE]
    J --> M[Calculate: Power equals mdot times W]
    L --> M
    I --> N[Calculate: Qc equals mdot times h2 minus h3]
    L --> N
    M --> O[Energy Balance Check: QE plus Power equals Qc]
    N --> O
    O --> P[Output: Final VCRS Performance Parameters]
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow of Cycle States (State-Point Network)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[State1: Saturated Vapour h1] --Compressor Work W--> S2[State2: Superheated Vapour h2]
    S2 --Heat Rejection Qc--> S3[State3: Saturated Liquid h3]
    S3 --Throttling Valve h4 equals h3--> S4[State4: Liquid Vapour Mix h4]
    S4 --Evaporator RE h1 minus h4--> S1
    S1 -.->|v1| Q[Swept Volume Vs equals mdot v1 over eta v]
    Q --> R[Compressor Cylinder Sizing]
```

## 4.3 Calculation Process Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[KTU VCRS Calculation Problem] --> B{Given Data Type}
    B -->|Temperatures T1, T2| C[Refrigerant Tables Lookup]
    B -->|Pressures P1, P2| C
    C --> D[Identify State Point Enthalpies]
    D --> E[Compute RE and W]
    E --> F[Compute COP]
    F --> G[Compute mdot for given Load]
    G --> H[Compute Power and Qc]
    H --> I[Energy Balance Verification]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** Every block represents a logical step in the KTU-style numerical solution. The cycle diagram (4.2) shows that the four state points form a closed loop with the **expansion valve** (S3 → S4) being the only process with **zero work and zero heat transfer** (isenthalpic).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]`
**Define Coefficient of Performance (COP) for a refrigerator. How does it differ from the COP of a heat pump?**

**Model Answer:**
COP of a refrigerator is the ratio of the desired refrigerating effect (heat removed from the cold space) to the work input to the compressor. Mathematically,

$$
COP_{ref} = \frac{RE}{W} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{in}}
$$

The COP of a heat pump is the ratio of desired heating effect (heat delivered to the hot space) to the work input. Mathematically,

$$
COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{in}}
$$

**Key relationship:**

$$
COP_{HP} = COP_{ref} + 1
$$

because the heat rejected at the condenser ($Q_H$) equals the refrigerating effect ($Q_L$) plus the work input ($W_{in}$) by the First Law of Thermodynamics.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]`
**State the effect of subcooling on the performance of a Vapour Compression Refrigeration System.**

**Model Answer:**
Subcooling is the process of cooling the liquid refrigerant below its saturation temperature at the condenser pressure. The effect on VCRS performance is as follows:

1. **Refrigerating Effect increases** because $h_4$ decreases while $h_1$ remains unchanged, so $RE = h_1 - h_4$ increases.
2. **Compressor work remains unchanged** because the inlet state (state 1) is unaffected by subcooling.
3. **COP increases** since RE increases while W stays the same.
4. **Condenser load decreases slightly** as the refrigerant enters the condenser at a lower temperature, allowing more heat rejection.

Hence, subcooling is a **desirable enhancement** to the simple VCRS and is widely used in industrial refrigeration systems.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model 2024 Pattern]`

> A Vapour Compression Refrigeration System uses **R-134a** as the refrigerant. The evaporator operates at **−15 °C** and the condenser at **45 °C**. The compressor takes in saturated vapour and the condenser produces saturated liquid. The system provides a cooling load of **10 TR**.
>
> Refrigerant properties:
> - At −15 °C: $h_1 = 389.2$ kJ/kg, $h_{fg} = 209.5$ kJ/kg, $v_1 = 0.0926$ m³/kg
> - At 45 °C: $h_3 = 263.4$ kJ/kg, $h_2$ (after isentropic compression) = 427.1 kJ/kg
>
> **Calculate:**
> **(a)** The COP, the mass flow rate, and the compressor power input.
> **(b)** The heat rejected at the condenser and the Carnot COP. Comment on the comparison.

### Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Identify enthalpies.
- $h_1 = 389.2$ kJ/kg
- $h_2 = 427.1$ kJ/kg
- $h_3 = 263.4$ kJ/kg
- $h_4 = h_3 = 263.4$ kJ/kg

**Step 2:** Calculate Refrigerating Effect (RE).

$$
RE = h_1 - h_4 = 389.2 - 263.4 = 125.8 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate Compressor Work (W).

$$
W = h_2 - h_1 = 427.1 - 389.2 = 37.9 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 4:** Calculate COP.

$$
COP = \frac{RE}{W} = \frac{125.8}{37.9} = 3.319
$$

**Step 5:** Convert cooling load to kW and find mass flow rate.

$$
Q_E = 10 \text{ TR} \times 3.517 = 35.17 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{Q_E}{RE} = \frac{35.17}{125.8} = 0.2796 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 6:** Calculate compressor power.

$$
P = \dot{m} \times W = 0.2796 \times 37.9 = 10.60 \text{ kW}
$$

**Results (a):**

$$
\boxed{COP = 3.319, \quad \dot{m} = 0.2796 \text{ kg/s}, \quad P = 10.60 \text{ kW}}
$$

### Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Calculate heat rejected at condenser.

$$
\dot{Q}_c = \dot{m} \times (h_2 - h_3) = 0.2796 \times (427.1 - 263.4) = 0.2796 \times 163.7 = 45.77 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2:** Convert temperatures to Kelvin.
- $T_1 = -15 + 273 = 258$ K
- $T_2 = 45 + 273 = 318$ K

**Step 3:** Calculate Carnot COP.

$$
COP_{Carnot} = \frac{T_1}{T_2 - T_1} = \frac{258}{318 - 258} = \frac{258}{60} = 4.30
$$

**Step 4:** Compare the two COPs.

$$
\text{Relative COP} = \frac{COP_{actual}}{COP_{Carnot}} = \frac{3.319}{4.30} = 0.7719 = 77.19\%
$$

**Results (b):**

$$
\boxed{\dot{Q}_c = 45.77 \text{ kW}, \quad COP_{Carnot} = 4.30, \quad \text{Second Law Efficiency} = 77.19\%}
$$

**Comment:** The actual VCOP (3.319) is lower than the Carnot COP (4.30) because the VCRS is an irreversible cycle. The irreversibilities are due to throttling, non-ideal compression, and pressure drops. The **Second Law (or Exergy) Efficiency of 77.19%** indicates the VCRS is performing reasonably well, but there is still scope for improvement through subcooling, multi-staging, or improved heat exchangers.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model 2024 Pattern]`

> A refrigeration system uses **R-12** with evaporator temperature **−20 °C** and condenser temperature **30 °C**. The refrigerant is **subcooled by 5 °C** below the condenser temperature. The volumetric efficiency of the compressor is **85%** and the compressor runs at **1200 rpm** with a clearance ratio of **4%**.
>
> Refrigerant properties:
> - At −20 °C: $h_1 = 178.3$ kJ/kg, $v_1 = 0.0767$ m³/kg
> - At 30 °C: $h_2 = 207.5$ kJ/kg (after isentropic compression), $h_3 = 64.6$ kJ/kg
> - Specific heat of liquid R-12: $c_p = 0.93$ kJ/kg·K
>
> **Calculate:**
> **(a)** The COP and refrigerating effect with subcooling.
> **(b)** The mass flow rate for a cooling load of 7.5 TR, the swept volume, and the bore × stroke dimensions of a single-cylinder compressor with stroke = 1.2 × bore.

### Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Calculate subcooled enthalpy at state 3.
Subcooling by 5 °C means $T_3' = 30 - 5 = 25$ °C. The enthalpy reduction due to subcooling is:

$$
\Delta h_{sc} = c_p \cdot \Delta T_{sc} = 0.93 \times 5 = 4.65 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 2:** New enthalpies with subcooling.
- $h_1 = 178.3$ kJ/kg (unchanged)
- $h_2 = 207.5$ kJ/kg (unchanged — depends only on state 1 and P₂)
- $h_3' = h_3 - \Delta h_{sc} = 64.6 - 4.65 = 59.95$ kJ/kg
- $h_4' = h_3' = 59.95$ kJ/kg (isenthalpic expansion)

**Step 3:** Calculate the new RE and W.

$$
RE_{sc} = h_1 - h_4' = 178.3 - 59.95 = 118.35 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
W = h_2 - h_1 = 207.5 - 178.3 = 29.2 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 4:** Calculate COP.

$$
COP_{sc} = \frac{RE_{sc}}{W} = \frac{118.35}{29.2} = 4.054
$$

**Results (a):**

$$
\boxed{RE_{sc} = 118.35 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad COP_{sc} = 4.054}
$$

### Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Convert 7.5 TR to kW.

$$
Q_E = 7.5 \times 3.517 = 26.378 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate mass flow rate.

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{Q_E}{RE_{sc}} = \frac{26.378}{118.35} = 0.2229 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate swept volume (m³/s).

$$
V_s = \frac{\dot{m} \times v_1}{\eta_v} = \frac{0.2229 \times 0.0767}{0.85} = 0.02011 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4:** Relate swept volume to compressor geometry.

For a single-cylinder, single-acting reciprocating compressor:

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L \cdot \frac{N}{60}
$$

Given: $L = 1.2 \cdot D$ and $N = 1200$ rpm, so $N/60 = 20$ rev/s.

**Step 5:** Substitute and solve for D.

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.02011 &= \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot (1.2D) \cdot 20 \\
0.02011 &= \frac{\pi \cdot 1.2 \cdot 20}{4} \cdot D^3 \\
0.02011 &= 18.85 \cdot D^3 \\
D^3 &= \frac{0.02011}{18.85} = 0.001067 \\
D &= 0.1023 \text{ m} = 102.3 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Calculate stroke.

$$
L = 1.2 \times D = 1.2 \times 102.3 = 122.8 \text{ mm}
$$

**Results (b):**

$$
\boxed{\dot{m} = 0.2229 \text{ kg/s}, \quad V_s = 0.02011 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}, \quad D = 102.3 \text{ mm}, \quad L = 122.8 \text{ mm}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Students commonly mix up **kJ/kg** with **kW/kg/s** during COP calculation. Always remember: $W$ in **kJ/kg** when computing COP because the **kJ** units cancel.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert TR to kW:** The 1 TR = 3.517 kW conversion is the most-skipped step, leading to a **2-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Throttling is Isenthalpic:** A common error is writing $h_4 = h_3$ incorrectly or assuming it equals $h_2$. Always remember $h_3 = h_4$ in a simple cycle.
> 4. **Sign Convention:** In KTU, all enthalpies are **positive numbers** and the difference is taken. Do not introduce negative signs during subtraction.
> 5. **Refrigerant Table Lookups:** State $h_1$ as **saturated vapour** (top line of dome), and $h_3$ as **saturated liquid** (bottom line of dome). Using the wrong phase costs **full marks** of that sub-part.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Module 2 Calculations:**

- **Four state points** of the VCRS cycle: **1 (sat. vap.) → 2 (sup. vap.) → 3 (sat. liq.) → 4 (L-V mix)**.
- **Key formulas** to memorize:
  - $RE = h_1 - h_4$ — per kg cooling
  - $W = h_2 - h_1$ — per kg work
  - $COP = RE / W$ — performance metric
  - $\dot{m} = Q_E / RE$ — mass flow
  - $COP_{Carnot} = T_1 / (T_2 - T_1)$ — upper limit
  - $Q_c = RE + W$ — energy balance
- **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 210 kJ/min** — must memorize.
- **Subcooling ↑ → COP ↑** (desirable); **Superheating (external) ↑ → COP ↓** (undesirable).
- **Isentropic compression** in the ideal cycle: $s_1 = s_2$.
- **Throttling is isenthalpic:** $h_3 = h_4$.
- **Second Law Efficiency** $\eta_{II} = COP_{actual} / COP_{Carnot}$ — always less than 1.
- **Volumetric efficiency** $\eta_v = 0.9 - 0.05 \cdot r_p$ — empirical formula.
- **Compressor swept volume:** $V_s = \dot{m} v_1 / \eta_v$ — used for cylinder sizing.
- **Units discipline:** Enthalpy in **kJ/kg**, mass flow in **kg/s**, cooling load in **kW** or **TR**, temperatures in **Kelvin** for Carnot formula.
- **Energy balance check:** $Q_L + W = Q_H$ — always verify at the end of every calculation.
- **Avoid pipe `\|` symbol in tables** — use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX (KTU answer scripts prefer clean notation).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maximum Coefficient of Performance of a Heat Operated Refrigerating Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Maximum Coefficient of Performance of a Heat Operated Refrigerating Machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Heat Operated Refrigerating Machine (HORM)** is a refrigeration device that produces a cooling effect (refrigeration) by utilizing **heat energy as the primary energy input** rather than mechanical work or electrical energy. These machines operate on a thermodynamic cycle and transfer heat from a low-temperature body (refrigerated space) to a high-temperature body (heat sink/ambient) using an externally supplied heat source.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A *heat operated refrigerating machine* is a system in which the **net work input is zero (W = 0)**, and **heat input Q_H from a high-temperature source** is used to drive the refrigeration effect Q_L at a low temperature, while the remaining heat Q_R is rejected to an intermediate temperature sink.

The **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** of a heat operated refrigerating machine is defined as the ratio of the desired refrigeration effect (Q_L) to the heat energy input (Q_H):

$$\text{COP}_{\text{HORM}} = \frac{\text{Desired Output}}{\text{Energy Input}} = \frac{Q_L}{Q_H}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Mountain Fire Brigade" 🔥

Imagine a small village in a valley (the **cold space at T_L**) that needs water. There is a river on top of a mountain (the **heat source at T_H**). The villagers cannot pump water uphill because they have no electricity (no work available). However, a clever engineer sets up a fire at the mountain top, which heats water into steam, the steam rises and condenses in the valley collecting water, and the system uses thermal energy to "move cooling" from low to high temperature.

> The **fire (Q_H)** does not directly cool the village. Instead, it **drives the cycle**, just like in an **absorption refrigeration system** where heat from a furnace, solar collector, or waste heat source drives the refrigeration process.

In this analogy:
- **Village (cold space)** = Refrigerated space at T_L
- **Mountain top fire** = Heat source at T_H  
- **Sky/Clouds (heat rejection)** = Ambient sink at T_R
- **Water collected (useful output)** = Refrigeration effect Q_L
- **Fuel burnt (energy input)** = Heat supplied Q_H

## 1.3 Why Study the Maximum COP?

In a **reversible cycle**, the heat operated refrigerating machine attains its **maximum possible COP** because there are no internal irreversibilities. This represents the **theoretical upper limit** of performance, just as Carnot COP represents the upper limit for work-operated refrigerators.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:**
