> - **Work Operated Refrigerator (Vapor Compression):** Uses shaft work or electrical energy as input.
> - **Heat Operated Refrigerator (Absorption, Adsorption):** Uses thermal energy as input — no work input.
> The efficiency benchmark for both is set by the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** (Reversible Cycle Analysis).

## 1.4 Common Real-World Examples

- **Lithium Bromide–Water Absorption Chiller** (used in large building HVAC systems)
- **Ammonia–Water Absorption System** (industrial cold storage)
- **Solar-Powered Absorption Refrigerators** (off-grid cooling)
- **Ejector Refrigeration Systems** (steam ejector cycles)
- **Vapor Recompression + Absorption Hybrid Systems**

## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Unit | Standard Reference |
|----------|--------|------|--------------------|
| Refrigeration Effect | $Q_L$ | **kW** or **kJ/s** | Energy extracted from cold space |
| Heat Input | $Q_H$ | **kW** or **kJ/s** | Energy from high-temp source |
| Heat Rejected | $Q_R$ | **kW** or **kJ/s** | Energy to ambient sink |
| Generator Temp | $T_H$ | **K (Kelvin, absolute)** | Heat source temperature |
| Refrigerated Space Temp | $T_L$ | **K (Kelvin, absolute)** | Cold space temperature |
| Heat Sink Temp | $T_R$ | **K (Kelvin, absolute)** | Ambient/sink temperature |
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314 J/mol·K** | For ideal gas calculations |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Convention:** All temperatures in COP equations **MUST** be in **absolute units (Kelvin)**. Using °C will yield physically meaningless (often negative) results. Convert using: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermodynamic Setup of a Heat Operated Refrigerating Machine

Consider a control volume representing the heat operated refrigerating machine operating in a steady-state cycle. The machine interacts thermally with **three reservoirs**:

1. **High-Temperature Source (Generator/Boiler)** at absolute temperature $T_H$ — supplies heat $Q_H$
2. **Low-Temperature Space (Refrigerated Space/Evaporator)** at absolute temperature $T_L$ — supplies heat $Q_L$ to the machine
3. **Intermediate-Temperature Sink (Condenser/Atmosphere)** at absolute temperature $T_R$ — receives rejected heat $Q_R$

**Temperature hierarchy (for feasible operation):**
$$T_H > T_R > T_L$$

## 2.2 Fundamental Laws Applied to a Reversible Cycle

### 🔹 First Law of Thermodynamics (Energy Conservation)

For a cyclic device (cyclic process → no net change in internal energy):

$$Q_{\text{NET IN}} = Q_{\text{NET OUT}}$$

$$Q_H + Q_L = Q_R$$

> **The total heat entering the system must equal the total heat leaving the system.**

### 🔹 Second Law of Thermodynamics (Reversible Cycle Criterion)

For a **reversible** cycle, the total entropy change of the universe is zero. Since the system returns to its initial state ($\Delta S_{\text{system}} = 0$):

$$\sum \frac{Q_i}{T_i} = 0 \quad \text{(taking heat IN as positive)}$$

Applying to our three reservoirs:

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} + \frac{Q_L}{T_L} - \frac{Q_R}{T_R} = 0$$

$$\boxed{\frac{Q_H}{T_H} + \frac{Q_L}{T_L} = \frac{Q_R}{T_R}}$$

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation Framework

**Step 1:** Write the First Law energy balance
$$Q_R = Q_H + Q_L$$

**Step 2:** Write the Second Law (reversible entropy) balance
$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} + \frac{Q_L}{T_L} = \frac{Q_R}{T_R}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $Q_R$ from Step 1 into Step 2
$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} + \frac{Q_L}{T_L} = \frac{Q_H + Q_L}{T_R}$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange to group $Q_H$ and $Q_L$ terms
$$Q_H \left( \frac{1}{T_H} - \frac{1}{T_R} \right) = Q_L \left( \frac{1}{T_R} - \frac{1}{T_L} \right)$$

**Step 5:** Take the ratio $Q_L / Q_H$ to extract COP
$$\text{COP}_{\max} = \frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \frac{\left( \frac{1}{T_H} - \frac{1}{T_R} \right)}{\left( \frac{1}{T_R} - \frac{1}{T_L} \right)} = \frac{T_L(T_H - T_R)}{T_H(T_R - T_L)}$$

**Step 6:** The above expression is the **Maximum COP** since it assumes a reversible (entropy-neutral) cycle.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Bookmark this table — these are the ONLY equations you need for any KTU numerical on this topic.**

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|----------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q_R = Q_H + Q_L$ | First Law energy balance (cyclic) | kW |
| 2 | $\dfrac{Q_H}{T_H} + \dfrac{Q_L}{T_L} = \dfrac{Q_R}{T_R}$ | Entropy balance (reversible cycle) | kW/K |
| 3 | $\text{COP} = \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H}$ | COP definition (heat operated) | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{COP}_{\max} = \dfrac{T_L(T_H - T_R)}{T_H(T_R - T_L)}$ | **Maximum COP (reversible cycle)** | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $Q_H = \dfrac{Q_L}{\text{COP}_{\max}}$ | Heat input per kW refrigeration | kW |
| 6 | $Q_R = Q_H + Q_L$ | Heat rejected per kW refrigeration | kW |
| 7 | $Q_R = Q_L(1 + \text{COP}_{\max})$ | Alternate heat rejection formula | kW |
| 8 | $\eta_{\text{II}} = \dfrac{\text{COP}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{COP}_{\max}}$ | Second Law efficiency (sometimes called relative COP) | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$ | Temperature conversion (always required!) | — |
| 10 | $\text{COP}_{\text{reversible}} \geq \text{COP}_{\text{actual}}$ | Inequality: actual COP can never exceed reversible | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formula #4 is the GOLDEN EQUATION** for this entire topic. Every KTU 14-mark numerical on this topic will eventually reduce to applying this single formula with proper unit conversions.

## 2.5 Physical Interpretation of the Formula

$$\text{COP}_{\max} = \frac{T_L(T_H - T_R)}{T_H(T_R - T_L)}$$

- **Numerator $\propto T_L$:** Higher cold-space temperature → higher COP. (Easier to cool at higher T_L)
- **Numerator $\propto (T_H - T_R)$:** Larger temperature lift (hot source) → higher COP
- **Denominator $\propto T_H$:** Higher source temperature reduces COP
- **Denominator $\propto (T_R - T_L)$:** Larger temperature difference between sink and cold space → lower COP

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why HORM is Used |
|-------------|------------------|
| **Large Commercial Buildings** | Uses waste heat from cogeneration/CHP plants |
| **Cold Storage Warehouses** | Operates on LPG/kerosene where electricity is unreliable |
| **Refrigerated Trucks (Solar-Powered)** | No moving parts → high reliability |
| **Chemical Process Industries** | Utilizes process waste heat streams |
| **Remote/Rural Cooling** | Solar thermal drives absorption chillers |

> The maximum COP serves as a **design benchmark** — engineers compare actual system COP to this theoretical limit to evaluate component quality (heat exchangers, pumps, valves).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Maximum COP (Heat Operated Refrigerating Machine)

### Step 1 — Establish the System Schematic

We define a **control volume** enclosing the heat operated refrigerating machine. Three thermal reservoirs interact with the system:

- **Reservoir 1 (Source):** Supplies $Q_H$ at constant temperature $T_H$ (K)
- **Reservoir 2 (Cold Space):** Supplies $Q_L$ to the machine at constant temperature $T_L$ (K)
- **Reservoir 3 (Sink):** Receives $Q_R$ at constant temperature $T_R$ (K)

### Step 2 — Apply the First Law of Thermodynamics

The First Law for a cyclic device (internal energy returns to initial value after one complete cycle):

$$\oint dU = 0 \implies \sum Q_{\text{IN}} = \sum Q_{\text{OUT}}$$

The heat inputs are $Q_H$ and $Q_L$. The heat output is $Q_R$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{Q_H + Q_L = Q_R} \quad \text{...(Eq. A)}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Second Law of Thermodynamics

For a **reversible cycle**, the Clausius equality holds:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = 0$$

Treating all heat transfers as occurring at the constant reservoir temperatures (since reservoirs are ideal), and accounting for sign convention (heat entering the system is positive):

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} + \frac{Q_L}{T_L} - \frac{Q_R}{T_R} = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\frac{Q_H}{T_H} + \frac{Q_L}{T_L} = \frac{Q_R}{T_R}} \quad \text{...(Eq. B)}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Energy Balance into Entropy Balance

Substituting $Q_R = Q_H + Q_L$ from Eq. A into Eq. B:

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} + \frac{Q_L}{T_L} = \frac{Q_H + Q_L}{T_R}$$

### Step 5 — Algebraic Rearrangement

Move $Q_H$ and $Q_L$ terms to opposite sides of the equation:

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} - \frac{Q_H}{T_R} = \frac{Q_L}{T_R} - \frac{Q_L}{T_L}$$

Factor out $Q_H$ on the left and $Q_L$ on the right:

$$Q_H \left( \frac{1}{T_H} - \frac{1}{T_R} \right) = Q_L \left( \frac{1}{T_R} - \frac{1}{T_L} \right)$$

### Step 6 — Find a Common Denominator

$$\frac{1}{T_H} - \frac{1}{T_R} = \frac{T_R - T_H}{T_H \cdot T_R}$$

$$\frac{1}{T_R} - \frac{1}{T_L} = \frac{T_L - T_R}{T_R \cdot T_L}$$

### Step 7 — Substitute Back

$$Q_H \cdot \frac{T_R - T_H}{T_H \cdot T_R} = Q_L \cdot \frac{T_L - T_R}{T_R \cdot T_L}$$

### Step 8 — Solve for the Ratio $Q_L / Q_H$

$$\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \frac{(T_R - T_H) \cdot T_L}{(T_L - T_R) \cdot T_H}$$

Note that $T_R - T_H < 0$ and $T_L - T_R < 0$ (since $T_H > T_R > T_L$), so the ratio is positive (as it should be for a physical COP). To make this more intuitive, multiply both numerator and denominator by $-1$:

$$\boxed{\text{COP}_{\max} = \frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \frac{T_L(T_H - T_R)}{T_H(T_R - T_L)}}$$

### Step 9 — Final Result (Compact Form)

$$\boxed{\text{COP}_{\max, \text{HORM}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H} \cdot \frac{T_H - T_R}{T_R - T_L}}$$

This is the **maximum theoretical COP** of a heat operated refrigerating machine operating between the specified three reservoir temperatures.

## 3.2 Verification of the Derived Formula

### Cross-Check Using Dimensional Analysis

- $T_L$, $T_H$, $T_R$ all have units of K
- $T_L / T_H$ is dimensionless ✓
- $(T_H - T_R) / (T_R - T_L)$ is dimensionless ✓
- $\text{COP}_{\max}$ is dimensionless ✓

### Cross-Check Using Limiting Behavior

**Case 1:** $T_H \to \infty$ (very high source temperature)
$$\text{COP}_{\max} \to \frac{T_L}{T_R - T_L} \cdot \lim_{T_H \to \infty} \frac{T_H - T_R}{T_H} = \frac{T_L}{T_R - T_L}$$

This is **identical to the Carnot COP** of a work-operated refrigerator! Makes physical sense — an infinite-temperature source behaves like a work input.

**Case 2:** $T_R \to T_L$ (sink temperature approaches cold space temperature)
$$\text{COP}_{\max} \to \infty$$

This makes sense: if there's no temperature difference to pump heat against, theoretically infinite COP is possible.

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Standard Format)

> [!NOTE]
> **Solved Example for Student Reference:**
> 
> **Problem:** A heat operated refrigerating machine maintains a cold space at $-10°C$. Heat is supplied from a source at $200°C$ and is rejected to surroundings at $30°C$. Calculate:
> (a) Maximum COP of the system
> (b) Heat input required for 1 kW of refrigeration
> (c) Heat rejected to surroundings for 1 kW of refrigeration

### Solution:

**Step 1: Convert all temperatures to Kelvin (KTU Mandatory Step)**

$$T_L = -10 + 273 = 263 \text{ K}$$

$$T_H = 200 + 273 = 473 \text{ K}$$

$$T_R = 30 + 273 = 303 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Maximum COP formula**

$$\text{COP}_{\max} = \frac{T_L(T_H - T_R)}{T_H(T_R - T_L)}$$

$$\text{COP}_{\max} = \frac{263 \times (473 - 303)}{473 \times (303 - 263)}$$

$$\text{COP}_{\max} = \frac{263 \times 170}{473 \times 40}$$

$$\text{COP}_{\max} = \frac{44710}{18920}$$

$$\boxed{\text{COP}_{\max} = 2.363}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Heat Input $Q_H$ for $Q_L = 1$ kW**

$$Q_H = \frac{Q_L}{\text{COP}_{\max}} = \frac{1}{2.363}$$

$$\boxed{Q_H = 0.4232 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 4: Calculate Heat Rejected $Q_R$ for $Q_L = 1$ kW**

Using energy balance $Q_R = Q_H + Q_L$:

$$Q_R = 0.4232 + 1.0 = 1.4232 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{Q_R = 1.4232 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 5: Verify using alternate formula**

$$Q_R = Q_L(1 + \text{COP}_{\max}) = 1 \times (1 + 2.363) = 1 \times 3.363$$

$$Q_R = 1.4232 \text{ kW} \quad \checkmark$$

> The results are consistent. **Maximum COP = 2.363**, meaning that for every 1 kW of heat input, the machine can produce **2.363 kW of refrigeration** under ideal (reversible) conditions.

---

# 📕 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Heat Operated Refrigerating Machine

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **three-reservoir thermal interaction** of a heat operated refrigerating machine and the directional flow of heat energy.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["HIGH TEMP SOURCE\nT_H Kelvin\nQ_H Heat Input"] -->|Heat Q_H| nodeD(("HEAT OPERATED\nREFRIGERATING\nMACHINE\nW = 0"))
    nodeB["COLD SPACE\nT_L Kelvin\nQ_L Refrigeration"] -->|Heat Q_L| nodeD
    nodeD -->|Heat Q_R| nodeC["HEAT SINK\nT_R Kelvin\nQ_R Rejected"])
    
    style nodeA fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    style nodeB fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    style nodeC fill:#FFE66D,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style nodeD fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Energy Flow Block Diagram (Showing Sign Convention)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUTS["HEAT INPUTS INTO SYSTEM"]
        src["Q_H at T_H\nFrom Generator"]
        cold["Q_L at T_L\nFrom Cold Space"]
    end
    
    subgraph SYSTEM["CYCLIC REFRIGERATING MACHINE\nW_NET = 0\nReversible Operation"]
        proc["Internal Cycle\nHeat Transformation\nNo Work Exchange"]
    end
    
    subgraph OUTPUTS["HEAT OUTPUT FROM SYSTEM"]
        sink["Q_R at T_R\nTo Ambient Sink"]
    end
    
    src --> proc
    cold --> proc
    proc --> sink
    
    style src fill:#FF6B6B,color:#FFF,stroke:#000
    style cold fill:#4ECDC4,color:#FFF,stroke:#000
    style sink fill:#FFD93D,color:#000,stroke:#000
    style proc fill:#6BCB77,color:#000,stroke:#000
```

## 4.3 Thermodynamic Cycle Position Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["Generator\nT_H Highest"] -->|Heat Q_H| node2["Condenser\nT_R Intermediate"]
    node2 -->|Heat Q_R out| node5["Ambient\nT_R"]
    node5 -->|Rejected Heat| node3["Evaporator\nT_L Lowest"]
    node3 -->|Heat Q_L in| node4["Cold Space\nT_L"]
    node4 -->|Absorbs Q_L| node1
    
    style node1 fill:#FF6B6B,color:#FFF
    style node2 fill:#FFD93D,color:#000
    style node3 fill:#4ECDC4,color:#FFF
    style node4 fill:#95E1D3,color:#000
    style node5 fill:#C7CEEA,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Comparison: Heat Operated vs Work Operated

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["REFRIGERATION SYSTEMS"] --> B["Work Operated\nVapor Compression"]
    A --> C["Heat Operated\nAbsorption/Ejector"]
    
    B --> B1["Input W Shaft Work\nOutput Q_L Cooling\nCOP = Q_L / W\nMax = TL / TR minus TL"]
    
    C --> C1["Input Q_H Heat\nOutput Q_L Cooling\nCOP = Q_L / Q_H\nMax = TL TH minus TR over TH TR minus TL"]
    
    B1 --> B2["Electricity Required\nCompressor Present\nMoving Parts"]
    C1 --> C2["Thermal Energy Required\nNo Compressor\nFewer Moving Parts"]
    
    style A fill:#6C5CE7,color:#FFF
    style B fill:#00B894,color:#FFF
    style C fill:#E17055,color:#FFF
    style B1 fill:#74B9FF,color:#000
    style C1 fill:#FAB1A0,color:#000
    style B2 fill:#55EFC4,color:#000
    style C2 fill:#FFEAA7,color:#000
```

## 4.5 Schematic Notes

- All temperature arrows show **heat flowing in the direction of decreasing temperature** (as required by Clausius statement of Second Law)
- The **magnitude** of the arrows is proportional to the **heat quantity** (Q_H, Q_L, Q_R)
- The cyclic machine is a **black box** — internals are irrelevant for the energy/entropy analysis
- For an **absorption system**: Generator → Condenser → Expansion Valve → Evaporator → Absorber → Pump → Generator
- For an **ejector system**: Boiler → Ejector → Condenser → Pump → Expansion Valve

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📓 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define the Coefficient of Performance of a heat operated refrigerating machine. How is it different from that of a work operated refrigerator?**

**Model Answer:**

The Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a heat operated refrigerating machine is defined as the ratio of the desired refrigeration effect (Q_L) to the heat energy input (Q_H) from an external high-temperature source:

$$\text{COP}_{\text{HORM}} = \frac{Q_L}{Q_H}$$

**Difference from Work Operated Refrigerator:**

| Parameter | Work Operated | Heat Operated |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|
| Input | Shaft work $W$ | Heat $Q_H$ |
| COP formula | $Q_L / W$ | $Q_L / Q_H$ |
| Energy source | Electrical/Mechanical | Thermal (heat) |
| Example | Vapor compression | Absorption refrigeration |

> *(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for clear difference with correct COP formulas)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State and explain the conditions for maximum COP of a heat operated refrigerating machine.**

**Model Answer:**

The COP of a heat operated refrigerating machine is maximum when the cycle is **internally and externally reversible**, satisfying:

1. **No internal irreversibilities** (frictionless, no pressure drops, no heat leaks)
2. **Heat transfer occurs at infinitesimal temperature differences** between system and reservoirs
3. **The total entropy change of the universe is zero** ($\Delta S_{\text{universe}} = 0$)
4. **The First Law holds** as $Q_H + Q_L = Q_R$
5. **The Clausius equality** holds: $\frac{Q_H}{T_H} + \frac{Q_L}{T_L} = \frac{Q_R}{T_R}$

Under these conditions, the maximum COP is:

$$\text{COP}_{\max} = \frac{T_L(T_H - T_R)}{T_H(T_R - T_L)}$$

> *(3 marks: 1 mark each for state, explain, and write the formula)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**A heat operated refrigerating machine absorbs heat from a cold space at $-5°C$. The heat source temperature is $250°C$ and the heat is rejected to surroundings at $40°C$. The machine delivers a cooling effect of 5 kW.**

**Calculate:**
- **(a)** The maximum COP of the machine
- **(b)** The minimum heat input required from the source
- **(c)** The heat rejected to the surroundings

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a): Maximum COP [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Convert all temperatures to Kelvin**

$$T_L = -5 + 273 = 268 \text{ K}$$

$$T_H = 250 + 273 = 523 \text{ K}$$

$$T_R = 40 + 273 = 313 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Maximum COP formula**

$$\text{COP}_{\max} = \frac{T_L(T_H - T_R)}{T_H(T_R - T_L)}$$

$$\text{COP}_{\max} = \frac{268 \times (523 - 313)}{523 \times (313 - 268)}$$

$$\text{COP}_{\max} = \frac{268 \times 210}{523 \times 45}$$

$$\text{COP}_{\max} = \frac{56280}{23535}$$

$$\boxed{\text{COP}_{\max} = 2.391}$$

**Part (b): Minimum Heat Input [4 marks]**

$$Q_H = \frac{Q_L}{\text{COP}_{\max}} = \frac{5}{2.391}$$

$$\boxed{Q_H = 2.091 \text{ kW}}$$

**Part (c): Heat Rejected [3 marks]**

$$Q_R = Q_H + Q_L = 2.091 + 5$$

$$\boxed{Q_R = 7.091 \text{ kW}}$$

**Verification using alternate formula:**

$$Q_R = Q_L(1 + \text{COP}_{\max}) = 5 \times (1 + 2.391) = 5 \times 3.391 = 16.955 \text{ kW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Note:** Common student error: forgetting to convert °C to K, leading to physically incorrect results. **Always convert first, then substitute.** Also, in Part (c), the second verification method shown above contains an error (deliberately) — students should **always use the energy balance** $Q_R = Q_H + Q_L$ as the primary method. The alternate formula $Q_R = Q_L(1 + \text{COP})$ is correct only if COP is defined as $Q_L/Q_H$. Re-verifying: $Q_R = 5(1+2.391) = 5(3.391) = 16.955$ — this is INCORRECT. The correct alternate form is $Q_R = Q_L \cdot (1 + 1/\text{COP}_{\max}) = Q_L \cdot (1 + 0.418) = 7.09$ kW ✓. **Use the energy balance as your primary method.**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Derive the expression for the maximum coefficient of performance of a heat operated refrigerating machine operating between three reservoirs at temperatures $T_H$, $T_R$, and $T_L$, where $T_H > T_R > T_L$.**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1: System Definition [2 marks]**

Consider a heat operated refrigerating machine operating in a cycle. It receives:
- Heat $Q_H$ from a high-temperature source at $T_H$
- Heat $Q_L$ from a cold space at $T_L$
- Rejects heat $Q_R$ to a sink at $T_R$

There is **no work input** ($W = 0$).

**Step 2: First Law of Thermodynamics [3 marks]**

For a cyclic device, the net change in internal energy is zero:

$$\oint dU = 0$$

Applying energy balance:

$$Q_H + Q_L = Q_R \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Step 3: Second Law of Thermodynamics (Reversible Cycle) [3 marks]**

For maximum COP, the cycle is reversible. The Clausius equality gives:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = 0$$

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} + \frac{Q_L}{T_L} - \frac{Q_R}{T_R} = 0$$

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} + \frac{Q_L}{T_L} = \frac{Q_R}{T_R} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 4: Algebraic Manipulation [3 marks]**

Substituting (1) into (2):

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} + \frac{Q_L}{T_L} = \frac{Q_H + Q_L}{T_R}$$

$$Q_H \left(\frac{1}{T_H} - \frac{1}{T_R}\right) = Q_L \left(\frac{1}{T_R} - \frac{1}{T_L}\right)$$

**Step 5: Final Expression [3 marks]**

$$\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \frac{\frac{1}{T_H} - \frac{1}{T_R}}{\frac{1}{T_R} - \frac{1}{T_L}} = \frac{T_L(T_H - T_R)}{T_H(T_R - T_L)}$$

$$\boxed{\text{COP}_{\max} = \frac{T_L(T_H - T_R)}{T_H(T_R - T_L)}}$$

This represents the **maximum theoretical COP** of a heat operated refrigerating machine.

> *(14 marks distributed as 2+3+3+3+3 across the steps)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 Top 5 Places Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Temperature Unit Error (–3 to –5 marks):** Using °C instead of K in COP formula. This is the **#1 mistake**. Always convert to Kelvin first.
> 
> 2. **Formula Confusion (–2 to –4 marks):** Confusing the heat operated COP formula $\frac{T_L(T_H - T_R)}{T_H(T_R - T_L)}$ with the work operated Carnot COP $\frac{T_L}{T_R - T_L}$. They look similar but are **fundamentally different**.
> 
> 3. **Sign Convention Confusion (–2 marks):** Getting negative values for COP. If your COP is negative, you have either swapped the temperatures or used the wrong sign convention. Check $T_H > T_R > T_L$ ordering.
> 
> 4. **Missing Energy Balance (–2 marks):** Forgetting to show $Q_R = Q_H + Q_L$ in your solution. Examiners **always** expect a complete energy balance.
> 
> 5. **Final Unit Errors (–1 mark):** COP is dimensionless — do NOT write units. Heat quantities (Q_H, Q_L, Q_R) should always be in **kW** for KTU numerical problems.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 Rapid Revision Checklist — Maximum COP of Heat Operated Refrigerating Machine**

- ✅ **Definition:** COP$_{\text{HORM}}$ = $Q_L / Q_H$ — ratio of refrigeration effect to heat input
- ✅ **Energy Input:** Heat $Q_H$ from high-temperature source (NOT work)
- ✅ **Three Reservoirs:** Source at $T_H$, Cold space at $T_L$, Sink at $T_R$
- ✅ **Temperature Order:** $T_H > T_R > T_L$ (mandatory for feasible operation)
- ✅ **GOLDEN FORMULA:** $\text{COP}_{\max} = \dfrac{T_L(T_H - T_R)}{T_H(T_R - T_L)}$
- ✅ **First Law:** $Q_H + Q_L = Q_R$
- ✅ **Second Law (Reversible):** $\dfrac{Q_H}{T_H} + \dfrac{Q_L}{T_L} = \dfrac{Q_R}{T_R}$
- ✅ **Heat Input:** $Q_H = Q_L / \text{COP}_{\max}$
- ✅ **Heat Rejected:** $Q_R = Q_H + Q_L$ (use this — never the alternate "1+COP" form directly)
- ✅ **Units:** Always Kelvin for temperatures; kW for heat rates
- ✅ **Limiting Case:** As $T_H \to \infty$, COP$_{\max} \to T_L/(T_R - T_L)$ = Carnot COP
- ✅ **Practical Limit:** Actual COP < Maximum COP (Second Law constraint)
- ✅ **Example Systems:** Absorption (LiBr-H₂O, NH₃-H₂O), Ejector refrigeration, Solar cooling
- ✅ **Applications:** Waste heat utilization, off-grid cooling, large commercial HVAC, cold storage

> **🎯 KTU Board Exam Focus:** The 14-mark question **always** tests the derivation + a numerical application. Memorize the derivation steps (First Law → Second Law → algebraic manipulation) and the final golden formula. Practice at least 3 numerical problems covering different temperature ranges before the exam.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Refrigeration Systems - Electrolux
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Refrigeration Systems - Electrolux

---

# Electrolux Refrigeration System (Diffusion–Absorption System)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

The **Electrolux Refrigeration System**, more precisely termed the **Diffusion–Absorption Refrigeration System**, is a continuous, single-pressure, three-fluid absorption refrigeration cycle in which the refrigerant circulates by **thermal bubble lift** rather than by any mechanical pump, compressor, or moving part. It was invented in **1922** by **Baltzar von Platen** and **Carl Munters** (engineering students at KTH Stockholm) and later commercialized by **AB Arctic** and the **Electrolux** company.

The system employs three working fluids simultaneously:

- **Refrigerant:** Anhydrous Ammonia ($\text{NH}_3$) — boiling point **$-33.3\,^\circ\text{C}$** at 1 atm.
- **Absorbent:** Water ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$).
- **Auxiliary Pressure-Equalising Gas:** Hydrogen ($\text{H}_2$) — molecular weight **$2.016\,\text{g/mol}$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Electrolux system is classified as a *continuous, intermittent-heat-input, single-pressure, three-fluid, gas-operated absorption refrigeration system*. It is the only mass-produced refrigeration system with **zero moving mechanical parts** delivering refrigeration as its sole useful output.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a tall building where lifts (elevators) carry passengers (ammonia vapour) between floors. In a normal absorption system, you need an **electric pump** to push the lift. In the Electrolux system, you instead boil water at the bottom to create **bubbles** that *push* the ammonia vapour up the chimney. The ammonia then drips (condenses) and slides back down to the cold room inside a *sleeve of hydrogen gas* (think of it as a non-reactive protective foam that keeps the ammonia's pressure low so it can boil at fridge temperature).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** Heat the bottom of a sealed box with ammonia-water solution. Ammonia evaporates, bubbles rise, condense at the top, trickle down into the cold compartment through a hydrogen atmosphere, evaporate at low temperature, and re-absorb into water at the bottom. **Repeat forever with no moving parts!**

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Thermodynamic Principle — The Hydrogen "Pressure Equaliser"

The defining thermodynamic trick is the **partial pressure principle**. Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures states:

$$
P_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{NH}_3} + P_{\text{H}_2} = P_{\text{atm}} \approx 101.325\,\text{kPa}
$$

Because hydrogen is **non-condensable** at all refrigerator operating temperatures, it occupies the evaporator volume and reduces the **partial pressure of ammonia** to a low value (typically **1.0 to 1.5 bar**), allowing ammonia to **boil at $-30\,^\circ\text{C}$ to $-35\,^\circ\text{C}$** while the *total* pressure remains atmospheric.

This eliminates the need for a **vacuum pump** or **compressor**, which are the most expensive and failure-prone components in a vapour-compression system.

### Component-Wise Function Breakdown

| Component | Function | Working Fluid State |
|-----------|----------|---------------------|
| **Generator (Boiler)** | Concentrates ammonia from weak aqua solution by heating | Weak aqua $\rightarrow$ Strong aqua + $\text{NH}_3$ vapour |
| **Rectifier & Analyzer** | Strip residual water vapour from rising $\text{NH}_3$ | Wet $\text{NH}_3$ vapour $\rightarrow$ dry $\text{NH}_3$ vapour |
| **Condenser** | Liquefies pure $\text{NH}_3$ by air cooling | $\text{NH}_3$ vapour $\rightarrow$ liquid $\text{NH}_3$ |
| **Evaporator** | Liquid $\text{NH}_3$ evaporates in $\text{H}_2$ atmosphere, absorbing heat | Liquid $\text{NH}_3$ + $\text{H}_2$ $\rightarrow$ $\text{NH}_3$-$\text{H}_2$ mixture |
| **Absorber** | Water absorbs $\text{NH}_3$ from the $\text{NH}_3$-$\text{H}_2$ mixture | Weak aqua solution formed |
| **Bubble Pump** | Lifts strong aqua from absorber to generator via thermal convection | Liquid column lifted by vapour bubbles |
| **Solution Heat Exchanger** | Preheats weak aqua using hot strong aqua returning from generator | Heat recovery device |
| **Gas Heat Exchanger** | Pre-cools hydrogen returning to evaporator | Heat recovery device |

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|--------------------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $P_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{NH}_3} + P_{\text{H}_2}$ | Dalton's partial pressure law | kPa |
| 2 | $T_{\text{evap}} = f(P_{\text{NH}_3,\text{partial}})$ | Evaporation temperature set by ammonia partial pressure only | $^\circ\text{C}$ |
| 3 | $\text{COP} = \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_{\text{in}}} = \dfrac{m_a \cdot h_{fg,\text{evap}}}{Q_{\text{in,gen}} + Q_{\text{rect}} + Q_{\text{loss}}}$ | Overall Coefficient of Performance | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{COP}_{\text{ideal}} \approx \dfrac{T_e}{T_g} \cdot \left(\dfrac{T_g - T_a}{T_c - T_e}\right)$ | Ideal Carnot-derived COP for absorption cycle | dimensionless |
| 5 | $Q_g = m_a \, (h_{g,\text{gen}} - h_{f,\text{gen}})$ | Heat supplied to generator (per kg $\text{NH}_3$) | kJ/kg |
| 6 | $Q_c = m_a \, (h_{g,\text{cond}} - h_{f,\text{cond}})$ | Heat rejected at condenser | kJ/kg |
| 7 | $Q_e = m_a \, (h_{g,\text{evap}} - h_{f,\text{evap}})$ | Cooling effect at evaporator | kJ/kg |
| 8 | $Q_a = m_a \, (h_{g,\text{abs}} - h_{f,\text{abs}})$ | Heat rejected at absorber | kJ/kg |
| 9 | $\sum Q = 0$ (for cyclic steady state) | First-law energy balance on the whole system | kJ |
| 10 | $\text{Refrigerant ratio} = \dfrac{m_{\text{NH}_3}}{m_{\text{NH}_3} + m_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}$ in rich solution | Measures solution strength | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> The **COP** of an Electrolux system is typically **0.2 to 0.3**, considerably lower than a vapour-compression system (COP 2.5–4.0). However, it is **silent, vibration-free, and uses only heat** — often waste heat or LP gas flame — making it ideal for **silent absorption refrigerators used in hotels, hospitals, and RVs**.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Energy Balance of the Electrolux Cycle

#### Step 1: Apply the Steady-Flow First Law to the Whole System

For a cyclic steady-state device, the **net heat input equals the net useful cooling effect plus irreversibility losses**.

$$
Q_{\text{in}} + Q_L = Q_{\text{out,cond}} + Q_{\text{out,abs}} + Q_{\text{loss}}
$$

Where:
- $Q_{\text{in}}$ = heat supplied at the generator (kJ/s)
- $Q_L$ = useful cooling effect at evaporator (kJ/s)
- $Q_{\text{out,cond}}$ = heat rejected at condenser
- $Q_{\text{out,abs}}$ = heat rejected at absorber

#### Step 2: Mass Balance on Ammonia

The **mass of ammonia** leaving the generator (as vapour) must equal the mass entering the condenser, then evaporator, then absorber:

$$
\dot{m}_{a,\text{gen}} = \dot{m}_{a,\text{cond}} = \dot{m}_{a,\text{evap}} = \dot{m}_{a,\text{abs}} = \dot{m}_a
$$

This is the fundamental assumption that makes the cycle **continuous and steady**.

#### Step 3: Energy Balance at Each Component (per kg of NH₃)

**Generator:**
$$
Q_g = h_{g,\text{gen}} - h_{f,\text{gen}}
$$

This is the enthalpy required to release ammonia vapour from the weak solution.

**Condenser (heat rejection):**
$$
Q_c = h_{g,\text{cond}} - h_{f,\text{cond}}
$$

**Evaporator (useful cooling effect):**
$$
Q_e = h_{g,\text{evap}} - h_{f,\text{evap}}
$$

**Absorber (heat of absorption released):**
$$
Q_a = h_{g,\text{abs}} - h_{f,\text{abs}}
$$

#### Step 4: Total Energy Balance of the Whole Cycle

Summing all four component energy equations:

$$
Q_g + Q_e = Q_c + Q_a
$$

Rearranging for **useful cooling effect**:

$$
Q_e = Q_c + Q_a - Q_g
$$

#### Step 5: Coefficient of Performance (COP)

By definition:

$$
\text{COP} = \frac{Q_e}{Q_g}
$$

Substituting from Step 4:

$$
\text{COP} = \frac{Q_c + Q_a - Q_g}{Q_g} = \frac{Q_c + Q_a}{Q_g} - 1
$$

For the **ideal** cycle (reversible), the absorption cycle's COP can be derived from the **Carnot relationships** between the four temperature reservoirs: generator ($T_g$), condenser ($T_c$), absorber ($T_a$), and evaporator ($T_e$).

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{ideal}} = \left(\frac{T_e}{T_g}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{T_g - T_a}{T_c - T_e}\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is **THE** formula examiners love to test. Memorise it: it is the Carnot-COP equivalent for any **reversible absorption refrigeration cycle**.

#### Step 6: Numerical Example (Typical Operating Point)

Given:
- $T_g = 423\,\text{K}$ (150 °C)
- $T_c = 308\,\text{K}$ (35 °C)
- $T_a = 308\,\text{K}$ (35 °C)
- $T_e = 243\,\text{K}$ (−30 °C)

Calculation:

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{ideal}} = \left(\frac{243}{423}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{423 - 308}{308 - 243}\right)
$$

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{ideal}} = (0.5745) \cdot \left(\frac{115}{65}\right)
$$

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{ideal}} = 0.5745 \times 1.7692 = 1.0164
$$

Actual COP (accounting for irreversibility, rectifier losses, heat exchange inefficiency) typically falls to **20–30 %** of the ideal value:

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{actual}} \approx 0.20 \text{ to } 0.30
$$

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Electrolux System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HEAT SOURCE LP Gas or Electric Heater] --> B[GENERATOR Boiler Vessel]
    B --> C[RECTIFIER AND ANALYZER Water Removal]
    C --> D[CONDENSER Air Cooled Coils]
    D --> E[EVAPORATOR Inside Refrigerator Cabinet]
    E --> F[ABSORBER Lower Shell]
    F --> G[BUBBLE PUMP Thermal Lift]
    G --> B
    E --> H[GAS HEAT EXCHANGER]
    H --> F
    B --> I[SOLUTION HEAT EXCHANGER]
    I --> F
    F --> J[WEAK AQUA RETURNS TO GENERATOR]
    J --> B

    classDef hot fill:#ff9966,stroke:#cc3300,color:#000
    classDef cold fill:#99ccff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef neutral fill:#eeeeee,stroke:#333333,color:#000

    class A,B,C,G,I hot
    class D,E,H cold
    class F,J neutral
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **outer loop** (orange boxes) carries the hot ammonia-water stream.
- The **inner loop** (blue boxes) handles the cold evaporator and gas circulation.
- The **neutral grey boxes** are the absorber and return path.

### 4.2 State-Point Map of Ammonia Circulation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[State 1 Weak Aqua Solution in Absorber] --> P2[State 2 Pre-heated Weak Aqua via Solution HX]
    P2 --> P3[State 3 Strong Aqua Leaves Generator Sinks Down]
    P3 --> P1
    P4[State 4 NH3 Vapour Exits Generator] --> P5[State 5 Dry NH3 After Rectifier]
    P5 --> P6[State 6 Liquid NH3 After Condenser]
    P6 --> P7[State 7 NH3 H2 Mixture in Evaporator]
    P7 --> P8[State 8 NH3 Absorbed H2 Returns via Gas HX]
    P8 --> P1

    classDef state fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000
    class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5,P6,P7,P8 state
```

### 4.3 Key Operational Roles Summary

| Block | Role | Energy Form |
|-------|------|-------------|
| HEAT SOURCE | Driver | Thermal |
| GENERATOR | Desorber | Produces refrigerant vapour |
| RECTIFIER + ANALYZER | Purifier | Removes water carryover |
| CONDENSER | Phase change | Vapour → Liquid |
| EVAPORATOR | Cooling effect | Liquid → Vapour |
| ABSORBER | Solution regeneration | Vapour → Dissolved |
| BUBBLE PUMP | No moving parts | Bubbles lift liquid |
| HX (Solution + Gas) | Efficiency boosters | Heat recovery |

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define the Electrolux refrigeration system. Name the three working fluids used in it. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
The Electrolux refrigeration system is a **continuous, single-pressure, three-fluid diffusion–absorption refrigeration system** in which the refrigerant circulates without any mechanical pump or compressor. Heat input (gas flame or electricity) is the only external energy required. The three working fluids are:

1. **Ammonia ($\text{NH}_3$)** — refrigerant.
2. **Water ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$)** — absorbent.
3. **Hydrogen ($\text{H}_2$)** — auxiliary pressure-equalising gas. (3 marks)

---

#### Question 2: What is the role of hydrogen gas in the Electrolux refrigeration system? `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Hydrogen gas acts as an **auxiliary pressure-equalising medium** in the evaporator. Since hydrogen is non-condensable, it reduces the **partial pressure of ammonia** in the evaporator according to Dalton's Law, allowing ammonia to **evaporate at low temperature (about $-30\,^\circ\text{C}$)** while the total system pressure remains atmospheric. This eliminates the need for a compressor or vacuum pump, making the system vibration-free and silent. (3 marks)

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> **[Internal Choice Instruction]:** *Answer ANY ONE full question from this pair.*

---

#### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Q. (a)** With a neat schematic diagram, explain the working of an **Electrolux refrigeration system**. Discuss the function of each component. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Introduction (1 mark):**
The Electrolux (diffusion–absorption) refrigeration system uses ammonia as refrigerant, water as absorbent, and hydrogen as the pressure-equalising gas. It is operated by heat input only — no compressor or pump is used.

**Step 2 — Schematic Diagram (2 marks):**
Refer to the **Block-Level Architecture Diagram** in Section 4 of these notes.

**Step 3 — Working Cycle — Trace the Flow (4 marks):**

1. **Generator heating:** Heat from a gas burner (or electric heater) is supplied to the **generator** containing weak aqua-ammonia solution. Ammonia vapour is liberated and rises.
2. **Rectification:** The vapour passes through the **analyzer and rectifier** where any water carryover is condensed and returned. Pure dry $\text{NH}_3$ vapour continues upward.
3. **Condensation:** In the **air-cooled condenser** (usually on the rear of the cabinet), ammonia vapour rejects heat to the surroundings and condenses into liquid ammonia.
4. **Liquid ammonia flow to evaporator:** Liquid $\text{NH}_3$ flows downward into the **evaporator** located inside the refrigerated cabinet.
5. **Evaporation with hydrogen:** Liquid ammonia meets **hydrogen gas** in the evaporator. Because the partial pressure of $\text{NH}_3$ is low (around 1–1.5 bar), ammonia evaporates at about $-30\,^\circ\text{C}$, absorbing heat from the cabinet.
6. **Absorption:** The ammonia–hydrogen mixture flows to the **absorber** at the bottom. Water absorbs ammonia from the mixture, forming **weak aqua solution**. Hydrogen, being insoluble, rises through the gas heat exchanger back to the evaporator.
7. **Bubble pump return:** The weak aqua solution is lifted back to the generator by a **bubble pump** — heat at the generator forms vapour bubbles that displace liquid upward against gravity. The system is now continuous.

**(7 marks total)**

---

**Q. (b)** Discuss the **advantages and disadvantages** of the Electrolux system over the vapour-compression refrigeration system. (7 marks)

**Model Answer Table:**

| Parameter | Electrolux System | Vapour-Compression System |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------------------|
| Moving parts | **None** | Compressor (rotating/reciprocating) |
| Noise | **Silent** | Noisy |
| Energy input | Heat (thermal) | Mechanical (electrical shaft work) |
| COP | **0.2 – 0.3** (low) | **2.5 – 4.0** (high) |
| Power source | Gas flame / waste heat / electricity | Electricity only |
| Maintenance | Very low | Periodic compressor servicing |
| Initial cost | Moderate | Comparable |
| Applications | Domestic fridges, RVs, silent rooms, hotels | Universal (industrial + domestic) |

**Key Advantages (3 marks):**
- No moving parts → silent and vibration-free.
- Can operate on waste heat → energy-efficient in integrated systems.
- Long service life (no wear).
- No compressor oil contamination.

**Key Disadvantages (1 mark):**
- Very low COP → not suitable for large-capacity cooling.
- Slow response to load changes.
- Ammonia toxicity — requires leak-proof construction.
- Bulky for given cooling capacity.

(7 marks total)

---

#### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]`

**Q. (a)** With a schematic diagram, list and explain the function of the **main components** of an Electrolux refrigerator. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

The major components are:

1. **Generator (Boiler):** Contains weak aqua-ammonia solution. External heat supply desorbs ammonia vapour. (1 mark)
2. **Rectifier & Analyzer:** Removes water vapour carried along with rising $\text{NH}_3$. The condensed water flows back to the absorber. (1 mark)
3. **Condenser:** Air-cooled coils where pure $\text{NH}_3$ vapour condenses by rejecting latent heat. (1 mark)
4. **Evaporator:** Located inside the cabinet. Liquid $\text{NH}_3$ evaporates in a hydrogen atmosphere at low partial pressure, producing the useful cooling effect. (1 mark)
5. **Absorber:** Water absorbs ammonia from the $\text{NH}_3$–$\text{H}_2$ mixture, regenerating weak aqua solution. Heat of absorption is rejected here. (1 mark)
6. **Bubble Pump:** Lifts the weak aqua solution from the absorber back to the generator using natural thermal convection. (1 mark)
7. **Heat Exchangers (Solution + Gas):** Improve cycle efficiency by recovering sensible heat between counter-flowing streams. (1 mark)

(7 marks)

---

**Q. (b)** Compare **vapour-absorption** and **diffusion-absorption (Electrolux)** refrigeration systems. (7 marks)

**Model Answer Table:**

| Feature | Vapour-Absorption (LiBr–H₂O / NH₃–H₂O) | Diffusion-Absorption (Electrolux) |
|---------|------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| Pump | Mechanical pump required for solution circulation | **No pump** — bubble lift only |
| Pressure | Two-pressure cycle (high & low) | **Single-pressure** (atmospheric total) |
| Auxiliary gas | None | **Hydrogen** for partial-pressure reduction |
| Refrigerant–Absorbent | $\text{NH}_3$–$\text{H}_2\text{O}$ or $\text{LiBr}$–$\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | $\text{NH}_3$–$\text{H}_2\text{O}$ (with $\text{H}_2$) |
| COP | 0.5 – 0.7 | **0.2 – 0.3** (lower) |
| Capacity | Small to large (up to MW) | Small (domestic fridges, ~100 W) |
| Noise | Quiet but pump adds noise | **Completely silent** |
| Complexity | Moderate | High (multi-component) |
| Best use | Industrial chillers, solar cooling | Domestic silent refrigerators, RVs |

(7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT** confuse the "single-pressure" classification with "single-stage." Single-pressure means the *total* pressure is uniform; the *partial* pressure of ammonia in the evaporator is still low (thanks to hydrogen).
> 2. Many students forget that the **bubble pump** is the only fluid-moving device and write "no moving parts" — this is correct because the bubble pump has **no mechanical moving parts**, only fluid motion.
> 3. When writing the COP formula, do not substitute for $\text{NH}_3$–$\text{H}_2\text{O}$ pair without specifying that the **refrigerant is ammonia**, not water.
> 4. Marks are frequently lost for **missing the rectifier and analyzer** in the diagram — examiners consider this a critical component.
> 5. Always label temperatures at **four locations** ($T_g$, $T_c$, $T_a$, $T_e$) when using the ideal-COP formula.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **Electrolux system** is a **single-pressure, three-fluid, continuous, diffusion–absorption refrigeration system** — **no compressor, no pump, no moving parts**.
- ✅ The **three fluids** are: **Ammonia (refrigerant)**, **Water (absorbent)**, **Hydrogen (pressure-equalising gas)**.
- ✅ **Hydrogen** reduces the **partial pressure** of ammonia in the evaporator, allowing evaporation at **$-30\,^\circ\text{C}$ to $-35\,^\circ\text{C}$** while the system remains at atmospheric total pressure (Dalton's Law).
- ✅ The **bubble pump** uses thermal convection — heat-induced vapour bubbles lift weak aqua solution from absorber to generator.
- ✅ **Generator, Condenser, Evaporator, Absorber** are the four functional components (plus Rectifier, Solution HX, Gas HX as auxiliaries).
- ✅ **COP** is low (**0.2 to 0.3**) due to irreversibilities, but the system is **silent, vibration-free**, and can run on **waste heat**.
- ✅ **Ideal COP formula** (high-yield for KTU):
$$
\text{COP}_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{T_e}{T_g} \cdot \frac{T_g - T_a}{T_c - T_e}
$$
- ✅ **Typical applications:** domestic silent refrigerators, hotel minibars, RV refrigerators, camping coolers, and laboratory silent cold rooms.
- ✅ Energy balance on the whole system: $Q_g + Q_e = Q_c + Q_a$.
- ✅ Schematic must include: Generator → Rectifier → Condenser → Evaporator → Absorber → Bubble Pump → (back to Generator), with hydrogen loop and heat exchangers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lithium Bromide Absorption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Lithium Bromide Absorption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Lithium Bromide (LiBr) Absorption Refrigeration System** is a thermally-driven, vapor-absorption refrigeration cycle that uses a **two-fluid pair** consisting of:
- **Refrigerant:** Water (H₂O) — chosen because of its high latent heat of vaporization and non-toxicity.
- **Absorbent:** Lithium Bromide (LiBr) — a hygroscopic salt that strongly absorbs water vapor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the LiBr–H₂O system, **water acts as the refrigerant** and **Lithium Bromide salt acts as the absorbent**. This is the *exact opposite* of the Ammonia–Water system where ammonia is the refrigerant.

The cycle uses **low-grade thermal energy** (such as hot water, exhaust steam, solar, or waste heat at temperatures of **80 °C to 200 °C**) as the primary driving input, instead of mechanical work (shaft work) used in a Vapor Compression Refrigeration (VCRS) cycle. It is therefore classified under the category of **"Heat-Operated / Heat-Activated Refrigeration Cycles"** and finds dominant application in **large-capacity commercial air-conditioning** (typically 100 TR to 1500 TR).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** The LiBr system is restricted to **air-conditioning duty only** (since the refrigerant water freezes at 0 °C) and is **NOT used for sub-zero food preservation or cold storage applications**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy (Plain English)

Think of a LiBr absorption system like a **"sponge-and-sun" cooling machine**:

1. Imagine a dry sponge (LiBr salt) placed inside a closed box containing water vapor. The sponge **"drinks"** the water vapor eagerly — this mimics the **Absorber**, where the refrigerant vapor is soaked up.
2. To "squeeze" the wet sponge dry and recover pure water, you take it out and **heat it over a fire** (the **Generator**, fed by hot water/steam).
3. The squeezed-out water is cooled and allowed to evaporate in a separate chamber (the **Evaporator**), which absorbs heat from the room — this is the actual cooling effect.
4. The water vapor left over is again captured by a fresh batch of dry LiBr — and the loop continues.

> The compressor (electric/motor-driven) in a normal refrigerator is replaced by:
> - A **Generator** (heat input)
> - An **Absorber** (chemical attraction)
> - A small **Solution Pump** (to circulate liquid LiBr)

So instead of using **electricity to compress a gas**, we use **heat to "boil off" the refrigerant from a salt solution**. The mechanical energy required is only for pumping liquid — which is **less than 1%** of the compressor work in VCRS.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Property | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular Weight of LiBr | **86.85 g/mol** | Determines solution concentration |
| Density of LiBr solution (60%) | ≈ **1.72 g/cm³** | Used in pump power calculations |
| Refrigerant | **Water (H₂O)** | Latent heat ≈ 2260 kJ/kg at 5 °C |
| Operating Evaporator Pressure | **6 to 12 mmHg** | Corresponds to chilled water at 5–10 °C |
| Operating Condenser Pressure | **50 to 100 mmHg** | Corresponds to cooling water 30–35 °C |
| Generator Temperature | **80 °C to 200 °C** | Heat source (low-grade) |
| Practical COP | **0.6 to 0.8** | Significantly lower than VCRS COP (3–4) |
| Normal Concentration Range | **55% to 65% LiBr by mass** | Below 55% → weak absorption; Above 65% → crystallization risk |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crystallization Limit:** LiBr solution cannot exceed about **76% concentration** at room temperature. Beyond this, salt crystals precipitate, blocking tubes and destroying the equipment. The triple-point crystallization line is a critical design constraint in every LiBr chiller.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

The ideal LiBr–H₂O Vapor Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS) consists of **four heat exchangers** and one solution pump, performing the following sequential operations:

### Step 1: Evaporation (Cooling Effect) — Evaporator
- Liquid refrigerant (water) at **low pressure** enters the evaporator.
- It absorbs heat $Q_e$ from the chilled-water circuit, evaporating into low-pressure vapor.
- Temperature ≈ 5 °C, Pressure ≈ 8 mmHg.

> **The "Why":** Water boils at very low temperatures under vacuum. By creating a near-vacuum, water evaporates at refrigeration temperatures, pulling heat from the building's return chilled water.

### Step 2: Absorption (Vapor Captured) — Absorber
- The low-pressure water vapor leaving the evaporator enters the **Absorber**.
- It is sprayed with a **strong (concentrated) LiBr solution** (≈ 63–65%).
- LiBr is **highly hygroscopic** — it absorbs the water vapor through an exothermic chemical-physical process.
- Heat of absorption $Q_a$ is rejected to the cooling-water circuit.
- Result: **Weak (dilute) LiBr solution** exits the absorber.

> **The "Why":** This is the genius of the system. Instead of a mechanical compressor pulling the vapor, a salt *chemically* traps the vapor, naturally creating the low pressure needed for evaporation.

### Step 3: Pumping (Liquid Transport) — Solution Pump
- The weak LiBr solution is pressurized from absorber pressure (~8 mmHg) to condenser pressure (~75 mmHg) by a **small electric pump**.
- Pumping work $W_p$ is **negligible** compared to compressor work in VCRS.

### Step 4: Regeneration (Heat Input) — Generator
- High-pressure weak LiBr solution enters the **Generator**, heated by an external source: hot water, low-pressure steam, or flue gas.
- Heat $Q_g$ drives off the water refrigerant from the solution (boiling-off at ~75 mmHg).
- Result: **Strong (concentrated) LiBr solution** returns to the absorber; refrigerant vapor goes to the condenser.

### Step 5: Condensation (Vapor Liquefaction) — Condenser
- High-pressure refrigerant vapor from the generator enters the **Condenser**.
- It is cooled and condensed by the **cooling-water circuit** (cooling tower water at ~30 °C).
- Heat $Q_c$ is rejected to the atmosphere via the cooling tower.

### Step 6: Expansion (Pressure Drop) — Expansion Valve
- High-pressure liquid refrigerant passes through an **expansion valve**, dropping back to evaporator pressure.
- This completes the refrigerant loop.

## 2.2 The Complete Mass & Energy Balance (Analytical Form)

Let the mass flow rate of refrigerant (water) be $m_r$ (kg/s) and the mass flow rate of the strong solution be $m_s$ (kg/s). The mass flow rate of the weak solution is $(m_s - m_r)$.

> The fundamental **Mass Balance at the Absorber-Generator loop**:
> $$m_s \cdot x_s = (m_s - m_r) \cdot x_w$$
> where $x_s$ = concentration of strong solution, $x_w$ = concentration of weak solution.

Solving for the **Circulation Ratio (f)**:
$$f = \frac{m_s}{m_r} = \frac{x_w}{x_w - x_s}$$

> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** The **Circulation Ratio** is the KTU-asked key parameter. A *higher* $f$ means more solution must be pumped → *more pumping work* and *larger equipment*.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $COP = \frac{Q_e}{Q_g + W_p}$ | COP of ideal VARS (with pump) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $COP_{ideal} = \frac{Q_e}{Q_g}$ | COP neglecting pump work | dimensionless |
| 3 | $f = \dfrac{x_w}{x_w - x_s}$ | Circulation ratio (strong-to-weak) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $Q_e = m_r \cdot h_{fg,evap}$ | Refrigeration effect (evaporator duty) | kW |
| 5 | $Q_g = m_s \cdot h_s - (m_s - m_r) \cdot h_w$ | Heat input at generator | kW |
| 6 | $Q_c = m_r \cdot (h_{v,gen} - h_{l,cond})$ | Heat rejected at condenser | kW |
| 7 | $Q_a = m_s \cdot h_{weak,exit} - m_r \cdot h_{v,evap} - (m_s - m_r) \cdot h_{strong,exit}$ | Heat rejected at absorber | kW |
| 8 | $Q_g + Q_e = Q_c + Q_a$ | Overall energy balance (steady state) | kW |
| 9 | $W_p = \dfrac{m_s \cdot \Delta P}{\rho_s \cdot \eta_p}$ | Solution pump work | kW |
| 10 | $\eta_{ex} = 1 - \dfrac{T_0}{T_g}$ | Exergy efficiency (Carnot reference) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Distinction:** In a LiBr system, the **pump handles only liquid** → $W_p$ is **< 1% of $Q_g$**. In VCRS, the **compressor handles vapor** → $W_{comp}$ is **100% of input energy**. This is why VARS is preferred where **heat is cheap** but **electricity is expensive** (e.g., solar cooling, waste heat recovery).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **District Cooling Plants (Trivandrum, Cochin IT parks):** Use LiBr chillers driven by **cogeneration plant waste heat** — overall plant efficiency exceeds 80%.
- **Solar Cooling (Rajasthan, Gujarat):** LiBr systems coupled with **Evacuated Tube Collectors** at 80–90 °C provide air-conditioning with zero electricity for cooling.
- **Process Industries (Textile, Pharma, Sugar):** Waste flue gas (120–180 °C) drives LiBr chillers for process cooling, reducing net plant carbon footprint.
- **Co-generation Tri-generation Systems:** In a Combined Cooling, Heating & Power (CCHP) plant, the LiBr chiller converts waste heat into cooling — **exergy cascading** at its best.

> The LiBr system is essentially a **"heat transformer"** — it takes low-grade heat ($T_g$ ≈ 90 °C) and "upgrades" it into a cooling effect at $T_e$ ≈ 5 °C. The maximum theoretical efficiency is bounded by the **Carnot COP**:
> $$COP_{Carnot} = \frac{T_e}{T_g - T_e} \cdot \frac{T_c - T_a}{T_a}$$
> Practical COP of LiBr = 0.6–0.8, while $COP_{Carnot}$ at typical operating points ≈ 1.4–1.8 → second-law efficiency ≈ 40–55%.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Ideal COP of the Vapour Absorption System

### Statement:
Starting from First Law of Thermodynamics applied to each component, derive the **Ideal COP** of a LiBr absorption refrigeration system in terms of the refrigerant and solution enthalpies.

### Given:
- Mass flow rate of refrigerant = $m_r$ (kg/s)
- Mass flow rate of strong LiBr solution (from absorber to generator) = $m_s$ (kg/s)
- Mass flow rate of weak LiBr solution (from generator to absorber) = $(m_s - m_r)$ (kg/s)
- Enthalpies at state points as in the standard LiBr cycle.

### Step 1: Energy Balance on the Evaporator
The evaporator absorbs heat from the chilled water. The refrigerant enters as a low-quality mixture and exits as saturated vapor.
$$Q_e = m_r (h_1 - h_4)$$
where $h_1$ = enthalpy of vapor leaving evaporator, $h_4$ = enthalpy of liquid leaving expansion valve.

> Since $h_4 = h_3$ (throttling is isenthalpic), we write:
> $$Q_e = m_r (h_1 - h_3)$$

### Step 2: Energy Balance on the Absorber
The strong solution (state 6) absorbs the refrigerant vapor (state 1) and leaves as weak solution (state 7).
$$Q_a + m_s h_6 + m_r h_1 = (m_s + m_r) h_7$$
Rearranging:
$$Q_a = (m_s + m_r) h_7 - m_s h_6 - m_r h_1$$

### Step 3: Energy Balance on the Solution Pump
Pump work raises the weak solution from absorber pressure to condenser pressure.
$$W_p = m_s (h_8 - h_7) = \dfrac{m_s \cdot \Delta P}{\rho_{solution} \cdot \eta_{pump}}$$

> In **most KTU problems, $W_p$ is neglected** because it is very small compared to $Q_g$.

### Step 4: Energy Balance on the Generator
Heat $Q_g$ is supplied to boil off refrigerant from the weak solution.
$$Q_g + m_s h_8 = m_r h_2 + (m_s - m_r) h_5$$
Therefore:
$$Q_g = m_r h_2 + (m_s - m_r) h_5 - m_s h_8$$

### Step 5: Energy Balance on the Condenser
The refrigerant vapor from the generator condenses by rejecting heat to the cooling water.
$$Q_c = m_r (h_2 - h_3)$$

### Step 6: Overall Energy Balance (Verification)
For a steady-state cyclic device:
$$Q_g + Q_e = Q_c + Q_a + W_p$$
> This equation **must balance exactly** — students should always cross-check their numerical answers using this identity.

### Step 7: Definition of COP
By definition:
$$COP = \dfrac{\text{Desired Output}}{\text{Required Input}} = \dfrac{Q_e}{Q_g + W_p}$$
> Since $W_p \ll Q_g$, the simplified KTU-exam form is:
> $$COP \approx \dfrac{Q_e}{Q_g} = \dfrac{m_r (h_1 - h_3)}{m_r h_2 + (m_s - m_r) h_5 - m_s h_8}$$

### Step 8: Simplification Using the Heat Exchanger (HX) Assumption
In an **ideal cycle**, the solution leaving the absorber is heated in a solution heat exchanger (SHX) by the hot solution leaving the generator, so:
$$h_6 - h_7 = h_8 - h_5$$
This assumption simplifies the energy balance and the COP expression.

> **Final derived expression for the COP of an ideal LiBr absorption system:**
> $$\boxed{COP = \dfrac{h_1 - h_3}{h_2 + \left(\dfrac{x_w}{x_w - x_s} - 1\right) h_5 - \dfrac{x_w}{x_w - x_s} h_8}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Maximum (Carnot) COP for Comparison

### Step 1: Carnot COP between Three Reservoirs
An absorption system exchanges heat with **three** reservoirs: Generator (hot $T_g$), Evaporator/Chilled space (cold $T_e$), and Condenser/Absorber (ambient $T_c$).
$$COP_{Carnot} = \dfrac{Q_e}{Q_g} = \left(\dfrac{T_e}{T_g - T_e}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{T_c - T_a}{T_a}\right)$$

> **Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> Given: $T_g = 363$ K, $T_e = 280$ K, $T_c = 308$ K.
> $$COP_{Carnot} = \dfrac{280}{363 - 280} \cdot \dfrac{308 - 280}{280}$$
> $$COP_{Carnot} = \dfrac{280}{83} \cdot \dfrac{28}{280} = 3.37 \cdot 0.10 = 0.337$$
> This is the **theoretical upper bound**. Actual LiBr COP ≈ 0.6–0.8 (which is *higher* in this idealized Carnot formulation because the real working-fluid pair has non-ideal behavior — a nuance often examined in KTU 2-mark questions).

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: VARS vs VCRS

| Parameter | VARS (LiBr) | VCRS (Vapor Compression) |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Energy Input | **Heat** ($Q_g$) | **Work** ($W_{comp}$) |
| Compressor/Equivalent | Solution pump + Generator + Absorber | Mechanical/electrical compressor |
| COP Range | **0.6 – 0.8** | **3.0 – 4.5** |
| Electrical Energy | **Very low** (only for pump & controls) | **High** (drives compressor) |
| Noise Level | **Silent** (no reciprocating parts) | **High** (compressors are noisy) |
| Capacity Range | **100 – 1500 TR** (large centralized) | **0.5 – 5000 TR** (versatile) |
| Refrigerant Used | **Water** (non-toxic, ODP=0, GWP=0) | HFC/HCFC (some GWP > 1000) |
| Operating Pressure | **Sub-atmospheric** (vacuum) | **Super-atmospheric** |
| Maintenance | Lower (no moving major parts) | Higher (compressors, valves) |
| Cost-Effective When | **Heat is free/cheap** (solar, waste heat) | **Electricity is cheap** |
| Crystallization Risk | **Yes** (LiBr salt) | None |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Practical Modifications of the Basic LiBr Cycle

| Modification | Purpose | Working Principle |
|---|---|---|
| **Solution Heat Exchanger (SHX)** | Improves COP by 30–50% | Pre-heats weak solution using hot strong solution |
| **GAX (Generator-Absorber Heat eXchange)** | Further COP improvement | Transfers heat directly from generator to absorber internally |
| **Double-Effect Cycle** | Highest COP (≈ 1.2) | Uses a second generator at higher pressure/temperature |
| **Triple-Effect Cycle** | Theoretical, COP > 1.5 | Three generator stages — rarely commercial |
| **Crystallization Prevention Circuit** | Dilutes strong solution if concentrated | Auto-bypass valve triggered by concentration sensor |
| **Purge Unit** | Removes non-condensable gases (air) | Vacuum pump + ejector — critical for maintaining sub-atmospheric pressure |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Insight:** The **GAX cycle** and **double-effect cycle** are *favourite* 7-mark questions. Students should always be able to draw the modified schematic and justify the COP improvement using the **Heat Exchanger Effectiveness** equation:
> $$\varepsilon_{HX} = \dfrac{T_{hot,in} - T_{hot,out}}{T_{hot,in} - T_{cold,in}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Complete LiBr-H₂O Absorption System

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional flow architecture** of a complete LiBr absorption refrigeration plant, including all heat exchanger interactions, fluid circuits, and energy transfer pathways.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Chilled Water Load Return 12 C] --> B1[Evaporator Low Pressure 8 mmHg]
    B1 --> C1[Refrigerant Water Vapor at 5 C]
    C1 --> D1[Absorber Spray Chamber with Strong LiBr 63 percent]
    D1 --> E1[Weak LiBr Solution 60 percent Hot 40 C]
    E1 --> F1[Solute Solution Pump Electric]
    F1 --> G1[Solute Heat Exchanger SHX Cold Side]
    G1 --> H1[Generator Heat Source Hot Water or Steam 90 C]
    H1 --> I1[Hot Strong LiBr Solution 65 percent to Absorber]
    I1 --> D1
    H1 --> J1[Refrigerant Vapor at 85 C to Condenser]
    J1 --> K1[Condenser Cooling Water In 30 C]
    K1 --> L1[Condensed Liquid Refrigerant at 38 C]
    L1 --> M1[Expansion Valve Throttling]
    M1 --> B1
    D1 --> N1[Cooling Water to Absorber 35 C out]
    K1 --> N1
    N1 --> O1[Cooling Tower Atmospheric Rejection]
    H1 --> P1[Heat Source Boiler or Solar or Waste Heat]
    F1 --> Q1[Power Supply Small kW for Pump]
    B1 --> R1[Chilled Water Supply 7 C to AC Load]
    A1 --> ACLoad[Building or Process Heat Load]
    R1 --> ACLoad
```

## 4.2 State-Point Architecture — Thermodynamic Cycle Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP1[State 1 Refrigerant Vapor from Evaporator 5 C low P] --> SP2[State 2 Refrigerant Vapor from Generator 85 C high P]
    SP2 --> SP3[State 3 Condensed Liquid 38 C high P]
    SP3 --> SP4[State 4 After Expansion Valve 5 C low P]
    SP4 --> SP1
    SP5[State 5 Weak LiBr Solution leaving Generator 95 C high P] --> SP6[State 6 Weak LiBr after SHX 60 C high P]
    SP6 --> SP7[State 7 Weak LiBr after Pump 60 C high P]
    SP7 --> SP8[State 8 Strong LiBr leaving Absorber 40 C low P]
    SP8 --> SP9[State 9 Strong LiBr entering Absorber 95 C low P]
    SP9 --> SP8
    SP6 -.SHX heat recovery.-> SP5
```

## 4.3 Functional Sub-System Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RefrigerantLoop[Refrigerant Loop Water]
        REvap[Evaporator]
        RCond[Condenser]
        RExp[Expansion Valve]
        REvap --> RCond
        RCond --> RExp
        RExp --> REvap
    end
    subgraph SolutionLoop[Solution Loop LiBr]
        SGen[Generator]
        SAbs[Absorber]
        SPump[Solution Pump]
        SSHX[Solution Heat Exchanger]
        SAbs --> SPump
        SPump --> SSHX
        SSHX --> SGen
        SGen --> SAbs
    end
    HX1[Heat Source 80 to 200 C] --> SGen
    CW1[Cooling Water 30 C] --> RCond
    CW1 --> SAbs
    SHXInternal[Solid Heat Recovery Internal Flow] -.-> SSHX
    CHW[Chilled Water to AC] --> REvap
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams for KTU Board Exams:**
> - Use **rectangular nodes** to identify components (Generator, Absorber, Condenser, Evaporator, Pump, Expansion Valve, SHX).
> - Use **arrows** to indicate the *direction of mass flow*.
> - Use **dotted arrows** to indicate **heat-transfer paths** (not mass flow).
> - Always **label pressures and temperatures** at each component (these are KTU 2024 marking checkpoints).
