<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the role of Lithium Bromide in a vapor absorption refrigeration system. Why is water used as the refrigerant in this system?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Role of LiBr (1 Mark):** Lithium Bromide acts as the **absorbent** in the system. It is a highly hygroscopic salt that strongly absorbs water vapor, replacing the mechanical compressor's role of raising refrigerant pressure.
- **Why water as refrigerant (2 Marks):** Water is used as the refrigerant because (i) it has a **high latent heat of vaporization** (≈ 2260 kJ/kg at 5 °C) giving high refrigeration effect per kg, (ii) it is **non-toxic, non-flammable, environmentally benign** (ODP = 0, GWP = 0), and (iii) it can produce **low-temperature cooling** under vacuum conditions, ideal for air-conditioning applications.
- **Limitation (Bonus, if asked):** Water freezes at 0 °C, so this system **cannot be used for sub-zero applications** like cold storage or frozen food.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the term "Circulation Ratio" in a LiBr absorption system. What does a high circulation ratio indicate?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (2 Marks):** The Circulation Ratio ($f$) is the ratio of the mass flow rate of the **strong LiBr solution** ($m_s$) leaving the absorber to the mass flow rate of the **refrigerant** ($m_r$) evaporated in the evaporator:
$$f = \dfrac{m_s}{m_r} = \dfrac{x_w}{x_w - x_s}$$
- **Interpretation (1 Mark):** A high circulation ratio means a **large quantity of solution** must be pumped and heated to produce a unit mass of refrigerant, leading to **higher pumping work**, **larger heat exchangers**, and **lower COP**. It occurs when the concentration difference $(x_w - x_s)$ between weak and strong solutions is small.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the working of a **Lithium Bromide-Water Vapor Absorption Refrigeration System** used for air-conditioning. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A LiBr absorption chiller produces **10 TR** of cooling. The generator temperature is 95 °C, evaporator temperature is 5 °C, and condenser temperature is 35 °C. If the heat input at the generator is **50 kW**, calculate: (i) the **actual COP**, and (ii) the **Carnot COP** between the same three reservoirs. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A:

#### Part (a) — Working of the LiBr-H₂O System (7 Marks)

The system consists of **four main components** and one solution pump. Refer to Section 4.1 for the schematic.

1. **Evaporator (1 Mark):** Liquid refrigerant (water) at low pressure (≈ 8 mmHg) evaporates by absorbing heat $Q_e$ from the chilled water return from the AC load, producing a cooling effect at ~5 °C.

2. **Absorber (1 Mark):** The low-pressure refrigerant vapor enters the absorber where it is sprayed with **strong LiBr solution** (≈ 63% concentration). LiBr absorbs the water vapor exothermically; heat $Q_a$ is rejected to the cooling tower water. The solution becomes **weak/dilute** (≈ 60%).

3. **Solution Pump (1 Mark):** The weak solution is pumped from absorber pressure (~8 mmHg) to condenser pressure (~75 mmHg). Pumping work is **negligible**.

4. **Generator (2 Marks):** High-pressure weak solution is heated by an external source (hot water, steam, or solar at 80–200 °C). Water refrigerant is **boiled off** as vapor, leaving behind a **stronger LiBr solution** which returns to the absorber.

5. **Condenser & Expansion Valve (2 Marks):** The refrigerant vapor from the generator is condensed in the **condenser** by cooling water (rejecting $Q_c$). The condensed liquid is throttled through the **expansion valve** back to evaporator pressure, completing the cycle.

> The **Solution Heat Exchanger (SHX)** is an additional internal component that preheats the weak solution using the hot strong solution, improving the COP.

#### Part (b) — COP Calculations (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Refrigeration capacity $Q_e$ = 10 TR
- 1 TR = 3.517 kW, so $Q_e = 10 \times 3.517 = 35.17$ kW
- $T_g = 95 + 273 = 368$ K
- $T_e = 5 + 273 = 278$ K
- $T_c = 35 + 273 = 308$ K
- $Q_g = 50$ kW

##### (i) Actual COP (3 Marks)
$$COP_{actual} = \dfrac{Q_e}{Q_g} = \dfrac{35.17}{50}$$
$$\boxed{COP_{actual} = 0.7034}$$

##### (ii) Carnot COP (4 Marks)
$$COP_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_e}{T_g - T_e} \cdot \dfrac{T_c - T_a}{T_a}$$

Assuming ambient $T_a = T_c = 308$ K (cooling water/ambient temperature is the heat-sink reservoir):
$$COP_{Carnot} = \dfrac{278}{368 - 278} \cdot \dfrac{308 - 308}{308}$$
$$COP_{Carnot} = \dfrac{278}{90} \cdot 0 = 0$$

> **Examiner's interpretation:** When the ambient and condenser temperatures are equal (idealized case), the Carnot COP between three reservoirs requires the sink temperature to be the **ambient (atmospheric)** reference, not the condenser. Using $T_{sink} = T_{ambient} = 308$ K and treating the system as a heat engine + refrigerator combination:
> $$COP_{Carnot,max} = \dfrac{T_e}{T_g - T_e} \cdot \dfrac{T_{sink} - T_e}{T_{sink}} = \dfrac{278}{90} \cdot \dfrac{308 - 278}{308}$$
> $$COP_{Carnot,max} = 3.089 \cdot 0.0974 = 0.301$$

> **Alternative Board Answer (commonly accepted):**
> $$COP_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_e (T_g - T_{sink})}{T_g (T_{sink} - T_e)} = \dfrac{278 (368 - 308)}{368 (308 - 278)}$$
> $$COP_{Carnot} = \dfrac{278 \times 60}{368 \times 30} = \dfrac{16680}{11040} = 1.511$$

**Relative COP (Second-Law Efficiency):**
$$\eta_{II} = \dfrac{COP_{actual}}{COP_{Carnot}} = \dfrac{0.7034}{1.511} = 0.4655 = 46.55\%$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative): [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**(a)** Compare the **Vapor Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS)** and **Vapor Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS)** on the basis of: energy input, COP, working fluid, environmental impact, noise, and applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Generator-Absorber Heat Exchange (GAX) cycle** and the **Double-Effect LiBr cycle**. State how each improves the COP of the basic cycle. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B:

#### Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 Marks)

| Parameter | VARS (LiBr) | VCRS (Standard) | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy Input** | Low-grade heat ($Q_g$) + negligible pump work | High-grade electrical/mechanical work | 1 |
| **COP** | 0.6 – 0.8 | 3.0 – 4.5 | 1 |
| **Refrigerant** | Water (H₂O) | HFC-134a / HFC-410A | 1 |
| **Absorbent** | LiBr salt | Not applicable | 1 |
| **Environmental Impact** | ODP=0, GWP=0, non-toxic | Some HFCs have GWP > 1000 | 1 |
| **Noise Level** | Silent (no compressor) | High (compressors) | 1 |
| **Applications** | Large centralized AC (100–1500 TR) | Domestic + commercial (wide range) | 1 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Comparison Questions:**
> Students commonly write only **3–4 points** and lose marks. KTU Board Examiners award **1 mark per *distinct, correctly-stated* comparison point**. To score full 7 marks, you must list **at least 7 distinct comparison parameters**, with at least one sentence of justification each. A tabular format is **strongly recommended** to maximize scoring.

#### Part (b) — GAX and Double-Effect Cycles (7 Marks)

##### GAX Cycle (3.5 Marks)
- The **Generator-Absorber Heat Exchange (GAX)** is an advanced modification in which a **special internal heat exchanger** transfers heat directly from the **hot generator** to the **cooler absorber**.
- In the basic cycle, the heat rejected at the absorber ($Q_a$) is wasted to the cooling tower. In GAX, this heat is *partially recovered* to assist the generator, reducing the external $Q_g$ required.
- The absorber pressure is slightly raised to allow temperature overlap between the hot strong solution (from generator) and the cool strong solution (entering absorber).
- **COP improvement:** Approximately **20–40%** higher than the basic single-effect cycle.

##### Double-Effect Cycle (3.5 Marks)
- In a **double-effect LiBr chiller**, the **vapor generated in the first (low-pressure) generator is used as the heat source for a second (high-pressure) generator**.
- The refrigerant boils **twice**, producing a "double-stage" separation — first at moderate temperature, then at higher temperature.
- This cascading boiling concentrates the solution more efficiently, requiring less external heat.
- **COP improvement:** Reaches **1.0 – 1.4**, approximately **2×** the basic single-effect LiBr cycle.
- **Disadvantage:** Higher generator temperature (≈ 150–200 °C) required, more complex controls, higher capital cost.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Cycle Modifications:**
> Many students confuse GAX with Double-Effect. Remember:
> - **GAX = internal heat recovery** between generator and absorber.
> - **Double-Effect = external staging** with two generators at different pressures.
> Drawing the **modified schematic** with clearly labeled pressures and temperatures is worth **at least 2 marks** in any 7-mark cycle-modification question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Memorize These 12 Points:**

1. **Refrigerant in LiBr system = Water; Absorbent = LiBr salt** (opposite of NH₃-H₂O system).
2. **Operating pressure = Sub-atmospheric** (vacuum); evaporator ≈ 8 mmHg, condenser ≈ 75 mmHg.
3. **No mechanical compressor** — replaced by Generator + Absorber + Solution Pump.
4. **Pump work is negligible** (< 1% of $Q_g$); the energy input is essentially **heat only**.
5. **Practical COP = 0.6 to 0.8** (single-effect); up to 1.2 (double-effect); up to 1.5 (GAX + double-effect).
6. **Circulation Ratio** $f = \dfrac{x_w}{x_w - x_s}$ — higher $f$ means lower COP.
7. **Crystallization risk** above 65% concentration — auto-dilution circuit mandatory in commercial units.
8. **Cannot be used for sub-zero applications** (water freezes at 0 °C) — **air-conditioning only**.
9. **COP formula:** $COP = Q_e / Q_g$; Carnot bound: $COP_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_e}{T_g - T_e} \cdot \dfrac{T_c - T_a}{T_a}$.
10. **Solution Heat Exchanger (SHX)** improves COP by 30–50% — must be shown in any schematic answer.
11. **Uses low-grade heat** (80–200 °C) — ideal for solar cooling, waste-heat recovery, and CHP/CCHP plants.
12. **Applications:** District cooling, solar air-conditioning, hospital & hotel HVAC, process cooling in textile & pharma industries.

> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Always draw the **flow schematic first** (worth 3–4 marks), label all **state points with temperature and pressure** (worth 1–2 marks), and then **derive the COP expression symbolically** (worth 2–3 marks) before substituting numbers (worth 2–3 marks). This sequence guarantees maximum partial credit even if numerical mistakes occur.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Absorption Type Solar and Steam Jet Refrigeration Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Absorption Type Solar and Steam Jet Refrigeration Systems ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Absorption Type Refrigeration System — Formal Definition An **Absorption Refrigeration System (ARS)** is a thermally-driven refrigeration cycle in which the mechanical work input of a conventional vapor compression system is replaced by **thermal energy** supplied to a generator. Compression of the refrigerant vapor is achieved through a thermo-chemical process involving an **absorbent** that has a strong affinity for the refrigerant, followed by regeneration of the refrigerant in the generator via external heat input. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Absorption refrigeration systems (NH₃–H₂O and LiBr–H₂O pairs), solar-assisted absorption refrigeration, and steam jet (ejector) refrigeration systems are core topics for KTU Board Examinations under the 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned, Outcome-Based Education). **Working Pair (Refrigerant–Absorbent):** - **NH₃–H₂O pair** — Ammonia is the refrigerant; Water is the absorbent. (Used for sub-zero applications, typically below 0 °C). - **LiBr–H₂O pair** — Water is the refrigerant; Lithium Bromide is the absorbent. (Used for air-conditioning applications, above 0 °C). ### 1.2 Solar Absorption Refrigeration — Formal Definition A **Solar Absorption Refrigeration System** is an absorption refrigeration cycle where the thermal energy required at the **generator** is supplied by **concentrated solar radiation** (using parabolic trough collectors, evacuated tube collectors, or compound parabolic concentrators) instead of fossil-fuel combustion. The solar collector replaces the boiler of a conventional absorption system. > [!NOTE] > **Why solar absorption?** The Coefficient of Performance (COP) of single-effect absorption systems is low (0.6–0.8). However, since the input is *low-grade thermal energy* (waste heat, geothermal, or solar), the **exergy efficiency** is competitive, and the cycle is attractive for off-grid, eco-friendly cooling. ### 1.3 Steam Jet Refrigeration System (SJRS) — Formal Definition A **Steam Jet Refrigeration System** is an open or closed-cycle cooling system that uses a **high-pressure motive steam** (or high-pressure vapor) to entrain and compress a secondary vapor (water vapor) from the evaporator, thereby creating the low pressure required for evaporation and cooling. The compression device is a **convergent-divergent ejector (steam jet)** rather than a mechanical compressor. ### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Sponge and the Squeezer" (Absorption):** > Imagine a wet sponge (the **absorbent** soaked with refrigerant). The sponge is gently squeezed (in the **generator**, using heat) so that the liquid drips out as pure refrigerant vapor. The dry sponge is then dipped into a pool of weak solution (the **absorber**), where it greedily soaks up refrigerant vapor from the evaporator — creating cooling. A small pump (lifting the weak solution back to the generator) replaces a heavy mechanical compressor. **Heat does the work, not electricity.** > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Cyclist and the Tailwind" (Steam Jet):** > Think of a fast-moving cyclist (high-pressure motive steam) drafting behind a slow jogger (low-pressure vapor from the evaporator). The cyclist pulls the jogger along using suction, raising the jogger's pressure. Similarly, high-velocity steam in the ejector entrains the evaporator vapor, compresses it, and discharges into the condenser. **No moving parts in the "compressor" — only fluid momentum.** ### 1.5 Key Performance Metrics (Standard References) - **COP (Coefficient of Performance):** For absorption systems, **COP = Q_E / Q_G**, where Q_E is the refrigeration effect and Q_G is the heat supplied to the generator. - **Performance Ratio (PR):** PR = COP_actual / COP_Carnot, typically **0.3 – 0.5** for single-stage absorption systems. - **Generator Temperature:** **80 – 200 °C** for NH₃–H₂O; **70 – 95 °C** for LiBr–H₂O. - **Solar Collector Operating Temperature:** **120 – 200 °C** (medium-temperature concentrators needed for NH₃–H₂O); **70 – 90 °C** (flat-plate collectors suffice for LiBr–H₂O). - **Motive Steam Pressure (SJRS):** Typically **3 – 10 bar**; entrainment ratio of ejector **μ ≈ 0.5 – 1.5**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Components of an Absorption Refrigeration System 1. **Generator (Boiler):** Receives external heat (Q_G) and separates the refrigerant from the strong solution by boiling off the refrigerant vapor. 2. **Condenser:** High-pressure refrigerant vapor rejects heat (Q_C) to the surroundings and condenses to a saturated liquid. 3. **Expansion Valve (High-Side):** Reduces pressure of condensed refrigerant from condenser pressure to evaporator pressure. 4. **Evaporator:** Low-pressure liquid refrigerant absorbs heat (Q_E) from the cold space and evaporates, producing the cooling effect. 5. **Absorber:** Low-pressure refrigerant vapor is absorbed by the weak solution (dilute absorbent), forming a strong solution. Heat of absorption (Q_A) is rejected to the surroundings. 6. **Solution Pump:** Pumps the strong solution from the absorber (low pressure) to the generator (high pressure) — this is the only significant **work input** of the cycle. 7. **Expansion Valve (Low-Side / Solution Valve):** Reduces pressure of the hot weak solution leaving the generator before it enters the absorber (recovers expansion work as a flash effect). 8. **Heat Exchanger (Solution Heat Exchanger — SHX):** Preheats the strong solution going to the generator using the hot weak solution leaving the generator. This **greatly improves COP**. ### 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic | Step | Component | Process | Description | |------|-----------|---------|-------------| | 1 | Absorber | Absorption | Refrigerant vapor absorbed by weak solution → strong solution formed. Heat Q_A rejected. | | 2 | Pump | Pumping | Strong solution pumped to generator pressure (small work input W_P). | | 3 | SHX | Heat Transfer | Strong solution pre-heated by hot weak solution from generator. | | 4 | Generator | Boiling | Heat Q_G supplied (solar/fossil) drives refrigerant out of strong solution. | | 5 | Condenser | Condensation | Refrigerant vapor condenses at high pressure, rejecting Q_C. | | 6 | Expansion Valve | Throttling | Liquid refrigerant throttled to evaporator pressure. | | 7 | Evaporator | Evaporation | Refrigerant evaporates by absorbing Q_E from cold space (cooling effect). | | 8 | Absorber | Absorption | Refrigerant vapor returns to absorber — cycle repeats. | ### 2.3 Maximum (Reversible) COP of an Absorption System For a totally reversible absorption refrigeration cycle operating between four reservoirs (T_L, T_A, T_G, T_0 = T_C), the **Maximum COP** is given by the Carnot-equivalent expression: $$ \text{COP}_{\max, \text{abs}} = \left( \frac{T_L}{T_0 - T_L} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{T_G - T_A}{T_G} \right) $$ Where: - $T_L$ = Evaporator (cold space) temperature, **K** - $T_0$ = Ambient (condenser + absorber) temperature, **K** - $T_G$ = Generator temperature, **K** - $T_A$ = Absorber temperature, **K** > [!IMPORTANT] > The product form of this equation reveals the **fundamental insight**: the absorption cycle behaves like a **cascade of two Carnot cycles** — one producing refrigeration (cold to ambient) and one consuming heat (ambient to generator). ### 2.4 Actual COP — Energy Balance Applying the **First Law of Thermodynamics** to the entire cycle (steady-state, neglecting pump work and heat exchanger losses): $$ Q_G + Q_E = Q_C + Q_A $$ The actual COP is: $$ \text{COP}_{\text{actual}} = \frac{Q_E}{Q_G + W_P} $$ Where $W_P$ is the small pump work. For practical analysis, $W_P$ is often neglected, and: $$ \text{COP} \approx \frac{Q_E}{Q_G} = \frac{m_R (h_{fg})_{\text{evap}}}{m_R h_{fg,\text{gen}} \cdot f} $$ Where $f$ is the **circulation ratio** (kg of strong solution circulated per kg of refrigerant generated). ### 2.5 Steam Jet Refrigeration — Components & Cycle | Component | Function | |-----------|----------| | **Boiler / Steam Source** | Generates high-pressure motive steam (3–10 bar). | | **Ejector (Steam Jet)** | High-velocity steam creates low pressure in suction chamber; entrains secondary vapor. | | **Evaporator** | Water evaporates at low pressure (~5–20 mbar), producing chilled water at **5–15 °C**. | | **Condenser** | Mixed motive + entrained vapor condenses; rejected to cooling tower. | | **Cooling Tower** | Rejects condenser heat. | | **Pump** | Returns condensed water back to boiler. | **Open vs. Closed SJRS:** - **Open SJRS:** Make-up water is supplied continuously; chilled water is produced directly. - **Closed SJRS:** Chilled water circulates in a closed loop; refrigerant (water) is sealed in the cycle. ### 2.6 Steam Jet Refrigeration — Key Formulae The **Coefficient of Performance** of SJRS: $$ \text{COP}_{\text{SJRS}} = \frac{Q_E}{Q_{\text{steam}} + W_P} $$ Since the ejector work is supplied by motive steam, the ratio is often expressed as: $$ \text{COP} = \frac{m_w (h_{f,\text{evap out}} - h_{f,\text{chilled out}})}{m_s (h_{g,\text{steam}} - h_{f,\text{cond out}})} $$ Where $m_w$ = mass flow rate of chilled water, $m_s$ = mass flow rate of motive steam. **Ejector Entrainment Ratio:** $$ \mu = \frac{m_s}{m_e} = \frac{\text{Entrained mass}}{\text{Motive steam mass}} \approx 0.5 \text{ to } 1.5 $$ ### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $\text{COP}_{\text{abs}} = \dfrac{Q_E}{Q_G + W_P}$ | Actual COP of absorption system | — | | 2 | $\text{COP}_{\max} = \left(\dfrac{T_L}{T_0 - T_L}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{T_G - T_A}{T_G}\right)$ | Maximum (reversible) COP | — | | 3 | $Q_E = m_R (h_{fg})_{\text{evap}}$ | Refrigeration effect | kW | | 4 | $Q_G = m_R \cdot f \cdot (h_{\text{strong,out}} - h_{\text{strong,in}})$ | Heat input at generator | kW | | 5 | $f = \dfrac{x_R}{x_S - x_W}$ | Circulation ratio (mass strong soln / mass refrigerant) | — | | 6 | $\text{COP}_{\text{VCRS}} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | Reference VCR cycle COP | — | | 7 | $\text{COP}_{\text{SJRS}} = \dfrac{Q_E}{Q_s}$ | COP of steam jet system (pump work neglected) | — | | 8 | $\mu = \dfrac{m_e}{m_s}$ | Ejector entrainment ratio | — | | 9 | $\eta_{\text{ex}} = \dfrac{\text{Exergy of cooling}}{\text{Exergy of heat input}}$ | Exergy efficiency (solar ARS) | — | | 10 | $P_{\text{ejector}} = \dfrac{2}{\gamma + 1}^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$ | Critical pressure ratio (converging nozzle) | bar | ### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility - **NH₃–H₂O Absorption Systems:** Industrial cold storage, ice plants, food processing warehouses, fisheries, breweries. Capacity: 10 kW to 10 MW. - **LiBr–H₂O Absorption Systems:** Large commercial building air-conditioning (hotels, hospitals, offices). Operates on waste steam, hot water, or solar thermal input. - **Solar Absorption:** Off-grid cold storage for vaccines, dairy, agricultural produce in rural areas. WHO-endorsed for **Solar Direct-Drive (SDD) vaccine refrigerators**. - **Steam Jet Refrigeration:** Process industries requiring chilled water (3–20 °C) above 0 °C, e.g., plastics, chemicals, air-separation plants. Capacity: 50 TR to 5000 TR. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum COP of an Absorption Refrigeration Cycle **Statement:** Show that the maximum COP of a totally reversible absorption refrigeration system is given by the product of two Carnot efficiencies. **Assumptions:** - The system operates reversibly between four thermal reservoirs. - Refrigerant cycle: Cold space at $T_L$, ambient at $T_0$. - Solution cycle: Ambient at $T_0$, generator at $T_G$, absorber at $T_A$. **Derivation:** Consider the overall absorption cycle as a combination of two Carnot cycles operating in series: - **Cycle 1 (Refrigeration):** Operates between $T_L$ (cold) and $T_0$ (hot). Carnot COP: $$ \text{COP}_{\text{Carnot,1}} = \frac{T_L}{T_0 - T_L} $$ - **Cycle 2 (Heat Pump — drives refrigerant separation):** Operates between $T_0$ (cold) and $T_G$ (hot), with $T_A$ representing the absorber heat rejection. Its thermal COP: $$ \eta_{\text{thermal,2}} = \frac{T_G - T_A}{T_G} $$ For a totally reversible system, the heat $Q_G$ supplied to the generator is the heat input. The refrigeration effect $Q_E$ is produced by this heat input through a reversible combination of these two cycles. Hence: $$ \text{COP}_{\max} = \frac{Q_E}{Q_G} = \left( \frac{Q_E}{Q_0} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{Q_0}{Q_G} \right) $$ where $Q_0$ is the heat rejected at the ambient temperature $T_0$ in the absorption process. Applying Carnot efficiency to each sub-cycle: $$ \frac{Q_E}{Q_0} = \frac{T_L}{T_0 - T_L} $$ $$ \frac{Q_0}{Q_G} = \frac{T_G - T_A}{T_G} $$ Multiplying: $$ \boxed{\text{COP}_{\max} = \left( \frac{T_L}{T_0 - T_L} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{T_G - T_A}{T_G} \right)} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **theoretical upper bound**. KTU Board Examinations often test the comparison between this $\text{COP}_{\max}$ and the actual COP obtained from energy balance. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Actual COP from Energy & Mass Balance (NH₃–H₂O System) **Given:** A single-effect NH₃–H₂O absorption system produces 10 TR of cooling. Generator temperature $T_G = 120°C$ ($393$ K), Condenser $T_C = 30°C$ ($303$ K), Absorber $T_A = 30°C$ ($303$ K), Evaporator $T_E = -10°C$ ($263$ K). Concentrations: weak solution $x_W = 0.30$, strong solution $x_S = 0.40$, refrigerant purity $x_R = 1.0$. Solution flow rate $m_S = 100$ kg/h. Find: COP, Q_G, and compare with COP_max. **Step 1 — Mass Balance (Refrigerant Mass Flow Rate):** For 1 TR = 3.517 kW, $Q_E = 10 \times 3.517 = 35.17$ kW. $$ m_R = \frac{Q_E}{h_{fg,\text{evap}}} = \frac{35.17}{1320 \text{ kJ/kg (at } -10°C \text{)}} = 0.0266 \text{ kg/s} = 95.9 \text{ kg/h} $$ **Step 2 — Circulation Ratio:** $$ f = \frac{x_R}{x_S - x_W} = \frac{1.0}{0.40 - 0.30} = 10 $$ **Step 3 — Heat Input at Generator (Energy Balance):** Using enthalpy of strong solution at generator outlet (assume $h_{\text{strong,out}} = 350$ kJ/kg) and inlet ($h_{\text{strong,in}} = 200$ kJ/kg): $$ Q_G = m_R \cdot f \cdot (h_{\text{strong,out}} - h_{\text{strong,in}}) = 0.0266 \times 10 \times (350 - 200) = 39.9 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 4 — Actual COP:** $$ \text{COP}_{\text{actual}} = \frac{Q_E}{Q_G} = \frac{35.17}{39.9} = 0.882 $$ **Step 5 — Maximum COP (Reversible):** $$ \text{COP}_{\max} = \left( \frac{263}{303 - 263} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{393 - 303}{393} \right) = \left( \frac{263}{40} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{90}{393} \right) = 6.575 \times 0.229 = 1.506 $$ **Step 6 — Performance Ratio (Second-Law Efficiency):** $$ \text{PR} = \frac{\text{COP}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{COP}_{\max}} = \frac{0.882}{1.506} = 0.586 \;\; (58.6\%) $$ **Analytical Matrix:** | Quantity | Value | Notes | |----------|-------|-------| | Refrigeration load, $Q_E$ | **35.17 kW** (10 TR) | Standard conversion | | Refrigerant flow, $m_R$ | **95.9 kg/h** | From evaporator enthalpy | | Circulation ratio, $f$ | **10** | Concentration-driven | | Heat input, $Q_G$ | **39.9 kW** | From generator energy balance | | Actual COP | **0.882** | $Q_E / Q_G$ | | Maximum (Carnot) COP | **1.506** | Reversible upper bound | | Performance Ratio | **58.6%** | Indicates significant irreversibility | --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Steam Jet Refrigeration — Cooling Water Outlet Temperature **Given:** SJRS produces 100 TR of cooling. Evaporator pressure = 0.0085 MPa (saturated at 5 °C). Condenser pressure = 0.007 MPa absolute (corresponds to ~38 °C wet bulb). Motive steam at 7 bar, 200 °C, mass flow = 0.5 kg/s. Entrainment ratio μ = 1.0. Find: chilled water outlet temperature. **Step 1 — Entrained Mass:** $$ m_e = \mu \cdot m_s = 1.0 \times 0.5 = 0.5 \text{ kg/s} $$ **Step 2 — Total Mass Through Condenser:** $$ m_{\text{total}} = m_s + m_e = 0.5 + 0.5 = 1.0 \text{ kg/s} $$ **Step 3 — Total Energy Balance (Steady-State):** Energy in = Energy out: $$ m_s h_s + m_e h_e + Q_E = m_{\text{cond}} h_{\text{cond}} + Q_C $$ Assuming adiabatic ejector and condenser: $$ m_s h_s + m_e h_e + m_w c_p (T_{\text{chilled,in}} - T_{\text{chilled,out}}) = m_{\text{cond}} h_{\text{cond}} $$ **Step 4 — Enthalpy Lookup (Steam Tables):** - $h_s$ at 7 bar, 200 °C: $h_s = 2844.8$ kJ/kg - $h_e$ at 0.0085 MPa (saturated vapor at 5 °C): $h_e = 2510.0$ kJ/kg - $h_{\text{cond}}$ at 0.007 MPa (saturated liquid at ~38 °C): $h_{\text{cond}} = 159.2$ kJ/kg - $c_p$ of water = **4.18 kJ/kg·K** - $T_{\text{chilled,in}} = 15°C$, chilled water flow $m_w = ?$ **Step 5 — Solving for Chilled Water Outlet Temperature:** For 100 TR = 351.7 kW: $$ 351.7 = m_w \times 4.18 \times (15 - T_{\text{out}}) $$ We need to iterate since the chilled water flow depends on the energy balance. After energy balance, $T_{\text{out}} \approx 5°C$, hence: $$ m_w = \frac{351.7}{4.18 \times 10} = 8.41 \text{ kg/s} $$ **Step 6 — Verify Mass Balance:** Mass of condensed water returning to boiler = $m_{\text{total}} = 1.0$ kg/s (in closed cycle). **Analytical Matrix:** | Parameter | Value | Unit | |-----------|-------|------| | Motive steam mass, $m_s$ | **0.5** | kg/s | | Entrained vapor mass, $m_e$ | **0.5** | kg/s | | Cooling capacity, $Q_E$ | **351.7** | kW | | Chilled water mass flow, $m_w$ | **8.41** | kg/s | | Chilled water outlet, $T_{\text{out}}$ | **≈ 5** | °C | | COP of SJRS | **0.45 – 0.55** | typical | > [!NOTE] > KTU Board Examination tip: When solving SJRS problems, always draw a clear block diagram showing motive steam, entrained vapor, condenser cooling water, and chilled water streams. Mark enthalpy points 1 through 7 and apply SFEE to each component. ## 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** State the working principle of a single-effect NH₃–H₂O absorption refrigeration system. Why is a solution heat exchanger (SHX) used in practice? **Model Answer:** The NH₃–H₂O absorption system uses **ammonia as refrigerant** and **water as absorbent**. Low-pressure ammonia vapor leaving the evaporator is absorbed by weak (dilute) aqua-ammonia solution in the **absorber**, forming strong (rich) aqua-ammonia solution. This strong solution is pumped to the **generator**, where external heat (Q_G) drives off ammonia vapor, which is then condensed in the **condenser**, throttled, and re-evaporated. The hot weak solution leaving the generator is throttled back to the absorber, completing the cycle. A **Solution Heat Exchanger (SHX)** is used to **preheat the strong solution** (going to generator) by the **hot weak solution** (leaving generator). This **reduces Q_G required at the generator** and **reduces heat rejected at the absorber Q_A**, thereby **increasing the COP by 30–60%**. It is a passive, high-impact energy-recovery device. *(3 marks — KTU key expects: 1 mark for working pair, 1 mark for cycle flow, 1 mark for SHX benefit.)* > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** With a neat sketch, explain the working of a steam jet refrigeration system. State its main limitation. **Model Answer:** A steam jet refrigeration system uses a **convergent-divergent ejector** as the compressor. **High-pressure motive steam** (3–10 bar) from a boiler is expanded through the converging nozzle of the ejector, emerging at very high velocity (supersonic). This creates a **low-pressure region** in the suction chamber, which draws in **secondary water vapor** from the evaporator (where chilled water is being cooled). The motive steam entrains the secondary vapor, and the mixture is compressed in the diffuser section of the ejector and discharged into the **condenser**, where it is condensed using cooling tower water. The condensate is pumped back to the boiler. **Main Limitation:** The COP is **very low (0.4 – 0.7)** because a large mass of high-grade motive steam is required to entrain a small mass of secondary vapor. It is economically viable only where **low-cost waste steam is available** and chilled water is required (not sub-zero cooling, as the refrigerant is water, freezing at 0 °C). *(3 marks — 1 mark sketch/labeling, 1 mark cycle, 1 mark limitation.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** > **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model — Part B Choice A]** > > **(a)** A single-effect NH₃–H₂O absorption refrigeration system operates between the following temperatures: Evaporator $T_E = -10°C$, Condenser $T_C = 30°C$, Absorber $T_A = 30°C$, Generator $T_G = 130°C$. Determine the **maximum (reversible) COP** of the system. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** For an actual system, the refrigeration load is **20 TR** and the heat input at the generator is **80 kW**. Calculate the **actual COP**, the **performance ratio (second-law efficiency)**, and the **heat rejected at the absorber + condenser combined**. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution to Q.A(a):** **Given (Convert to Kelvin):** - $T_L = T_E = -10 + 273 = 263$ K - $T_0 = T_C = T_A = 30 + 273 = 303$ K - $T_G = 130 + 273 = 403$ K - $T_A = 303$ K (same as $T_0$) **Formula:** $$ \text{COP}_{\max} = \left( \frac{T_L}{T_0 - T_L} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{T_G - T_A}{T_G} \right) $$ **Substitution:** $$ \text{COP}_{\max} = \left( \frac{263}{303 - 263} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{403 - 303}{403} \right) $$ $$ \text{COP}_{\max} = \left( \frac{263}{40} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{100}{403} \right) $$ $$ \text{COP}_{\max} = 6.575 \times 0.2481 $$ $$ \boxed{\text{COP}_{\max} = 1.631} $$ --- **Model Solution to Q.A(b):** **Given:** - $Q_E = 20$ TR $= 20 \times 3.517 = 70.34$ kW - $Q_G = 80$ kW **Step 1 — Actual COP:** $$ \text{COP}_{\text{actual}} = \frac{Q_E}{Q_G} = \frac{70.34}{80} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{COP}_{\text{actual}} = 0.879} $$ **Step 2 — Performance Ratio:** $$ \text{PR} = \frac{\text{COP}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{COP}_{\max}} = \frac{0.879}{1.631} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{PR} = 0.539 \;\; (53.9\%)} $$ **Step 3 — Heat Rejected:** By overall energy balance (neglecting pump work): $$ Q_{\text{rejected}} = Q_E + Q_G = 70.34 + 80 $$ $$ \boxed{Q_{\text{rejected}} = 150.34 \text{ kW}} $$ This is split between the condenser (Q_C) and absorber (Q_A), with Q_A typically being the larger share due to the heat of absorption released. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** > **Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model — Part B Choice B]** > > **(a)** Explain the working of a **LiBr–H₂O absorption refrigeration system** with a neat block diagram. State why water cannot be used as the absorbent in a system designed to produce sub-zero cooling. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A solar absorption refrigeration system uses **LiBr–H₂O pair** with $T_E = 5°C$, $T_C = 35°C$, $T_G = 85°C$, $T_A = 35°C$. Solar collector area = **50 m²**, solar flux = **700 W/m²**, collector efficiency = **0.55**. Find the **maximum refrigeration capacity** (in TR) that can be achieved, and the **COP_max**. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution to Q.B(a):** In a **LiBr–H₂O absorption system**, **water acts as the refrigerant** and **Lithium Bromide (LiBr) salt solution acts as the absorbent**. The cycle is identical in principle to the NH₃–H₂O system: - **Generator:** External heat (solar / hot water / waste steam) drives water vapor out of the weak LiBr solution. - **Condenser:** Water vapor condenses at high pressure, rejecting heat to ambient. - **Evaporator:** Liquid water evaporates at low pressure (~5–10 °C) producing chilled water. - **Absorber:** Water vapor is absorbed by strong LiBr solution; heat of absorption rejected to cooling water. **Why water cannot be used as absorbent for sub-zero cooling:** If water is used as the **absorbent** in an absorption system meant to produce sub-zero cooling, the **refrigerant must evaporate at temperatures below 0 °C**. Since water **freezes at 0 °C**, it cannot be used as a refrigerant for sub-zero applications. Therefore, **NH₃ (which boils at –33.3 °C at 1 atm) is used as the refrigerant**, with water as the absorbent. Conversely, in the **LiBr–H₂O system**, water IS the refrigerant and LiBr (which is highly hygroscopic and has very low vapor pressure) is the absorbent — but this system is limited to **chilled water applications above 0 °C** (typically 5–10 °C chilled water), which is sufficient for **air-conditioning** purposes. --- **Model Solution to Q.B(b):** **Step 1 — Solar Heat Input to Generator:** $$ Q_G = A \times I \times \eta_{\text{coll}} = 50 \times 700 \times 0.55 $$ $$ Q_G = 19{,}250 \text{ W} = 19.25 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 2 — Maximum COP (Reversible):** Convert all temperatures to Kelvin: - $T_L = 5 + 273 = 278$ K - $T_0 = T_C = T_A = 35 + 273 = 308$ K - $T_G = 85 + 273 = 358$ K $$ \text{COP}_{\max} = \left( \frac{T_L}{T_0 - T_L} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{T_G - T_A}{T_G} \right) $$ $$ \text{COP}_{\max} = \left( \frac{278}{308 - 278} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{358 - 308}{358} \right) $$ $$ \text{COP}_{\max} = \left( \frac{278}{30} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{50}{358} \right) $$ $$ \text{COP}_{\max} = 9.267 \times 0.1397 $$ $$ \boxed{\text{COP}_{\max} = 1.294} $$ **Step 3 — Maximum Refrigeration Capacity:** $$ Q_E = \text{COP}_{\max} \times Q_G = 1.294 \times 19.25 $$ $$ Q_E = 24.91 \text{ kW} $$ Converting to Tons of Refrigeration (1 TR = 3.517 kW): $$ Q_E = \frac{24.91}{3.517} $$ $$ \boxed{Q_E = 7.08 \text{ TR}} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting Kelvin Conversion:** Students frequently plug Celsius values directly into the COP formula, yielding drastically wrong answers. **Always convert °C to K** by adding 273.15. > 2. **Confusing $T_0$ and $T_C$:** In the maximum COP formula, $T_0$ is the **ambient** temperature (assumed equal to $T_C = T_A$ for typical single-effect systems). Mixing these up costs 2–3 marks. > 3. **Ignoring the Solution Heat Exchanger (SHX):** When computing actual COP from energy balance, the SHX reduces the effective Q_G. Students often use uncorrected generator enthalpies, leading to overestimation of Q_G and underestimation of COP. > 4. **Wrong Pair Selection:** NH₃–H₂O for **sub-zero** applications; LiBr–H₂O for **AC (above 0 °C)**. Reversing this is a classic KTU mistake. > 5. **Steam Jet — assuming sub-zero capability:** SJRS uses water as refrigerant, so **minimum chilled water is ~3–5 °C**, not sub-zero. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this. > 6. **Skipping the Block Diagram:** Even with correct math, a missing or poorly labeled block diagram costs at least 1 mark in KTU ESE Part B. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Absorption, Solar & Steam Jet Refrigeration** - [ ] **ARS Principle:** Heat replaces mechanical work; thermal compressor = Absorber + Generator + Pump + SHX. - [ ] **Two Main Pairs:** * **NH₃–H₂O** → Refrigerant: NH₃; Absorbent: H₂O. **Sub-zero** applications. * **LiBr–H₂O** → Refrigerant: H₂O; Absorbent: LiBr. **Air-conditioning** (above 0 °C). - [ ] **Maximum COP (Reversible):** $\text{COP}_{\max} = \left(\frac{T_L}{T_0 - T_L}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{T_G - T_A}{T_G}\right)$ — convert all T to Kelvin. - [ ] **Actual COP:** $\text{COP}_{\text{actual}} = Q_E / Q_G$ (neglecting pump work). - [ ] **Performance Ratio (Second-Law Efficiency):** $\text{PR} = \text{COP}_{\text{actual}} / \text{COP}_{\max}$ — typically 30–60%. - [ ] **Circulation Ratio:** $f = \dfrac{x_R}{x_S - x_W}$ — where $x_R$ = refrigerant purity, $x_S$ = strong solution concentration, $x_W$ = weak solution concentration. - [ ] **SHX Function:** Preheats strong solution, cools weak solution — **increases COP by 30–60%**. - [ ] **Energy Balance:** $Q_G + Q_E = Q_C + Q_A$ (steady state, no work terms). - [ ] **Solar ARS — Key Feature:** Solar collector replaces boiler. NH₃–H₂O needs medium-temperature CPC (120–200 °C); LiBr–H₂O works with flat-plate / low-temperature collectors (70–90 °C). - [ ] **Solar Collector Heat:** $Q_G = A \times I \times \eta_{\text{coll}}$, where $A$ = area (m²), $I$ = solar flux (W/m²), $\eta$ = collector efficiency. - [ ] **SJRS Components:** Boiler → Ejector (steam jet) → Evaporator (chilled water) → Condenser → Cooling Tower → Pump → Boiler. - [ ] **SJRS Ejector:** Convergent-divergent nozzle; motive steam creates low pressure via supersonic expansion; entrains secondary vapor. - [ ] **SJRS Entrainment Ratio:** $\mu = m_e / m_s \approx 0.5 - 1.5$. - [ ] **SJRS Limitation:** Low COP (0.4–0.7); minimum chilled water ~3–5 °C; requires waste steam for economy. - [ ] **Standard Conversions:** 1 TR = 3.517 kW; T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15. - [ ] **Compare COP's:** VCRS (2.5–4.0) > ARS (0.5–1.2) > SJRS (0.4–0.7) — but ARS/SJRS use **low-grade or free heat**, so exergy-wise they can be competitive. - [ ] **Generator Temperature:** NH₃–H₂O: 80–200 °C; LiBr–H₂O: 70–95 °C. - [ ] **Absorber Heat Rejection:** $Q_A$ is typically the largest heat term due to the **heat of absorption** of the refrigerant into the absorbent.

topics.title: Common Refrigerants - Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Common Refrigerants - Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Refrigerant:** A refrigerant is a heat-absorbent working fluid that undergoes a phase-change cycle (typically liquid $\leftrightarrow$ vapor) inside a refrigeration system, absorbing latent heat at low temperature and pressure from the refrigerated space (evaporator) and rejecting it along with the work of compression at high temperature and pressure (condenser).

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *PEAGT411*, refrigerants are classified as the *blood of the refrigeration cycle* — a substance whose thermophysical properties, thermodynamic behaviour, and environmental impact govern the entire system's performance, safety, energy efficiency, and global-warming footprint.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **mason carrying bricks in a bucket across a hill**:
- At the **bottom of the hill (low pressure, low temperature)**, the mason scoops up bricks of heat (latent heat absorbed in the evaporator).
- He then climbs the **hill (compressor consumes work)**.
- At the **top of the hill (high pressure, high temperature)**, he dumps the bricks of heat (rejected in the condenser).
- The **bucket** is the refrigerant — it merely transports heat; the **climbing energy** is the compressor work.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A *good refrigerant* is one that carries **maximum heat per kilogram** (high latent heat of vaporization), requires **minimum work to climb the hill** (low compression work), is **safe**, **non-toxic**, **non-flammable**, **chemically stable**, and **environmentally benign**.

## 1.3 Why Classification is Necessary

No single refrigerant is "perfect". Engineers must choose among hundreds of fluids based on the application, temperature range, legislation, and environmental norms. **Classification** provides a systematic taxonomy that simplifies:

1. **Selection** for a given application
2. **Nomenclature** for global communication (ASHRAE R-numbers)
3. **Compliance** with environmental protocols (Montreal Protocol, Kigali Amendment)
4. **Safety handling** and risk categorisation (ASHRAE 34 Safety Groups)
5. **Performance estimation** through thermodynamic property comparison

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Reference Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The key standard reference metrics used in refrigerant classification are:
> - **Normal Boiling Point (NBP)** in °C at 101.325 kPa
> - **Latent Heat of Vaporization ($h_{fg}$)** in kJ/kg, target range **150 – 250 kJ/kg**
> - **Critical Temperature ($T_{cr}$)** in K, should be much higher than condensing temperature
> - **Critical Pressure ($P_{cr}$)** in MPa, ideally between **2 – 4 MPa**
> - **Specific Volume of Vapor** in m³/kg, ideally **0.05 – 0.20 m³/kg**
> - **Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP)** relative to R-11 (ODP of R-11 = 1.0)
> - **Global Warming Potential (GWP)** relative to CO₂ (GWP of CO₂ = 1.0) over 100 years
> - **ASHRAE Safety Group** denoted by letter **A** (lower toxicity) or **B** (higher toxicity) and number **1** (no flame propagation) or **2/3** (flammable)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Refrigerants

Refrigerants are broadly classified along **five independent taxonomies** in the KTU syllabus. A single refrigerant can belong to multiple categories simultaneously (e.g., R-134a is *synthetic, halocarbon, HFC, single-component, low-pressure*).

### A. By Origin / Method of Manufacture

| Class | Sub-Class | Examples | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Natural Refrigerants** | Inorganic | Ammonia (R-717), Water (R-718), Air (R-729), CO₂ (R-744) | Occur in nature, zero ODP, GWP ≤ 1 |
| | Organic (Hydrocarbons) | Propane (R-290), Isobutane (R-600a), Ethylene (R-1150), Propene (R-1270) | Zero ODP, low GWP, mildly flammable |
| **Synthetic Refrigerants** | Halocarbons (saturated) | CFCs, HCFCs, HFCs | Engineered; phased out due to ODP |
| | Halocarbons (unsaturated) | HFOs (R-1234yf, R-1234ze) | Low GWP, mildly flammable |
| | Zeotropes / Azeotropes | R-407C, R-410A, R-502 | Multi-component blends |

### B. By Chemical Family

| Family | Full Form | General Formula | Halogen Content | ODP |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CFC** | ChloroFluoroCarbon | $CCl_{n}F_{2-n}$ | Cl + F, no H | **High** (0.6 – 1.0) |
| **HCFC** | HydroChloroFluoroCarbon | $CCl_{n}F_{2-n-x}H_{x}$ | Cl + F + H | **Moderate** (0.005 – 0.2) |
| **HFC** | HydroFluoroCarbon | $CF_{n}H_{2-n+1}$ (saturated) | F + H, no Cl | **Zero** |
| **HFO** | HydroFluoroOlefin | Unsaturated C=C + F + H | No Cl | **Zero** |
| **HC** | HydroCarbon | $C_{n}H_{2n+2}$ | No halogen | **Zero** |
| **Inorganic** | — | e.g., NH₃, H₂O, CO₂ | — | **Zero** |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Halogen Rule:** ODP is caused by **chlorine (Cl)** atoms being released in the stratosphere. Therefore, **CFC > HCFC > HFC > HFO** in terms of ODP. Replacing Cl with H reduces ODP; replacing Cl with F and adding C=C unsaturation gives the cleanest (zero-ODP, low-GWP) HFOs.

### C. By Phase Behaviour (Blends)

| Type | Description | Temperature Glide | Distillation | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pure Fluid (Single Component)** | One chemical species | **0 K** (none) | Fixed boiling point | R-12, R-22, R-134a, R-717 |
| **Azeotrope** | Mixture behaving as pure fluid | **0 K** (negligible) | Single composition boils at single T | R-502, R-507A, R-508B |
| **Zeotrope** | Non-azeotropic mixture | **> 0 K** (significant) | Components vaporize at different T | R-407C (≈ 7 °C glide), R-410A (≈ 0.2 °C glide) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **zeotrope** exhibits *temperature glide* during phase change — this causes **fractional distillation** if any leakage occurs, permanently changing the blend composition. Always leak-test zeotropes carefully and top-up with fresh liquid, never with vapor.

### D. By Primary vs. Secondary Function

| Type | Role | Phase Change in Cycle? | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Refrigerant** | Undergoes phase change; absorbs/rejects heat directly | **Yes** | R-134a, R-717, R-290 |
| **Secondary Refrigerant (Brine)** | Transports heat from cooled space to evaporator of primary cycle | **No** (sensible cooling only) | Brine, ethylene glycol, propylene glycol, salt solutions |

### E. By Application Temperature Range

| Category | Evaporator T | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **High-Temperature (HT)** | $T_{evap} > 0\,°C$ | R-11, R-113, R-123, R-141b |
| **Medium-Temperature (MT)** | $-20\,°C \leq T_{evap} \leq 0\,°C$ | R-12, R-22, R-134a, R-404A, R-410A |
| **Low-Temperature (LT)** | $-40\,°C \leq T_{evap} < -20\,°C$ | R-502, R-13, R-23, R-508B |
| **Cryogenic** | $T_{evap} < -40\,°C$ | R-14, R-744 (CO₂ cascade), He, N₂ |

## 2.2 Desirable Thermodynamic & Chemical Properties of an Ideal Refrigerant

1. **Low boiling point** (below the desired refrigeration temperature)
2. **High latent heat of vaporization** ($h_{fg}$ large → high refrigerating effect per kg)
3. **Low specific volume of vapor** (smaller compressor displacement)
4. **Low specific heat of liquid** (less flash gas at throttling)
5. **High critical temperature & pressure** (so condensation occurs via sensible cooling above critical T)
6. **Low freezing point** (no solidification in evaporator)
7. **Low viscosity** (better heat transfer, lower pumping power)
8. **Chemically stable** (no decomposition in cycle)
9. **Non-corrosive** to metals, **miscible** with lubricating oil
10. **Non-toxic, non-flammable, non-explosive**
11. **Zero ODP and low GWP** (environmentally safe)
12. **Easily detectable** if leakage occurs
13. **Inexpensive, abundant, and easy to handle/transport**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table compiles every **board-exam-essential** equation for refrigerant classification and basic vapour compression cycle analysis.

| # | Equation / Relationship | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Refrigerating Effect (RE)** | $RE = h_{1} - h_{4}$ | kJ/kg | Heat absorbed per kg in evaporator |
| 2 | **Compressor Work** | $W_{c} = h_{2} - h_{1}$ | kJ/kg | Work input per kg |
| 3 | **Heat Rejected in Condenser** | $Q_{c} = h_{2} - h_{3}$ | kJ/kg | Heat dumped per kg |
| 4 | **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** | $COP = \dfrac{h_{1} - h_{4}}{h_{2} - h_{1}}$ | dimensionless | Cycle efficiency (real) |
| 5 | **Carnot COP (theoretical max)** | $COP_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_{L}}{T_{H} - T_{L}}$ | dimensionless | Reversible benchmark |
| 6 | **Mass Flow Rate of Refrigerant** | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q_{e}}{h_{1} - h_{4}}$ | kg/s | For given cooling load $Q_{e}$ |
| 7 | **Compressor Displacement Volume** | $V_{disp} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot v_{1}}{\eta_{vol}}$ | m³/s | Compressor sizing |
| 8 | **ASHRAE Nomenclature (saturated)** | $\text{Num} = (n_{F} + n_{Cl}) \cdot 10 + (n_{H} + 1)$ | — | R-Number identification |
| 9 | **ASHRAE Nomenclature (unsaturated)** | Add **+90** to base | — | For C=C double bond |
| 10 | **CFC-12 (R-12) Molecular Form** | $CCl_{2}F_{2}$ | — | n(F) = 2, n(Cl) = 2, n(H) = 0 |
| 11 | **CFC-11 (R-11) Molecular Form** | $CCl_{3}F$ | — | n(F) = 1, n(Cl) = 3, n(H) = 0 |
| 12 | **HFC-134a Molecular Form** | $CH_{2}F\text{-}CF_{3}$ | — | Tetrafluoroethane |
| 13 | **HFO-1234yf Molecular Form** | $CF_{3}\text{-}CF{=}CH_{2}$ | — | 2,3,3,3-tetrafluoropropene |
| 14 | **GWP Relation** | $GWP = \dfrac{\int_{0}^{TH} a_{x} \cdot c_{x}(t) \, dt}{\int_{0}^{TH} a_{r} \cdot c_{r}(t) \, dt}$ | — | Relative to reference (CO₂ = 1) |
| 15 | **ODP Relation** | $ODP = \dfrac{\text{Ozone change by 1 kg of fluid}}{\text{Ozone change by 1 kg of R-11}}$ | — | R-11 reference = 1.0 |
| 16 | **Zeotrope Glide** | $\Delta T_{glide} = T_{dew} - T_{bubble}$ | K | At constant P in two-phase region |
| 17 | **Energy Efficiency Ratio** | $EER = \dfrac{Q_{c}}{W} \cdot 3.412$ | BTU/hr per W | Equivalent COP × 3.412 |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU exam tip:** Always quote **refrigerating effect in kJ/kg** (SI) and remember that $1$ ton of refrigeration (TR) $= 3.517$ kW $= 210$ kJ/min.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cold Storage Design (Module 5 onward):** Choice of refrigerant governs evaporator pressure, compressor power, and hence operating cost. Ammonia (R-717) dominates large cold stores; R-404A dominates frozen food displays.
- **HVAC Systems:** R-410A has replaced R-22 in modern split ACs due to zero ODP.
- **Automotive AC:** R-1234yf is replacing R-134a due to GWP regulations (GWP 4 vs 1430).
- **Pharmaceutical & Food:** HFOs and CO₂ cascades are now preferred for low-temperature pharma freezers.
- **Cryogenics:** Helium and nitrogen (both ODP/GWP = 0) handle temperatures below $-150\,°C$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: ASHRAE Refrigerant Nomenclature Rules

The **ASHRAE R-Number** system is a positional numerical code that identifies the molecular composition of halogenated refrigerants. Let us derive each rule systematically.

### Step 1: Identify the Carbon Skeleton

The reference compound is the **saturated alkane with $n_{C}$ carbon atoms** (for a single-carbon refrigerant, it is methane $CH_4$).

### Step 2: Apply the General Molecular Formula

For a saturated halocarbon with one carbon atom:

$$C_{1} H_{n_H} Cl_{n_{Cl}} F_{n_F}$$

with the **saturation constraint** (each carbon has 4 bonds):

$$n_{H} + n_{Cl} + n_{F} = 4$$

### Step 3: Determine the Last Digit (Units Place)

The units digit is computed by:

$$U = n_{H} + 1$$

> **Logic:** For methane ($CH_4$), $n_{H}=4$, giving $U=5$? No — the rule is **$n_{H} + 1$ for the original saturated hydrocarbon (with H replaced) where the actual number is the F+Cl count** + 1. The *correct* formula, as taught in KTU, is:

$$\boxed{U = n_{H} + 1 \quad \text{(units digit of the R-Number)}}$$

For example, **R-12 (CFC-12, $CCl_2F_2$):** $n_{H} = 0 \Rightarrow U = 0+1 = 1$. ✓
For **R-22 (HCFC-22, $CHClF_2$):** $n_{H} = 1 \Rightarrow U = 1+1 = 2$. ✓

### Step 4: Determine the Tens Digit

The tens digit gives the count of **Fluorine atoms**:

$$T = n_{F}$$

For **R-12 ($CCl_2F_2$):** $T = 2$. ✓
For **R-22 ($CHClF_2$):** $T = 2$. ✓
For **R-134a ($CH_2F\text{-}CF_3$):** It has **2 carbons**, so we use a different formula — see Step 6.

### Step 5: Verify with Chlorine Count

Once $n_{H}$ and $n_{F}$ are known, $n_{Cl}$ is found from the saturation constraint:

$$n_{Cl} = 4 - n_{H} - n_{F} \quad \text{(for 1-carbon refrigerant)}$$

**Example — R-12:** $n_{Cl} = 4 - 0 - 2 = 2$ → molecular formula $CCl_2F_2$. ✓

### Step 6: Generalization to Multi-Carbon Refrigerants

For an **$n_C$-carbon** saturated refrigerant, the rule extends to:

$$U = n_{H} + 1 \quad ; \quad T = n_{F} \quad ; \quad H = n_{C} - 1 \quad \text{(hundreds digit)}$$

**Example — R-134a (HFC, 2 carbons):** $H = 2-1 = 1$, $T = n_F = 4$, $U = n_H + 1 = 2+1 = 3$. Number $= 134$. The *"a"* indicates an isomer (in this case, 1,1,1,2-tetrafluoroethane). ✓

### Step 7: Unsaturated Refrigerants (HFOs)

For unsaturated refrigerants (containing a $C{=}C$ double bond), the hundreds digit is incremented by **+90**:

$$H_{unsaturated} = (n_{C} - 1) + 90$$

**Example — R-1234yf (HFO, 3 carbons, unsaturated):** $H = 2 + 90 = 92$, $T = n_F = 4$, $U = n_H + 1 = 1+1 = 2$. Number $= 1234$. ✓

### Step 8: Special Series for Blends

| Blend Type | Series Offset | Example | Glide |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Zeotrope** | $+400$ | R-407C, R-410A, R-404A | non-zero |
| **Azeotrope** | $+500$ | R-502, R-507A, R-508B | ≈ 0 |

**Example — R-410A:** Designed to replace R-22, contains R-32 (50%) + R-125 (50%), zeotropic, glide ≈ 0.2 K.

## 3.2 Worked Derivation: COP of an Ideal Vapour Compression Cycle

For a complete VCRS in saturated state with no subcooling/superheating:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{RE per kg} &= h_1 - h_4 \\
\text{Work input} &= h_2 - h_1 \\
\text{COP}_{cycle} &= \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting $h_4 = h_3$ (isenthalpic throttling) and $h_3 = h_{f@P_{cond}}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
COP &= \frac{h_1 - h_3}{h_2 - h_1} \\
    &= \frac{h_{g@P_{evap}} - h_{f@P_{cond}}}{h_{g@P_{cond}} - h_{g@P_{evap}}}
\end{aligned}$$

The **Carnot (theoretical maximum) COP** is:

$$COP_{Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$

> For a typical AC operating at $T_L = 270\,K$ ($ -3\,°C$) and $T_H = 320\,K$ ($47\,°C$):

$$COP_{Carnot} = \frac{270}{320 - 270} = \frac{270}{50} = 5.4$$

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Common Refrigerants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following comparison matrix is the *single most high-yield table* in KTU exams. **Memorise the trends, not the exact values** — exact numbers are provided in property tables in the data handbook.

| Property | R-12 (CFC) | R-22 (HCFC) | R-134a (HFC) | R-717 (NH₃) | R-290 (HC) | R-744 (CO₂) | R-1234yf (HFO) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Molecular Formula | $CCl_2F_2$ | $CHClF_2$ | $CH_2FCF_3$ | $NH_3$ | $C_3H_8$ | $CO_2$ | $CF_3CF{=}CH_2$ |
| NBP (°C) | $-29.8$ | $-40.8$ | $-26.1$ | $-33.3$ | $-42.1$ | $-78.4$ (sublimes) | $-29.4$ |
| $T_{cr}$ (°C) | $112$ | $96$ | $101$ | $133$ | $97$ | $31$ | $95$ |
| $P_{cr}$ (MPa) | $4.11$ | $4.97$ | $4.06$ | $11.3$ | $4.25$ | $7.38$ | $3.38$ |
| ODP | $0.82$ | $0.055$ | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| GWP (100 yr) | $10900$ | $1810$ | $1430$ | $0$ | $3$ | $1$ | $4$ |
| Flammability | None | None | None | Mild | High | None | Mild |
| Toxicity | Low | Low | Low | High | Low | Low | Low |
| Latent Heat (kJ/kg) | $167$ | $234$ | $216$ | $1369$ | $426$ | $571$ (at 0°C) | $194$ |
| Lubricant | Mineral | Mineral/AB | POE | Mineral (separate) | Mineral | POE | POE |
| **Application** | Legacy AC | Legacy AC/HP | Modern AC/Refrig | Cold Storage | Domestic Fridge | Cascade, Transcritical | Auto AC |
| **Status** | **Phased Out** (1987) | **Phasing Out** | Transitional | In use | In use (small scale) | Growing | Growing |

## 3.4 The Montreal Protocol & Kigali Amendment Timeline

> [!NOTE]
> The **Montreal Protocol (1987)** mandated phase-out of CFCs by 1996 (Developed) / 2010 (Developing). The **Kigali Amendment (2016)** adds HFC phase-down (85% reduction by 2047 for India). This is why R-12 and R-22 are now *banned or restricted* in new equipment.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart of Refrigerants

```mermaid
    class C1c,C1c1,C1c2,C1c3 hfc
    class C2,C2a,C2a1,C2a2 hfo
    class C3,C3a,C3a1,C3a2,C3a3,C3b,C3b1,C3b2 blend
```

## 4.2 ASHRAE R-Number Decoding Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INP[ENTER R-NUMBER]:::io --> STRIP[REMOVE SUFFIX a b yf ze ETC]:::proc

    STRIP --> CHK{IS NUMBER BELOW 200 OR ABOVE 200}:::decision

    CHK --> LOW[UNDER 200 SATURATED 1 CARBON]:::proc
    CHK --> MID[200 TO 900 HFC MULTI CARBON SATURATED]:::proc
    CHK --> HIGH[OVER 1000 UNSATURATED HFO]:::proc
    CHK --> BLND[WITH 400 OR 500 OFFSET BLEND]:::proc

    LOW --> ULOW[UNITS DIGIT U MINUS 1 GIVES HYDROGEN COUNT]:::calc
    LOW --> TLOW[TENS DIGIT T GIVES FLUORINE COUNT]:::calc
    LOW --> CLOW[CHLORINE COUNT 4 MINUS H MINUS F]:::calc

    MID --> HCAL[HUNDREDS DIGIT PLUS 1 GIVES CARBON COUNT]:::calc
    MID --> TMID[TENS DIGIT GIVES FLUORINE COUNT]:::calc
    MID --> UMID[UNITS DIGIT MINUS 1 GIVES HYDROGEN COUNT]:::calc

    HIGH --> SUB[IF NUMBER BETWEEN 1000 AND 1999 SUBTRACT 90]:::calc
    SUB --> HCAL2[APPLY SAME RULES AS MULTI CARBON CASE]:::calc

    BLND --> TYPE{400 OR 500 OFFSET}:::decision
    TYPE --> ZEO[400 SUFFIX ZEOTROPE HAS TEMPERATURE GLIDE]:::proc
    TYPE --> AZO[500 SUFFIX AZEOTROPE BEHAVES AS PURE FLUID]:::proc

    ULOW --> OUT[MOLECULAR FORMULA IDENTIFIED]:::out
    TLOW --> OUT
    CLOW --> OUT
    HCAL --> OUT
    TMID --> OUT
    UMID --> OUT
    HCAL2 --> OUT
    ZEO --> OUT
    AZO --> OUT

    classDef io fill:#3498db,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef proc fill:#fef9e7,stroke:#b7950b,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21,color:#000
    classDef calc fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#abebc6,stroke:#1d8348,color:#000

    class INP,OUT io
    class STRIP,LOW,MID,HIGH,BLND,SUB,ZEO,AZO proc
    class CHK,TYPE decision
    class ULOW,TLOW,CLOW,HCAL,TMID,UMID,HCAL2 calc
```

## 4.3 Refrigerant Selection Decision Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{WHAT IS THE EVAPORATION TEMPERATURE RANGE}:::q --> A1[HIGH T 0 DEG C AND ABOVE]:::a
    Q1 --> A2[MEDIUM T MINUS 20 TO 0 DEG C]:::a
    Q1 --> A3[LOW T MINUS 40 TO MINUS 20 DEG C]:::a
    Q1 --> A4[CRYOGENIC BELOW MINUS 40 DEG C]:::a

    A1 --> S1[USE R-123 OR R-134a OR HFOS]:::sel
    A2 --> S2[USE R-134a R-410A R-407C]:::sel
    A3 --> S3[USE R-404A R-507A R-508B]:::sel
    A4 --> S4[USE R-744 CO2 CASCADE OR R-23]:::sel

    Q2{WHAT IS THE LOAD MAGNITUDE}:::q --> L1[SMALL DOMESTIC LESS THAN 5 KW]:::l
    Q2 --> L2[MEDIUM COMMERCIAL 5 TO 500 KW]:::l
    Q2 --> L3[LARGE INDUSTRIAL ABOVE 500 KW]:::l

    L1 --> S5[USE R-600a R-290 R-134a]:::sel
    L2 --> S6[USE R-134a R-404A R-410A]:::sel
    L3 --> S7[USE R-717 AMMONIA OR R-744]:::sel

    Q3{ANY ENVIRONMENTAL CONSTRAINTS}:::q --> E1[NO ODP ALLOWED]:::e
    Q3 --> E2[LOW GWP BELOW 150]:::e
    Q3 --> E3[ZERO FLAMMABILITY NEEDED]:::e

    E1 --> S8[AVOID CFC AND HCFC]:::sel
    E2 --> S9[USE HFOS R-1234yf OR R-744 OR R-290]:::sel
    E3 --> S10[USE R-134a R-410A R-744]:::sel

    classDef q fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21,color:#000
    classDef a fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#b7950b,color:#000
    classDef l fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000
    classDef e fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#1d8348,color:#000
    classDef sel fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#6c3483,color:#000

    class Q1,Q2,Q3 q
    class A1,A2,A3,A4 a
    class L1,L2,L3 l
    class E1,E2,E3 e
    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5,S6,S7,S8,S9,S10 sel
```
