# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q: Classify refrigerants based on their chemical composition. Give two examples for each class.**

**Model Answer:**

Refrigerants are chemically classified into the following families:

1. **Chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs)** — Fully halogenated alkanes with Cl and F only. Examples: **R-11 (CCl₃F)**, **R-12 (CCl₂F₂)**
2. **Hydrochlorofluorocarbons (HCFCs)** — Contain H, Cl, and F. Examples: **R-22 (CHClF₂)**, **R-123 (CHCl₂CF₃)**
3. **Hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs)** — Contain H and F only (no Cl). Examples: **R-134a (CH₂FCF₃)**, **R-32 (CH₂F₂)**
4. **Hydrocarbons (HCs)** — Pure alkanes. Examples: **R-290 (propane C₃H₈)**, **R-600a (isobutane C₄H₁₀)**
5. **Inorganic Refrigerants** — Examples: **R-717 (ammonia NH₃)**, **R-744 (CO₂)**, **R-718 (water)**

*(3 marks — 1 mark for classification, 2 marks for examples with chemical formulas)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q: Explain the hydrolysis reaction of R-12 with moisture and state its significance in refrigeration systems.**

**Model Answer:**

When R-12 (CCl₂F₂) comes into contact with moisture (water) inside a refrigeration system, it undergoes **hydrolysis** to form corrosive by-products:

$$CCl_2F_2 + H_2O \rightarrow 2HCl + 2HF + CO_2 + C$$

The reaction produces **hydrochloric acid (HCl)** and **hydrofluoric acid (HF)**, both of which are highly corrosive.

**Significance:**
- These acids **corrode copper tubing**, **damage compressor windings**, and **degrade insulation materials**
- They cause **copper plating** on bearing surfaces, leading to compressor failure
- This is why **filter driers** (containing molecular sieves/activated alumina) are mandatory in refrigeration systems to **remove moisture** before it reacts with the refrigerant

*(3 marks — 1 mark for reaction, 1 mark for products, 1 mark for significance)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Option 1)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Main Question Paper]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: Explain the chemical stability of CFCs and HCFCs. How does the C–H bond in HCFCs contribute to their lower ODP compared to CFCs?**

**Model Answer:**

**Chemical Stability of CFCs:**

CFCs are **fully halogenated** alkanes in which all hydrogen atoms are replaced by chlorine and fluorine. The key chemical bonds present are:

- **C–F bond**: Bond energy ≈ **485 kJ/mol** (very strong)
- **C–Cl bond**: Bond energy ≈ **327 kJ/mol** (strong)

Due to the **absence of C–H bonds**, CFCs are **extremely stable** in the lower atmosphere (troposphere). They do not react with hydroxyl radicals (·OH), water vapor, or other tropospheric species. Consequently, they have **very long atmospheric lifetimes** (R-12: ~100 years) and **diffuse intact to the stratosphere**, where UV-C radiation (λ < 220 nm) causes **photolytic dissociation**, releasing **chlorine radicals (Cl·)**.

**Chemical Stability of HCFCs:**

HCFCs contain **at least one C–H bond** (bond energy ≈ **413 kJ/mol**). This C–H bond is **weaker** than the C–F and C–Cl bonds and is **susceptible to attack by hydroxyl radicals (·OH)** in the troposphere:

$$CHClF_2 + \cdot OH \rightarrow \cdot CClF_2 + H_2O$$

This **tropospheric degradation pathway** means a significant fraction of HCFC molecules are **destroyed before reaching the stratosphere**. Therefore, less chlorine is delivered to the ozone layer, resulting in a **lower ODP**.

**Quantitative Comparison:**

| Refrigerant | Cl Atoms | ODP | Atmospheric Lifetime |
|---|---|---|---|
| R-12 (CFC) | 2 | 1.0 | 100 years |
| R-22 (HCFC) | 1 | 0.055 | 12 years |
| R-123 (HCFC) | 2 | 0.02 | 1.3 years |

**Conclusion:** The presence of the C–H bond in HCFCs enables **tropospheric removal by ·OH radicals**, dramatically reducing ODP and atmospheric lifetime compared to CFCs.

*(7 marks — 3 marks for CFC stability, 2 marks for HCFC degradation, 1 mark for bond energies, 1 mark for ODP comparison)*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: Describe the chemical compatibility of R-134a with lubricants and system materials. Why is POE oil used with R-134a and not mineral oil?**

**Model Answer:**

**Chemical Nature of R-134a:**

R-134a (1,1,1,2-tetrafluoroethane, CH₂FCF₃) is a **polar molecule** due to the asymmetric distribution of electronegative fluorine atoms. Its **dipole moment is approximately 2.06 Debye**, significantly higher than R-12 (≈ 0.51 Debye).

**Lubricant Compatibility — The Miscibility Problem:**

For proper compressor lubrication, the lubricant oil must **circulate throughout the system** and **return to the compressor** via the suction line. This requires **miscibility** between the refrigerant and oil.

- **Mineral Oil (MO):** A **non-polar** hydrocarbon-based lubricant. It mixes well with non-polar refrigerants (CFCs, HCFCs) but **does NOT mix with polar R-134a**. This causes oil to **accumulate in the evaporator and suction line**, starving the compressor of lubrication.

- **Polyolester (POE) Oil:** A **polar** synthetic lubricant with ester functional groups (–COO–). Its polar nature provides **excellent miscibility with R-134a**, ensuring proper oil return to the compressor.

**System Material Compatibility:**

| Material | Compatibility with R-134a | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Copper | Excellent | Standard tubing material |
| Steel | Excellent | Compressor housings |
| Aluminum | Excellent | Evaporator coils |
| **Neoprene** | Limited | May swell; use **HNBR** instead |
| **NBR (Nitrile)** | Not recommended | Swelling and extraction |
| **Butyl Rubber** | Compatible | O-rings and gaskets |
| Polyolester Oil | Compatible | Seals must be POE-resistant |

**Chemical Reaction Concerns:**

R-134a + water → **HF** + **carbonyl fluoride (COF₂)** + organic acids
- HF attacks glass, ceramics, and some metals
- COF₂ is **toxic** to humans
- This necessitates **moisture control** with appropriate filter driers (molecular sieves type 3A or 4A)

**Conclusion:** POE oil is chemically matched (polar-polar interaction) to R-134a, whereas mineral oil's non-polar nature causes phase separation. Material selection must also account for R-134a's polar chemistry and hydrolysis by-products.

*(7 marks — 2 marks for R-134a polarity, 2 marks for oil miscibility, 2 marks for material compatibility table, 1 mark for hydrolysis concern)*

---

### Question B (Option 2)

**[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024, Alternative Choice]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: Discuss the environmental impact of refrigerants. Explain ODP and GWP with reference to the Montreal Protocol and Kigali Amendment.**

**Model Answer:**

**Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP):**

ODP is a **relative measure** of a refrigerant's ability to destroy stratospheric ozone. It is defined as:

$$ODP = \frac{\text{Steady-state ozone destruction by 1 kg of substance}}{\text{Steady-state ozone destruction by 1 kg of R-11}}$$

R-11 is the reference (ODP = 1.0). CFCs have high ODP because they release chlorine radicals in the stratosphere:

$$Cl\cdot + O_3 \rightarrow ClO\cdot + O_2$$
$$ClO\cdot + O \rightarrow Cl\cdot + O_2$$

The chlorine radical is **regenerated**, creating a **catalytic cycle** that destroys thousands of ozone molecules per Cl atom.

**Global Warming Potential (GWP):**

GWP measures a substance's **infrared radiation absorption** effect over a specified time horizon (typically 100 years), relative to CO₂:

$$GWP_{100} = \frac{\int_0^{100} a_i \cdot [C_i(t)] \, dt}{\int_0^{100} a_{CO_2} \cdot [C_{CO_2}(t)] \, dt}$$

where $a_i$ is the infrared radiative efficiency and $C_i(t)$ is the atmospheric concentration decay function.

**Regulatory Framework:**

1. **Montreal Protocol (1987):** International treaty to **phase out CFCs and halons** due to ozone depletion. Result: CFC production ended in developed countries by 1996.

2. **London Amendment (1990):** Added HCFCs to the phase-out list.

3. **Kigali Amendment (2016):** Extended the protocol to **phase down HFCs** (high-GWP refrigerants) by **85% over the next 25 years**. This is because while HFCs have zero ODP, their high GWP (R-134a GWP = 1,430) makes them potent greenhouse gases.

**Current Status of Major Refrigerants:**

| Refrigerant | ODP | GWP₁₀₀ | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| R-12 | 1.0 | 10,900 | **Banned** (Montreal Protocol) |
| R-22 | 0.055 | 1,810 | **Phasing out** by 2030 |
| R-134a | 0 | 1,430 | **Phasing down** (Kigali) |
| R-410A | 0 | 2,088 | **Phasing down** (Kigali) |
| R-1234yf | 0 | < 1 | **Future** standard |

*(7 marks — 2 marks ODP concept, 2 marks GWP concept, 1 mark Montreal Protocol, 1 mark Kigali Amendment, 1 mark status table)*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: Explain the chemistry of ammonia (R-717) as a refrigerant. Discuss its advantages, hazards, and applications in industrial refrigeration.**

**Model Answer:**

**Chemical Properties of Ammonia (NH₃):**

Ammonia is an **inorganic compound** with the molecular formula **NH₃**. Its molecular structure is **trigonal pyramidal** with:
- N–H bond length: **1.012 Å**
- H–N–H bond angle: **107.8°**
- Molecular weight: **17.03 kg/kmol**
- Normal boiling point: **−33.34°C**
- Dipole moment: **1.47 Debye** (highly polar)

**Chemical Reactions and Stability:**

Ammonia is **thermally stable** under normal refrigeration conditions. It decomposes only at very high temperatures (> 500°C):

$$2NH_3 \rightarrow N_2 + 3H_2 \quad (\text{at } > 500°C)$$

With water, ammonia forms **ammonium hydroxide**:

$$NH_3 + H_2O \rightarrow NH_4OH$$

With acids, it forms **ammonium salts**:

$$NH_3 + HCl \rightarrow NH_4Cl$$

**Material Compatibility — Chemical Incompatibility with Copper:**

Ammonia **reacts aggressively with copper and copper alloys** (brass, bronze):

$$2NH_3 + Cu^{2+} \rightarrow [Cu(NH_3)_2]^{2+} \quad (\text{copper amine complex})$$

This causes **stress corrosion cracking** in copper tubing. Therefore, ammonia systems must use **steel piping, aluminum, or stainless steel** instead of copper.

**Advantages of Ammonia:**

1. **Zero ODP and Zero GWP** — environmentally benign
2. **Excellent thermodynamic properties** — high latent heat (1,377 kJ/kg at 0°C)
3. **High COP** — typically 15-20% better than R-22/R-134a systems
4. **Low cost** — widely available as agricultural/industrial chemical
5. **Easily detectable** — pungent odor at 5 ppm (well below toxic levels)
6. **Positive pressure** in most applications — prevents air/moisture ingress

**Hazards of Ammonia:**

| Hazard | Details | Limit |
|---|---|---|
| **Toxicity** (B2) | Causes respiratory damage at high concentrations | TLV = 25 ppm (8-hr) |
| **Flammability** (2L) | Flammable in air between 15% – 28% by volume | LEL = 15% |
| **Corrosivity** | Attacks copper and copper alloys | Material restriction |
| **Odor** | Very pungent; detectable at 5 ppm | — |

**Applications:**

- **Industrial cold storage** (large warehouses, food processing)
- **Ice plants and ice rinks**
- **Brewery and dairy refrigeration**
- **Petrochemical process cooling**
- **District cooling systems**
- **Absorption refrigeration** (NH₃-H₂O system for cooling)

**Safety Measures:**
- Leak detection systems (NH₃ sensors)
- Emergency ventilation with water scrubbers
- Pressure relief valves on all vessels
- Restricted access zones (charge limits per ASHRAE 15)

*(7 marks — 2 marks for chemical properties, 1 mark for material incompatibility, 2 marks for advantages, 1 mark for hazards, 1 mark for applications)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing ODP and GWP:** ODP relates to **ozone destruction** (Cl-related), while GWP relates to **infrared absorption** (climate change). Examiners specifically look for this distinction.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Tetravalency Rule:** When asked to determine if a chemical formula is a valid refrigerant, students often forget the constraint $y + z + w = 2x + 2$. This is a **favourite KTU question**.
>
> 3. **Missing Chemical Equations:** Many students describe hydrolysis verbally but **do not write the balanced chemical equation**. Always provide a **balanced equation** for full marks.
>
> 4. **Ignoring Units:** Molecular weight in **kg/kmol** (not g/mol), latent heat in **kJ/kg**, GWP as a **dimensionless** number. Unit mistakes cost 0.5–1 mark.
>
> 5. **Confusing R-134a and R-12:** These are **chemically different** molecules (R-134a is CH₂FCF₃; R-12 is CCl₂F₂). Misnaming refrigerants is a common error.
>
> 6. **Not mentioning Montreal/Kigali Protocols:** Environmental questions almost always require a **regulatory reference**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Chemical Aspects of Refrigerants**

### 🔬 Core Chemical Families
- **CFCs**: Fully halogenated; high ODP/GWP; **phased out** (Montreal Protocol 1987)
- **HCFCs**: Contain C–H bond; **degraded by ·OH** in troposphere; transitional refrigerants
- **HFCs**: Zero ODP but **high GWP**; current standard (R-134a); **phasing down** (Kigali 2016)
- **HFOs**: Contain C=C double bond; **ultra-low GWP** (< 1); future standard
- **HCs**: Natural alkanes; **highly flammable** (A3); excellent efficiency
- **Inorganics**: NH₃ (toxic), CO₂ (high pressure), H₂O (absorption only)

### 📐 Must-Know Formulas
- **Tetravalency constraint**: $y + z + w = 2x + 2$
- **Blend molecular weight**: $M = \sum m_i M_i$
- **Blend Cp**: $C_p = \sum m_i C_{p,i}$
- **Raoult's Law (ideal)**: $P = \sum x_i P_i$

### ⚠️ Critical Hazards to Remember
- **Hydrolysis** → HCl + HF → **corrosion** (use filter driers)
- **Thermal decomposition** → COF₂, phosgene (toxic)
- **NH₃ + Cu** → stress corrosion cracking (avoid copper)
- **HFC + MO oil** → immiscible (use POE oil)

### 🌍 Environmental Metrics
- **ODP** reference: R-11 = 1.0
- **GWP** reference: CO₂ = 1.0 (100-year horizon)
- **ASHRAE 34 Classification**: Letter (A/B toxicity) + Number (1/2/3 flammability)
  - A1 = Low toxicity, no flame propagation (R-134a, R-410A)
  - A2L = Low toxicity, mild flammability (R-1234yf)
  - B2 = Higher toxicity, flammable (NH₃)
  - A3 = Low toxicity, high flammability (R-290, R-600a)

### 📋 Key Regulatory Milestones
- **1987** — Montreal Protocol (CFC phase-out)
- **1990** — London Amendment (HCFC phase-out)
- **2016** — Kigali Amendment (HFC phase-down, 85% reduction by 2047)

### 🧪 Standard Test Refrigerants by Application
- **Domestic refrigeration**: R-600a, R-134a
- **Automotive A/C**: R-1234yf, R-134a
- **Commercial refrigeration**: R-404A, R-407F, R-410A
- **Industrial**: R-717 (ammonia), R-744 (CO₂)
- **Chillers**: R-123, R-513A
- **Low temperature**: R-744 (CO₂ transcritical), R-23

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When a question asks for "chemical" properties of a refrigerant, structure your answer around **(1) molecular formula and structure, (2) stability and reactivity, (3) toxicity and flammability, (4) environmental impact, (5) material compatibility**. This five-point framework guarantees comprehensive coverage and full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Safety

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🛡️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Safety" in Refrigeration, Air Conditioning & Cold Storage?

### 📘 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Safety** in Refrigeration, Air-Conditioning and Cold Storage engineering refers to the integrated set of engineering practices, design protocols, operational guidelines, and protective measures implemented to prevent hazards arising from **high-pressure refrigerants, low-temperature surfaces, toxic gases, flammable substances, electrical components, and confined-space environments** within RAC systems. It encompasses refrigerant classification, personal protection, equipment safeguards, fire prevention, and emergency response procedures as mandated by ASHRAE Standard 15, ASHRAE Standard 34, and the Indian Refrigeration & Air-Conditioning Act (BIS Standards IS 732, IS 5129).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The safety module is critical because it directly maps to **CO5 (Design with responsibility)** of the PEAGT411 course — students are expected to identify hazards, classify refrigerants, and specify appropriate safety devices for a given RAC application.

### 1.2 Classification of Refrigerant Safety (ASHRAE 34)

The most fundamental safety concept is the **Safety Group Classification** of refrigerants, defined by two parameters: **Toxicity** (lowercase letter) and **Flammability** (uppercase letter).

| Toxicity Class | Meaning | Flammability Class | Meaning |
|----------------|---------|---------------------|---------|
| **A** | Lower Toxicity | **1** | No Flame Propagation |
| **B** | Higher Toxicity | **2L** | Mild Flammability, Burning Velocity ≤ 10 cm/s |
| | | **2** | Flammable, Burning Velocity > 10 cm/s |
| | | **3** | Highly Flammable / Explosive |

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Examples:**
> - **R-134a** → A1 (Safest category — non-toxic, non-flammable)
> - **R-410A** → A1 (used in modern split ACs)
> - **R-32** → A2L (mildly flammable, low GWP)
> - **R-290 (Propane)** → A3 (highly flammable, but natural)
> - **R-717 (Ammonia)** → B2L (higher toxicity, mildly flammable)
> - **R-744 (CO₂)** → A1 (high pressure, non-toxic)

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

> [!TIP]
> **Think of refrigerants like household chemicals under your kitchen sink:**
> 
> Imagine you have three bottles — **cooking gas (LPG)**, **bleach**, and **mineral water**.
> - **Cooking gas** = highly flammable, dangerous if leaked near a flame → *R-290, R-32*
> - **Bleach** = toxic, don't drink it → *R-717 Ammonia*
> - **Mineral water** = safe to handle → *R-134a, R-410A*
> 
> A refrigeration engineer must know which "bottle" they are dealing with and wear the right "gloves" (safety devices). That's exactly what **Safety Group Classification** does — it tells you how dangerous the refrigerant is and what protective equipment you need.

### 1.4 Why Safety is Non-Negotiable in RAC Systems

The hazards in an RAC system arise from **four fundamental physical phenomena**:

1. **High Pressure** — Compressors and condensers operate at **15–30 bar** (R-410A even reaches **40+ bar**)
2. **Low Temperature** — Evaporator temperatures can reach **-40°C to -80°C** causing frostbite
3. **Chemical Toxicity** — Ammonia (NH₃), CO₂, and some HFCs pose inhalation hazards
4. **Flammability** — Hydrocarbons and HFOs can ignite under specific concentrations

> [!WARNING]
> **Key Statistic:** The OEL (Occupational Exposure Limit) for **Ammonia (R-717)** is only **25 ppm (8-hour TWA)** and **35 ppm (STEL)** — meaning even a small leak in a poorly ventilated cold storage can be lethal.

### 1.5 Standards Governing RAC Safety

- **ASHRAE Standard 15** — Safety Code for Mechanical Refrigeration
- **ASHRAE Standard 34** — Designation and Safety Classification of Refrigerants
- **BIS IS 732** — Code of Practice for Electrical Wiring
- **BIS IS 5129** — Code of Practice for Cold Storage
- **IIAR Ammonia Refrigeration Standards** (for industrial systems)
- **NFPA 1 / NFPA 10** — Fire Prevention and Portable Fire Extinguishers

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars of Refrigerant Safety: Toxicity and Flammability

### 🧪 Pillar 1 — Toxicity

Toxicity is determined by the **OEL (Occupational Exposure Limit)** and the **LC₅₀ (Lethal Concentration 50%)** of the refrigerant.

- **Class A (Lower Toxicity):** OEL ≥ 400 ppm
- **Class B (Higher Toxicity):** OEL < 400 ppm

> For a refrigerant, exposure limits are tested on animals and humans. Ammonia has a very low OEL because even at 25 ppm, the human nose detects it sharply (a built-in safety feature).

### 🔥 Pillar 2 — Flammability

Flammability is measured by three key parameters:

| Parameter | Definition | Units |
|-----------|------------|-------|
| **LFL (Lower Flammability Limit)** | Minimum concentration in air that can ignite | % by volume |
| **UFL (Upper Flammability Limit)** | Maximum concentration that can ignite | % by volume |
| **Burning Velocity (Bv)** | Speed of flame propagation through the gas-air mixture | cm/s |
| **Heat of Combustion (Hc)** | Energy released per unit mass | kJ/kg or MJ/kg |
| **Autoignition Temperature (AIT)** | Minimum temperature at which gas ignites spontaneously | °C |

**Flammability Classification Rules:**
- **Class 1 (No Propagation):** Tested per ASHRAE 34 — no flame propagation at 60°C and 100% RH
- **Class 2L (Mildly Flammable):** Burning velocity ≤ 10 cm/s AND Hc ≤ 19 MJ/kg
- **Class 2 (Flammable):** Burning velocity > 10 cm/s
- **Class 3 (Highly Flammable):** Bv > 10 cm/s AND Hc > 19 MJ/kg

## 2.2 Safety Group Matrix — Complete Mapping

| Refrigerant | Chemical Formula | Safety Group | ODP | GWP (100-yr) | Application |
|-------------|------------------|--------------|-----|---------------|-------------|
| R-11 | CCl₃F | A1 | 1.0 | 4,750 | Centrifugal chillers (legacy) |
| R-12 | CCl₂F₂ | A1 | 1.0 | 10,900 | Legacy automotive/domestic |
| R-22 | CHClF₂ | A1 | 0.055 | 1,810 | Legacy AC (phase-out) |
| R-134a | CH₂FCF₃ | A1 | 0 | 1,430 | Modern domestic AC, chillers |
| R-410A | R-32/R-125 blend | A1 | 0 | 2,088 | Modern split AC |
| R-32 | CH₂F₂ | A2L | 0 | 675 | Next-gen AC |
| R-1234yf | CF₃CF=CH₂ | A2L | 0 | 4 | Automotive AC |
| R-290 (Propane) | C₃H₈ | A3 | 0 | 3.3 | Domestic refrigerators |
| R-717 (Ammonia) | NH₃ | B2L | 0 | 0 | Industrial cold storage, ice plants |
| R-744 (CO₂) | CO₂ | A1 | 0 | 1 | Transcritical systems |

> [!NOTE]
> **ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential)** is referenced to R-11 (=1.0). **GWP (Global Warming Potential)** is referenced to CO₂ (=1).

## 2.3 High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋

### 🧮 Core Safety Equations

| Formula / Concept | Equation | Variables & Units |
|-------------------|----------|-------------------|
| **LFL / UFL Range (Combustible Limits)** | $\text{Flammable Range} = \text{UFL} - \text{LFL}$ | % by volume |
| **Lower Flammability Limit (Le Chatelier's Rule for mixtures)** | $\text{LFL}_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{100}{\sum_i \dfrac{y_i}{\text{LFL}_i}}$ | $y_i$ = mole fraction of component $i$ |
| **Refrigerant Charge Limit (per ASHRAE 15)** | $m_{\text{max}} = k \cdot V_{\text{room}}$ | $m$ in kg, $V$ in m³, $k$ = safety factor (kg/m³) |
| **Toxicity Dose Relationship (Haber's Law)** | $C \cdot t = k$ | $C$ = concentration (ppm), $t$ = time (min), $k$ = constant |
| **Pressure Relief Valve Sizing (API 520)** | $A = \dfrac{W_{\text{required}}}{C \cdot K \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{Z \cdot T_1}}}$ | $A$ = orifice area, $W$ = mass flow, $M$ = molar mass |
| **OEL Time-Weighted Average (TWA)** | $\text{TWA} = \dfrac{\sum (C_i \cdot t_i)}{\sum t_i}$ | ppm |
| **Oxygen Deficiency Concentration** | $\text{O}_2\% = 21\% \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{V_{\text{refr}}}{V_{\text{room}}}\right)$ | % by volume |
| **ODP Index** | $\text{ODP} = \dfrac{\text{Ozone loss by refrigerant}}{\text{Ozone loss by R-11}}$ | dimensionless |
| **GWP Index** | $\text{GWP} = \dfrac{\text{IR effect of 1 kg gas over 100 yr}}{\text{IR effect of 1 kg CO}_2 \text{ over 100 yr}}$ | dimensionless |
| **TEWI (Total Equivalent Warming Impact)** | $\text{TEWI} = GWP \cdot L \cdot n + n \cdot E \cdot \beta$ | $L$ = leakage, $n$ = system life, $E$ = energy, $\beta$ = CO₂/kWh |
| **Heat of Combustion for Flammability Class** | $H_c \text{ threshold} = 19 \text{ MJ/kg}$ | Threshold between 2L and 3 |
| **Autoignition (Activation Energy, Arrhenius)** | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R \cdot T)}$ | $E_a$ in J/mol |

## 2.4 Safety Devices in Refrigeration Systems ⚙️

### Mechanical / Pressure Safety Devices

| Device | Function | Location |
|--------|----------|----------|
| **Pressure Relief Valve (PRV)** | Vents refrigerant when pressure exceeds set limit | High-side (discharge line, condenser, receiver) |
| **Rupture Disc** | Single-use burst disc for over-pressure | Parallel to PRV on high-pressure side |
| **Pressure Cut-out Switch (HP/LP)** | Stops compressor at unsafe pressures | Discharge (HP) and suction (LP) lines |
| **Safety Pressure Regulator** | Maintains safe operating pressure | Condenser, receiver outlet |
| **Internal Overload Protector** | Trips motor on over-current/over-temp | Compressor windings |

### Electrical / Control Safety Devices

| Device | Function |
|--------|----------|
| **Thermostat (LP/HP Cut-out)** | Cuts compressor on temperature/pressure extremes |
| **Oil Pressure Safety Switch** | Stops compressor on low oil pressure |
| **Phase Reversal Relay** | Prevents reverse rotation of 3-phase motors |
| **Earth Leakage Circuit Breaker (ELCB)** | Protects personnel from electric shock |
| **Motor Protection Circuit Breaker (MPCB)** | Protects compressor motor from overload |

### Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) for RAC Engineers

| PPE Item | Used For |
|----------|----------|
| **Safety Goggles / Face Shield** | Protection from refrigerant splash (liquid burns) |
| **Chemical-Resistant Gloves** | Handling ammonia, hydrocarbons, oils |
| **Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus (SCBA)** | Entering ammonia-leak spaces |
| **Safety Harness** | Confined spaces (cold storage racks) |
| **Thermal Gloves / Apron** | Handling low-temperature surfaces (-40°C) |
| **Ear Protection** | Compressor rooms (85+ dB noise) |

## 2.5 The LFL/UFL Triangle & Concentration Hazards

For a refrigerant leak in a closed room, the **concentration C** as a function of leaked mass is:

$$C = \dfrac{m_{\text{leak}}}{V_{\text{room}} \cdot \rho_{\text{air}}} \times 10^6 \quad \text{[ppm]}$$

Where:
- $m_{\text{leak}}$ = mass of refrigerant leaked (kg)
- $V_{\text{room}}$ = room volume (m³)
- $\rho_{\text{air}}$ = air density ≈ **1.2 kg/m³**
- The factor $10^6$ converts mass fraction to ppm (by volume, assuming ideal gas)

**Critical Safety Zones in a Leaked Room:**

$$\text{Oxygen Level} < 19.5\% \rightarrow \text{Asphyxiation Hazard (IDLH)}$$

$$\text{C} > \text{UFL} \rightarrow \text{Too Rich, No Combustion}$$

$$\text{LFL} < \text{C} < \text{UFL} \rightarrow \text{EXPLOSIVE / FLAMMABLE ZONE}$$

$$\text{C} > \text{OEL} \rightarrow \text{Toxic Hazard Zone}$$

## 2.6 Engineering Application Scenarios

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Relevance:**
> 1. **Cold Storage with Ammonia (R-717):** The IDLH (Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health) value is **300 ppm**. Ventilation must dilute any leak to below this value within seconds.
> 2. **Domestic Refrigerator with R-600a (Isobutane, A3):** Charge limited to **150 g** per ASHRAE 15; entire room cannot reach LFL even if all refrigerant leaks.
> 3. **Supermarket R-744 (CO₂) Systems:** High pressure (130 bar transcritical) — requires special PRV settings.
> 4. **Chillers with R-123 (B1):** Toxicity concern drives sealed mechanical-room requirements.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Refrigerant Charge Limit for Occupied Spaces

### Problem Statement
A small cold storage room uses **R-134a** (Safety Group A1, LFL = None, OEL = 1,000 ppm). The room dimensions are **4 m × 5 m × 3 m**. Determine the **maximum permissible refrigerant charge** per ASHRAE 15, given that the safety factor $k$ for A1 refrigerants in occupied spaces is **0.4 kg/m³** for institutional occupancies.

### Given Data
- Room dimensions: $L = 4$ m, $W = 5$ m, $H = 3$ m
- Safety factor: $k = 0.4 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (R-134a, A1, institutional)
- OEL (R-134a) = 1,000 ppm
- LFL (R-134a) = Not applicable (Class 1 — non-flammable)

### Step 1 — Calculate Room Volume

$$V_{\text{room}} = L \times W \times H$$

$$V_{\text{room}} = 4 \times 5 \times 3 = 60 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 2 — Apply ASHRAE 15 Charge Limit Formula

$$m_{\text{max}} = k \cdot V_{\text{room}}$$

$$m_{\text{max}} = 0.4 \times 60 = 24 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 3 — Verification by OEL Criterion
Check whether this charge, if fully leaked, would exceed OEL of 1,000 ppm:

$$C_{\text{leak}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{leak}}}{V_{\text{room}} \cdot \rho_{\text{air}}} \times 10^6$$

$$C_{\text{leak}} = \dfrac{24}{60 \times 1.2} \times 10^6 = \dfrac{24}{72} \times 10^6 = 333,333 \text{ ppm}$$

This is **far above** OEL = 1,000 ppm. However, ASHRAE 15 is based on **$RCL$ (Refrigerant Concentration Limit)** which accounts for the fact that the refrigerant may stratify or vent. The OEL check applies for toxic refrigerants (B class) primarily. For R-134a (A1), the charge limit dominates.

### Final Answer
$$\boxed{m_{\text{max, R-134a}} = 24 \text{ kg in a 60 m}^3 \text{ room}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Le Chatelier's Rule for Refrigerant Blend LFL

### Problem Statement
A refrigeration system uses a **zeotropic blend R-407C** with the following composition by mass:
- **R-32: 23%**, LFL = 0.307 (vol fraction, *approximate as mole fraction for low pressure*)
- **R-125: 25%**, LFL = 0.000 (non-flammable in mixture conditions)
- **R-134a: 52%**, LFL = 0.000 (non-flammable in mixture conditions)

Calculate the LFL of the blend using Le Chatelier's Rule:

$$\text{LFL}_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{100}{\sum_i \dfrac{y_i}{\text{LFL}_i}}$$

### Given Data
- $y_{\text{R-32}} = 0.23$, $\text{LFL}_{\text{R-32}} = 0.307$
- $y_{\text{R-125}} = 0.25$, $\text{LFL}_{\text{R-125}} = 1.00$ (assumed non-flammable, high value)
- $y_{\text{R-134a}} = 0.52$, $\text{LFL}_{\text{R-134a}} = 1.00$ (assumed non-flammable)

### Step 1 — Compute Individual Terms
For each component, compute the ratio $\dfrac{y_i}{\text{LFL}_i}$:

$$\dfrac{y_{\text{R-32}}}{\text{LFL}_{\text{R-32}}} = \dfrac{0.23}{0.307} = 0.749$$

$$\dfrac{y_{\text{R-125}}}{\text{LFL}_{\text{R-125}}} = \dfrac{0.25}{1.00} = 0.250$$

$$\dfrac{y_{\text{R-134a}}}{\text{LFL}_{\text{R-134a}}} = \dfrac{0.52}{1.00} = 0.520$$

### Step 2 — Sum the Ratios
$$\sum \dfrac{y_i}{\text{LFL}_i} = 0.749 + 0.250 + 0.520 = 1.519$$

### Step 3 — Apply Le Chatelier's Formula
$$\text{LFL}_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{100}{1.519} = 65.83 \text{ (relative units, this is an unusual result)}$$

This shows that when only **small amounts of R-32 (mildly flammable)** are present in a blend with non-flammable components, the overall mixture becomes **practically non-flammable**. This is why R-407C is classified as **A1** even though it contains R-32.

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** In practice, ASHRAE 34 uses more sophisticated criteria (burning velocity tests). Le Chatelier's rule is an *engineering estimate* for preliminary hazard analysis.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Oxygen Deficiency Calculation in Ammonia Leak

### Problem Statement
An industrial cold storage (volume **1,000 m³**) suffers an ammonia (R-717) leak of **10 kg**. The room is initially at standard conditions (21% O₂). Calculate the oxygen concentration after the leak and determine the hazard level.

### Given Data
- $V_{\text{room}} = 1{,}000$ m³
- $m_{\text{NH}_3} = 10$ kg
- Initial $O_2 = 21\%$
- $M_{\text{NH}_3} = 17$ g/mol

### Step 1 — Convert Ammonia Mass to Volume Equivalent (at STP)
Moles of ammonia:
$$n_{\text{NH}_3} = \dfrac{m}{M} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{17} = 588.24 \text{ mol}$$

Volume at STP (1 mol = 22.4 L):
$$V_{\text{NH}_3} = 588.24 \times 22.4 = 13,177 \text{ L} = 13.18 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 2 — Compute Volume Fraction of Ammonia in Air
$$y_{\text{NH}_3} = \dfrac{V_{\text{NH}_3}}{V_{\text{room}}} = \dfrac{13.18}{1{,}000} = 0.01318 = 1.318\%$$

Convert to ppm:
$$C_{\text{NH}_3} = 13{,}180 \text{ ppm}$$

### Step 3 — Compute New Oxygen Concentration
$$\text{O}_2\%_{\text{new}} = 21\% \times (1 - y_{\text{NH}_3}) = 21 \times (1 - 0.01318)$$

$$\text{O}_2\%_{\text{new}} = 21 \times 0.98682 = 20.72\%$$

> Note: Oxygen depletion is **not severe** at 1.3% leak — the bigger hazard is **toxicity** (IDLH for NH₃ = 300 ppm, our concentration is **13,180 ppm = 44× IDLH!**).

### Step 4 — Hazard Determination
- **Toxicity:** $13{,}180$ ppm $\gg$ 300 ppm IDLH → **IMMEDIATE DEADLY HAZARD** ☠️
- **Flammability:** LFL of NH₃ = 15% by volume → 1.3% is **below LFL, not flammable** ✅
- **Oxygen:** 20.72% > 19.5% IDLH threshold → **No asphyxiation from O₂** ✅

### Final Answer
$$\boxed{C_{\text{NH}_3} = 13{,}180 \text{ ppm (44× IDLH) — LETHAL TOXIC HAZARD}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — PRV Orifice Sizing for Ammonia Receiver

### Problem Statement
An ammonia receiver has a **design pressure of 21 bar (gauge)** and is exposed to fire. The required mass flow for fire case is **$W = 0.5$ kg/s** of NH₃. Calculate the **minimum PRV orifice area**.

### Given Data
- $W = 0.5$ kg/s
- $M = 17$ g/mol
- $P_1 = 21 \text{ bar} + 1.013 \text{ bar} = 22.013 \text{ bar} = 2.2013 \text{ MPa (absolute)}$
- $T_1 = 363$ K (90°C — fire exposure)
- $C = 0.85$ (discharge coefficient, NH₃ vapour)
- $K = 1.0$ (superheat correction for saturated vapour)
- $Z = 0.65$ (compressibility of NH₃ at 90°C, 22 bar)

### Step 1 — Apply API 520 Sizing Equation

$$A = \dfrac{W}{C \cdot K \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{Z \cdot T_1}}}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Values

$$A = \dfrac{0.5}{0.85 \times 1.0 \times 2.2013 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{17}{0.65 \times 363}}}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Square Root Term

$$\dfrac{17}{0.65 \times 363} = \dfrac{17}{235.95} = 0.07205$$

$$\sqrt{0.07205} = 0.2684$$

### Step 4 — Compute Denominator

$$\text{Denom} = 0.85 \times 1.0 \times 2.2013 \times 0.2684 = 0.5022$$

### Step 5 — Compute Orifice Area

$$A = \dfrac{0.5}{0.5022} = 0.9956 \text{ cm}^2$$

### Final Answer
$$\boxed{A_{\text{PRV, min}} \approx 1.0 \text{ cm}^2 \text{ for ammonia receiver fire case}}$$
