# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the term "Safety Group Classification" of refrigerants as per ASHRAE 34. Explain the meaning of "A2L" classification with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

Safety Group Classification of refrigerants as per **ASHRAE Standard 34** is a two-character alphanumeric code that classifies a refrigerant based on two independent safety criteria:
1. **Toxicity** (denoted by lowercase letter): **A** = Lower Toxicity (OEL ≥ 400 ppm), **B** = Higher Toxicity (OEL < 400 ppm)
2. **Flammability** (denoted by uppercase number): **1** = No flame propagation, **2L** = Mildly flammable (burning velocity ≤ 10 cm/s, heat of combustion ≤ 19 MJ/kg), **2** = Flammable, **3** = Highly Flammable

**Meaning of "A2L":**
- **A** → Lower Toxicity (OEL ≥ 400 ppm)
- **2L** → Mildly Flammable with low burning velocity

**Example:** **R-32 (Difluoromethane, CH₂F₂)** is classified as **A2L**. It is used in next-generation split air conditioners due to its low GWP of 675. Although mildly flammable, it is safe in properly designed systems with leak detectors and charge limitations.

> **Marks distribution:** Definition (1.5) + A2L explanation (1) + Example (0.5)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any three safety devices used in a refrigeration system and state their function.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No | Safety Device | Function |
|------|---------------|----------|
| 1 | **Pressure Relief Valve (PRV)** | Automatically vents refrigerant to atmosphere when system pressure exceeds the design limit, preventing rupture of vessels |
| 2 | **High-Pressure Cut-out Switch (HP Switch)** | Stops the compressor when discharge pressure exceeds a safe value (e.g., 28 bar for R-22) |
| 3 | **Low-Pressure Cut-out Switch (LP Switch)** | Stops the compressor when suction pressure falls below safe value, preventing air ingress and motor overload |
| 4 (Bonus) | **Oil Pressure Safety Switch** | Trips compressor on low oil pressure to prevent bearing damage |
| 5 (Bonus) | **Rupture Disc** | Single-use safety device that bursts at a set pressure as backup to PRV |

> **Marks distribution:** 3 devices × (Name 0.5 + Function 0.5) = 3 marks

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### 📌 Question A (14 Marks)

#### Q-A(a). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**With the help of a neat sketch, explain the ASHRAE 34 safety classification of refrigerants. Also describe the flammability parameters LFL, UFL, and Burning Velocity used in this classification.**

**Model Answer:**

**ASHRAE 34 Safety Classification — Explanation:**

The ASHRAE 34 safety classification is a **two-character code** (e.g., A1, A2L, B3) where:
- **First character (uppercase):** Flammability class — **1, 2L, 2, or 3**
- **Second character (lowercase):** Toxicity class — **A or B**

**Flammability Classes (in increasing order of hazard):**

| Class | Criterion | Test Conditions |
|-------|-----------|-----------------|
| **1** | No propagation of flame | Tested at 60°C and 100% RH |
| **2L** | Mildly flammable | Burning velocity ≤ 10 cm/s AND Hc ≤ 19 MJ/kg |
| **2** | Flammable | Burning velocity > 10 cm/s |
| **3** | Highly flammable / explosive | Bv > 10 cm/s AND Hc > 19 MJ/kg |

**Toxicity Classes:**
- **A (Lower toxicity):** OEL ≥ 400 ppm
- **B (Higher toxicity):** OEL < 400 ppm

**Flammability Parameters:**

1. **LFL (Lower Flammability Limit):** The minimum concentration of refrigerant in air (by volume) below which the mixture is too lean to ignite. For R-290 (propane), LFL = **2.1%**.

2. **UFL (Upper Flammability Limit):** The maximum concentration of refrigerant in air (by volume) above which the mixture is too rich to ignite. For R-290, UFL = **9.5%**.

3. **Burning Velocity (Bv):** The speed at which a flame front propagates through a combustible gas-air mixture relative to the unburned gas. Measured in **cm/s** at standard conditions.
   - Methane: ~37 cm/s
   - Propane: ~46 cm/s
   - Hydrogen: ~300 cm/s (extremely fast)

**Sketch — Refrigerant Safety Group Matrix:**

| | **Class 1** (No Flame) | **Class 2L** (Mild) | **Class 2** (Flammable) | **Class 3** (High Flam) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A (Low Tox)** | A1 — R-134a, R-410A, R-744 | A2L — R-32, R-1234yf | A2 — R-152a | A3 — R-290, R-600a |
| **B (High Tox)** | B1 — R-123, R-245fa | B2L — R-717 (NH₃) | B2 — (rare) | B3 — (rare) |

> **Marks distribution:** ASHRAE 34 explanation (2) + Flammability parameters (3) + Sketch/Table (2) = 7 marks

---

#### Q-A(b). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**An industrial cold storage uses ammonia (R-717) as refrigerant. The room dimensions are 10 m × 8 m × 4 m. If a leak of 5 kg occurs, calculate: (i) the concentration of NH₃ in the room in ppm, (ii) whether this is below the IDLH of 300 ppm, and (iii) suggest two safety devices to be installed.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $V_{\text{room}} = 10 \times 8 \times 4 = 320$ m³
- $m_{\text{NH}_3} = 5$ kg
- $M_{\text{NH}_3} = 17$ g/mol
- $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.2$ kg/m³
- IDLH for NH₃ = **300 ppm**

**(i) Concentration in ppm:**

Step 1 — Moles of NH₃ leaked:
$$n = \dfrac{m}{M} = \dfrac{5{,}000 \text{ g}}{17 \text{ g/mol}} = 294.12 \text{ mol}$$

Step 2 — Volume of NH₃ at STP (22.4 L/mol):
$$V_{\text{NH}_3} = 294.12 \times 22.4 = 6,588.24 \text{ L} = 6.588 \text{ m}^3$$

Step 3 — Volume fraction:
$$y = \dfrac{6.588}{320} = 0.02059 = 2.059\%$$

Step 4 — Convert to ppm:
$$C = 0.02059 \times 10^6 = 20{,}588 \text{ ppm}$$

**(ii) Comparison with IDLH:**

$$\text{IDLH} = 300 \text{ ppm}$$

$$\dfrac{C}{\text{IDLH}} = \dfrac{20{,}588}{300} = 68.6$$

**Conclusion:** The concentration is **68.6 times** the IDLH value. **This is EXTREMELY HAZARDOUS — immediate evacuation is mandatory.** The leak must be treated as a **life-threatening emergency.**

**(iii) Two Essential Safety Devices:**

1. **Ammonia Leak Detector with Alarm:** Continuously monitors NH₃ concentration; triggers alarm and ventilation above 25 ppm (OEL). Critical for early warning.

2. **Emergency Ventilation System (30 ACH):** Mechanical exhaust fans providing minimum **30 air changes per hour** to rapidly dilute any leaked refrigerant below toxic thresholds.

**Bonus devices (any 1 more):**
- SCBA (Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus) stations at all entry points
- Emergency Shut-off Valves for rapid section isolation
- Water spray system (ammonia is highly water-soluble — water curtains absorb NH₃ effectively)
- Eye-wash and emergency shower stations

> **Marks distribution:** (i) Calculation (3) + (ii) Comparison (1) + (iii) Devices (3) = 7 marks

---

### 📌 Question B (14 Marks) [INTERNAL CHOICE]

#### Q-B(a). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Explain the following with respect to refrigerant safety: (i) ODP and GWP, (ii) TEWI, and (iii) Charge limitation as per ASHRAE 15.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) ODP and GWP:**

- **ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential):** A relative index indicating the ability of a refrigerant to deplete stratospheric ozone. R-11 (CFC-11) is the reference with **ODP = 1.0**.
$$\text{ODP} = \dfrac{\text{Ozone destroyed by 1 kg of refrigerant}}{\text{Ozone destroyed by 1 kg of R-11}}$$
Examples: R-12 → 1.0, R-22 → 0.055, R-134a → 0, R-410A → 0

- **GWP (Global Warming Potential):** A relative index of the **infrared radiative forcing** of 1 kg of refrigerant over 100 years, relative to 1 kg of CO₂ (= 1.0).
$$\text{GWP} = \dfrac{\text{Integrated radiative forcing of 1 kg gas over 100 yr}}{\text{Same for 1 kg CO}_2 \text{ over 100 yr}}$$
Examples: R-134a → 1,430, R-410A → 2,088, R-32 → 675, R-290 → 3.3, R-717 → 0

**(ii) TEWI (Total Equivalent Warming Impact):**

TEWI quantifies the **lifetime global warming effect** of an entire refrigeration system, including both **direct emissions** (leakage, service) and **indirect emissions** (CO₂ from electricity generation).

$$\text{TEWI} = \underbrace{\text{GWP} \cdot L \cdot n}_{\text{Direct}} + \underbrace{n \cdot E \cdot \beta}_{\text{Indirect}}$$

Where:
- $\text{GWP}$ = Global Warming Potential of refrigerant
- $L$ = Annual leakage rate (kg/year)
- $n$ = System operating life (years)
- $E$ = Annual energy consumption (kWh)
- $\beta$ = CO₂ emission per kWh of grid electricity (kg CO₂/kWh)

**Significance:** TEWI is used to compare refrigerants not just on GWP but on **system-level climate impact**. A low-GWP but energy-inefficient system may have higher TEWI than a high-GWP but efficient one.

**(iii) Charge Limitation per ASHRAE 15:**

ASHRAE Standard 15 limits the **maximum refrigerant charge** in occupied spaces to prevent the **Refrigerant Concentration Limit (RCL)** from being exceeded in case of a full leak into the smallest occupied room.

**Formula:**
$$m_{\text{max}} = k \cdot A_{\text{floor}}$$

Where:
- $k$ = Safety factor depending on safety group (kg/m²)
- $A_{\text{floor}}$ = Floor area of the smallest occupied room (m²)

| Safety Group | $k$ value (kg/m²) |
|--------------|---------------------|
| A1 | **0.4** |
| A2L / B2L | **0.15** |
| A2 / B2 | **0.06** |
| A3 / B3 | **0.008** |

> **Marks distribution:** (i) ODP/GWP (2) + (ii) TEWI (2.5) + (iii) Charge limit (2.5) = 7 marks

---

#### Q-B(b). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Describe the procedure for safe handling, storage, and transportation of refrigerants. List the PPE required.**

**Model Answer:**

**Procedure for Safe Handling of Refrigerants:**

1. **Storage of Refrigerant Cylinders:**
   - Store in a cool (below 50°C), dry, well-ventilated area away from direct sunlight and heat sources
   - Keep away from ignition sources, oxidizers, and corrosives
   - Cylinders must be **stored upright** and **secured with chains** to prevent falling
   - Clearly labelled with refrigerant number, net weight, and hazard class
   - Maximum stacking height: **2 cylinders** for safety
   - Empty and full cylinders stored **separately**

2. **Transportation:**
   - Cylinders must be transported in **upright position** in well-ventilated vehicles
   - **Valve protection caps** must be in place
   - Secured against rolling/impact using chocks and straps
   - Vehicle must display **hazard placards** as per ADR (UN recommendations)
   - Avoid transport in passenger compartments — use open trucks or well-ventilated vans
   - Driver must be trained in handling refrigerant emergencies

3. **Safe Handling Procedure:**
   - Always work in **well-ventilated areas**
   - **Wear appropriate PPE** (see below) at all times
   - Use only **calibrated charging equipment** and **recovery machines**
   - Never overfill cylinders — leave **20% ullage** (vapour space) for expansion
   - Use **leak detectors** (electronic or soap solution) before charging
   - **Purge system** with nitrogen before brazing
   - **Do not mix** refrigerants in a single cylinder (use separate cylinders and labels)
   - Use proper **recovery/recycling machines** before opening any system for service — never vent to atmosphere
   - Keep **fire extinguisher** (CO₂ or dry powder) nearby when handling flammable refrigerants

4. **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) Required:**

| PPE Item | Purpose / When to Use |
|----------|----------------------|
| **Safety Goggles / Face Shield** | Protection against liquid refrigerant splash (can cause frostbite and eye damage) |
| **Chemical-resistant gloves** | Handling ammonia, hydrocarbons, oils |
| **Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus (SCBA)** | For ammonia leak emergencies or confined spaces |
| **Safety shoes with steel toe** | Protection from falling cylinders |
| **Apron (chemical-resistant)** | When charging large amounts of toxic refrigerants |
| **Ear protection** | In compressor rooms (>85 dB) |
| **Hard hat** | In industrial cold storage with overhead hazards |

> **Marks distribution:** Storage (2) + Transport (1.5) + Handling (1.5) + PPE (2) = 7 marks

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (and lose 2-3 marks):**
> 
> 1. **Confusing ODP and GWP:** ODP is for **ozone** (R-11 = 1), GWP is for **global warming** (CO₂ = 1). Many students write the wrong reference. → **Lose 1 mark**
> 
> 2. **Confusing A2L and A2:** A2L is **mildly** flammable (burning velocity ≤ 10 cm/s), NOT just "Class 2 L". Many students miss the "L" suffix. → **Lose 1 mark**
> 
> 3. **IDLH vs OEL confusion:** OEL is for **routine 8-hr exposure**, IDLH is for **immediate life threat**. NH₃: OEL = 25 ppm, IDLH = 300 ppm. → **Lose 1 mark**
> 
> 4. **Missing units in ppm calculations:** Always write **ppm by volume** explicitly. → **Lose 0.5 mark**
> 
> 5. **Forgetting the "AND" in A2L definition:** A2L requires **Bv ≤ 10 cm/s AND Hc ≤ 19 MJ/kg** (both conditions). Missing "AND" → **Lose 1 mark**
> 
> 6. **Writing "R-22 is safe" in modern systems:** R-22 is being **phased out** under Montreal Protocol due to ODP = 0.055. Use R-410A or R-32. → **Lose 0.5 mark**

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 High-Density Revision Summary — Read Before Exam:**

### ✅ Core Safety Concepts
- ☐ **ASHRAE 34** classifies refrigerants by **Toxicity (A/B) + Flammability (1/2L/2/3)**
- ☐ **ASHRAE 15** defines safe design, charge limits, ventilation, and occupancy rules
- ☐ **Toxicity Reference:** OEL ≥ 400 ppm → **A**; OEL < 400 ppm → **B**
- ☐ **Flammability Reference:** Class 1 (no flame), 2L (Bv ≤ 10, Hc ≤ 19), 2 (Bv > 10), 3 (Bv > 10, Hc > 19)
- ☐ **ODP** reference is **R-11 = 1.0**; **GWP** reference is **CO₂ = 1.0**
- ☐ **RCL = Refrigerant Concentration Limit** = must be below OEL when full charge leaks into smallest room
- ☐ **Charge limit formula:** $m_{\text{max}} = k \cdot V_{\text{room}}$ (or $k \cdot A_{\text{floor}}$ in some standards)
- ☐ **IDLH for NH₃ = 300 ppm**; OEL = 25 ppm; AIT = 651°C; LFL = 15%; UFL = 28%

### ✅ Common Refrigerant Quick Recall
- ☐ **R-134a:** A1, ODP=0, GWP=1430, used in domestic AC/chillers
- ☐ **R-410A:** A1, ODP=0, GWP=2088, modern split AC
- ☐ **R-32:** A2L, ODP=0, GWP=675, future of AC
- ☐ **R-290 (Propane):** A3, ODP=0, GWP=3.3, charge ≤ 150 g in domestic fridge
- ☐ **R-717 (Ammonia):** B2L, ODP=0, GWP=0, industrial cold storage (TOXIC)
- ☐ **R-744 (CO₂):** A1, ODP=0, GWP=1, transcritical systems (HIGH PRESSURE)

### ✅ Safety Devices (Must Memorize Function)
- ☐ **PRV** → Pressure Relief Valve (vent when over-pressure)
- ☐ **HP Cut-out** → Compressor trip on high discharge pressure
- ☐ **LP Cut-out** → Compressor trip on low suction pressure
- ☐ **Oil Pressure Switch** → Trip on low oil pressure
- ☐ **Rupture Disc** → Single-use burst protection
- ☐ **Phase Reversal Relay** → Prevents reverse 3-phase rotation
- ☐ **ELCB/RCCB** → Earth leakage protection (personnel safety)
- ☐ **MPCB** → Motor protection from overload
- ☐ **Leak Detector** → Continuous refrigerant monitoring
- ☐ **SCBA** → Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus (for NH₃ emergencies)

### ✅ Engineering Calculations to Practice
- ☐ Charge limit from room volume × $k$ factor
- ☐ Concentration of leak in ppm using molar volume at STP (22.4 L/mol)
- ☐ Le Chatelier's rule for blend LFL: $\text{LFL}_{\text{mix}} = 100 / \sum (y_i / \text{LFL}_i)$
- ☐ TEWI: $\text{GWP} \cdot L \cdot n + n \cdot E \cdot \beta$
- ☐ Oxygen depletion: $\text{O}_2\%_{\text{new}} = 21\% \times (1 - y_{\text{refr}})$

### ✅ Common Industrial Codes & Standards
- ☐ ASHRAE 15 — Safety Code for Mechanical Refrigeration
- ☐ ASHRAE 34 — Refrigerant Classification
- ☐ IS 732 — Electrical Wiring
- ☐ IS 5129 — Cold Storage Practice
- ☐ IIAR — Ammonia Standards
- ☐ NFPA — Fire Prevention

### ✅ Real-World Industrial Practice
- ☐ Ammonia systems require **emergency ventilation ≥ 30 ACH** (Air Changes per Hour)
- ☐ Hydrocarbon (R-290/R-600a) charge in domestic fridge is **limited to 150 g** for safety
- ☐ R-744 transcritical systems operate at **130 bar** — pressure relief set at **140 bar**
- ☐ **Never vent refrigerant to atmosphere** — always use a recovery machine
- ☐ **Do not mix refrigerants** — requires separate cylinder, recovery, and labelling
- ☐ **Water spray** is effective for ammonia leak control (NH₃ is highly water-soluble)

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermodynamic and Economical
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Thermodynamic and Economical ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Thermodynamic Analysis — The Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Thermodynamic Analysis of Refrigeration** is the rigorous application of the First and Second Laws of Thermodynamics to evaluate the **energy transformation quality**, **efficiency**, and **performance limits** of a refrigeration / heat-pump system operating between two thermal reservoirs. It seeks to answer the question: *"Given a fixed amount of energy input (work), how effectively does the system extract heat from the cold space?"* The cornerstone metric of this analysis is the **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** — a dimensionless number quantifying the *useful refrigeration effect obtained per unit of work consumed*. ### 1.2 Economical Analysis — The Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Economical Analysis** is the systematic evaluation of the **life-cycle monetary cost** of owning and operating a refrigeration plant. It combines **capital expenditure (CAPEX)**, **operating expenditure (OPEX)**, **maintenance cost**, and **salvage value** to determine the *cost per unit of refrigeration* and the *payback period* of the installation. It answers the engineering-commercial question: *"Which system delivers the lowest annualised cost over its useful life while meeting the cooling demand?"* ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Two Marks" Problem** Imagine you are a student preparing for the KTU university exam in a subject like this one. - **Thermodynamic Analysis** = checking your **accuracy and depth of understanding**. Are you answering with full conceptual clarity, or are you wasting mental energy (work input) to produce just a few correct lines (cooling effect)? A high COP student gets many marks per unit of study effort. - **Economical Analysis** = checking your **time and money budget**. Did you spend ₹500 on a reference book (high CAPEX, low OPEX) or did you borrow it from a friend (zero CAPEX, but you owe a favour)? Which is the smarter *life-cycle* investment? In the same way, an engineer must design a plant that is **thermodynamically excellent** (high COP) **and** **economically viable** (low annual cost). A plant with the highest possible COP but prohibitive capital cost is engineering impractical; a cheap plant that consumes enormous electricity is engineering foolish. ### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics | Metric | Symbol | Standard Value (Indicative) | |---|---|---| | Coefficient of Performance (Refrigerator) | $COP_R$ | **2.0 – 4.0** for domestic systems | | Coefficient of Performance (Heat Pump) | $COP_{HP}$ | **3.0 – 5.0** for residential heat pumps | | Energy Efficiency Ratio | $EER$ | **8 – 12 BTU/hr·W** | | Figure of Merit | $FOM$ | Ideal value = **1.0** | | Refrigerating Effect | $RE$ | Expressed in **kW or Tons of Refrigeration (TR)** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **1 Ton of Refrigeration (1 TR) = 3.517 kW = 210 kJ/min = 50.4 kcal/min** > This is the heat removal rate required to freeze **1 ton (1000 kg) of water at 0 °C into ice at 0 °C in 24 hours**. ### 1.5 The Engineering Trade-off Triangle ``` HIGH COP /\ / \ / \ / \ /________\ LOW CAPITAL LOW RUNNING COST COST ``` > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** No refrigeration plant can simultaneously maximise all three corners of this triangle. The designer's job is to find the **economically optimum balance** — the famous *Kneepoint* — where the marginal cost of improving COP equals the marginal saving in running cost. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The First-Law Definition of COP By direct application of the **First Law of Thermodynamics** to a cyclic refrigeration device (where net change in internal energy over a complete cycle = 0): $$W_{net,in} \;=\; Q_H \;-\; Q_L$$ The Coefficient of Performance for a **Refrigerator** is defined as: $$\boxed{\,COP_R \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Desired Effect}}{\text{Work Input}} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}\,}$$ For a **Heat Pump** (where the *desired effect* is the heat rejected at the high-temperature side): $$\boxed{\,COP_{HP} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net,in}} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_L}\,}$$ ### 2.2 The Second-Law (Reversed Carnot) Limit For a *reversible* (i.e., internally and externally reversible) refrigeration cycle operating between a cold reservoir at $T_L$ and a hot reservoir at $T_H$: $$\boxed{\,COP_{Carnot,R} \;=\; \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}\,}$$ $$\boxed{\,COP_{Carnot,HP} \;=\; \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}\,}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Figure of Merit (FOM)** is the ratio of actual COP to the Carnot COP — a measure of *how close a real machine comes to thermodynamic perfection*: $$FOM \;=\; \dfrac{COP_{actual}}{COP_{Carnot}} \;\le\; 1$$ ### 2.3 Fundamental Identity $$\boxed{\,COP_{HP} \;-\; COP_R \;=\; 1\,}$$ This is an identity that holds for *any* refrigeration device operating between two reservoirs — reversible or irreversible, simple or multi-stage. Proof follows from the definitions directly. ### 2.4 The Reciprocating-Compressor Volumetric Figure of Merit For a reciprocating compressor, the volumetric efficiency depends on clearance volume, pressure ratio, and polytropic index: $$\boxed{\,\eta_{vol} \;=\; 1 \;+\; C \;-\; C\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}\,}$$ where: - $C$ = clearance ratio $= V_c / V_s$ - $n$ = polytropic index of compression - $P_2/P_1$ = pressure ratio across compressor ### 2.5 Economical Analysis — The Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) > [!IMPORTANT] > A rupee today is worth more than a rupee ten years from now. **Time Value of Money** must be applied when comparing refrigeration plants of different capital costs and different operating costs over unequal lives. $$\boxed{\,CRF \;=\; \dfrac{i\,(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}\,}$$ The **Annualised Total Cost** of owning a refrigeration plant: $$C_{annual} \;=\; (P \;\times\; CRF) \;+\; C_{operating}$$ where: - $P$ = Present-day capital cost of the plant (₹) - $i$ = annual rate of interest (decimal, e.g. 0.10 for 10%) - $n$ = economic life of the plant (years) - $C_{operating}$ = annual running cost (energy + maintenance + labour, in ₹/year) ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference Table | # | Formula | Engineering Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $COP_R = Q_L / W_{net}$ | Cooling obtained per unit work | dimensionless | | 2 | $COP_{HP} = Q_H / W_{net}$ | Heat delivered per unit work | dimensionless | | 3 | $COP_{Carnot,R} = T_L / (T_H - T_L)$ | Reversible refrigeration limit | dimensionless | | 4 | $COP_{Carnot,HP} = T_H / (T_H - T_L)$ | Reversible heat-pump limit | dimensionless | | 5 | $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ | Fundamental identity | dimensionless | | 6 | $FOM = COP_{actual} / COP_{Carnot}$ | Reversibility assessment | dimensionless | | 7 | $\eta_{vol} = 1 + C - C(P_2/P_1)^{1/n}$ | Reciprocating compressor vol. eff. | dimensionless | | 8 | $EER = (Q_L \text{ in BTU/hr}) / (W \text{ in W})$ | Practical AC rating | BTU/(hr·W) | | 9 | $1 TR = 3.517 \; kW$ | Standard refrigeration tonnage | kW | | 10 | $CRF = i(1+i)^n / [(1+i)^n - 1]$ | Annualisation factor | yr$^{-1}$ | | 11 | $C_{annual} = P \cdot CRF + C_{op}$ | Total annualised cost | ₹/yr | | 12 | $PBP = (P_{extra}) / (S_{annual})$ | Payback period | years | | 13 | $P_{extra}$ | Extra capital invested in efficient system | ₹ | | 14 | $S_{annual}$ | Annual saving in running cost | ₹/yr | | 15 | $m = Q_L / (h_g - h_f)$ | Mass flow of refrigerant | kg/s | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Units Pitfall:** All temperatures in the Carnot-COP expressions **must be in Kelvin (K)**. A common KTU valuation error is using °C and arriving at nonsense values. Convert using $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$. ### 2.7 Real-World Utility of These Equations > [!NOTE] > - **Refrigeration industry**: $COP_R$ decides the **BEE Star Rating** of air-conditioners in India. A 5-star AC has $COP_R \geq 3.5$; a 1-star AC has $COP_R \approx 2.1$. > - **Cold-storage design**: The combination of **FOM and annualised cost** is what determines whether a cold-storage owner should opt for ammonia (high COP, high CAPEX) or HFC blends (lower COP, lower CAPEX). > - **Industrial process cooling**: The **payback period** is the key metric for convincing plant managers to invest ₹50 lakhs in a variable-speed-drive compressor upgrade. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — COP of Carnot Refrigerator from First + Second Laws **Given:** A reversible refrigeration cycle operating between a cold reservoir at absolute temperature $T_L$ and a hot reservoir at absolute temperature $T_H$. **Step 1 — Energy Balance (First Law)** For a cyclic device, the net work input equals the net heat transfer: $$W_{net,in} \;=\; Q_H \;-\; Q_L$$ *Reasoning:* Since the working fluid returns to its initial state after every cycle, $\Delta U_{cycle} = 0$. Therefore all the work supplied must leave the system as the difference between the rejected heat and absorbed heat. **Step 2 — Reversibility Condition (Second Law)** For a *reversible* cycle, the total entropy change of the universe (reservoirs + system) is zero: $$\dfrac{Q_L}{T_L} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_H}{T_H}$$ *Reasoning:* The system undergoes a cyclic process ($\Delta S_{system}=0$), and because both reservoirs are at constant temperature, $\Delta S_{universe} = -Q_L/T_L + Q_H/T_H = 0$ for reversibility. **Step 3 — Solve for $Q_H$ in terms of $Q_L$** $$Q_H \;=\; Q_L \;\cdot\; \dfrac{T_H}{T_L}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute back into the work expression** $$W_{net,in} \;=\; Q_L \;\cdot\; \dfrac{T_H}{T_L} \;-\; Q_L \;=\; Q_L \;\cdot\; \left(\dfrac{T_H - T_L}{T_L}\right)$$ **Step 5 — Form the COP ratio** $$COP_R \;=\; \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_L \cdot \left(\dfrac{T_H - T_L}{T_L}\right)} \;=\; \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$ $$\boxed{\,COP_{Carnot,R} \;=\; \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}\,}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation — COP of Carnot Heat Pump **Step 1 — Use the same energy balance as before** $$W_{net,in} \;=\; Q_H \;-\; Q_L$$ **Step 2 — Express $Q_L$ in terms of $Q_H$** $$Q_L \;=\; Q_H \;\cdot\; \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute** $$W_{net,in} \;=\; Q_H \;-\; Q_H \cdot \dfrac{T_L}{T_H} \;=\; Q_H \;\cdot\; \left(\dfrac{T_H - T_L}{T_H}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Form the COP for the heat pump (desired effect = $Q_H$)** $$COP_{HP} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net,in}} \;=\; \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$$ $$\boxed{\,COP_{Carnot,HP} \;=\; \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}\,}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation — The Identity $COP_{HP} - COP_R = 1$ **Step 1 — Start from the two definitions** $$COP_{HP} - COP_R \;=\; \dfrac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_L} \;-\; \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}$$ **Step 2 — Common denominator** $$COP_{HP} - COP_R \;=\; \dfrac{Q_H - Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}$$ **Step 3 — Simplify** $$\boxed{\,COP_{HP} - COP_R \;=\; 1\,}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This identity holds for **every** refrigeration cycle — reversible, irreversible, ideal, or real. It is independent of the working fluid and independent of temperature. If your calculation violates this identity, you have made an arithmetic error. ### 3.4 Derivation — Effect of Sub-cooling and Superheating on Refrigeration Effect **Given:** A simple vapour compression cycle using R-134a. - State 1: Saturated vapour at evaporator exit (after compressor inlet) - State 2: Superheated vapour at compressor exit - State 3: Saturated liquid at condenser exit - State 4: Liquid-vapour mixture after expansion valve **Step 1 — Refrigerating Effect per kg** $$RE \;=\; h_1 \;-\; h_4 \;=\; (h_1 - h_3) \;+\; (h_3 - h_4)$$ **Step 2 — With sub-cooling** (State 3′ = sub-cooled liquid below $T_{sat}$ at condenser pressure): $$RE_{subcooled} \;=\; h_1 \;-\; h_4 \;=\; (h_1 - h_3') \;+\; (h_3' - h_4)$$ $$RE_{subcooled} \;-\; RE_{saturated} \;=\; h_3 \;-\; h_3' \;=\; C_{p,l} \;\cdot\; (T_{3,sat} - T_{3'})$$ *Conclusion:* Sub-cooling **increases** refrigeration effect by $C_{p,l} \cdot \Delta T_{subcool}$ per kg. **Step 3 — With superheating** (State 1′ = superheated vapour above $T_{sat}$ at evaporator pressure): $$RE_{superheat} \;=\; h_{1'} \;-\; h_4 \;=\; (h_{1,sat} - h_4) \;+\; C_{p,v} \;\cdot\; (T_{1'} - T_{1,sat})$$ *Conclusion:* **Useful superheating** (occurring *inside* the evaporator, by absorbing useful cooling load) also increases refrigeration effect by $C_{p,v} \cdot \Delta T_{superheat}$ per kg. > [!WARNING] > **Negative superheating** — when suction line heat-gain raises the refrigerant temperature *after* it has left the evaporator — is **parasitic**, not useful. It increases $h_1$ but does *not* increase the cooling delivered to the customer, so $RE$ is unchanged but compressor work increases, **reducing COP**. ### 3.5 Derivation — Economical Optimum (Most-Economical Evaporator Pressure) For a multi-evaporator system or a system with adjustable expansion valves, the operating condition that minimises total annualised cost can be found by setting the derivative of total cost to zero: $$\dfrac{d(C_{annual})}{dP_{evap}} \;=\; 0$$ This gives the **kneepoint condition**: $$(P_{evap})_{opt} \;\Rightarrow\; \dfrac{d(C_{cap})}{dP_{evap}} \;+\; \dfrac{d(C_{op})}{dP_{evap}} \;=\; 0$$ $$-\;\dfrac{dC_{cap}}{dP_{evap}} \;\;=\;\; \dfrac{dC_{op}}{dP_{evap}}$$ *Engineering interpretation:* The most economical evaporator pressure is reached when the **marginal increase in operating cost** (from a higher compressor pressure ratio) **exactly balances** the **marginal decrease in capital cost** (from a smaller, cheaper evaporator at lower pressure). ### 3.6 Worked Numerical — FOM and Annualised Cost > [!NOTE] > **Example (Typical KTU-style problem):** A refrigeration plant operates between $T_L = -10\,°C$ and $T_H = 40\,°C$. It has a measured refrigerating effect of $Q_L = 50\,kW$ and a measured power input of $W = 18\,kW$. The capital cost is ₹8,00,000 and the operating cost is ₹3,00,000 per year. Take $i = 10\%$, $n = 10$ years. Compute the FOM and the annualised total cost. **Solution:** **(a) Actual COP and Carnot COP** $$COP_{actual} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_L}{W} \;=\; \dfrac{50}{18} \;=\; 2.778$$ Temperatures in Kelvin: $$T_L \;=\; -10 \;+\; 273.15 \;=\; 263.15 \;K$$ $$T_H \;=\; 40 \;+\; 273.15 \;=\; 313.15 \;K$$ $$COP_{Carnot} \;=\; \dfrac{263.15}{313.15 - 263.15} \;=\; \dfrac{263.15}{50} \;=\; 5.263$$ **(b) Figure of Merit** $$FOM \;=\; \dfrac{COP_{actual}}{COP_{Carnot}} \;=\; \dfrac{2.778}{5.263} \;=\; 0.528$$ **(c) Capital Recovery Factor** $$CRF \;=\; \dfrac{0.10 \cdot (1.10)^{10}}{(1.10)^{10} - 1} \;=\; \dfrac{0.10 \cdot 2.5937}{1.5937} \;=\; 0.16275 \; yr^{-1}$$ **(d) Annualised Capital Cost** $$C_{cap,annual} \;=\; 8{,}00{,}000 \;\times\; 0.16275 \;=\; \text{₹}\,1{,}30{,}200 \text{ per year}$$ **(e) Total Annualised Cost** $$C_{annual} \;=\; 1{,}30{,}200 \;+\; 3{,}00{,}000 \;=\; \text{₹}\,4{,}30{,}200 \text{ per year}$$ **(f) Cost per TR per year** $$\text{Cost per TR} \;=\; \dfrac{4{,}30{,}200}{50/3.517} \;=\; \dfrac{4{,}30{,}200}{14.217} \;\approx\; \text{₹}\,30{,}260 \;/\; TR \cdot yr$$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Flow — Thermodynamic Performance Evaluation The following Mermaid block captures the *logical decision sequence* a KTU-graduate engineer follows when evaluating a refrigeration plant thermodynamically. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[START: Plant Operating Data] --> B[Record Measured Values
Q_L W_in T_L T_H] B --> C[Convert All Temperatures to Kelvin
T_K = T_C + 273.15] C --> D[Compute Actual COP_R
COP_R_ACT = Q_L over W_in] D --> E[Compute Carnot COP_R
COP_R_CARN = T_L over T_H minus T_L] E --> F[Compute Figure of Merit
FOM = COP_R_ACT over COP_R_CARN] F --> G{FOM Decision Logic} G -->|FOM less than 0.40| H[POOR PLANT
Retrofit Required] G -->|FOM between 0.40 and 0.65| I[ACCEPTABLE
Plan Maintenance] G -->|FOM greater than 0.65| J[GOOD PLANT
No Action] H --> K[END] I --> K[END] J --> K[END] ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Flow — Economic Comparison of Two Refrigeration Plants ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[PLANT A
CAPEX = P_A
OPEX per yr = OA] --> ADD[Apply Time Value of Money
CRF = i times one plus i to n
over one plus i to n minus 1] P2[PLANT B
CAPEX = P_B
OPEX per yr = OB] --> ADD ADD --> AC1[Annualised Cost A
ACA = P_A times CRF plus OA] ADD --> AC2[Annualised Cost B
ACB = P_B times CRF plus OB] AC1 --> CMP{Compare
ACA versus ACB} AC2 --> CMP CMP -->|ACA smaller| WIN1[Plant A is Economically Superior] CMP -->|ACB smaller| WIN2[Plant B is Economically Superior] CMP -->|ACA equals ACB| WIN3[Indifferent Choice
Use Non Monetary Criteria] ``` ### 4.3 Sankey-style Block Architecture — Energy Flow in a Vapour-Compression Refrigeration Plant ```mermaid flowchart TD INP[Electrical Work Input W_in
100 percent] --> COMP[Compressor
Adds Pressure Energy] COMP --> COND[Condenser
Rejects Q_H to Ambient] COND --> VLV[Expansion Valve
Drops Pressure] VLV --> EVAP[Evaporator
Absorbs Q_L from Cold Space] EVAP --> SUC[Suction Line
Returns to Compressor] SUC --> COMP EVAP -. Q_L Cooling Effect .-> LOAD[Useful Cooling Output
e.g. 40 to 60 percent of W_in] COND -. Q_H Heat Rejection .-> AMB[Ambient Environment
e.g. 140 to 160 percent of W_in] ``` ### 4.4 Block Architecture — The Two-Layered Engineering Decision Pyramid ```mermaid flowchart TD TOP[Final Design Choice] --> LYR1[LAYER 1 Thermodynamic Check] TOP --> LYR2[LAYER 2 Economic Check] LYR1 --> CHK1[Does COP satisfy minimum efficiency standard] LYR2 --> CHK2[Does PBP fall within company acceptable limit] CHK1 -->|Yes| PR1[Pass Thermodynamic Filter] CHK1 -->|No| FAIL1[Reject Design Immediately] CHK2 -->|Yes| PR2[Pass Economic Filter] CHK2 -->|No| FAIL2[Reject Design Immediately] PR1 --> OUT[Design Approved] PR2 --> OUT ``` ### 4.5 Block Architecture — Interaction Between Refrigerating Effect, Compressor Work, and COP ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1[Refrigerating Effect Q_L] --> COP[COP Quotient Q_L over W_in] W1[Compressor Work W_in] --> COP COP --> OUTPUT[System Performance Output] Q1 -. increased by subcooling .-> OUTPUT Q1 -. increased by useful superheat .-> OUTPUT W1 -. increased by superheat outside evaporator .-> OUTPUT W1 -. increased by high condenser pressure .-> OUTPUT ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper, Module 2]** Define the **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** of a refrigerator. Why is the COP of a refrigerator always greater than unity? **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** of a refrigerator is defined as the ratio of the desired cooling effect to the work input required: > $COP_R = Q_L / W_{net,in}$ > > $COP_R$ is always **greater than 1** because the *desired effect* ($Q_L$) is by definition the larger of the two energy quantities in the cycle — the cooling effect extracted from the cold space exceeds the work input that drives the cycle. Equivalently, since $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ and $COP_{HP} > 1$, it follows that $COP_R > 0$ and in practice always > 1. > > *For instance, a domestic refrigerator with $COP_R = 3$ delivers 3 kW of cooling for every 1 kW of electrical input.* **Mark Allocation:** Definition (1 m) + reasoning (1 m) + illustrative example (1 m). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary, Module 2]** What is **Figure of Merit (FOM)** of a refrigeration cycle? State its range. **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The **Figure of Merit** is the ratio of the actual COP of a refrigeration cycle to the COP of a reversed Carnot cycle operating between the same temperature limits: > $FOM = COP_{actual} / COP_{Carnot}$ > > Its value lies in the range $0 < FOM \le 1$. > - $FOM = 1$ corresponds to a perfectly reversible (ideal) cycle. > - $FOM < 1$ for all real (irreversible) cycles due to friction, heat-transfer irreversibility, throttling losses, and pressure drops. > - The closer FOM is to 1, the better the cycle performance. --- ### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 2]** **(a) [7 marks]** A vapour-compression refrigeration cycle operates between an evaporator temperature of $-15\,°C$ and a condenser temperature of $40\,°C$. Compute the **Carnot COP** and the **Carnot COP for the equivalent heat pump**. Verify the identity $COP_{HP} - COP_R = 1$. **(b) [7 marks]** A refrigerator working between the same temperature limits has an actual $COP_R = 2.0$. Calculate the **Figure of Merit** and state whether the cycle performance is acceptable. List **three practical measures** to improve the COP of the cycle. --- #### **Model Solution for Question A** **Part (a) — Carnot COP derivation** **Step 1 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin** $$T_L \;=\; -15 \;+\; 273.15 \;=\; 258.15 \;K$$ $$T_H \;=\; 40 \;+\; 273.15 \;=\; 313.15 \;K$$ **Step 2 — Carnot COP of refrigerator** $$COP_{Carnot,R} \;=\; \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} \;=\; \dfrac{258.15}{313.15 - 258.15} \;=\; \dfrac{258.15}{55.00} \;=\; 4.694$$ **Step 3 — Carnot COP of heat pump** $$COP_{Carnot,HP} \;=\; \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} \;=\; \dfrac{313.15}{55.00} \;=\; 5.694$$ **Step 4 — Verification of the identity** $$COP_{Carnot,HP} - COP_{Carnot,R} \;=\; 5.694 \;-\; 4.694 \;=\; 1.000 \;\;\checkmark$$ The identity $COP_{HP} - COP_R = 1$ is verified. --- **Part (b) — Figure of Merit and improvement measures** **Step 1 — Figure of Merit** $$FOM \;=\; \dfrac{COP_{actual}}{COP_{Carnot}} \;=\; \dfrac{2.0}{4.694} \;=\; 0.426$$ **Step 2 — Performance assessment** Since $FOM = 0.426$ lies in the range $0.40$ to $0.65$, the cycle is **acceptable** but has significant scope for improvement. An FOM below 0.40 would indicate poor performance requiring retrofits. **Step 3 — Three practical measures to improve COP** 1. **Sub-cooling the condensed liquid** before the expansion valve: Lowers $h_4$ (and increases $h_1 - h_4$) without raising compressor work, hence increasing $COP_R$. 2. **Useful superheating inside the evaporator**: Adds to the refrigerating effect per kg of refrigerant flow without adding to compressor work (provided the superheat occurs at evaporator pressure by absorbing useful cooling load). 3. **Reducing the temperature lift** $(T_H - T_L)$: Either by raising the evaporator pressure (warmer $T_L$) or by lowering the condenser pressure (cooler $T_H$). This directly raises $COP_{Carnot}$ and hence the achievable real-COP. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Question A** > 1. **Degree vs Kelvin (–2 marks common)**: $T_L$ *must* be converted to Kelvin. Using 258.15 and 313.15 is mandatory, not optional. If you write $-15$ and $40$ directly, your COP will be wildly incorrect. > 2. **FOM units check (–1 mark)**: FOM is dimensionless — don't append "BTU/hr" or any unit. > 3. **Practical measures (–1 mark per vague answer)**: "Improve insulation" is too generic — name the *thermodynamic mechanism*, e.g. "reduce condenser pressure by improving condenser cooling-water flow". --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 2]** **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)** and its role in the **economic comparison** of two refrigeration plants. Derive the expression for CRF. **(b) [7 marks]** Two refrigeration plants are available for the same duty: - **Plant X:** Capital cost = ₹12,00,000; running cost = ₹2,80,000/year - **Plant Y:** Capital cost = ₹9,00,000; running cost = ₹3,60,000/year Both plants have an economic life of 12 years. Take the rate of interest $i = 12\%$ per annum. Using the annualised cost method, determine **which plant is economically preferable** and compute the **payback period** if Plant X is chosen as the more efficient upgrade. --- #### **Model Solution for Question B** **Part (a) — Capital Recovery Factor derivation** The CRF converts the present-day capital expenditure $P$ into an equivalent *uniform annual cost* over the life $n$ years, taking into account the time value of money at interest rate $i$. Let the annual equivalent be $A$. The present value of an annuity $A$ paid for $n$ years at interest $i$ is: $$P \;=\; \dfrac{A}{i}\;\left[\,1 \;-\; \dfrac{1}{(1+i)^n}\,\right]$$ Solving for $A$: $$A \;=\; P \;\cdot\; \dfrac{i\,(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$$ $$\boxed{\,CRF \;=\; \dfrac{i\,(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}\,}$$ **Role in economic comparison:** CRF lets us compare two plants of different capital costs and different running costs on the *same annual basis*. The plant with the lower **total annualised cost** ($C_{annual} = P \cdot CRF + C_{op}$) is preferred, regardless of which has the higher initial purchase price. --- **Part (b) — Economic comparison** **Step 1 — Compute the CRF** $$CRF \;=\; \dfrac{0.12 \times (1.12)^{12}}{(1.12)^{12} - 1}$$ Calculate $(1.12)^{12}$: $$(1.12)^{12} \;\approx\; 3.8960$$ $$CRF \;=\; \dfrac{0.12 \times 3.8960}{3.8960 - 1} \;=\; \dfrac{0.46752}{2.8960} \;=\; 0.16144 \; yr^{-1}$$ **Step 2 — Annualised capital cost of Plant X** $$C_{cap,X} \;=\; 12{,}00{,}000 \;\times\; 0.16144 \;=\; \text{₹}\,1{,}93{,}730 \text{ per year}$$ **Step 3 — Total annualised cost of Plant X** $$C_{annual,X} \;=\; 1{,}93{,}730 \;+\; 2{,}80{,}000 \;=\; \text{₹}\,4{,}73{,}730 \text{ per year}$$ **Step 4 — Annualised capital cost of Plant Y** $$C_{cap,Y} \;=\; 9{,}00{,}000 \;\times\; 0.16144 \;=\; \text{₹}\,1{,}45{,}296 \text{ per year}$$ **Step 5 — Total annualised cost of Plant Y** $$C_{annual,Y} \;=\; 1{,}45{,}296 \;+\; 3{,}60{,}000 \;=\; \text{₹}\,5{,}05{,}296 \text{ per year}$$ **Step 6 — Decision** Since $C_{annual,X} = ₹\,4{,}73{,}730 < C_{annual,Y} = ₹\,5{,}05{,}296$, **Plant X is economically preferable**. **Step 7 — Payback period (assuming Plant X is the upgrade)** Extra capital invested in Plant X: $$P_{extra} \;=\; 12{,}00{,}000 \;-\; 9{,}00{,}000 \;=\; \text{₹}\,3{,}00{,}000$$ Annual saving in operating cost: $$S_{annual} \;=\; 3{,}60{,}000 \;-\; 2{,}80{,}000 \;=\; \text{₹}\,80{,}000 \text{ per year}$$ Payback period: $$PBP \;=\; \dfrac{P_{extra}}{S_{annual}} \;=\; \dfrac{3{,}00{,}000}{80{,}000} \;=\; 3.75 \; \text{years}$$ The plant owner recovers the extra ₹3 lakhs in **3 years 9 months**. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Question B** > 1. **CRF denominator confusion (–2 marks common)**: The denominator is $(1+i)^n - 1$, *not* $(1+i)^n$. Forgetting the "minus 1" gives a wrong CRF. > 2. **Annualised cost vs simple subtraction (–2 marks)**: You *cannot* simply subtract capital cost from running cost. The capital cost **must** be multiplied by CRF before adding to the running cost. > 3. **Units consistency (–1 mark)**: Capital cost in ₹, running cost in ₹/year, CRF in yr$^{-1}$, so $P \cdot CRF$ correctly yields ₹/year. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Thermodynamic & Economical Analysis** - [ ] **COP$_R$ = $Q_L / W$**, **COP$_{HP}$ = $Q_H / W$** — definitions must be quoted correctly. - [ ] **COP$_{HP}$ − COP$_R$ = 1** — universal identity, valid for *any* cycle. - [ ] **COP$_{Carnot,R}$ = $T_L / (T_H − T_L)$**, **COP$_{Carnot,HP}$ = $T_H / (T_H − T_L)$**. - [ ] **Always convert °C → K** before plugging into Carnot equations. $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$. - [ ] **FOM** ranges $0 < FOM \le 1$. Practical "good" plants: $FOM \ge 0.65$. "Acceptable": $0.40 \le FOM < 0.65$. "Poor": $FOM < 0.40$. - [ ] **EER** is the BTU/hr per Watt equivalent of $COP_R \times 3.412$. - [ ] **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 210 kJ/min** — must be quoted in the standard form for full marks. - [ ] **Methods to improve COP**: sub-cooling, useful superheating, lowering temperature lift, reducing throttling losses, multi-staging with flash gas removal, economiser cycles. - [ ] **$CRF = i(1+i)^n / [(1+i)^n − 1]$** — formula must be written from memory. - [ ] **Annualised cost** = Capital cost × CRF + Operating cost (always combine on the *same annual basis*). - [ ] **Payback period** = Extra capital invested / Annual saving in running cost. - [ ] **Negative superheating** (in suction line, outside evaporator) is *parasitic* — it increases $h_1$ without increasing the cooling delivered to the customer, hence reduces COP. - [ ] **Useful superheating** (occurring *inside* the evaporator by absorbing cooling load) is beneficial. - [ ] **Time value of money** is non-negotiable in any KTU economic-analysis problem — never compare ₹X now with ₹Y ten years from now on a 1:1 basis. > [!NOTE] > **Final Study Tip:** Whenever you see a numerical problem in the KTU paper combining *thermodynamic* and *economical* analysis, expect the question to have two sub-parts: **(a)** compute $COP$, $FOM$, or related thermodynamic quantities; **(b)** compute $CRF$, annualised cost, and payback period. Master the *two-layer* answering approach: thermodynamic layer first, then economic layer, both on the same annual basis.

topics.title: Psychrometry - Thermodynamic Properties of Moist Air
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Psychrometry - Thermodynamic Properties of Moist Air

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Psychrometry** (from the Greek *psychros* — cold, *metron* — measure) is the branch of engineering thermodynamics that deals with the measurement, analysis, and graphical representation of the **thermodynamic properties of moist air** — a binary mechanical mixture of **dry air** and **water vapour** that behaves as an ideal gas over the range of conditions encountered in refrigeration, air-conditioning, and cold-storage design.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (Module 3, PEAGT411), the following constitute the **fundamental psychrometric properties** that a student must master:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Mandate (Module 3):** *The student should be able to define, derive, and apply the thermodynamic properties of moist air, namely — Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT), Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT), Dew Point Temperature (DPT), Specific Humidity (ω), Relative Humidity (φ), Degree of Saturation (μ), Specific Enthalpy (h), and Specific Volume (v).*

| S.No | Property | Symbol | Unit (SI) |
|------|----------|--------|-----------|
| 1 | Dry Bulb Temperature | DBT / $t$ | °C or K |
| 2 | Wet Bulb Temperature | WBT / $t_w$ | °C or K |
| 3 | Dew Point Temperature | DPT / $t_{dp}$ | °C or K |
| 4 | Specific Humidity (Humidity Ratio) | $\omega$ | kg water vapour / kg dry air |
| 5 | Relative Humidity | $\phi$ | dimensionless (0 to 1, or %) |
| 6 | Degree of Saturation | $\mu$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 7 | Specific Enthalpy | $h$ | kJ / kg dry air |
| 8 | Specific Volume | $v$ | m³ / kg dry air |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sponge Model"

Imagine dry air as a clean, dry kitchen **sponge**, and atmospheric water vapour as the **water** the sponge can absorb.

- **Cold mornings** (low DBT) → the sponge is stiff and can hold only a little water → low humidity capacity.
- **Hot afternoons** (high DBT) → the sponge becomes soft and porous → it can soak up a huge amount of water.
- **Relative Humidity (φ)** = (water currently in sponge) ÷ (maximum water the sponge *can* hold at this temperature) × 100.
- **Dew Point** = the temperature at which the sponge becomes fully saturated and any extra water would start dripping out (i.e., condensation begins).
- **Wet Bulb Temperature** = the equilibrium temperature reached when the sponge is wrapped in a wet cloth and exposed to a controlled air stream (energy lost by evaporation = energy gained by the air).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why study psychrometry?** Because human thermal comfort, food preservation in cold storage, pharmaceutical manufacturing, textile processing, and HVAC energy consumption are ALL governed by the moisture content of the air. Without a clear handle on these eight properties, no refrigeration or air-conditioning system can be correctly designed or diagnosed.

## 1.3 Composition of Atmospheric Air (Standard Reference)

Standard dry air by volume is approximated as:
- Nitrogen ($\text{N}_2$) — **78.03 %**
- Oxygen ($\text{O}_2$) — **20.99 %**
- Argon (Ar) — **0.94 %**
- Carbon Dioxide ($\text{CO}_2$) — **0.04 %** (variable, climate-relevant)

The **apparent molecular weight of dry air** is taken as:

$$M_a = 28.97 \; \text{kg/kmol}$$

The **gas constant of dry air** is:

$$R_a = \frac{\bar{R}}{M_a} = \frac{8.314}{28.97} = 0.287 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$$

where $\bar{R} = 8.314 \; \text{kJ/(kmol·K)}$ is the **universal gas constant**, and the gas constant for water vapour is:

$$R_v = \frac{\bar{R}}{M_v} = \frac{8.314}{18.015} = 0.4615 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$$

The **specific heat constants** most frequently used:

$$c_{pa} = 1.005 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)} \quad \text{(dry air)}$$

$$c_{pv} = 1.88 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)} \quad \text{(water vapour)}$$

$$c_pw = 4.186 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)} \quad \text{(liquid water)}$$

$$h_{fg} = 2500 \; \text{kJ/kg} \; \text{at} \; 0^\circ\text{C} \quad \text{(latent heat of vaporisation of water)}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Thermodynamic Properties — Concept by Concept

### (A) Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT)
The temperature of moist air measured by a standard mercury-in-glass or digital thermometer, **shielded from radiation and moisture**. It is the *true thermodynamic temperature* of the mixture and forms the **x-axis (abscissa)** of the psychrometric chart.

### (B) Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT)
Obtained by wrapping the thermometer bulb in a water-saturated muslin cloth and exposing it to a gentle, high-velocity air stream. The adiabatic evaporation of water cools the bulb until a steady temperature $t_w$ is reached. The **WBT is always less than or equal to the DBT** for unsaturated air.

### (C) Dew Point Temperature (DPT)
The temperature to which moist air must be cooled (at constant pressure and constant humidity ratio) to become **saturated** — i.e., the temperature at which the partial pressure of water vapour equals the saturation pressure. Below DPT, water begins to condense.

### (D) Specific Humidity / Humidity Ratio ($\omega$)
**Definition:** The mass of water vapour associated with **one kilogram of dry air**.

$$\omega = \frac{m_v}{m_a} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{kg water vapour}}{\text{kg dry air}} \right]$$

- It is measured per kg of **dry air (not moist air)**, because dry air mass does not change during condensation or evaporation, whereas total mass does.
- Typical range in comfort air-conditioning: $\omega = 0.005$ to $0.020$ kg/kg.

### (E) Relative Humidity ($\phi$)
**Definition:** Ratio of the **actual partial pressure** of water vapour to the **saturation pressure** of water vapour at the prevailing DBT.

$$\phi = \frac{P_v}{P_s} \times 100 \; [\%]$$

- $\phi = 100\%$ → **saturated air** (fog, clouds, mist)
- $\phi = 0\%$ → **bone-dry air** (theoretical, never occurs naturally)
- Comfort zone: $\phi \approx 40\% - 60\%$.

### (F) Degree of Saturation ($\mu$)
**Definition:** Ratio of the **actual humidity ratio** to the **humidity ratio of saturated air at the same DBT**.

$$\mu = \frac{\omega}{\omega_s}$$

For unsaturated air, $0 \le \mu \le 1$. It quantifies how *close* the air is to saturation.

### (G) Specific Enthalpy of Moist Air ($h$)
**Definition:** Enthalpy of the dry air + enthalpy of the associated water vapour, per kg of dry air. Reference states: dry air at **0 °C**, liquid water at **0 °C**.

$$h = c_{pa} \cdot t + \omega \cdot (h_{fg,0} + c_{pv} \cdot t) \; [\text{kJ/kg dry air}]$$

### (H) Specific Volume of Moist Air ($v$)
**Definition:** Volume occupied by unit mass of dry air **plus** its associated water vapour, per kg of dry air.

$$v = \frac{R_a \, T}{P - P_v} \; [\text{m}^3/\text{kg dry air}]$$

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise the eight equations below. KTU 14-mark numerical problems ALWAYS start from one of these.**

| # | Property | Governing Equation | Units | Typical Range |
|---|----------|--------------------|-------|---------------|
| 1 | Specific Humidity | $\omega = 0.622 \, \dfrac{P_v}{P - P_v}$ | kg/kg da | 0 – 0.03 |
| 2 | Relative Humidity | $\phi = \dfrac{P_v}{P_s}$ at DBT | – | 0 – 1 |
| 3 | Degree of Saturation | $\mu = \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_s}$ | – | 0 – 1 |
| 4 | Specific Enthalpy | $h = 1.005\,t + \omega\,(2500 + 1.88\,t)$ | kJ/kg da | 0 – 120 |
| 5 | Specific Volume | $v = \dfrac{R_a\,T}{P - P_v} = 0.287\,T/(P - P_v)$ | m³/kg da | 0.78 – 0.95 |
| 6 | Humidity Ratio (alt. form) | $\omega = \dfrac{0.622\,\phi\,P_s}{P - \phi\,P_s}$ | kg/kg da | – |
| 7 | Dew Point | $P_v = P_{s,t_{dp}}$ (find $t_{dp}$ from steam tables) | °C | – |
| 8 | Wet Bulb Energy Balance | $c_{pa}(t - t_w) = \omega_s' \cdot h_{fg,w} - \omega \cdot c_{pv}(t - t_w)$ | – | – |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall Prevention:** Notice the **0.622** factor. Many students incorrectly use **0.622 × 18/29** or other variants. The correct origin is $M_a / M_v = 28.97/18.015 = 1.608$, and its reciprocal is **0.622**. Memorise the origin, not just the number.

## 2.3 The "Why & How" Behind Each Property

- **Why specific humidity and not absolute humidity?** Absolute humidity ($m_v / V$) changes with temperature and pressure even when no moisture is added. Specific humidity is **conserved** during heating, cooling (above dew point), and pressure changes — making it the correct property to track in HVAC analysis.
- **Why is WBT ≤ DBT ≤ DPT?** The WBT is reached under *evaporative cooling* (energy loss), DPT under *isobaric cooling to saturation* (energy neutral until condensation), and DPT is the lowest temperature the air-water mixture can attain by any cooling-only process.
- **Why is DPT independent of DBT?** DPT depends **only** on the actual partial pressure $P_v$, which equals the saturation pressure at the dew point temperature. Once you know $P_v$ from $\omega$, you can read DPT directly from steam tables.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Specific Humidity from Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures

**Starting hypothesis:** Moist air obeys the ideal-gas law, and dry air and water vapour occupy the same total volume $V$ at the same temperature $T$ (Dalton's Law).

**Step 1 — Apply Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures.**
The total pressure of the moist air equals the sum of the partial pressures of its components:

$$P = P_a + P_v$$

where $P_a$ is the partial pressure of dry air, and $P_v$ is the partial pressure of water vapour.

**Step 2 — Apply the Ideal Gas Equation to Each Component.**

For dry air:

$$P_a \cdot V = m_a \cdot R_a \cdot T$$

For water vapour:

$$P_v \cdot V = m_v \cdot R_v \cdot T$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Masses per Unit Volume.**

$$\frac{m_a}{V} = \frac{P_a}{R_a \, T}$$

$$\frac{m_v}{V} = \frac{P_v}{R_v \, T}$$

**Step 4 — Form the Ratio (which is the specific humidity).**

$$\omega = \frac{m_v}{m_a} = \frac{P_v / (R_v T)}{P_a / (R_a T)} = \frac{P_v}{P_a} \cdot \frac{R_a}{R_v}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the Ratio of Gas Constants.**

The ratio of the specific gas constants equals the inverse ratio of molecular weights:

$$\frac{R_a}{R_v} = \frac{\bar{R}/M_a}{\bar{R}/M_v} = \frac{M_v}{M_a} = \frac{18.015}{28.97} = 0.622$$

**Step 6 — Replace $P_a$ Using Dalton's Law.**

$$P_a = P - P_v$$

**Step 7 — Final Result.**

$$\boxed{\;\omega = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P - P_v}\;} \quad \text{[kg water vapour / kg dry air]}$$

**Alternate form using Relative Humidity:** Since $P_v = \phi \, P_s$ (where $P_s$ is saturation pressure at DBT from steam tables):

$$\boxed{\;\omega = \frac{0.622 \, \phi \, P_s}{P - \phi \, P_s}\;}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Specific Enthalpy of Moist Air

**Reference States:** Dry air enthalpy = 0 at $0^\circ\text{C}$. Liquid water enthalpy = 0 at $0^\circ\text{C}$. Latent heat of vaporisation at $0^\circ\text{C} = h_{fg,0} = 2500 \; \text{kJ/kg}$.

**Step 1 — Enthalpy of the Dry-Air Component (per kg da).**

$$h_a = c_{pa} \cdot t = 1.005 \cdot t \quad [\text{kJ/kg da}]$$

**Step 2 — Enthalpy of the Water-Vapour Component (per kg da).**

To vaporise $\omega$ kg of water at $0^\circ\text{C}$ to saturated liquid water takes $\omega \cdot h_{fg,0}$. To then heat the vapour from $0^\circ\text{C}$ to the air temperature $t$ takes $\omega \cdot c_{pv} \cdot t$:

$$h_v = \omega \cdot (h_{fg,0} + c_{pv} \cdot t) = \omega \cdot (2500 + 1.88 \cdot t)$$

**Step 3 — Total Enthalpy (per kg da).**

$$h = h_a + h_v$$

$$\boxed{\;h = 1.005 \, t + \omega \, (2500 + 1.88 \, t)\;} \quad [\text{kJ/kg da}]$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Specific Volume of Moist Air

**Starting point:** The volume occupied by 1 kg of dry air plus its associated $\omega$ kg of water vapour.

**Step 1 — Use Ideal Gas Law for the Total Moist-Air Mass.**
Total specific volume (per kg of moist air) is:

$$v_{moist} = \frac{R_{mix} \, T}{P}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to "per kg of dry air" by multiplying by $(1 + \omega)$.**

$$v = (1 + \omega) \cdot v_{moist} = \frac{(1 + \omega) \, R_{mix} \, T}{P}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting $R_{mix}$ in terms of $P_a$ and $P_v$.**
The mixture gas constant is:

$$R_{mix} = \frac{R_a}{1 + \omega} \cdot \frac{P_a}{P} + \frac{R_v \, \omega}{1 + \omega} \cdot \frac{P_v}{P}$$

This simplifies (with $P_a = P - P_v$) to a clean form:

$$v = \frac{R_a \, T}{P - P_v}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical value of $R_a$.**

$$\boxed{\;v = \frac{0.287 \, T}{P - P_v}\;} \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{kg da}]$$

**Useful engineering observation:** Because $P - P_v \approx P$ when $P_v$ is small (it usually is, $< 5\%$ of $P$), the specific volume is **very nearly** the specific volume of dry air at the same conditions.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — The Wet-Bulb Energy Balance

The WBT is reached when the **rate of heat loss from the bulb (by evaporation) equals the rate of sensible heat gain from the surrounding air**. A simplified energy balance on the air-stream per kg of dry air:

**Energy given up by air in cooling from $t$ to $t_w$ (sensible):**

$$q_{sens} = c_{pa}(t - t_w) \quad [\text{kJ/kg da}]$$

**Energy absorbed as latent heat by the water evaporated into the stream:**

$$q_{lat} = (\omega_s' - \omega) \cdot h_{fg,w} \quad [\text{kJ/kg da}]$$

where $\omega_s'$ is the humidity ratio of saturated air at $t_w$.

**Energy carried away by the vapour itself (small correction):**

$$q_{vap} = (\omega_s' - \omega) \cdot c_{pv}(t - t_w) \quad [\text{kJ/kg da}]$$

**Setting $q_{sens} = q_{lat} - q_{vap}$:**

$$\boxed{\;c_{pa}(t - t_w) = (\omega_s' - \omega)\, h_{fg,w} - (\omega_s' - \omega)\, c_{pv}(t - t_w)\;}$$

This implicit equation in $t_w$ is solved iteratively (or read directly off the psychrometric chart).

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Illustration

**Given:** Atmospheric air at $t = 35^\circ\text{C}$ (DBT) and $\phi = 60\%$. Total pressure $P = 101.325 \; \text{kPa}$.

From the steam tables:
- $P_s$ at $35^\circ\text{C} = 5.628 \; \text{kPa}$

**Step 1 — Compute $P_v$.**

$$P_v = \phi \cdot P_s = 0.60 \times 5.628 = 3.3768 \; \text{kPa}$$

**Step 2 — Specific Humidity.**

$$\omega = 0.622 \cdot \frac{3.3768}{101.325 - 3.3768} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{3.3768}{97.9482}$$

$$\omega = 0.02145 \; \text{kg/kg da}$$

**Step 3 — Dew Point Temperature.**
Find $t_{dp}$ such that $P_s(t_{dp}) = 3.3768 \; \text{kPa}$. Interpolating steam tables: $t_{dp} \approx 25.7^\circ\text{C}$.

**Step 4 — Specific Enthalpy.**

$$h = 1.005 \times 35 + 0.02145 \times (2500 + 1.88 \times 35)$$

$$h = 35.175 + 0.02145 \times 2565.8 = 35.175 + 55.04 = 90.21 \; \text{kJ/kg da}$$

**Step 5 — Specific Volume.**

$$v = \frac{0.287 \times (35 + 273.15)}{101.325 - 3.3768} = \frac{0.287 \times 308.15}{97.9482} = 0.903 \; \text{m}^3/\text{kg da}$$

**Step 6 — Degree of Saturation.**
At $35^\circ\text{C}$, $\omega_s = 0.622 \times 5.628 / (101.325 - 5.628) = 0.0366 \; \text{kg/kg da}$.

$$\mu = \frac{0.02145}{0.0366} = 0.586 \; (58.6\%)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Cross-check via the psychrometric chart:** Enter at $t = 35^\circ\text{C}$ vertically, then move diagonally upward-right to $\phi = 60\%$. Reading horizontally at the saturation curve gives $t_w \approx 27.5^\circ\text{C}$ — a good sanity check on the entire calculation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Mapping — The Eight Properties and Their Interdependencies

The Mermaid block below maps the eight primary psychrometric properties, their **inputs (independent variables)**, and the **derived (dependent) variables**, exactly as a KTU numerical problem is structured.

```mermaid
graph TD
    P_a[Independent Variable Atmospheric Pressure P kPa] --> N1
    P_b[Independent Variable Dry Bulb Temperature t deg C] --> N1
    P_c[Independent Variable Relative Humidity phi percent] --> N1
    P_d[Independent Variable Specific Humidity omega kg per kg da] --> N1
    P_e[Independent Variable Wet Bulb Temperature t_w deg C] --> N1
    P_f[Independent Variable Dew Point Temperature t_dp deg C] --> N1
    ST[Steam Tables for P_s at DBT and at DPT] --> N1
    N1[Intermediate Step 1 Partial Pressure P_v phi times P_s] --> N2
    N1 --> N3
    N1 --> N4
    N1 --> N5
    N2[Derived Property 1 Humidity Ratio omega equals 0.622 P_v over P minus P_v] --> N6
    N3[Derived Property 2 Specific Volume v equals 0.287 T over P minus P_v] --> N6
    N4[Derived Property 3 Specific Enthalpy h equals 1.005 t plus omega times 2500 plus 1.88 t] --> N6
    N5[Derived Property 4 Degree of Saturation mu equals omega over omega s] --> N6
    N6[Final Psychrometric State of Moist Air] --> APP1[Application 1 Comfort Air Conditioning Design]
    N6 --> APP2[Application 2 Cold Storage Humidity Control]
    N6 --> APP3[Application 3 Evaporative Cooling Load Estimate]
    N6 --> APP4[Application 4 Dehumidification Equipment Sizing]
```

## 4.2 The "Three-Temperature Triangle" — DBT, WBT, DPT

The relationship between the three characteristic temperatures is the most-tested concept in Part A. The block-level schematic below shows their **physical meaning** in a vertical saturation-curve geometry.

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph SAT[Saturation Curve phi equals 100 percent]
        S1[Point A Saturated at DBT, omega equals omega s] --> S2[Point B Saturated at WBT, omega equals omega s at t_w]
        S3[Point C Saturated at DPT, omega equals omega s at t_dp]
    end
    AIR[Actual Unsaturated Air State at DBT and omega] --> MOVE1
    MOVE1[Adiabatic Saturation Line cooling and humidification, h approximately constant] --> S2
    AIR --> MOVE2
    MOVE2[Isobaric Cooling at constant omega to saturation] --> S3
    AIR --> MOVE3
    MOVE3[Constant omega line horizontal on chart] --> S3
    S2 --> WBTNOTE[Reading: WBT is on saturation curve directly to the LEFT of state]
    S3 --> DPTNOTE[Reading: DPT is on saturation curve directly BELOW state]
    AIR --> DBTNOTE[Reading: DBT is the x-coordinate of the state point]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Reading Rule:** On any psychrometric chart, if you draw a **horizontal line** (constant $\omega$) from the state point to the saturation curve, you hit the **DPT**. If you draw a **line parallel to the wet-bulb (adiabatic saturation) lines** from the state point to the saturation curve, you hit the **WBT**.

## 4.3 The "Hidden Architecture" of a Psychrometric Chart

A full psychrometric chart has nine principal regions / lines. Mapped in block form:

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph AXES[Chart Axes]
        X[Abscissa Dry Bulb Temperature t deg C]
        Y[Ordinate Specific Humidity omega kg per kg da or Water Vapour Pressure P_v kPa]
    end
    subgraph CURVES[Principal Curves and Lines]
        C1[Saturation Curve phi equals 100 percent, upper boundary]
        C2[Relative Humidity Curves phi equals 10, 20, 30 ... 90 percent]
        C3[Wet Bulb or Adiabatic Saturation Lines sloping up and to the right]
        C4[Constant Enthalpy Lines h constant, nearly parallel to WBT lines]
        C5[Constant Specific Volume Lines v constant, slope gently upward]
        C6[Specific Enthalpy Scale on left curved boundary]
        C7[Specific Volume Scale on right boundary]
        C8[Dew Point Scale, coincides with saturation curve reading]
    end
    AXES --> CURVES
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### **Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the following properties of moist air, stating their SI units:*
(a) Specific Humidity
(b) Relative Humidity
(c) Dew Point Temperature

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Specific Humidity (ω):** The mass of water vapour, in kg, present in the moist air mixture per kg of **dry air**. Unit: kg water vapour / kg dry air. Governed by $\omega = 0.622 \, P_v / (P - P_v)$.

(b) **Relative Humidity (φ):** The ratio of the actual partial pressure of water vapour in the air to the **saturation pressure of water vapour** at the prevailing dry-bulb temperature. Unit: dimensionless, expressed as a percentage. $\phi = P_v / P_s \times 100\%$.

(c) **Dew Point Temperature (DPT, $t_{dp}$):** The temperature at which the water vapour present in the air becomes saturated and **condensation (dew) begins**, when the air is cooled at constant pressure. It is numerically read from steam tables as the saturation temperature corresponding to the actual partial pressure $P_v$. Unit: °C.

---

### **Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Dry Bulb Temperature, Wet Bulb Temperature, and Dew Point Temperature. State the relationship WBT ≤ DBT for unsaturated air.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | DBT ($t$) | WBT ($t_w$) | DPT ($t_{dp}$) |
|---------|-----------|-------------|----------------|
| Thermometer type | Ordinary bulb | Bulb wrapped in wet muslin | Mirror / cooled surface observing condensation |
| Process reaching it | Direct reading | Adiabatic evaporative cooling equilibrium | Isobaric cooling to saturation |
| Energy involved | None | Latent + sensible | Sensible only (until condensation) |
| Numerical comparison | Highest | Intermediate | Lowest |
| Reading on chart | x-coordinate | On saturation curve, on adiabatic-saturation line from state | On saturation curve, horizontal line from state |

**Mathematical inequality for unsaturated air:**

$$t_{dp} \le t_w \le t$$

**Equality holds only at $\phi = 100\%$** (saturated air), where all three temperatures coincide.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 marks each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question is divided into **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. The student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### **Question A. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, 14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Derive the expression for the specific humidity (humidity ratio) of moist air from Dalton's law of partial pressures and the ideal gas equation.*

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Dalton's law gives:*
$$P = P_a + P_v$$
where $P$ = total pressure (Pa), $P_a$ = partial pressure of dry air, $P_v$ = partial pressure of water vapour.

*Step 2 — Apply the ideal gas equation to each constituent in the same volume V:*
$$P_a V = m_a R_a T \quad ; \quad P_v V = m_v R_v T$$

*Step 3 — Solve for the masses:*
$$m_a = \frac{P_a V}{R_a T} \quad ; \quad m_v = \frac{P_v V}{R_v T}$$

*Step 4 — Define specific humidity and substitute:*
$$\omega = \frac{m_v}{m_a} = \frac{P_v / R_v T}{P_a / R_a T} = \frac{P_v}{P_a} \cdot \frac{R_a}{R_v}$$

*Step 5 — Simplify the gas-constant ratio using molecular weights:*
$$\frac{R_a}{R_v} = \frac{M_v}{M_a} = \frac{18.015}{28.97} = 0.622$$

*Step 6 — Replace $P_a = P - P_v$:*
$$\boxed{\;\omega = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P - P_v}\;} \quad \text{kg water vapour / kg dry air}$$

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Atmospheric air is at 40 °C DBT and 30 % relative humidity. The total pressure is 101.325 kPa. Determine:*
*(i) Specific humidity, (ii) Dew point temperature, (iii) Specific enthalpy, (iv) Specific volume.*

**Model Solution:**

*Given:* $t = 40^\circ\text{C}$, $\phi = 0.30$, $P = 101.325 \; \text{kPa}$.
*From steam tables:* $P_s$ at $40^\circ\text{C} = 7.384 \; \text{kPa}$.

*(i) Specific humidity:*
$$P_v = \phi \cdot P_s = 0.30 \times 7.384 = 2.2152 \; \text{kPa}$$

$$\omega = 0.622 \times \frac{2.2152}{101.325 - 2.2152} = 0.622 \times \frac{2.2152}{99.1098}$$

$$\omega = 0.01390 \; \text{kg/kg da}$$

*(ii) Dew point temperature:*
Find $t_{dp}$ such that $P_s(t_{dp}) = 2.2152 \; \text{kPa}$.
From steam tables (interpolation):
- At $18^\circ\text{C}$: $P_s = 2.065 \; \text{kPa}$
- At $20^\circ\text{C}$: $P_s = 2.339 \; \text{kPa}$

Linear interpolation:
$$t_{dp} = 18 + \frac{2.2152 - 2.065}{2.339 - 2.065} \times 2 = 18 + 1.106 = 19.1^\circ\text{C}$$

*(iii) Specific enthalpy:*
$$h = 1.005 \times 40 + 0.01390 \times (2500 + 1.88 \times 40)$$

$$h = 40.20 + 0.01390 \times 2575.2 = 40.20 + 35.80 = 76.00 \; \text{kJ/kg da}$$

*(iv) Specific volume:*
$$v = \frac{0.287 \times (40 + 273.15)}{101.325 - 2.2152} = \frac{0.287 \times 313.15}{99.1098} = 0.907 \; \text{m}^3/\text{kg da}$$

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $\omega$ | 0.01390 kg/kg da |
| $t_{dp}$ | 19.1 °C |
| $h$ | 76.00 kJ/kg da |
| $v$ | 0.907 m³/kg da |

---

### **Question B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, 14 Marks]** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Define the terms Degree of Saturation, Wet Bulb Temperature, and Adiabatic Saturation Temperature. Show that for unsaturated air, WBT lies between DBT and DPT.*

**Model Solution:**

*Degree of Saturation ($\mu$):* The ratio of the actual specific humidity of the air to the specific humidity of saturated air at the **same DBT**.

$$\mu = \frac{\omega}{\omega_s} \Big|_{t=\text{DBT}}$$

*Wet Bulb Temperature ($t_w$):* The steady-state temperature indicated by a thermometer whose bulb is covered with a water-saturated wick and exposed to a high-velocity air stream. It reflects the dynamic equilibrium between the heat supplied by the air and the heat consumed by evaporation.

*Adiabatic Saturation Temperature ($t_{as}$):* The temperature attained when a stream of unsaturated air is passed in a thermally insulated chamber over a pool of circulating water, with continuous water circulation. For many engineering purposes, $t_w \approx t_{as}$ (thermodynamic equivalence, not strict identity).

*Proof that $t_{dp} \le t_w \le t$ (DBT):*

(i) **DPT ≤ DBT:** Cooling at constant $\omega$ reduces $P_s$ (saturation pressure decreases with temperature). The condition for saturation is $P_v = P_s(t_{dp})$. Since $P_v = \phi \cdot P_s(t)$ with $\phi \le 1$, we have $P_s(t_{dp}) \le P_s(t)$. Since $P_s$ is monotonically increasing with $T$, this implies $t_{dp} \le t$.

(ii) **DPT ≤ WBT:** WBT is reached via an evaporative process that *adds* water vapour to the air, so $\omega_s'(t_w) > \omega$. Since $P_s$ increases with $T$, $P_s(t_{dp}) = P_v < P_s(t_w)$, which gives $t_{dp} < t_w$.

(iii) **WBT ≤ DBT:** Evaporation extracts latent heat, cooling the air. The wet bulb is by definition the equilibrium temperature below the original DBT, so $t_w \le t$.

*Combining:*

$$\boxed{\;t_{dp} \le t_w \le t\;}$$

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Moist air at 25 °C DBT and 50 % RH is heated sensibly to 40 °C. Find: (i) $\omega$ before and after heating, (ii) RH after heating, (iii) Heat added per kg of dry air.*

**Model Solution:**

*Given (State 1):* $t_1 = 25^\circ\text{C}$, $\phi_1 = 0.50$, $P = 101.325 \; \text{kPa}$.
*From steam tables:* $P_{s1} = 3.169 \; \text{kPa}$; $P_{s2} = 7.384 \; \text{kPa}$.

*Step 1 — Humidity ratio (no change during sensible heating, since $\omega$ is constant):*
$$P_{v1} = 0.50 \times 3.169 = 1.5845 \; \text{kPa}$$

$$\omega_1 = \omega_2 = 0.622 \times \frac{1.5845}{101.325 - 1.5845} = 0.622 \times \frac{1.5845}{99.7405} = 0.00988 \; \text{kg/kg da}$$

*Step 2 — Relative Humidity at state 2 (40 °C):*
$$P_{v2} = P_{v1} = 1.5845 \; \text{kPa} \quad (\text{no moisture added/removed})$$

$$\phi_2 = \frac{P_{v2}}{P_{s2}} = \frac{1.5845}{7.384} = 0.2146 = 21.46\%$$

*Step 3 — Enthalpies:*
$$h_1 = 1.005 \times 25 + 0.00988 \times (2500 + 1.88 \times 25) = 25.125 + 25.16 = 50.29 \; \text{kJ/kg da}$$

$$h_2 = 1.005 \times 40 + 0.00988 \times (2500 + 1.88 \times 40) = 40.20 + 25.44 = 65.64 \; \text{kJ/kg da}$$

*Step 4 — Heat added (sensible heating, per kg of dry air):*
$$q = h_2 - h_1 = 65.64 - 50.29 = 15.35 \; \text{kJ/kg da}$$

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $\omega_1 = \omega_2$ | 0.00988 kg/kg da |
| $\phi_2$ | 21.5 % |
| $q$ | 15.35 kJ/kg da |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Three Recurring Mistakes:**
> 1. **Using $P_{s1}$ instead of $P_{s2}$** to compute $\phi_2$ — half a mark lost.
> 2. **Forgetting that $\omega$ is constant during sensible heating** — if a student writes $\omega_2 \ne \omega_1$, they lose the **2 marks** allocated to the constant-ω step.
> 3. **Mixing $c_{pv}$ and $c_pw$ in the enthalpy equation** — the 1.88 factor is for **water vapour**, not liquid water. Use $c_{pv} = 1.88$, NOT $c_{pw} = 4.186$. Two marks gone if swapped.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Memory Anchors — Revise These in 5 Minutes Before the Exam**

- **Master Formula 1:** $\omega = 0.622 \, P_v / (P - P_v)$ — originates from $M_v / M_a = 18.015 / 28.97 = 0.622$.
- **Master Formula 2:** $h = 1.005 \, t + \omega \, (2500 + 1.88 \, t)$ — reference at $0^\circ\text{C}$, liquid water.
- **Master Formula 3:** $v = 0.287 \, T / (P - P_v)$ — per kg of **dry air**.
- **Master Inequality:** $t_{dp} \le t_w \le t$ for **unsaturated** air; equality at $\phi = 100\%$.
- **Reading the Chart:** Constant $\omega$ line → horizontal to saturation curve → reads **DPT**. Adiabatic saturation line → diagonal to saturation curve → reads **WBT**.
- **Sensible Heating:** $\omega$ constant, $P_v$ constant, $\phi$ **decreases**, $h$ increases, $v$ increases.
- **Sensible Cooling (above DPT):** $\omega$ constant, $P_v$ constant, $\phi$ **increases**, $h$ decreases, $v$ decreases.
- **Cooling below DPT (dehumidification):** $\omega$ **decreases** (condensation), $h$ decreases, $v$ decreases.
- **Evaporative Cooling (adiabatic saturation):** $h \approx$ constant, $\omega$ **increases**, $t$ **decreases**, $\phi$ **increases**.
- **Constants to Memorise:** $R_a = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K, $R_v = 0.4615$ kJ/kg·K, $c_{pa} = 1.005$, $c_{pv} = 1.88$, $h_{fg,0} = 2500$ kJ/kg.
- **Real-World Applications:** Comfort AC (40–60% RH), cold-storage for fruits (85–90% RH), pharmaceuticals (35–45% RH), data centres (40–55% RH), textile mills (60–70% RH), paper storage (45–55% RH).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"Always carry out the calculation per kg of DRY air, not per kg of moist air. Always use the steam-table value of $P_s$ at the prevailing DBT. Always report $\omega$ in kg/kg, $h$ in kJ/kg da, and $v$ in m³/kg da."* These three "always" rules eliminate 80% of common marking deductions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Perfect Gas Relationship for Approximate Calculation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Perfect Gas Relationship for Approximate Calculation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Perfect Gas** (also called an **Ideal Gas**) is a theoretical gas whose molecules occupy negligible space and have no intermolecular forces, and which obeys the **Perfect Gas Equation of State** exactly under all conditions of pressure and temperature. In refrigeration engineering, real refrigerant vapours at pressures well below their critical pressure (typically below **0.5 P_critical**) and at temperatures well above their critical temperature behave approximately as a perfect gas, allowing the use of simplified equations.

The **Perfect Gas Relationship** used for approximate calculation in RAC is expressed as:

$$P \cdot V = m \cdot R \cdot T$$

where:
- $P$ = Absolute pressure of the gas (kPa)
- $V$ = Total volume occupied by the gas (m³)
- $m$ = Mass of the gas (kg)
- $R$ = Specific gas constant (kJ/kg·K)
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The perfect gas approximation is *only* valid for **superheated refrigerant vapours** (region outside the saturation dome) and for **air and combustion products**. It is **NOT** valid inside the two-phase region (wet mixture) or near the critical point.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a large, empty indoor badminton court filled with hundreds of rubber balls bouncing randomly and elastically off the walls, the floor, and each other. If we ignore the **size of each ball** (point-mass assumption) and pretend they have **no attraction or repulsion** between them (no sticky or magnetic behavior), then predicting the total pressure they exert on the walls becomes incredibly simple — it depends *only* on how many balls are inside, how fast they move, and the temperature of the room.

That is precisely how a **Perfect Gas** behaves:

| Real Rubber Ball | Molecule in Perfect Gas |
|------------------|--------------------------|
| Ball has a finite size | Molecule size is negligible |
| Balls have friction on impact | Perfectly elastic collisions |
| Balls may attract/repel each other | No intermolecular forces |

In a refrigerator's **compressor suction line**, refrigerant vapour is at low pressure and high temperature — exactly like those rubber balls in a spacious court. Hence, the perfect gas equation gives very accurate results, and engineers avoid the cumbersome refrigerant property tables.

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Gas Constant ($R_u$):** $R_u = \mathbf{8.314\ kJ/kgmol \cdot K} = 8314\ J/kgmol \cdot K$. This is the same for *every* gas. The **specific gas constant** $R$ differs for each gas because it is $R_u$ divided by the molar mass.

### 1.3 Key Standard Constants

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Units |
|----------|--------|-------|-------|
| Universal Gas Constant | $R_u$ | **8.314** | kJ/kgmol·K |
| Universal Gas Constant | $R_u$ | **0.08206** | L·atm/(mol·K) |
| Standard Temperature | $T_{std}$ | **273.15** | K (0 °C) |
| Standard Pressure | $P_{std}$ | **101.325** | kPa (1 atm) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

The perfect gas relationship rests on three fundamental empirical laws combined into one master equation:

**Step 1 — Boyle's Law (Constant Temperature):**
At constant temperature, volume is inversely proportional to pressure.

$$V \propto \frac{1}{P} \quad \Rightarrow \quad PV = \text{constant}$$

**Step 2 — Charles's Law (Constant Pressure):**
At constant pressure, volume is directly proportional to absolute temperature.

$$V \propto T \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{V}{T} = \text{constant}$$

**Step 3 — Avogadro's Law:**
Equal volumes of all perfect gases at the same temperature and pressure contain equal numbers of molecules (1 kgmol occupies **22.414 m³** at STP).

**Step 4 — Combining All Three:**
This combination yields the universal gas equation, which, when applied to a specific mass $m$ of a gas, becomes the working formula:

$$P V = m R T$$
