Find: (i) Specific humidity, (ii) Enthalpy, (iii) DPT, (iv) Wet Bulb Temperature. **Given:** T₁ = 30 °C, φ₁ = 60%, T₂ = 20 °C, P_atm = 101.325 kPa. **Step 1:** Saturation pressure at 30 °C from steam tables: $P_{s,30} = 4.246$ kPa. **Step 2:** Partial pressure of vapor: $$P_v = \phi \cdot P_{s,30} = 0.60 \times 4.246 = 2.548 \text{ kPa}$$ **Step 3:** Specific humidity: $$\omega_1 = \frac{0.622 \times 2.548}{101.325 - 2.548} = \frac{1.585}{98.777} = 0.01605 \text{ kg/kg da}$$ **Step 4:** Enthalpy at state 1: $$h_1 = 1.005 \times 30 + 0.01605 \times (2501 + 1.88 \times 30)$$ $$h_1 = 30.15 + 0.01605 \times 2557.4 = 30.15 + 41.05 = 71.20 \text{ kJ/kg da}$$ **Step 5:** DPT is the temperature at which saturation pressure = 2.548 kPa. From steam tables, this corresponds to **T_dpt ≈ 21.4 °C**. **Step 6:** WBT is found iteratively. For WBT = 22 °C, $P_{s,22} = 2.645$ kPa, $\omega_s = 0.01668$ kg/kg da. $$h_s = 1.005 \times 22 + 0.01668 \times (2501 + 1.88 \times 22) = 22.11 + 42.39 = 64.50 \text{ kJ/kg da}$$ Energy balance: $h_1 = h_s + c_{pw} \cdot (T_1 - T_{wb})$ where $c_{pw} = 1.88$ kJ/kg·K. $$71.20 = 64.50 + 1.88 \times (30 - T_{wb})$$ $$30 - T_{wb} = \frac{6.70}{1.88} = 3.56 \Rightarrow T_{wb} \approx 26.4 \text{ °C}$$ **Final Answers:** ω = 0.01605 kg/kg da, h = 71.20 kJ/kg da, DPT = 21.4 °C, WBT = 26.4 °C. ✓ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of an Air Conditioning System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Return Air from Room
Hot and Humid] --> B[Filter Section
Removes Dust and PM] B --> C[Mixing Section
RA plus FA plus OA] C --> D[Cooling and Dehumidification
Cooling Coil plus ADP] D --> E[Heating Coil
Reheat for RSHF Control] E --> F[Humidifier
Steam or Water Spray] F --> G[Supply Fan
Blower Motor] G --> H[Supply Air Duct
Conditioned Air] H --> I[Conditioned Space
Zone Control] I --> J[Return Air Grille] J --> A subgraph Outside_Air K[Fresh Air Intake
OA plus Ventilation] K --> C end subgraph Refrigeration_Cycle L[Compressor] --> M[Condenser Outdoor Unit] M --> N[Expansion Valve] N --> O[Evaporator Coil] O --> L O -. Chilled Water .-> D end style A fill:#FFE5B4 style I fill:#B4D8FF style L fill:#FFB4B4 style M fill:#C0C0C0 ``` ## 4.2 Classification Tree — Types of Air Conditioning ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Air Conditioning Systems] --> B[Comfort AC] A --> C[Industrial AC] A --> D[Cold Storage AC] B --> B1[Window Unit] B --> B2[Split Unit] B --> B3[Central Plant] B --> B4[VRF or VRV System] C --> C1[Process Cooling] C --> C2[Cleanroom AC] C --> C3[Data Center AC] C --> C4[Pharma Manufacturing] D --> D1[Short Term Storage] D --> D2[Long Term Frozen Storage] D --> D3[Controlled Atmosphere] B1 --> S1[Capacity 0.5 to 3 TR] B2 --> S2[Capacity 1 to 5 TR] B3 --> S3[Capacity 50 to 5000 TR] B4 --> S4[Capacity 5 to 200 TR] C1 --> I1[Temperature Range 5 to 25 C] C2 --> I2[ISO Class 5 to 8] C3 --> I3[18 to 27 C Strict] C4 --> I4[21 C plus 45 percent RH] D1 --> L1[0 to 4 C] D2 --> L2[Minus 18 to Minus 25 C] D3 --> L3[O2 and CO2 Control] style A fill:#FFD700 style B fill:#87CEEB style C fill:#FF6347 style D fill:#90EE90 ``` ## 4.3 Psychrometric Process Flow During Air Conditioning ```mermaid flowchart LR ODB[Outside Air
35 C DBT 70 percent RH] --> M[Mixing with Return Air] RA[Return Air
25 C DBT 50 percent RH] --> M M --> C[Cooling and Dehumidification
to ADP along Saturation Line] C --> H[Optional Reheat to RSHF] H --> S[Supply Air to Room
13 to 16 C DBT] S --> R[Room Condition Achieved
25 C DBT 50 percent RH] R --> RA style ODB fill:#FF7F50 style RA fill:#FFE4B5 style C fill:#87CEEB style S fill:#98FB98 style R fill:#90EE90 ``` ## 4.4 Vapor Compression Refrigeration Cycle — Block Flow (T-s Diagram Equivalent) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[State 1
Saturated Vapor
Low Pressure] --> COMP[Compressor
Work Input Wc] COMP --> P2[State 2
Superheated Vapor
High Pressure] P2 --> COND[Condenser
Heat Rejected Qc] COND --> P3[State 3
Saturated Liquid
High Pressure] P3 --> VALVE[Throttle Valve
Isenthalpic Expansion] VALVE --> P4[State 4
Two Phase Mixture
Low Pressure] P4 --> EVAP[Evaporator
Heat Absorbed Qe] EVAP --> P1 style P1 fill:#FFB6C1 style P2 fill:#FF6347 style P3 fill:#4682B4 style P4 fill:#87CEEB style COMP fill:#FFD700 style COND fill:#C0C0C0 style VALVE fill:#DDA0DD style EVAP fill:#98FB98 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Define Air Conditioning. List any four functions of air conditioning.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Air conditioning is the simultaneous control of temperature, humidity, air movement, air cleanliness, and sound level within an enclosed space to maintain predetermined environmental conditions for human comfort or industrial processes. **Four Functions (1.5 marks):** 1. **Temperature Control** — maintaining air temperature between 22–26 °C in summer. 2. **Humidity Control** — maintaining relative humidity between 40–60%. 3. **Air Movement Control** — providing fresh air circulation of 0.1–0.5 m/s. 4. **Air Cleanliness Control** — filtration of dust, pollen, and PM 2.5/10 particles. ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Distinguish between Comfort Air Conditioning and Industrial Air Conditioning.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Comfort AC | Industrial AC | |-----------|-----------|---------------| | Purpose | Human comfort | Product/Process integrity | | Temperature Range | 22–26 °C (broad) | Often narrow (e.g., 21 ± 1 °C) | | Humidity Range | 40–60% (broad) | Tight (e.g., 50 ± 2%) | | Air Cleanliness | Basic filtration | HEPA/ULPA filters (ISO 5–8) | | Design Priority | Energy efficiency, low noise | Precision, reliability, redundancy | | Examples | Offices, malls, homes | Pharma, semiconductor, hospitals | --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### QUESTION A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Model] **A refrigeration plant of 8 TR capacity is used to air-condition a hall. The total heat load is 80 kW, of which 50 kW is sensible heat. Calculate:** **(a)** The Sensible Heat Factor (SHF) and the latent heat load. **(b)** The mass of dry air circulated per second if the air temperature is to be reduced by 10 °C. Also find the volume of air handled by the blower per hour, assuming specific volume = 0.85 m³/kg. #### Part (a) Solution — 7 Marks **Step 1:** Sensible heat = 50 kW, Total heat = 80 kW. **Step 2:** SHF calculation: $$\text{SHF} = \frac{Q_s}{Q_t} = \frac{50}{80} = 0.625$$ **Step 3:** Latent heat: $$Q_l = Q_t - Q_s = 80 - 50 = 30 \text{ kW}$$ **Results (2 marks):** SHF = **0.625**, $Q_l$ = **30 kW**. #### Part (b) Solution — 7 Marks **Step 1:** Sensible heat equation: $$\dot{Q}_s = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_{p,a} \cdot \Delta T_{db}$$ $$50 = \dot{m}_a \cdot 1.005 \cdot 10$$ $$\dot{m}_a = \frac{50}{10.05} = 4.975 \text{ kg/s}$$ **Step 2:** Mass flow per hour: $$m_a = 4.975 \times 3600 = 17{,}910 \text{ kg/hr}$$ **Step 3:** Volume flow rate: $$\dot{V}_a = \dot{m}_a \cdot v = 4.975 \times 0.85 = 4.229 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ $$V_{hour} = 4.229 \times 3600 = 15{,}224 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$$ **Final Answers (2 marks):** $\dot{m}_a$ = **4.975 kg/s** = **17,910 kg/hr**, $V_{hour}$ = **15,224 m³/hr**. --- ### QUESTION B [KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model] **(a) Explain the different types of Air Conditioning systems based on application and equipment arrangement. (7 marks)** **(b) A room has sensible heat load of 30 kW and latent heat load of 12 kW. The air enters the cooling coil at 30 °C DBT and 70% RH and leaves at 15 °C DBT. Calculate: (i) SHF (ii) BPF if ADP is 8 °C (iii) the mass of dry air circulated per second. (7 marks)** #### Part (a) Solution — 7 Marks **Types of AC Systems (Classification):** **1. Based on Application (3 marks):** - **Comfort Air Conditioning:** Used for human comfort in homes, offices, hotels, malls. Standard: 22–26 °C, 40–60% RH. - **Industrial Air Conditioning:** Used for process control in pharma, electronics, food. Tight tolerances (e.g., 21 ± 0.5 °C). - **Cold Storage Air Conditioning:** For preservation of perishables at 0 to −25 °C with controlled humidity. **2. Based on Equipment Arrangement (4 marks):** - **Unitary Systems:** Window AC, Split AC. All components in one/two factory-sealed units. 0.5–5 TR. Self-contained. - **Central Systems:** Central chiller plant + AHU + ductwork. 50–5000 TR. Used in large buildings, hospitals, airports. - **Packaged Systems:** Pre-assembled DX unit with insulated cabinet. 5–50 TR. Roof-top or ground-mounted. - **VRF/VRV Systems:** Variable Refrigerant Flow. Direct refrigerant to multiple indoor units. 5–200 TR. High efficiency. #### Part (b) Solution — 7 Marks **Given:** $Q_s$ = 30 kW, $Q_l$ = 12 kW, $T_1$ = 30 °C, $T_2$ = 15 °C, $T_{ADP}$ = 8 °C. **Step 1 — SHF (2 marks):** $$\text{SHF} = \frac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_l} = \frac{30}{30 + 12} = \frac{30}{42} = 0.714$$ **Step 2 — BPF (2.5 marks):** $$\text{BPF} = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1 - T_{ADP}} = \frac{30 - 15}{30 - 8} = \frac{15}{22} = 0.682$$ **Step 3 — Mass of dry air (2.5 marks):** $$\dot{m}_a = \frac{Q_s}{1.005 \cdot (T_1 - T_2)} = \frac{30}{1.005 \times 15} = \frac{30}{15.075} = 1.99 \text{ kg/s}$$ **Final Answers:** SHF = **0.714**, BPF = **0.682**, $\dot{m}_a$ = **1.99 kg/s**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **BPF = 0.682 is unusually high.** Typical 6-row chilled water coils have BPF = 0.05–0.20. A BPF > 0.5 indicates a poorly designed coil or wrong ADP value. Recheck your inputs in the exam. > 2. **Mistaking SHF for RSHF** — SHF is at the coil, RSHF is at the room. They are equal **only** if BPF = 0. > 3. **Forgetting the constant 0.622** in specific humidity — students often write 0.66 or 0.6, losing 1 mark. > 4. **Not using 2501 kJ/kg** as the latent heat — using 2257 (boiling point value) gives wrong answers. > 5. **Unit mismatch** — TR is 3.517 kW. If the problem gives TR, convert to kW **before** all calculations. > 6. **Using DBT instead of WBT** for ADP calculations — ADP is found on the saturation curve, not the DBT line. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER - **Definition:** Air conditioning controls **5 parameters** — Temperature, Humidity, Air movement, Cleanliness, Sound. - **Standard Comfort Conditions:** 25 °C DBT, 50% RH, 0.25 m/s air velocity, < 40 dB(A) sound. - **ASHRAE** is the standard-setting body. ASHRAE 55-2023 is the latest comfort standard. - **Ton of Refrigeration (TR):** 1 TR = **3.517 kW** = 12,000 BTU/hr. - **Key Constants to Memorize:** - $c_{p,a}$ = **1.005 kJ/kg·K** - $c_{p,v}$ = **1.88 kJ/kg·K** - $h_{fg,0}$ = **2501 kJ/kg** - Molecular weight ratio $M_a/M_v$ = **28.97/18.015 = 1.608** → 0.622 - **SHF vs RSHF:** SHF = coil heat ratio; RSHF = room heat ratio. Use RSHF-BPF chart (Gatley Chart) for AC design. - **BPF Range:** Typical cooling coil BPF = **0.05 to 0.20** (lower is better). - **Types of AC:** Comfort, Industrial, Cold Storage. Equipment: Unitary, Central, Packaged, VRF. - **VCRC Cycle:** 4 processes — Compression, Condensation, Expansion, Evaporation. - **COP Formula:** $\text{COP} = Q_e / W_c$. For ideal VCRC, COP = $T_e / (T_c - T_e)$ (Carnot equivalent). - **Energy Efficiency Ratings:** EER (3.0+ is good for residential), SEER (15+ is excellent), ISEER (Indian Standard, BEE Star Rating). - **Moist Air Formula:** $h = 1.005 T_{db} + \omega(2501 + 1.88 T_{db})$ — **memorize verbatim**. - **Psychrometric Chart:** Use this to solve graphical problems in KTU exams. Master reading DBT, WBT, DPT, ω, h, φ, v. - **Process Names:** Heating, Cooling, Dehumidification, Humidification, Mixing, Sensible Heating, Sensible Cooling — be ready to draw them. - **KTU Hot Topics (2024–2026 Expected):** - RSHF-BPF-ADP numericals - Year-Round AC (YAC) systems - VRF/VRV technology - Green building integration (IGBC, LEED) - Refrigerant transition (R-32, R-410A, R-290) > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Revision Formula Card:** > $$\text{SHF} = \frac{1.005 \Delta T}{1.005 \Delta T + 2501 \Delta \omega} \quad | \quad \text{BPF} = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1 - T_{ADP}} \quad | \quad \omega = \frac{0.622 P_v}{P_{atm} - P_v}$$ > $$\dot{m}_a = \frac{Q_s}{1.005 \Delta T} \quad | \quad 1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW} \quad | \quad \text{COP} = \frac{Q_e}{W_c}$$

topics.title: Physiological Principles in Air Conditioning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLLOW STORAGE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Physiological Principles in Air Conditioning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: PHYSIOLOGICAL PRINCIPLES IN AIR CONDITIONING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physiological Principles in Air Conditioning** refer to the study of the human body's response to the surrounding thermal environment and the engineering parameters that must be controlled (air temperature, humidity, velocity, and radiant temperature) to create conditions of **thermal comfort, health, and productivity** for occupants.

According to **ASHRAE Standard 55**, thermal comfort is defined as *"that condition of mind which expresses satisfaction with the thermal environment."* It is achieved when the heat generated by the human body (metabolic heat) is balanced by the heat lost to the surrounding environment without any strain on the body's **thermoregulatory system**.

### 1.2 The Human Body as a Heat Engine Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy**: Think of the human body as a small internal combustion engine running at approximately 37°C. Just as a car engine constantly generates heat from fuel combustion and needs a radiator to dissipate this heat to prevent overheating, the human body continuously burns food (glucose) to produce energy and generates heat as a byproduct. The "radiator" of the human body is the skin surface, where heat is dissipated to the surrounding air via four mechanisms: conduction, convection, radiation, and evaporation of sweat.

### 1.3 The Six Classical Factors of Comfort

The **Fanger Comfort Equation** (developed by Professor Povl Ole Fanger in 1970) identifies **six primary variables** that govern human thermal comfort:

1. **Air Temperature ($T_a$)** — measured in °C
2. **Mean Radiant Temperature ($\bar{T}_r$)** — measured in °C
3. **Relative Humidity (RH)** — measured in %
4. **Air Velocity ($V_a$)** — measured in m/s
5. **Metabolic Rate ($M$)** — measured in **met units (1 met = 58.2 W/m²)**
6. **Clothing Insulation ($I_{cl}$)** — measured in **clo units (1 clo = 0.155 m²·K/W)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Insight**: A common student mistake is considering only air temperature. In reality, an air-conditioned room at 25°C can still feel uncomfortably warm if the surrounding walls are at 35°C (due to solar radiation through glass), because the body absorbs extra radiant heat from the walls.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Human Body Heat Generation — Metabolism

The human body converts chemical energy from food into mechanical work and heat. The metabolic rate depends on the activity level.

> [!NOTE]
> The average **resting metabolic rate** of a sedentary person is approximately **1 met (≈ 58.2 W/m² of body surface area)**. The average adult body surface area is approximately **1.8 m²**.

### 2.2 Mechanisms of Heat Loss from the Human Body

The body loses heat to the environment through **four fundamental physical mechanisms**:

#### (a) Heat Loss by Conduction ($Q_{cd}$)
Heat transfer from the skin surface to the air molecules in direct contact.
- Normally a **very minor component** because air is a poor conductor.
- Becomes significant only when the body is in contact with a cooler solid (e.g., sitting on a cold chair).

#### (b) Heat Loss by Convection ($Q_{cv}$)
Heat transfer from the skin surface to the surrounding air by bulk fluid motion.
- This is a **major mode of heat loss** in air-conditioned spaces.
- Increases with air velocity.
- The convective heat loss from the body is given by:

$$Q_{cv} = h_c \cdot A_s \cdot (T_{sk} - T_a)$$

where $h_c$ is the convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K), $A_s$ is the body surface area, $T_{sk}$ is mean skin temperature, and $T_a$ is air temperature.

#### (c) Heat Loss by Radiation ($Q_r$)
Heat transfer from the skin surface to surrounding surfaces (walls, windows, ceiling) by electromagnetic waves.
- Independent of air temperature.
- Depends on the **Mean Radiant Temperature (MRT)** of surrounding surfaces.

$$Q_r = h_r \cdot A_s \cdot (T_{sk} - \bar{T}_r)$$

where $h_r$ is the linear radiative heat transfer coefficient (≈ 4.7 W/m²·K for skin emissivity of 0.95 at 30°C).

#### (d) Heat Loss by Evaporation ($Q_e$)
Heat loss due to evaporation of moisture (sweat and insensible perspiration) from the skin surface and respiratory tract.
- This is the body's **most important cooling mechanism** in hot environments.
- The latent heat of vaporization of water is **≈ 2430 kJ/kg**.
- Evaporative heat loss depends on the **vapor pressure difference** between the skin and ambient air:

$$Q_e = h_e \cdot A_s \cdot (P_{sk} - P_a)$$

where $h_e$ is the evaporative heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·kPa), $P_{sk}$ is the saturated vapor pressure at skin temperature, and $P_a$ is the ambient vapor pressure.

### 2.3 Heat Balance Equation of the Human Body

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The First Law of Thermodynamics applied to the human body**: At steady-state thermal comfort, the rate of heat generated internally (metabolism) equals the rate of heat loss to the environment, with no storage of heat in the body tissues.

$$M - W = Q_{cd} + Q_{cv} + Q_r + Q_e$$

For the human body:
- $M$ = Total metabolic heat production (W/m²)
- $W$ = External mechanical work done (W/m²)
- $(M - W)$ = Internal heat that must be dissipated to maintain body temperature

In an air-conditioned space, evaporation occurs from both **skin (sweating)** and **respiratory tract (breathing)**:

$$Q_e = Q_{sw} + Q_{res}$$

where $Q_{sw}$ is evaporative heat loss from skin and $Q_{res}$ is latent heat loss from respiration.

### 2.4 Heat Loss Through Respiration

Respiration causes both **sensible (dry) heat loss** and **latent (moisture) heat loss** due to the warming and humidification of inspired air:

$$Q_{res,dry} = 0.0014 \cdot M \cdot (T_{ex} - T_a)$$

$$Q_{res,lat} = 0.0173 \cdot M \cdot (p_{ex} - p_a)$$

where $T_{ex}$ is expired air temperature (≈ 35°C) and $p_{ex}$ is expired vapor pressure (≈ 5.62 kPa at 35°C), and $M$ is in W/m².

### 2.5 ASHRAE Comfort Conditions (Summer & Winter)

> [!NOTE]
> **ASHRAE Standard 55-2020** recommends the following ranges for thermal comfort in typical indoor environments:

| Parameter | Summer (Cooling) | Winter (Heating) |
|---|---|---|
| **Air Temperature** | 23 – 26 °C | 20 – 24 °C |
| **Mean Radiant Temp (MRT)** | Close to air temp | Close to air temp |
| **Relative Humidity (RH)** | 30 – 60 % | 30 – 60 % |
| **Air Velocity** | 0.25 m/s (max) | 0.15 m/s (max) |
| **Clothing (clo)** | 0.5 clo | 1.0 clo |
| **Metabolic Rate** | 1.0 – 1.3 met | 1.0 – 1.3 met |

### 2.6 Effective Temperature (ET)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Effective Temperature** is an empirical index developed by **Houghton & Yaglou (1923)** at the ASHVE research laboratory in Pittsburgh. It is defined as the temperature of a **saturated (100% RH) enclosure** in which a person would exchange the **same total heat (sensible + latent)** as in the actual environment.

ET accounts for the combined effect of air temperature, humidity, and air velocity. It is determined from the **ASHRAE Comfort Chart**.

**Limitations of ET:**
1. Does not account for radiant heat load.
2. Based on subjects in light clothing at low activity levels.
3. Equal weighting of temperature and humidity is debatable.

### 2.7 Corrected Effective Temperature (CET)

> [!NOTE]
> **CET** is an improved index proposed by **Vernon (1932)**. It is the **Effective Temperature corrected for radiant heat load**. It is read from a **Corrected Effective Temperature Chart** which includes mean radiant temperature as an additional parameter.

CET is the most accurate single-number index for thermal comfort assessment and is widely used in KTU numerical problems.

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Table for Examination**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Standard Values |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Body Heat Balance | $M - W = Q_{cd} + Q_{cv} + Q_r + Q_e$ | W/m² |
| 2 | 1 met unit | $1 \text{ met}$ | $= 58.2 \text{ W/m}^2$ |
| 3 | 1 clo unit | $1 \text{ clo}$ | $= 0.155 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$ |
| 4 | Convective heat loss | $Q_{cv} = h_c \cdot A_s \cdot (T_{sk} - T_a)$ | W/m² |
| 5 | Radiative heat loss | $Q_r = h_r \cdot A_s \cdot (T_{sk} - \bar{T}_r)$ | W/m² |
| 6 | Evaporative heat loss | $Q_e = h_e \cdot A_s \cdot (P_{sk} - P_a)$ | W/m² |
| 7 | Respiration — dry heat | $Q_{res,dry} = 0.0014 \cdot M \cdot (T_{ex} - T_a)$ | W/m² |
| 8 | Respiration — latent heat | $Q_{res,lat} = 0.0173 \cdot M \cdot (p_{ex} - p_a)$ | W/m² |
| 9 | Latent heat of vaporization | $h_{fg}$ | $= 2430 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (at 30°C) |
| 10 | Mean skin temperature | $T_{sk}$ | ≈ 33.7 °C (comfort) |
| 11 | Body surface area (Dubois) | $A_s = 0.203 \cdot H^{0.725} \cdot W^{0.425}$ | m² (H in m, W in kg) |
| 12 | ET Comfort range | Summer: 22 – 26 °C ET | — |
| 13 | Sensible heat from person | $q_s = 0.6 \cdot M - 60$ | W/person (for M in W/m², A_s = 1.8 m²) |
| 14 | Latent heat from person | $q_L = 0.4 \cdot M + 60$ | W/person |

> **Engineering Utility**: The Fanger Comfort Equation and these formulas form the foundation of **HVAC design loads**. In real engineering practice, these values are used in software like **TRACE, HAP, and Carrier E20** to compute occupant loads, duct sizing, and chiller capacity. Underestimating latent heat from occupants leads to under-sized dehumidification systems, causing mold growth and discomfort in tropical climates like Kerala.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Fanger PMV-PPD Model (Predicted Mean Vote)

The **Predicted Mean Vote (PMV)** model was developed by P.O. Fanger in 1970 and is recommended by ISO 7730 and ASHRAE 55. It predicts the mean thermal sensation vote on a 7-point scale (cold, cool, slightly cool, neutral, slightly warm, warm, hot) on a scale of -3 to +3, where 0 = neutral.

### Step 1: Internal Heat Production

The total metabolic heat produced by the body must be balanced by heat loss. The internal heat production in the body (after subtracting external mechanical work $W$) is:

$$H = M - W \quad \text{(W/m}^2\text{)}$$

### Step 2: Heat Loss from Skin Surface (Convective + Radiative)

The combined dry heat loss from skin by convection and radiation:

$$Q_{sk} = \frac{(T_{sk} - T_{cl})}{0.155 \cdot I_{cl}} \quad \text{(W/m}^2\text{)}$$

where $T_{cl}$ is the clothing surface temperature (°C), $I_{cl}$ is the clothing insulation in clo, and 0.155 is the conversion factor to SI units.

### Step 3: Clothing Surface Temperature Derivation

Applying an energy balance on the outer clothing surface: heat conducted through clothing equals heat lost by convection + radiation from the clothing surface:

$$\frac{(T_{sk} - T_{cl})}{0.155 \cdot I_{cl}} = f_{cl} \cdot h_c \cdot (T_{cl} - T_a) + f_{cl} \cdot h_r \cdot (T_{cl} - \bar{T}_r)$$

where $f_{cl}$ is the clothing area factor (ratio of clothed body surface to nude body surface):

$$f_{cl} = 1.0 + 0.2 \cdot I_{cl} \quad \text{(for } I_{cl} \leq 0.078 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W)}$$

$$f_{cl} = 1.05 + 0.1 \cdot I_{cl} \quad \text{(for } I_{cl} > 0.078 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W)}$$

### Step 4: Evaporative Heat Loss from Skin

$$E_{sk} = \frac{P_{sk} - P_a}{(0.155 \cdot I_{cl} / f_{cl}) + 1 / (f_{cl} \cdot h_e)}$$

where $P_{sk}$ is the saturated vapor pressure at skin temperature, and $h_e = 16.5 \cdot h_c$ (Lewis relation).

### Step 5: Heat Loss from Respiration

$$Q_{res} = 0.0014 \cdot M \cdot (34 - T_a) + 0.0173 \cdot M \cdot (5.62 - p_a)$$

where $T_{ex} = 34°C$ and $p_{ex} = 5.62$ kPa (standard Fanger values).

### Step 6: Final Heat Balance

$$M - W = Q_{sk} + E_{sk} + Q_{res}$$

### Step 7: PMV Equation

Fanger simplified this energy balance into the famous PMV equation:

$$\text{PMV} = \left[0.303 \cdot e^{-0.036 \cdot M} + 0.028\right] \cdot L$$

where $L$ is the **thermal load** defined as:

$$L = (M - W) - Q_{sk} - E_{sk} - Q_{res}$$

- $L > 0$ → Warm (PMV > 0)
- $L = 0$ → Neutral (PMV = 0)
- $L < 0$ → Cool (PMV < 0)

### Step 8: PPD (Predicted Percentage of Dissatisfied)

$$\text{PPD} = 100 - 95 \cdot e^{-0.03353 \cdot \text{PMV}^4 - 0.2179 \cdot \text{PMV}^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Even at PMV = 0 (perfectly neutral), **PPD = 5%** due to individual biological variation. ASHRAE recommends keeping PMV between -0.5 and +0.5.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — KTU Standard Pattern

### **Problem Statement (Model KTU Exam Question):**

> A person weighing **70 kg** and **1.75 m** tall is engaged in light office work in an air-conditioned room. The following data are given:
> - **Air dry-bulb temperature:** $T_a = 26°C$
> - **Mean radiant temperature:** $\bar{T}_r = 28°C$
> - **Relative humidity:** RH = 50%
> - **Air velocity:** $V_a = 0.15$ m/s
> - **Metabolic rate:** $M = 1.2$ met
> - **Clothing insulation:** $I_{cl} = 0.6$ clo
> - **Ambient vapor pressure:** $p_a = 1.6$ kPa
>
> **Calculate:**
> (a) The sensible heat loss and latent heat loss from the body.
> (b) Comment on whether the person will feel thermally comfortable.

### **Given Data:**
- $M = 1.2 \times 58.2 = 69.84$ W/m²
- $W = 0$ (no external work)
- $I_{cl} = 0.6 \times 0.155 = 0.093$ m²·K/W
- $T_{sk} = 33.7°C$ (assumed mean skin temperature)
- $T_{ex} = 34°C$, $p_{ex} = 5.62$ kPa

### **Step 1: Body Surface Area (Dubois Formula)**

$$A_s = 0.203 \cdot H^{0.725} \cdot W^{0.425}$$

Substituting $H = 1.75$ m and body mass $M_b = 70$ kg:

$$A_s = 0.203 \cdot (1.75)^{0.725} \cdot (70)^{0.425}$$

$$A_s = 0.203 \cdot 1.515 \cdot 5.595 = 1.721 \text{ m}^2$$

### **Step 2: Clothing Area Factor**

Since $I_{cl} = 0.093 > 0.078$ m²·K/W:

$$f_{cl} = 1.05 + 0.1 \cdot I_{cl} = 1.05 + 0.1 \cdot 0.6 = 1.11$$

### **Step 3: Convective Heat Transfer Coefficient**

For low air velocities, $h_c \approx 3.0$ W/m²·K (natural convection).

### **Step 4: Radiative Heat Transfer Coefficient**

$$h_r = 4 \cdot \epsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot [(T_{sk} + \bar{T}_r)/2 + 273]^{3}$$

For skin emissivity $\epsilon = 0.95$:

$$h_r \approx 4.7 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

### **Step 5: Total Convective + Radiative Heat Loss (Dry)**

$$Q_{dry} = f_{cl} \cdot h_c \cdot (T_{cl} - T_a) + f_{cl} \cdot h_r \cdot (T_{cl} - \bar{T}_r)$$

Since $T_{cl}$ is unknown, we use the standard approximation for low-activity indoor conditions:

$$Q_{dry} \approx 0.6 \cdot (M - W) \cdot A_s = 0.6 \cdot 69.84 \cdot 1.721 = 72.1 \text{ W}$$

### **Step 6: Evaporative Heat Loss (Latent)**

$$Q_{latent} \approx 0.4 \cdot (M - W) \cdot A_s = 0.4 \cdot 69.84 \cdot 1.721 = 48.1 \text{ W}$$

### **Step 7: Verify Heat Balance**

$$\text{Total Loss} = 72.1 + 48.1 = 120.2 \text{ W}$$

$$\text{Total Generation} = M \cdot A_s = 69.84 \cdot 1.721 = 120.2 \text{ W} \quad \checkmark$$

### **Step 8: Comfort Analysis**

Since the heat balance is satisfied with $L \approx 0$, the person is in **thermal neutrality**. The room conditions fall within the **ASHRAE summer comfort envelope** ($T_a = 26°C$, MRT = 28°C, RH = 50%, $V_a = 0.15$ m/s, 0.6 clo, 1.2 met), so the occupant is **thermally comfortable**.

### **Analytical Matrix Summary:**

| Quantity | Calculated Value | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| $A_s$ (Body Surface Area) | 1.721 m² | Dubois formula |
| Sensible Heat Loss ($q_s$) | 72.1 W | Convection + Radiation |
| Latent Heat Loss ($q_L$) | 48.1 W | Evaporation + Respiration |
| Total Heat Generated | 120.2 W | At 1.2 met |
| Comfort Status | ✅ Comfortable | Within ASHRAE zone |

> **Engineering Takeaway:** Note that a single sedentary person contributes ~120 W of cooling load to the AC system — equivalent to a 120 W incandescent bulb. In a packed conference room of 50 people, this is **6 kW** of heat load from occupants alone, which must be added to the cooling load calculation along with heat from walls, lighting, and equipment.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Human Body Heat Transfer Mechanism (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Metabolic Heat Production M = 58.2 W per sq.m per met"] --> B["Body Core Temperature Regulation at 37 degree C"]
    B --> C{"Heat Loss Pathway"}
    C --> D["Conduction Qcd - Minor Loss to Contact Surfaces"]
    C --> E["Convection Qcv - Major Loss to Air"]
    C --> F["Radiation Qr - Loss to Walls and Windows"]
    C --> G["Evaporation Qe - Sweat and Respiration"]
    E --> E1["Depends on Air Velocity"]
    F --> F1["Depends on Mean Radiant Temperature"]
    G --> G1["Depends on Ambient Humidity"]
    D --> H["Thermal Equilibrium at Comfort"]
    E1 --> H
    F1 --> H
    G1 --> H
    H --> I{"PMV Index"}
    I --> J["PMV equals 0 - Neutral Comfort"]
    I --> K["PMV greater than 0 - Warm"]
    I --> L["PMV less than 0 - Cool"]
    style A fill:#FFD700
    style B fill:#FF6347
    style H fill:#90EE90
    style J fill:#87CEEB
```

## 4.2 Six Factors of Thermal Comfort (Fanger Model) — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENV ["ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS"]
        E1["Air Temperature Ta"]
        E2["Mean Radiant Temperature Tbar r"]
        E3["Relative Humidity RH"]
        E4["Air Velocity Va"]
    end
    subgraph PERS ["PERSONAL FACTORS"]
        P1["Metabolic Rate M"]
        P2["Clothing Insulation Icl"]
    end
    ENV --> OUT["THERMAL SENSATION PMV"]
    PERS --> OUT
    OUT --> R1["PMV = -3 Cold to +3 Hot"]
    R1 --> R2{"Comfortable?"}
    R2 -- Yes --> R3["ASHRAE Comfort Zone"]
    R2 -- No --> R4["Adjust AC System"]
    style ENV fill:#FFFACD
    style PERS fill:#E0FFFF
    style OUT fill:#FFB6C1
    style R3 fill:#90EE90
    style R4 fill:#FFA07A
```

## 4.3 Body Thermoregulation System — Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Skin Temperature Sensor - Cold and Warm Receptors"] --> S2["Hypothalamus - Body Thermostat"]
    S3["Core Temperature Sensor - Hypothalamic Neurons"] --> S2
    S2 --> R1{"Temperature Deviation?"}
    R1 -- Core Too Hot --> A1["Vasodilation - Increase Skin Blood Flow"]
    R1 -- Core Too Hot --> A2["Sweating - Evaporative Cooling"]
    R1 -- Core Too Cold --> B1["Vasoconstriction - Reduce Skin Blood Flow"]
    R1 -- Core Too Cold --> B2["Shivering - Increase Metabolic Heat"]
    R1 -- Core Too Cold --> B3["Piloerection - Trapping Air Layer"]
    A1 --> OUT1["Body Temperature Returns to 37 degree C"]
    A2 --> OUT1
    B1 --> OUT1
    B2 --> OUT1
    B3 --> OUT1
    style S2 fill:#FF6347
    style OUT1 fill:#90EE90
```

## 4.4 ASHRAE Comfort Chart — Conceptual Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CHART ["ASHRAE COMFORT CHART - REGIONS"]
        R1["Region A - Cold - ET less than 14 degree C"]
        R2["Region B - Cool - ET 14 to 18 degree C"]
        R3["Region C - Slightly Cool - ET 18 to 22 degree C"]
        R4["Region D - COMFORT ZONE - ET 22 to 26 degree C"]
        R5["Region E - Slightly Warm - ET 26 to 28 degree C"]
        R6["Region F - Warm - ET 28 to 30 degree C"]
        R7["Region G - Hot - ET greater than 30 degree C"]
    end
    R4 --> R4A["Recommended for Indoor AC Design in India"]
    style R4 fill:#90EE90
    style R4A fill:#FFD700
```

## 4.5 Effective Temperature (ET) Concept Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Actual Conditions: Ta, RH, Va"] --> B["Equivalent Saturated Air at Same Total Heat Exchange"]
    B --> C["Effective Temperature ET read from ASHVE Chart"]
    C --> D["Comfort or Discomfort Determination"]
    E["If Radiant Heat Present"] --> F["Use Corrected Effective Temperature CET"]
    F --> D
    style B fill:#FFB6C1
    style C fill:#87CEEB
    style F fill:#FFD700
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the term "Effective Temperature" (ET). State its limitations.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

> **Definition (2 marks):** Effective Temperature is an empirical index of thermal comfort that combines the effect of air temperature, humidity, and air velocity into a single value. It is defined as **the temperature of a saturated (100% RH) enclosure, in still air, in which a person would exchange the same total heat (sensible + latent) from skin and lungs as in the actual environment.**

> **Limitations (1 mark):**
> 1. Does not account for radiant heat from warm/cold surfaces.
> 2. Equal importance is given to humidity as to temperature, which is physiologically incorrect.
> 3. Based on experiments with lightly clothed subjects in still air.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List the six factors affecting human thermal comfort as per Fanger's model.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

The six factors are:
1. **Air Temperature** ($T_a$) — environmental
2. **Mean Radiant Temperature** ($\bar{T}_r$) — environmental
3. **Relative Humidity** (RH) — environmental
4. **Air Velocity** ($V_a$) — environmental
5. **Metabolic Rate** ($M$) — personal
6. **Clothing Insulation** ($I_{cl}$) — personal

(1 mark for naming, 1 mark for grouping into environmental vs personal, 1 mark for correct SI units)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the various mechanisms of heat transfer from the human body to the surrounding environment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A man weighing 70 kg, 1.7 m tall, performs light sedentary work in an air-conditioned office. Calculate:
- (i) Body surface area
- (ii) Total metabolic heat generated (assume M = 1.1 met)
- (iii) Sensible heat loss (assume 60% goes as sensible)
- (iv) Latent heat loss

**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Heat Transfer Mechanisms from Human Body:**

The human body, maintained at ~37°C core temperature, continuously rejects metabolic heat to the environment through **four distinct mechanisms**:

**1. Heat Loss by Conduction ($Q_{cd}$) — 1.5 marks:**
- Transfer of heat by direct molecular contact from skin to surrounding air or objects.
- Air is a poor conductor, so this is a **minor** contributor (~2-3%).
- Becomes significant only when body contacts a cooler surface (cold chair, cold floor).

**2. Heat Loss by Convection ($Q_{cv}$) — 1.5 marks:**
- Heat transfer from skin to surrounding air by bulk fluid motion.
- Governed by: $Q_{cv} = h_c \cdot A_s \cdot (T_{sk} - T_a)$
- Natural convection at low velocities; forced convection with fans/AC.
- Increasing air velocity enhances heat loss — basis for ceiling fans.

**3. Heat Loss by Radiation ($Q_r$) — 1.5 marks:**
- Heat transfer via electromagnetic waves from skin to surrounding surfaces (walls, ceiling, windows).
- Governed by: $Q_r = h_r \cdot A_s \cdot (T_{sk} - \bar{T}_r)$
- Independent of air temperature; explains why sun-facing glass walls feel hot.

**4. Heat Loss by Evaporation ($Q_e$) — 2 marks:**
- Heat consumed in converting sweat/lung moisture to vapor.
- Latent heat of vaporization ≈ 2430 kJ/kg.
- Two sources: (a) **Skin evaporation** (sweating), and (b) **Respiratory evaporation** (breathing).
- Depends on vapor pressure gradient: $Q_e = h_e \cdot A_s \cdot (P_{sk} - P_a)$
- Becomes dominant in hot/humid climates — **most critical mechanism in Kerala**.

**Sketch (1 mark):** Diagram showing body with 4 arrows labeled conduction, convection, radiation, evaporation.

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** Mass $W_b = 70$ kg, Height $H = 1.7$ m, M = 1.1 met.

**(i) Body Surface Area (Dubois Formula) — 2 marks:**

$$A_s = 0.203 \cdot H^{0.725} \cdot W_b^{0.425}$$

$$A_s = 0.203 \cdot (1.7)^{0.725} \cdot (70)^{0.425}$$

$$A_s = 0.203 \cdot 1.481 \cdot 5.595 = 1.683 \text{ m}^2$$

**(ii) Total Metabolic Heat Generated — 2 marks:**

Convert metabolic rate to SI:

$$M = 1.1 \text{ met} = 1.1 \times 58.2 = 64.02 \text{ W/m}^2$$

Total heat generated:

$$Q_{total} = M \times A_s = 64.02 \times 1.683 = 107.7 \text{ W}$$

**(iii) Sensible Heat Loss — 1.5 marks:**

$$q_s = 0.6 \times Q_{total} = 0.6 \times 107.7 = 64.6 \text{ W}$$

**(iv) Latent Heat Loss — 1.5 marks:**

$$q_L = 0.4 \times Q_{total} = 0.4 \times 107.7 = 43.1 \text{ W}$$

> **Verification:** $q_s + q_L = 64.6 + 43.1 = 107.7$ W ✓

**Engineering Note (Bonus):** This person adds 107.7 W to the cooling load — equivalent to running a 100 W light bulb continuously. For a room of 20 people, total occupant cooling load = ~2.15 kW.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Define the terms "met" and "clo" as used in physiological aspects of air conditioning. State typical values for various activities and clothing combinations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** State and explain the heat balance equation of the human body. A person at rest produces 100 W of metabolic heat. If 65 W is lost by evaporation and the rest by convection, what must be the combined air and MRT for thermal neutrality? Take skin temperature as 34°C, body surface area = 1.8 m², $h_c = 4$ W/m²·K, $h_r = 5$ W/m²·K, and clothing factor $f_{cl} = 1.1$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Definition of "met" (2 marks):**
The **met** is a physiological unit of metabolic rate per unit body surface area.
$$1 \text{ met} = 58.2 \text{ W/m}^2$$

It represents the metabolic heat produced by a sedentary person at rest. Typical values:

| Activity | Metabolic Rate (met) |
|---|---|
| Sleeping | 0.7 |
| Seated, quiet (office work) | 1.0 |
| Standing, light work | 1.2 – 1.6 |
| Walking (3 km/h) | 2.0 |
| Heavy work / sports | 3.0 – 8.0 |

**Definition of "clo" (2 marks):**
The **clo** is a unit of thermal resistance of clothing.
$$1 \text{ clo} = 0.155 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

Typical values:

| Clothing Combination | Clo Value |
|---|---|
| Naked | 0.0 |
| Shorts only | 0.1 |
| Light tropical business suit | 0.5 |
| Standard business suit | 1.0 |
| Heavy winter suit + overcoat | 1.5 – 2.0 |

**Relation and significance (3 marks):**
The met and clo together quantify the heat generation and insulation of a person, allowing HVAC engineers to compute cooling loads accurately. In tropical India, designers typically use M = 1.0–1.2 met and Icl = 0.5 clo for commercial spaces.

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Heat Balance Equation (3 marks):**

$$M - W = Q_{cd} + Q_{cv} + Q_r + Q_e$$

For a person at rest in steady state, $W = 0$ and $Q_{cd}$ is negligible. The equation simplifies to:

$$M = Q_{cv} + Q_r + Q_e$$

**Given:**
- $M = 100$ W
- $Q_e = 65$ W (evaporative)
- $T_{sk} = 34°C$
- $A_s = 1.8 \text{ m}^2$
- $h_c = 4$ W/m²·K
- $h_r = 5$ W/m²·K
- $f_{cl} = 1.1$

**Step 1: Heat lost by dry (sensible) means — convection + radiation (2 marks):**

$$Q_{dry} = M - Q_e = 100 - 65 = 35 \text{ W}$$

$$Q_{dry} = f_{cl} \cdot (h_c + h_r) \cdot A_s \cdot (T_{sk} - T_{eff})$$

where $T_{eff}$ is the combined effective temperature (assumed average of $T_a$ and $\bar{T}_r$ for simplicity).

**Step 2: Solve for $T_{eff}$ (2 marks):**

$$35 = 1.1 \cdot (4 + 5) \cdot 1.8 \cdot (34 - T_{eff})$$

$$35 = 19.8 \cdot (34 - T_{eff})$$

$$34 - T_{eff} = \frac{35}{19.8} = 1.768°C$$

$$T_{eff} = 34 - 1.768 = 32.23°C$$

This represents the **uniform equivalent temperature** (weighted average of air and MRT) required for thermal neutrality under the given metabolic and evaporation rates.

> **Critical Engineering Insight (Bonus):** Note that if the dry-bulb air temperature alone is 25°C but the wall surface temperature is 38°C (sun-exposed wall), the actual MRT can be much higher, causing occupant discomfort despite cool air. This is why **insulating sun-exposed walls and using reflective glass** is as important as cooling the air.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert met → W/m²** — Always multiply by 58.2 before calculations. Many students write "M = 1.2 met" directly into heat formulas and get wrong answers, losing 2-3 marks.
>
> 2. **Confusing clo and met units** — Clo is clothing insulation; met is metabolic rate. Using clo where met is required (or vice versa) is a critical error.
>
> 3. **Using body mass in kg for the W exponent instead of weight** — The Dubois formula uses $W_b$ (body mass in kg), NOT force in Newtons.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to add respiration heat loss** — Many students compute only $Q_{sk}$ and miss the respiration terms ($Q_{res,dry}$ and $Q_{res,lat}$). Always include respiration in Fanger-type problems.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the factor $f_{cl}$ (clothing area factor)** — Clothing increases the effective radiating surface area, so the factor must be multiplied with convective/radiative terms.
>
> 6. **Mixing up Sensible Heat Factor (SHF) with sensible heat from occupants** — Occupant sensible/latent heat split is typically 60:40 at standard indoor conditions, but SHF refers to the entire cooling coil's sensible-to-total heat ratio.
>
> 7. **Not mentioning the limitations of ET** in definition questions — Examiners specifically look for the limitation regarding radiant heat (full 1 mark lost if missed).

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Physiological Principles in AC:**
>
> - ⭐ **Heat Balance Equation:** $M - W = Q_{cd} + Q_{cv} + Q_r + Q_e$ — Always write this in the first line of any comfort problem.
> - ⭐ **1 met = 58.2 W/m²**, **1 clo = 0.155 m²·K/W** — Non-negotiable constants.
> - ⭐ **Dubois Formula** for body surface area: $A_s = 0.203 \cdot H^{0.725} \cdot W_b^{0.425}$ (H in m, mass in kg).
> - ⭐ **ASHRAE Summer Comfort:** 23–26°C, RH 30–60%, Velocity ≤ 0.25 m/s.
> - ⭐ **ASHRAE Winter Comfort:** 20–24°C, RH 30–60%, Velocity ≤ 0.15 m/s.
> - ⭐ **Six Fanger Factors:** 4 environmental ($T_a$, $\bar{T}_r$, RH, $V_a$) + 2 personal (M, $I_{cl}$).
> - ⭐ **Effective Temperature (ET)** — empirical index combining temperature + humidity + velocity; does NOT account for radiation.
> - ⭐ **Corrected Effective Temperature (CET)** — ET corrected for mean radiant temperature; most accurate single-number comfort index.
> - ⭐ **Four heat loss mechanisms:** Conduction (minor), Convection (major), Radiation (major), Evaporation (most critical in hot climates).
> - ⭐ **Evaporative cooling** is the body's most powerful defense against overheating — depends on vapor pressure difference.
> - ⭐ **Respiratory heat loss** has both sensible (dry, ~10%) and latent (90%) components.
> - ⭐ **PMV = 0** at perfect thermal neutrality; **PPD ≥ 5%** even at PMV = 0 due to individual variation.
> - ⭐ **60:40 split** between sensible and latent heat from occupants is the standard KTU assumption for indoor sedentary conditions.
> - ⭐ **Clothing Area Factor** $f_{cl} = 1.0 + 0.2 \cdot I_{cl}$ for $I_{cl} \leq 0.078$ m²·K/W; $f_{cl} = 1.05 + 0.1 \cdot I_{cl}$ for $I_{cl} > 0.078$ m²·K/W.
> - ⭐ **Hygienic ventilation** typically requires **25–50 m³/h per person** of fresh air (ASHRAE 62.1).
> - ⭐ **Mean skin temperature** in comfort = 33.7°C; drops to ~30°C in cold stress; rises to ~35°C in heat stress.
> - ⭐ **In Kerala's tropical climate**, latent load dominates (60-70% of total cooling load) due to high ambient humidity (70-90% RH).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adiabatic Mixing of Two Air Streams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Adiabatic Mixing of Two Air Streams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Adiabatic Mixing of Two Air Streams** is the process by which two streams of moist air, characterized by different thermodynamic states (dry-bulb temperature, wet-bulb temperature, relative humidity, specific humidity, and enthalpy), are combined in a mixing chamber such that:

1. **No heat is exchanged with the surroundings** (i.e., the mixing chamber is perfectly insulated → $Q = 0$).
2. **No work is done on or by the system** ($W = 0$).
3. The combined mixture attains a new equilibrium state whose properties are governed by the **simultaneous conservation of dry-air mass, water-vapour mass, and total energy (enthalpy)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** *Adiabatic mixing is a steady-flow, steady-state process in which the resultant mixture state lies on the straight line joining the two parent states on the Psychrometric Chart, with its position determined by the mass-flow ratio of the streams.*

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you are making **nimbu paani (lemonade)** in a large insulated steel jug:
- You pour in **Stream 1** — a strong, very sweet, warm concentrate (think of it as **hot, humid return air** from a room).
- You pour in **Stream 2** — chilled, very dilute water (think of it as **cool, dry fresh air** from the outdoor atmosphere).
- You stir them inside a perfectly insulated, closed jug. **No heat escapes**, no extra ice melts, no evaporation to the room occurs.

The resulting lemonade is a blend — *not as hot as Stream 1, not as cold as Stream 2, somewhere in between*. Importantly:
- The **sweetness** (analogous to specific humidity $W$) and the **total warmth content** (analogous to enthalpy $h$) of the mixture depend on **how much** of each stream you poured in.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world AC analogy:** Every air-handling unit (AHU) in a central plant performs this exact operation — it mixes **return air** from the conditioned space with a controlled fraction of **outside air (fresh air)** to produce a mixed-air stream that is then cooled, dehumidified, or heated as required. This is governed by the **minimum outside-air code (ASHRAE 62.1)** but is physically governed by adiabatic mixing equations.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Specific heat of dry air | $c_{pa}$ | **1.005 kJ/kg·K** |
| Specific heat of water vapour | $c_{pv}$ | **1.88 kJ/kg·K** |
| Latent heat at 0 °C | $h_{fg,0}$ | **2501 kJ/kg** |
| Universal gas constant (water vapour) | $R_v$ | **0.4615 kJ/kg·K** |
| Atmospheric pressure (standard) | $P_t$ | **101.325 kPa** |

> [!TIP]
> Memorize $c_{pa} = 1.005$, $c_{pv} = 1.88$, and $h_{fg,0} = 2501$ — these three constants appear in **nearly every** KTU psychrometric numerical.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Governing Physical Principles (Step-by-Step Logic)

Consider a perfectly insulated mixing chamber with **two inlet streams** (1 and 2) and **one outlet stream** (3). Apply the three fundamental conservation laws:

**Step 1 — Conservation of Dry-Air Mass (continuity for dry air)**

Since no dry air enters or leaves except through the ducts, the total mass flow rate of dry air is conserved:

$$\dot{m}_{a1} + \dot{m}_{a2} = \dot{m}_{a3}$$

**Step 2 — Conservation of Water-Vapour Mass**

The moisture carried by each stream (kg water vapour per second) must balance:

$$\dot{m}_{a1} W_1 + \dot{m}_{a2} W_2 = \dot{m}_{a3} W_3$$

This is because $\dot{m}_v = \dot{m}_a \cdot W$ by definition of specific humidity.

**Step 3 — Conservation of Energy (First Law for Open System, Steady State)**

With $Q = 0$ and $W = 0$, the total enthalpy (per unit time) entering equals that leaving. Since each stream carries enthalpy per kg of **dry air** ($h$), not per kg of moist air:

$$\dot{m}_{a1} h_1 + \dot{m}_{a2} h_2 = \dot{m}_{a3} h_3$$

---

### 2.2 Algebraic Solution — Mixture Properties

Solving the three simultaneous equations:

$$\boxed{W_3 = \frac{\dot{m}_{a1} W_1 + \dot{m}_{a2} W_2}{\dot{m}_{a1} + \dot{m}_{a2}}}$$

$$\boxed{h_3 = \frac{\dot{m}_{a1} h_1 + \dot{m}_{a2} h_2}{\dot{m}_{a1} + \dot{m}_{a2}}}$$

These are **mass-weighted harmonic-mean averages** — note the weighted average is taken with respect to **dry-air mass**, not total moist-air mass.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why dry-air mass?** Because $h$ and $W$ in psychrometric tables are universally defined **per kg of dry air** — this convention makes the conservation equations linear and easy to solve.

---

### 2.3 The "Lever Rule" (Inverse Lever / Mixing Ratio)

Rearranging the conservation equations, the **ratio of inlet mass flow rates** can be written as:

$$\boxed{\frac{\dot{m}_{a1}}{\dot{m}_{a2}} = \frac{h_3 - h_2}{h_1 - h_3} = \frac{W_3 - W_2}{W_1 - W_3}}$$

This is the famous **lever rule** — physically, state 3 lies on the line segment 1—2 such that its distance from state 2 (divided by its distance from state 1) equals $\dot{m}_{a1}/\dot{m}_{a2}$.

| Quantity | Symbol | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Mass flow rate of dry air | $\dot{m}_a$ | **kg/s** |
| Specific humidity | $W$ | **kg water vapour / kg dry air** |
| Moist-air enthalpy | $h$ | **kJ/kg dry air** |
| Dry-bulb temperature | $DBT$ or $t$ | **°C** |
| Wet-bulb temperature | $WBT$ | **°C** |
| Dew-point temperature | $DPT$ | **°C** |
| Relative humidity | $\phi$ | **dimensionless (%)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** Of the **6 psychrometric properties** ($t$, $WBT$, $DPT$, $W$, $\phi$, $h$, $v$), only **$W$ and $h$** mix linearly. All other properties ($t$, $\phi$, $v$, $WBT$, $DPT$) of the mixture must be **read off the chart** at the mixture point $(W_3, h_3)$.

### 2.4 Mixture Property Limits (Bounding Cases)

| Case | Condition | Resulting $W_3$ and $h_3$ |
|---|---|---|
| Stream 2 is zero (only stream 1) | $\dot{m}_{a2} \to 0$ | $W_3 = W_1$, $h_3 = h_1$ |
| Stream 1 is zero (only stream 2) | $\dot{m}_{a1} \to 0$ | $W_3 = W_2$, $h_3 = h_2$ |
| Equal flows ($\dot{m}_{a1} = \dot{m}_{a2}$) | Equal mass | $W_3 = \frac{W_1 + W_2}{2}$, $h_3 = \frac{h_1 + h_2}{2}$ |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Role of Adiabatic Mixing |
|---|---|
| **Central AHUs (HVAC)** | Mixing return air with fresh air to meet ventilation codes |
| **Air washer / Spray chambers** | Recirculation mixing for adiabatic evaporative cooling |
| **Industrial drying** | Recirculating partially dried air back into the dryer |
| **Cleanrooms / Hospitals** | Mixing primary and secondary air streams for precise T & RH control |
| **Aircraft ECS** | Mixing bleed air from engine with recirculated cabin air |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering use:** The mixing equations are the **first step** in designing any cooling/heating coil — the coil must handle the **mixed-air condition**, not the outdoor condition alone. This is why mixing-chamber calculation is a compulsory sub-question in KTU board exams.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation of Mixture Properties

We start from the three governing equations established in §2.1:

**Equation A** — Dry-air mass balance: $\dot{m}_{a1} + \dot{m}_{a2} = \dot{m}_{a3}$

**Equation B** — Water-vapour mass balance: $\dot{m}_{a1} W_1 + \dot{m}_{a2} W_2 = \dot{m}_{a3} W_3$

**Equation C** — Enthalpy balance: $\dot{m}_{a1} h_1 + \dot{m}_{a2} h_2 = \dot{m}_{a3} h_3$

---

**Derivation of $W_3$:**

Start with Equation B:

$$\dot{m}_{a1} W_1 + \dot{m}_{a2} W_2 = \dot{m}_{a3} W_3$$

Substitute $\dot{m}_{a3}$ from Equation A:

$$\dot{m}_{a1} W_1 + \dot{m}_{a2} W_2 = (\dot{m}_{a1} + \dot{m}_{a2}) W_3$$

Divide throughout by $(\dot{m}_{a1} + \dot{m}_{a2})$:

$$W_3 = \frac{\dot{m}_{a1} W_1 + \dot{m}_{a2} W_2}{\dot{m}_{a1} + \dot{m}_{a2}}$$

This is the **mass-weighted average** of the two inlet specific humidities. ✔ **Proved.**

---

**Derivation of $h_3$:**

Same procedure applied to Equation C:

$$\dot{m}_{a1} h_1 + \dot{m}_{a2} h_2 = \dot{m}_{a3} h_3$$

Substitute $\dot{m}_{a3}$:

$$\dot{m}_{a1} h_1 + \dot{m}_{a2} h_2 = (\dot{m}_{a1} + \dot{m}_{a2}) h_3$$

Divide throughout by $(\dot{m}_{a1} + \dot{m}_{a2})$:

$$h_3 = \frac{\dot{m}_{a1} h_1 + \dot{m}_{a2} h_2}{\dot{m}_{a1} + \dot{m}_{a2}}$$

This is the **mass-weighted average** of the two inlet enthalpies. ✔ **Proved.**

---

**Derivation of the Lever Rule:**

From Equation A: $\dot{m}_{a3} = \dot{m}_{a1} + \dot{m}_{a2}$. Substituting into B:

$$\dot{m}_{a1} W_1 + \dot{m}_{a2} W_2 = (\dot{m}_{a1} + \dot{m}_{a2}) W_3$$

Expand the right-hand side:

$$\dot{m}_{a1} W_1 + \dot{m}_{a2} W_2 = \dot{m}_{a1} W_3 + \dot{m}_{a2} W_3$$

Group terms containing $\dot{m}_{a1}$ on the left:

$$\dot{m}_{a1} (W_1 - W_3) = \dot{m}_{a2} (W_3 - W_2)$$

Rearrange to obtain the ratio:

$$\frac{\dot{m}_{a1}}{\dot{m}_{a2}} = \frac{W_3 - W_2}{W_1 - W_3}$$

Identical manipulation with enthalpies yields:

$$\frac{\dot{m}_{a1}}{\dot{m}_{a2}} = \frac{h_3 - h_2}{h_1 - h_3}$$

**Both must be equal** — this is the consistency check examiners love to ask. ✔ **Proved.**

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical (KTU Board Style)

> **[Worked Example 1 — KTU Board Pattern, 14-Mark Standard]**

**Problem Statement:**
Two air streams are adiabatically mixed in an AHU mixing chamber.
- **Stream 1 (Return Air):** $\dot{m}_{a1} = 4.0$ kg/s of dry air, $DBT_1 = 26$ °C, $RH_1 = 50\%$
- **Stream 2 (Outside Air):** $\dot{m}_{a2} = 1.0$ kg/s of dry air, $DBT_2 = 38$ °C, $RH_2 = 30\%$

Find: (a) Specific humidity $W_3$ and enthalpy $h_3$ of the mixture. (b) DBT and RH of the mixture. (c) Mass-flow ratio of return air to outside air.

---

**Step 1 — Determine $W_1$ and $h_1$ (Stream 1: Return Air)**

Saturation pressure at 26 °C: $P_{g1} = 3.36$ kPa (from steam tables / Antoine equation).

$$P_{v1} = \phi_1 \cdot P_{g1} = 0.50 \times 3.36 = 1.68 \text{ kPa}$$

$$W_1 = 0.622 \times \frac{P_{v1}}{P_t - P_{v1}} = 0.622 \times \frac{1.68}{101.325 - 1.68}$$

$$W_1 = 0.622 \times \frac{1.68}{99.645} = 0.622 \times 0.01686 = 0.01049 \text{ kg/kg dry air}$$

Enthalpy of Stream 1:

$$h_1 = c_{pa} t_1 + W_1 (2501 + c_{pv} t_1)$$

$$h_1 = 1.005 \times 26 + 0.01049 \times (2501 + 1.88 \times 26)$$

$$h_1 = 26.13 + 0.01049 \times (2501 + 48.88)$$

$$h_1 = 26.13 + 0.01049 \times 2549.88$$

$$h_1 = 26.13 + 26.75 = 52.88 \text{ kJ/kg dry air}$$

---

**Step 2 — Determine $W_2$ and $h_2$ (Stream 2: Outside Air)**

Saturation pressure at 38 °C: $P_{g2} = 6.625$ kPa.

$$P_{v2} = \phi_2 \cdot P_{g2} = 0.30 \times 6.625 = 1.9875 \text{ kPa}$$

$$W_2 = 0.622 \times \frac{1.9875}{101.325 - 1.9875} = 0.622 \times \frac{1.9875}{99.3375}$$

$$W_2 = 0.622 \times 0.02001 = 0.01244 \text{ kg/kg dry air}$$

Enthalpy of Stream 2:

$$h_2 = 1.005 \times 38 + 0.01244 \times (2501 + 1.88 \times 38)$$

$$h_2 = 38.19 + 0.01244 \times (2501 + 71.44)$$

$$h_2 = 38.19 + 0.01244 \times 2572.44$$

$$h_2 = 38.19 + 32.00 = 70.19 \text{ kJ/kg dry air}$$

---

**Step 3 — Compute Mixture Properties $W_3$ and $h_3$**

$$W_3 = \frac{\dot{m}_{a1} W_1 + \dot{m}_{a2} W_2}{\dot{m}_{a1} + \dot{m}_{a2}}$$

$$W_3 = \frac{4.0 \times 0.01049 + 1.0 \times 0.01244}{4.0 + 1.0}$$

$$W_3 = \frac{0.04196 + 0.01244}{5.0} = \frac{0.05440}{5.0} = 0.01088 \text{ kg/kg dry air}$$

$$h_3 = \frac{\dot{m}_{a1} h_1 + \dot{m}_{a2} h_2}{\dot{m}_{a1} + \dot{m}_{a2}}$$

$$h_3 = \frac{4.0 \times 52.88 + 1.0 \times 70.19}{5.0} = \frac{211.52 + 70.19}{5.0}$$

$$h_3 = \frac{281.71}{5.0} = 56.34 \text{ kJ/kg dry air}$$

---

**Step 4 — Determine DBT$_3$ and RH$_3$**

The enthalpy formula rearranged for DBT:

$$h_3 = c_{pa} \cdot t_3 + W_3 (h_{fg,0} + c_{pv} \cdot t_3)$$

$$56.34 = 1.005 \cdot t_3 + 0.01088 \times (2501 + 1.88 \cdot t_3)$$

$$56.34 = 1.005 t_3 + 27.21 + 0.02045 \cdot t_3$$

$$56.34 - 27.21 = (1.005 + 0.02045) t_3$$

$$29.13 = 1.02545 \cdot t_3$$

$$t_3 = \frac{29.13}{1.02545} = 28.41 \text{ °C}$$

Saturation pressure at 28.41 °C: $P_{g3} \approx 3.876$ kPa.

$$P_{v3} = \frac{W_3 \cdot P_t}{0.622 + W_3} = \frac{0.01088 \times 101.325}{0.622 + 0.01088}$$

$$P_{v3} = \frac{1.1024}{0.63288} = 1.7419 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\phi_3 = \frac{P_{v3}}{P_{g3}} = \frac{1.7419}{3.876} = 0.4494 = 44.94\%$$

---

**Step 5 — Verify Mass-Flow Ratio Using the Lever Rule**

$$\frac{\dot{m}_{a1}}{\dot{m}_{a2}} = \frac{h_3 - h_2}{h_1 - h_3} = \frac{56.34 - 70.19}{52.88 - 56.34} = \frac{-13.85}{-3.46} = 4.00$$

$$\text{Check with } W:\quad \frac{W_3 - W_2}{W_1 - W_3} = \frac{0.01088 - 0.01244}{0.01049 - 0.01088} = \frac{-0.00156}{-0.00039} = 4.00$$

✔ **Both equal 4.00**, perfectly consistent with $\dot{m}_{a1}/\dot{m}_{a2} = 4/1$.

---

**Final Answers Table**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $W_3$ | **0.01088 kg/kg dry air** |
| $h_3$ | **56.34 kJ/kg dry air** |
| DBT$_3$ | **28.41 °C** |
| RH$_3$ | **44.94 %** |
| Mass-flow ratio $\dot{m}_{a1}/\dot{m}_{a2}$ | **4.00** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Physical Setup — Adiabatic Mixing Chamber

Below is a functional schematic showing the inlets, outlet, and the key psychrometric state points. In a typical KTU drawing question, you would render this as a labelled box with two arrows in and one arrow out, marked with state points 1, 2, and 3.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["STREAM 1 RETURN AIR m_a1, W1, h1"] --> MIX["ADIBATIC MIXING CHAMBER Q=0, Insulated Walls, Steady State"]
    A2["STREAM 2 OUTSIDE AIR m_a2, W2, h2"] --> MIX
    MIX --> A3["MIXED AIR STREAM 3 m_a3, W3, h3 to Cooling or Heating Coil"]
```

### 4.2 Psychrometric Chart Representation (Lever Rule Visual)

The mixture state always lies on the straight line joining states 1 and 2. Below is a schematic of the *W*–*h* chart with the lever rule shown.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["STATE 1 RA Warm Humid Low enthalpy Low W"] --- P3["STATE 3 MIXTURE Midpoint on line 1-2 Higher h than 1 Higher W than 2"]
    P2["STATE 2 OA Hot Less humid High enthalpy Low W"] --- P3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The straight line 1 → 3 → 2 on the *W*–*h* chart is the locus of all possible mixtures. The exact location of point 3 is set by the mass-flow ratio via the lever rule. The closer point 3 sits to point 1, the larger $\dot{m}_{a1}$ is relative to $\dot{m}_{a2}$.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Detailed Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RA["RETURN AIR DUCT State 1: t1, W1, h1, phi1"] -->|m_a1| VALVE["MIXING DAMPER Adjustable Louver"]
    OA["FRESH AIR INTAKE State 2: t2, W2, h2, phi2"] -->|m_a2| VALVE
    VALVE --> CHAMBER["MIXING CHAMBER Insulated No heat gain or loss Steady State"]
    CHAMBER -->|m_a3| COIL["COOLING COIL OR HEATING COIL Downstream Process"]
    COIL --> SUPPLY["SUPPLY AIR State 4: Conditioned for Room"]

    CHAMBER -.->|"Mass Balance: m_a1 + m_a2 = m_a3"| MB["BALANCE 1"]
    CHAMBER -.->|"Moisture Balance: m_a1 W1 + m_a2 W2 = m_a3 W3"| MB2["BALANCE 2"]
    CHAMBER -.->|"Energy Balance: m_a1 h1 + m_a2 h2 = m_a3 h3"| MB3["BALANCE 3"]
```

### 4.4 Decision Matrix — Which Property Mixes Linearly?

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q{"Which property of two streams is being mixed?"}
    Q -->|"Specific Humidity W"| YES1["LINEAR MASS WEIGHTED AVERAGE Direct Equation"]
    Q -->|"Enthalpy h"| YES2["LINEAR MASS WEIGHTED AVERAGE Direct Equation"]
    Q -->|"DBT, WBT, DPT, RH, Specific Volume"| NO["NOT LINEAR Read off Psychrometric Chart at Mixture Point W3, h3"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3, 3 Marks]**

*State the assumptions made during the analysis of adiabatic mixing of two air streams.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The following three fundamental assumptions are made:

1. **Steady-state, steady-flow process** — mass flow rates and properties do not vary with time.
2. **Perfectly insulated mixing chamber** — no heat transfer between the chamber and surroundings ($Q = 0$).
3. **No work transfer** — no shaft work or electrical work crosses the system boundary ($W = 0$). Only enthalpy is carried in and out by the moving air streams.

*(Examiner allocates 1 mark per assumption — write all three in a numbered list for full credit.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3, 3 Marks]**

*Why does only specific humidity ($W$) and enthalpy ($h$) mix linearly, while dry-bulb temperature ($DBT$) and relative humidity ($RH$) do not mix linearly during adiabatic mixing of two air streams?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

$W$ and $h$ mix linearly because they are **conserved quantities per unit mass of dry air** during the mixing process — they are governed directly by the conservation of mass (for $W$) and conservation of energy (for $h$), which are linear algebraic equations. In contrast, $DBT$ and $RH$ are **derived (non-linear) properties** that depend on the partial pressure of water vapour in a non-linear relationship (e.g., $W = 0.622 \cdot P_v / (P_t - P_v)$ and $\phi = P_v / P_g$). Hence, the DBT and RH of the mixture cannot be obtained by simple averaging — they must be **read off the psychrometric chart** at the mixture point $(W_3, h_3)$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]*

**(a)** Derive the expressions for specific humidity $W_3$ and enthalpy $h_3$ of the resultant mixture when two air streams are adiabatically mixed. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In an air-conditioning system, 100 m³/min of air at 40 °C DBT and 25 °C WBT is adiabatically mixed with 50 m³/min of air at 30 °C DBT and 18 °C WBT. Find the DBT, WBT, specific humidity, and enthalpy of the mixture. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A(a):**

The three governing conservation equations for the mixing chamber (Stream 1, Stream 2 → Stream 3) are:

$$\dot{m}_{a1} + \dot{m}_{a2} = \dot{m}_{a3} \quad \text{(dry-air mass balance)}$$

$$\dot{m}_{a1} W_1 + \dot{m}_{a2} W_2 = \dot{m}_{a3} W_3 \quad \text{(moisture balance)}$$

$$\dot{m}_{a1} h_1 + \dot{m}_{a2} h_2 = \dot{m}_{a3} h_3 \quad \text{(energy balance)}$$

Substituting $\dot{m}_{a3} = \dot{m}_{a1} + \dot{m}_{a2}$ from the first equation into the second and third:

$$W_3 = \frac{\dot{m}_{a1} W_1 + \dot{m}_{a2} W_2}{\dot{m}_{a1} + \dot{m}_{a2}}$$

$$h_3 = \frac{\dot{m}_{a1} h_1 + \dot{m}_{a2} h_2}{\dot{m}_{a1} + \dot{m}_{a2}}$$

**Assumptions (write all three for full 7 marks):**

1. The mixing chamber is perfectly insulated → $Q = 0$.
2. No work interaction with surroundings → $W = 0$.
3. Steady-state, steady-flow process; properties uniform at each inlet and outlet.

---

**Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Convert volumetric flows to dry-air mass flow rates using specific volume.**

Assume standard atmospheric pressure $P_t = 101.325$ kPa. From the psychrometric chart / steam tables:

- **Stream 1** ($40$ °C DBT, $25$ °C WBT) → $W_1 \approx 0.0130$ kg/kg d.a., $h_1 \approx 71.5$ kJ/kg d.a., $v_1 \approx 0.890$ m³/kg d.a.
- **Stream 2** ($30$ °C DBT, $18$ °C WBT) → $W_2 \approx 0.0100$ kg/kg d.a., $h_2 \approx 53.1$ kJ/kg d.a., $v_2 \approx 0.870$ m³/kg d.a.

Mass flow rates (dry air):

$$\dot{m}_{a1} = \frac{\dot{V}_1}{v_1} = \frac{100}{60 \times 0.890} = 1.873 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\dot{m}_{a2} = \frac{\dot{V}_2}{v_2} = \frac{50}{60 \times 0.870} = 0.958 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute mixture properties.**

$$W_3 = \frac{1.873 \times 0.0130 + 0.958 \times 0.0100}{1.873 + 0.958} = \frac{0.02435 + 0.00958}{2.831} = 0.01197 \text{ kg/kg d.a.}$$

$$h_3 = \frac{1.873 \times 71.5 + 0.958 \times 53.1}{2.831} = \frac{133.92 + 50.87}{2.831} = 65.15 \text{ kJ/kg d.a.}$$

**Step 3 — Find DBT and WBT from chart.**

With $W_3 = 0.01197$ and $h_3 = 65.15$, reading the psychrometric chart:

- **DBT$_3 \approx 36.7$ °C**
- **WBT$_3 \approx 22.8$ °C**

**Final Results:**

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| $W_3$ | **0.01197 kg/kg d.a.** |
| $h_3$ | **65.15 kJ/kg d.a.** |
| DBT$_3$ | **≈ 36.7 °C** |
| WBT$_3$ | **≈ 22.8 °C** |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]*

**(a)** Two air streams are adiabatically mixed. State and prove the "lever rule" relating the inlet mass-flow ratio to the mixture properties. Show that the same ratio is obtained whether $W$ or $h$ is used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A return-air stream of 0.20 kg/s dry air at 28 °C DBT and 60 % RH is mixed adiabatically with 0.05 kg/s of outside air at 36 °C DBT and 35 % RH. Determine the specific humidity, enthalpy, dry-bulb temperature, and relative humidity of the mixture. Use the lever rule to confirm the mass-flow ratio. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.B(a):**

Starting from the conservation equations (proved in §3.1):

$$\dot{m}_{a1} W_1 + \dot{m}_{a2} W_2 = (\dot{m}_{a1} + \dot{m}_{a2}) W_3$$

Expanding and rearranging:

$$\dot{m}_{a1}(W_1 - W_3) = \dot{m}_{a2}(W_3 - W_2)$$

$$\therefore \frac{\dot{m}_{a1}}{\dot{m}_{a2}} = \frac{W_3 - W_2}{W_1 - W_3}$$

Identical derivation with $h$ in place of $W$ gives:

$$\frac{\dot{m}_{a1}}{\dot{m}_{a2}} = \frac{h_3 - h_2}{h_1 - h_3}$$

Since both ratios equal $\dot{m}_{a1}/\dot{m}_{a2}$, they must equal each other:

$$\frac{W_3 - W_2}{W_1 - W_3} = \frac{h_3 - h_2}{h_1 - h_3}$$

This is the **Lever Rule** (or Inverse Lever Rule) — physically, state 3 partitions the segment 1–2 in inverse proportion to the inlet mass flow rates.

---

**Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Stream 1 (Return):** $t_1 = 28$ °C, $\phi_1 = 0.60$

$P_{g1}$ at 28 °C $\approx 3.778$ kPa; $P_{v1} = 0.60 \times 3.778 = 2.267$ kPa.

$$W_1 = 0.622 \times \frac{2.267}{101.325 - 2.267} = 0.622 \times 0.02290 = 0.01424 \text{ kg/kg d.a.}$$

$$h_1 = 1.005 \times 28 + 0.01424 \times (2501 + 1.88 \times 28) = 28.14 + 36.37 = 64.51 \text{ kJ/kg d.a.}$$

**Stream 2 (Outside):** $t_2 = 36$ °C, $\phi_2 = 0.35$

$P_{g2}$ at 36 °C $\approx 5.943$ kPa; $P_{v2} = 0.35 \times 5.943 = 2.080$ kPa.

$$W_2 = 0.622 \times \frac{2.080}{101.325 - 2.080} = 0.622 \times 0.02095 = 0.01303 \text{ kg/kg d.a.}$$

$$h_2 = 1.005 \times 36 + 0.01303 \times (2501 + 1.88 \times 36) = 36.18 + 33.46 = 69.64 \text{ kJ/kg d.a.}$$

**Mixture:**

$$W_3 = \frac{0.20 \times 0.01424 + 0.05 \times 0.01303}{0.20 + 0.05} = \frac{0.002848 + 0.000652}{0.25} = 0.01400 \text{ kg/kg d.a.}$$

$$h_3 = \frac{0.20 \times 64.51 + 0.05 \times 69.64}{0.25} = \frac{12.902 + 3.482}{0.25} = 65.54 \text{ kJ/kg d.a.}$$

**DBT$_3$** from $h_3 = 1.005 t_3 + W_3(2501 + 1.88 t_3)$:

$$65.54 = 1.005 t_3 + 0.01400 (2501 + 1.88 t_3) = 1.005 t_3 + 35.014 + 0.02632 t_3$$

$$t_3 = \frac{65.54 - 35.014}{1.03132} = \frac{30.526}{1.03132} = 29.60 \text{ °C}$$

$$P_{v3} = \frac{W_3 \cdot P_t}{0.622 + W_3} = \frac{0.01400 \times 101.325}{0.622 + 0.01400} = \frac{1.4186}{0.6360} = 2.2305 \text{ kPa}$$

$P_{g3}$ at 29.60 °C $\approx 4.140$ kPa.

$$\phi_3 = \frac{2.2305}{4.140} = 0.5388 \approx 53.88\%$$

**Lever-rule verification:**

$$\frac{\dot{m}_{a1}}{\dot{m}_{a2}} = \frac{W_3 - W_2}{W_1 - W_3} = \frac{0.01400 - 0.01303}{0.01424 - 0.01400} = \frac{0.00097}{0.00024} = 4.04$$

$$\frac{\dot{m}_{a1}}{\dot{m}_{a2}} = \frac{h_3 - h_2}{h_1 - h_3} = \frac{65.54 - 69.64}{64.51 - 65.54} = \frac{-4.10}{-1.03} = 3.98$$

Small difference is due to rounding — both equal **≈ 4.00**, matching $\dot{m}_{a1}/\dot{m}_{a2} = 0.20/0.05 = 4.00$. ✔

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in mixing problems:**
>
> 1. **Mixing DBT or RH directly by arithmetic average.** This is the **#1 mistake**. Always mix $W$ and $h$ first, then read DBT/RH from the chart.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert volumetric flow to mass flow** when the problem gives m³/min instead of kg/s — divide by specific volume $v$.
> 3. **Using total mass flow instead of dry-air mass flow.** In psychrometrics, all formulas are per kg of **dry air**. Using $\dot{m}_{total}$ instead of $\dot{m}_a$ gives wrong answers.
> 4. **Mixing wet-bulb or dew-point temperature** linearly — these do **not** mix linearly.
> 5. **Not verifying the lever rule** — examiners often give 1 mark for the consistency check.
> 6. **Reading $W_1$ and $h_1$ from wrong points** — always use the same property definition throughout (e.g., $h = c_{pa} t + W(h_{fg,0} + c_{pv} t)$, NOT per kg moist air).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] Adiabatic mixing assumes **$Q = 0$** (insulated) and **$W_{sh} = 0$** (no shaft work).
- [x] Three conservation laws: **dry-air mass**, **water-vapour mass**, **enthalpy (energy)**.
- [x] Mixture $W_3$ and $h_3$ are **mass-weighted averages** of inlet values (with respect to dry-air mass).
- [x] Mixture DBT, WBT, DPT, RH, and $v$ are **NOT** arithmetic averages — read off the **psychrometric chart** at $(W_3, h_3)$.
- [x] Mixture point lies on the **straight line** joining inlet states on the $W$–$h$ chart.
- [x] **Lever rule:** $\dfrac{\dot{m}_{a1}}{\dot{m}_{a2}} = \dfrac{W_3 - W_2}{W_1 - W_3} = \dfrac{h_3 - h_2}{h_1 - h_3}$.
- [x] Always convert volumetric flows to dry-air mass flows using $v$ from the chart before mixing.
- [x] The closer the mixture point is to inlet 1, the **larger** $\dot{m}_{a1}$ is (inverse-distance rule).
- [x] In real AHUs, adiabatic mixing is the **first step** before the cooling/heating coil.
- [x] When $\dot{m}_{a1} = \dot{m}_{a2}$, simple arithmetic mean: $W_3 = (W_1 + W_2)/2$, $h_3 = (h_1 + h_2)/2$.
- [x] Three constants to memorize: $c_{pa} = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $c_{pv} = 1.88$ kJ/kg·K, $h_{fg,0} = 2501$ kJ/kg.
- [x] Lever rule verification is a **free 1-mark consistency check** — always show it in 14-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensible Heat Factor (SHF)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sensible Heat Factor (SHF)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensible Heat Factor (SHF)** is defined as the ratio of the **Sensible Heat (SH)** to the **Total Heat (TH)** of the air supplied to the conditioned space. Total heat is the arithmetic sum of Sensible Heat and Latent Heat (LH).

Mathematically expressed as:

$$\text{SHF} = \frac{\text{Sensible Heat (SH)}}{\text{Total Heat (SH + LH)}}$$

The SHF is a **dimensionless quantity** (no units), and it represents the fraction of the total cooling load that is attributed to temperature (dry-bulb) change as opposed to moisture (humidity) change.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (The Shower Analogy):**
> Imagine taking a hot shower in a closed bathroom. The air feels "hot and stuffy" — there are two discomfort sources:
> 1. **Sensible heat** (the "hot" part) — the air temperature is too high.
> 2. **Latent heat** (the "humid" part) — the moisture content is too high.
>
> When the AC cools this air, it must remove **both** the "hotness" (sensible) and the "moistness" (latent). **SHF simply asks: out of all the heat being removed, what fraction is just the "hot" component?**
>
> - If SHF = 1.0 → Only dry heat is removed (a desert climate cooling).
> - If SHF = 0.5 → Equal amounts of dry and wet heat are removed (typical humid tropical climate).
> - If SHF = 0.0 → Only moisture is removed (dehumidification only).

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The standard psychrometric constants used in SHF calculations are:

| Parameter | IP Units | SI Units |
|---|---|---|
| Air Density (ρ) | **0.075 lb/ft³** | **1.20 kg/m³** |
| Specific Heat (cₚ) | **0.240 BTU/lb·°F** | **1.005 kJ/kg·K** |
| Latent Heat (h_fg) | **1061 BTU/lb** | **2501 kJ/kg** |
| Std. Air Volume | **1 ft³/lb** | **0.834 m³/kg** |

### Why SHF is Important in KTU Curriculum

> [!IMPORTANT]
> SHF is the **single most important design parameter** for selecting the cooling coil in an air-handling unit (AHU). The intersection of the SHF line with the saturation curve of a psychrometric chart gives the **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)** — the temperature to which the air must be theoretically cooled to satisfy the given load.

### Variants of SHF Covered in KTU Module 3

1. **RSHF (Room Sensible Heat Factor):** Based purely on room loads.
2. **GSHF (Grand Sensible Heat Factor):** Considers the heat added by the apparatus (fan motor, return air).
3. **ESHF (Effective Sensible Heat Factor):** Considers only room SH over total load including ventilation latent heat.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Theoretical Breakdown of the Sensible Heat Factor Concept

The cooling process of air in a conditioned space is fundamentally a two-dimensional operation on the psychrometric chart:

- **Sensible cooling** → moves the air state horizontally to the **left** (temperature decreases, humidity ratio constant).
- **Latent cooling / Dehumidification** → moves the air state along a line of constant wet-bulb temperature downwards (moisture is condensed out).

The total cooling process follows a straight line from the **Room Condition (R)** to the **Supply Condition (S)** on the psychrometric chart. The slope of this line is governed by SHF.

### Step-by-Step Logic of SHF Derivation

1. **Step 1 — Identify Sensible Heat (SH):** This is the heat that causes a measurable change in dry-bulb temperature, calculated as:
   $$SH = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_1 - T_2) = 1.21 \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta t \quad \text{(SI: kW)}$$

2. **Step 2 — Identify Latent Heat (LH):** This is the heat associated with moisture removal (condensation), calculated as:
   $$LH = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg} \cdot (W_1 - W_2) = 3010 \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta W \quad \text{(SI: kW)}$$

3. **Step 3 — Compute Total Heat (TH):** The total cooling load is the sum:
   $$TH = SH + LH$$

4. **Step 4 — Form the Ratio:**
   $$\text{SHF} = \frac{SH}{SH + LH} = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{LH}{SH}}$$

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HVAC Coil Selection:** Engineers trace the SHF line on a psychrometric chart from the room state to the saturation curve to find the ADP. This ADP dictates the chilled water temperature required.
- **Capacity Estimation:** A typical residential AC in Kerala has an SHF between **0.65 and 0.80**, while a desert AC in Rajasthan has SHF > **0.90**.
- **Energy Auditing:** SHF helps identify whether an existing system is over-dehumidifying or under-cooling.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Table — All SHF Formulas for KTU Board Exam**

| # | Formula | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SHF (Basic) | $\text{SHF} = \frac{SH}{SH + LH}$ | dimensionless | Definition |
| 2 | SHF (SI form) | $\text{SHF} = \frac{1.21 \cdot \Delta t}{1.21 \cdot \Delta t + 3010 \cdot \Delta W}$ | dimensionless | Direct from Δt, ΔW |
| 3 | SH (SI) | $SH = 1.21 \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta t$ | kW | Sensible heat load |
| 4 | LH (SI) | $LH = 3010 \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta W$ | kW | Latent heat load |
| 5 | SH (IP) | $SH = 1.08 \cdot \text{CFM} \cdot \Delta t$ | BTU/hr | Sensible heat (English) |
| 6 | LH (IP) | $LH = 0.68 \cdot \text{CFM} \cdot \Delta W_{gr}$ | BTU/hr | Latent heat (English) |
| 7 | TH (IP) | $TH = 4.5 \cdot \text{CFM} \cdot \Delta h$ | BTU/hr | Total heat (English) |
| 8 | RSHF | $\text{RSHF} = \frac{RSH}{RSH + RLH}$ | dimensionless | Room only |
| 9 | GSHF | $\text{GSHF} = \frac{RSH + ASH}{RSH + ASH + RLH + ALH}$ | dimensionless | With apparatus heat |
| 10 | ESHF | $\text{ESHF} = \frac{RSH}{RSH + RLH + ALH + VentLH}$ | dimensionless | Effective for coil |
| 11 | ADP from SHF | $\text{ADP from chart: trace line from R with slope} = \frac{1 - \text{SHF}}{\text{SHF}}$ | °C | Coil selection |
| 12 | Capacity (TR) | $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW} = 12000 \text{ BTU/hr}$ | conversion | Unit conversion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Exam Note:** In all SI calculations, the moisture difference **ΔW** must be in **kg of water per kg of dry air (kg/kg)**, NOT in grams. A common student error is plugging in g/kg directly, which gives a wrong answer by a factor of 1000.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: SHF in Terms of Air Properties (SI Units)

**Given:** A stream of air at condition 1 (T₁, W₁) is conditioned to condition 2 (T₂, W₂).

**Step 1:** The mass flow rate of dry air is:
$$\dot{m}_a = \rho \cdot \dot{V} = 1.20 \cdot \dot{V} \quad \text{(kg/s)}$$

**Step 2:** Sensible heat is the heat associated with the temperature change:
$$SH = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_p \cdot (T_1 - T_2) = 1.20 \cdot \dot{V} \cdot 1.005 \cdot \Delta t = 1.21 \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 3:** Latent heat is the heat associated with the moisture removal:
$$LH = \dot{m}_a \cdot h_{fg} \cdot (W_1 - W_2) = 1.20 \cdot \dot{V} \cdot 2501 \cdot \Delta W = 3001.2 \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta W \approx 3010 \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta W$$

**Step 4:** Total heat is the sum:
$$TH = SH + LH = 1.21 \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta t + 3010 \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta W$$

**Step 5:** Taking the ratio:
$$\text{SHF} = \frac{SH}{TH} = \frac{1.21 \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta t}{1.21 \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta t + 3010 \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta W}$$

**Step 6:** The volumetric flow $\dot{V}$ cancels out (this is why SHF is independent of air quantity):
$$\boxed{\text{SHF} = \frac{1.21 \cdot \Delta t}{1.21 \cdot \Delta t + 3010 \cdot \Delta W}}$$

---

### Derivation 2: Relationship Between SHF, Bypass Factor (BF), and ADP

**Given:** A cooling coil with Bypass Factor **BF** processes air from Room condition (R) to Supply condition (S). The Apparatus Dew Point is at **ADP**.

**Step 1:** The dry-bulb temperature of supply air is:
$$T_s = T_{ADP} + BF \cdot (T_R - T_{ADP})$$

**Step 2:** The humidity ratio of supply air is:
$$W_s = W_{ADP} + BF \cdot (W_R - W_{ADP})$$

**Step 3:** On the saturation curve, $T_{ADP} = T_{sat}(W_{ADP})$ approximately, but more precisely, on the psychrometric chart, the SHF line is drawn from the room state.

**Step 4:** For a numerical verification, take a typical case:
- Room: T_R = 30 °C, W_R = 0.015 kg/kg
- ADP = 10 °C, BF = 0.2

**Step 5:** Calculate supply conditions:
$$T_s = 10 + 0.2 \cdot (30 - 10) = 10 + 4 = 14 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 6:** For the humidity ratio at ADP, use the saturation curve approximation. At 10 °C, $W_{ADP} \approx 0.0075$ kg/kg:
$$W_s = 0.0075 + 0.2 \cdot (0.015 - 0.0075) = 0.0075 + 0.0015 = 0.009 \text{ kg/kg}$$

**Step 7:** Compute the temperature and humidity differences:
$$\Delta t = 30 - 14 = 16 \text{ °C}, \quad \Delta W = 0.015 - 0.009 = 0.006 \text{ kg/kg}$$

**Step 8:** Substitute into the SHF formula:
$$\text{SHF} = \frac{1.21 \cdot 16}{1.21 \cdot 16 + 3010 \cdot 0.006} = \frac{19.36}{19.36 + 18.06} = \frac{19.36}{37.42}$$

$$\boxed{\text{SHF} = 0.517 \approx 0.52}$$

---

### Derivation 3: SHF in English (IP) Units

**Step 1:** Sensible heat in BTU/hr:
$$SH = 0.018 \cdot \text{CFM} \cdot \Delta t \quad \text{(for one minute basis)} \Rightarrow SH = 1.08 \cdot \text{CFM} \cdot \Delta t \quad \text{(BTU/hr)}$$

**Step 2:** Latent heat in BTU/hr:
