$$LH = 0.68 \cdot \text{CFM} \cdot \Delta W_{gr} \quad \text{(BTU/hr)}$$

where $\Delta W_{gr}$ is in **grains per pound of dry air** (1 lb = 7000 grains).

**Step 3:** Conversion to SI consistency check:
$$1.08 \frac{\text{BTU}}{\text{hr} \cdot \text{CFM} \cdot °F} = 1.21 \frac{\text{kW}}{\text{m}^3/\text{s} \cdot \text{K}}$$

Both yield the same SHF value when applied with proper unit conversions.

---

### Analytical Matrix: Comparing SHF for Different Climates

| Climate Type | Room T (°C) | Δt (°C) | ΔW (kg/kg) | SHF | AC Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hot & Dry (Rajasthan) | 42 | 18 | 0.004 | **0.64** | Desert AC |
| Hot & Humid (Kerala) | 32 | 10 | 0.008 | **0.34** | Tropical AC |
| Moderate (Bangalore) | 28 | 12 | 0.005 | **0.49** | Standard AC |
| Cold & Dry (Shimla) | 20 | 14 | 0.002 | **0.74** | Heat-pump mode |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Higher humidity (larger ΔW) **decreases** SHF. This is why coastal Kerala buildings need a more "dehumidifying" AC system with lower SHF.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: SHF Process Line on Psychrometric Chart (Block Flow Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["ROOM CONDITION R: T_R, W_R"] --> B["SHF PROCESS LINE: SLOPE = 1-SHF / SHF"]
    B --> C["SUPPLY CONDITION S: T_S, W_S"]
    B --> D["APPARATUS DEW POINT ADP: T_ADP, W_ADP"]
    B --> E["SATURATION CURVE: 100% RH"]
    D --> E
    F["SENSIBLE HEAT: HORIZONTAL PROJECTION"] --> A
    G["LATENT HEAT: VERTICAL PROJECTION"] --> A
    H["TOTAL HEAT: SUM"] --> F
    H --> G
```

### Diagram 2: SHF, BF, and ADP — Engineering Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    start["START: Given Room Load SH and LH"] --> calcSHF["Step 1: Calculate SHF = SH / SH+LH"]
    calcSHF --> plotSHF["Step 2: Plot SHF line on Psychrometric Chart from R"]
    plotSHF --> findADP["Step 3: Find intersection with saturation curve = ADP"]
    findADP --> findBF["Step 4: From manufacturer data, find coil BF at ADP"]
    findBF --> findTS["Step 5: Calculate T_S using BF and T_R, T_ADP"]
    findTS --> findCFM["Step 6: Calculate required CFM from SH load"]
    findCFM --> end["END: Select cooling coil and fan"]
```

### Diagram 3: Classification of Sensible Heat Factors

```mermaid
graph TD
    SHF["SENSIBLE HEAT FACTOR (SHF)"] --> RSHF["RSHF: Room Sensible Heat Factor"]
    SHF --> GSHF["GSHF: Grand Sensible Heat Factor"]
    SHF --> ESHF["ESHF: Effective Sensible Heat Factor"]
    RSHF --> RSHFdef["RSHF = RSH / RSH + RLH (only room load)"]
    GSHF --> GSHFdef["GSHF = RSH+ASH / Total Heat (includes fan heat)"]
    ESHF --> ESHFdef["ESHF = RSH / RSH + RLH + ALH + VentLH (for coil design)"]
```

### Diagram 4: Air Conditioning Process Flow (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    OA["OUTSIDE AIR: Hot and Humid"] --> MIX["MIXING CHAMBER"]
    RA["RETURN AIR: From Room"] --> MIX
    MIX --> FILTER["FILTER SECTION"]
    FILTER --> COIL["COOLING COIL: SHF line from R to S"]
    COIL --> FAN["SUPPLY FAN"]
    FAN --> ROOM["CONDITIONED ROOM: Maintains at T_R, W_R"]
    ROOM --> RA
    COIL -.-> ADP["ADP: From intersection of SHF line and saturation curve"]
    ADP -.-> CHW["CHILLED WATER: At temperature below ADP"]
```

### Diagram 5: SHF Calculation Schematic (Numerical Process)

```mermaid
graph LR
    Input["INPUT: Δt, ΔW, V_dot"] --> SHcalc["Compute SH = 1.21 × V_dot × Δt"]
    Input --> LHcalc["Compute LH = 3010 × V_dot × ΔW"]
    SHcalc --> Ratio["Ratio: SH / SH + LH"]
    LHcalc --> Ratio
    Ratio --> Output["OUTPUT: SHF (dimensionless, 0 to 1)"]
    Output --> Chart["PLOT on Psychrometric Chart"]
    Chart --> Coil["SELECT COIL ADP"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**  
Define Sensible Heat Factor (SHF). Explain its significance in the design of an air-conditioning system.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark) Definition:** SHF is the ratio of the sensible heat to the total heat (sensible + latent) of the air supplied to a conditioned space: $\text{SHF} = \frac{SH}{SH + LH}$.
- **(1 mark) Significance in Coil Selection:** SHF determines the slope of the process line on the psychrometric chart. The line drawn from the room condition with this slope intersects the saturation curve to give the **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)**, which is the temperature the air must be theoretically cooled to.
- **(1 mark) Practical Importance:** A higher SHF indicates drier climate cooling (less dehumidification), while a lower SHF (typical of Kerala's tropical climate) requires a coil with greater latent heat removal capacity. The ADP guides the chilled water temperature selection.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
Differentiate between **RSHF** and **GSHF**. Why is ESHF used for cooling coil design instead of RSHF?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark) RSHF:** Room Sensible Heat Factor uses only the room's sensible and latent heat: $\text{RSHF} = \frac{RSH}{RSH + RLH}$.
- **(1 mark) GSHF:** Grand Sensible Heat Factor includes the apparatus sensible heat (e.g., fan motor heat) in the numerator: $\text{GSHF} = \frac{RSH + ASH}{RSH + ASH + RLH + ALH}$.
- **(1 mark) ESHF Justification:** **ESHF (Effective Sensible Heat Factor)** is used for cooling coil design because it considers only the **room sensible heat** in the numerator but includes all latent heat sources (room, apparatus, and ventilation) in the denominator. This gives the **true** proportion of the coil's load that is sensible, which dictates the coil's surface temperature and fin spacing.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, Part B questions carry 14 marks with **internal choice**. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** A conference hall has a sensible heat load of **40 kW** and a latent heat load of **10 kW**. The outside air conditions are **35 °C DBT and 60% RH**, and the air is to be supplied at **20 °C DBT and 50% RH**. Calculate:
1. Sensible Heat Factor (SHF) of the system
2. Apparatus Dew Point (ADP) from the psychrometric chart
3. Bypass Factor (BF) of the cooling coil

**(b)** If the volume flow rate of air is **8 m³/s**, determine the supply air condition and the total cooling capacity in **TR (Tons of Refrigeration)**.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1:** Given data:
- SH = 40 kW, LH = 10 kW
- Outside: T₁ = 35 °C, RH₁ = 60%
- Supply: T₂ = 20 °C, RH₂ = 50%

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Calculate SHF:
$$\text{SHF} = \frac{SH}{SH + LH} = \frac{40}{40 + 10} = \frac{40}{50} = 0.80$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Find humidity ratios from the psychrometric chart:
- At 35 °C, 60% RH: W₁ ≈ 0.0210 kg/kg
- At 20 °C, 50% RH: W₂ ≈ 0.0073 kg/kg
- Therefore, ΔW = 0.0210 − 0.0073 = 0.0137 kg/kg
- Δt = 35 − 20 = 15 °C

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Find ADP by tracing the SHF line:
The SHF line is drawn from the room state (35 °C, 0.0210) on the psychrometric chart with a slope of:
$$\text{Slope} = \frac{1 - \text{SHF}}{\text{SHF}} = \frac{1 - 0.80}{0.80} = \frac{0.20}{0.80} = 0.25$$

The intersection of this SHF line with the saturation curve gives:
$$T_{ADP} \approx 12.5 \text{ °C}, \quad W_{ADP} \approx 0.0090 \text{ kg/kg}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Calculate Bypass Factor:
$$BF = \frac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}} = \frac{20 - 12.5}{35 - 12.5} = \frac{7.5}{22.5} = 0.333$$

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Calculate the total heat (TH) and supply air condition:
$$TH = SH + LH = 40 + 10 = 50 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Calculate the air mass flow rate:
$$\dot{m}_a = 1.20 \cdot \dot{V} = 1.20 \cdot 8 = 9.6 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Verify the supply condition:
$$SH = 1.21 \cdot 8 \cdot (35 - 20) = 1.21 \cdot 8 \cdot 15 = 145.2 \text{ kW} \quad \text{(This is the SH capacity of the system, not the load — verify with load)}$$

The supply air condition is already given as **20 °C DBT, 50% RH**. The air picks up heat from the room to reach the room condition.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Total cooling capacity in TR:
$$\text{TR} = \frac{TH}{3.517} = \frac{50}{3.517} = 14.22 \text{ TR}$$

$$\boxed{\text{SHF} = 0.80, \quad \text{ADP} = 12.5 \text{ °C}, \quad \text{BF} = 0.333, \quad \text{Capacity} = 14.22 \text{ TR}}$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 — Alternative]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch how the **SHF line** is drawn on the **psychrometric chart** to determine the **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)**. Mention the role of bypass factor in this determination.

**(b)** An air-conditioning system supplies air at **15 °C DBT and 90% RH** to a room maintained at **28 °C DBT and 50% RH**. The total heat load of the room is **80 kW** and the volume flow rate is **10 m³/s**. Calculate:
1. The Sensible Heat Factor (SHF) and Room Sensible Heat Factor (RSHF)
2. The Apparatus Dew Point (ADP) using the SHF line
3. The Bypass Factor of the cooling coil

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Concept of SHF line drawing:
- Mark the **Room Condition (R)** on the psychrometric chart.
- Calculate SHF = SH / (SH + LH).
- The SHF line is drawn as a straight line passing through R with a slope determined by SHF.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Procedure to find ADP:
- Extend the SHF line until it intersects the **saturation curve (100% RH line)**.
- The point of intersection is the **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)** — the temperature to which the air would have to be cooled if the coil had 100% contact (zero bypass).
- The numerical value of ADP is read from the dry-bulb temperature scale.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Role of Bypass Factor (BF):
$$BF = \frac{T_S - T_{ADP}}{T_R - T_{ADP}}$$

- BF = 0 → Perfect coil (no bypass), supply air equals ADP.
- BF = 1 → No cooling, supply air equals room air.
- Typical BF ranges from **0.05 to 0.40** for commercial cooling coils.
- **Higher BF** → coil surface area can be smaller (economical) but less precise temperature control.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Final relation:
The supply air condition lies on the line connecting ADP to R, with BF determining the position.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1:** Given:
- Supply: T_S = 15 °C, RH_S = 90%
- Room: T_R = 28 °C, RH_R = 50%
- Total heat TH = 80 kW, V̇ = 10 m³/s

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Find humidity ratios from psychrometric chart:
- At 15 °C, 90% RH: W_S ≈ 0.0097 kg/kg
- At 28 °C, 50% RH: W_R ≈ 0.0117 kg/kg
- Δt = 28 − 15 = 13 °C
- ΔW = 0.0117 − 0.0097 = 0.0020 kg/kg

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Calculate SH and LH:
$$SH = 1.21 \cdot 10 \cdot 13 = 157.3 \text{ kW}$$
$$LH = 3010 \cdot 10 \cdot 0.0020 = 60.2 \text{ kW}$$
$$TH = SH + LH = 157.3 + 60.2 = 217.5 \text{ kW}$$

**Note:** The discrepancy with the given TH = 80 kW indicates the actual operating condition is in a system with mixed return air; the SHF is the property of the process line regardless of absolute load.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Calculate SHF:
$$\text{SHF} = \frac{157.3}{157.3 + 60.2} = \frac{157.3}{217.5} = 0.723$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Calculate RSHF:
For RSHF, we need the room heat split. Assuming the 80 kW total room load has the same ratio as the air process (since RSHF uses room-only loads):
$$\text{RSHF} = \frac{SH_{room}}{SH_{room} + LH_{room}} = 0.723$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Find ADP from SHF line:
The SHF line is drawn from R (28 °C, 0.0117) with slope:
$$\text{Slope} = \frac{1 - 0.723}{0.723} = 0.383$$

Extending the line to the saturation curve gives:
$$T_{ADP} \approx 18.5 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 7:** Calculate Bypass Factor:
$$BF = \frac{T_S - T_{ADP}}{T_R - T_{ADP}} = \frac{15 - 18.5}{28 - 18.5} = \frac{-3.5}{9.5} = -0.368$$

> [!WARNING]
> **A negative BF is physically impossible!** This indicates the given supply condition is **inconsistent** with the room load ratio. The supply air is colder (deeper) than the ADP, meaning the SHF of the actual process should be higher. The correct interpretation: the given data is hypothetical, and the **theoretical ADP** for the load ratio of 0.723 is what the coil is **targeting** — the actual supply depends on BF.

$$\boxed{\text{SHF} = 0.723, \quad \text{RSHF} = 0.723, \quad T_{ADP} \approx 18.5 \text{ °C}}$$

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU SHF Problems:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency in ΔW:** Many students use ΔW in **g/kg** instead of **kg/kg**. Since 3010 kJ/kg is based on kg/kg, using g/kg will give a wrong answer **1000× larger** for LH. **Always convert first!**
> 2. **Confusing SHF with RSHF:** SHF may equal RSHF only when there is no ventilation or apparatus heat. In problems with fresh air intake, they are different.
> 3. **Wrong Slope on Chart:** The slope of the SHF line is **NOT** SHF. The correct slope is $\frac{1 - \text{SHF}}{\text{SHF}}$ on a chart with W on y-axis and h on x-axis. Many students plot the line with the wrong inclination.
> 4. **Forgetting ΔW Sign:** ΔW = W_room − W_supply (always positive if cooling/dehumidifying). Reversing the sign gives negative SHF, which is impossible.
> 5. **Bypass Factor Calculation:** Always use **dry-bulb temperatures** in the BF formula, not wet-bulb or enthalpy.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision — Key Bullets for SHF:**

- ✅ **SHF is dimensionless** — no units. It is a ratio, always between **0 and 1**.
- ✅ **SHF = SH / (SH + LH)** — never write SHF = SH / LH (a common student error).
- ✅ **Formula (SI):** $\text{SHF} = \frac{1.21 \cdot \Delta t}{1.21 \cdot \Delta t + 3010 \cdot \Delta W}$ with ΔW in **kg/kg**.
- ✅ **Formula (IP):** $\text{SHF} = \frac{1.08 \cdot \Delta t}{4.5 \cdot \Delta h}$ using CFM and BTU/hr basis.
- ✅ **ADP** is found by extending the SHF line to the **saturation curve** on the psychrometric chart.
- ✅ **BF = (T_S − T_ADP) / (T_R − T_ADP)** — uses **dry-bulb temperatures only**.
- ✅ **RSHF** uses only room loads; **GSHF** adds apparatus heat; **ESHF** is the design value for cooling coils.
- ✅ **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 12,000 BTU/hr** — standard conversion for capacity.
- ✅ **High humidity → low SHF** (Kerala: SHF ≈ 0.3 to 0.5); **Dry climate → high SHF** (Rajasthan: SHF ≈ 0.7 to 0.9).
- ✅ **Typical BF range for commercial cooling coils: 0.05 to 0.40.** Lower BF = more efficient coil.
- ✅ **SHF is independent of air quantity** (V̇ cancels out) — depends only on the **process line slope** on the psychrometric chart.
- ✅ **For KTU numerical problems**, always state the assumptions (sea-level atmospheric pressure, standard air density 1.20 kg/m³) at the beginning of your solution to earn the first 1–2 marks.
- ✅ **Master the psychrometric chart reading** for Δt, ΔW, Δh — this is the single most-tested skill in Module 3.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RSHF
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** RSHF # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Room Sensible Heat Factor (RSHF)** is defined as the **ratio of the Room Sensible Heat (SH_R)** to the **Total Room Heat (TRH = SH_R + LH_R)**. It is a dimensionless quantity (between **0 and 1**) that describes how much of the cooling load inside the conditioned space is due to *dry-bulb temperature change* versus *moisture removal*. $$\text{RSHF} = \frac{\text{Room Sensible Heat (SH}_R\text{)}}{\text{Room Sensible Heat (SH}_R\text{)} + \text{Room Latent Heat (LH}_R\text{)}}$$ $$\boxed{\text{RSHF} = \frac{SH_R}{SH_R + LH_R}}$$ It is also called the **Sensible Heat Ratio (SHR)** when referring specifically to the room load. ### Sub-Categories of SHF (for full clarity) | Symbol | Full Form | Definition | |---|---|---| | **RSHF** | Room Sensible Heat Factor | Ratio of room sensible heat to **total room heat** (no outside air) | | **GSHF** | Grand Sensible Heat Factor | Ratio of room sensible heat to **grand total heat** (room + outside air) | | **ESHF** | Effective Sensible Heat Factor | Modified RSHF used when supply air enthalpy is below outside air enthalpy (reheat / dehumidification) | > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Constant:** In SI units, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K and $h_{fg} = 2501$ kJ/kg at 0 °C. The ratio $h_{fg}/c_p \approx \mathbf{2488.6}$. Many KTU textbooks use the empirical factor **1.08** in place of $h_{fg}/c_p$ because it includes small corrections for fan heat, duct gains, and unit conversion to BTU/lb·°F. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition Imagine you are boiling water on a stove. The stove delivers **two kinds of energy**: the energy that actually raises the temperature of the water (**sensible** — you can "sense" it), and the energy that converts water at 100 °C into steam without raising temperature further (**latent** — "hidden" as phase change). In a room being air-conditioned, the cooling coil must similarly handle two types of load: - **Sensible cooling**: warm people, sunlight through windows, electronic equipment, walls, ceiling, doors → these raise the **dry-bulb temperature**. - **Latent cooling**: respiration, cooking, mopping, open tea-kettles, infiltration → these add **moisture (humidity)** without a big temperature rise. **RSHF simply tells you what fraction of the room's cooling problem is "sensible" (temperature-driven).** - A **movie theatre in summer** has lots of people packed inside → high latent load → **low RSHF** (maybe 0.55–0.65). - A **server room or data centre** has no moisture sources, only heat-generating servers → **RSHF ≈ 0.95–1.0** (almost pure sensible load). - A **comfort AC bedroom** typically has **RSHF ≈ 0.75–0.80**. ## 1.3 Why is RSHF so Important in KTU Exams? It is the **gateway** to four downstream calculations: 1. **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)** — read from psychrometric chart. 2. **Bypass Factor (BPF)** of the cooling coil. 3. **Contact Factor (CF)** = 1 − BPF. 4. **Cooling coil capacity** (total + sensible + latent split). > [!TIP] > If a KTU question gives you RSHF and the cooling coil's BPF (or coil performance curve), the **intersection of the room sensible-heat-ratio line and the coil saturation curve on the psychrometric chart gives the Supply Air State (point S)**. This single intersection is the most exam-relevant idea of the entire topic. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step by Step The conditioner handles air in **three streams**: - **Recirculated air** from the room (state 1 — *return air*). - **Outside (fresh) air** (state 2 — *OA*). - These mix to form **entering air to cooling coil** (state 3). - The **cooling coil** cools and dehumidifies this mixture to **Supply Air** (state S). Let $\dot{m}_s$ = mass flow rate of supply air (kg/s) delivered to the room. Let $\dot{m}_r$ = mass of recirculated room air. Let $\dot{m}_o$ = mass of outside (fresh) air. By mass balance: $\dot{m}_s = \dot{m}_r + \dot{m}_o$. ### Heat Loads (per unit mass of supply air) **Room Sensible Heat (SH_R)** — heat picked up from room air per kg of supply: $$SH_R = c_p \,(t_1 - t_S)$$ **Room Latent Heat (LH_R)** — moisture picked up per kg of supply: $$LH_R = h_{fg}\,(W_1 - W_S)$$ **Outside Air Sensible Heat (SH_O)** — sensible heat picked up by mixing in OA: $$SH_O = \frac{\dot{m}_o}{\dot{m}_s}\,c_p\,(t_2 - t_1)$$ **Outside Air Latent Heat (LH_O)** — latent heat from OA: $$LH_O = \frac{\dot{m}_o}{\dot{m}_s}\,h_{fg}\,(W_2 - W_1)$$ **Total Room Heat (TRH)**: $$TRH = SH_R + LH_R$$ **Grand Total Heat (GTH)**: $$GTH = SH_R + LH_R + SH_O + LH_O$$ ## 2.2 The Three Sensible Heat Factors | Factor | Formula | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---| | **RSHF** | $\dfrac{SH_R}{SH_R + LH_R}$ | Fraction of room load that is sensible | | **GSHF** | $\dfrac{SH_R}{SH_R + LH_R + SH_O + LH_O}$ | Fraction of *total* plant load that is sensible | | **ESHF** | $\dfrac{SH_R}{SH_R + LH_R + SH_{coil}}$ | Fraction after including any reheat load added by the coil | > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** Students write GSHF = (Room SH)/(Room SH + Room LH). That is **wrong**. GSHF divides by the *Grand Total Heat* which **includes the outside-air heat components**. Whenever the percentage of outside air is **> 0**, **GSHF < RSHF**. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorise this table. Nearly every KTU 14-mark question on this module uses ≥ 3 of these equations. | # | Formula | Description / Units | |---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{RSHF} = \dfrac{SH_R}{SH_R + LH_R}$ | Definition (dimensionless) | | 2 | $\text{RSHF} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{h_{fg}}{c_p}\cdot\dfrac{W_1-W_S}{t_1-t_S}}$ | In terms of state points | | 3 | $\text{RSHF} \approx \dfrac{1}{1 + 1.08\,\dfrac{\Delta W}{\Delta t}}$ | Approx. form used in KTU problems (with $\Delta W$ in grains/lb or kg/kg per °C) | | 4 | $\text{GSHF} = \dfrac{SH_R}{SH_R + LH_R + SH_O + LH_O}$ | Includes OA loads | | 5 | $\text{ESHF} = \dfrac{SH_R}{SH_R + LH_R + SH_{coil(reheat)}}$ | For systems with reheat | | 6 | $\text{BPF} = \dfrac{t_3 - t_S}{t_3 - t_{ADP}}$ | Bypass factor (coil characteristic) | | 7 | $\text{Contact Factor (CF)} = 1 - \text{BPF}$ | Fraction of air that touches coil | | 8 | $\dot{Q}_{total} = \dot{m}_s \cdot c_p(t_1 - t_S) + \dot{m}_s \cdot h_{fg}(W_1 - W_S)$ | Coil total capacity (kW) | | 9 | $\dot{Q}_s = \dot{m}_s \cdot c_p (t_1 - t_S)$ | Coil sensible capacity | | 10 | $\dot{Q}_L = \dot{m}_s \cdot h_{fg}(W_1 - W_S)$ | Coil latent capacity | | 11 | $t_S = t_3 - \text{BPF}\cdot(t_3 - t_{ADP})$ | Supply air temperature from BPF | | 12 | $W_S = W_3 - \text{BPF}\cdot(W_3 - W_{ADP})$ | Supply humidity ratio from BPF | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **HVAC Designers** plot the RSHF line on a psychrometric chart through the room condition point and choose a coil whose coil-saturation curve intersects this line at a supply-air state giving the required mass flow rate. - **Energy Auditors** use GSHF to size chilled-water plants and outside-air dampers correctly. - **Energy Recovery** systems target the difference RSHF → GSHF (the OA load) to decide where to install heat-recovery wheels. - In **data-centre cooling** (where RSHF → 1), chilled-water setpoints can be raised because no dehumidification is needed → huge energy savings. ## 2.5 Graphical Interpretation (the "SHR Line") If the room air (state 1) and supply air (state S) are joined on the psychrometric chart, the line slope is: $$\left.\frac{dW}{dt}\right|_{\text{RSHF line}} = -\frac{c_p}{h_{fg}}\cdot\frac{\text{RSHF}}{1-\text{RSHF}}$$ This line, when projected until it touches the **saturation curve** (RH = 100 %), defines the **ADP** for the coil that would *just barely* satisfy the room load without bypass. In practice, real coils have a BPF > 0, so the actual supply point lies **above** the ADP on this RSHF line. > [!TIP] > **Slope rule for KTU charts:** if RSHF > 0.5, the RSHF line is **more horizontal** than the 45° wet-bulb line; if RSHF < 0.5, it is **more vertical**. Use this quick visual check. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Working Form of RSHF We start from the basic definition applied to a per-unit-mass-of-supply-air basis: $$SH_R = c_p\,(t_1 - t_S) \quad \text{[kJ/kg of supply air]}$$ $$LH_R = h_{fg}\,(W_1 - W_S) \quad \text{[kJ/kg of supply air]}$$ By definition: $$\text{RSHF} = \frac{SH_R}{SH_R + LH_R}$$ ### Step 1 — Substitute the expressions $$\text{RSHF} = \frac{c_p(t_1-t_S)}{c_p(t_1-t_S) + h_{fg}(W_1-W_S)}$$ ### Step 2 — Divide numerator and denominator by $c_p(t_1-t_S)$ $$\text{RSHF} = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{h_{fg}}{c_p}\cdot\dfrac{W_1-W_S}{t_1-t_S}}$$ ### Step 3 — Insert the standard SI numerical values Using $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K and $h_{fg} = 2501$ kJ/kg (at 0 °C): $$\frac{h_{fg}}{c_p} = \frac{2501}{1.005} = 2488.6 \;\; \text{(dimensionless ratio)}$$ ### Step 4 — Engineering approximation used in KTU textbooks $$\text{RSHF} \approx \frac{1}{1 + 1.08\,\dfrac{\Delta W}{\Delta t}}$$ where $\Delta W = W_1 - W_S$ and $\Delta t = t_1 - t_S$. > [!NOTE] > The factor **1.08** is **not equal to 2488.6** — it appears when the equation is written in IP (Imperial) units with $W$ in **grains/lb** (1 lb = 7000 grains) and $t$ in °F. In SI units, retain $h_{fg}/c_p \approx 2488.6$ unless the problem statement explicitly says "use 1.08". ### Step 5 — Final boxed form $$\boxed{\text{RSHF} = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{h_{fg}}{c_p}\cdot\dfrac{W_1-W_S}{t_1-t_S}}}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation of the Slope of the RSHF Line Starting from the equation of the line passing through points 1 and S on the $W$–$t$ chart: $$\frac{W_1 - W_S}{t_1 - t_S} = \frac{h_{fg}}{c_p}\cdot\frac{1-\text{RSHF}}{\text{RSHF}}$$ This is **inverted** to give: $$\frac{dW}{dt} = -\frac{c_p}{h_{fg}}\cdot\frac{\text{RSHF}}{1-\text{RSHF}}$$ **Why negative?** Because supply air is *cooler* (lower $t$) AND *drier* (lower $W$) than room air, so as we move from room to supply, both decrease together. ## 3.3 Derivation of Bypass Factor and Contact Factor Let $t_3$ = temperature of air entering the cooling coil (mixed air). Let $t_{ADP}$ = apparatus dew point (the temperature the coil surface effectively is). Let $t_S$ = temperature of air leaving the coil (supply air). **Definition of BPF:** BPF is the fraction of air that *misses* the coil. So: - Air that **bypasses** (fraction BPF) leaves at $t_3$. - Air that **contacts** the coil (fraction 1 − BPF) leaves at $t_{ADP}$. By energy balance on the leaving stream: $$t_S = \text{BPF}\cdot t_3 + (1-\text{BPF})\cdot t_{ADP}$$ Rearranging: $$\boxed{\text{BPF} = \frac{t_S - t_{ADP}}{t_3 - t_{ADP}}}$$ But KTU commonly writes this as: $$\boxed{\text{BPF} = \frac{t_3 - t_S}{t_3 - t_{ADP}}}$$ (Either form is correct; **check the order** of subtraction in your reference text.) The **Contact Factor** is defined as: $$\text{CF} = 1 - \text{BPF} = \frac{t_3 - t_S}{t_3 - t_{ADP}}$$ ## 3.4 Worked Analytical Matrix — Linking All Quantities Given: $t_1, W_1$ (room), $t_2, W_2$ (outside), %OA, coil BPF. | Step | Output | Equation Used | |---|---|---| | 1 | Mixed-air DBT $t_3$ | $t_3 = (1-\text{OA fraction})\cdot t_1 + \text{OA fraction}\cdot t_2$ | | 2 | Mixed-air $W_3$ | $W_3 = (1-\text{OA fraction})\cdot W_1 + \text{OA fraction}\cdot W_2$ | | 3 | RSHF (given/computed) | From problem statement or from SH_R/(SH_R+LH_R) | | 4 | Draw RSHF line through state 1 on psychrometric chart | Slope from §3.2 | | 5 | Locate ADP | Intersection of RSHF line with saturation curve | | 6 | Compute $t_S, W_S$ | $t_S = t_3 - \text{BPF}(t_3 - t_{ADP})$, $W_S = W_3 - \text{BPF}(W_3 - W_{ADP})$ | | 7 | Coil capacity | $\dot{Q}_T = \dot{m}_s [c_p(t_3-t_S) + h_{fg}(W_3-W_S)]$ | | 8 | Air mass flow | $\dot{m}_s = \dfrac{SH_R}{c_p(t_1-t_S)}$ | > [!WARNING] > **Step 1 trap:** "20 % outside air" by **mass** vs. **volume** vs. **enthalpy** differ slightly. KTU almost always means **% by mass or volume** (treat air density as constant). Don't use OA % to mean *enthalpy-weighted*. ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Canonical KTU Style) **Problem:** A room has DBT = 26 °C, W = 0.0115 kg/kg. Supply air leaves the coil at DBT = 14 °C, W = 0.0085 kg/kg. Compute RSHF. ### Step A — Substitute $$SH_R = 1.005\,(26 - 14) = 12.06 \;\text{kJ/kg}$$ $$LH_R = 2501\,(0.0115 - 0.0085) = 7.503 \;\text{kJ/kg}$$ ### Step B — RSHF $$\text{RSHF} = \frac{12.06}{12.06 + 7.503} = \frac{12.06}{19.563} = 0.6165$$ $$\boxed{\text{RSHF} \approx 0.617}$$ ### Step C — Verification with the alternate formula $$\text{RSHF} = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{2501}{1.005}\cdot\frac{0.0030}{12}} = \frac{1}{1 + 2488.6 \cdot 0.00025} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.6222} = 0.6165 \;\checkmark$$ Both forms agree — this confirms numerical correctness. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Air-Handling Process Flow (Mermaid) The diagram below shows the **physical flow of air** from room → return duct → mixed with outside air → cooling coil → supply duct → room, identifying every state point (1, 2, 3, S, ADP). ```mermaid flowchart LR ROOM["ROOM
State 1: Return Air
DBT = t1, W = W1"] ODA["OUTSIDE AIR
State 2: Fresh Air
DBT = t2, W = W2"] MIX["MIXING CHAMBER
State 3: Mixed Air
t3 = (1-OA)x t1 + OA x t2"] COIL["COOLING COIL
ADP on Saturation Curve
BPF = bypass factor"] SUP["SUPPLY DUCT
State S
tS, WS"] BACK["RETURN DUCT
back to ROOM"] ROOM -- "Recirculated Air" --> MIX ODA -- "Outside Air
(OA fraction)" --> MIX MIX -- "Mixed Air t3, W3" --> COIL COIL -- "Cooled + Dehumidified" --> SUP SUP -- "Supply Air" --> ROOM ROOM -- "Returns" --> BACK BACK -.-> ROOM ``` ## 4.2 RSHF Logical Reasoning Flow (Mermaid Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Given: Room state 1
DBT t1, Humidity W1"] --> B["Compute SH_R per kg supply
= cp x t1 - tS"] C["Given: Coil supply state S
DBT tS, Humidity WS"] --> B D["Compute LH_R per kg supply
= hfg x W1 - WS"] --> E["Apply Definition"] B --> E["RSHF = SH_R / SH_R + LH_R"] D --> E E --> F{"Plot RSHF Line
on Psychrometric Chart"} F --> G["Find ADP at saturation curve"] G --> H["Use Coil BPF
to locate actual Supply point S"] H --> I["Compute Coil Capacity
Total / Sensible / Latent"] ``` ## 4.3 Sensible Heat Factor Hierarchy (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD P["Air Conditioning Plant
Total Heat Load"] --> R["Room Heat Load
SH_R + LH_R"] P --> O["Outside Air Load
SH_O + LH_O"] R --> RS["RSHF = SH_R / TRH"] P --> G["GTH = Room + OA Loads"] G --> GS["GSHF = SH_R / GTH"] R --> ES["ESHF (when reheat used)
= SH_R / TRH + SH_coil"] RS --> COMP["Always RSHF > GSHF
when OA fraction > 0"] GS --> COMP ``` ## 4.4 BPF vs Contact Factor Visual ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph COIL["COOLING COIL"] direction TB BP["Bypass Stream
Fraction = BPF
Leaves at t3"] CT["Contact Stream
Fraction = 1 - BPF
Leaves at t_ADP"] end MIX2["Mixed Air t3, W3"] --> COIL COIL --> OUT["Supply Air tS, WS"] OUT -.->|"CF = 1 - BPF
controls how close S is to ADP"| MIX2 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** Each node labelled with state numbers (1, 2, 3, S) corresponds to a **point on the psychrometric chart**. The ADP node corresponds to a point on the **saturation curve** (RH = 100 %). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define Room Sensible Heat Factor (RSHF) and state its significance. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** RSHF is the ratio of room sensible heat to total room heat: $\text{RSHF} = SH_R / (SH_R + LH_R)$. - **Expression (1 mark):** Expressed as $\text{RSHF} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{h_{fg}}{c_p}\cdot\dfrac{W_1-W_S}{t_1-t_S}}$. - **Significance (1 mark):** RSHF determines the slope of the sensible-heat-ratio line on the psychrometric chart; its intersection with the saturation curve gives the ADP, which is needed to size the cooling coil. --- ### Q2. Differentiate between RSHF and GSHF. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 3]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | RSHF | GSHF | |---|---|---| | Numerator | Room Sensible Heat (SH_R) | Room Sensible Heat (SH_R) | | Denominator | Room SH + Room LH = **Room Heat** | Room SH + Room LH + **Outside-air SH + Outside-air LH** = Grand Total Heat | | Use | Sizing the *coil* and ADP | Sizing the *complete plant* (coil + fans + ducts) | | Magnitude | Larger | Always $\leq$ RSHF when OA% > 0 | | Formula | $SH_R/(SH_R+LH_R)$ | $SH_R/(SH_R+LH_R+SH_O+LH_O)$ | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Pattern with Internal Choice) ### ⭐ Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]` **(a)** Derive an expression for Room Sensible Heat Factor (RSHF) in terms of room air and supply air states. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A room has DBT = 27 °C, WBT = 19 °C. Outside air is at DBT = 35 °C, WBT = 25 °C. The conditioned supply air leaves the coil at DBT = 14 °C. Outside air fraction = 20 %. The cooling coil has BPF = 0.15. Determine: **(i)** RSHF, **(ii)** ADP from chart, **(iii)** Coil total capacity if 0.4 m³/s of supply air is delivered (assume 1.2 kg/m³). **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — (a) **Step 1:** Define sensible and latent room heat per kg of supply air: $$SH_R = c_p(t_1 - t_S), \quad LH_R = h_{fg}(W_1 - W_S)$$ **Step 2:** Apply definition: $$\text{RSHF} = \frac{SH_R}{SH_R + LH_R} = \frac{c_p(t_1-t_S)}{c_p(t_1-t_S) + h_{fg}(W_1-W_S)}$$ **Step 3:** Divide numerator and denominator by $c_p(t_1-t_S)$: $$\text{RSHF} = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{h_{fg}}{c_p}\cdot\dfrac{W_1-W_S}{t_1-t_S}}$$ **Step 4:** Plug numerical constants $c_p = 1.005$, $h_{fg} = 2501$, ratio $\approx 2488.6$. **Step 5:** Mention the engineering approximation: $$\text{RSHF} \approx \frac{1}{1 + 1.08\,\dfrac{\Delta W}{\Delta t}}$$ (Each step ≈ 1 mark; final boxed expression carries weightage.) --- #### Model Solution — (b) **Step 1 — Locate states from psychrometric chart (2 marks):** - State 1 (room): 27 °C DBT, 19 °C WBT → $W_1 \approx 0.0108$ kg/kg - State 2 (outside): 35 °C DBT, 25 °C WBT → $W_2 \approx 0.0164$ kg/kg - Mixed state 3: $$t_3 = 0.8(27) + 0.2(35) = 28.6 \text{ °C}$$ $$W_3 = 0.8(0.0108) + 0.2(0.0164) = 0.01192 \text{ kg/kg}$$ **Step 2 — Compute RSHF (2 marks):** Approximate supply humidity $W_S$ using BPF + ADP (assume ADP ≈ 10 °C from chart): $$t_S = t_3 - \text{BPF}(t_3 - t_{ADP}) = 28.6 - 0.15(28.6-10) = 25.81 \text{ °C}$$ But supply is *cold* (14 °C given!). The problem intends the coil output to be **14 °C**, so we take $t_S = 14$ °C directly. Find $W_S$ by assuming air leaves at ≈ 90 % RH (chart): $W_S \approx 0.0089$ kg/kg. $$\text{RSHF} = \frac{1.005(27-14)}{1.005(27-14) + 2501(0.0108-0.0089)}$$ $$= \frac{13.065}{13.065 + 4.752} = \frac{13.065}{17.817} = 0.733$$ $$\boxed{\text{RSHF} \approx 0.733}$$ **Step 3 — ADP from chart (1 mark):** Draw RSHF line (slope = $-c_p/(h_{fg})\cdot \text{RSHF}/(1-\text{RSHF})$) through state 1. Where it meets saturation curve → ADP ≈ **9.5–10 °C**. **Step 4 — Coil total capacity (2 marks):** Mass flow: $\dot{m}_s = 0.4 \times 1.2 = 0.48$ kg/s. $$\dot{Q}_T = \dot{m}_s[c_p(t_3 - t_S) + h_{fg}(W_3 - W_S)]$$ $$= 0.48\,[1.005(28.6-14) + 2501(0.01192 - 0.0089)]$$ $$= 0.48\,[14.69 + 7.55] = 0.48 \times 22.24 = 10.68 \;\text{kW}$$ $$\boxed{\dot{Q}_T \approx 10.7 \text{ kW}}$$ --- ### ⭐ Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Module 3]` **(a)** Explain the concept of Bypass Factor (BPF) and Contact Factor. Derive the relation between BPF and ADP. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A conference hall has the following data: - Inside: DBT = 25 °C, RH = 50 % (so W₁ ≈ 0.0099 kg/kg) - Outside: DBT = 34 °C, RH = 60 % (W₂ ≈ 0.0195 kg/kg) - 25 % outside air is used. - Coil ADP = 8 °C, BPF = 0.20. Calculate: **(i)** Mixed-air state, **(ii)** Supply-air DBT and humidity ratio, **(iii)** RSHF and GSHF, **(iv)** Total coil load (TR) for 1.0 kg/s supply air. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — (a) **Definition (2 marks):** - **BPF** = fraction of air that *bypasses* the coil without undergoing heat/moisture exchange. - **Contact Factor (CF)** = 1 − BPF = fraction of air that actually contacts the cooling coil surface. **Derivation (5 marks):** Let $t_3$ = mixed-air entering temperature; $t_{ADP}$ = coil ADP temperature; $t_S$ = supply temperature. Energy balance on the leaving air: $$t_S = (1-\text{BPF})\,t_{ADP} + \text{BPF}\,t_3$$ Solving for BPF: $$\text{BPF} = \frac{t_S - t_{ADP}}{t_3 - t_{ADP}}$$ Or equivalently (commonly used in KTU): $$\boxed{\text{BPF} = \frac{t_3 - t_S}{t_3 - t_{ADP}}, \quad \text{CF} = 1 - \text{BPF} = \frac{t_S - t_{ADP}}{t_3 - t_{ADP}}}$$ Similar relations hold for $W$ and enthalpy $h$ on the psychrometric chart. --- #### Model Solution — (b) **(i) Mixed-air state (2 marks):** $$t_3 = 0.75(25) + 0.25(34) = 27.25 \text{ °C}$$ $$W_3 = 0.75(0.0099) + 0.25(0.0195) = 0.01230 \text{ kg/kg}$$ **(ii) Supply-air state (2 marks):** $$t_S = t_3 - \text{BPF}(t_3 - t_{ADP}) = 27.25 - 0.20(27.25 - 8) = 27.25 - 3.85 = 23.4 \text{ °C}$$ For $W_{ADP}$ at 8 °C saturation: $W_{ADP} = 0.0080$ kg/kg (chart). $$W_S = W_3 - \text{BPF}(W_3 - W_{ADP}) = 0.01230 - 0.20(0.01230 - 0.0080) = 0.01230 - 0.00086 = 0.01144 \text{ kg/kg}$$ **(iii) RSHF and GSHF (2 marks):** $$SH_R = 1.005(25 - 23.4) = 1.607 \text{ kJ/kg supply}$$ $$LH_R = 2501(0.0099 - 0.01144) = -3.85 \text{ kJ/kg supply (negative means supply is *wetter* than room — reheat needed!)}$$ When LH_R becomes negative → the system is in **cooling + reheat / dehumidification mode**, and the **Effective SHF** must be used. For typical exam answers: $$\text{RSHF (apparent)} = \frac{1.607}{1.607 + 3.85} = 0.295$$ $$\text{GSHF} = \frac{SH_R}{SH_R + LH_R + SH_O + LH_O}$$ $$SH_O = 0.25 \times 1.005 \times (34 - 25) = 2.26 \text{ kJ/kg supply}$$ $$LH_O = 0.25 \times 2501 \times (0.0195 - 0.0099) = 6.00 \text{ kJ/kg supply}$$ $$\text{GSHF} = \frac{1.607}{1.607 + 3.85 + 2.26 + 6.00} = \frac{1.607}{13.72} = 0.117$$ **(iv) Total coil load (1 mark):** $$\dot{Q}_T = 1.0\,[1.005(27.25-23.4) + 2501(0.01230-0.01144)]$$ $$= 1.0\,[3.87 + 2.15] = 6.02 \text{ kW}$$ $$\boxed{\dot{Q}_T \approx 6.02 \text{ kW}}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks in RSHF problems:** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert OA% properly.** If 20 % is by volume, with $\rho_a$ ≈ constant, the *mass* fraction is also 20 %. But if the problem says "20 % by enthalpy", you must weight by $h$. KTU usually gives it by *volume/mass* — read carefully. **Loss: 1–2 marks.** > > 2. **Mixing RSHF and GSHF.** Many students write RSHF when the question asks for GSHF (or vice versa). Remember: **RSHF ignores outside air, GSHF includes it**. **Loss: 2–3 marks.** > > 3. **Forgetting the BPF equation direction.** $\text{BPF} = (t_3 - t_S)/(t_3 - t_{ADP})$ or $(t_S - t_{ADP})/(t_3 - t_{ADP})$ — both exist in different textbooks. **Always state which form you are using.** **Loss: 1 mark.** > > 4. **Ignoring that $W_S$ may come from RH at $t_S$**, not from BPF if coil is not given. Many students skip the RH step. **Loss: 2 marks.** > > 5. **Not labelling units on the final answer.** KTU examiners deduct **half to one mark** if the unit is missing on the answer line. > > 6. **Skipping the psychrometric chart sketch.** Even if the values are computed, KTU awards 1–2 marks for a *neat, properly-labelled chart* with the RSHF line drawn and ADP marked. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **RSHF = SH_R / (SH_R + LH_R)** — dimensionless, always ≤ 1. - **GSHF < RSHF** whenever outside-air fraction > 0 (because GSHF has larger denominator). - **BPF** depends on coil design (fins, rows, air velocity) — **NOT on room conditions**. - **ADP** depends on both coil design and RSHF (intersection of RSHF line with saturation curve). - **CF = 1 − BPF** — represents "how effectively the coil contacts air". - **$h_{fg}/c_p \approx 2488.6$** in SI; **factor 1.08** is the IP-units equivalent (with $W$ in grains/lb). - **Mass flow rate of supply air:** $\dot{m}_s = \dfrac{\text{Room Sensible Heat}}{c_p(t_1-t_S)}$ — use this as the *master equation* when sizing ducts. - **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)** is read from the chart at saturation curve where the RSHF line intersects. - **Slope of RSHF line** on $W$–$t$ chart: $dW/dt = -(c_p/h_{fg})\cdot \text{RSHF}/(1-\text{RSHF})$. - **Always check** whether supply-air humidity $W_S$ comes from (a) BPF + ADP, or (b) RH at $t_S$ — different sources. - **When $W_S > W_1$** (negative LH_R), the system is in dehumidification/reheat mode — switch to **ESHF**. - **Always show the psychrometric chart** in 14-mark answers — KTU awards marks for the diagram. - **Practice** the standard 5-step matrix (compute mixed state → RSHF → ADP → supply state → coil capacity). - **Memorise** $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $h_{fg} = 2501$ kJ/kg, $\rho_a = 1.2$ kg/m³, $1.08$ factor — these are the "magic numbers" of this topic. - **Final sanity check:** Coil capacity in **kW**, air flow in **kg/s**, temperatures in **°C**, humidity ratios in **kg/kg**. Mixing units loses marks.

topics.title: GSHF, ERSHF
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** GSHF, ERSHF # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is SHF? (Foundation Concept) Before diving into **GSHF** and **ERSHF**, we must firmly grasp the parent concept — the **Sensible Heat Factor (SHF)**. In any air-conditioning (A/C) system, the cooling coil has to remove two distinct types of heat from the air passing through it: 1. **Sensible Heat (SH)** — The "dry" heat that changes the **dry-bulb temperature** of air (e.g., heat from people, equipment, walls, roof). 2. **Latent Heat (LH)** — The "hidden" heat tied up in **moisture** that must be condensed out (e.g., moisture from people breathing, cooking, infiltration). The Sensible Heat Factor is simply the *fraction* of the total heat that is sensible: $$ \text{SHF} = \frac{\text{Sensible Heat (SH)}}{\text{Sensible Heat (SH)} + \text{Latent Heat (LH)}} $$ On the **psychrometric chart**, the SHF is geometrically represented by the slope of the line drawn from the **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)** to the room condition. This is the single most important geometric fact you must remember for KTU exams. > [!IMPORTANT] > **SHF on the Psychrometric Chart = Ratio of Horizontal Distance to Total Diagonal Distance** between ADP and Room point. The line from ADP to Room has a slope equal to the **SHR** (Sensible Heat Ratio). SHF ≈ SHR for normal comfort A/C ranges. --- ## 1.2 GSHF — Grand Sensible Heat Factor ### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) The **Grand Sensible Heat Factor (GSHF)** is the ratio of the **Total Sensible Heat** (Room SH + Ventilation SH) to the **Grand Total Heat** (Total SH + Total LH) handled by the cooling coil. It accounts for **all** heat sources in the conditioned space, including the sensible and latent components of the **ventilation (outside) air** introduced to maintain indoor air quality. $$ \text{GSHF} = \frac{\text{Room SH} + \text{Ventilation SH}}{\text{Room SH} + \text{Ventilation SH} + \text{Room LH} + \text{Ventilation LH}} $$ ### Intuitive Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen" Imagine you run a restaurant kitchen that must: - **Cook food** (sensible heat — visible flame work) and - **Steam dumplings** (latent heat — invisible moisture work). Now imagine you also have to **pre-condition the fresh air** coming in from the hot, humid street outside. The *grand* sensible heat factor is the *overall* proportion of "fire work" you must do in the entire kitchen, including the extra hot air you're forced to bring in for the dining hall. You're not just cooking for the chefs inside — you're also handling the outside air's heat. > [!NOTE] > **GSHF** is *always* computed for the **apparent load on the cooling coil** (i.e., for the *mixed air* — the actual air entering the coil, not the air in the room). It is the SHF of **Point C** (mixed air) on the psychrometric chart with respect to ADP. --- ## 1.3 ERSHF — Effective Room Sensible Heat Factor ### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) The **Effective Room Sensible Heat Factor (ERSHF)** is the ratio of the **Room Sensible Heat** to the **Effective Total Heat** of the room, where the effective total includes the Room SH, the Room LH, **and the Latent Heat of the Ventilation Air** (but excludes the sensible heat of the ventilation air — because the ventilation air's sensible heat is "handled" by adjusting the supply air quantity, not by changing the coil's dehumidification load). $$ \text{ERSHF} = \frac{\text{Room SH}}{\text{Room SH} + \text{Room LH} + \text{Ventilation LH}} $$ ### Intuitive Analogy — "The Two Buckets" Think of the cooling coil as a busy toll booth: - **Bucket 1 (Sensible bucket)**: Only collects tickets from the **room's occupants** (cars leaving the room). - **Bucket 2 (Latent bucket)**: Collects tickets from **room occupants AND from the fresh air trucks** that just pulled in. The room occupants contribute *both* sensible and latent tickets, but the fresh-air trucks only contribute *latent* tickets (their sensible work was already counted in the room's total — they "feel" the same temperature as the room once they mix in). The ERSHF is essentially the proportion of the *sensible bucket* relative to the *combined buckets* of room heat + outside-air's *moisture* load. > [!NOTE] > **ERSHF** is the SHF of the **Room point (R)** with respect to ADP on the psychrometric chart, *modified* to account for the extra moisture load from ventilation air. It determines the **supply air conditions** and is what the engineer actually designs for. --- ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics to Remember | Parameter | Typical Value (Comfort A/C) | |---|---| | Room temperature | **24–26 °C DBT** | | Room relative humidity | **50–55 % RH** | | Outdoor design temperature (KTU Kerala context) | **32–35 °C DBT, 70–80 % RH** | | Indoor SHF (typical office) | **0.70 – 0.85** | | GSHF (with ventilation) | **0.75 – 0.90** | | ERSHF (with ventilation) | **0.65 – 0.80** | | Air quantity per person | **0.3–0.5 m³/min** fresh air | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Always remember:** For a typical comfort A/C problem, **GSHF > ERSHF**, because the outdoor air in summer (Kerala climate) carries more sensible heat than moisture, and adding it to the numerator boosts GSHF. The line from ADP → Room represents ERSHF, while the line from ADP → Mixed Air (C) represents GSHF. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three SHFs — A Clear Hierarchy In any A/C problem involving ventilation (outside) air, **three** distinct SHFs appear, each with a precise role: | SHF | Symbol | What it measures | Used for | |---|---|---|---| | **Room Sensible Heat Factor** | **RSHF** | Pure room load split | Initial room analysis | | **Ventilation SHF** | **Vent SHF** | Pure outside air load split | Ventilation load calculation | | **Effective Room SHF** | **ERSHF** | Room SH ÷ (Room SH + Room LH + Vent LH) | **Designing supply air conditions, BPF** | | **Grand SHF** | **GSHF** | (Room SH + Vent SH) ÷ Grand Total | **Apparent coil load, ADP & BPF using mixed air** | ### 2.1.1 Why Two Different SHFs for the Coil? This is the conceptual *punchline* every KTU examiner loves to test: - The **coil physically receives mixed air (Point C)**, so its **apparent SHF is GSHF**. - However, the **supply air quantity** must be sized to remove the **room's** sensible load (not the coil's apparent SHF), so the **effective SHF is ERSHF**. > [!IMPORTANT] > On the psychrometric chart: > - Line from **ADP → Room (R)** has slope = **ERSHF** > - Line from **ADP → Mixed Air (C)** has slope = **GSHF** > - Since C lies "above and to the right" of R, the line ADP→C is **steeper**, confirming **GSHF > ERSHF**. --- ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic — From Loads to Coil Selection 1. **Calculate Room Loads**: $\text{RSH}$, $\text{RLH}$ (from walls, roof, people, lights, equipment, doors). 2. **Calculate Ventilation Loads**: $\text{Vent SH}$, $\text{Vent LH}$ (using mass flow of outside air × enthalpy difference). 3. **Form Grand Totals**: - $\text{Total SH} = \text{RSH} + \text{Vent SH}$ - $\text{Total LH} = \text{RLH} + \text{Vent LH}$ 4. **Compute GSHF**: $\text{GSHF} = \dfrac{\text{Total SH}}{\text{Total SH} + \text{Total LH}}$ 5. **Compute ERSHF**: $\text{ERSHF} = \dfrac{\text{RSH}}{\text{RSH} + \text{RLH} + \text{Vent LH}}$ 6. **Locate ADP and BPF**: Use ERSHF line (ADP→R) to size coil and find Bypass Factor. 7. **Find Supply Air State (S)**: From $\text{Supply air enthalpy} = h_R - \dfrac{\text{RSH} + \text{RLH}}{\dot{m}_s}$ and using BPF on the ADP→R line. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are high-yield for KTU Part B (14-mark) numerical problems. Memorize them in this exact form. | # | Formula | Meaning / Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{RSHF} = \dfrac{\text{RSH}}{\text{RSH} + \text{RLH}}$ | Room sensible heat factor (no ventilation) | | 2 | $\text{Vent SHF} = \dfrac{\text{Vent SH}}{\text{Vent SH} + \text{Vent LH}}$ | SHF of outside (ventilation) air | | 3 | $\text{GSHF} = \dfrac{\text{RSH} + \text{Vent SH}}{(\text{RSH} + \text{Vent SH}) + (\text{RLH} + \text{Vent LH})}$ | Grand SHF — coil's apparent SHF | | 4 | $\text{ERSHF} = \dfrac{\text{RSH}}{\text{RSH} + \text{RLH} + \text{Vent LH}}$ | Effective Room SHF — used for supply air | | 5 | $\text{GSHF} = \dfrac{\text{RSHF}\cdot \text{RTH} + \text{Vent SHF}\cdot \text{VTH}}{\text{RTH} + \text{VTH}}$ | Weighted average form of GSHF | | 6 | $\text{ERSHF} = \dfrac{\text{RSH}}{\text{RSH} + \text{Vent LH} + \text{RLH}}$ | Alternate (and more common) form | | 7 | $\text{BPF} = \dfrac{h_C - h_S}{h_C - h_{ADP}}$ | Bypass factor using coil's apparent SHF | | 8 | $\dot{m}_s = \dfrac{\text{RSH}}{1.005\,(T_R - T_S)}$ | Supply air mass flow from room SH | | 9 | $\dot{m}_s = \dfrac{\text{RSH} + \text{RLH}}{h_R - h_S}$ | Supply air mass flow from total room heat | | 10 | $\text{Vent SH} = \dot{m}_v \cdot c_p \cdot (T_o - T_R)$ | Sensible heat of ventilation air | | 11 | $\text{Vent LH} = \dot{m}_v \cdot (h_o - h_R) - \text{Vent SH}$ | Latent heat of ventilation air | > [!WARNING] > **MOST COMMON KTU MISTAKE**: Students compute Vent LH as $\dot{m}_v (W_o - W_R) \cdot h_{fg}$ but forget the **enthalpy of vaporization** varies with temperature. Use $h_{fg}$ at room temperature or compute $\text{Vent LH} = \dot{m}_v (h_o - h_R) - \text{Vent SH}$ to avoid this. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **HVAC System Design**: GSHF and ERSHF drive the **selection of cooling coil rows, fin spacing, and face velocity**. High SHR coils are slimmer; low SHR (more dehumidification) need more rows. - **Chilled Water Temperature Selection**: A higher ERSHF allows higher chilled water temperature (saves compressor power). - **Energy Modeling**: Engineers use these SHFs in **HAP, TRACE, EnergyPlus** to auto-select coils and fans. - **Storage Application (KTU Cold Storage Module)**: GSHF/ERSHF principles extend to **product heat of respiration** (fruits/vegetables) and **latent load from door opening** in cold rooms. --- ## 2.5 Boundary Conditions & Validity | Condition | Implication | |---|---| | No ventilation air | $\text{ERSHF} = \text{GSHF} = \text{RSHF}$ | | All air is recirculated (100 %) | $\text{ERSHF} = \text{GSHF} = \text{RSHF}$ | | Ventilation air at room condition | $\text{ERSHF} = \text{GSHF} = \text{RSHF}$ | | Outdoor air very hot, very dry | $\text{GSHF} \gg \text{ERSHF}$ (e.g., desert climate) | | Outdoor air cool, very humid | $\text{GSHF} < \text{ERSHF}$ (rare; monsoon-edge case) | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of ERSHF from First Principles ### Step 1 — Define the Three Mass Flows Let: - $\dot{m}_R$ = mass flow rate of **return air** (from room) - $\dot{m}_v$ = mass flow rate of **ventilation (outside) air** - $\dot{m}_s$ = mass flow rate of **supply air** (leaving coil) By mass balance for the conditioned space: $$ \dot{m}_s = \dot{m}_R + \dot{m}_v $$ ### Step 2 — Sensible Heat Balance on the Room The sensible heat removed from the **room itself** equals: $$ \text{RSH} = \dot{m}_s \cdot c_p \cdot (T_R - T_S) $$ This is the heat that *must* be removed regardless of whether outside air is being introduced. **The ventilation air's sensible heat is "absorbed" into the supply air mass flow**, so it does **not** enter the numerator of the effective room SHF. ### Step 3 — Latent Heat Balance on the Room The latent heat removed from the room itself: $$ \text{RLH} = \dot{m}_s \cdot (W_R - W_S) \cdot h_{fg,R} $$ Additionally, the ventilation air introduces **its own latent load** because the outside air, once mixed in, has to be dehumidified down to the room's humidity level. This latent load is: $$ \text{Vent LH} = \dot{m}_v \cdot (W_o - W_R) \cdot h_{fg} $$ ### Step 4 — The "Effective" Total Heat at the Coil (Room-Perspective) The cooling coil's dehumidification burden includes: - Room latent heat (moisture from people, equipment) - **Plus** the moisture carried in by the ventilation air The *effective total heat* that the coil must remove **with respect to the room's cooling duty** is: $$ Q_{\text{eff, total}} = \text{RSH} + \text{RLH} + \text{Vent LH} $$ > **Why not include Vent SH in this denominator?** > Because Vent SH is handled by increasing $\dot{m}_s$ (supply air flow). The coil's *dehumidification* requirement is dictated by **moisture**, and that is dominated by the moisture content of the mixed air. The numerator of ERSHF (Room SH) measures the *temperature drop* the supply air must provide for the **room itself**. ### Step 5 — The Definition By definition: $$ \boxed{\;\text{ERSHF} = \frac{\text{RSH}}{\text{RSH} + \text{RLH} + \text{Vent LH}}\;} $$ This is the *effective* SHF — the SHF that **the room experiences** when the coil is loaded with the extra moisture from the fresh air. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of GSHF ### Step 1 — Identify the Coil Inlet The cooling coil receives **mixed air at Point C**: $$ h_C = \frac{\dot{m}_R \cdot h_R + \dot{m}_v \cdot h_o}{\dot{m}_R + \dot{m}_v} $$ ### Step 2 — Total Sensible Heat on Coil The total sensible heat to be removed by the coil is the sum of room SH and ventilation SH: $$ \text{Total SH} = \text{RSH} + \text{Vent SH} = \text{RSH} + \dot{m}_v \cdot c_p \cdot (T_o - T_R) $$ ### Step 3 — Total Latent Heat on Coil $$ \text{Total LH} = \text{RLH} + \text{Vent LH} = \text{RLH} + \dot{m}_v \cdot (W_o - W_R) \cdot h_{fg} $$ ### Step 4 — Grand Sensible Heat Factor By definition: $$ \boxed{\;\text{GSHF} = \frac{\text{RSH} + \text{Vent SH}}{\text{RSH} + \text{Vent SH} + \text{RLH} + \text{Vent LH}}\;} $$ ### Step 5 — Proof of the Weighted-Average Form Multiply numerator and denominator by the appropriate total heat values. Let $H_R = \text{RSH} + \text{RLH}$ and $H_v = \text{Vent SH} + \text{Vent LH}$. Then: $$ \text{GSHF} = \frac{\text{RSHF}\cdot H_R + \text{Vent SHF}\cdot H_v}{H_R + H_v} $$ **Derivation (continuation):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{GSHF} &= \frac{\text{RSH} + \text{Vent SH}}{H_R + H_v} \\[6pt] &= \frac{\text{RSH} + \text{Vent SH}}{H_R + H_v} \cdot \frac{H_R/H_R + H_v/H_v}{1} \\[6pt] &= \frac{\text{RSHF} \cdot H_R + \text{Vent SHF} \cdot H_v}{H_R + H_v} \end{aligned} $$ This confirms that GSHF is the **heat-flow-weighted average** of the room SHF and the ventilation SHF. This is the form KTU examiners love to see in derivations. --- ## 3.3 Derivation of the GSHF ≥ ERSHF Inequality **Claim:** For typical outdoor conditions (hot, humid outside air), $\text{GSHF} \geq \text{ERSHF}$. **Proof:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{GSHF} - \text{ERSHF} &= \frac{\text{RSH} + \text{Vent SH}}{\text{RSH} + \text{Vent SH} + \text{RLH} + \text{Vent LH}} - \frac{\text{RSH}}{\text{RSH} + \text{RLH} + \text{Vent LH}} \\[6pt] \end{aligned} $$ Let $A = \text{RSH}$, $B = \text{Vent SH}$, $C = \text{RLH}$, $D = \text{Vent LH}$: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{GSHF} - \text{ERSHF} &= \frac{A+B}{A+B+C+D} - \frac{A}{A+C+D} \\[6pt] &= \frac{(A+B)(A+C+D) - A(A+B+C+D)}{(A+B+C+D)(A+C+D)} \\[6pt] &= \frac{A^2 + AC + AD + AB + BC + BD - A^2 - AB - AC - AD}{(A+B+C+D)(A+C+D)} \\[6pt] &= \frac{BC + BD}{(A+B+C+D)(A+C+D)} \\[6pt] &= \frac{B(C+D)}{(A+B+C+D)(A+C+D)} \end{aligned} $$ Since $B \geq 0$ (Vent SH ≥ 0), $C \geq 0$, $D \geq 0$, and the denominators are positive, we conclude: $$ \boxed{\;\text{GSHF} - \text{ERSHF} = \frac{\text{Vent SH} \cdot (\text{RLH} + \text{Vent LH})}{(\text{Grand Total})(\text{Effective Room Total})} \geq 0\;} $$ Thus, $\text{GSHF} \geq \text{ERSHF}$ **always**, with equality only when $\text{Vent SH} = 0$ (no ventilation sensible heat — i.e., outdoor temperature = room temperature). --- ## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style) **Given:** A conference hall with the following loads: - RSH = 30 kW - RLH = 12 kW - Vent SH = 6 kW - Vent LH = 3 kW - Outside air: 35 °C DBT, 28 °C WBT - Room: 25 °C DBT, 50 % RH **Find:** RSHF, GSHF, ERSHF. **Solution:** $$ \text{RSHF} = \frac{30}{30+12} = \frac{30}{42} = 0.714 $$ $$ \text{GSHF} = \frac{30+6}{(30+6)+(12+3)} = \frac{36}{51} = 0.706 $$ $$ \text{ERSHF} = \frac{30}{30+12+3} = \frac{30}{45} = 0.667 $$ **Verification using the inequality:** $$ \text{GSHF} - \text{ERSHF} = 0.706 - 0.667 = 0.039 $$ Using derived formula: $\dfrac{6 \times (12+3)}{51 \times 45} = \dfrac{90}{2295} = 0.0392 \; \checkmark$ > [!NOTE] > Notice in this problem GSHF (0.706) is *barely* higher than ERSHF (0.667) because the outdoor air (35 °C, monsoon-like) has a similar moisture-to-sensible ratio as the room. In a hot-dry climate (e.g., Rajasthan, 42 °C, 25 % RH), Vent LH is small, GSHF would be much higher. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which SHF | Scenario | Use RSHF? | Use ERSHF? | Use GSHF? | |---|---|---|---| | Recirculated air, no ventilation | ✓ | = RSHF | = RSHF | | With ventilation, find supply air | — | ✓ | — | | With ventilation, find ADP & BPF | — | ✓ (line ADP→R) | ✓ (line ADP→C) | | With ventilation, find coil load | — | — | ✓ | | With reheat or fan heat | ✓ (modified) | ✓ (modified) | ✓ (modified) | | Bypass factor using mixed air (coil inlet C) | — | — | ✓ | | Bypass factor for room condition R | — | ✓ | — | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — A/C System with Ventilation The following Mermaid block diagram traces the air streams and the **two SHF lines** on the psychrometric chart simultaneously. This is the **block-level functional architecture** that KTU examiners expect students to draw before starting a numerical. ```mermaid flowchart LR OA[Outside Air State O] --> MIX RA[Return Air State R] --> MIX MIX{Mixing Chamber} MIX -->|Mixed Air State C| COIL[/Cooling Coil ADP/] COIL -->|Supply Air State S| ROOM[Conditioned Room R] ROOM -->|Return Air RA| RA OA -.Vent SH Vent LH.-> MIX classDef state fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,color:#1f2937,stroke-width:2px classDef coil fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#1f2937,stroke-width:2px classDef room fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#1f2937,stroke-width:2px class OA,RA,C,S state class COIL coil class ROOM room ``` **Reading the diagram:** - **O** = Outside air (hot, humid — Kerala context) - **R** = Room air (conditioned, 25 °C, 50 % RH) - **C** = Mixed air (O + R mixed in the mixing chamber) - **ADP** = Apparatus Dew Point (on the saturation curve, below C) - **S** = Supply air (leaving coil, on the line ADP→C with BPF offset) ## 4.2 Psychrometric Chart Logic — The Two SHF Lines ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PSYCH["Psychrometric Chart - SHF Lines"] ADP1["Apparatus Dew Point ADP

from ADP to C"| SUP classDef sat fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#1f2937 classDef roompt fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#1f2937 classDef mixpt fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#c2410c,color:#1f2937 classDef suppt fill:#f5d0fe,stroke:#7e22ce,color:#1f2937 class ADP1 sat class ROOMP roompt class MIXP mixpt class SUP suppt ``` ## 4.3 Heat Load Decomposition Matrix ```mermaid flowchart TB TOTAL[Grand Total Heat Q total] TOTAL --> SH["Total Sensible Heat SH total"] TOTAL --> LH["Total Latent Heat LH total"] SH --> RSH["Room SH
