People respiration infiltration"] LH --> VLH["Ventilation LH
Outside air moisture minus room moisture"] RSH -.Used in numerator.-> GSHFBOX[GSHF and ERSHF formulas] VSH -.Numerator of GSHF only.-> GSHFBOX RLH -.Denominator of both.-> GSHFBOX VLH -.Denominator of both.-> GSHFBOX GSHFBOX --> GSHF2["GSHF = SH total divided by Q total"] GSHFBOX --> ERSHF2["ERSHF = RSH divided by RSH plus RLH plus VLH"] classDef heat fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#a16207,color:#1f2937 classDef formula fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#4338ca,color:#1f2937 class TOTAL,SH,LH,RSH,VSH,RLH,VLH heat class GSHFBOX,GSHF2,ERSHF2 formula ``` ## 4.4 Decision Tree — Which SHF to Apply? ```mermaid flowchart TD START([Problem statement with loads]) --> Q1{Is there ventilation air?} Q1 -->|No| USE1["Use RSHF = RSH divided by RSH plus RLH"] Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{What do you need?} Q2 -->|A1 Coil apparent load or BPF from C| USE2["Use GSHF = Total SH divided by Grand Total"] Q2 -->|A2 Supply air state or BPF from R| USE3["Use ERSHF = RSH divided by RSH plus RLH plus Vent LH"] Q2 -->|A3 Compare room vs coil performance| USE4["Use both and apply inequality GSHF greater than or equal to ERSHF"] classDef q fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#1f2937 classDef a fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#047857,color:#1f2937 classDef startend fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,color:#1f2937 class START,USE1,USE2,USE3,USE4 startend class Q1,Q2 q class A1,A2,A3 a ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 — Conceptual Short-Answer `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Q: Define Grand Sensible Heat Factor (GSHF) and Effective Room Sensible Heat Factor (ERSHF). How are they related when ventilation air is introduced into the system?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition of GSHF (1 mark):** GSHF is the ratio of the total sensible heat (Room SH + Ventilation SH) to the grand total heat (Total SH + Total LH) handled by the cooling coil. It represents the *apparent* sensible heat factor of the **mixed air** entering the coil. $$ \text{GSHF} = \frac{\text{RSH} + \text{Vent SH}}{\text{RSH} + \text{Vent SH} + \text{RLH} + \text{Vent LH}} $$ **Definition of ERSHF (1 mark):** ERSHF is the ratio of the room sensible heat to the effective total room heat (Room SH + Room LH + Vent LH). It excludes ventilation sensible heat from the denominator because that heat is compensated by increasing supply air mass flow, not by altering the coil's dehumidification duty. $$ \text{ERSHF} = \frac{\text{RSH}}{\text{RSH} + \text{RLH} + \text{Vent LH}} $$ **Relationship (1 mark):** For typical outdoor conditions, $\text{GSHF} \geq \text{ERSHF}$, with equality only when there is no ventilation air. The difference equals: $$ \text{GSHF} - \text{ERSHF} = \frac{\text{Vent SH} \cdot (\text{RLH} + \text{Vent LH})}{(\text{Grand Total})(\text{Effective Room Total})} $$ This difference is always non-negative because both Vent SH and the latent loads are positive. --- ### Question A2 — Formula-Based `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: A room has RSH = 25 kW, RLH = 10 kW, and ventilation contributes Vent SH = 4 kW, Vent LH = 1.5 kW. Calculate the GSHF and ERSHF. Comment on which value the designer should use for determining the supply air condition and why.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Calculation of GSHF (1 mark):** $$ \text{GSHF} = \frac{25 + 4}{(25+4) + (10+1.5)} = \frac{29}{40.5} = 0.716 $$ **Calculation of ERSHF (1 mark):** $$ \text{ERSHF} = \frac{25}{25 + 10 + 1.5} = \frac{25}{36.5} = 0.685 $$ **Designer comment (1 mark):** The designer should use **ERSHF (0.685)** to determine the supply air condition. The reason: the supply air temperature is governed by the **room's** sensible heat requirement. ERSHF represents the line from ADP → Room point R on the psychrometric chart, which directly gives the supply air state for a given BPF. GSHF (0.716) is used to determine the **apparent coil load** and to size the cooling coil, not the supply air. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) ### Question Set B — ESE Module 3 Question > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3]** #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Complete SHF & Coil Analysis **(a)** Define GSHF and ERSHF. Derive the expression for ERSHF from first principles, clearly stating the assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An air-conditioned hall has the following data: - Room sensible heat (RSH) = 45 kW - Room latent heat (RLH) = 18 kW - Outside air conditions: 34 °C DBT, 28 °C WBT - Room conditions: 25 °C DBT, 50 % RH - Quantity of outside air supplied: 0.4 m³/s - Atmospheric pressure: 101.325 kPa - Bypass Factor (BPF) of the cooling coil: 0.20 Calculate: (i) RSHF, (ii) Vent SH and Vent LH, (iii) GSHF, (iv) ERSHF, and (v) the supply air DBT. **(7 marks)** --- ##### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks) **Definitions (2 marks):** - **GSHF** is the ratio of the total sensible heat to the grand total heat of the system, considering room and ventilation loads. It represents the apparent SHF at the cooling coil's mixed-air inlet. - **ERSHF** is the ratio of the room sensible heat to the effective total room heat (Room SH + Room LH + Vent LH). It represents the SHF seen by the **room itself** when ventilation moisture is included. **Derivation (5 marks):** Let $\dot{m}_s$ = supply air mass flow, $\dot{m}_v$ = ventilation air mass flow. By mass balance: $\dot{m}_s = \dot{m}_R + \dot{m}_v$. The room's sensible heat removed is: $$ \text{RSH} = \dot{m}_s \cdot c_p \cdot (T_R - T_S) $$ The room's latent heat removed is: $$ \text{RLH} = \dot{m}_s \cdot (W_R - W_S) \cdot h_{fg} $$ The ventilation air's latent load is: $$ \text{Vent LH} = \dot{m}_v \cdot (W_o - W_R) \cdot h_{fg} $$ The cooling coil's dehumidification burden (effective total heat from the room's perspective) is: $$ Q_{\text{eff}} = \text{RSH} + \text{RLH} + \text{Vent LH} $$ Since the room sensible heat is the numerator, dividing: $$ \text{ERSHF} = \frac{\text{RSH}}{\text{RSH} + \text{RLH} + \text{Vent LH}} $$ **Assumptions:** 1. Steady-state conditions 2. Perfect mixing of return and ventilation air 3. Vent SH handled by increased supply air mass flow (not by coil) 4. No reheat or fan heat (or these are lumped into loads) --- ##### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks) **Step 1: Mass flow of outside air (1 mark)** Using ideal gas: $\rho_{\text{air}} \approx 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$ $$ \dot{m}_v = 0.4 \times 1.2 = 0.48 \text{ kg/s} $$ **Step 2: Vent SH and Vent LH (2 marks)** Approximating $c_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$: $$ \text{Vent SH} = 0.48 \times 1.005 \times (34 - 25) = 0.48 \times 1.005 \times 9 = 4.34 \text{ kW} $$ From psychrometric chart: $W_o$ at 34 °C, 28 °C WBT ≈ **0.0198 kg/kg**; $W_R$ at 25 °C, 50 % RH ≈ **0.0099 kg/kg**; $h_{fg}$ at 25 °C ≈ 2450 kJ/kg. $$ \text{Vent LH} = 0.48 \times (0.0198 - 0.0099) \times 2450 = 0.48 \times 0.0099 \times 2450 = 11.64 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 3: RSHF, GSHF, ERSHF (2 marks)** $$ \text{RSHF} = \frac{45}{45+18} = \frac{45}{63} = 0.714 $$ $$ \text{GSHF} = \frac{45 + 4.34}{(45+4.34)+(18+11.64)} = \frac{49.34}{78.98} = 0.625 $$ $$ \text{ERSHF} = \frac{45}{45 + 18 + 11.64} = \frac{45}{74.64} = 0.603 $$ **Step 4: Supply Air DBT (2 marks)** Using the ERSHF line (ADP → R) and BPF = 0.20: Supply air enthalpy: $h_S = h_C - \text{BPF} \times (h_C - h_{ADP})$ But more directly, from room energy balance: $$ \dot{m}_s = \frac{\text{RSH}}{1.005 (T_R - T_S)} $$ And from ERSHF line geometry on psychrometric chart, with BPF = 0.20, the supply air DBT can be found by: $$ T_S = T_{ADP} + \text{BPF} \times (T_R - T_{ADP}) $$ To find $T_{ADP}$, use the ERSHF line property. The line from ADP to R has slope = ERSHF on the psychrometric chart. Reading from a standard psychrometric chart (or solving iteratively), $T_{ADP} \approx 12.5\text{ °C}$. Therefore: $$ T_S = 12.5 + 0.20 \times (25 - 12.5) = 12.5 + 2.5 = 15.0 \text{ °C} $$ **Final Answers:** - RSHF = **0.714** - Vent SH = **4.34 kW**, Vent LH = **11.64 kW** - GSHF = **0.625** - ERSHF = **0.603** - Supply Air DBT = **15.0 °C** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice **(a)** What is the relationship between GSHF, ERSHF, and RSHF? Show mathematically that GSHF ≥ ERSHF for any non-zero ventilation air with positive sensible heat. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A pharmaceutical clean room has the following data: - RSH = 32 kW, RLH = 8 kW - Vent SH = 5 kW, Vent LH = 4 kW - Bypass Factor = 0.15 - Inside conditions: 22 °C DBT, 55 % RH Determine: (i) RSHF, GSHF, and ERSHF, (ii) the apparatus dew point temperature, and (iii) the supply air DBT and WBT. **(7 marks)** --- ##### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks) **Relationship (3 marks):** - **RSHF** is the sensible heat factor of the room alone (no ventilation). - **ERSHF** accounts for the extra moisture (latent load) brought in by ventilation air. - **GSHF** is the apparent SHF of the cooling coil considering *both* SH and LH of ventilation air. They satisfy: $\text{GSHF} \geq \text{ERSHF} \geq \text{RSHF} \times \dfrac{\text{RSH} + \text{RLH}}{\text{RSH} + \text{RLH} + \text{Vent LH}}$ (under typical conditions). **Mathematical Proof (4 marks):** Define $A = \text{RSH}$, $B = \text{Vent SH}$, $C = \text{RLH}$, $D = \text{Vent LH}$. Then: $$ \text{GSHF} - \text{ERSHF} = \frac{A+B}{A+B+C+D} - \frac{A}{A+C+D} $$ Common denominator $(A+B+C+D)(A+C+D)$: $$ = \frac{(A+B)(A+C+D) - A(A+B+C+D)}{(A+B+C+D)(A+C+D)} $$ Expand numerator: $$ \begin{aligned} (A+B)(A+C+D) &= A^2 + AC + AD + AB + BC + BD \\ A(A+B+C+D) &= A^2 + AB + AC + AD \end{aligned} $$ Subtract: $$ A^2 + AC + AD + AB + BC + BD - A^2 - AB - AC - AD = BC + BD = B(C+D) $$ Therefore: $$ \text{GSHF} - \text{ERSHF} = \frac{B(C+D)}{(A+B+C+D)(A+C+D)} = \frac{\text{Vent SH}\cdot(\text{RLH} + \text{Vent LH})}{(\text{Grand Total})(\text{Effective Room Total})} $$ Since $B, C, D \geq 0$ and all terms positive, the result is $\geq 0$. Thus $\text{GSHF} \geq \text{ERSHF}$, with equality when $\text{Vent SH} = 0$. --- ##### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks) **Step 1: SHF calculations (2 marks)** $$ \text{RSHF} = \frac{32}{32+8} = 0.800 $$ $$ \text{GSHF} = \frac{32+5}{(32+5)+(8+4)} = \frac{37}{49} = 0.755 $$ $$ \text{ERSHF} = \frac{32}{32+8+4} = \frac{32}{44} = 0.727 $$ **Step 2: Apparatus Dew Point (ADP) (2 marks)** On the psychrometric chart, the ERSHF line is drawn from inside condition R (22 °C, 55 % RH) with slope = 0.727. Following this line down to the saturation curve gives the ADP. From standard psychrometric calculations, the ADP is at: $$ T_{ADP} \approx 11.8 \text{ °C, } W_{ADP} \approx 0.0084 \text{ kg/kg} $$ (This is found by solving $\dfrac{h_R - h_{ADP}}{1.005(T_R - T_{ADP})} = \text{ERSHF}$ iteratively, or by geometric construction on the chart.) **Step 3: Supply air state with BPF = 0.15 (3 marks)** The supply air point S lies on the **ADP → R** line, with BPF = 0.15 measured from ADP: $$ T_S = T_{ADP} + \text{BPF} \times (T_R - T_{ADP}) = 11.8 + 0.15 \times (22 - 11.8) = 11.8 + 1.53 = 13.33 \text{ °C} $$ $$ W_S = W_{ADP} + \text{BPF} \times (W_R - W_{ADP}) = 0.0084 + 0.15 \times (W_R - 0.0084) $$ For room at 22 °C, 55 % RH: $W_R \approx 0.0092 \text{ kg/kg}$: $$ W_S = 0.0084 + 0.15 \times (0.0092 - 0.0084) = 0.0084 + 0.15 \times 0.0008 = 0.0084 + 0.00012 = 0.00852 \text{ kg/kg} $$ **Final Answers:** - RSHF = **0.800**, GSHF = **0.755**, ERSHF = **0.727** - ADP ≈ **11.8 °C** - Supply air: **13.33 °C DBT, 0.00852 kg/kg WBT** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost 2-3 Marks in KTU Valuation:** > 1. **Forgetting to add Vent LH to the ERSHF denominator** — students use just RSH + RLH, getting RSHF instead of ERSHF. This is the **#1 most common error** in KTU papers. > 2. **Using GSHF where ERSHF is needed** (or vice versa) — remember: GSHF → **coil/ADP/BPF using mixed air C**; ERSHF → **supply air state and BPF using room R**. > 3. **Units inconsistency** — Vent SH and Vent LH must be in the **same units** (kW) as RSH and RLH. Convert from TR or kJ/h. > 4. **Wrong BPF interpretation** — BPF = (h_C - h_S) / (h_C - h_ADP) is along the **coil process line** (ADP → C), not ADP → R. Some texts use the ERSHF line for BPF; in KTU, use whichever line corresponds to the process described. > 5. **Skipping the psychrometric chart construction** — even in numerical problems, KTU examiners award partial marks for a clear chart drawing with all points (O, R, C, ADP, S) labelled. > 6. **Forgetting to multiply by mass flow** when computing Vent SH/LH from the volumetric flow rate. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam** ### Core Definitions - **RSHF** = RSH ÷ (RSH + RLH) — room-only SHF - **GSHF** = (RSH + Vent SH) ÷ (Total SH + Total LH) — apparent SHF at coil inlet - **ERSHF** = RSH ÷ (RSH + RLH + Vent LH) — effective SHF for room + ventilation moisture ### Key Inequalities - GSHF ≥ ERSHF (when Vent SH > 0) - ERSHF ≤ RSHF (when Vent LH > 0; rarely, ERSHF can equal RSHF if no ventilation) - All three lie in [0, 1] ### Geometric Interpretation on Psychrometric Chart - **ADP → R line slope = ERSHF** - **ADP → C line slope = GSHF** - C lies between R and O (with weighting by mass flows) - S lies on ADP → R line at distance BPF × (R - ADP) from ADP ### Application Rules (Remember These Cold) - Use **GSHF** to: size coil, find apparent coil load, locate ADP via coil process from C - Use **ERSHF** to: find supply air state, design duct sizes, calculate room mass flow - Use **RSHF** only when there is **no ventilation** (recirculated air only) ### Quick Numerical Trick - If Vent SH = 0 (outdoor temperature = room temperature), then GSHF = ERSHF. - If Vent LH = 0 (outside air very dry, e.g., desert), then ERSHF = RSHF. - If both = 0, all three SHFs collapse to RSHF. ### Common Mistake-Proofing - Always **draw the psychrometric chart first** before plugging numbers. - Always **label all five points** (O, R, C, ADP, S). - Always **state units** (kW, kJ/h, or TR). - Always **verify GSHF ≥ ERSHF** at the end of every problem. ### Kerala-Specific Notes (KTU Local Context) - Kerala design outdoor conditions (per ISHRAE): **34 °C DBT, 28 °C WBT** (coastal) or **35 °C DBT, 27 °C WBT** (inland). - Monsoon months (June–August): outdoor WBT drops to 24–26 °C, lowering GSHF. - Typical comfort indoor: **24 ± 1 °C DBT, 55 ± 5 % RH**.

topics.title: Air Distribution and Duct Design Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Air Distribution and Duct Design Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Air Distribution and Duct Design?

**Air Distribution** is the controlled delivery of conditioned air (cooled, heated, dehumidified, or filtered air) from the Air Handling Unit (AHU) to every occupied zone of a building, followed by the return of stale air back to the AHU. **Duct Design** is the engineering process of sizing, routing, and selecting the ductwork that physically carries this air with minimum energy consumption, acceptable noise levels, and uniform thermal comfort.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Textbook Standard):**
> Air distribution in an air-conditioning system refers to the manner in which the conditioned air is supplied to the conditioned space through supply outlets and removed through return inlets, such that the desired temperature, humidity, and air cleanliness are maintained uniformly throughout the space.
> **Duct Design** is the methodology used to determine the optimal cross-sectional dimensions of supply, return, and fresh air ducts in an HVAC system, balancing pressure loss, air velocity, noise, and installation cost.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Plumbing of Air"

Imagine the duct network as the **plumbing system of a building**, except instead of carrying water, it carries **invisible air**. Just like a plumber must choose pipe diameters to deliver enough water to every tap without bursting pipes or wasting energy, an HVAC engineer must choose duct sizes to deliver the right amount of cooled air to every room without making whistling noises, wasting fan power, or creating drafts.

Three engineering dilemmas a duct designer constantly balances:

| Dilemma | What Happens If Ignored |
|---|---|
| Duct too **small** | High velocity → whistling noise + high fan power bill |
| Duct too **large** | Wasted ceiling space + higher material cost |
| Duct **wrong route** | Long runs → excessive friction loss → weak airflow to far rooms |

## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics

The four universal metrics that govern every duct design decision:

- **Air Velocity (V)** in m/s — measured at the duct cross-section.
- **Volume Flow Rate (Q)** in m³/s or m³/h — amount of air moving per second.
- **Friction Loss per Unit Length (R)** in Pa/m — energy lost per metre of duct due to air friction.
- **Static Pressure (P_s)** and **Velocity Pressure (P_v)** in Pa — energies required to push and accelerate the air.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Continuity Equation (Energy of Air):**
> $$P_t = P_s + P_v$$
> where $P_t$ = total pressure, $P_s$ = static pressure, $P_v = \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}$ = velocity pressure.
> This is the energy equation that flows through every duct, branch, and grille. Any duct design method is essentially a strategy to keep these pressures balanced throughout the network.

## 1.4 The Three Families of Duct Design Methods

| Method | Core Idea | Most Common Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Constant Velocity Method** | Keep air velocity same in main and branch ducts | Industrial ventilation, exhaust systems |
| **Equal Friction Method (Velocity Reduction)** | Keep friction loss per metre (R) constant; velocity drops in branches | Commercial offices, hotels, hospitals — **most widely used** |
| **Static Regain Method** | Velocity pressure lost in main duct is converted back to static pressure at each branch take-off | Large central plants, big open-floor offices, clean rooms |

## 1.5 Duct Materials & Geometry

Ducts are made from:
- **Galvanized Iron (GI) sheets** — most common; thickness 0.5 mm to 1.6 mm based on duct size.
- **Aluminium sheets** — light, corrosion-resistant (coastal areas).
- **Fibre-glass insulated ducts (DUCT boards)** — pre-insulated, used where space is critical.
- **Fabric ducts (fabric dispersion)** — used in clean rooms and food industries.

**Shapes:**
- **Rectangular** — easy to fit into ceiling plenums; high aspect ratios cause noise.
- **Round (circular)** — best aerodynamic performance, lowest friction per unit area.
- **Flat oval** — compromise between rectangular and circular.
- **Flexible (non-metallic)** — only for short terminal connections (≤ 4–6 m).

> [!TIP]
> **Why Round Ducts Are Aerodynamically Superior:**
> For the same cross-sectional area and air flow, a round duct has the **lowest perimeter-to-area ratio**, which means **less frictional surface**, **lower pressure drop**, and therefore **less fan power**. That is why designers convert rectangular dimensions to an **equivalent round diameter (D_e)** when using friction charts.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Pressure Loss in a Duct

When air flows through a duct, it loses energy due to three causes:

1. **Frictional resistance** along the straight duct walls (skin friction).
2. **Dynamic (shock) losses** at fittings — bends, elbows, tees, reducers, dampers, diffusers.
3. **Stack/elevation effects** (negligible in typical HVAC rooms).

The total pressure loss between two points (1 and 2) in a duct system:

$$\Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_{friction} + \Delta P_{dynamic} + \Delta P_{elevation}$$

For most AC duct design, the elevation term is neglected, leaving:

$$\Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_{friction} + \Delta P_{dynamic}$$

## 2.2 The Darcy–Weisbach Friction Equation

The foundational equation of duct friction loss:

$$\Delta P_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\Delta P_f$ | Frictional pressure drop | Pa |
| $f$ | Darcy friction factor (dimensionless) | — |
| $L$ | Length of duct | m |
| $D$ | Hydraulic diameter of duct | m |
| $\rho$ | Density of air (≈ **1.20 kg/m³** at 20 °C, sea level) | kg/m³ |
| $V$ | Mean air velocity | m/s |

For ducts, the more practical form using friction loss per unit length $R$ (Pa/m):

$$R = \dfrac{\Delta P_f}{L} = \dfrac{f}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}$$

The friction factor $f$ depends on the **Reynolds Number** $Re = \dfrac{VD}{\nu}$ and the relative roughness of the duct wall.

- For **smooth GI ducts** in typical AC applications: $Re$ ranges from $10^4$ to $10^6$.
- The **Colebrook equation** (implicit) gives $f$ for turbulent flow in rough pipes:

$$\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{2.51}{Re\sqrt{f}}\right)$$

where $\varepsilon$ is the roughness height (GI sheet ≈ **0.15 mm**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU exam purposes, designers use the **ASHRAE Friction Chart** (or Carrier's equivalent) which already accounts for the Colebrook equation graphically. You are expected to read values from the chart or use the simplified equations given in the question.

## 2.3 Equivalent Diameter for Rectangular Ducts

Round ducts are aerodynamically superior, but rectangular ducts fit ceilings better. We therefore convert any rectangular section of width $a$ and height $b$ to an **equivalent round diameter** $D_e$:

$$D_e = \dfrac{1.30 \,(a \cdot b)^{0.625}}{(a + b)^{0.25}}$$

with $a$ and $b$ in metres (or consistent units).

For a **square duct** ($a = b$), $D_e = a \cdot \dfrac{1.30}{2^{0.25 \cdot 2}}$ — reducing algebra gives $D_e \approx 1.30 \cdot a^{0.625} / (2a)^{0.25}$.

> [!NOTE]
> This $D_e$ is then used in friction charts/tables exactly as if it were a round duct. The actual rectangular velocity is still $V = Q/A$, where $A = a \times b$.

## 2.4 Aspect Ratio

$$\text{Aspect Ratio} = AR = \dfrac{\text{Width } a}{\text{Height } b}$$

**KTU recommendation:** Keep $AR \leq 4$ for supply ducts, $\leq 6$ for return ducts to limit noise and avoid duct instability.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 📋 Master Formula Table

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = A \cdot V$ | Volume flow rate | m³/s |
| 2 | $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | Circular duct area | m² |
| 3 | $A = a \cdot b$ | Rectangular duct area | m² |
| 4 | $P_v = \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}$ | Velocity pressure | Pa |
| 5 | $\Delta P_f = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}$ | Darcy–Weisbach friction loss | Pa |
| 6 | $R = \dfrac{\Delta P_f}{L}$ | Friction loss rate | Pa/m |
| 7 | $D_e = 1.30 \,(ab)^{0.625}/(a+b)^{0.25}$ | Equivalent round diameter | m |
| 8 | $\Delta P_{dyn} = C_o \cdot P_v$ | Dynamic loss at fitting (with loss coefficient $C_o$) | Pa |
| 9 | $\text{SR} = P_{v,\text{before}} - P_{v,\text{after}}$ | Static Regain | Pa |
| 10 | $Q_{\text{space}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{space}} \times \text{ACH}}{3600}$ | Air Changes per Hour (volume method) | m³/s |
| 11 | $Re = \dfrac{V D_e}{\nu}$ | Reynolds Number | dimensionless |
| 12 | $V_{\text{design}} = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | Design velocity check | m/s |

### 📋 Recommended Design Velocities (ASHRAE / KTU reference)

| Location | Recommended V (m/s) |
|---|---|
| Main supply duct | **6 – 9** |
| Branch supply duct | **4.5 – 7** |
| Final branch (near diffuser) | **3 – 5** |
| Return air duct (main) | **5 – 7.5** |
| Above occupied zone grilles | **≤ 2.5 – 3.5** |

## 2.6 The Three Design Methods — Logic Compared

### Method 1 — Equal Velocity Method
- Designer selects **one velocity** for the entire duct system.
- Volume flow $Q$ varies along the duct, so duct cross-section $A = Q/V$ also varies.
- **Drawback:** Friction loss increases dramatically toward the AHU end; energy is wasted.

### Method 2 — Equal Friction (Velocity Reduction) Method
- Designer selects **one friction rate $R$** (Pa/m) for the **longest run** (index run).
- All other runs are designed so that the friction per metre of straight duct is **constant at $R$**.
- Because $Q$ decreases in branches, $V$ also decreases (velocity reduction), giving **lower noise in branches**.
- **Most popular for commercial systems.**

### Method 3 — Static Regain Method
- At each branch take-off, the **velocity pressure decrease** (because air slows down after branching) is converted into **static pressure rise** for the downstream duct.
- Therefore, the main duct can be sized so that static pressure remains **approximately constant** along its length.
- **Best for large VAV systems** with long duct runs.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

This topic is the **backbone of HVAC engineering practice**. Every consulting engineer designing an office complex, hospital OT, data centre, hotel, or shopping mall uses one of these three methods. Energy-efficient duct design can reduce fan power by **15–30 %**, which translates into lakhs of rupees in annual electricity savings for large buildings.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Equivalent Diameter $D_e$

We start from the fundamental requirement: **same frictional pressure drop** and **same air velocity** for both rectangular and circular ducts.

The Darcy–Weisbach equation, applied to a circular duct of diameter $D$:

$$\Delta P_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}$$

For a rectangular duct of sides $a$ and $b$, the **hydraulic diameter** (used in flow analysis) is:

$$D_h = \dfrac{4A}{P} = \dfrac{4ab}{2(a+b)} = \dfrac{2ab}{a+b}$$

But $D_h$ alone does not give equal friction loss, because the friction factor $f$ depends on the cross-section shape (corner effects). Experiments by **Huebscher (1948)** and **Wright (1945)** established that for equal friction loss at the same velocity:

$$D_e = 1.30 \,\dfrac{(ab)^{0.625}}{(a+b)^{0.25}}$$

This is the empirical correlation universally used in ASHRAE / ISHRAE / Carrier handbooks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the constant 1.30?**
> The constant 1.30 and the exponents 0.625 and 0.25 come from fitting experimental pressure-drop data for turbulent flow in rectangular ducts to the form of the Darcy–Weisbach equation, where the corner zones cause extra "form drag" not captured by $D_h$ alone.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Equal Friction Method (Full Derivation)

**Problem (KTU-Model Question):**
A supply duct system has the following layout. Air leaves the AHU at **2.0 m³/s**. Duct AB is the main, B is the first take-off, and branches BC and BD serve two zones. The longest run is AB → BC of total length **40 m**. Choose a friction rate $R = 0.8$ Pa/m. Design all duct sections using the equal friction method.

Assume circular GI ducts, $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³, and use the relationship:

$$D = \left(\dfrac{8 f L \rho Q^2}{\pi^2 \Delta P}\right)^{1/5}$$

with $f = 0.02$.

### Step 1 — Compute Index Run Pressure Drop

$$\Delta P_{index} = R \cdot L = 0.8 \times 40 = 32 \, \text{Pa}$$

### Step 2 — Solve for Duct Diameter on Index Run (Section BC)

We are designing run AB → BC together; the design is often performed **section by section**, starting from the **farthest outlet** and working backward to the fan.

Suppose section BC carries $Q_{BC} = 1.0$ m³/s, length $L_{BC} = 15$ m, allowable $\Delta P = R \cdot L = 0.8 \times 15 = 12$ Pa.

From $R = \dfrac{f}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}$ and $V = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$:

$$R = \dfrac{8 f \rho Q^2}{\pi^2 D^5}$$

Solving for $D$:

$$D = \left(\dfrac{8 f \rho Q^2}{\pi^2 R}\right)^{1/5}$$

Substituting $f = 0.02$, $\rho = 1.2$, $Q = 1.0$, $R = 0.8$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \left(\dfrac{8 \times 0.02 \times 1.2 \times (1.0)^2}{\pi^2 \times 0.8}\right)^{1/5} \\[6pt]
&= \left(\dfrac{0.192}{7.895}\right)^{1/5} \\[6pt]
&= (0.02432)^{0.2} \\[6pt]
&\approx 0.476 \, \text{m} = 476 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Use next **standard size**: 500 mm diameter.

Verify velocity:
$$V = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{1.0}{\pi (0.5)^2/4} = \dfrac{1.0}{0.1963} \approx 5.09 \, \text{m/s}$$

This is within the recommended 4.5–7 m/s for branch ducts. ✅

### Step 3 — Design Section AB

Section AB carries $Q_{AB} = 2.0$ m³/s (full supply), length $L_{AB} = 25$ m.

Recompute using $R = 0.8$ Pa/m:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{AB} &= \left(\dfrac{8 \times 0.02 \times 1.2 \times (2.0)^2}{\pi^2 \times 0.8}\right)^{1/5} \\[6pt]
&= \left(\dfrac{8 \times 0.02 \times 1.2 \times 4}{7.895}\right)^{1/5} \\[6pt]
&= \left(\dfrac{0.768}{7.895}\right)^{1/5} \\[6pt]
&= (0.0973)^{0.2} \\[6pt]
&\approx 0.625 \, \text{m} = 625 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Adopt standard size **650 mm**. Verify:

$$V_{AB} = \dfrac{2.0}{\pi (0.65)^2 / 4} = \dfrac{2.0}{0.3318} \approx 6.03 \, \text{m/s}$$

Within 6–9 m/s for main duct. ✅

### Step 4 — If Rectangular Duct Required

For a typical ceiling plenum height of 300 mm, choose $b = 0.30$ m. Compute $a$ for equivalent area of $D_e = 0.625$ m.

For round duct of $D = 0.625$ m, $A = \pi (0.625)^2/4 = 0.3068$ m².

For $b = 0.30$ m:
$$a = \dfrac{A}{b} = \dfrac{0.3068}{0.30} \approx 1.023 \, \text{m}$$

**Check $D_e$ consistency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_e &= \dfrac{1.30 \,(1.023 \times 0.30)^{0.625}}{(1.023 + 0.30)^{0.25}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{1.30 \times (0.3069)^{0.625}}{(1.323)^{0.25}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{1.30 \times 0.4614}{1.0729} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{0.5998}{1.0729} \approx 0.559 \, \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Slight reduction in $D_e$ from 0.625 → 0.559 m because rectangular ducts have higher friction than the equivalent round. Iterate if necessary.

### Step 5 — Friction Loss Verification

For round section BC ($D = 0.5$ m, $V = 5.09$ m/s, $L = 15$ m, $f = 0.02$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P_f &= 0.02 \times \dfrac{15}{0.5} \times \dfrac{1.2 \times (5.09)^2}{2} \\[6pt]
&= 0.02 \times 30 \times \dfrac{1.2 \times 25.91}{2} \\[6pt]
&= 0.6 \times 15.546 \\[6pt]
&= 9.33 \, \text{Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

$R = 9.33 / 15 = 0.622$ Pa/m — close to the target $0.8$ Pa/m. Small variation is acceptable.

### Step 6 — Add Dynamic Losses

For each elbow ($C_o \approx 0.3$), each tee ($C_o \approx 0.5$), and a reducer at fan outlet, sum:

$$\Delta P_{dyn,total} = \sum C_{o,i} \cdot P_{v,i}$$

This is added to the fan's external static pressure requirement.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Static Regain Method

**Problem:** A main duct $ABCD$ has take-offs at $B$, $C$, $D$. At $A$ (leaving AHU), $V_A = 10$ m/s, $Q_A = 4.0$ m³/s. At each branch, equal flow of $Q/3$ leaves. Duct area reduces by $1/3$ at each take-off. Find the **static pressure regain** at each branch.

**Step 1 — Velocity before each branch:**
$V_A = 10$ m/s, $V_B = 10 \times (2/3) = 6.67$ m/s, $V_C = 4.44$ m/s, $V_D = 2.96$ m/s.

**Step 2 — Velocity pressure at each point:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{v,A} &= 0.5 \times 1.2 \times (10)^2 = 60 \, \text{Pa} \\
P_{v,B} &= 0.5 \times 1.2 \times (6.67)^2 = 26.7 \, \text{Pa} \\
P_{v,C} &= 0.5 \times 1.2 \times (4.44)^2 = 11.83 \, \text{Pa} \\
P_{v,D} &= 0.5 \times 1.2 \times (2.96)^2 = 5.26 \, \text{Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Static Regain at each branch:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SR}_B &= P_{v,A} - P_{v,B} = 60 - 26.7 = 33.3 \, \text{Pa} \\
\text{SR}_C &= P_{v,B} - P_{v,C} = 26.7 - 11.83 = 14.87 \, \text{Pa} \\
\text{SR}_D &= P_{v,C} - P_{v,D} = 11.83 - 5.26 = 6.57 \, \text{Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — In practice**, the static regain is reduced by an efficiency factor $\eta \approx 0.5$ to $0.75$ due to turbulence and shock losses at the branch.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> As the main duct proceeds from the AHU, air leaves through branches, so velocity drops, and the kinetic energy is partially recovered as static pressure. This is why static regain ducts can be designed with a **near-constant static pressure along the main**, which is ideal for VAV terminals.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Method Selection

| Criterion | Equal Velocity | Equal Friction | Static Regain |
|---|---|---|---|
| Velocity trend | Constant | Reduces along run | Reduces progressively |
| Static pressure | Drops rapidly | Drops moderately | Approximately constant |
| Complexity | Simple | Moderate | Complex |
| Fan power | High | Medium | Lowest |
| Noise | Loud in branches | Quiet | Quietest |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Best for | Exhaust, industrial | Commercial, residential | Large VAV, clean rooms |
| KTU exam weight | 1–2 marks | **8–10 marks** | 5–7 marks |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Duct Network Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AHU[AHU Supply Fan] --> main1A[Main Duct Section AB]
    main1A --> main2B[Main Duct Section BC]
    main2B --> main3C[Main Duct Section CD]
    main3C --> endCap[End Cap]

    main1A --> t1[Tee 1: Branch to Zone 1]
    t1 --> d1[Diffuser Z1]

    main2B --> t2[Tee 2: Branch to Zone 2]
    t2 --> d2[Diffuser Z2]

    main3C --> t3[Tee 3: Branch to Zone 3]
    t3 --> d3[Diffuser Z3]

    RA[Return Air Grille] --> rmain[Return Main]
    rmain --> RAHU[Return to AHU]
```

## 4.2 Equal Friction Method — Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Get total cooling load and Q total] --> B[Compute Q per branch from load]
    B --> C[Identify longest run as INDEX RUN]
    C --> D[Choose friction rate R from chart 0.6 to 1.0 Pa per m]
    D --> E[For each section back to fan: compute D from Q, R, L]
    E --> F{Round D available in standard size?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Adopt standard size and verify V]
    F -->|No| H[Adopt next higher standard size]
    G --> I[Check V within recommended range]
    H --> I
    I --> J{V within limits?}
    J -->|No| K[Adjust R and restart]
    J -->|Yes| L[Sum friction and dynamic losses for fan ESP]
    L --> M[Select fan and motor]
```

## 4.3 Static Regain Method — Pressure Balance Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Section AB: P stat,1] --> P2[Section BC: P stat,2]
    P2 --> P3[Section CD: P stat,3]

    P1 -->|velocity drop| PR1[Static Regain 1]
    PR1 --> P2
    P2 -->|velocity drop| PR2[Static Regain 2]
    PR2 --> P3

    P1 -.-> L1[Friction loss AB]
    P2 -.-> L2[Friction loss BC]
    P3 -.-> L3[Friction loss CD]

    P3 --> FAN[Total ESP to fan selection]
    L1 --> FAN
    L2 --> FAN
    L3 --> FAN
```

## 4.4 Pressure Recovery in a Branch Take-Off

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    UP[Upstream Main Duct V1 high] --> BR[Branch Take Off]
    BR --> DOWN[Downstream Main V2 lower]
    BR --> SIDE[Branch Duct Vb]

    UP -->|PV1| EV[Velocity Pressure PV1]
    DOWN -->|PV2| EV
    EV --> SR[Static Regain equals PV1 minus PV2]
    SR --> DOWN

    UP -->|P static up| NET1[Net P static at branch equals P static up plus SR minus friction loss]
    NET1 --> DOWN
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Duct Design

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[Design Inputs]
        I1[Room cooling loads]
        I2[Layout drawing with duct lengths]
        I3[Ceiling plenum height]
        I4[ASHRAE recommended velocities]
    end

    subgraph COMP[Computation Core]
        C1[Compute Q per branch and main]
        C2[Choose design method Equal Friction or Static Regain]
        C3[Select R or V from charts]
        C4[Compute D for each section]
        C5[Convert to rectangular if needed]
        C6[Add equivalent length for fittings]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Engineering Outputs]
        O1[Duct diameters or sizes]
        O2[Friction loss per section]
        O3[Dynamic losses at fittings]
        O4[Total External Static Pressure for fan]
        O5[Material take off and cost]
    end

    I1 --> C1
    I2 --> C1
    I3 --> C5
    I4 --> C2

    C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 --> C5 --> C6
    C6 --> O1
    C6 --> O2
    C6 --> O3
    O2 --> O4
    O3 --> O4
    O1 --> O5
    O4 --> O5
```

## 4.6 Comparison Matrix (Visual Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Method 1: Equal Velocity] --> P1[+ Simple]
    M1 --> N1[minus High fan power]

    M2[Method 2: Equal Friction] --> P2[+ Widely used]
    M2 --> N2[minus Velocity drops]

    M3[Method 3: Static Regain] --> P3[+ Best for large systems]
    M3 --> N3[minus Complex calculations]
```
