<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
Define the **Equal Friction Method** of duct design. State **two assumptions** made in this method.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Equal Friction Method (also called the **Velocity Reduction Method**) is a duct sizing technique in which the **frictional pressure loss per unit length (R, in Pa/m)** is kept **constant** for all sections of the duct system.

**Two assumptions:**

1. The friction rate $R$ is chosen for the **longest run (index run)** and the same value of $R$ is applied to all other runs and sections.
2. The **velocity of air progressively reduces** in the branches as the volume flow rate decreases, keeping the frictional resistance per metre nearly equal in all sections.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for each assumption = 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
What is **Static Regain**? Write the expression used for it in duct design.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Static Regain (SR)** is the recovery of static pressure in the downstream section of a duct when the air velocity decreases after a branch take-off. As the main duct proceeds forward and air leaves through branches, its velocity reduces, and the kinetic (velocity) energy lost is partially recovered as useful static pressure.

**Expression:**

$$\text{Static Regain} = P_{v,\text{upstream}} - P_{v,\text{downstream}}$$

$$\text{SR} = \dfrac{\rho}{2}\left(V_{up}^2 - V_{down}^2\right)$$

In practice, an efficiency factor $\eta = 0.5$ to $0.75$ is applied to account for turbulence and shock losses at the take-off.

*(1.5 marks for definition + 1.5 marks for expression = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Always convert** rectangular dimensions to equivalent diameter $D_e$ using $1.30(ab)^{0.625}/(a+b)^{0.25}$ — students often forget the constant 1.30.
> 2. **Standard sizes** must be adopted (e.g., 300, 350, 400, 450, 500, 550, 600, 650, 700 mm for round). Designing non-standard sizes **loses 1 mark**.
> 3. **Velocity check** is mandatory after sizing. If $V$ exceeds 9 m/s in mains or 7 m/s in branches, the design is invalid.
> 4. **Dynamic losses** (tee, elbow, damper) must be added as equivalent lengths or loss coefficients. Skipping them is a common error.
> 5. Show **units clearly** in every step. Half-mark deductions for missing units.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the **Equal Friction Method** of duct design in detail. Derive the expression used to compute the duct diameter for a given section. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A supply duct system handles a total airflow of **3.0 m³/s**. The layout has three sections in series (A→B→C→D), with airflow division as $Q_{AB} = 3.0$ m³/s, $Q_{BC} = 2.0$ m³/s, $Q_{CD} = 1.0$ m³/s. Section lengths are 20 m, 25 m, and 30 m respectively. Take $f = 0.02$, $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³, and friction rate $R = 1.0$ Pa/m. Determine the **circular duct diameter** for each section and verify the design velocity. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Definition [2 marks]:**
The Equal Friction Method is a duct sizing procedure in which the **frictional pressure loss per unit length ($R$)** is kept the same in every straight section of the supply duct network. The velocity of air is allowed to decrease along the run as the flow rate reduces at branch take-offs, but $R$ is held constant.

**Derivation of diameter formula [4 marks]:**

Starting from Darcy–Weisbach:

$$\Delta P_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}$$

Friction rate:

$$R = \dfrac{\Delta P_f}{L} = \dfrac{f}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}$$

Express velocity in terms of flow rate $Q$ and duct diameter $D$:

$$V = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$$

Substitute:

$$R = \dfrac{f}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho}{2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}\right)^2 = \dfrac{f \cdot \rho \cdot 16 \cdot Q^2}{2 \pi^2 D^5} = \dfrac{8 f \rho Q^2}{\pi^2 D^5}$$

Rearranging for $D$:

$$\boxed{D = \left(\dfrac{8 f \rho Q^2}{\pi^2 R}\right)^{1/5}}$$

**Key features [1 mark]:**
- Index run is identified first.
- $R$ chosen typically between 0.6 and 1.2 Pa/m.
- Standard sizes adopted; velocity re-checked.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $Q_{AB} = 3.0$ m³/s, $L_{AB} = 20$ m, $R = 1.0$ Pa/m
- $Q_{BC} = 2.0$ m³/s, $L_{BC} = 25$ m
- $Q_{CD} = 1.0$ m³/s, $L_{CD} = 30$ m
- $f = 0.02$, $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³

**Compute denominator for $D^5$:**

$$\dfrac{8 f \rho}{\pi^2 R} = \dfrac{8 \times 0.02 \times 1.2}{\pi^2 \times 1.0} = \dfrac{0.192}{9.8696} = 0.01945$$

**Section CD:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{CD}^5 &= \dfrac{Q_{CD}^2}{0.01945} = \dfrac{(1.0)^2}{0.01945} = 51.41 \\[4pt]
D_{CD} &= (51.41)^{0.2} \approx 2.198
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — recompute. Using the formula $D = (8 f \rho Q^2 / \pi^2 R)^{1/5}$ directly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{CD} &= \left(\dfrac{8 \times 0.02 \times 1.2 \times (1.0)^2}{\pi^2 \times 1.0}\right)^{1/5} \\[4pt]
&= \left(\dfrac{0.192}{9.8696}\right)^{1/5} = (0.01945)^{0.2} \approx 0.451 \, \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Adopt standard **450 mm**.

Verify velocity: $V = 4 \times 1.0 / (\pi \times 0.45^2) = 4 / 0.6362 = 6.29$ m/s ✅ (within 4.5–7 m/s for branch).

**Section BC:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{BC} &= \left(\dfrac{8 \times 0.02 \times 1.2 \times (2.0)^2}{\pi^2 \times 1.0}\right)^{1/5} \\[4pt]
&= \left(\dfrac{0.768}{9.8696}\right)^{1/5} = (0.0778)^{0.2} \approx 0.597 \, \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Adopt standard **600 mm**.

$V_{BC} = 4 \times 2.0 / (\pi \times 0.6^2) = 8 / 1.131 = 7.07$ m/s — slightly high but acceptable for main duct transition.

**Section AB:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{AB} &= \left(\dfrac{8 \times 0.02 \times 1.2 \times (3.0)^2}{\pi^2 \times 1.0}\right)^{1/5} \\[4pt]
&= \left(\dfrac{1.728}{9.8696}\right)^{1/5} = (0.1751)^{0.2} \approx 0.713 \, \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Adopt standard **750 mm**.

$V_{AB} = 4 \times 3.0 / (\pi \times 0.75^2) = 12 / 1.767 = 6.79$ m/s ✅ (within 6–9 m/s for main duct).

**Summary table [1 mark]:**

| Section | Q (m³/s) | D (mm) | V (m/s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| AB | 3.0 | 750 | 6.79 |
| BC | 2.0 | 600 | 7.07 |
| CD | 1.0 | 450 | 6.29 |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Model Question]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Static Regain Method** of duct design. Discuss its advantages over the Equal Friction Method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rectangular supply duct of dimensions **600 mm × 400 mm** carries **2.5 m³/s** of air at $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³. Compute the **(i) equivalent round diameter**, **(ii) air velocity in the duct**, **(iii) velocity pressure**, and **(iv) dynamic loss** at an elbow with loss coefficient $C_o = 0.3$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Definition & Concept [3 marks]:**
The **Static Regain Method** is a duct sizing technique used for large systems where the main duct has several take-offs. After each take-off, the velocity of air in the main duct decreases (because some flow has left). The reduction in velocity pressure is **partially recovered** as static pressure in the downstream section — this recovery is called **static regain**.

**Block diagram [2 marks]:**

```
AHU → [Main duct section 1] → Tee → [Section 2] → Tee → [Section 3] → End
            ↓ V1 → V2 ↓                                    ↓ V2 → V3 ↓
        Regain SR1                                    Regain SR2
```

Mathematically:

$$P_{s,\text{down}} = P_{s,\text{up}} - \Delta P_{\text{friction, down}} + \eta(P_{v,\text{up}} - P_{v,\text{down}})$$

where $\eta = 0.5$ to $0.75$ (regain efficiency).

**Advantages over Equal Friction Method [2 marks]:**

1. **Lower fan power** — static pressure remains nearly constant along the main, so the fan only needs to overcome friction, not velocity losses.
2. **Better airflow distribution** — every branch receives nearly equal static pressure, ensuring uniform diffuser performance.
3. **Lower noise** in terminal devices because duct velocities are kept low in far sections.
4. Suitable for **large buildings with long duct runs** (e.g., shopping malls, airports).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $a = 0.6$ m, $b = 0.4$ m, $Q = 2.5$ m³/s, $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³, $C_o = 0.3$.

**(i) Equivalent round diameter [2 marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_e &= \dfrac{1.30 \,(a \cdot b)^{0.625}}{(a + b)^{0.25}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{1.30 \,(0.6 \times 0.4)^{0.625}}{(0.6 + 0.4)^{0.25}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{1.30 \,(0.24)^{0.625}}{(1.0)^{0.25}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{1.30 \times 0.4058}{1.0} \\[6pt]
&= 0.5275 \, \text{m} \approx 528 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Air velocity [2 marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= a \cdot b = 0.6 \times 0.4 = 0.24 \, \text{m}^2 \\[4pt]
V &= \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{2.5}{0.24} = 10.42 \, \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

⚠️ *Note: This velocity is **high** — exceeds typical supply duct recommendation. Designer should consider increasing duct size.*

**(iii) Velocity pressure [1.5 marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_v &= \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2} = \dfrac{1.2 \times (10.42)^2}{2} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{1.2 \times 108.6}{2} = 65.14 \, \text{Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Dynamic loss at elbow [1.5 marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P_{dyn} &= C_o \cdot P_v = 0.3 \times 65.14 \\[4pt]
&= 19.54 \, \text{Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Bullets — Air Distribution & Duct Design**

- ⭐ **Three design methods:** Equal Velocity (rare), Equal Friction (most common), Static Regain (best for large systems).
- ⭐ **Index run** = longest run from fan to farthest outlet. Always design this first.
- ⭐ **Friction rate** $R$ typically **0.6 to 1.2 Pa/m** for AC ducts.
- ⭐ **Recommended velocities:** Main 6–9 m/s, Branch 4.5–7 m/s, Near diffuser 3–5 m/s.
- ⭐ **Equivalent diameter formula:** $D_e = 1.30(ab)^{0.625}/(a+b)^{0.25}$ — remember **1.30**.
- ⭐ **Aspect ratio** for rectangular ducts should be **≤ 4** (supply) and **≤ 6** (return).
- ⭐ **Static Regain** = $P_{v,up} - P_{v,down}$, applied with efficiency 0.5 to 0.75.
- ⭐ **Velocity pressure** $P_v = 0.5 \rho V^2$; with $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³, $P_v = 0.6 V^2$.
- ⭐ **Dynamic loss** at a fitting: $\Delta P_{dyn} = C_o \cdot P_v$.
- ⭐ **Air density at 20 °C sea level** = **1.20 kg/m³** (use **1.20** unless stated otherwise).
- ⭐ **Standard round duct sizes:** 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 350, 400, 450, 500, 550, 600, 700, 800, 900, 1000 mm.
- ⭐ **Fan External Static Pressure (ESP)** = sum of all friction + dynamic + coil + filter losses.
- ⭐ **Continuity at branch:** $Q_{up} = Q_{down} + Q_{branch}$.
- ⭐ **Round ducts save ~15–30 % fan power** compared to rectangular ducts of same cross-section.
- ⭐ Always re-verify velocity after adopting standard size; iterate if outside limits.
- ⭐ In **KTU exams**, show all steps, draw duct schematic, label all dimensions with units, and conclude with a **summary table**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Humidifiers and Dehumidifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Humidifiers and Dehumidifiers # 🧊 MODULE 3 — HUMIDIFIERS AND DEHUMIDIFIERS ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Humidifier:** A device that **adds moisture (water vapour)** to an air stream, thereby increasing its **humidity ratio ($W$)** and **relative humidity ($\phi$)** while generally lowering its dry-bulb temperature (due to evaporative cooling). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Dehumidifier:** A device that **removes moisture (water vapour)** from an air stream, thereby decreasing its **humidity ratio ($W$)** and **relative humidity ($\phi$)**. It is an essential component wherever latent heat loads must be neutralised (e.g., cold rooms, museums, pharmaceutical plants). > [!NOTE] > **Why study both together?** > KTU groups them in the same module because they represent the two complementary **latent-load handling processes** in any central/secondary air-conditioning system. Every comfort or process AC application uses at least one of these. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Thirsty Air" 🍃 Imagine a dry sponge travelling through a pipe: - A **humidifier** is like *dripping water onto the sponge* — it soaks up vapour and becomes moist. - A **dehumidifier** is like *squeezing the wet sponge* — the vapour is wrung out and the air becomes dry. In real engineering terms: - Humidification is **evaporative mass transfer** (water → vapour). - Dehumidification is **condensative mass transfer** (vapour → water) or **absorption** (vapour captured by a desiccant). > [!TIP] > The International Standard reference temperature is **0 °C (273.15 K)** and pressure is **101.325 kPa (1 atm)**. All psychrometric equations in this module use these as boundary conditions unless otherwise stated. ### 1.3 Classification Snapshot (KTU High-Yield) | Direction | Method Category | Typical Equipment | |-----------|-----------------|-------------------| | Humidification | Steam injection | Hospital / clean-room steam humidifier | | Humidification | Water spray | Air washer, Spray chamber | | Humidification | Heated pan | Pan-type humidifier | | Humidification | Wetted element | Cell-type humidifier | | Humidification | Atomising | Centrifugal / ultrasonic | | Dehumidification | Cooling & condensation | Cooling coil (DX / chilled water) | | Dehumidification | Absorption (chemical) | Lithium-chloride wheel | | Dehumidification | Adsorption (physical) | Silica-gel / molecular-sieve rotor | | Dehumidification | Compression | Vapour-compression dehumidifier | | Dehumidification | Spray washer | Adiabatic contact dehumidifier | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Working Principles — Step-by-Step Logic #### A. Adiabatic (Evaporative) Humidification 1. Warm, dry air enters the chamber. 2. A fine spray of water at a temperature **close to the air's wet-bulb temperature** is injected. 3. Latent heat required for evaporation comes *entirely* from the air's sensible heat. 4. The dry-bulb temperature **falls**, but the wet-bulb temperature remains **nearly constant**. 5. Humidity ratio $W$ rises; enthalpy $h$ is conserved. > This is the **isothermal-line** to **constant-wet-bulb-line** process on the psychrometric chart. #### B. Steam Humidification 1. Live low-pressure dry steam is injected into the duct. 2. Steam carries both **latent heat ($h_{fg} \approx 2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$ at 100 °C)** and sensible heat. 3. The air's dry-bulb temperature **rises** and humidity ratio **rises**. 4. No evaporative cooling occurs. > Steam humidification is preferred when **sterility, no cooling, and precise control** are required (hospitals, pharma, electronics). #### C. Heated-Pan Humidifier 1. A shallow pan of water is heated by electricity or hot water. 2. Vapour rises from the water surface and is entrained in the air. 3. The air gains both sensible and latent heat. > Disadvantage: slow response and high scaling — mostly used in residential furnaces. #### D. Wetted-Element / Cell-Type Humidifier 1. A porous element (aluminum, cellulose, fiberglass) is fed with water. 2. Air is forced through the wet element; evaporation occurs. 3. Air leaves near saturation; can be combined with a heater to control final DBT. #### E. Atomising / Centrifugal / Ultrasonic Humidifier 1. Water is broken into very fine droplets. 2. High surface-area droplets evaporate almost instantly. 3. Droplet diameter: ultrasonic $\approx \mathbf{1\text{–}5 \mu m}$ ; centrifugal $\approx \mathbf{10\text{–}50 \mu m}$. --- #### F. Cooling-Coil Dehumidification 1. Air is passed over a coil whose surface temperature is **below the dew-point** of the entering air. 2. Vapour condenses on the coil; the condensate is drained. 3. Both DBT and WBT fall, but humidity ratio $W$ also falls. > The **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)** of the coil is the imaginary surface temperature that, if air were brought into perfect contact with it, would result in the actual leaving state — the gap is described by the **Bypass Factor (BPF)**. #### G. Absorption (Chemical) Dehumidification 1. Air passes over a hygroscopic liquid (e.g., LiBr, triethylene glycol). 2. The liquid absorbs vapour; the solution is then regenerated in a separate heater. 3. Typical liquids: **Lithium Bromide (LiBr)**, **Calcium Chloride (CaCl₂)**, **Lithium Chloride (LiCl)**. #### H. Adsorption (Physical) Dehumidification 1. Air passes through a rotor coated with **silica gel** or a **molecular sieve** (zeolite 3Å, 4Å, 13X). 2. Vapour is trapped in the pore structure. 3. A hot regeneration stream (reactivation air) drives off the captured moisture. 4. Typical rotor speed: **6 – 20 rev/hr**. #### I. Compression Dehumidification 1. Vapour-laden air is compressed → partial pressure of vapour increases. 2. Air is cooled below its new (higher) dew point → condensation occurs. 3. Energy-intensive; used only for low-volume, low-temperature applications. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | |---|---------|-------------| | 1 | $W = 0.622 \dfrac{P_v}{P_t - P_v}$ | Humidity ratio from partial pressure of vapour | | 2 | $h = 1.005\,T + W\,(2501 + 1.88\,T)$ | Enthalpy of moist air (kJ/kg da), T in °C | | 3 | $\dot{m}_w = \dot{m}_a(W_2 - W_1)$ | Mass of water added per kg of dry air | | 4 | $W_2 - W_1 = \dfrac{c_{pw}(T_{w1} - T_{w2})}{h_{fg,2}}$ | Adiabatic-saturation relation (recirculating spray) | | 5 | $W_2 - W_1 = \dfrac{c_p(T_1 - T_2)}{h_{fg,2}}$ | Air washer (evaporative cooling) – energy balance | | 6 | $\dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1 - T_{w1}} = \dfrac{W_2 - W_1}{W_{s1} - W_1}$ | Combined heat-and-mass transfer in air washer | | 7 | $\dot{m}_w \, h_{fg} = \dot{m}_a (h_1 - h_2)$ | Energy balance for adiabatic humidifier | | 8 | $BPF = \dfrac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}}$ | Bypass factor of cooling coil (sensible basis) | | 9 | $CF = 1 - BPF$ | Contact factor of cooling coil | | 10 | $\eta_{satur} = \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1 - T_{s,1}}$ | Saturation efficiency of air washer | | 11 | $\dfrac{h_1 - h_2}{h_1 - h_s} = \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1 - T_s}$ | Spray washer contact factor equivalence | > [!NOTE] > **Units: $T$ in °C, $W$ in kg-water/kg-da, $h$ in kJ/kg-da, $P$ in kPa.** > **Constants: $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K (dry air), $c_{pw} = 4.186$ kJ/kg·K (water), $h_{fg} \approx 2501$ kJ/kg at 0 °C.** ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Comfort AC:** Dehumidification of monsoon air using chilled-water coils. - **Pharma & Electronics:** Steam humidification at **35–45 % RH** to prevent ESD. - **Cold Storage:** Dehumidification prevents frost, microbial growth, and product weight loss. - **Data Centres:** Adsorption wheels maintain **40 % RH** strictly to avoid condensation on circuit boards. - **Textile / Paper:** Spray-type humidifiers keep fibres pliable to reduce breakage. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — Adiabatic (Evaporative) Humidification Mass & Energy Balance Consider an **adiabatic humidifier** with a single spray of water at temperature $T_{w1}$, where the water fully evaporates. Let the entering air be at state 1 (DBT $T_1$, humidity ratio $W_1$, enthalpy $h_1$) and the leaving air be at state 2 ($T_2$, $W_2$, $h_2$). **Step 1 — Mass balance on water (per kg of dry air):** $$ \dot{m}_{w,\text{in}} + \dot{m}_a W_1 = \dot{m}_a W_2 $$ Since all the water evaporates into the same kg of dry air, the mass of water added is: $$ \dot{m}_{w,\text{add}} = \dot{m}_a (W_2 - W_1) $$ **Step 2 — Energy balance (steady, adiabatic, no shaft work):** The enthalpy carried in by the dry air plus the enthalpy of the water (sensible + latent contributions) must equal the enthalpy leaving as moist air. $$ \dot{m}_a h_1 + \dot{m}_a (W_2 - W_1)\, h_{f,w1} = \dot{m}_a h_2 $$ where $h_{f,w1}$ is the enthalpy of the spray water (≈ $c_{pw} T_{w1}$ in kJ/kg). **Step 3 — Subtract $h_1$ from both sides and rearrange:** $$ h_2 - h_1 = (W_2 - W_1)\, h_{f,w1} $$ **Step 4 — Expand $h_1$ and $h_2$ using the standard moist-air equation:** $$ \begin{aligned} h_1 &= 1.005\,T_1 + W_1(2501 + 1.88\,T_1) \\ h_2 &= 1.005\,T_2 + W_2(2501 + 1.88\,T_2) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — For a perfectly adiabatic case where $h_2 \approx h_1$ (no external heat), the energy balance simplifies to:** $$ \dot{m}_a (h_1 - h_2) = \dot{m}_a (W_2 - W_1)\, h_{fg,2} $$ where $h_{fg,2} = h_{g,2} - h_{f,2}$ is the latent heat of vaporisation evaluated at $T_2$. **Step 6 — Solving for $(W_2 - W_1)$:** $$ \boxed{\,W_2 - W_1 \;=\; \dfrac{h_1 - h_2}{h_{fg,2}} \;=\; \dfrac{c_p (T_1 - T_2)}{h_{fg,2}}\,} $$ **Step 7 — Physical interpretation:** - The air **loses** sensible heat ($T$ falls). - The air **gains** latent heat (now carried as water vapour). - The **enthalpy is conserved** along the adiabatic saturation line. --- ### 3.2 Derivation — Apparatus Dew Point (ADP) and Bypass Factor for Cooling-Coil Dehumidification Let air at state 1 enter a cooling coil. The coil surface temperature is $T_s$. If **100 % contact** occurred, the air would leave at $T_s$ saturated — this imaginary state is the **ADP**. Real air leaves at state 2 with $T_2 > T_s$. **Step 1 — Define Bypass Factor on sensible-temperature basis:** $$ BPF = \dfrac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}} $$ **Step 2 — Define Contact Factor:** $$ CF = 1 - BPF = \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1 - T_{ADP}} $$ **Step 3 — In a cooling coil, the ADP is essentially the saturation temperature corresponding to the leaving humidity ratio $W_2$:** $$ T_{ADP} = T_{sat} \big|_{W = W_2} $$ **Step 4 — Energy balance on coil (steady state):** $$ \dot{m}_a (h_1 - h_2) = \dot{Q}_{coil} $$ **Step 5 — Express $\dot{Q}_{coil}$ in terms of the coil surface area $A$ and overall heat-transfer coefficient $U$:** $$ \dot{Q}_{coil} = U A \,(T_{ADP} - T_{avg,\,air}) \quad \text{(LMTD form, simplified)} $$ **Step 6 — Substitute $T_2 = T_1 - CF\,(T_1 - T_{ADP})$ into the energy balance and solve for required $A$:** $$ \boxed{\,A \;=\; \dfrac{\dot{m}_a\, c_p\,(T_1 - T_2)}{U\,(T_{ADP} - T_{m})}\,} $$ **Step 7 — Typical KTU BPF range:** Direct-expansion coil = **0.10 – 0.20**, chilled-water coil = **0.20 – 0.40**. --- ### 3.3 Derivation — Spray-Washer (Air-Washer) Saturation Efficiency In a recirculating spray washer, the spray water is recirculated. After sufficient time, the spray water temperature approaches the **thermodynamic wet-bulb** of the air. **Step 1 — Mass balance (steady):** $$ \dot{m}_{w,\text{make-up}} = \dot{m}_a (W_2 - W_1) $$ **Step 2 — Energy balance (adiabatic — no heat loss to ambient):** $$ \dot{m}_a h_1 + \dot{m}_{w,m}\, c_{pw}\, T_{w1} = \dot{m}_a h_2 $$ Since the make-up water is small, neglect its enthalpy. The result is the **adiabatic saturation relation**: $$ \boxed{\,W_2 - W_1 \;=\; \dfrac{c_p\,(T_1 - T_2)}{h_{fg,2}}\,} $$ **Step 3 — Define saturation efficiency $\eta_{sat}$:** $$ \eta_{sat} = \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1 - T_{s,1}} $$ where $T_{s,1}$ is the saturation temperature at $W_1$. **Step 4 — Apply to design problem:** If $\eta_{sat}$ is specified, the leaving DBT becomes: $$ T_2 = T_1 - \eta_{sat}(T_1 - T_{s,1}) $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** > Many students confuse **wet-bulb temperature (WBT)** with **adiabatic-saturation temperature (AST)**. For air–water systems they are *numerically* almost identical (within ±0.5 °C), but **thermodynamically distinct**. Use AST in air-washer energy balance; use WBT in evaporative surface calculations. --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical — KTU Board Style > **Problem:** Air at 38 °C DBT and 25 °C WBT is passed through an adiabatic air washer. The spray water is recirculated and reaches thermodynamic equilibrium. Determine the leaving DBT and humidity ratio. Take atmospheric pressure = **101.325 kPa**. Assume $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $h_{fg,2} = 2501$ kJ/kg. **Step 1 — For an air washer with recirculated spray, the leaving air reaches the adiabatic saturation temperature, which for an air-water system ≈ WBT of the entering air.** $$ T_2 \approx T_{wb,1} = 25\ ^\circ\text{C} $$ **Step 2 — At $T_2 = 25\ ^\circ\text{C}$ saturated, look up saturation pressure of water.** $$ P_{vs}(25^\circ\text{C}) = 3.169\ \text{kPa} $$ **Step 3 — Compute $W_2$ (saturated):** $$ W_2 = 0.622 \times \dfrac{3.169}{101.325 - 3.169} = 0.02007\ \text{kg/kg da} $$ **Step 4 — Energy balance (check):** $$ W_2 - W_1 = \dfrac{1.005\,(38 - 25)}{2501} = 0.00522\ \text{kg/kg da} $$ **Step 5 — Back-calculate $W_1$:** $$ W_1 = W_2 - 0.00522 = 0.02007 - 0.00522 = 0.01485\ \text{kg/kg da} $$ **Step 6 — Final answer summary:** | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Leaving DBT, $T_2$ | **25 °C** | | Leaving W, $W_2$ | **0.0201 kg/kg da** | | Humidity added, $\Delta W$ | **0.00522 kg/kg da** | | Cooling achieved, $\Delta T$ | **13 °C** | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Adiabatic Air Washer (Humidifier) ```mermaid flowchart LR A1["Inlet Air
State 1 DBT T1 WBT Wb1
Humidity W1"] --> F1["F1 Pre Filter
Removes Dust"] F1 --> C1["C1 Spray Chamber
Recirculated Water
from Pump P1"] C1 --> SLU["S1 Spray Nozzle Array
Droplet 50 to 200 micron"] SLU --> M1["M1 Eliminator Plates
Drops Removal"] M1 --> A2["Outlet Air
State 2 DBT T2 WBT Wb1
Humidity W2 Greater"] T1["T1 Sump Tank
Capacity V litres"] --> PUMP["P1 Centrifugal Pump"] PUMP --> SLU T1 --- DR1["D1 Drain with Float Valve"] ``` ### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Cooling-Coil Dehumidifier ```mermaid flowchart LR A1["Inlet Air
DBT 32 C WBT 28 C
W1 0.0218 kgkg da"] --> F1["F1 Filter"] F1 --> COIL["DX Cooling Coil
ADP 10 C
BPF 0.15"] COIL --> COND["D1 Condensate Drain
and Trap"] COIL --> A2["Outlet Air
DBT 14 C WBT 13.5 C
W2 0.0098 kgkg da"] A2 --> HEAT["Optional Reheat Coil
to 24 C DBT"] HEAT --> A3["Supply Air
W2 same as A2
DBT 24 C"] ``` ### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Adsorption (Desiccant) Wheel Dehumidifier ```mermaid flowchart LR PA["Process Air
W1 High RH"] --> WH1["W1 Process Half
of Rotor"] WH1 --> OUT1["Dry Process Air
W2 Very Low"] RA["Reactivation Air
Heated to 130 C"] --> WH2["W2 Reactivation
Half of Rotor"] WH2 --> EXH["Moist Exhaust Air
Carries Adsorbed Vapour"] WH1 --- ROT["Rotor
Silica Gel or 3A Zeolite
6 to 20 rev per hr"] WH2 --- ROT ``` ### 4.4 Comparison Matrix — Humidifier vs Dehumidifier ```mermaid flowchart TD Q{"Process Air
Condition?"} Q -->|"RH Less than Setpoint"| H["Humidification
Add Moisture"] Q -->|"RH Greater than Setpoint"| D["Dehumidification
Remove Moisture"] H --> H1["Adiabatic Spray"] H --> H2["Steam Injection"] H --> H3["Heated Pan"] D --> D1["Cooling Coil Below DP"] D --> D2["Desiccant Wheel"] D --> D3["Spray with Cold Water"] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📘 PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Direct / Short Answer) #### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]** *Distinguish between adiabatic humidification and steam humidification. In which application would you prefer the latter and why?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Adiabatic Humidification | Steam Humidification | |---------|--------------------------|----------------------| | Heat source | Sensible heat of air (cooling) | Latent heat of steam (heating) | | DBT change | Decreases | Increases | | WBT change | Almost constant | Increases slightly | | Air enthalpy | Almost constant | Increases | | Sterility | Not sterile | Sterile | | Typical use | Industrial, textile, comfort AC | Hospitals, pharma, clean rooms | Steam humidification is preferred in **hospitals and clean rooms** because it is **sterile, controllable and does not cool the air**. --- #### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]** *Define Bypass Factor (BPF) and Contact Factor (CF) of a cooling coil. How is BPF related to coil performance?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Bypass Factor (BPF):** The fraction of the total temperature difference between entering air and the Apparatus Dew Point (ADP) that is *not* removed by the coil. > > $BPF = \dfrac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}}$ > > **Contact Factor (CF):** Fraction of the total temperature difference that *is* removed. > > $CF = 1 - BPF = \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1 - T_{ADP}}$ > > A **low BPF (≤ 0.1)** indicates good coil effectiveness and low carry-over of untreated air; a **high BPF (> 0.4)** indicates poor coil contact, slime build-up, or insufficient rows. --- ### 📗 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice — Choose ONE) --- #### ✅ **Question A (14 marks) — Air Washer / Adiabatic Humidifier** > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]** > > **(a)** Derive the energy-balance equation for an adiabatic air washer with recirculated spray and show that the leaving DBT equals the wet-bulb temperature of the entering air. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Outside air at **35 °C DBT** and **40 % RH** is to be conditioned by an adiabatic air washer using recirculated water. Atmospheric pressure = **101.325 kPa**. Find: > - (i) Humidity ratio of entering air, $W_1$ > - (ii) Humidity ratio of leaving air, $W_2$ > - (iii) Mass of water evaporated per kg of dry air > - (iv) Saturation efficiency if the leaving DBT is 24 °C **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** Energy balance per kg of dry air (steady, adiabatic): $$ \dot{m}_a h_1 = \dot{m}_a h_2 + \dot{m}_a(W_2 - W_1)\,h_{f,2} $$ Since the water entering as spray is at $T_{w1} \approx T_{wbt,1}$, its enthalpy equals $c_{pw} T_{wbt,1}$. With no external heat input/output and recirculation equilibrium: $$ h_1 - h_2 = (W_2 - W_1)\,h_{fg,2} $$ Expanding $h$: $$ 1.005\,T_1 + W_1(2501 + 1.88\,T_1) - 1.005\,T_2 - W_2(2501 + 1.88\,T_2) = (W_2 - W_1)\,h_{fg,2} $$ For a recirculated spray in thermal equilibrium, $T_2 \rightarrow T_{wbt,1}$ and $W_2 \rightarrow W_{s,2}$ (saturation at $T_2$). Thus the leaving DBT equals the entering WBT. **Hence proved.** **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** *Step 1 — Saturation pressure at 35 °C:* $$ P_{vs}(35^\circ\text{C}) = 5.628\ \text{kPa} $$ *Step 2 — Partial pressure of vapour:* $$ P_v = 0.40 \times 5.628 = 2.251\ \text{kPa} $$ *Step 3 — Humidity ratio $W_1$:* $$ W_1 = 0.622 \times \dfrac{2.251}{101.325 - 2.251} = 0.01413\ \text{kg/kg da} $$ *Step 4 — At leaving DBT = 24 °C (assume near-saturation, $W_2 \approx W_{s,24}$):* $$ P_{vs}(24^\circ\text{C}) = 2.985\ \text{kPa} $$ $$ W_2 = 0.622 \times \dfrac{2.985}{101.325 - 2.985} = 0.01887\ \text{kg/kg da} $$ *Step 5 — Mass of water evaporated:* $$ \boxed{\,\dot{m}_w/\dot{m}_a = W_2 - W_1 = 0.00474\ \text{kg water / kg da}\,} $$ *Step 6 — Saturation temperature at $W_1$:* From $P_{vs}(T_{s,1}) = 2.251$ kPa → $T_{s,1} \approx 19.2\ ^\circ\text{C}$ *Step 7 — Saturation efficiency:* $$ \eta_{sat} = \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1 - T_{s,1}} = \dfrac{35 - 24}{35 - 19.2} = \dfrac{11}{15.8} = 0.696 $$ $$ \boxed{\,\eta_{sat} \approx 69.6\ \%\,} $$ --- #### ✅ **Question B (14 marks) — Cooling-Coil Dehumidifier (Alternative Choice)** > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]** > > **(a)** Explain, with a neat sketch, the working of a **cooling-coil dehumidifier**. Define the terms **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)** and **Bypass Factor (BPF)**. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Air at **30 °C DBT** and **22 °C WBT** passes over a cooling coil whose surface temperature (ADP) is **8 °C**. If the bypass factor is **0.20**, determine: > - (i) Leaving DBT > - (ii) Leaving WBT > - (iii) Heat removed per kg of dry air > - (iv) Moisture condensed per kg of dry air **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Theory (7 marks):** A **cooling-coil dehumidifier** consists of a finned-tube heat exchanger through which a refrigerant (DX) or chilled water circulates. Air forced across the coil is cooled. When the coil surface temperature falls **below the dew-point of the entering air**, water vapour condenses on the fins and drips into a condensate pan. - **ADP (Apparatus Dew Point):** The saturation temperature corresponding to the leaving humidity ratio; equivalently, the temperature to which the air would have been cooled **if 100 % thermal contact** with the coil had occurred. - **BPF (Bypass Factor):** Ratio of uncontacted (bypassed) temperature difference to total available temperature difference; numerically $0$ for a perfect coil and $1$ for a non-functional coil. **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** *Step 1 — Leaving DBT:* $$ T_2 = T_1 - CF\,(T_1 - T_{ADP}) = T_1 - (1 - BPF)\,(T_1 - T_{ADP}) $$ $$ T_2 = 30 - (0.80)\,(30 - 8) = 30 - 17.6 = 12.4\ ^\circ\text{C} $$ *Step 2 — $W_1$ at 30 °C DBT, 22 °C WBT:* Using psychrometric chart or standard table: $W_1 = 0.01437\ \text{kg/kg da}$, $h_1 = 64.1\ \text{kJ/kg da}$. *Step 3 — Leaving WBT:* For a cooling coil, the WBT also follows the bypass factor (approximately, on a non-saturated basis): $$ T_{wb,2} = T_{wb,1} - (1 - BPF)\,(T_{wb,1} - T_{ADP,wb}) $$ ADP on WBT basis ≈ $T_{ADP} = 8\ ^\circ\text{C}$ (assuming WBT of saturated air at 8 °C ≈ 8 °C): $$ T_{wb,2} = 22 - 0.80\,(22 - 8) = 22 - 11.2 = 10.8\ ^\circ\text{C} $$ *Step 4 — Leaving enthalpy $h_2$ at DBT = 12.4 °C, WBT = 10.8 °C:* From standard tables: $h_2 \approx 31.4\ \text{kJ/kg da}$ and $W_2 \approx 0.00785\ \text{kg/kg da}$. *Step 5 — Heat removed per kg of dry air:* $$ \boxed{\,q = h_1 - h_2 = 64.1 - 31.4 = 32.7\ \text{kJ/kg da}\,} $$ *Step 6 — Moisture condensed per kg of dry air:* $$ \boxed{\,m_{cond}/\dot{m}_a = W_1 - W_2 = 0.01437 - 0.00785 = 0.00652\ \text{kg water / kg da}\,} $$ --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students LOSE marks in Humidifier/Dehumidifier problems:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to K** in temperature-difference terms (it cancels, but the working shows examiner your understanding). > 2. **Confusing WBT and AST** — always state which you are using and why. > 3. **Neglecting spray-water enthalpy** in the energy balance when asked for *exact* values. > 4. **Using BPF on the DBT basis for an *unmixed* by-pass** — a real coil has a complex local BPF; for KTU assume **uniform by-pass** unless stated. > 5. **No unit on $\Delta W$** — examiners will deduct 0.5 mark for missing kg/kg da. > 6. **Skipping the assumption list** (steady state, adiabatic, single kg da, atmospheric pressure) — even if the answer is right, an unmarked assumption panel costs 1 mark. > 7. **In steam humidification** — forgetting to add the sensible heat of steam at 100 °C ($4.186 \times 100 = 418.6$ kJ/kg) when computing total heat input. --- ### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✔️ **Humidification** = adds water; **Dehumidification** = removes water. Both change the **humidity ratio $W$**. - ✔️ Adiabatic humidification → **constant WBT**, **falling DBT**, **constant $h$**. - ✔️ Steam humidification → **rising DBT**, **rising $W$**, **rising $h$**. - ✔️ Cooling-coil dehumidification works **only if coil T < dew-point** of entering air. - ✔️ **BPF + CF = 1** (always). - ✔️ **Saturation efficiency $\eta_{sat} \le 1$**; typical good air washer = 0.80 – 0.95. - ✔️ Standard air-washer relation: $W_2 - W_1 = c_p(T_1 - T_2)/h_{fg,2}$. - ✔️ **Desiccant wheels** use **silica gel or 3 Å molecular sieve**; rotor speed 6–20 rev/hr. - ✔️ **Absorbents** (LiBr, LiCl, glycols) are *liquid*; **Adsorbents** (silica, zeolites) are *solid porous*. - ✔️ Always specify: pressure = **101.325 kPa**, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $h_{fg} = 2501$ kJ/kg (0 °C). - ✔️ Reheating **after** cooling-coil dehumidification is mandatory for comfort AC (to supply air at ~24 °C DBT, low $W$). - ✔️ KTU loves numericals from air-washers and cooling coils — practice plotting the process on the **psychrometric chart** and identifying ADP, BPF, and saturation efficiency. > 📌 **KTU 2024 Weightage Hint (Module 3):** Typical 14-mark question on **Air Washer** OR **Cooling-Coil Dehumidifier** appears every semester. Two 3-mark questions on **definitions** of BPF, ADP, CF, and saturation efficiency are *almost guaranteed*.

topics.title: Cooling Load Calculations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cooling Load Calculations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Cooling Load** is defined as the *rate at which heat energy must be removed from a conditioned space* in order to maintain the specified indoor design conditions of air temperature, relative humidity, and air purity. It is measured in **kW** (SI) or **TR (Tonnes of Refrigeration)**, where **1 TR = 3.517 kW**.

Mathematically, the cooling load is the time-derivative of the total instantaneous heat gain from all internal and external sources:

$$Q_{CL} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(\frac{dQ_i}{dt}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** There is a fundamental difference between **Heat Gain** and **Cooling Load**.
> - **Heat Gain** ($Q_g$): Instantaneous rate of heat entering the space (radiative + convective).
> - **Cooling Load** ($Q_{CL}$): Rate at which heat must be *removed* by the AC system. It accounts for the **thermal storage effect** of the building mass (walls, furniture), which delays and attenuates the convective contribution.
> - Therefore: $Q_{CL} \leq Q_{g}$ for the same instant, due to the time lag and decrement factor of opaque surfaces.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Human Body in Kerala's Summer"

Imagine you (a human body) standing in **Thiruvananthapuram** during April at 2:00 PM:
- The **sun's radiation** striking your skin is the *solar heat gain through glass*.
- The **hot air outside** leaking in through door gaps is *infiltration*.
- The **body heat** from people around you is the *occupant load*.
- The **heat from your phone and laptop** is the *equipment load*.
- The **roof above you**, though warmed by the sun, does not transfer heat instantly — it stores it. This delay is the **CLTD effect**.

Your body's "cooling load" is the sweat and the fan working together to remove all of this. The AC system does exactly the same — it senses the *net* rate of heat accumulation and removes it. **That net rate is the cooling load.**

### 1.3 Classification of Cooling Loads

Cooling loads are classified based on the *form* of heat they carry:

| Load Type | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Caused By |
|-----------|--------|------------------|-----------|
| Sensible Heat Load | $Q_s$ | Causes temperature rise | Walls, glass conduction, occupants (dry), lights |
| Latent Heat Load | $Q_L$ | Causes moisture increase | Occupants (perspiration), infiltration humidity, processes |
| Total Cooling Load | $Q_T$ | Combined effect | All sources |

The **Sensible Heat Factor (SHF)** is a critical design parameter:

$$SHF = \frac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_L} = \frac{Q_s}{Q_T}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Knowing SHF helps in selecting the appropriate **air-handling unit (AHU) coil** and determining the **bypass factor (BPF)** of the cooling coil. For typical comfort applications in Kerala, SHF ranges between **0.65 to 0.85**.

### 1.4 Key Engineering Constants for Cooling Load

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Values used in KTU Examinations:**
> - Specific heat of air, $c_p$ = **1.005 kJ/kg·K**
> - Latent heat of vaporization at 0°C, $h_{fg}$ = **2501 kJ/kg**
> - Density of air at standard conditions, $\rho$ = **1.2 kg/m³**
> - 1 TR (Tonne of Refrigeration) = **3.517 kW** = **210 kJ/min** = **12000 BTU/hr**
> - Specific volume of air, $v$ = **0.833 m³/kg**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven Sources of Heat Gain (KTU 2024 Framework)

A complete cooling load calculation systematically evaluates the heat gain from **seven primary sources**, often remembered by the mnemonic **"WGLIEV + O"**:

1. **W**alls, **R**oof, **F**loor (Opaque Construction)
2. **G**lass (Windows, Skylights)
3. **L**ights
4. **I**nfiltration
5. **E**quipment and Appliances
6. **V**entilation
7. **O**ccupants

### 2.2 Detailed Source-wise Analysis

#### Source 1: Heat Gain Through Opaque Construction (Walls, Roof, Floor)

Heat is transferred by **conduction** through the building envelope. The Cooling Load Temperature Difference (CLTD) method is the KTU/ASHRAE standard.

$$Q_1 = U \times A \times \text{CLTD} \quad [\text{W}]$$

Where:
- $U$ = Overall heat transfer coefficient **(W/m²K)** — depends on material, thickness, insulation.
- $A$ = Surface area exposed to outside **(m²)**.
- CLTD = Cooling Load Temperature Difference **(K or °C)** — obtained from ASHRAE tables accounting for solar radiation, time lag, color of surface, and orientation.

> [!NOTE]
> For simplified KTU problems, when CLTD tables are not provided, the design temperature difference $\Delta T = T_o - T_i$ is used: $Q_1 = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$.

#### Source 2: Heat Gain Through Glass (Windows)

Glass has **two parallel heat transfer mechanisms**:

**(a) Conduction Component (due to outside–inside temperature difference):**
$$Q_{2,\text{cond}} = U_g \times A_g \times \Delta T$$

**(b) Solar Radiation Component (the dominant load in tropics):**
$$Q_{2,\text{solar}} = A_g \times \text{SHGF} \times \text{CLF}$$

Or using the modern **SHGC (Solar Heat Gain Coefficient)** approach:
$$Q_{2,\text{solar}} = A_g \times I \times \text{SHGC} \times \text{CLF}$$

Where:
- $U_g$ = U-value of glass (single = 5.8, double = 2.8 W/m²K).
- SHGF = Solar Heat Gain Factor on glass **(W/m²)**.
- CLF = Cooling Load Factor (typically 0.5 to 1.0, dimensionless).
- $I$ = Incident solar intensity **(W/m²)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Many students forget that *shaded glass* uses a different SHGF than unshaded. Internal shading reduces SHGF by 50–60%, external shading by 80–90%.

#### Source 3: Heat Gain from Occupants

Humans release both **sensible** (raising temperature) and **latent** (raising humidity) heat, varying with activity level and indoor temperature.

$$Q_3 = N \times (q_s + q_L) \quad [\text{W}]$$

| Activity Level | $q_s$ (W) | $q_L$ (W) | Total (W) |
|----------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| Seated, office work | 75 | 55 | 130 |
| Walking, light work | 75 | 75 | 150 |
| Restaurant dining | 80 | 100 | 180 |
| Heavy factory work | 165 | 285 | 450 |
| Dancing | 140 | 180 | 320 |

#### Source 4: Heat Gain from Lights

$$Q_4 = W \times UF \times BF \quad [\text{W}]$$

Where:
- $W$ = Total wattage of all lamps.
- $UF$ = Use factor (1.0 for general lighting, 0.5 for local).
- $BF$ = Ballast factor (1.0 for incandescent, 1.2 for fluorescent with choke).

For incandescent bulbs, the *entire* electrical input becomes heat (100% conversion). For fluorescent tubes, ~80% becomes heat plus the ballast adds 20% extra.

#### Source 5: Heat Gain from Equipment and Appliances

$$Q_5 = P \times UF \times \text{(Motor efficiency factor)} \quad [\text{W}]$$

For motor-driven equipment:
$$Q_5 = P \times \text{Use Factor} + P \times (1 - \eta) \quad \text{(for motorized)}$$

Where $\eta$ is the motor efficiency. Common values:
- Electric stove: 1500–2000 W per burner
- Computer: 150–300 W
- Photocopier: 1000–1500 W (intermittent)
- Refrigerator in room: 300–800 W (net after considering its own cooling)

#### Source 6: Infiltration Load

Uncontrolled air leakage through cracks, doors, and openings.

$$Q_6 = \rho \times V \times \Delta h \quad [\text{W}] = 1.2 \times V \times \Delta h \quad [\text{kW}]$$

Where $V$ is the volumetric flow rate of infiltrated air in m³/s and $\Delta h$ is the enthalpy difference (kJ/kg) between outside and inside air.

**Quick rule:** $Q_6 = 0.018 \times V \times \Delta h$ BTU/hr (when $V$ in CFM, $\Delta h$ in Btu/lb).

Number of air changes method: $V = \text{Air Changes per Hour} \times \text{Room Volume} / 3600$.

Typical air changes: Tight room = 0.5 ACH, Loose room = 1.5–2.0 ACH.

#### Source 7: Ventilation Load (Fresh Air)

$$Q_7 = \rho \times V_{OA} \times \Delta h \quad [\text{W}]$$

The minimum outdoor air requirement is mandated by ASHRAE 62.1: 8.5 L/s per person + 0.6 L/s per m² for offices.

### 2.3 The Master Formula

$$\boxed{Q_{\text{Total}} = Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3 + Q_4 + Q_5 + Q_6 + Q_7 \quad [\text{kW}]}$$

$$\text{Equipment Capacity (TR)} = \frac{Q_{\text{Total}}}{3.517} \times \text{Safety Factor (1.1 to 1.2)}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Source | Formula | Units | Critical Variables |
|---|--------|---------|-------|-------------------|
| 1 | Opaque wall | $Q_1 = U \cdot A \cdot \text{CLTD}$ | W | $U$, $A$, CLTD |
| 2a | Glass conduction | $Q_{2a} = U_g \cdot A_g \cdot \Delta T$ | W | $U_g$, $A_g$, $\Delta T$ |
| 2b | Glass solar | $Q_{2b} = A_g \cdot \text{SHGF} \cdot \text{CLF}$ | W | SHGF, CLF |
| 3 | Occupants | $Q_3 = N \cdot (q_s + q_L)$ | W | $N$, activity |
| 4 | Lights | $Q_4 = W \cdot UF \cdot BF$ | W | $W$, UF, BF |
| 5 | Equipment | $Q_5 = P \cdot UF$ | W | $P$, UF |
| 6 | Infiltration | $Q_6 = \rho \cdot V \cdot \Delta h$ | W | $V$, $\Delta h$ |
| 7 | Ventilation | $Q_7 = \rho \cdot V_{OA} \cdot \Delta h$ | W | $V_{OA}$, $\Delta h$ |
| 8 | TR conversion | $\text{TR} = Q_T / 3.517$ | TR | Capacity |
| 9 | SHF | $\text{SHF} = Q_s / Q_T$ | — | Coil selection |
| 10 | RSHF | $\text{RSHF} = Q_s / (Q_s + Q_L)$ | — | Apparatus dew point |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Cooling load calculations are the foundation for **HVAC equipment selection**, **duct sizing**, **chiller tonnage rating**, and **energy audit** in building services engineering.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Comprehensive Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Question)

**Problem Statement:**
Calculate the total cooling load in **kW and TR** for a small air-conditioned office in Kochi with the following specifications:

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Room dimensions | 10 m × 8 m × 3.5 m (L × W × H) |
| Outside design temperature ($T_o$) | 34°C, RH 70% |
| Inside design temperature ($T_i$) | 24°C, RH 50% |
| Gross wall area (excluding glass) | 60 m² |
| Glass area (single pane, unshaded, west-facing) | 12 m² |
| Roof area (RCC with insulation) | 80 m² |
| U-value of wall | 1.5 W/m²K |
| U-value of glass | 5.8 W/m²K |
| U-value of roof | 0.8 W/m²K |
| CLTD for wall | 12 K |
| CLTD for roof | 18 K |
| SHGF for west glass | 450 W/m² |
| CLF for glass | 0.75 |
| Number of occupants | 8 (office work) |
| Total lighting load | 1200 W (fluorescent) |
| Equipment load | 2000 W |
| Infiltration air changes | 1.0 ACH |
| Minimum fresh air | 8.5 L/s per person |
| Outside air enthalpy | 92 kJ/kg |
| Inside air enthalpy | 48 kJ/kg |
| Enthalpy difference $\Delta h$ | 44 kJ/kg |

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

#### **Step 1: Heat Gain Through Opaque Walls**

Using the CLTD method:

$$Q_1 = U_w \times A_w \times \text{CLTD}_w$$

$$Q_1 = 1.5 \;\text{W/m}^2\text{K} \times 60 \;\text{m}^2 \times 12 \;\text{K}$$

$$Q_1 = 90 \times 12 = 1080 \;\text{W}$$

**Conversion logic:** $U \cdot A$ gives W/K, multiplying by temperature difference (K) yields W.

#### **Step 2: Heat Gain Through Roof**

$$Q_2 = U_r \times A_r \times \text{CLTD}_r$$

$$Q_2 = 0.8 \times 80 \times 18 = 64 \times 18 = 1152 \;\text{W}$$

#### **Step 3: Heat Gain Through Glass — Conduction Part**

$$Q_{3a} = U_g \times A_g \times \Delta T$$

$$Q_{3a} = 5.8 \times 12 \times (34 - 24) = 69.6 \times 10 = 696 \;\text{W}$$

#### **Step 4: Heat Gain Through Glass — Solar Part**

$$Q_{3b} = A_g \times \text{SHGF} \times \text{CLF}$$

$$Q_{3b} = 12 \times 450 \times 0.75 = 5400 \times 0.75 = 4050 \;\text{W}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notice:** Solar load through glass (4050 W) is the **largest single load** in tropical climates — typically 30–40% of total. This justifies the KTU emphasis on shading devices in design.

#### **Step 5: Heat Gain From Occupants**

For office work at 24°C: $q_s = 75$ W, $q_L = 55$ W (per person, total 130 W).

$$Q_4 = N \times (q_s + q_L)$$

$$Q_4 = 8 \times (75 + 55) = 8 \times 130 = 1040 \;\text{W}$$

Breaking into components:
- Sensible from occupants: $Q_{4s} = 8 \times 75 = 600$ W
- Latent from occupants: $Q_{4L} = 8 \times 55 = 440$ W

#### **Step 6: Heat Gain From Lights (Fluorescent)**

For fluorescent tubes: UF = 1.0, BF = 1.2 (ballast losses).

$$Q_5 = W \times UF \times BF$$

$$Q_5 = 1200 \times 1.0 \times 1.2 = 1440 \;\text{W}$$

#### **Step 7: Heat Gain From Equipment**

For general office equipment with UF = 0.75:

$$Q_6 = P \times UF$$

$$Q_6 = 2000 \times 0.75 = 1500 \;\text{W}$$

#### **Step 8: Infiltration Load**

Room volume: $V_{\text{room}} = 10 \times 8 \times 3.5 = 280$ m³.

Infiltration volume flow rate:

$$V_{\text{inf}} = \frac{\text{ACH} \times V_{\text{room}}}{3600} = \frac{1.0 \times 280}{3600} = 0.0778 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

Infiltration load:

$$Q_7 = \rho \times V_{\text{inf}} \times \Delta h$$

$$Q_7 = 1.2 \times 0.0778 \times 44 = 0.0934 \times 44 = 4.11 \;\text{W (incorrect — let's redo)}$$

**Correction with proper unit handling** (using $h$ in kJ/kg):

$$Q_7 = 1.2 \;\text{kg/m}^3 \times 0.0778 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s} \times 44 \;\text{kJ/kg}$$

$$Q_7 = 1.2 \times 0.0778 \times 44 \times 1000 \;\text{W (converting kJ to J)}$$

$$Q_7 = 1.2 \times 0.0778 \times 44000 = 4108 \;\text{W} \approx 4.11 \;\text{kW}$$

#### **Step 9: Ventilation (Fresh Air) Load**

$$V_{OA} = 8.5 \;\text{L/s/person} \times 8 \;\text{people} = 68 \;\text{L/s} = 0.068 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$Q_8 = \rho \times V_{OA} \times \Delta h$$

$$Q_8 = 1.2 \times 0.068 \times 44 = 0.0816 \times 44 \times 1000 = 3590 \;\text{W} \approx 3.59 \;\text{kW}$$

#### **Step 10: Sum Total Heat Gains**

| Source | Heat Gain (W) |
|--------|---------------|
| Walls ($Q_1$) | 1,080 |
| Roof ($Q_2$) | 1,152 |
| Glass conduction ($Q_{3a}$) | 696 |
| Glass solar ($Q_{3b}$) | 4,050 |
| Occupants ($Q_4$) | 1,040 |
| Lights ($Q_5$) | 1,440 |
| Equipment ($Q_6$) | 1,500 |
| Infiltration ($Q_7$) | 4,108 |
| Ventilation ($Q_8$) | 3,590 |
| **Total** | **18,656 W** |

$$Q_{\text{Total}} = 18.656 \;\text{kW}$$

#### **Step 11: Equipment Capacity in TR**

Applying safety factor of 1.15:

$$Q_{\text{design}} = 18.656 \times 1.15 = 21.45 \;\text{kW}$$

$$\text{Capacity} = \frac{21.45}{3.517} = 6.10 \;\text{TR}$$

#### **Step 12: Sensible Heat Factor Calculation**

$$Q_s = 1080 + 1152 + 696 + 4050 + 600 + 1440 + 1500 = 10,518 \;\text{W}$$

$$Q_L = 440 + (4110 - 440) \text{ latent part} + 3590 \text{ latent part}$$

**Careful re-partitioning of infiltration/ventilation:**

Infiltration and ventilation carry both sensible and latent portions proportional to their dry-bulb and humidity ratio differences. For a quick KTU approximation using the $h$-difference method (which we used), the entire $Q_7$ and $Q_8$ are split:

Dry-bulb portion (sensible):
- $\Delta T = 10$ K, so sensible fraction $= c_p \Delta T / \Delta h = (1.005 \times 10)/44 = 0.228$

Latent fraction $= 1 - 0.228 = 0.772$

- Sensible from $Q_7$: $4108 \times 0.228 = 937$ W
- Latent from $Q_7$: $4108 \times 0.772 = 3171$ W
- Sensible from $Q_8$: $3590 \times 0.228 = 819$ W
- Latent from $Q_8$: $3590 \times 0.772 = 2771$ W

$$Q_s = 1080 + 1152 + 696 + 4050 + 600 + 1440 + 1500 + 937 + 819 = 12,274 \;\text{W}$$

$$Q_L = 440 + 3171 + 2771 = 6382 \;\text{W}$$

$$Q_T = 12274 + 6382 = 18,656 \;\text{W} \;\checkmark$$

$$\text{SHF} = \frac{12274}{18656} = 0.658$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Interpretation:** SHF = 0.658 falls in the typical comfort AC range (0.65–0.85), confirming the design is for a comfort-cooling application, not a process cooling application where SHF would be much lower (0.4–0.6).

### 3.3 Summary Result Table

| Result | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Total Sensible Heat ($Q_s$) | 12,274 W (12.27 kW) |
| Total Latent Heat ($Q_L$) | 6,382 W (6.38 kW) |
| Grand Total Heat Gain | 18.66 kW |
| Design Capacity (with 15% safety) | 21.45 kW |
| Equipment Tonnage | 6.10 TR |
| Sensible Heat Factor | 0.658 |

### 3.4 KTU 2024 Concept: Room Sensible Heat Factor (RSHF)

For coil selection, the **Room Sensible Heat Factor** is used:

$$\text{RSHF} = \frac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_L} = \frac{Q_s}{Q_T} = \text{SHF}$$

And the **Effective Sensible Heat Factor (ESHF)** accounts for coil bypass:

$$\text{ESHF} = \frac{Q_s - Q_{s,\text{bypass}}}{Q_T - Q_{\text{bypass}}}$$

This is used in **psychrometric chart analysis** to plot the *room condition point* and *coil apparatus dew point*.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Sources of Cooling Load

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TotalLoad[Total Cooling Load Q Total]
    WallLoad[Heat Through Walls and Roof]
    GlassLoad[Heat Through Glass]
    OccupLoad[Heat From Occupants]
    LightLoad[Heat From Lights]
    EquipLoad[Heat From Equipment]
    InfLoad[Infiltration Load]
    VentLoad[Ventilation Load]

    WallLoad --> WallSub1[Conduction Q UxAxCLTD]
    WallLoad --> WallSub2[Storage Effect Time Lag]

    GlassLoad --> GlassSub1[Conduction Part UxAxDeltaT]
    GlassLoad --> GlassSub2[Solar Part AxSHGFxCLF]

    OccupLoad --> OccupSub1[Sensible Heat Nx qs]
    OccupLoad --> OccupSub2[Latent Heat Nx qL]

    LightLoad --> LightSub1[Incandescent Full Conversion]
    LightLoad --> LightSub2[Fluorescent With Ballast Loss]

    EquipLoad --> EquipSub1[Direct Electric Heating]
    EquipLoad --> EquipSub2[Motor Driven With Efficiency Loss]

    InfLoad --> InfSub1[Air Leakage Through Cracks]
    InfLoad --> InfSub2[Enthalpy Driven rho VxDelta h]

    VentLoad --> VentSub1[ASHRAE 62.1 Mandated]
    VentLoad --> VentSub2[Per Person Based]

    WallLoad --> TotalLoad
    GlassLoad --> TotalLoad
    OccupLoad --> TotalLoad
    LightLoad --> TotalLoad
    EquipLoad --> TotalLoad
    InfLoad --> TotalLoad
    VentLoad --> TotalLoad
```

### 4.2 Sequential Flow Diagram: Cooling Load Calculation Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start Calculation]) --> Step1[Step 1 Gather Room Geometry and Design Conditions]
    Step1 --> Step2[Step 2 Identify All External and Internal Heat Sources]
    Step2 --> Step3[Step 3 Calculate Heat Gain Through Opaque Walls UxAxCLTD]
    Step3 --> Step4[Step 4 Calculate Glass Heat Gain Conduction plus Solar]
    Step4 --> Step5[Step 5 Calculate Occupant Load N times q per person]
    Step5 --> Step6[Step 6 Calculate Lighting Load WxUFxBF]
    Step6 --> Step7[Step 7 Calculate Equipment Load PxUF]
    Step7 --> Step8[Step 8 Calculate Infiltration Load rhoVxDelta h]
    Step8 --> Step9[Step 9 Calculate Ventilation Load rhoVoxDelta h]
    Step9 --> Step10[Step 10 Sum All Sources to Get Q Total]
    Step10 --> Step11[Step 11 Apply Safety Factor 1.1 to 1.2]
    Step11 --> Step12[Step 12 Convert to TR Divide by 3.517]
    Step12 --> Step13[Step 13 Calculate SHF and RSHF for Coil Selection]
    Step13 --> End([Final Equipment Capacity])

    Step3 -.->|If CLTD tables unavailable| Fallback[Use Delta T Directly]
    Fallback --> Step4
```

### 4.3 Heat Flow Hierarchy Diagram (Sensible vs Latent Partitioning)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Cooling Load Q Total] --> B[Sensible Load Qs]
    A --> C[Latent Load QL]

    B --> B1[Wall and Roof Conduction]
    B --> B2[Glass Conduction and Solar]
    B --> B3[Occupant Sensible]
    B --> B4[Lighting]
    B --> B5[Equipment]
    B --> B6[Infiltration Sensible Portion]
    B --> B7[Ventilation Sensible Portion]

    C --> C1[Occupant Latent Perspiration]
    C --> C2[Infiltration Latent Portion]
    C --> C3[Ventilation Latent Portion]
    C --> C4[Process Sources if any]

    B --> Calc[SHF equals Qs divided by Q Total]
    C --> Calc
    Calc --> Coil[Determine Coil ADP on Psychrometric Chart]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram matters:** KTU examiners frequently test whether students can correctly *partition* total load into sensible and latent components. The SHF value directly drives the selection of the cooling coil apparatus dew point (ADP) and the bypass factor (BPF) — a recurring 7-mark question in the KTU 2024 ESE pattern.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 3 Marks]**

**Define the following terms with their significance in cooling load calculations:**
**(a) Sensible Heat Factor (SHF)**
**(b) Cooling Load Factor (CLF)**
**(c) Cooling Load Temperature Difference (CLTD)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Sensible Heat Factor (SHF):** The ratio of sensible heat load to the total cooling load. It is a dimensionless parameter used to characterize the nature of the load — whether it is more temperature-driven (high SHF) or humidity-driven (low SHF).

$$\text{SHF} = \frac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_L}$$

For comfort cooling, SHF typically lies between **0.65 and 0.85**, while for process cooling it can drop to 0.4–0.6. SHF determines the position of the **room condition point** on the psychrometric chart and influences coil selection.

**(b) Cooling Load Factor (CLF):** The ratio of the actual cooling load at a given time to the maximum possible heat gain at that instant, accounting for the time lag and thermal storage of the building. CLF is a function of:
- Time of day (solar position)
- Orientation of the surface
- Type of construction (mass)
- Internal shading

**Range:** $0 \leq \text{CLF} \leq 1$. CLF is **always ≤ 1** because storage effects reduce the instantaneous load on the AC system.

**(c) Cooling Load Temperature Difference (CLTD):** The effective temperature difference across a wall or roof that, when multiplied by U × A, gives the actual cooling load. CLTD is **not equal to $(T_o - T_i)$** because it incorporates:
- The sol-air temperature effect (incident solar radiation absorbed by the surface)
- The decrement and time-lag effects of the wall construction

$$\text{CLTD} = \text{Equiv. } \Delta T_{\text{sol-air}} - \text{Time lag correction}$$

CLTD values are tabulated for different wall types, orientations, and latitudes — typically ranging from 5 K to 25 K for comfort applications in Indian cities.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 3 Marks]**

**Enumerate the major sources of heat gain in an air-conditioned space. Which source typically dominates in tropical climates like Kerala?**

**Model Answer:**

The major sources of heat gain in an air-conditioned space are:

1. **Heat transmission through opaque construction** (walls, roof, floor) — by conduction.
2. **Heat gain through glass** (windows, partitions) — by conduction and solar radiation.
3. **Heat gain from occupants** — both sensible (metabolic) and latent (perspiration/breathing).
4. **Heat gain from lighting** — 100% of electrical input becomes heat.
5. **Heat gain from equipment and appliances** — motors, computers, kitchen equipment.
6. **Infiltration load** — uncontrolled air leakage through cracks and door openings.
7. **Ventilation (fresh air) load** — mandatory introduction of outdoor air for IAQ.

**Dominant Source in Kerala:**

In tropical coastal climates like **Kerala**, the **solar heat gain through glass** typically dominates, contributing 30–40% of the total cooling load. This is due to:
- High solar intensity (peak SHGF ≈ 450–600 W/m²)
- East- and west-facing exposures receiving intense morning/afternoon sun
- Often inadequate external shading in older buildings

**Next dominant sources** are usually the **roof load** (due to intense tropical sun on flat/terraced roofs) and **ventilation/infiltration load** (due to the high outside air enthalpy in humid coastal weather).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model]**

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** A conference room in a building in Thrissur has the following data:
- Room dimensions: 12 m × 10 m × 3.5 m
- Walls: Exposed area = 90 m², U-value = 1.8 W/m²K, CLTD = 14 K
- Roof: Area = 120 m², U-value = 0.9 W/m²K, CLTD = 20 K
- Glass windows: 20 m² area, single-pane unshaded, west-facing, U = 5.8 W/m²K, $\Delta T = 11$ K
- Solar heat: SHGF = 480 W/m², CLF = 0.7

**Calculate the total heat gain through the building envelope (walls, roof, glass).**

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** For the same room, the additional data is:
- Number of occupants: 25 people (light seated work)
- Total lighting: 1800 W (fluorescent tubes)
- Equipment: 2500 W (laptops, projector, audio system)
- Infiltration: 0.8 air changes per hour
- Outside enthalpy: 90 kJ/kg, Inside enthalpy: 48 kJ/kg

**Calculate the total cooling load in kW and TR, and determine the SHF.**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Envelope Heat Gain:**

*Walls:*
$$Q_1 = U_w \times A_w \times \text{CLTD}_w = 1.8 \times 90 \times 14 = 2268 \;\text{W}$$

*Roof:*
$$Q_2 = U_r \times A_r \times \text{CLTD}_r = 0.9 \times 120 \times 20 = 2160 \;\text{W}$$

*Glass Conduction:*
$$Q_{3a} = U_g \times A_g \times \Delta T = 5.8 \times 20 \times 11 = 1276 \;\text{W}$$

*Glass Solar:*
$$Q_{3b} = A_g \times \text{SHGF} \times \text{CLF} = 20 \times 480 \times 0.7 = 6720 \;\text{W}$$

*Total Envelope Load:*
$$Q_{\text{env}} = 2268 + 2160 + 1276 + 6720 = 12,424 \;\text{W} = 12.42 \;\text{kW}$$

**Part (b) — Remaining Loads and Total:**

*Occupants (seated office work at 24°C: $q_s = 70$ W, $q_L = 55$ W — adjusted for seated):*
$$Q_4 = 25 \times (70 + 55) = 25 \times 125 = 3125 \;\text{W}$$

*Sensible:* $25 \times 70 = 1750$ W
*Latent:* $25 \times 55 = 1375$ W

*Lighting (fluorescent, UF = 1.0, BF = 1.2):*
$$Q_5 = 1800 \times 1.0 \times 1.2 = 2160 \;\text{W}$$

*Equipment (with UF = 0.7):*
$$Q_6 = 2500 \times 0.7 = 1750 \;\text{W}$$

*Infiltration:*
Room volume: $V = 12 \times 10 \times 3.5 = 420$ m³
$$V_{\text{inf}} = \frac{0.8 \times 420}{3600} = 0.0933 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\Delta h = 90 - 48 = 42 \;\text{kJ/kg}$$

$$Q_7 = \rho \times V_{\text{inf}} \times \Delta h = 1.2 \times 0.0933 \times 42 \times 1000 = 4702 \;\text{W}$$

*Ventilation:* Not separately given; the infiltration load is taken as the only outside air load.

**Grand Total:**
$$Q_T = 12{,}424 + 3125 + 2160 + 1750 + 4702 = 24{,}161 \;\text{W} = 24.16 \;\text{kW}$$

**Sensible and Latent Partition:**

Infiltration split: Sensible fraction $= c_p \Delta T / \Delta h = (1.005 \times 11)/42 = 0.263$
- Sensible from $Q_7$: $4702 \times 0.263 = 1237$ W
- Latent from $Q_7$: $4702 \times 0.737 = 3465$ W

$$Q_s = 2268 + 2160 + 1276 + 6720 + 1750 + 2160 + 1750 + 1237 = 19{,}321 \;\text{W}$$

$$Q_L = 1375 + 3465 = 4840 \;\text{W}$$

$$\text{SHF} = \frac{19{,}321}{19{,}321 + 4840} = \frac{19{,}321}{24{,}161} = 0.80$$

**Equipment Capacity:**
$$Q_{\text{design}} = 24.16 \times 1.15 = 27.78 \;\text{kW}$$

$$\text{Capacity} = \frac{27.78}{3.517} = 7.90 \;\text{TR}$$

**Final Answer for Question A:**

| Result | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Envelope load (a) | 12.42 kW |
| Total cooling load (b) | 24.16 kW |
| Sensible Heat Factor | 0.80 |
| Equipment Capacity | 7.90 TR (with 15% safety) |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **CLTD method of cooling load calculation**. Discuss the significance of:
- Solar Heat Gain Factor (SHGF)
- Cooling Load Factor (CLF)
- Sol-air temperature
- Decrement factor and time lag

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** A west-facing single-pane glass window has the following specifications:
- Area: 6 m²
- U-value: 5.8 W/m²K
- SHGF (peak): 500 W/m²
- CLF: 0.6
- Outside design temperature: 36°C
- Inside design temperature: 24°C

**Calculate:** (i) Heat gain due to conduction through the glass. (ii) Heat gain due to solar radiation. (iii) Total instantaneous heat gain through the window. (iv) Effective cooling load assuming CLTD correction factor of 0.85.

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Theory of CLTD Method:**

The **Cooling Load Temperature Difference (CLTD) Method** is a simplified procedure developed from ASHRAE research for calculating instantaneous cooling loads from heat conduction through building envelopes.

**Procedure:**

1. **Identify the design conditions** — outside dry-bulb temperature, inside dry-bulb temperature, and the design day (typically the hottest day of the year).

2. **Compute the sol-air temperature** — the fictitious outside temperature that would produce the same heat flow as the combination of incident solar radiation and convection:
$$T_{\text{sol-air}} = T_o + \frac{\alpha \cdot I}{h_o}$$
where $\alpha$ is the surface absorptivity, $I$ is the incident solar radiation (W/m²), and $h_o$ is the outside surface film coefficient (typically 18 W/m²K).

3. **Determine CLTD** — the difference between sol-air temperature and inside air temperature, modified by the time-lag of the construction:
$$\text{CLTD} = (T_{\text{sol-air}} - T_i) \cdot \text{Decrement Factor}$$

4. **Calculate heat gain:** $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \text{CLTD}$

**Key Concepts:**

- **SHGF (Solar Heat Gain Factor):** The total solar radiation (direct + diffuse + reflected) falling on a surface of given orientation at a given hour. Values are tabulated for different latitudes, months, and orientations. Peak values in India reach 600–700 W/m² on west walls.

- **CLF (Cooling Load Factor):** The ratio of the *cooling load* to the *heat gain* at a given time, accounting for thermal storage. It is **not a constant** but varies with construction type, time, and orientation.

- **Decrement Factor:** The ratio of the temperature variation on the inner surface to that on the outer surface of a wall. For heavy walls, decrement factor is small (0.1–0.3), meaning much less heat reaches inside.

- **Time Lag:** The delay (in hours) between the peak outside temperature and the peak inside surface temperature. Heavy masonry walls have 6–10 hours lag; light walls have 1–3 hours.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The CLTD method is preferred for **manual calculations** in board exams because it eliminates the need for complex hourly heat balance computations. However, for final building design, ASHRAE's **Transfer Function Method (TFM)** or **CLF/SCL/CLTD combined method** is more accurate.

**Part (b) — Glass Window Heat Gain:**

**(i) Conduction Component:**

$$Q_{\text{cond}} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$

$$Q_{\text{cond}} = 5.8 \times 6 \times (36 - 24) = 5.8 \times 6 \times 12 = 417.6 \;\text{W}$$

**(ii) Solar Radiation Component:**

$$Q_{\text{solar}} = A \times \text{SHGF} \times \text{CLF}$$

$$Q_{\text{solar}} = 6 \times 500 \times 0.6 = 1800 \;\text{W}$$

**(iii) Total Instantaneous Heat Gain:**

$$Q_{\text{total}} = Q_{\text{cond}} + Q_{\text{solar}} = 417.6 + 1800 = 2217.6 \;\text{W}$$

**(iv) Effective Cooling Load (with CLTD correction = 0.85):**

The CLTD correction factor accounts for thermal storage in the room contents and structural mass:

$$Q_{\text{CL,eff}} = Q_{\text{total}} \times 0.85 = 2217.6 \times 0.85 = 1884.96 \;\text{W} \approx 1.88 \;\text{kW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Trap:** Students frequently forget to convert kJ/kg to J/kg in the $Q = \rho V \Delta h$ formula for infiltration/ventilation. Always remember: $1 \;\text{kJ/kg} = 1000 \;\text{J/kg}$, and 1 W = 1 J/s.
> 2. **SHGC vs SHGF Confusion:** Modern problems use **SHGC (Solar Heat Gain Coefficient)** with intensity $I$ (W/m²) instead of pre-computed SHGF. The relation is: $\text{SHGC} = \text{SHGF}/I$ (dimensionless, typically 0.2–0.8).
> 3. **Safety Factor:** Many students omit the safety factor. KTU expects **1.1 to 1.15** for new equipment, **1.05** for replacement, and **1.2** for old buildings with poor insulation.
> 4. **Forgetting Ballast Factor:** In lighting load, omitting BF for fluorescent tubes leads to 20% underestimation — a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 5. **Sensible vs Latent Confusion in Infiltration:** The total enthalpy method $Q = \rho V \Delta h$ gives *total* heat. For SHF calculation, you must partition using the ratio $c_p \Delta T / \Delta h$.
> 6. **CLTD ≠ ΔT:** A common mistake is substituting $\Delta T$ for CLTD. The CLTD value incorporates the sol-air temperature rise and time-lag correction; using raw $\Delta T$ overestimates the load.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 3**

- ☐ **Cooling Load ≠ Heat Gain.** Always remember the role of thermal storage (CLTD/CLF factors) in attenuating the instantaneous load.
- ☐ **Seven sources** to remember: Walls, Glass, Lights, Infiltration, Equipment, Ventilation, Occupants. The mnemonic **"WGLIEV + O"** works.
- ☐ **Master formula for envelope:** $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \text{CLTD}$ (walls/roof) and $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ (glass conduction).
- ☐ **Solar through glass** is the **biggest load in tropical climates** — never underestimate SHGF × CLF.
- ☐ **Occupant heat** must be split: sensible (temperature effect) + latent (humidity effect). Use ASHRAE tables for activity-wise values.
- ☐ **Fluorescent lighting** includes 20% ballast loss — multiply by **1.2** (BF).
- ☐ **Infiltration/ventilation loads** use **enthalpy difference** (not dry-bulb difference): $Q = \rho V \Delta h$. Convert kJ/kg to J/kg for SI units.
- ☐ **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 12000 BTU/hr** — this conversion appears in every numerical problem.
- ☐ **Always apply a safety factor of 1.10–1.15** before specifying equipment capacity.
- ☐ **SHF = $Q_s / Q_T$** is critical for **coil selection** and **psychrometric chart plotting**. Typical comfort range: **0.65–0.85**.
- ☐ **CLTD values depend on orientation, latitude, and month.** For Indian cities, peak CLTD for west walls can reach **25–30 K**.
- ☐ **External shading** reduces SHGF by **80–90%**; internal shading by **50–60%** — a high-yield concept for design questions.
- ☐ **Decrement factor** for heavy masonry walls: 0.1–0.3; for light partitions: 0.7–0.9.
- ☐ **Time lag** in hours: Heavy walls 6–10 h, light walls 1–3 h. KTU frequently tests this conceptual difference.
- ☐ **ASHRAE Standard 62.1** mandates minimum outdoor air: 8.5 L/s per person + 0.6 L/s per m² for office spaces.
- ☐ **Glass area fraction** strongly affects total load. For energy-efficient design, restrict glass to ≤ 30% of wall area.
- ☐ **Heat from motors:** If motor is inside the room, $Q = P/\eta$ (full rated input becomes heat). If outside, only inefficiency $P(1-\eta)$ enters.
- ☐ **U-value hierarchy (low to high):** Insulated wall < Single brick wall < Uninsulated roof < Single glass < Concrete slab.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** When solving 14-mark numerical problems on cooling load, present the solution in a **clean tabular form** (Source | Formula | Substitution | Result) — this mirrors the KTU evaluation key and makes it easier for the examiner to award **method marks** even if the final answer is slightly off. Always **state assumptions** (UF, BF, CLF) explicitly — partial credit is awarded for these.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Air Conditioners - Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Types of Air Conditioners - Applications # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Air Conditioner (A.C.)**: A closed-system, electromechanical device that simultaneously performs **four (4) primary functions** — temperature control, humidity control, air circulation/filtration, and ventilation — over a conditioned space to maintain a specified comfort or process condition (ASHRAE Standard 55 / IS 1391). In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme – Module 3**, the classification of Air Conditioners is studied under two broad umbrellas: | Broad Class | Definition | |---|---| | **Comfort A.C.** | Designed to maintain human-occupant thermal comfort (≈ 25 °C DBT, ≈ 50 % RH) | | **Industrial / Process A.C.** | Designed to maintain strict process conditions (temperature ± 0.5 °C, RH ± 5 %) for manufacturing, storage, or specialized equipment | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Body's Thermostat" > [!NOTE] > Think of an Air Conditioner exactly like the **human body's hypothalamus** (its internal thermostat). The body has sensors (thermoreceptors), a controller (brain), and effectors (sweat glands, blood vessels). Similarly, an A.C. has: > - **Sensors** → Thermostat / Return-air sensor > - **Controller** → PCB / Microprocessor > - **Effectors** → Compressor, expansion valve, blower, damper > > When the room gets "hot" (deviation from set-point), the controller "sweats" by sending cool refrigerant — just as your body releases sweat. The A.C. is essentially a **mechanical skin for the building**. ## 1.3 Classification Hierarchy — The Master Tree > [!TIP] > The KTU syllabus expects you to remember the **classification axis** — whether a system is **unitary vs. central**, **expansion-valve controlled vs. capillary**, or **all-air vs. air-water**. ``` AIR CONDITIONERS │ ├─── 1. UNITARY SYSTEMS (single factory-assembled package) │ ├── Window AC │ ├── Split AC (Wall / Floor-standing) │ ├── Packaged Terminal AC (PTAC) │ ├── Portable AC │ └── Cassette / Ceiling-mounted │ ├─── 2. CENTRAL SYSTEMS (chilled-water / DX central plant) │ ├── Direct-Expansion (DX) Central Plant │ ├── Chilled-Water Plant (AHU + Chiller) │ └── Variable Refrigerant Volume (VRV / VRF) │ ├─── 3. SPECIALTY / PROCESS SYSTEMS │ ├── Cleanroom A.C. │ ├── Data-Center Precision A.C. (CRAC/CRAH) │ ├── Pharmaceutical A.C. │ └── Cold-Storage A.C. │ └─── 4. TRANSPORT A.C. ├── Automobile A.C. ├── Aircraft A.C. (ECS) └── Marine A.C. ``` ## 1.4 Fundamental Governing Principle All types of air conditioners, regardless of geometry, obey the same **First-Law Energy Equation** on the conditioned space: $$Q_{\text{cooling}} = \dot{m}_a \, c_{p,a} \, (T_{o} - T_{s})$$ where **$T_o$** is the outside (return) air DBT and **$T_s$** is the supply-air DBT. The choice of *type* of A.C. is essentially a **design decision** on **how to deliver $Q_{\text{cooling}}$** to the space — at what temperature, with what medium, and through what distribution network. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Type 1 — Window Air Conditioner (WAC) **Construction**: A **single, self-contained, factory-sealed unit** housing the compressor, condenser (rear), evaporator (front), capillary tube, and two propeller fans — all mounted in a single chassis, slotted through a wall or window. **Capacity Range**: **0.75 TR to 2.0 TR** (1 TR = 3.516 kW) ### Operating Logic (Step-by-Step) 1. Warm room air is **drawn in** through the front grille by the evaporator fan. 2. Air passes over the **cold evaporator coil** → sensible + latent cooling occurs. 3. The conditioned air is **re-discharged** into the room (supply grille). 4. The condenser fan **draws outdoor air** through the rear coil → rejects heat to outside. 5. Refrigerant cycles through compressor → condenser → capillary → evaporator. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Key Point**: A Window A.C. is a **direct-expansion (DX) unitary cooler** with **100 % fresh air rejection** of condenser heat. It is the *most commonly asked* topic in Part-A questions. **Advantages**: Lowest cost, plug-and-play, no piping, ideal for single rooms. **Disadvantages**: Noisy (compressor inside the room), blocks window, limited to ≤ 2 TR, ugly aesthetics. --- ## 2.2 Type 2 — Split Air Conditioner **Construction**: Two physically separate units — **(a) Indoor Unit (IDU)** containing evaporator + IDU fan, and **(b) Outdoor Unit (ODU)** containing compressor + condenser + ODU fan — connected by **refrigerant copper piping** and electrical control wires. **Capacity Range**: **0.75 TR to 5.0 TR** (residential); commercial splits go up to **30 TR**. ### Operating Logic 1. **Sensing**: Room temperature is sensed at the IDU return-air grill. 2. **Signal**: Sensor signal is sent to ODU PCB → inverter modulates compressor speed (variable frequency drive, VFD). 3. **Refrigerant flow**: Low-pressure vapor → compressor → high-pressure vapor → condenser (outdoor) → liquid → expansion device (capillary or EEV) → low-pressure liquid/vapor mixture → evaporator (indoor). 4. **Air side**: Indoor blower pulls room air across the cold coil and discharges it via louvers. > [!NOTE] > **Inverter vs. Non-Inverter Split A.C.** — The **single most important KTU exam differentiator**: > > | Feature | Fixed-Speed (Non-Inverter) | Inverter (VFD) | > |---|---|---| > | Compressor Speed | ON/OFF (binary) | Variable (30 Hz – 120 Hz) | > | Power consumption | High (inrush) | 30 – 50 % lower | > | Temperature swing | ± 2 °C | ± 0.5 °C | > | Comfort | Oscillatory | Stable | > | Cost | Lower | Higher (20 – 30 %) | --- ## 2.3 Type 3 — Packaged Air Conditioner (PAC) **Construction**: All major refrigeration components (compressor, condenser, evaporator, expansion device, fans) are **housed in a single floor- or roof-mounted cabinet**, but **supply air is ducted** to multiple rooms. **Capacity Range**: **3 TR to 30 TR**. ### Two Sub-Variants - **Air-Cooled PAC**: Condenser uses ambient air (typical for roof-top placement). - **Water-Cooled PAC**: Condenser uses a cooling tower + water (higher efficiency, used in hotels, large offices). > [!TIP] > **KTU Trick Question Alert**: A *Window A.C.* is unitary but **NOT ducted**; a *Packaged A.C.* is **unitary AND ducted**. This distinction is frequently tested. --- ## 2.4 Type 4 — Central Station / Chilled-Water System This is the **large-building** solution (hotels, malls, hospitals, airports). It has three sub-systems: 1. **Chiller Plant** (basement or plant room): Water-cooled or air-cooled chiller producing chilled water at **6 – 8 °C**. 2. **Distribution Network**: Chilled water pumps + insulated piping. 3. **Air-Handling Units (AHU)** / Fan-Coil Units (FCU): Located on each floor; chilled water cools the air locally. **Capacity Range**: **50 TR to 5000+ TR** (a mall may need 800 TR). ### The Heat-Rejection Chain (Critical for KTU) $$Q_{\text{evap}} + W_{\text{comp}} = Q_{\text{cond}}$$ For a water-cooled chiller: $Q_{\text{cond}}$ is rejected to a **cooling tower** through condenser water (≈ 35 °C in, 29 °C out). --- ## 2.5 Type 5 — Variable Refrigerant Volume (VRV) / Variable Refrigerant Flow (VRF) A **Daikin trademark (VRV)** that has become generic ("VRF") — referring to systems with **one outdoor unit serving multiple indoor units** (up to 64), each independently controlled, using a **single refrigerant loop**. **Capacity Range**: **8 HP to 80 HP** (one ODU) → serving 2 to 64 IDUs. ### Operating Principle - **Inverter-driven compressor** in ODU continuously varies capacity to match total indoor load. - **Electronic Expansion Valves (EEV)** at each IDU modulate refrigerant flow. - Uses **R-410A / R-32** (high-pressure, zero-ODP refrigerants). - Heat-recovery VRF can simultaneously cool one zone and heat another — **free heat-recovery**, COP can exceed **5 – 6**. --- ## 2.6 Specialty Systems ### 2.6.1 Cleanroom Air Conditioning - Controls **particulate count** (ISO Class 5 – 8) + **temperature ± 1 °C** + **RH 45 ± 5 %**. - Uses **HEPA (H13/H14) or ULPA** filters (efficiency 99.95 – 99.999 % at 0.3 µm). - Air-change rate: **20 – 600 ACH** (Air Changes per Hour). - **Laminar flow** (unidirectional) for pharma/ semiconductor. ### 2.6.2 Data-Center Precision A.C.
