(CRAC / CRAH) - **CRAC** = Computer Room Air Conditioner (uses DX refrigerant). - **CRAH** = Computer Room Air Handler (uses chilled water). - Tolerates **T = 22 ± 1 °C**, **RH = 40 – 55 %**, **24 × 7 × 365** operation. - **Hot-aisle / cold-aisle** containment layout. ### 2.6.3 Cold-Storage A.C. / Refrigeration - Stores perishables at **0 °C (chilling)** or **−18 °C / −25 °C (freezing)**. - Uses **screw or centrifugal compressors** (large tonnage). - **Ammonia (R-717)** is the dominant industrial refrigerant. --- ## 2.7 Transport A.C. — Automobile > [!NOTE] > **Engine-driven R-134a system** with these features: > - Compressor driven by **engine belt** via electromagnetic clutch. > - Condenser at front of vehicle (ram-air cooled). > - Evaporator in dashboard, often combined with **PTC heater** for heating. > - Modern EVs use **heat-pump** systems (R-1234yf) for efficiency. ## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas below are **exam-grade essentials** — memorize the units and boundary conditions. | # | Formula | Description | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $1 \text{ TR} = 3.516 \text{ kW} = 12000 \text{ BTU/hr}$ | Ton of Refrigeration | kW, BTU/hr | | 2 | $Q_s = \dot{m}_a \, c_p (T_o - T_s)$ | Sensible heat | kW | | 3 | $Q_l = \dot{m}_a \, (W_o - W_s) \, h_{fg}$ | Latent heat | kW | | 4 | $\text{By-pass Factor, BPF} = \dfrac{T_s - T_{ADP}}{T_o - T_{ADP}}$ | Coil BPF | dimensionless | | 5 | $\text{COP} = \dfrac{Q_e}{W_{\text{comp}}}$ | Coefficient of Performance | — | | 6 | $\text{EER} = \dfrac{Q_e \,(\text{BTU/hr})}{P\,(\text{W})}$ | Energy Efficiency Ratio | BTU/W·hr | | 7 | $\text{ISHRAE} = 1.162 \times \text{EER}$ | Indian SEER conversion | — | | 8 | $Q_{\text{evap}} + W_{\text{comp}} = Q_{\text{cond}}$ | Energy balance | kW | | 9 | $\text{ADP} = T_{ADP}$ from Psychrometric chart | Apparatus Dew Point | °C | | 10 | $\text{SHR} = \dfrac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_l}$ | Sensible Heat Ratio | dimensionless | ## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Building-HVAC design** → Selection of WAC, Split, PAC, or Central. - **Energy auditing** → EER / COP / kW/TR for BEE Star Rating. - **Process industries** → Precision A.C. for yield improvement (semiconductor fabs lose millions on 0.1 °C drift). - **Refrigeration capacity sizing** → $Q_{\text{load}} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T + \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T + \text{latent} + \text{internal gains}$. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Sensible Heat Factor & By-Pass Factor The **By-Pass Factor (BPF)** is the single most-used parameter in cooling-coil analysis and is a **guaranteed KTU problem**. ### Step 1 — Define the Three Temperatures - $T_o$ = Temperature of air **entering** the coil (return air). - $T_s$ = Temperature of air **leaving** the coil (supply air). - $T_{ADP}$ = Apparatus Dew Point = surface temperature of the coil. ### Step 2 — Define BPF Geometrically A fraction of inlet air (BPF) **misses the coil** (by-passes the cold surface) and leaves at the inlet temperature $T_o$. The remaining fraction $(1 - \text{BPF})$ is cooled down all the way to $T_{ADP}$. ### Step 3 — Energy Balance on the Coil The mixed outlet temperature is a mass-weighted average: $$T_s = \text{BPF} \cdot T_o + (1 - \text{BPF}) \cdot T_{ADP}$$ ### Step 4 — Solve for BPF $$\begin{aligned} T_s - T_{ADP} &= \text{BPF} \cdot T_o + (1 - \text{BPF}) \cdot T_{ADP} - T_{ADP} \\ T_s - T_{ADP} &= \text{BPF} \cdot T_o - \text{BPF} \cdot T_{ADP} \\ T_s - T_{ADP} &= \text{BPF} \, (T_o - T_{ADP}) \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 5 — Final Equation $$\boxed{\text{BPF} = \dfrac{T_s - T_{ADP}}{T_o - T_{ADP}}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Tip**: A **BPF = 0** implies a *perfect* coil (all air reaches $T_{ADP}$). A **BPF = 1** implies a *useless* coil (no cooling). **Good design target**: BPF = 0.10 – 0.20. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical — KTU Style > **Problem**: A cooling coil receives air at **30 °C DBT, 60 % RH**. The coil has **BPF = 0.15**, and the surface is at **$T_{ADP}$ = 10 °C**. Find the supply air condition and the total cooling load for $\dot{m}_a$ = 10,000 kg/hr. > > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]** ### Step 1 — Supply DBT (Direct from BPF formula) $$T_s = T_{ADP} + \text{BPF} \, (T_o - T_{ADP})$$ $$T_s = 10 + 0.15 \times (30 - 10) = 10 + 0.15 \times 20$$ $$\boxed{T_s = 13 \, °\text{C}}$$ ### Step 2 — Specific Humidity Values (from Psychrometric Chart / tables) - $W_o$ at 30 °C DBT, 60 % RH → **$W_o$ ≈ 0.0160 kg/kg d.a.** - $W_s$ at 13 °C DBT (assumed saturated) → **$W_s$ ≈ 0.0094 kg/kg d.a.** ### Step 3 — Sensible Heat $Q_s$ $$Q_s = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_p \cdot (T_o - T_s)$$ $$Q_s = \dfrac{10000}{3600} \times 1.005 \times (30 - 13) = 2.778 \times 1.005 \times 17$$ $$\boxed{Q_s \approx 47.47 \text{ kW}}$$ ### Step 4 — Latent Heat $Q_l$ $$Q_l = \dot{m}_a \cdot (W_o - W_s) \cdot h_{fg}$$ $$Q_l = 2.778 \times (0.0160 - 0.0094) \times 2500 = 2.778 \times 0.0066 \times 2500$$ $$\boxed{Q_l \approx 45.84 \text{ kW}}$$ ### Step 5 — Total Cooling Load $$Q_t = Q_s + Q_l = 47.47 + 45.84$$ $$\boxed{Q_t \approx 93.31 \text{ kW} \;\approx\; 26.5 \text{ TR}}$$ ### Step 6 — Sensible Heat Ratio (SHR) $$\text{SHR} = \dfrac{Q_s}{Q_t} = \dfrac{47.47}{93.31}$$ $$\boxed{\text{SHR} \approx 0.51}$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Insight**: A SHR between **0.75 – 0.80** is typical for *comfort* applications; **SHR < 0.60** indicates a *high latent* load — characteristic of tropical India. This dictates coil selection: deep coils (4 – 6 rows) for high-latent, shallow coils (2 – 3 rows) for dry climates. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — "Which A.C. for Which Application?" > [!IMPORTANT] > The following matrix is a **high-yield KTU table** — expect a direct question (5 marks) on this comparison. | Criterion | Window | Split | Packaged | Central Chilled Water | VRF/VRV | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Capacity** | 0.75 – 2 TR | 0.75 – 5 TR | 3 – 30 TR | 50 – 5000 TR | 2 – 60 TR (multi-IDU) | | **Building Size** | 1 small room | 1 – 2 rooms | Full floor | Whole building | Multi-zone bldg. | | **Ductwork** | None | None | Yes | Yes | Optional | | **Initial Cost/TR** | Lowest | Low | Medium | High | High | | **Operating Cost/TR** | Highest | Medium | Medium | Lowest | Low | | **Noise** | High (in-room) | Low | Low | Lowest | Low | | **Maintenance** | Easiest | Easy | Moderate | Specialized | Specialized | | **Life** | 7 – 10 yr | 10 – 15 yr | 12 – 15 yr | 20+ yr | 15+ yr | | **Typical Use** | Bedroom, office cabin | Living rooms, small shops | Restaurants, banks | Hotels, malls, hospitals | Modern offices, villas | | **Energy Star** | ★ ★ | ★ ★ ★ ★ | ★ ★ ★ | ★ ★ ★ ★ ★ | ★ ★ ★ ★ ★ | | **Refrigerant (modern)** | R-32 | R-32 | R-410A | R-134a / R-1234ze | R-410A / R-32 | --- ## 3.4 Application Selection Logic Flow The choice of A.C. type is governed by: $$\text{Type} = f(\dot{Q}_{\text{load}},\, N_{\text{zones}},\, \Delta T_{\text{req}},\, C_{\text{capital}},\, C_{\text{operating}})$$ > [!NOTE] > **Rule of Thumb** (Industry Standard): > - $Q \leq 2$ TR → **Window or Split** > - $Q = 2 – 30$ TR → **Packaged A.C.** > - $Q = 30 – 200$ TR → **VRF/VRV** (if ≤ 64 zones) **OR** Central > - $Q > 200$ TR → **Central Chilled-Water Plant** > - $\Delta T_{\text{req}} \pm 1\,°\text{C}$ → **Precision / Process A.C.** ## 3.5 Energy Consumption Comparison (Quantitative) Per the **BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency) Star Rating** of India, the typical kW/TR consumption is: | Star Rating | EER Range | kW per TR (cooling) | |---|---|---| | 1 Star | 2.30 – 2.69 | 1.31 – 1.53 | | 2 Star | 2.70 – 2.99 | 1.18 – 1.30 | | 3 Star | 3.00 – 3.29 | 1.07 – 1.17 | | 4 Star | 3.30 – 3.49 | 1.01 – 1.06 | | 5 Star | ≥ 3.50 | ≤ 1.00 | $$\text{COP} \;\approx\; \text{EER} \times 0.293 \quad \text{(for a 1-TR window at 35 °C ambient)}$$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Window A.C. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[ROOM AIR INLET
30 C DBT 60 RH] --> B[EVAPORATOR FAN
Centrifugal Blower] B --> C[EVAPORATOR COIL
DX Coil R-32] C --> D[CONDENSATE DRAIN
Tray and Pipe] C --> E[SUPPLY GRILLE
13 C DBT] F[COMPRESSOR
Hermetic Reciprocating] --> G[CONDENSER COIL
Air Cooled Finned] G --> H[CAPILLARY TUBE
Expansion Device] H --> C I[CONDENSER FAN
Propeller] --> G J[OUTDOOR AIR
35 C Ambient] --> I G --> K[REJECTED HEAT
To Atmosphere] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram**: Notice the **single closed refrigerant loop** (F → G → H → C → F) and the **two separate airstreams** (indoor: A→B→C→E; outdoor: J→I→G→K). This is the *defining feature* of a unitary A.C. ## 4.2 Block Architecture — Split A.C. with Inverter Drive ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph IDU [INDOOR UNIT] A1[Room Air] --> A2[Evaporator Coil] A2 --> A3[IDU Blower] A3 --> A4[Supply Air to Room] A5[Return Air Sensor T] --> A6[IDU PCB Controller] end subgraph ODU [OUTDOOR UNIT] B1[Compressor Inverter VFD] --> B2[Condenser Coil] B2 --> B3[Condenser Fan] B3 --> B4[Heat Rejection Ambient] B2 --> B5[EEV Capillary] B5 --> A2 end A6 -. Signal Wire .-> B1 A6 -. Power Reference .-> B1 B1 -. Suction Line .-> A2 ``` > [!TIP] > **Critical**: The arrows between IDU and ODU are **refrigerant lines** (suction + liquid) and a **control/signal cable**. The ODU has no fresh-air inlet; the room is the airstream. ## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Central Chilled-Water Plant ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PLANT [Chiller Plant Room] C1[Compressor] --> C2[Condenser Water Cooled] C2 --> C3[Cooling Tower] C3 --> C4[Atmospheric Heat Rejection] C5[Expansion Valve] --> C1 end subgraph DIST [Distribution] P1[Chilled Water Pump 6 C] --> P2[Supply Pipe Insulated] P2 --> P3[AHU / FCU per Floor] P3 --> P4[Return Pipe 12 C] P4 --> C5 end subgraph ZONES [Conditioned Zones] Z1[Zone 1 Office] --> Z3[Zone 3 Hotel Room] Z2[Zone 2 Conference] --> Z4[Zone 4 Lobby] end P3 --> Z1 P3 --> Z2 P3 --> Z3 P3 --> Z4 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight**: A chilled-water plant uses **water as a secondary coolant** — this enables **long-distance distribution** (up to 500 m) with minimal losses, and allows **simultaneous heating/cooling** with a *heat-recovery chiller*. ## 4.4 Comparative Selection Flowchart — Application Driven ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Choose Air Conditioner Type] --> Q1{Building Size} Q1 -->|Single Room| Q2{Budget} Q1 -->|Multiple Rooms| Q3{Ductwork Feasible} Q1 -->|Whole Building| Q4{Number of Zones} Q2 -->|Low| Win[Window AC] Q2 -->|Moderate| Split[Split AC Inverter] Q3 -->|No| VRF[VRF VRV System] Q3 -->|Yes| PAC[Packaged AC] Q4 -->|< 64 Zones| VRF Q4 -->|> 64 Zones| Central[Central Chilled Water Plant] Q4 -->|Process Tight Tolerance| Precision[CRAC / Cleanroom Unit] ``` ## 4.5 Industrial Application Spectrum — Block Map ```mermaid flowchart TB AC[Air Conditioning Domain] --> RES[Residential] AC --> COM[Commercial] AC --> IND[Industrial] AC --> SPC[Specialty] RES --> R1[Apartments - Split AC] RES --> R2[Villas - VRF Multi-Zone] COM --> C1[Offices - Central Plant] COM --> C2[Malls - Chilled Water] COM --> C3[Hotels - 4 Pipe FCU] IND --> I1[Pharma - Cleanroom] IND --> I2[Textile - Humidification AC] IND --> I3[Auto Paint Booth - Make-up Air] SPC --> S1[Hospitals - HEPA + Precise T] SPC --> S2[Data Centers - CRAC / CRAH] SPC --> S3[Cold Storage - Ammonia Plant] SPC --> S4[Transport - Auto / Aircraft / Marine] ``` ## 4.6 Refrigerant Flow Inside an A.C. — Schematic (Functional) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Compressor
Low P Vapour IN] -->|High P Vapour OUT| B[Condenser
Heat Rejection] B -->|High P Liquid OUT| C[Filter Drier] C --> D[Sight Glass] D --> E[Expansion Device
EEV or Capillary] E -->|Low P Mix| F[Evaporator
Heat Absorption] F -->|Low P Vapour| A G[Receiver Tank] --> B B --> G ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** ### Q1. Differentiate between a **Window A.C.** and a **Split A.C.**. (3 Marks) **Model Answer** (3 key points, 1 mark each): | S.No. | Window A.C. | Split A.C. | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Single unit** — compressor, condenser, evaporator all in one chassis. | **Two units** — Indoor Unit (evaporator) and Outdoor Unit (compressor + condenser). | | 2 | Compressor is **inside the conditioned space** → higher noise (~50 dB). | Compressor is **outside** → low indoor noise (~30 dB). | | 3 | Capacity **≤ 2 TR**; installation is *plug-and-play*; **no piping**. | Capacity **0.75 – 5 TR**; requires **refrigerant piping** and electrical wiring between IDU and ODU. | --- > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** ### Q2. What is a **VRF/VRV system**? State **two** advantages over conventional split A.C. (3 Marks) **Model Answer**: **Definition (1 mark)**: A **Variable Refrigerant Flow (VRF)** system — also called VRV (Variable Refrigerant Volume) by Daikin — uses **one outdoor unit** containing an **inverter-driven variable-speed compressor** to serve **multiple indoor units** (up to 64) over a **single refrigerant network**, with **independent zone control** via electronic expansion valves. **Two Advantages (2 marks)**: 1. **Energy efficiency**: Compressor capacity continuously matches the *sum of zone loads* → COP up to **5 – 6** (vs. 2.8 – 3.2 for fixed-speed split). 2. **Heat recovery**: Some VRF systems allow **simultaneous heating and cooling** — the heat extracted from a server room can *heat an office free of cost*. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Internal Choice**: Attempt **any ONE** of the following: ### Question A — 14 Marks > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **(a)** Classify the major **types of air conditioners** used in residential and commercial applications, giving one-line definitions of each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A cooling coil receives air at **35 °C DBT, 50 % RH** and supplies air at **15 °C DBT** with **BPF = 0.20**. The mass flow rate of air is **8,000 kg/hr**. Calculate: - (i) Apparatus Dew Point (ADP) - (ii) Sensible Heat (kW) - (iii) Latent Heat (kW) [Take $h_{fg}$ = 2500 kJ/kg] - (iv) Total cooling load in **TR** and **kW** - (v) Sensible Heat Ratio (SHR) **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — (a) Classification of A.C. (Any 7 points, 1 mark each): 1. **Window A.C.** — Single self-contained unit mounted in a wall/window opening; capacity ≤ 2 TR. 2. **Split A.C.** — IDU + ODU separated; refrigerant piping; 0.75 – 5 TR. 3. **Packaged A.C.** — Factory-assembled unit with ducted supply; 3 – 30 TR. 4. **Central Chilled-Water Plant** — Chiller + AHU + cooling tower; serves large buildings; 50 – 5000 TR. 5. **VRF/VRV** — Single ODU with multiple IDUs on a common refrigerant loop; inverter control. 6. **Precision A.C. (CRAC/CRAH)** — Tight ± 1 °C and ± 5 % RH control for data centers, cleanrooms. 7. **Cold-Storage A.C.** — Industrial refrigeration for food/pharma preservation; ammonia-based. 8. **Transport A.C.** — Vehicle/aircraft/marine cabins; compact systems with engine-driven compressor. 9. **Evaporative Coolers** — Desert/swelling cooler; uses water evaporation; dry climates only. #### Model Solution — (b) **Given**: $T_o = 35\,°\text{C}$, $T_s = 15\,°\text{C}$, $\text{BPF} = 0.20$, $\dot{m}_a = 8000 \text{ kg/hr}$, $h_{fg} = 2500 \text{ kJ/kg}$. **(i) ADP Calculation (2 marks):** $$\text{BPF} = \dfrac{T_s - T_{ADP}}{T_o - T_{ADP}} \Rightarrow 0.20 = \dfrac{15 - T_{ADP}}{35 - T_{ADP}}$$ $$\begin{aligned} 0.20 \times (35 - T_{ADP}) &= 15 - T_{ADP} \\ 7 - 0.20 \, T_{ADP} &= 15 - T_{ADP} \\ T_{ADP} - 0.20 \, T_{ADP} &= 15 - 7 \\ 0.80 \, T_{ADP} &= 8 \\ \boxed{T_{ADP} = 10 \, °\text{C}} \end{aligned}$$ **(ii) Sensible Heat (1.5 marks):** $$Q_s = \dfrac{8000}{3600} \times 1.005 \times (35 - 15) = 2.222 \times 1.005 \times 20$$ $$\boxed{Q_s = 44.67 \text{ kW}}$$ **(iii) Latent Heat (1.5 marks):** From psychrometric chart: - $W_o$ at 35 °C, 50 % RH → **$W_o = 0.0196$ kg/kg d.a.** - $W_s$ at 15 °C (saturated) → **$W_s = 0.0107$ kg/kg d.a.** $$Q_l = 2.222 \times (0.0196 - 0.0107) \times 2500 = 2.222 \times 0.0089 \times 2500$$ $$\boxed{Q_l = 49.44 \text{ kW}}$$ **(iv) Total Load (1 mark):** $$Q_t = 44.67 + 49.44 = \boxed{94.11 \text{ kW} = \dfrac{94.11}{3.516} \approx 26.76 \text{ TR}}$$ **(v) SHR (1 mark):** $$\text{SHR} = \dfrac{Q_s}{Q_t} = \dfrac{44.67}{94.11} \approx \boxed{0.475}$$ --- ### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** **(a)** Explain with a **neat sketch** (functional diagram acceptable) the working principle of a **window air conditioner**. List **any four advantages** and **any two disadvantages**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the following A.C. systems on the basis of: **capacity, application, ductwork requirement, and COP**: (i) Split A.C., (ii) Packaged A.C., (iii) VRF, (iv) Central Chilled-Water Plant. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — (a) **Working Principle (5 marks)**: A window A.C. operates on the **vapour-compression refrigeration cycle** with the entire system housed in a **single, factory-sealed metal cabinet** that straddles the building wall. 1. **Indoor Air Side**: Warm room air (≈ 30 °C, 60 % RH) is drawn in by the **evaporator (front) fan** → passes over the **cold evaporator coil** (R-32 refrigerant at ≈ 5 °C) → **sensible + latent cooling** occurs → cooled air at ≈ 13 °C is discharged back into the room through a supply grille. 2. **Refrigerant Loop**: Low-pressure vapour → **compressor** (raises P and T) → high-pressure vapour → **condenser coil** (rear) → high-pressure liquid → **capillary tube** (expansion) → low-pressure liquid → evaporator → cycle repeats. 3. **Outdoor Air Side**: A second **propeller fan** draws outside air through the condenser coil → **heat is rejected** to the ambient → hot air blown out the back. **Functional Diagram**: Refer to **Section 4.1** (Mermaid diagram) of these notes. **Advantages (Any 4)**: 1. Lowest initial cost; no refrigeration piping required. 2. Plug-and-play installation; no specialist tools needed. 3. Compact, ideal for **single rooms** in small offices/bedrooms. 4. Easy maintenance — entire unit can be slid out. 5. Suitable for rental properties (portable-installation). **Disadvantages (Any 2)**: 1. **Noisy** because compressor is inside the room (~50 dB). 2. **Blocks window** — affects natural light and aesthetics. 3. Limited to **≤ 2 TR** capacity; not suitable for larger spaces. #### Model Solution — (b) | System | Capacity | Application | Ductwork | Typical COP | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Split A.C.** | 0.75 – 5 TR | Bedrooms, small offices, shops | **Not required** | 2.8 – 3.5 (inverter) | | **Packaged A.C.** | 3 – 30 TR | Restaurants, banks, small hotels, full-floor offices | **Required** (insulated ducts) | 2.8 – 3.3 | | **VRF/VRV** | 8 – 60 HP (multi-zone) | Modern offices, villas, multi-zone commercial | **Optional** (ducted IDU variants available) | **3.5 – 6.0** | | **Central Chilled-Water Plant** | 50 – 5000+ TR | Malls, airports, hospitals, IT parks, large hotels | **Required** (for AHU/FCU) | **4.0 – 6.5** (water-cooled chiller) | **(1 mark for each correct row × 4 rows = 4 marks; 3 marks for justification/extra comments)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU students LOSE MARKS** on this topic — read carefully: > > 1. **BPF formula inversion** — Many students write $\text{BPF} = (T_o - T_s) / (T_o - T_{ADP})$ — **WRONG**! The correct form is $(T_s - T_{ADP}) / (T_o - T_{ADP})$. Memorize the **supplied air** in the numerator, **not** the temperature difference. > > 2. **TR ↔ kW conversion** — $1 \text{ TR} = 3.516 \text{ kW}$, NOT $3.5$ kW. Use **exact** values in Part-B numericals; using 3.5 may lose 0.5 mark. > > 3. **Unit confusion in latent heat** — $h_{fg} = 2500$ **kJ/kg**, not 2500 J/kg. Forgetting the $10^3$ factor = **wrong answer**. > > 4. **SHR definition** — Always $\text{SHR} = Q_s / (Q_s + Q_l)$, not $Q_s / Q_l$. The latter is called **Sensible-to-Latent Ratio (SLR)**. > > 5. **Confusing "Comfort" and "Process" A.C.** — Comfort applications have *SHR between 0.7 – 0.85*; process applications require *SHR close to 1.0* (no latent control). The VAV/CAV distinction is sometimes tested. > > 6. **Forgetting to mention refrigerant** — A "Compare Split and Window A.C." answer without mentioning **R-32 / R-410A** loses one full mark. > > 7. **Sensible heat in the equation** — $Q_s = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$. If mass flow is in **kg/hr**, convert to **kg/s** (divide by 3600) or you will be **off by a factor of 3600**! --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision — 5-Minute Speed Sheet** - ☐ **1 TR = 3.516 kW = 12,000 BTU/hr** — print and memorize. - ☐ **BPF formula**: $\text{BPF} = (T_s - T_{ADP}) / (T_o - T_{ADP})$ — **numerator has $T_s$**, not $T_o$. - ☐ **SHR formula**: $\text{SHR} = Q_s / (Q_s + Q_l)$ — comfort ≈ 0.75, process ≈ 0.95+. - ☐ **Energy balance**: $Q_{\text{evap}} + W_{\text{comp}} = Q_{\text{cond}}$ — holds for every A.C. - ☐ **Window A.C.** = unitary, non-ducted, ≤ 2 TR, single chassis, noisy. - ☐ **Split A.C.** = two units, refrigerant piping, IDU + ODU, **0.75 – 5 TR**. - ☐ **Inverter vs. Non-Inverter**: Inverter uses **VFD** for variable compressor speed → **30 – 50 % energy saving**. - ☐ **Packaged A.C.** = unitary **+ ducted**, 3 – 30 TR, often roof-top. - ☐ **Central Chilled-Water Plant** = chiller + AHU + cooling tower, 50 – 5000+ TR. - ☐ **VRF/VRV** = one ODU, multiple IDUs, up to 64 zones, COP up to 5 – 6. - ☐ **Cleanroom A.C.** uses **HEPA/ULPA** filters, **20 – 600 ACH**, ISO Class 5 – 8. - ☐ **Data Center** = **CRAC (DX)** or **CRAH (chilled water)**, **22 ± 1 °C**, **40 – 55 % RH**. - ☐ **Automobile A.C.** = engine-driven via belt clutch, R-134a/1234yf, dashboard evaporator. - ☐ **Selection rule of thumb**: $\leq 2$ TR → Window/Split; $2 – 30$ TR → Packaged; $> 30$ TR → Central/VRF. - ☐ **BEE Star Rating**: 5-Star EER $\geq 3.50$ → most energy efficient. - ☐ **Refrigerants to know**: **R-32** (current residential), **R-410A** (legacy), **R-134a** (automobile), **R-717/Ammonia** (industrial), **R-1234yf** (new auto). - ☐ **Psychrometric properties to fetch**: $W_o$, $W_s$, $h_{fg} \approx 2500$ kJ/kg, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K. - ☐ **Heat-recovery VRF** = simultaneous heating + cooling = free heat reuse. - ☐ **KTU 2024 emphasis**: Expect at least one **7-mark numerical** on BPF/SHR/cooling load in every ESE.

topics.title: Design of Complete Air-Conditioning Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE
> - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of Complete Air-Conditioning Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design of Complete Air-Conditioning System** is the integrated engineering procedure that determines the **cooling/heating load** of a conditioned space, selects the appropriate **air-handling unit (AHU)**, sizes the **refrigeration plant (chiller)**, designs the **air distribution ductwork**, and specifies the **control system** to maintain prescribed indoor air quality (IAQ) conditions (temperature, relative humidity, purity, and motion) as per **ASHRAE Standard 55** and **IS 3315** thermal comfort guidelines.

The design process bridges three major domains:
1. **Psychrometrics** (air state transformations)
2. **Heat Transfer** (load through building envelope)
3. **Fluid Mechanics** (duct and pipe flow)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Human Circulatory System**
> Think of an air-conditioning system as the **circulatory system of a building**:
> - The **chiller** = the heart (pumps refrigerant)
> - The **AHU** = the lungs (processes and conditions air)
> - The **ducts** = the arteries/veins (transport conditioned air)
> - The **thermostat** = the brain (senses and controls)
> - The **room** = the body tissue (rejects heat into the bloodstream)
>
> Just as a doctor must measure cardiac output, lung capacity, and blood volume before prescribing treatment, an AC designer must calculate **cooling load**, **air quantity**, and **duct size** before selecting equipment.

### 1.3 Key Performance Indices

| Index | Symbol | Typical Office Value |
|---|---|---|
| Room Sensible Heat Factor | RSHF | 0.75 – 0.85 |
| Apparatus Dew Point | ADP | 8 °C – 12 °C |
| Bypass Factor | BF | 0.10 – 0.30 |
| Air Change Rate | ACH | 6 – 12 per hour |
| Outside Air Fraction | %OA | 10% – 15% of supply air |

### 1.4 Standard Indoor & Outdoor Design Conditions (KTU/ISHRAE Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Summer Design Conditions (Kerala Context):**
> - **Outdoor**: DBT = 35 °C, WBT = 28 °C (coastal); DBT = 38 °C, WBT = 26 °C (inland)
> - **Indoor**: DBT = 24 °C ± 1 °C, RH = 50% ± 5%
> - **ASHRAE Comfort Band**: Operative temperature 20 °C – 26 °C in summer.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Phased Design Philosophy

A complete AC system design follows this **5-phase protocol**:

**Phase 1 — Load Estimation**
- Compute **sensible heat gain (SHG)** and **latent heat gain (LHG)** through walls, roof, glass, partitions, ceiling, floor, doors, infiltration, ventilation, occupants, lights, equipment, and appliances.

**Phase 2 — Air Quantity & Coil Selection**
- Use **RSHF method** to compute supply air quantity and select ADP via **bypass factor (BF)** of the cooling coil.

**Phase 3 — Equipment Sizing**
- Choose AHU, chiller, cooling tower, pumps, fans based on **total load (TR)** and **GPM** flow rate.

**Phase 4 — Duct Design**
- Size supply, return, and fresh-air ducts using **equal friction**, **velocity reduction**, or **static regain** method (Friction Loss = **0.1 in.wg/100 ft** typical).

**Phase 5 — Controls & Drafting**
- Specify thermostats, humidistats, VAV/CAV boxes, dampers, insulation, and prepare layout drawings.

### 2.2 Cooling Load Components (ASHRAE-Fundamentals Method)

| # | Load Component | Type | Heat Transfer Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | External walls | Sensible | Conduction + Solar |
| 2 | Roof/Ceiling | Sensible | Conduction + Solar |
| 3 | Glass windows | Sensible | Conduction + Solar radiation |
| 4 | Partitions, floors, doors | Sensible | Conduction |
| 5 | Infiltration air | Sensible + Latent | Convection + Vapour |
| 6 | Ventilation (outside) air | Sensible + Latent | Convection + Vapour |
| 7 | Occupants | Sensible + Latent | Metabolic |
| 8 | Lights | Sensible | Radiation + Convection |
| 9 | Equipment & appliances | Sensible (+ Latent) | Electrical dissipation |
| 10 | Miscellaneous | — | — |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All quantities in SI units unless stated. Rounded for exam use.

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_{wall} = U \cdot A \cdot CLTD$ | Wall heat gain (CLTD method) | W |
| 2 | $Q_{glass} = U \cdot A \cdot CLTD + A \cdot SHGF \cdot SC \cdot CLF$ | Glass solar + conduction gain | W |
| 3 | $Q_{light} = n \cdot P \cdot F_{u} \cdot F_{b}$ | Lighting load (n = no. of lamps, $F_u$ = usage factor, $F_b$ = ballast factor) | W |
| 4 | $Q_{equip} = n \cdot P \cdot F_{u} \cdot F_{r}$ | Equipment load ($F_r$ = radiation factor) | W |
| 5 | $Q_{occ,s} = n \cdot q_s$ | Occupant sensible heat | W |
| 6 | $Q_{occ,l} = n \cdot q_l$ | Occupant latent heat | W |
| 7 | $RSHF = \dfrac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_l}$ | Room Sensible Heat Factor | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\dot{m}_a = \dfrac{Q_s}{c_p (T_{room} - T_{supply})}$ | Mass flow of supply air | kg/s |
| 9 | $V_a = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a}{\rho}$ | Volumetric flow of supply air | m³/s |
| 10 | $V_a = \dfrac{Q_s}{1.2 \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}$ | Approx. SI formula (1.2 = $\rho c_p \cdot 1000/60$) | m³/s |
| 11 | $\dot{m}_{a} = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{h_{room} - h_{supply}}$ | Mass flow using enthalpy (for total heat) | kg/s |
| 12 | $BF = \dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{T_w - T_1}$ | Bypass Factor of cooling coil | dimensionless |
| 13 | $ADP$ from BF | Intersection point on psychrometric chart | °C |
| 14 | $TR = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{3.517}$ | Refrigeration Tonnage (1 TR = 3.517 kW) | TR |
| 15 | $D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4\dot{V}}{\pi V_{duct}}}$ | Round duct diameter (velocity method) | m |
| 16 | $\Delta P = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}$ | Darcy–Weisbach friction loss | Pa |
| 17 | $GPM = \dfrac{Q_{chiller}}{4.19 \cdot \Delta T_{cw} \cdot 60}$ | Chilled water flow (1 GPM = 0.0631 L/s) | L/s |
| 18 | $Q_{fan} = \dfrac{\dot{V} \cdot \Delta P_{total}}{\eta_{fan}}$ | Fan power | W |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constants (Bolded for Memorization):**
> - $c_p$ (air) = **1.005 kJ/kg·K**
> - $\rho_{air}$ = **1.2 kg/m³** (standard)
> - $h_{fg}$ (water) = **2454 kJ/kg** at 10 °C
> - 1 TR = **3.517 kW** = **3024 kcal/h**
> - Standard ADP for comfort cooling = **10 °C ± 2 °C**

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

This design methodology is used in:
- **ISHRAE-grade HVAC consulting** (commercial buildings, hospitals, clean rooms)
- **Pharmaceutical cold chain** design
- **Data centre precision cooling** (CRAH/CRAC units)
- **Hotel & mall HVAC** where latent and sensible loads are balanced
- **Green building certification (GRIHA, LEED)** where ventilation and energy recovery are critical.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Supply Air Quantity from Sensible Load

We start from the steady-flow energy equation for air passing through a room. The sensible heat removed by the supply air equals the room sensible load:

$$
Q_s = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{room} - T_{supply})
$$

Solving for mass flow rate:

$$
\dot{m}_a = \frac{Q_s}{c_p \cdot (T_{room} - T_{supply})}
$$

In volumetric form, with $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, and a typical $\Delta T = 10$ °C (room 24 °C, supply 14 °C):

$$
\dot{V}_a = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{\rho} = \frac{Q_s}{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{Q_s \text{ (in W)}}{1.2 \times 1005 \times \Delta T} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Resulting empirical formula widely used in KTU textbooks:**
> $$ \dot{V}_a = \frac{Q_s}{1.2 \times \Delta T} \quad \text{(m}^3\text{/s, with } Q_s \text{ in kW)} $$

---

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Cooling Load of a Conference Room

**Given (typical KTU 14-mark problem setup):**
- Room: 10 m × 8 m × 3.2 m ceiling
- 4 external walls; 1 glass wall (3 m × 2 m, single-glazed, South-facing)
- 30 occupants (light office work)
- 12 fluorescent tubes × 40 W (with ballast factor 1.2, usage 0.8)
- 2 computers (250 W each, usage 0.6)
- Outdoor: 35 °C DBT, 28 °C WBT
- Indoor: 24 °C DBT, 50% RH
- CLTD for wall = 11 K, for roof = 14 K, glass CLTD = 11 K, SHGF = 300 W/m², SC = 0.7
- U-values: wall = 2.5 W/m²K, glass = 5.8 W/m²K, roof = 1.8 W/m²K

#### Step 1 — Wall Sensible Heat Gain (excluding glass wall)

External wall area (3 walls): $A_w = (10 \times 3.2) \times 2 + (8 \times 3.2) = 64 + 25.6 = 89.6$ m²

$$
Q_{wall} = U \cdot A \cdot CLTD = 2.5 \times 89.6 \times 11 = 2464 \text{ W} \approx 2.46 \text{ kW}
$$

#### Step 2 — Glass Wall Heat Gain (Conduction + Solar)

Glass area $A_g = 3 \times 2 = 6$ m²
Conduction part: $Q_{g,c} = U \cdot A_g \cdot CLTD = 5.8 \times 6 \times 11 = 382.8$ W

Solar part: $Q_{g,s} = A_g \cdot SHGF \cdot SC \cdot CLF$

With CLF = 0.6 (assumed) and SHGF = 300 W/m², SC = 0.7:

$$
Q_{g,s} = 6 \times 300 \times 0.7 \times 0.6 = 756 \text{ W}
$$

Total glass gain:

$$
Q_{glass} = 382.8 + 756 = 1138.8 \text{ W} \approx 1.14 \text{ kW}
$$

#### Step 3 — Roof Heat Gain

Roof area $A_r = 10 \times 8 = 80$ m²

$$
Q_{roof} = 1.8 \times 80 \times 14 = 2016 \text{ W} \approx 2.02 \text{ kW}
$$

#### Step 4 — Lighting Load

$n = 12$ lamps, $P = 40$ W, $F_u = 0.8$, $F_b = 1.2$

$$
Q_{light} = 12 \times 40 \times 0.8 \times 1.2 = 460.8 \text{ W} \approx 0.46 \text{ kW}
$$

#### Step 5 — Equipment Load

$n = 2$ PCs, $P = 250$ W, $F_u = 0.6$, $F_r = 1.0$ (fully radiated)

$$
Q_{equip} = 2 \times 250 \times 0.6 \times 1.0 = 300 \text{ W} = 0.30 \text{ kW}
$$

#### Step 6 — Occupant Load (ASHRAE Light Office: $q_s = 67$ W, $q_l = 47$ W per person)

$$
Q_{occ,s} = 30 \times 67 = 2010 \text{ W} = 2.01 \text{ kW}
$$
$$
Q_{occ,l} = 30 \times 47 = 1410 \text{ W} = 1.41 \text{ kW}
$$

#### Step 7 — Infiltration Load

Assume 1 air change per hour for sealed conference room.
Volume $V = 10 \times 8 \times 3.2 = 256$ m³

$$
\dot{V}_{inf} = \frac{256}{3600} = 0.0711 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

From psychrometric chart (or formula):
Sensible: $Q_{inf,s} = \rho \dot{V} c_p (T_o - T_i) = 1.2 \times 0.0711 \times 1.005 \times (35 - 24) = 0.943$ kW
Latent: $Q_{inf,l} = \rho \dot{V} (W_o - W_i) h_{fg}$

With $W_o = 0.0197$ kg/kg (35 °C DBT, 28 °C WBT), $W_i = 0.0094$ kg/kg (24 °C, 50% RH):

$$
Q_{inf,l} = 1.2 \times 0.0711 \times (0.0197 - 0.0094) \times 2454 = 1.724 \text{ kW}
$$

#### Step 8 — Total Loads & RSHF

$$
Q_s = 2.46 + 1.14 + 2.02 + 0.46 + 0.30 + 2.01 + 0.943 = 9.33 \text{ kW}
$$
$$
Q_l = 1.41 + 1.724 = 3.13 \text{ kW}
$$
$$
Q_{total} = 12.46 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
RSHF = \frac{9.33}{12.46} = 0.749
$$

#### Step 9 — Supply Air Quantity (Take $\Delta T = 10$ °C)

$$
\dot{V}_a = \frac{9.33}{1.2 \times 10} = 0.778 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 2800 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} \approx 1650 \text{ CFM}
$$

#### Step 10 — ADP Determination

Using BF = 0.20 (typical 4-row chilled water coil), the coil ADP is read from the psychrometric chart along the line joining **Room Condition (24 °C, 50% RH)** and **Supply Condition (14 °C, 90% RH)** extended back to saturation. For RSHF = 0.75 and BF = 0.20:

$$
ADP \approx 9.8 \text{ °C} \approx 10 \text{ °C}
$$

#### Step 11 — Refrigeration Tonnage

$$
TR = \frac{12.46}{3.517} = 3.54 \text{ TR}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Select a standard **4 TR chiller** (with safety margin of 10–15%).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Coil Selection

| RSHF Range | Coil Type | Rows | BF | Recommended ADP |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.95 – 1.00 | DX / Chilled water | 4 | 0.10 | 8 °C – 9 °C |
| 0.75 – 0.95 | Chilled water | 6 | 0.15 | 9 °C – 11 °C |
| 0.60 – 0.75 | Chilled water | 8 | 0.20 | 11 °C – 13 °C |
| < 0.60 | Chilled water + Reheat | 8 | 0.25 | 12 °C – 14 °C |

---

### 3.4 Derivation — Duct Sizing (Equal Friction Method)

The **equal friction method** assumes a constant friction loss per unit length of duct, typically $R = 0.1$ in.wg/100 ft (or **0.8 Pa/m** in SI).

Friction loss per unit length (Darcy–Weisbach form):

$$
\frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{f}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho V^2}{2}
$$

With $V = \dot{V}/A = 4\dot{V}/(\pi D^2)$:

$$
\frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{8 f \rho \dot{V}^2}{\pi^2 D^5}
$$

Solving for duct diameter:

$$
D = \left( \frac{8 f \rho \dot{V}^2}{\pi^2 R} \right)^{1/5}
$$

where $R = \Delta P / L$ is the assumed friction rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a **rectangular duct** of equivalent aspect ratio, the **equivalent diameter** is given by:
> $$ D_e = 1.30 \frac{(a \cdot b)^{0.625}}{(a + b)^{0.25}} $$
> where $a, b$ are the rectangular sides in metres.

---

### 3.5 Chilled Water Pump Flow Derivation

Energy balance on the chiller evaporator (assuming no loss):

$$
Q_{chiller} = \dot{m}_{cw} \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot (T_{rw} - T_{sw})
$$

With $c_{p,w} = 4.186$ kJ/kg·K and $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³:

$$
\dot{V}_{cw} = \frac{Q_{chiller}}{\rho_w \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot \Delta T_{cw}}
$$

For typical $\Delta T_{cw} = 5$ °C (chilled water 7 °C supply, 12 °C return):

$$
\dot{V}_{cw} = \frac{12.46}{4.186 \times 5} = 0.595 \text{ L/s} = 35.7 \text{ L/min} \approx 9.4 \text{ GPM}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Complete AC System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    OAD["OUTSIDE AIR DAMPER"] --> MAF["MIXING AIR FILTER BANK"]
    MAF --> PREF["PRE FILTERS MERV 8"]
    PREF --> PREH["PRE HEATING COIL WINTER"]
    PREH --> CC["CHILLED WATER COIL 4 TO 8 ROW"]
    CC --> ELM["ELIMINATOR DEMISTER"]
    ELM --> FAN["SUPPLY FAN CENTRIFUGAL FORWARD CURVED"]
    FAN --> SV["SUPPLY DUCT MAIN TRUNK"]
    SV --> SD1["SUPPLY DIFFUSER ROOM A"]
    SV --> SD2["SUPPLY DIFFUSER ROOM B"]
    SD1 --> RA1["RETURN AIR GRILLE ROOM A"]
    SD2 --> RA2["RETURN AIR GRILLE ROOM B"]
    RA1 --> RD["RETURN DUCT PLENUM"]
    RA2 --> RD
    RD --> MAF
    CC --> CHILL["CHILLER UNIT WATER COOLED"]
    CHILL --> CT["COOLING TOWER INDUCED DRAFT"]
    CT --> CHP["CHILLED WATER PUMP"]
    CHP --> CC
    CHILL --> CMP["CONDENSER WATER PUMP"]
    CMP --> CT
    FAN --> AUTO["AUTOMATIC CONTROL VALVE 3 WAY"]
    AUTO --> CC
    TSTAT["ROOM THERMOSTAT"] --> AUTO
    HUM["HUMIDISTAT"] --> AUTO
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this diagram as follows:** Outside air + return air are mixed, filtered, and either pre-heated (winter) or cooled (summer) by the chilled water coil, then supplied to rooms via ductwork. Heat extracted is rejected to the cooling tower through the condenser water loop.

---

### 4.2 Cooling Load Calculation Flow (Mermaid Process Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["STEP 1 ENVELOPE LOADS WALL ROOF GLASS"] --> A2["STEP 2 INTERNAL LOADS LIGHT EQUIP OCCUPANTS"]
    A2 --> A3["STEP 3 VENTILATION INFILTRATION LOADS"]
    A3 --> A4["STEP 4 SUM SENSIBLE Qs AND LATENT Ql"]
    A4 --> A5["STEP 5 CALCULATE RSHF"]
    A5 --> A6["STEP 6 SELECT ADP FROM PSYCHROMETRIC CHART"]
    A6 --> A7["STEP 7 COMPUTE SUPPLY AIR QUANTITY"]
    A7 --> A8["STEP 8 SIZE COIL FAN DUCT CHILLER"]
    A8 --> A9["STEP 9 SELECT PUMPS AND TOWER"]
    A9 --> A10["STEP 10 DESIGN CONTROLS AND INSULATION"]
```

---

### 4.3 Year-Round 4-Pipe System (Hot & Cold Water)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CH["CHILLER 7 C SUPPLY 12 C RETURN"] --> CWP["CHILLED WATER PUMP"]
    BOI["BOILER 80 C SUPPLY 65 C RETURN"] --> HWP["HOT WATER PUMP"]
    CWP --> FCU1["FCU ZONE A COOLING COIL"]
    CWP --> FCU2["FCU ZONE B COOLING COIL"]
    HWP --> FCU1H["FCU ZONE A HEATING COIL"]
    HWP --> FCU2H["FCU ZONE B HEATING COIL"]
    FCU1H --> FCU1
    FCU2H --> FCU2
    FCU1 --> RM1["ZONE A ROOM"]
    FCU2 --> RM2["ZONE B ROOM"]
    CH --> CWRT["CHILLED WATER RETURN TO CHILLER"]
    BOI --> HWRT["HOT WATER RETURN TO BOILER"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **4-pipe system** allows simultaneous heating and cooling in different zones — essential in **hotels, hospitals, and offices** with different orientations and internal gains.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3 Mark Questions

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1]**
> **Q: Define Room Sensible Heat Factor (RSHF) and Effective Room Sensible Heat Factor (ERSHF). State the relationship between them.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **RSHF** is the ratio of room sensible heat to total (sensible + latent) room heat: $RSHF = Q_s / (Q_s + Q_l)$. (1 mark)
- **ERSHF** accounts for the heat re-emitted from lights and equipment into the return air (radiant fraction), and is used for coil selection. (1 mark)
- **Relationship:** $ERSHF = \dfrac{Q_s - F_r Q_{light} - F_r Q_{equip}}{Q_{total} - F_r Q_{light} - F_r Q_{equip}}$, where $F_r$ is the radiation factor. (1 mark)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q2]**
> **Q: Define Bypass Factor (BF) of a cooling coil. How is the Apparatus Dew Point (ADP) determined from BF?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **BF** is the fraction of air that passes through the coil without contacting the cooling surface: $BF = (T_2 - T_1)/(T_w - T_1)$, where $T_w$ = coil surface temp, $T_1$ = entering air temp, $T_2$ = leaving air temp. (1.5 marks)
- **ADP** is the temperature of air if it were cooled to saturation along the **coil line** (constant BF line) on the psychrometric chart. It is found by drawing the line from **Room Condition** to **Supply Condition** and extending it to the saturation curve. (1.5 marks)

---

### PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]

**A) (7 marks)** A conference hall of size 20 m × 12 m × 4 m has 60 occupants (light office work), 30 fluorescent tubes of 40 W (ballast factor 1.2, usage 0.6), 5 personal computers (200 W each, usage 0.5, $F_r = 0.5$). One external wall (20 × 4) with one glass window 4 m × 3 m, double-glazed, West-facing. Outdoor 38 °C DBT, 27 °C WBT. Indoor 24 °C DBT, 50% RH. Take CLTD for wall = 12 K, roof = 18 K, glass CLTD = 9 K, SHGF (West) = 400 W/m², SC = 0.78, U_glass = 3.0 W/m²K, U_wall = 2.0 W/m²K, U_roof = 1.5 W/m²K, CLF = 0.6. Take infiltration = 1.5 ACH. Compute (i) Room Sensible Heat (RSH), (ii) Room Latent Heat (RLH), and (iii) RSHF.

**B) (7 marks)** Using the values from part (A), assuming a coil BF of 0.18, determine (i) Apparatus Dew Point (ADP), (ii) supply air quantity in m³/s and CFM, and (iii) refrigeration tonnage. Take $\Delta T = 10$ °C. State the standard chiller size.

---

##### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION A**

**Part (A) – 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Wall (excluding glass)**
$A_w = 20 \times 4 - 4 \times 3 = 80 - 12 = 68$ m²
$Q_{wall} = 2.0 \times 68 \times 12 = 1632$ W = 1.632 kW

**Step 2 — Glass Wall**
$Q_{g,cond} = 3.0 \times 12 \times 9 = 324$ W
$Q_{g,sol} = 12 \times 400 \times 0.78 \times 0.6 = 2246.4$ W
$Q_{glass} = 2570.4$ W ≈ 2.57 kW

**Step 3 — Roof**
$A_r = 20 \times 12 = 240$ m²
$Q_{roof} = 1.5 \times 240 \times 18 = 6480$ W = 6.48 kW

**Step 4 — Lighting**
$Q_{light} = 30 \times 40 \times 0.6 \times 1.2 = 864$ W = 0.864 kW

**Step 5 — Equipment**
$Q_{equip} = 5 \times 200 \times 0.5 \times 1.0 = 500$ W = 0.50 kW

**Step 6 — Occupants (Light office: $q_s = 67$ W, $q_l = 47$ W)**
$Q_{occ,s} = 60 \times 67 = 4020$ W = 4.02 kW
$Q_{occ,l} = 60 \times 47 = 2820$ W = 2.82 kW

**Step 7 — Infiltration**
Volume = $20 \times 12 \times 4 = 960$ m³
$\dot{V}_{inf} = 1.5 \times 960 / 3600 = 0.4$ m³/s
$Q_{inf,s} = 1.2 \times 0.4 \times 1.005 \times (38 - 24) = 6.75$ kW
$W_o$ (38 °C, 27 °C WBT) ≈ 0.0162; $W_i$ (24 °C, 50% RH) = 0.0094
$Q_{inf,l} = 1.2 \times 0.4 \times (0.0162 - 0.0094) \times 2454 = 8.0$ kW

**Step 8 — Totals**
$Q_s = 1.632 + 2.57 + 6.48 + 0.864 + 0.50 + 4.02 + 6.75 = 22.82$ kW
$Q_l = 2.82 + 8.0 = 10.82$ kW
$Q_{total} = 33.64$ kW

**RSHF** = $22.82 / 33.64 = 0.678$ (≈ 0.68)

**Part (B) – 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — ADP Determination**
On psychrometric chart, join Room point (24 °C, 50% RH) to Supply point (14 °C, ~90% RH). For BF = 0.18 line from the room condition, ADP ≈ **10.5 °C** (read off the saturation curve). (2 marks)

**Step 2 — Supply Air Quantity**
$Q_s = 22.82$ kW, $\Delta T = 10$ °C
$\dot{V}_a = 22.82 / (1.2 \times 10) = 1.902$ m³/s
Convert to CFM: $1.902 \times 2118.88 = 4029$ CFM (since 1 m³/s = 2118.88 CFM). (2 marks)

**Step 3 — Refrigeration Tonnage**
$TR = 33.64 / 3.517 = 9.56$ TR
With 15% safety margin → 11.0 TR.
**Select a standard 11 TR (or 12 TR) chiller.** (1 mark)

**Step 4 — Pump Sizing (Bonus)**
$\dot{V}_{cw} = Q_{chiller} / (4.186 \times \Delta T_{cw}) = 33.64 / (4.186 \times 5) = 1.608$ L/s = 96.5 L/min = 25.5 GPM. (1 mark)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**A) (7 marks)** Explain with a neat sketch the **Equal Friction Method** of duct design. Derive the expression for round duct diameter in terms of flow rate, friction rate, and air density.

**B) (7 marks)** A 5 TR air-conditioning system uses a chilled water coil with BF = 0.20. Room conditions are 26 °C DBT and 20 °C WBT. Coil inlet air temperature is 26 °C. Determine (i) the ADP of the coil, (ii) the leaving air DBT and humidity ratio, and (iii) the apparatus sensible heat factor. State one assumption.

##### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION B**

**Part (A) – 7 Marks**

**Equal Friction Method** (assumes constant pressure drop per unit length, $R \approx 0.8$ Pa/m or 0.1 in.wg/100 ft).

From Darcy–Weisbach:
$$\frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{f}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho V^2}{2}$$

Substituting $V = \dfrac{4 \dot{V}}{\pi D^2}$:

$$\frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{8 f \rho \dot{V}^2}{\pi^2 D^5}$$

Solving for $D$:

$$D = \left( \frac{8 f \rho \dot{V}^2}{\pi^2 R} \right)^{1/5}$$

> A sketch showing the main trunk with branch take-offs, a velocity profile, and pressure gradient should accompany. (4 marks derivation + 3 marks sketch/explanation)

**Part (B) – 7 Marks**

Given: Room = 26 °C DBT, 20 °C WBT. From chart: $W_{room} \approx 0.0117$ kg/kg, $h_{room} \approx 55.5$ kJ/kg.

Coil inlet = room condition. BF = 0.20.

(i) **ADP**:
On chart, along the saturation curve (100% RH), reading along the constant BF line from room condition, **ADP ≈ 13.0 °C**, $W_{ADP} \approx 0.0091$ kg/kg, $h_{ADP} \approx 36.5$ kJ/kg. (2 marks)

(ii) **Leaving air DBT**: 
Using $BF = (T_1 - T_2)/(T_1 - T_{ADP})$:
$0.20 = (26 - T_2)/(26 - 13)$
$T_2 = 26 - 0.20 \times 13 = 26 - 2.6 = 23.4$ °C. (2 marks)

$W_2 = W_{room} - BF \cdot (W_{room} - W_{ADP}) = 0.0117 - 0.20(0.0117 - 0.0091) = 0.0111$ kg/kg. (1 mark)

(iii) **Apparatus Sensible Heat Factor (ASHF)**:
Using enthalpy:
$ASHF = \dfrac{h_{room} - h_{supply}}{c_p (T_{room} - T_{supply})}$ — calculated from process on chart.
$ASHF \approx 0.80$ to 0.85 (coil specific). (1 mark)

**Assumption:** Uniform air distribution over coil face, single-pass heat exchanger, no moisture condensation on fins above ADP. (1 mark)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Do NOT mix TR (Refrigeration Tonnage) with electrical ton.** 1 TR = 3.517 kW of cooling. Many students write "ton of refrigeration = 3.5 kW" — that's OK, but **never** use "tons of AC" loosely.
> 2. **RSHF ≠ ERSHF.** Using RSHF for coil ADP selection without subtracting the **radiant portion** of lights/equipment leads to **oversized cooling coil** — common 1-mark loss in valuation.
> 3. **Unit errors in psychrometric calculations** ($W$ in kg/kg dry air, $h$ in kJ/kg dry air) — students often write $W$ in g/kg and lose 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting the 1.2 kg/m³ air density constant** in $V_a = Q_s / (1.2 \cdot \Delta T)$ is a frequent mistake. Always show it.
> 5. **Duct design questions** require BOTH derivation (4 marks) AND the equivalent rectangular conversion or a sketch (3 marks). Skipping the sketch loses a full mark band.
> 6. **In CLTD method**, do NOT forget to multiply by **CLF (Cooling Load Factor)** for glass solar gains — forgetting it makes the load unrealistic by 30–40%.
> 7. **Chilled water flow rate** is always based on **TOTAL load (sensible + latent)**, not just sensible. Common mistake.

---

### 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **Cooling Load = Sensible (envelope + lights + equipment + occupants + infiltration) + Latent (occupants + infiltration + ventilation)**
- ✅ **RSHF** governs **ADP selection**; **ERSHF** is for **coil selection** (accounts for radiant fraction of lights/equipment)
- ✅ **Supply air temp** typically 12 °C – 14 °C for comfort (i.e., $\Delta T \approx 10$ °C)
- ✅ **ADP** is read from the **psychrometric chart** at the intersection of the room–supply line with the **saturation curve**
- ✅ **Bypass Factor** ranges 0.10–0.30; more rows → lower BF → lower ADP
- ✅ **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 3024 kcal/h** — internalise this constant
- ✅ **Duct sizing** = Equal Friction (constant $\Delta P/L$) or Velocity Reduction or Static Regain
- ✅ **Chilled water $\Delta T$** typically 5 °C (7 °C in, 12 °C out)
- ✅ **Condenser water $\Delta T$** typically 5 °C (32 °C in, 37 °C out)
- ✅ **4-pipe system** = simultaneous heating and cooling in different zones (use FCU/AHU)
- ✅ **Safety margin** = 10–15% on TR for chiller selection
- ✅ **Design sequence**: Load → RSHF → ADP → Air quantity → Coil → Duct → Pump → Tower
- ✅ **ASHRAE** Standard 55 (thermal comfort) + IS 3315 govern indoor design conditions
- ✅ **CLTD/CLF/SHGF/SC** are the four key solar heat gain parameters — used **only for glass** load calculation
- ✅ **Infiltration assumption**: 0.5–1.5 ACH for tight commercial spaces, 1.5–2.0 ACH for loose residential

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Handling and Distribution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Handling and Distribution # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Handling and Distribution** refers to the integrated set of operations, technologies, equipment, and management practices employed to move perishable products (fruits, vegetables, dairy, meat, seafood, pharmaceuticals) from the point of harvest/production through pre-cooling, processing, storage, transport, and final retail delivery while preserving their quality, safety, and shelf-life under controlled temperature conditions. In the strict KTU 2024 engineering terminology, this topic encompasses three interrelated subsystems: 1. **Material Handling** — Physical movement of goods within and between cold chain nodes using mechanical aids (forklifts, conveyors, pallet jacks, automated guided vehicles). 2. **Distribution Logistics** — The refrigerated transport network (road, rail, marine, air) connecting producers to consumers. 3. **Cold Chain Integrity Management** — Monitoring temperature, humidity, and product condition continuously throughout the journey. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Refrigerated Highway" > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Think of the cold chain like a **refrigerated highway system**. The product is a "passenger" that must never leave the "cool comfort" of an air-conditioned vehicle. If at any toll booth (handling point) or rest stop (distribution center) the AC fails, the passenger suffers. Each link in the chain — from harvest → cold store → truck → retail freezer → home fridge — must maintain the temperature. The "Handling and Distribution" topic is the engineering of how we keep the AC running across all toll booths and rest stops on this highway. ## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance | Term | Definition | |------|------------| | **Cold Chain** | Temperature-controlled supply chain from production to consumption | | **Pre-cooling** | Rapid removal of field heat from produce before storage/transport | | **Reefer** | Refrigerated container used in marine/road transport | | **Respiration Heat** | Metabolic heat generated by living fruits/vegetables (W/tonne) | | **Pull-down Time** | Time required to lower product temperature to storage temperature | | **Transit Heat Load** | Total heat entering the transport vehicle from all sources | | **HACCP** | Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points — food safety protocol | | **Specific Heat of Produce** | Typically **3.5 to 4.0 kJ/kg·K** above freezing, **1.7 to 2.0 kJ/kg·K** below | | **Latent Heat of Freezing (water)** | **335 kJ/kg** | | **Recommended Storage RH** | **85% – 95%** for most fruits and vegetables | ## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in the KTU 2024 Scheme > [!IMPORTANT] > In a country like India, where **~30% of fresh produce is wasted** due to inadequate cold chain infrastructure, mastering handling and distribution is critical. KTU 2024 specifically tests: > - **CO4 (Module 4):** Design cold storage systems and refrigeration distribution networks. > - **CO5:** Apply psychrometrics and load calculations to real industrial systems. This topic directly contributes to the **"Design and Application"** cognitive level of Bloom's Taxonomy as prescribed by NEP 2020. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Cold Chain Architecture (Step-by-Step Logic) The cold chain is broken into **six sequential phases**. Each phase has specific engineering requirements. ### Step 1: Harvest / Collection Point - **Why:** Field heat must be removed quickly because every hour of delay reduces shelf-life. - **How:** Mobile pre-cooling units or immediate transfer to a nearby cold store. - **Critical metric:** Field heat is the **enthalpy difference** between the harvested product temperature (typically **25°C – 35°C** in tropical climates) and the storage temperature. ### Step 2: Pre-cooling Station - **Methods used:** - **Room cooling** (slow, low-cost) - **Forced-air cooling** (10× faster, most common) - **Hydro-cooling** (fast, for apples, carrots) - **Vacuum cooling** (fastest, for leafy vegetables) - **Ice cooling** (contact icing for fish) - **Goal:** Drop product temperature to within **5%** of the storage temperature within **2–6 hours**. ### Step 3: Cold Storage Warehouse - **Temperature zones:** - Chilled storage: **0°C to 8°C** (fruits, vegetables, dairy) - Frozen storage: **-18°C to -25°C** (meat, seafood, ice cream) - Deep frozen: **below -25°C** - **Engineering features:** Insulated panels (PUF/PIR), vapor barriers, ammonia or HFC refrigeration, humidity control. ### Step 4: Distribution Center (DC) - A cross-docking hub where pallets are consolidated, route-optimized, and loaded onto delivery vehicles. - Includes **ripening rooms** (for bananas, mangoes, tomatoes) with ethylene control. ### Step 5: Transportation (Refrigerated Vehicles) - The "moving cold store" — see Section 2.3 for detailed analysis. ### Step 6: Retail Display & Home Refrigerator - Final leg with **open refrigerated display cabinets** (chiller: **2°C – 8°C**, freezer: **-18°C**). ## 2.2 Heat Load in a Refrigerated Transport Vehicle > [!NOTE] > The total refrigeration load on a transport vehicle is the sum of **six distinct heat sources**. Mastering this is the most-tested KTU concept in this module. The six sources are: 1. **Transmission Load ($Q_1$)** — Heat through walls, roof, floor of the container. 2. **Infiltration Load ($Q_2$)** — Heat from warm air entering when doors open. 3. **Product Load ($Q_3$)** — Heat removed from the cargo itself. 4. **Respiration Load ($Q_4$)** — Metabolic heat of living produce. 5. **Internal Equipment Load ($Q_5$)** — Heat from fans, lights, refrigeration units inside. 6. **Miscellaneous Load ($Q_6$)** — Heat from defrost cycles, occupants, solar radiation through glass. ## 2.3 Modes of Refrigerated Distribution | Mode | Capacity | Temperature Range | Application | |------|----------|-------------------|-------------| | **Refrigerated Road Vehicle** | 1–25 tonnes | -25°C to +10°C | Local / regional delivery, dairy, ice cream | | **Insulated Container (Reefer)** | 20–40 ft TEU | -30°C to +30°C | International sea freight | | **Refrigerated Rail Wagon** | 30–60 tonnes | -20°C to +10°C | Long-distance bulk (potatoes, apples) | | **Air Freight** | Variable | +2°C to +8°C | High-value, time-critical (pharma, seafood) | | **Multi-modal Intermodal** | Combined | All ranges | End-to-end logistics | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains **all formulas** required for solving any KTU 2024 numerical on this topic. Memorize the units alongside each formula. | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $Q_1 = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Transmission heat load through insulated walls | $U$ = W/m²·K, $A$ = m², $\Delta T$ = K | | 2 | $U = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{x}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}}$ | Overall heat transfer coefficient (series resistance) | W/m²·K | | 3 | $Q_2 = V_{infiltration} \cdot \rho \cdot (h_o - h_i)$ | Infiltration load via door openings | kW | | 4 | $Q_3 = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat of product cooling (above freezing) | kW | | 5 | $Q_3^* = m \cdot L_f$ | Latent heat of freezing (water content basis) | kW | | 6 | $Q_3^{mixed} = m [c_{pf}(T_1 - T_f) + L_f + c_{ps}(T_f - T_2)]$ | Total heat for freezing fresh product | kW | | 7 | $Q_4 = m_{prod} \cdot r$ | Respiration heat of living produce | $r$ = W/tonne | | 8 | $Q_5 = \sum P_{motor} \cdot \eta_{usage}$ | Internal equipment load (fans, lights) | kW | | 9 | $t_{cool} = \dfrac{m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}{h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}}$ | Pre-cooling / pull-down time | hours | | 10 | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log Mean Temperature Difference | K | | 11 | $COP_{transport} = \dfrac{Q_{evap}}{W_{comp}}$ | Coefficient of Performance of transport unit | dimensionless | | 12 | $m_{insulation} = \rho_{PUF} \cdot V_{panel}$ | Insulation mass balance for cost | kg | > [!NOTE] > **Boundary Conditions to Remember:** > - **$U$** for a typical insulated truck wall: **0.3 – 0.6 W/m²·K** > - **$k_{PUF}$** (Polyurethane Foam): **0.020 – 0.024 W/m·K** > - **$k_{EPS}$** (Expanded Polystyrene): **0.035 W/m·K** > - **$c_p$** of fresh produce above freezing: **3.5 – 4.0 kJ/kg·K** > - **$c_p$** of frozen produce: **1.7 – 2.1 kJ/kg·K** > - **Respiration rate** of apples at **0°C**: **10 – 15 W/tonne**; of bananas at **15°C**: **100+ W/tonne** ## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility This topic is the backbone of the **cold chain logistics industry** (worth **~$300+ billion globally**). Engineers design: - **Reefer containers** for Maersk, CMA CGM shipping lines - **Cold chain hubs** for BigBasket, Amazon Fresh, Flipkart - **Pharma cold chain** (Covishield vaccine at **2°C – 8°C**, mRNA vaccines at **-70°C**) - **Ripening chambers** for Kerala's banana and mango exports - **CIFT (Cochin) seafood cold chain** for marine exports from Kerala coast # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Total Refrigeration Load of a Transport Vehicle > [!IMPORTANT] > This derivation is the **single most important numerical** you must master for KTU ESE. Examiners set this or a variant almost every cycle. ### Problem Setup A refrigerated truck is used to transport **5000 kg of fresh fish** from the harbor to an inland cold store. The fish is to be cooled from an initial temperature of **15°C** down to **-5°C** (for ice-stored fish). The truck dimensions are **5.0 m × 2.2 m × 2.2 m**. The ambient temperature is **35°C**, and the desired cargo temperature is **-5°C**. The journey time is **6 hours**. **Given additional data:** - Insulation: Polyurethane Foam, thickness **100 mm**, $k = 0.022$ W/m·K - Inner surface coefficient $h_i = 9$ W/m²·K, outer $h_o = 23$ W/m²·K - Specific heat of fish above freezing $c_{pf} = 3.5$ kJ/kg·K - Specific heat of fish below freezing $c_{ps} = 1.8$ kJ/kg·K - Freezing point of fish $T_f = -1°C$ - Latent heat of fish $L_f = 230$ kJ/kg (lower than water because of oil/fat content) - Internal fan load: **0.4 kW**, Lights: **0.1 kW**, Compressor in cargo space: negligible - Respiration heat: **not applicable** (fish is non-living after harvest) ### Step 1 — Compute Surface Areas of the Container $$ \begin{aligned} A_{walls} &= 2 \times (L \times H) + 2 \times (W \times H) \\ &= 2 \times (5.0 \times 2.2) + 2 \times (2.2 \times 2.2) \\ &= 22.0 + 9.68 = 31.68 \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} A_{roof} &= L \times W = 5.0 \times 2.2 = 11.0 \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} A_{floor} &= L \times W = 5.0 \times 2.2 = 11.0 \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} A_{total} &= 31.68 + 11.0 + 11.0 = 53.68 \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** Every insulated panel that separates the cold interior from the warm exterior contributes to transmission loss. We sum all six faces. ### Step 2 — Compute Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient $U$ $$ \begin{aligned} U &= \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{x}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}} \\ &= \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{9} + \dfrac{0.100}{0.022} + \dfrac{1}{23}} \\ &= \frac{1}{0.1111 + 4.5455 + 0.0435} \\ &= \frac{1}{4.7001} \\ U &= 0.2128 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K} \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** Heat must pass through three resistances in series — the inner air film, the insulation, and the outer air film. The insulation dominates here. ### Step 3 — Transmission Heat Load $Q_1$ $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta T &= T_{outside} - T_{inside} = 35 - (-5) = 40 \text{ K} \\ Q_1 &= U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T \\ &= 0.2128 \times 53.68 \times 40 \\ Q_1 &= 457.0 \text{ W} \approx 0.457 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** Heat leaks inward due to the temperature gradient. A higher $\Delta T$ or thinner insulation dramatically increases $Q_1$. ### Step 4 — Infiltration Load $Q_2$ Assume **2 door openings** per hour, each lasting **15 seconds**, with a door area of **1.5 m × 1.8 m**. Air exchange volume per opening (assume 50% volume replacement): $$ \begin{aligned} V_{open} &= 0.5 \times A_{door} \times v_{air} \times t \\ &\approx 0.5 \times 1.5 \times 1.8 \times 1.0 \times 15 = 20.25 \text{ m}^3/\text{opening} \end{aligned} $$ Number of openings in 6 hours = **12**. $$ \begin{aligned} V_{total} &= 12 \times 20.25 = 243 \text{ m}^3 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} Q_2 &= \frac{V_{total} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot c_{p,air} \cdot \Delta T}{t \cdot 3600} \\ &= \frac{243 \times 1.2 \times 1.005 \times 40}{6 \times 3600} \\ Q_2 &= \frac{11716}{21600} = 0.542 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** Warm air enters when doors open. We count total air volume exchanged and multiply by its enthalpy difference. ### Step 5 — Product (Fish) Cooling Load $Q_3$ Fish must be cooled from **15°C** to **-5°C** crossing the freezing point at **-1°C**. Three stages: $$ \begin{aligned} Q_3 &= m \left[ c_{pf}(T_1 - T_f) + L_f + c_{ps}(T_f - T_2) \right] \\ &= 5000 \left[ 3.5 \times (15 - (-1)) + 230 + 1.8 \times (-1 - (-5)) \right] \\ &= 5000 \left[ 3.5 \times 16 + 230 + 1.8 \times 4 \right] \\ &= 5000 \left[ 56 + 230 + 7.2 \right] \\ &= 5000 \times 293.2 \\ Q_3 &= 1{,}466{,}000 \text{ kJ for the entire 6 hours} \end{aligned} $$ Converting to average kW over 6 hours: $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{Q}_3 &= \frac{1{,}466{,}000}{6 \times 3600} = \frac{1{,}466{,}000}{21{,}600} \\ \dot{Q}_3 &= 67.87 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** Latent heat removal (the freezing of water inside the fish cells) is the **largest single term** by far. This is why freezing is energy-intensive and why pre-chilling is crucial. ### Step 6 — Internal Equipment Load $Q_5$ $$ \begin{aligned} Q_5 &= P_{fan} + P_{light} = 0.4 + 0.1 = 0.5 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 7 — Total Refrigeration Load $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{total} &= Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3 + Q_5 \\ &= 0.457 + 0.542 + 67.87 + 0.5 \\ \boxed{Q_{total} &= 69.37 \text{ kW}} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 8 — Selecting the Transport Refrigeration Unit Compressor capacity needed (assuming a typical $COP = 1.8$ for a transport unit): $$ \begin{aligned} W_{comp} &= \frac{Q_{total}}{COP} = \frac{69.37}{1.8} = 38.5 \text{ kW} \\ &\approx 52 \text{ HP compressor} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Heat Load Contribution Ranking | Heat Source | Magnitude (kW) | % of Total | Mitigation Strategy | |-------------|---------------:|-----------:|---------------------| | Product cooling ($Q_3$) | **67.87** | **97.83%** | Pre-cool at harbor before loading | | Infiltration ($Q_2$) | 0.542 | 0.78% | Use air curtains, strip curtains at doors | | Internal equipment ($Q_5$) | 0.500 | 0.72% | Use DC fans, LED lights | | Transmission ($Q_1$) | 0.457 | 0.66% | Thicker PUF, vacuum insulated panels | | Respiration ($Q_4$) | 0 (fish) | 0% | N/A for non-living cargo | | **Total** | **69.37 kW** | **100%** | — | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for KTU:** For non-living cargo (fish, meat), $Q_3$ dominates. For living produce (fruits), $Q_4$ (respiration) can rival $Q_3$ for tropical fruits. Always identify the cargo type first. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical 2 — Pre-cooling Time Calculation **Problem:** Cool **2000 kg of mangoes** from **28°C** to **13°C** in a forced-air cooler. Use $c_p = 3.8$ kJ/kg·K. Air enters at **5°C** with $h = 25$ W/m²·K. Effective contact surface area **A = 15 m²**. $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta T_{lm} &= \frac{(28 - 5) - (13 - 5)}{\ln\left(\frac{28 - 5}{13 - 5}\right)} \\ &= \frac{23 - 8}{\ln(23/8)} = \frac{15}{\ln(2.875)} = \frac{15}{1.0566} \\ \Delta T_{lm} &= 14.20 \text{ K} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} t &= \frac{m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}{h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} \\ &= \frac{2000 \times 3.8 \times 15}{25 \times 15 \times 14.20} \\ &= \frac{114000}{5325} \\ \boxed{t &= 21.4 \text{ hours}} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Notice that **$\Delta T_{lm}$** (log-mean) is used, not the simple arithmetic mean. This is because temperature difference reduces exponentially during cooling. KTU examiners award **2 marks** specifically for using $\Delta T_{lm}$ correctly. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Cold Chain Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[HARVEST SITE] -->|Field Heat 30C| B[PRE COOLING UNIT] B -->|Forced Air or Hydro| C[COLD STORAGE WAREHOUSE] C -->|Cross Docking| D[DISTRIBUTION CENTER] D -->|Reefer Truck| E[RETAIL DISPLAY] E -->|Home Fridge| F[END CONSUMER] C -.->|Backup| G[FROZEN STORAGE -18C] D -.->|Air Freight| H[EXPORT PORT] H -.->|Reefer Container 40ft| I[OVERSEAS MARKET] style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513 style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B style C fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B style D fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400 style E fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000 style F fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082 style G fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B style H fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8000 style I fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000 ``` ## 4.2 Heat Load Architecture Flow for a Refrigerated Vehicle ```mermaid flowchart TB Q1[Q1 Transmission Load
U x A x dT] --> SUM[TOTAL REFRIGERATION LOAD Qtotal] Q2[Q2 Infiltration Load
Door Openings] --> SUM Q3[Q3 Product Load
m x cp x dT] --> SUM Q4[Q4 Respiration Load
m x r] --> SUM Q5[Q5 Equipment Load
Fans and Lights] --> SUM Q6[Q6 Solar and Misc] --> SUM SUM --> COP{COP of Unit} COP -->|Divide by COP| COMP[Compressor kW Required] COMP --> UNIT[Select Transport Unit] style Q1 fill:#FFB6C1 style Q2 fill:#FFB6C1 style Q3 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000 style Q4 fill:#90EE90 style Q5 fill:#87CEEB style Q6 fill:#DDA0DD style SUM fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B8000 style COMP fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400 ``` ## 4.3 Pre-cooling Methods Selection Matrix ```mermaid flowchart TB START[Choose Pre Cooling Method] --> Q{Product Type?} Q -->|Leafy Vegetables| VAC[Vacuum Cooling] Q -->|Apples and Carrots| HYD[Hydro Cooling] Q -->|Fish and Seafood| ICE[Ice Cooling] Q -->|Stone Fruits Berries| FAC[Forced Air Cooling] Q -->|Bulk Potatoes| ROOM[Room Cooling] VAC --> T1[Time 30 min] HYD --> T2[Time 1 hour] ICE --> T3[Time 2 hours] FAC --> T4[Time 2 to 4 hours] ROOM --> T5[Time 12 to 24 hours] style START fill:#FFD700 style VAC fill:#90EE90 style HYD fill:#87CEEB style ICE fill:#ADD8E6 style FAC fill:#FFB6C1 style ROOM fill:#F0E68C ``` ## 4.4 Distribution Mode Selection Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Perishable Cargo] --> B{Distance?} B -->|< 200 km| R[Refrigerated Truck] B -->|200 to 1000 km| R2[Insulated Truck with Ice Packs] B -->|> 1000 km| R3[Reefer Container Rail] B -->|International| R4[Reefer Container Sea Freight] R --> RFM[Capacity 1 to 25 tonnes] R2 --> RFM2[Capacity 5 to 15 tonnes] R3 --> RFM3[Capacity 30 to 60 tonnes] R4 --> RFM4[Capacity 20 to 40 TEU] style A fill:#FFD700 style R fill:#98FB98 style R2 fill:#87CEEB style R3 fill:#FFB6C1 style R4 fill:#DDA0DD ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Q: What is a "cold chain"? List any four components of a cold chain.** **Model Answer:** A cold chain is an uninterrupted sequence of refrigerated production, storage, and distribution activities that maintains perishable products at a specified temperature from harvest to consumption. **Four Components:** 1. **Pre-cooling facility** at the production site 2. **Cold storage warehouse** for intermediate holding 3. **Refrigerated transport** (reefer trucks/containers) 4. **Retail cold storage** (display cabinets and home refrigerators) *(1 mark definition + 0.5 each for 4 components = 3 marks)* ### Question A2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q: Differentiate between "field heat" and "respiration heat" with one example each.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Field Heat | Respiration Heat | |-----------|------------|------------------| | **Source** | Solar + ambient heating of harvested produce | Metabolic activity (oxidation of sugars) | | **Example** | Mango harvested at **32°C** to be cooled to **13°C** | Bananas releasing **CO₂** and heat during ripening | | **Removal** | One-time, by pre-cooling | Continuous throughout storage life | | **Magnitude** | High initially, then zero | Continuous, increases with temperature | *(1.5 marks each side = 3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### 📝 Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the various methods of pre-cooling of fruits and vegetables. Compare their relative cooling rates, energy consumption, and suitable applications. **(b) [7 Marks]** A refrigerated truck with internal dimensions **6 m × 2.4 m × 2.4 m** is used to transport **8000 kg of bananas** from a packing station to a port, a journey of **8 hours**. Bananas enter at **20°C** and must reach **13°C**. The ambient temperature is **32°C**. Insulation is **PUF of thickness 80 mm** with $k = 0.024$ W/m·K, $h_i = 8$ W/m²·K, $h_o = 22$ W/m²·K. The banana respiration rate is **35 W/tonne** at 15°C. Internal fan load = **0.5 kW**, lights = **0.15 kW**. Calculate: 1. The transmission heat load 2. The product cooling load ($c_p = 3.5$ kJ/kg·K) 3. The total refrigeration load --- ### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question A #### Part (a) — Pre-cooling Methods [7 Marks] > [!NOTE] > This question carries 7 marks: 1 for the introduction, 4 for the method descriptions (1 each), 1 for the comparison table, and 1 for the application summary. **Introduction:** Pre-cooling is the rapid removal of field heat from freshly harvested produce to slow down respiration, retard ripening, and extend shelf-life. **Method 1: Room Cooling [1 mark]** - Produce placed in a cold room with cool air circulating slowly. - Cooling time: **12–24 hours**. - Energy efficient but very slow. Used for potatoes, citrus. **Method 2: Forced-Air Cooling [1 mark]** - Cool air is **forced through stacked produce** using fans and pressure differentials. - Cooling time: **2–6 hours**, **10× faster** than room cooling. - Most widely used commercially for strawberries, grapes, stone fruits. **Method 3: Hydro-cooling [1 mark]** - Produce drenched or immersed in chilled water (**0°C – 2°C**). - Cooling time: **30 min – 2 hours**. - Excellent heat transfer. Used for apples, carrots, sweet corn, peaches. **Method 4: Vacuum Cooling [1 mark]** - Produce placed in a sealed chamber; pressure reduced to **4–6 mbar**; water evaporates at **0°C**, removing latent heat. - Cooling time: **20–40 minutes**. - Most expensive. Best for **leafy vegetables** (lettuce, spinach) with high surface-to-volume ratio. **Method 5: Contact/Ice Cooling [1 mark]** - Crushed ice placed in direct contact with produce (often packed in cartons). - Used for fish, broccoli, leafy greens during transit. **Comparison Table:** | Method | Cooling Time | Cost | Best For | |--------|--------------|------|----------| | Room | 12–24 hr | Low | Potatoes, citrus | | Forced Air | 2–6 hr | Medium | Berries, grapes | | Hydro | 0.5–2 hr | Medium | Apples, carrots | | Vacuum | 20–40 min | High | Leafy vegetables | | Ice | 2–4 hr | Low | Fish, broccoli | *(1 mark for the comparison summary + 1 mark for application conclusion = 7 marks total)* --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks] **Given:** - Dimensions: 6 × 2.4 × 2.4 m - Mass of bananas: **8000 kg** - $T_1 = 20°C$, $T_2 = 13°C$, $T_{amb} = 32°C$, $t = 8$ hours - PUF: $x = 0.08$ m, $k = 0.024$ W/m·K, $h_i = 8$, $h_o = 22$ - Respiration: $r = 35$ W/tonne - Fans: 0.5 kW, Lights: 0.15 kW **Step 1 — Surface Area of Container [0.5 mark]** $$ \begin{aligned} A_{walls} &= 2(6 \times 2.4) + 2(2.4 \times 2.4) \\ &= 28.8 + 11.52 = 40.32 \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} A_{roof+floor} &= 2 \times (6 \times 2.4) = 28.8 \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} A_{total} &= 40.32 + 28.8 = 69.12 \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient [1 mark]** $$ \begin{aligned} U &= \frac{1}{\frac{1}{8} + \frac{0.08}{0.024} + \frac{1}{22}} \\ &= \frac{1}{0.125 + 3.333 + 0.0455} \\ &= \frac{1}{3.504} \\ U &= 0.2854 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Transmission Load $Q_1$ [1 mark]** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta T &= 32 - 13 = 19 \text{ K} \\ Q_1 &= U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 0.2854 \times 69.12 \times 19 \\ Q_1 &= 374.7 \text{ W} \approx 0.375 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Product Cooling Load $Q_3$ [2 marks]** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{3,\text{total}} &= m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \\ &= 8000 \times 3.5 \times (20 - 13) \\ &= 8000 \times 3.5 \times 7 = 196{,}000 \text{ kJ} \end{aligned} $$ Average rate over 8 hours: $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{Q}_3 &= \frac{196{,}000}{8 \times 3600} = \frac{196{,}000}{28{,}800} = 6.806 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Respiration Load $Q_4$ [1 mark]** $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{Q}_4 &= 35 \text{ W/tonne} \times 8 \text{ tonnes} = 280 \text{ W} = 0.28 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Equipment Load $Q_5$ [0.5 mark]** $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{Q}_5 &= 0.5 + 0.15 = 0.65 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7 — Total Load [1 mark]** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{total} &= Q_1 + Q_3 + Q_4 + Q_5 \\ &= 0.375 + 6.806 + 0.28 + 0.65 \\ \boxed{Q_{total} &= 8.11 \text{ kW}} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting $Q_4$ (respiration)** for living produce — costs **1 full mark**. > 2. **Using arithmetic mean temperature** instead of log-mean — costs **1 mark** in any cooling-time problem. > 3. **Not converting kJ to kW** by dividing by time — gives wrong units, **0.5 mark lost**. > 4. **Forgetting the floor area** when computing surface area — costs **0.5 mark**. > 5. **Ignoring COP** when selecting compressor — final answer selection is incomplete. --- ### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **cold chain concept** for horticultural produce. With a neat block diagram, explain the **six phases** of the cold chain. Discuss the **quality losses** that occur at each phase if temperature is broken. **(b) [7 Marks]** A marine reefer container of internal volume **60 m³** carries **25,000 kg of frozen shrimp** at **-20°C**. Container dimensions: **12.0 m × 2.4 m × 2.6 m**. Outside temperature is **40°C** with **90% RH**. Insulation is **PUF of 120 mm** thickness with $k = 0.022$ W/m·K. Surface coefficients: $h_i = 7$ W/m²·K, $h_o = 25$ W/m²·K. Internal load from fans = **0.6 kW**. Compute: 1. Overall $U$ value 2. Transmission heat load 3. Total refrigeration load (assume shrimp $c_p = 1.9$ kJ/kg·K, no latent load since already frozen) --- ### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question B #### Part (a) — Cold Chain Concept [7 Marks] *(This is a descriptive question: 1 mark for definition + 1 for the block diagram description + 5 marks for the six phases, distributed as 1 mark each minus 1 for quality losses synthesis.)* **Definition [1 mark]:** A cold chain is a continuous, unbroken sequence of refrigerated handling, storage, and distribution activities that preserves the quality and safety of perishable products from the point of origin to the final consumer. **Six Phases of Cold Chain:** | Phase | Temperature Range | Quality Risk if Broken | |-------|-------------------|------------------------| | 1. **Harvest / Collection** | 25°C – 35°C | Rapid field-heat buildup, water loss | | 2. **Pre-cooling** | Drops to 5°C – 13°C | Respiration continues, ripening accelerates | | 3. **Cold Storage** | 0°C – 13°C (chilled) / -18°C (frozen) | Microbial growth, senescence | | 4. **Distribution Center** | Same as storage | Condensation, rewarming at edges | | 5. **Transport** | Same as storage | Mechanical damage, thawing, refreezing burns | | 6. **Retail / Consumer** | 2°C – 8°C (chiller) / -18°C (freezer) | Display thaw, microbial contamination | **Quality Loss Discussion [3 marks]:** - **At harvest:** Each hour delay = **1 day shelf-life lost** for many tropical fruits. - **At pre-cooling:** Without it, **30–40% weight loss** in apples within 2 weeks. - **At transport:** Temperature fluctuation causes **ice crystal growth** in frozen foods, leading to texture damage (drip loss on thawing). - **At retail:** Open chillers cause **temperature stratification** of **3–5°C** between top and bottom shelves. *(Block diagram marks: 0.5 mark for each phase box — use the mermaid diagram from Section 4.1 as a model.)* --- #### Part (b) — Marine Reefer Numerical [7 Marks] **Given:** - $V = 60$ m³, $m = 25{,}000$ kg, $T_{cargo} = -20°C$, $T_{amb} = 40°C$ - Dimensions: 12.0 × 2.4 × 2.6 m - PUF: $x = 0.12$ m, $k = 0.022$ W/m·K - $h_i = 7$, $h_o = 25$, Fan = 0.6 kW - $c_p$ shrimp = 1.9 kJ/kg·K (already frozen, no latent load) **Step 1 — Surface Area [1 mark]** $$ \begin{aligned} A_{walls} &= 2(12 \times 2.6) + 2(2.4 \times 2.6) \\ &= 62.4 + 12.48 = 74.88 \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} A_{roof+floor} &= 2 \times (12 \times 2.4) = 57.6 \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} A_{total} &= 74.88 + 57.6 = 132.48 \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Overall $U$ [1.5 marks]** $$ \begin{aligned} U &= \frac{1}{\frac{1}{7} + \frac{0.12}{0.022} + \frac{1}{25}} \\ &= \frac{1}{0.1429 + 5.4545 + 0.04} \\ &= \frac{1}{5.6374} \\ U &= 0.1774 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Transmission Load $Q_1$ [1.5 marks]** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta T &= 40 - (-20) = 60 \text{ K} \\ Q_1 &= U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 0.1774 \times 132.48 \times 60 \\ Q_1 &= 1410 \text{ W} = 1.41 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Product Load $Q_3$ [1.5 marks]** Frozen shrimp has no latent load, so: $$ \begin{aligned} Q_3 &= m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T_{in-out} \\ &\approx 25{,}000 \times 1.9 \times 0 = 0 \text{ (steady-state)} \end{aligned} $$ Since the shrimp is already at the desired temperature and only needs to be maintained, $Q_3 \approx 0$. However, if there's a small steady-state sensible cooling of, say, **0.5°C** due to door openings during unloading, it becomes: $$ \begin{aligned} Q_3 &= \frac{25000 \times 1.9 \times 0.5}{3600 \times 1} = 6.6 \text{ kW (pulse)} \end{aligned} $$ For continuous average, assume **$Q_3 \approx 0.5$ kW** as a margin. **Step 5 — Equipment Load [0.5 mark]** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_5 &= 0.6 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Total Refrigeration Load [1 mark]** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{total} &= Q_1 + Q_3 + Q_5 \\ &= 1.41 + 0.5 + 0.6 \\ \boxed{Q_{total} &= 2.51 \text{ kW}} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall Callout for Frozen Cargo Problems:** > - Many students wrongly include a **latent load** for already-frozen shrimp.
