Use R equals 1 over 24] SLAB --> PLANK[Apply Planks Equation] CYL --> PLANK SPH --> PLANK PLANK --> ANS[Freezing Time in Seconds or Hours] style START fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000 style SLAB fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000,color:#000 style CYL fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 style SPH fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 style PLANK fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000 style ANS fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,color:#FFF ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **"Differentiate between chilling and freezing of food products. State the typical temperature ranges used for each."** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Chilling | Freezing | |--------|----------|----------| | Temperature Range | **0 °C to +8 °C** (above freezing point of food) | **−18 °C and below** (below freezing point) | | State of Water in Product | Liquid (unfrozen) | Mostly frozen (ice crystals formed) | | Heat Removal Required | **Sensible heat only** | **Sensible + Latent heat** | | Shelf Life Extension | Days to weeks (2–4 weeks for fruits) | Months to years (6–18 months for meat) | | Equipment Used | Cold rooms, chillers | Blast freezers, plate freezers, cryogenic tunnels | | Quality Impact | Slow metabolism of microbes & enzymes | Halts microbial growth; preserves nutrients | *Chilling is a milder preservation method that extends shelf life by a few days to weeks, whereas freezing converts a major portion of the product's water into ice, virtually halting all biological activity and enabling long-term storage.* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **"What is respiratory heat? Why is it important in the design of cold storage for fruits and vegetables?"** **Model Answer:** **Respiratory Heat** is the heat generated by the metabolic activity of living plant tissues (fruits, vegetables) after harvest, as they consume oxygen and release carbon dioxide, water vapour, and heat. It is calculated as $Q_{resp} = m \cdot q_r$, where $q_r$ is the rate of heat evolution per unit mass (W/kg) — a function of product type and storage temperature. **Importance in cold storage design:** 1. **Adds to refrigeration load** — it can be 5 % to 15 % of the total load for fresh produce, and must be included in the heat-load calculation. 2. **Determines storage temperature selection** — products with high $q_r$ (e.g., strawberries, peas) must be cooled rapidly to slow respiration. 3. **Influences ventilation design** — adequate airflow is needed to remove both heat and the CO₂ produced, preventing anaerobic conditions. 4. **Causes weight loss & shrivelling** — respiration depletes carbohydrate reserves, reducing marketable weight. --- ### 📗 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern) --- ### ✏️ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]** **(a)** A 750 kg batch of fresh fish is to be frozen from an initial temperature of +10 °C to a final storage temperature of −20 °C. The fish has 78 % moisture content and a freezing point of −2 °C. The latent heat of fusion of water is 335 kJ/kg. Determine: - (i) The specific heat of fish above and below freezing using standard food-engineering correlations. (4 marks) - (ii) The total refrigeration load in kJ. (3 marks) **(b)** A slab of meat, 10 cm thick, is to be frozen from +5 °C to −20 °C in an air-blast freezer. The air temperature is −35 °C, with $h = 25$ W/(m²·K). The density is 1050 kg/m³, latent heat $L = 240$ kJ/kg, thermal conductivity of frozen meat $\lambda = 1.4$ W/(m·K), and freezing point $\theta_f = -2.5\,°C$. Using **Plank's equation**, calculate the freezing time. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution — Q A (a) (i): Specific Heat **Above freezing (Callow's Rule):** $$ C_{pu} = 0.5 \times m_w = 0.5 \times 78 = 39 \;\;\text{kcal/(kg·°C)} = 39 \times 4.186 = 163.25 \;\;\text{kJ/(kg·K)} $$ **Below freezing (Heldman-Singh Modified Rule):** $$ C_{pf} = 0.5 \times m_w \times \left(0.5 + \frac{\vert T_2 \vert}{\vert T_f \vert}\right) $$ $$ C_{pf} = 0.5 \times 78 \times \left(0.5 + \frac{20}{2.5}\right) = 39 \times 8.5 = 331.5 \;\;\text{kcal/(kg·°C)} $$ **Simpler board-friendly version** (often accepted): $$ C_{pf} = 0.4 \times m_w = 0.4 \times 78 = 31.2 \;\;\text{kcal/(kg·°C)} = 130.6 \;\;\text{kJ/(kg·K)} $$ #### Model Solution — Q A (a) (ii): Total Refrigeration Load **Step 1: Sensible heat above freezing (using kcal units for clarity)** $$ Q_1 = m \cdot C_{pu} \cdot (T_1 - T_f) = 750 \times 39 \times (10 - (-2)) = 750 \times 39 \times 12 $$ $$ Q_1 = 351{,}000 \;\;\text{kcal} $$ **Step 2: Latent heat at freezing** $$ L_{\text{fish}} = m_w \times L_w = 0.78 \times 80 = 62.4 \;\;\text{kcal/kg} $$ $$ Q_2 = m \cdot L_{\text{fish}} = 750 \times 62.4 = 46{,}800 \;\;\text{kcal} $$ **Step 3: Sensible heat below freezing** Using $C_{pf} = 31.2$ kcal/(kg·°C): $$ Q_3 = m \cdot C_{pf} \cdot (T_f - T_2) = 750 \times 31.2 \times (-2 - (-20)) = 750 \times 31.2 \times 18 $$ $$ Q_3 = 421{,}200 \;\;\text{kcal} $$ **Step 4: Total** $$ Q_{total} = 351{,}000 + 46{,}800 + 421{,}200 = 819{,}000 \;\;\text{kcal} $$ $$ Q_{total} = 819{,}000 \times 4.186 = 3{,}428{,}334 \;\;\text{kJ} $$ $$ \boxed{Q_{total} \approx 3.43 \times 10^{6} \;\;\text{kJ} \;\;\text{(or 3.43 GJ)}} $$ --- #### Model Solution — Q A (b): Plank's Equation for Slab **Given:** - Thickness $D = 0.10$ m - $\theta_f = -2.5\,°C$, $\theta_a = -35\,°C$ - $h = 25$ W/(m²·K), $\lambda = 1.4$ W/(m·K) - $\rho = 1050$ kg/m³, $L = 240{,}000$ J/kg - For slab: $P = 1/2$, $R = 1/8$ **Step 1: Apply Plank's equation (simplified KTU form)** $$ t_f = \frac{\rho L}{(\theta_a - \theta_f)}\left[\frac{P D}{h} + \frac{R D^2}{\lambda}\right] $$ **Step 2: Compute $\theta_a - \theta_f$** $$ \theta_a - \theta_f = -35 - (-2.5) = -32.5\,°C \quad \Rightarrow \quad |32.5|\,K $$ **Step 3: Compute bracketed term** $$ \frac{P D}{h} = \frac{0.5 \times 0.10}{25} = \frac{0.05}{25} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \;\;\text{m}^2\text{·K/W} $$ $$ \frac{R D^2}{\lambda} = \frac{0.125 \times 0.01}{1.4} = \frac{1.25 \times 10^{-3}}{1.4} = 8.93 \times 10^{-4} \;\;\text{m}^2\text{·K/W} $$ $$ [\;\;\;] = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} + 8.93 \times 10^{-4} = 2.893 \times 10^{-3} \;\;\text{m}^2\text{·K/W} $$ **Step 4: Compute $\rho L$** $$ \rho L = 1050 \times 240{,}000 = 2.52 \times 10^{8} \;\;\text{J/m}^3 $$ **Step 5: Multiply** $$ t_f = \frac{2.52 \times 10^{8}}{32.5} \times 2.893 \times 10^{-3} $$ $$ = 7.754 \times 10^{6} \times 2.893 \times 10^{-3} = 22{,}432 \;\;\text{seconds} $$ **Step 6: Convert to hours** $$ t_f = \frac{22{,}432}{3600} = 6.23 \;\;\text{hours} $$ $$ \boxed{t_f \approx 6.23 \;\;\text{hours} \;\;\text{(i.e., 6 h 14 min)}} $$ --- ### ✏️ Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative* **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** **(a)** Explain in detail the various **methods of freezing** food products. Compare air-blast freezing, plate freezing, and cryogenic freezing in terms of freezing rate, product quality, and typical applications. (7 marks) **(b)** A cold storage warehouse holds 50 tonnes of apples at +2 °C. The apples enter the store at +25 °C and must be cooled to +2 °C within 24 hours. The specific heat of apples is 3.6 kJ/(kg·K), and the rate of respiratory heat evolution is 15 W per tonne. Determine: - (i) The sensible heat load from the apples. (3 marks) - (ii) The respiratory heat load over 24 hours. (2 marks) - (iii) The total refrigeration capacity required in kW and TR. (2 marks) --- #### Model Solution — Q B (a): Methods of Freezing **1. Air-Blast Freezing:** Cold air at −25 °C to −40 °C is circulated at high velocity (3–6 m/s) over products on trolleys or conveyors. - **Freezing rate:** Moderate (4–18 h for typical cuts). - **Product quality:** Good; slight surface dehydration ("freezer burn") if unprotected. - **Applications:** Bulk meat carcasses, fish, general frozen foods. **2. Plate (Contact) Freezing:** Products are pressed between hollow metal plates carrying a refrigerant. - **Freezing rate:** Fast (2–4 h). - **Product quality:** Excellent — uniform freezing, minimal dehydration, intimate contact gives high heat transfer. - **Applications:** Fish fillets, meat patties, packaged thin products. **3. Cryogenic Freezing:** Product is sprayed with or immersed in liquid nitrogen (−196 °C) or liquid CO₂ (−78 °C). - **Freezing rate:** Very fast (5–30 min). - **Product quality:** Premium — extremely small ice crystals preserve cell structure. - **Applications:** Berries, shrimp, ice cream, gourmet items; high operating cost. **4. Immersion Freezing:** Products dipped in cold brine or glycol (−20 °C to −30 °C). - **Freezing rate:** Fast (0.5–2 h). - **Product quality:** Good if package is leak-proof. - **Applications:** Poultry, packaged seafood. **5. Fluidized Bed Freezing:** Cold air is blown upward through a bed of small products (peas, diced carrot), suspending them — like a fluid. - **Freezing rate:** Very fast (10–20 min) due to high surface area. - **Product quality:** Excellent for small uniform products. - **Applications:** Individually Quick Frozen (IQF) vegetables and fruits. --- #### Model Solution — Q B (b): Cold Storage Refrigeration Load **Given:** - Mass $m = 50{,}000$ kg - $T_1 = +25\,°C$, $T_2 = +2\,°C$ - $C_p = 3.6$ kJ/(kg·K) - $q_r = 15$ W/tonne → total = $50 \times 15 = 750$ W - Time $t = 24$ h = 86,400 s **Step (i): Sensible heat load** $$ Q_{sensible} = m \cdot C_p \cdot (T_1 - T_2) = 50{,}000 \times 3.6 \times (25 - 2) $$ $$ Q_{sensible} = 50{,}000 \times 3.6 \times 23 = 4{,}140{,}000 \;\;\text{kJ} $$ **Step (ii): Respiratory heat load over 24 hours** $$ Q_{resp} = \dot{Q}_{resp} \times t = 750 \;\;\text{W} \times 86{,}400 \;\;\text{s} $$ $$ Q_{resp} = 64{,}800{,}000 \;\;\text{J} = 64{,}800 \;\;\text{kJ} $$ **Step (iii): Total refrigeration capacity** $$ Q_{total} = Q_{sensible} + Q_{resp} = 4{,}140{,}000 + 64{,}800 = 4{,}204{,}800 \;\;\text{kJ} $$ Average power required over 24 hours: $$ \dot{Q} = \frac{4{,}204{,}800}{86{,}400} = 48.67 \;\;\text{kW} $$ Converting to **Tonnes of Refrigeration** (1 TR = 3.517 kW): $$ \dot{Q} = \frac{48.67}{3.517} = 13.84 \;\;\text{TR} $$ Adding 10 % safety factor for transmission, infiltration, and internal loads: $$ \boxed{\dot{Q}_{\text{design}} \approx 53.5 \;\;\text{kW} \approx 15.2 \;\;\text{TR}} $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Unit trap in Plank's equation:** $\rho$ in kg/m³, $L$ in J/kg, $D$ in m, $h$ in W/m²K, $\lambda$ in W/mK. Many students mix up kcal/hr and kW. Always write units at every step — a KTU marker deducts **½ mark for each missing unit** in a derivation. > 2. **Sign convention in Plank's equation:** Use absolute value of $(\theta_f - \theta_a)$ — a negative value gives nonsense time. Always take the **magnitude**. > 3. **Latent heat for food ≠ 80 kcal/kg of food:** It is 80 × (moisture fraction). For 75 % moisture food, $L = 60$ kcal/kg of food, not 80. > 4. **Respiratory heat is for FRESH produce only:** Including it in a frozen meat load calculation is a common mistake that **costs 1 mark** on the KTU board. > 5. **Callow's Rule vs. Heldman-Singh:** Callow is for **above** freezing; Heldman-Singh (with the $|T|/|T_f|$ term) is for **below** freezing. Confusing these is the **single most common error** in Module 4 numerical problems. --- ### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This!** 🖨️ - ⭐ **Chilling** = 0 °C to +8 °C (sensible heat only); **Freezing** = −18 °C and below (sensible + latent). - ⭐ **Callow's Rule:** $C_{pu} = 0.5 \times m_w$ (above freezing) — moisture content is in **percentage**. - ⭐ **Heldman-Singh Rule:** $C_{pf} = 0.5 \times m_w \times (0.5 + |T_2|/|T_f|)$ (below freezing). - ⭐ **Plank's equation** P and R constants: - Slab → P = 1/2, R = 1/8 - Cylinder → P = 1/4, R = 1/16 - Sphere → P = 1/6, R = 1/24 - ⭐ **Respiratory heat:** $Q_{resp} = m \cdot q_r$ — only for **fresh** fruits/vegetables. - ⭐ **Latent heat of food** = 80 × (moisture fraction) kcal/kg. - ⭐ **Latent heat dominates:** ~60–70 % of total freezing load is the phase-change heat. - ⭐ **Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP):** N₂ + CO₂ mix extends shelf life 2× to 4×. - ⭐ **Chilling injury:** Bananas stored below 13 °C suffer irreversible damage — know your product's safe temperature! - ⭐ **Cryogenic freezing** = best quality, but costliest; **air-blast** = most common industrial method. - ⭐ **Freezer burn** = surface dehydration due to poor packaging — engineering problem, not just cosmetic. - ⭐ **Cold chain integrity:** A single break in the chain (e.g., 2-hour truck breakdown) can ruin an entire shipment. - ⭐ **Display cabinet** heat load: $Q = A \cdot U \cdot \Delta T$ — common in KTU supermarket-refrigeration problems. - ⭐ **Freezing point of food** < 0 °C due to dissolved salts/sugars (Raoult's depression). - ⭐ **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 3024 kcal/hr** — must memorise this conversion cold. ---

topics.title: Order Picking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Order Picking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Order Picking** is defined as the process of retrieving specific quantities of finished or semi-finished products from their storage locations (racks, bins, pallets, or cold rooms) within a warehouse or **cold storage facility**, in accordance with a customer order, dispatch instruction, or replenishment request, while maintaining the integrity of the **cold chain**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus context (Module 4 of Cold Storage Operations), order picking is positioned as a critical **downstream logistics activity** of the cold chain that directly determines:

- Order **cycle time** and **dispatch lead time**
- **Product quality** and **shelf life retention**
- **Energy cost** of the cold store (longer picking = more door openings)
- **Worker productivity** in a hostile (sub-zero) environment
- **Order accuracy** and customer satisfaction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> In the 2024 Scheme, order picking is studied under the umbrella of *Cold Store Operations and Material Handling*. Students are expected to know picking strategies, equipment, ergonomic and safety constraints unique to sub-zero environments, and the basic productivity metrics used by cold store managers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you are inside a giant, **walk-in freezer the size of a football field** (a typical cold storage warehouse). The temperature is around **−18 °C to −25 °C**. A customer has phoned in an order: "I need 50 kg of frozen fish, 30 kg of peas, and 20 kg of mango pulp."

Your job — as an order picker — is to:

1. Read the **pick list** (a paper or digital slip telling you *what* to pick, *how much*, and *where*).
2. **Walk** to the correct rack or pallet location.
3. **Identify** the right SKU (Stock Keeping Unit) in a foggy, cold aisle.
4. **Lift / retrieve** the cartons, weighing them, and place them on your pallet jack.
5. **Move** them to the dispatch staging area — which itself must be refrigerated.

Now imagine doing this for **hundreds of orders a day**, wearing heavy thermal clothing, with limited time (because the cold is dangerous and the cold room door must not stay open too long).

> That entire activity — from "reading the pick list" to "placing the goods in the dispatch bay" — is what the industry calls **Order Picking**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Insight:**
> Order picking is often called the **"highest-cost activity in a warehouse"**. Studies (e.g., Frazelle, 2002) consistently show that picking can consume **55 % – 65 %** of total warehouse operating cost. In a **cold storage**, this cost is even higher because of:
> - Energy losses when freezer doors are open
> - Reduced worker speed in thermal clothing
> - Higher wage premiums for cold-store workers

## 1.3 Key Technical Terms (KTU Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|------|---------|--------------|
| **SKU** | Stock Keeping Unit (one product variant) | — |
| **Pick List** | Document / digital instruction listing items to pick | — |
| **Pick Face** | The forward-facing storage location from which a picker retrieves goods | — |
| **Pick Rate** | Number of picks completed per unit time | picks/hour |
| **Pick Accuracy** | Percentage of correct picks | **%** |
| **Order Cycle Time** | Time from order receipt to dispatch readiness | minutes / hours |
| **Cold Chain** | End-to-end temperature-controlled supply chain | — |
| **Door Opening Cycle** | Each open-close of a cold room door causing heat ingress | cycles/shift |

## 1.4 Critical Importance of Order Picking in Cold Storage

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Order Picking Matters More in Cold Stores than in Ambient Warehouses:**
>
> 1. **Thermal Penalty** — Every extra minute of door-open time causes **refrigeration load** and **frost build-up**.
> 2. **Product Safety** — Pickers touching frozen product with bare hands cause **partial thaw** → refreezing degrades quality.
> 3. **Worker Safety** — Prolonged exposure to **−18 °C to −25 °C** causes cold burns, hypothermia, reduced dexterity.
> 4. **Energy Bill** — A typical cold store spends **40 %–60 %** of its operating cost on energy; inefficient picking multiplies this.
> 5. **Order Integrity** — One wrong pick in a frozen-food chain can result in **complete rejection** of a consignment.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Order Picking Process (Step-by-Step Logic)

The complete order picking operation in a cold storage facility is structured into **seven sequential phases**:

### Phase 1 — Order Receipt and Validation
- Customer order arrives via **EDI, e-mail, phone, or WMS portal**.
- The **Warehouse Management System (WMS)** validates stock availability.
- Credit check and order confirmation are completed.

> **Why:** Prevents dispatching an order that cannot be fulfilled → reduces **wasted picking effort**.

### Phase 2 — Pick List Generation
- The WMS generates a **pick list** (or pick wave) specifying:
  - SKU codes
  - Quantities
  - Storage location addresses (e.g., *Aisle 04, Rack 12, Level 3, Position 07*)
  - Batch / lot numbers (essential for **FIFO** in cold chain)
- Pick lists are usually **sequenced** to minimize travel distance (S-shape, return, midpoint strategies).

### Phase 3 — Picker Assignment and Equipment Allocation
- A picker is **assigned** based on:
  - Zone responsibility
  - Current workload
  - Equipment availability (manual cart, electric pallet jack, low-level order picker, etc.)

### Phase 4 — Travel to Pick Location
- Picker travels through the cold room aisle.
- **Travel time** is a function of distance and walking/driving speed.

### Phase 5 — Item Identification and Retrieval
- Picker confirms SKU via **barcode scan / RFID / pick-to-light**.
- Quantity is verified (counted or weighed).
- Picker physically lifts or slides the carton onto a pallet or roll cage.

### Phase 6 — Consolidation and Staging
- Picked items are moved to a **refrigerated staging area** (often a separate ante-chamber at **0 °C to +4 °C** for chilled, or **−18 °C** for frozen).
- Orders are consolidated, re-checked, and packed in insulated containers.

### Phase 7 — Loading and Dispatch
- Loaded onto **refrigerated trucks** for transport.
- Sealed and temperature loggers attached.

## 2.2 Classification of Order Picking Strategies (KTU-Favorite Topic)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasises the **comparison** of picking strategies under *performance, cost, and cold-store suitability*.

### Strategy 1 — Discrete (Single-Order) Picking
- **One picker completes one order at a time.**
- Simple to implement; suitable for **low order volume** cold stores (e.g., boutique seafood exporters).
- **Disadvantage:** High travel time per order; inefficient in large cold rooms.

### Strategy 2 — Batch Picking
- **One picker picks items for multiple orders in a single trip.**
- Total travel time is amortised across many orders.
- Common in **frozen vegetable plants** where many orders share common SKUs.

### Strategy 3 — Zone Picking
- Cold room divided into **zones** (Zone A: frozen meat, Zone B: dairy, Zone C: ice cream).
- Each picker is **permanently or temporarily assigned** to a zone.
- Reduces walking distance and improves **ergonomics** (worker stays in one cold zone).
- Often combined with a **conveyor** that moves totes to the next zone.

### Strategy 4 — Wave Picking
- Orders are released in **time-based waves** (e.g., morning wave at 06:00, afternoon wave at 14:00).
- Aligns picking with **truck loading schedules** — critical for cold chain because refrigerated trucks must leave on time.
- Reduces **door opening duration** by concentrating all pick activity into defined windows.

### Strategy 5 — Cluster Picking
- A single picker picks into a **multi-compartment cart**, separating items for several orders simultaneously.
- Excellent for **pharmaceutical cold chains** and **specialty foods** where accuracy is paramount.

### Strategy 6 — Pick-and-Pass
- A hybrid of zone + batch: totes move along a conveyor or trolley from one zone picker to the next.
- Common in **large dairy and meat distribution centres**.

## 2.3 Equipment Used in Cold Storage Order Picking

| Equipment | Function | Cold Store Suitability |
|-----------|----------|------------------------|
| Manual Pallet Jack | Move single pallets | Low (limited use, manual effort) |
| Electric Pallet Jack | Powered movement of pallets | High (reduces worker fatigue in cold) |
| Low-Level Order Picker | Picker stands on platform, picks from first 2 levels | **Most common in cold stores** |
| High-Level Order Picker (VNA) | Picker is lifted to 6–12 m height | Used in **automated high-bay cold stores** |
| Reach Truck | Picks from high racking with extending forks | High (good for narrow aisles) |
| Counterbalance Forklift | Heavy pallet movement | Moderate (slow in cold) |
| Conveyor System | Continuous tote movement | Used in chilled (+4 °C) zones |
| Carousel / Vertical Lift Module (VLM) | Automated bin retrieval | Excellent for small-part cold storage (vaccines) |
| AS/RS (Automated Storage & Retrieval System) | Crane-based automated storage | Best for large cold stores (−25 °C) |
| Pick-to-Light / Voice Picking / RFID | Hands-free guidance | **Strongly recommended** in cold stores because workers' hands are often gloved |

## 2.4 Ergonomic and Safety Considerations (Cold-Specific)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Cold-store pickers face **three major ergonomic risks** that affect picking design:
>
> 1. **Reduced Manual Dexterity** at low temperatures → favour **voice / pick-to-light** over paper lists.
> 2. **Cold-Induced Fatigue** → rotation of pickers every **45–60 minutes** to warm-up zones.
> 3. **Slip Hazard** from frost on floors → aisles must be **anti-skid epoxy coated**.

## 2.5 📊 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **minimum** set of formulas a KTU 2024 student must memorise for the *Order Picking* topic. Pay special attention to units.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Pick Rate (PR) | $PR = \dfrac{N_p}{T_p}$ | picks / hour | $N_p$ = picks completed, $T_p$ = picking time |
| 2 | Order Picking Accuracy (A) | $A = \dfrac{N_c}{N_t} \times 100$ | **%** | $N_c$ = correct picks, $N_t$ = total picks |
| 3 | Travel Time | $T_t = \dfrac{D}{v}$ | seconds / minutes | $D$ = travel distance, $v$ = picker speed |
| 4 | Total Pick Cycle Time | $T_{cyc} = T_t + T_s + T_p + T_r$ | minutes | travel + search + pick + return |
| 5 | Lines per Labor-Hour | $L_{lh} = \dfrac{N_L}{H \times T_{sh}}$ | lines / labor-hour | $H$ = pickers, $T_{sh}$ = shift hours |
| 6 | Cost per Pick | $C_p = \dfrac{C_{total}}{N_p}$ | INR / pick | $C_{total}$ = total picking cost |
| 7 | Order Fill Rate | $F_r = \dfrac{N_{of}}{N_{or}} \times 100$ | **%** | orders fulfilled ÷ orders received |
| 8 | Picker Utilization | $U = \dfrac{T_{prod}}{T_{avail}} \times 100$ | **%** | productive ÷ available time |
| 9 | Heat Ingress per Door Opening | $Q = V \times \rho_a \times c_a \times \Delta T$ | kJ | Cold-store specific penalty term |
| 10 | Order Picking Productivity | $P = \dfrac{N_o}{H \times T_{sh}}$ | orders / picker-shift | Used for staff planning |

> **Anti-Break Note:** All absolute values in the table are deliberately written with `\vert` or `×` — **no raw `|` symbol** — to keep the markdown table parser happy.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cold Chain Logistics Companies** (e.g., Snowman Logistics, ColdEx) use these formulas daily for shift planning.
- **FSSAI / HACCP audits** in India require documented order picking accuracy ≥ **99 %**.
- **Industrial Engineers** in cold stores use pick-rate benchmarking to decide between manual and AS/RS investment (payback period typically **3–7 years** for an AS/RS).
- **Energy auditors** calculate door-opening penalty using formula (9) above to justify rapid pick-path optimization.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📒 Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Order Picking Cost per Line

### Step 1 — Define Total Picking Cost

The **total cost** of the order picking operation in a cold store is the sum of:

- Direct labor cost of pickers
- Equipment usage cost
- Overhead cost (lighting, refrigeration penalty, supervision, software)

$$
C_{total} = C_{labour} + C_{equip} + C_{overhead}
$$

### Step 2 — Expand Labour Cost

If $H$ pickers work for $T_{sh}$ hours each, with hourly wage $w$ INR/hour:

$$
C_{labour} = H \times T_{sh} \times w
$$

### Step 3 — Combine All Cost Components

$$
C_{total} = (H \times T_{sh} \times w) + C_{equip} + C_{overhead}
$$

### Step 4 — Define Cost per Line (Cost per Pick)

$$
C_{line} = \dfrac{C_{total}}{N_L}
$$

### Step 5 — Substituting

$$
C_{line} = \dfrac{H \cdot T_{sh} \cdot w + C_{equip} + C_{overhead}}{N_L}
$$

**Where:**
- $H$ = number of pickers
- $T_{sh}$ = shift duration (hours)
- $w$ = hourly wage (INR/hr)
- $C_{equip}$ = equipment cost per shift
- $C_{overhead}$ = overhead per shift
- $N_L$ = total number of lines picked

> **Engineering Use:** A cold store manager uses this formula to decide between *adding another picker* (low capital cost, but increases door-open time) versus *buying an automated picker* (high capital, but consistent speed).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pick Rate and Labor Productivity

### Step 1 — Define the Pick Rate

Pick rate is picks per hour per picker:

$$
PR = \dfrac{N_L}{H \times T_{sh}}
$$

### Step 2 — Average Picking Time per Line

If the **average pick time per line** is $t_p$ minutes, the maximum theoretical pick rate is:

$$
PR_{max} = \dfrac{60}{t_p}
$$

### Step 3 — Actual vs Theoretical Pick Rate

Real pick rate is always less than theoretical due to:
- Travel time
- Search time
- Breaks
- Equipment idle time

$$
PR_{actual} = \eta \times PR_{max}
$$

Where $\eta$ is the **efficiency factor** (typical cold store: **0.65 – 0.80**).

### Step 4 — Total Lines Picked in a Day

$$
N_L = PR_{actual} \times H \times T_{sh}
$$

### Step 5 — Substituting

$$
N_L = \eta \times \dfrac{60}{t_p} \times H \times T_{sh}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Travel Time for a S-Shape Pick Path

In a **rectangular cold store aisle** of length $L$ and width $W$:

### Step 1 — Picker Speed
Let $v$ = picker walking speed (m/s) inside the cold room.

### Step 2 — Number of Aisles Traversed
If a batch pick visits $k$ aisles, the **total horizontal travel** is:

$$
D_{horiz} = k \times W
$$

### Step 3 — Total Vertical Travel (S-Shape)
In an **S-shape** path, the picker walks the full length of every visited aisle:

$$
D_{vert} = k \times L
$$

### Step 4 — Total Distance

$$
D_{total} = D_{horiz} + D_{vert} = k(W + L)
$$

### Step 5 — Travel Time

$$
T_t = \dfrac{D_{total}}{v} = \dfrac{k(W + L)}{v}
$$

**Where:**
- $L$ = aisle length (m)
- $W$ = aisle width (m)
- $k$ = number of aisles visited
- $v$ = picker speed in m/s

> **Cold-Store Adjustment:** In a **−20 °C** cold room, $v$ drops by **15 %–25 %** due to bulky thermal clothing, so effective $v_{cold} = 0.75\,v_{ambient}$.

---

## 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem)

> **Problem:**
> A cold storage facility in Kerala receives **480 orders/day**, each with an average of **4 lines/order**. The store operates **one shift of 8 hours** with **12 pickers**. Average pick time per line = **3 minutes**. Equipment cost = **₹1500/shift**; overhead = **₹3500/shift**; hourly wage = **₹220/hr**.

**Calculate:**
1. Total pick rate of the facility
2. Lines per labor-hour
3. Cost per line
4. Picker utilization if actual picks = 1900 lines

### Given Data

$$
N_{orders} = 480, \quad N_{L/order} = 4, \quad H = 12, \quad T_{sh} = 8\,\text{hr}
$$
$$
t_p = 3\,\text{min}, \quad C_{equip} = 1500, \quad C_{overhead} = 3500, \quad w = 220
$$

### Solution — Step 1: Total Lines per Day

$$
N_L = N_{orders} \times N_{L/order} = 480 \times 4 = 1920\ \text{lines/day}
$$

### Solution — Step 2: Theoretical Pick Rate

$$
PR_{max} = \dfrac{60}{t_p} = \dfrac{60}{3} = 20\ \text{picks/hr}
$$

### Solution — Step 3: Actual Pick Rate (overall)

$$
PR_{actual} = \dfrac{N_L}{H \times T_{sh}} = \dfrac{1920}{12 \times 8} = \dfrac{1920}{96} = 20\ \text{picks/hr}
$$

### Solution — Step 4: Lines per Labor-Hour

$$
L_{lh} = \dfrac{N_L}{H \times T_{sh}} = \dfrac{1920}{96} = 20\ \text{lines/labor-hour}
$$

### Solution — Step 5: Total Cost

$$
C_{labour} = H \times T_{sh} \times w = 12 \times 8 \times 220 = 21120\ \text{INR}
$$
$$
C_{total} = C_{labour} + C_{equip} + C_{overhead} = 21120 + 1500 + 3500 = 26120\ \text{INR}
$$

### Solution — Step 6: Cost per Line

$$
C_{line} = \dfrac{C_{total}}{N_L} = \dfrac{26120}{1920} = 13.60\ \text{INR/line}
$$

### Solution — Step 7: Picker Utilization (using 1900 actual lines)

Time actually spent picking = $1900 \times 3\ \text{min} = 5700\ \text{min} = 95\ \text{hr}$

Total available time = $12 \times 8 = 96\ \text{hr}$

$$
U = \dfrac{95}{96} \times 100 = 98.96\ \%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A utilization above 95 % is **unsustainable** in a cold store because workers need short warm-up breaks. A target of **80 % – 90 %** is industry-recommended.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Picking Strategy to Choose?

| Strategy | Order Volume Suitability | Cold-Store Ergonomics | Accuracy | Capital Cost | Recommended For |
|----------|--------------------------|------------------------|----------|--------------|------------------|
| Discrete Picking | Low | Poor (long travel) | High | Very Low | Small seafood exporters |
| Batch Picking | Medium-High | Good | Medium | Low | Frozen vegetable plants |
| Zone Picking | High | **Excellent** | High | Medium | Multi-product cold stores |
| Wave Picking | High | Good | High | Medium | Refrigerated truck dispatch |
| Cluster Picking | Medium | Good | **Very High** | Medium | Pharma / vaccines |
| AS/RS Automated | Very High | **Best** | **Highest** | **Very High** | Large meat / dairy DC |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📙 Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — End-to-End Order Picking Process Flow in a Cold Store

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Customer Order Received] --> B[WMS Validation and Credit Check]
    B --> C{Stock Available?}
    C -->|No| Z[Backorder Notification]
    C -->|Yes| D[Pick List Generation with FIFO Lots]
    D --> E[Picking Strategy Selection]
    E --> F[Picker and Equipment Assignment]
    F --> G[Pre-cool Picker Clothing at Ante-Room]
    G --> H[Travel to First Pick Location]
    H --> I[Scan SKU and Verify Batch]
    I --> J[Retrieve Item and Weigh]
    J --> K[Place on Pallet or Tote]
    K --> L{All Items Picked?}
    L -->|No| H
    L -->|Yes| M[Move to Refrigerated Staging Area]
    M --> N[Quality Check and Re-weigh]
    N --> O[Pack in Insulated Container]
    O --> P[Load onto Refrigerated Truck]
    P --> Q[Generate Dispatch Document and Temperature Log]
```

> **Reading Guide:** Every decision diamond (e.g., *Stock Available?*, *All Items Picked?*) represents a validation gate that protects cold chain integrity. The "Pre-cool Picker Clothing" step is **unique to cold stores** and absent in ambient warehouses.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Classification of Order Picking Strategies

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[Order Picking Strategies] --> B[Discrete Single Order]
    A --> C[Batch Picking]
    A --> D[Zone Picking]
    A --> E[Wave Picking]
    A --> F[Cluster Picking]
    A --> G[Pick and Pass]
    B --> B1[One order per picker]
    C --> C1[Many orders in one trip]
    D --> D1[Picker stays in one zone]
    E --> E1[Time based wave release]
    F --> F1[Multi compartment cart]
    G --> G1[Tote moves across zones]
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Cold Storage Order Picking Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Customer Order Portal] --> B[Warehouse Management System WMS]
    B --> C[Order Validation Engine]
    C --> D[Inventory Database]
    D --> E[Pick Path Optimizer]
    E --> F[Zone A Frozen Meat]
    E --> G[Zone B Dairy]
    E --> H[Zone C Ice Cream]
    E --> I[Zone D Fruits and Vegetables]
    F --> J[Refrigerated Staging 0 to 4 deg C]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Quality Verification Station]
    K --> L[Insulated Container Packing]
    L --> M[Refrigerated Truck Dispatch Bay]
    M --> N[Customer Delivery with Cold Chain Log]
```

> **Reading Guide:** This block-level diagram maps the *functional* flow of data and material from order entry to delivery. Notice that each **Zone** has its own pick line; all zones converge at the **Refrigerated Staging** ante-chamber, which acts as a buffer between the cold store and the dispatch bay.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Worker Safety and Rotation Logic in Cold Picking

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Picker Enters Cold Room] --> B[Set Timer to 45 Minutes]
    B --> C[Perform Picking Tasks]
    C --> D{Timer Expired?}
    D -->|No| C
    D -->|Yes| E[Exit to Warm-up Room 20 deg C]
    E --> F[Rest for 15 Minutes]
    F --> G[Don Fresh Thermal Wear]
    G --> H[Re-enter Cold Room for Next Cycle]
    H --> B
```

> **Engineering Note:** Most cold-store occupational health standards (OSHA, Indian Factory Act) mandate a **maximum continuous cold exposure of 45–60 minutes** at **−20 °C** to prevent **hypothermia** and **frostbite**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define order picking. List any four order picking strategies used in cold storage operations.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Order picking is the warehouse function of retrieving the right quantity of the right SKU from its storage location, in accordance with a customer order, while maintaining cold chain integrity in a refrigerated environment.

**Four strategies (0.375 each = 1.5 marks):**
1. Discrete order picking
2. Batch picking
3. Zone picking
4. Wave picking

(Acceptable alternatives: Cluster picking, Pick-and-Pass)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Explain the importance of order picking accuracy in cold chain management.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Product Safety (1 mark):** A wrong pick in a cold store can result in delivery of the wrong SKU, leading to **FSSAI rejection** and complete loss of the temperature-sensitive consignment.

2. **Economic Loss (1 mark):** Frozen products have **high unit value**; one wrong pick can waste ₹ lakhs in damaged product and reverse logistics.

3. **Customer Trust and Compliance (1 mark):** Modern retail and pharmaceutical clients demand **≥ 99 %** picking accuracy. Any deviation breaks the **Service Level Agreement (SLA)**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> *KTU 2024 Scheme mandates Module-level internal choice. Question A and Question B are completely independent alternative selections.*

---

### 📌 Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain in detail the different order picking strategies with their advantages and limitations in cold storage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Strategy | Working Principle | Advantages | Limitations in Cold Store |
|----------|-------------------|------------|---------------------------|
| **Discrete Picking** | One picker → one order | Simple; high accuracy; minimal training | High travel time → long door open time → energy loss |
| **Batch Picking** | One picker → multiple orders in single trip | Less travel per order; better pick rate | Risk of cross-order mixing; harder in foggy cold rooms |
| **Zone Picking** | Cold store divided into temperature/product zones; picker stays in one zone | Lower walking; better ergonomics; localised expertise | Needs conveyor; possible imbalance between zones |
| **Wave Picking** | Orders released in time-based batches aligned with truck loading | Aligns with cold-chain dispatch schedule | Complex scheduling; needs strong WMS |
| **Cluster Picking** | Picker uses a multi-compartment cart | Very high accuracy; minimal handling | Cart weight increases; not for heavy frozen items |
| **Pick-and-Pass** | Tote moves from zone to zone | Highly scalable; suitable for big DC | Capital-intensive; rare in small cold stores |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** For most Indian cold stores in 2024, **Zone Picking combined with Wave Picking** is the industry best practice because it balances productivity, cold-chain integrity, and worker safety.

---

#### (b) A cold storage facility in Kochi processes 600 orders per day with an average of 6 lines per order. The store has 10 pickers, each working an 8-hour shift. The average pick time per line is 4 minutes. The hourly wage is ₹250, equipment cost is ₹2000 per shift, and overhead is ₹4000 per shift. Calculate the pick rate, lines per labor-hour, and cost per line. **(7 marks)**

**Given:**

$$
N_{orders} = 600,\quad N_{L/o} = 6,\quad H = 10,\quad T_{sh} = 8\ \text{hr}
$$
$$
t_p = 4\ \text{min},\quad w = 250\ \text{INR/hr},\quad C_{equip} = 2000,\quad C_{over} = 4000
$$

**Step 1 — Total lines per day**

$$
N_L = 600 \times 6 = 3600\ \text{lines}
$$

**Step 2 — Pick rate (per picker-hour)**

$$
PR = \dfrac{N_L}{H \times T_{sh}} = \dfrac{3600}{10 \times 8} = \dfrac{3600}{80} = 45\ \text{picks/hr}
$$

**Step 3 — Lines per labor-hour**

$$
L_{lh} = \dfrac{N_L}{H \times T_{sh}} = 45\ \text{lines/labor-hour}
$$

**Step 4 — Labour cost**

$$
C_{labour} = 10 \times 8 \times 250 = 20000\ \text{INR}
$$

**Step 5 — Total cost**

$$
C_{total} = 20000 + 2000 + 4000 = 26000\ \text{INR}
$$

**Step 6 — Cost per line**

$$
C_{line} = \dfrac{26000}{3600} = 7.22\ \text{INR/line}
$$

**Final Answer:** PR = 45 picks/hr, $L_{lh}$ = 45 lines/labor-hour, $C_{line}$ = ₹7.22/line.

---

### 📌 Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

#### (a) Discuss the equipment used in cold storage order picking and the ergonomic considerations for workers. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Equipment (3.5 marks):**

1. **Low-level order pickers** — most common in cold stores because the picker is close to the goods and exposure time is short.
2. **Electric pallet jacks and reach trucks** — reduce manual effort, hence reduce cold-induced fatigue.
3. **Voice-picking headsets** — hands-free; essential because workers wear thick gloves.
4. **Pick-to-light and RFID systems** — improve accuracy in foggy cold rooms where barcode scanning is unreliable.
5. **Automated Storage and Retrieval Systems (AS/RS)** — used in large dairy and pharmaceutical cold stores to **eliminate human presence** in the coldest zones.
6. **Conveyor systems in chilled ante-rooms** — allow totes to move between zones without opening freezer doors repeatedly.

**Ergonomic considerations (3.5 marks):**

1. **Work-rest cycle:** pickers must be rotated every 45–60 min to a warm-up room.
2. **Protective clothing:** layered thermal wear, insulated gloves, anti-fog goggles, anti-slip footwear.
3. **Pre-cooling of clothing** in an ante-room to avoid condensation inside the cold store.
4. **Anti-skid flooring** to prevent frost-related falls.
5. **Lighting** must be high-intensity (≥ 500 lux) because cold-store vision is impaired.
6. **Acoustic warnings** since workers may be visually isolated by fog and vapour.

---

#### (b) Derive the formula for order picking cost per line and calculate the cost for the following data: 5000 lines picked, 3 pickers, hourly wage = ₹220, shift duration = 8 hours, equipment cost = ₹2500, overhead = ₹6000. **(7 marks)**

**Derivation (3.5 marks):**

$$
C_{labour} = H \cdot T_{sh} \cdot w
$$
$$
C_{total} = C_{labour} + C_{equip} + C_{over}
$$
$$
C_{line} = \dfrac{C_{total}}{N_L} = \dfrac{H \cdot T_{sh} \cdot w + C_{equip} + C_{over}}{N_L}
$$

**Calculation (3.5 marks):**

$$
C_{labour} = 3 \times 8 \times 220 = 5280\ \text{INR}
$$
$$
C_{total} = 5280 + 2500 + 6000 = 13780\ \text{INR}
$$
$$
C_{line} = \dfrac{13780}{5000} = 2.756 \approx 2.76\ \text{INR/line}
$$

**Final Answer:** Cost per line = **₹2.76/line**.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in formula sheet answers** — KTU examiners *deduct 0.5 mark* if units are missing for a derived quantity.
> 2. **Confusing "pick rate" with "picks per day"** — Pick rate is *per hour*, not *per shift*.
> 3. **Treating cold-store picking like ambient picking** — Always mention **door-open penalty, worker rotation, and thermal clothing** in 7-mark answers. Examiners specifically test this.
> 4. **Skipping the derivation step in cost-per-line problems** — KTU awards *partial marks (1–2)* for correct formula even if calculation is wrong. Do not skip the derivation.
> 5. **Wrong use of absolute value symbol `|` in tables** — Use `\vert` or `\mid` (or simply remove the bars) in markdown-based answer sheets.
> 6. **Missing FIFO / FEFO logic** — For perishable cold-chain items, the picking strategy *must* mention **First-Expiry-First-Out** logic. Examiners expect this phrase.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for the KTU 2024 Exam — *Order Picking* Module 4**

- **Definition:** Order picking = retrieving the right SKU in the right quantity from storage to fulfil a customer order while preserving cold chain integrity.
- **Picking Strategies:** Discrete, Batch, Zone, Wave, Cluster, Pick-and-Pass, and AS/RS-based fully automated.
- **Best Cold-Store Choice:** **Zone + Wave Picking** combined.
- **Key Equipment:** Low-level order pickers, electric pallet jacks, reach trucks, voice picking, pick-to-light, AS/RS.
- **Unique Cold-Store Step:** Pre-cooling of picker clothing in an ante-room.
- **Worker Rotation Cycle:** 45–60 min cold exposure → 15 min warm-up.
- **Master Formulas:**
  - $PR = N_p / T_p$
  - $A = (N_c / N_t) \times 100$
  - $C_{line} = (H \cdot T_{sh} \cdot w + C_{equip} + C_{over}) / N_L$
  - $L_{lh} = N_L / (H \cdot T_{sh})$
  - $T_t = D / v$
- **Efficiency Factor** $\eta$ in cold store ≈ **0.65 – 0.80** (lower than ambient).
- **Door-Open Penalty:** A cold room door open for 1 min can cost **₹ 5 – ₹ 20** in extra refrigeration load.
- **Accuracy Benchmark:** Industry standard = **≥ 99 %**; pharma = **≥ 99.99 %**.
- **FIFO / FEFO:** Always mention in cold-chain picking answers.
- **KTU-Mandated Sub-Topics:** Picking strategies, equipment, ergonomics, productivity formulas, cost analysis.
- **Diagrams Expected:** Process flow of picking; strategy classification; cold-store functional architecture.
- **Examiner Loves:** Numerical problems on cost-per-line, pick rate, and labor productivity.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Refrigerated Vans
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Refrigerated Vans

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Refrigerated Van** (also termed a **Reefer Van** or **Insulated Transport Vehicle**) is a road transport vehicle fitted with a thermally insulated body and an active or passive refrigeration unit designed to maintain the cargo compartment at a predetermined, controlled low temperature for the safe, hygienic, and time-bound transportation of perishable commodities (fruits, vegetables, dairy, meat, seafood, pharmaceuticals, vaccines, etc.) from the point of production to the point of consumption, in compliance with the cold chain integrity standards.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 4):**
> Refrigerated transport is the controlled-temperature movement of perishable goods through insulated and mechanically refrigerated vehicles. A refrigerated van is a mobile cold-room on wheels that combines thermal insulation with an integrated refrigeration system to ensure the cold chain is unbroken from warehouse to retailer.

**Authoritative Regulatory Reference:** Internationally, the design and performance of refrigerated transport equipment is governed by the **ATP (Agreement on the International Carriage of Perishable Foodstuffs and on the Special Equipment to be used for such Carriage)** administered by the **UNECE (United Nations Economic Commission for Europe)**. India recognizes similar standards under the **FSSAI** (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India) and **BIS** guidelines.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **tiffin box carrying hot food** — to keep it warm, you wrap it in a thick cloth (insulation) and place it in a thermos (vacuum-insulated container). Now flip the concept: instead of keeping food **hot**, you want to keep fish, ice-cream, or vaccines **cold** during a 12-hour highway journey through the Kerala summer heat of 35 °C.

A **refrigerated van is essentially a giant thermos flask on wheels with a small air-conditioner attached to it.** The "thermos" part (thick insulated walls, CFC-free polyurethane foam, vapour barrier) prevents outside heat from creeping in, and the "air-conditioner" part (compressor + condenser + evaporator driven by a small diesel engine or electric motor) actively removes any heat that does manage to leak in, plus the heat generated by the product itself (called *respiration heat* in fruits and vegetables, or *metabolic heat* in live seafood).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cold Chain Analogy:**
> Think of the cold chain as a relay race 🏃‍♂️ — the reefer van is the **middle runner** who must hand over the "baton" (chilled product) at exactly the same temperature it was received. If the middle runner drops the baton (temperature rises), the entire chain is broken and the product is wasted.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard engineering values are universally used in refrigerated van design and must be memorized:

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Ambient design temperature (Kerala/tropical) | **+ 45 °C** | °C |
| Maximum permissible product temperature rise | **+ 2 °C** above setpoint | °C |
| Standard insulation thickness (PUF) | **80 – 150 mm** | mm |
| Thermal conductivity of PUF ($k$) | **0.020 – 0.024** | W/m·K |
| Typical overall heat transfer coefficient ($U$) of van body | **0.30 – 0.60** | W/m²·K |
| Recommended air circulation inside cargo | **0.05 – 0.10** | m³/s per kW of refrigeration |
| Air-changer rate for fresh produce vans | **30 – 60** | air changes per hour |
| Engine-driven unit capacity range | **2 – 15** | kW (≈ 0.5 – 4 TR) |
| ATP class 'C' mean heat transfer coefficient | **0.70** | W/m²·K |
| ATP class 'A' mean heat transfer coefficient | **0.40** | W/m²·K |
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Tip:** The single most exam-relevant number is the overall heat transfer coefficient **U ≤ 0.40 W/m²·K** for a class-A ATP-certified van. Always quote it with units to earn full marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Refrigerated Vans

Refrigerated vans are classified based on the **refrigeration principle** employed and the **temperature range** maintained.

### 2.1.1 Classification by Refrigeration System Type

1. **Ice-Cooled Vans (Pre-cooled Ice System):**
   - Uses block ice or crushed ice as the primary cooling medium.
   - Simple, low-cost, no moving parts; suited for short-distance fish/vegetable transport.
   - Limitation: Cannot go below **0 °C**; ice mass reduces payload.

2. **Mechanical Vapour Compression Refrigeration (MVCR) Vans:**
   - Uses a compact R-134a / R-404A / R-410A system driven by either a **diesel engine** (stand-alone "engine-driven unit") or the **vehicle engine** (shaft or belt-driven).
   - Most widely used; can maintain temperatures from **+10 °C down to −25 °C**.
   - Components: Hermetic/open-type compressor, condenser (often roof-mounted with engine-driven fan), evaporator (ceiling-mounted with circulation fans), TXV/EEV, receiver, filter-drier.

3. **Cryogenic (Liquid Nitrogen / CO₂) Vans:**
   - Uses **LN₂ (−196 °C)** or **LCO₂ (−78 °C)** stored in vacuum-insulated cylinders; liquid is sprayed into the cargo space and vaporizes, absorbing latent heat.
   - Rapid "pull-down" capability, no moving refrigeration parts.
   - Disadvantage: One-shot operation, refilling cost, frosting risk on product.

4. **Eutectic Plate (Cold Accumulator) Vans:**
   - The van is plugged in at the depot overnight; eutectic plates (sealed containers of saline solution like *Glauber's salt* or organic phase-change material) freeze solid. During the journey, they absorb heat by melting.
   - Ideal for **multi-stop delivery** in milk and ice-cream distribution.

5. **Hybrid / Multi-Temperature Vans:**
   - Segregated compartments with independent temperature control, useful for delivering frozen + chilled + ambient goods simultaneously.

### 2.1.2 Classification by Temperature Class (ATP/Indian Standard)

| Class | Mean Internal Temp | Typical Cargo | Insulation $U$ (W/m²·K) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Class A | **0 °C to +7 °C** | Fresh fruits, dairy, vaccines | **≤ 0.40** |
| Class B | **−10 °C to 0 °C** | Frozen meat, ice-cream | **≤ 0.40** |
| Class C | **+7 °C to +12 °C** | Fresh produce, pharma | **≤ 0.70** |
| Class D | **< −10 °C** | Deep-frozen foods | **≤ 0.40** |

## 2.2 Construction of a Refrigerated Van Body

A well-engineered refrigerated van has **five** distinct structural layers, applied from outside to inside:

1. **Outer Skin:** Pre-painted galvanized steel / aluminium / FRP (Fibreglass Reinforced Plastic) sheet of **0.6 – 1.2 mm** thickness, providing mechanical strength and weather resistance.
2. **Vapour Barrier (Outer):** A continuous **aluminium foil or polyethylene film** of thickness **0.05 – 0.1 mm**, *laminated* to the inner face of the outer skin. Its purpose is to block moisture ingress into the insulation (water vapour moving inward from the hot, humid outside air would otherwise condense inside the foam and destroy its insulating value).
3. **Insulation Core:** Rigid **Polyurethane Foam (PUF)** in-situ foamed, or **Extruded Polystyrene (XPS)** panels. PUF is preferred because it has a very low $k$ value and zero joints (after foaming), eliminating thermal bridges.
4. **Vapour Barrier (Inner) + Inner Skin:** Another vapour barrier followed by an inner skin of **food-grade pre-coated galvanised iron (PCGI) / aluminium / FRP**. The inner surface is typically ribbed or embossed to allow free circulation of chilled air.
5. **Floor Reinforcement:** The floor carries the highest load; it is built as a sandwich of marine plywood / aluminium tread-plate bonded to PUF, supported by transverse steel Z-channels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two vapour barriers?**
> During the day, water vapour tries to move *inward* (hot outside → cold inside). When the van is parked in a cold room at night, the gradient reverses. *Two* barriers protect the foam in both directions.

## 2.3 Heat Load Components in a Refrigerated Van

The refrigeration system of a reefer van must be sized to balance **all** the following heat inputs simultaneously. The total heat load $Q_{total}$ is the algebraic sum of:

$$Q_{total} = Q_{transmission} + Q_{product} + Q_{infiltration} + Q_{internal} + Q_{engine}$$

Each term is derived from first principles in **Section 3**.

### 2.3.1 Transmission Load ($Q_{transmission}$)
Heat conducted through the six faces of the van (walls, roof, floor, doors) due to the temperature difference between the inside and outside:

$$Q_{transmission} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} U_i \cdot A_i \cdot \Delta T$$

where $U_i$ is the overall heat transfer coefficient, $A_i$ the area, and $\Delta T$ the temperature difference.

### 2.3.2 Product Load ($Q_{product}$)
Heat that must be removed to bring the product from its loading temperature down to the design storage temperature. It has two sub-components:

- **Sensible Heat Load (Sensible cooling):**
$$Q_{sens} = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{load} - T_{final})$$

- **Latent Heat of Freezing (only for frozen goods):**
$$Q_{latent} = m \cdot h_{fg}$$

- **Respiration Heat (only for fresh fruits/vegetables):**
$$Q_{resp} = m \cdot r$$
where $r$ is the respiration rate in W/tonne (highly temperature dependent — doubling every 10 °C rise, per Van't Hoff's rule).

### 2.3.3 Air Infiltration / Door-Opening Load ($Q_{infiltration}$)
Each door opening allows warm ambient air to enter, which then must be re-cooled. Empirical formula:

$$Q_{infiltration} = \frac{V \cdot n \cdot \rho_a \cdot c_{pa} \cdot \Delta T}{3600} + V \cdot n \cdot \rho_a \cdot h_{fg,air} \cdot W_{ratio}$$

where $V$ = cargo volume, $n$ = door openings per hour, $W_{ratio}$ = humidity ratio difference.

### 2.3.4 Internal Heat Sources ($Q_{internal}$)
- Evaporator fan motors: typically **150 – 400 W** per fan.
- Defrost heaters (cyclic): **500 – 1500 W**.
- Driver's cabin heater bleed (negligible).
- Personnel working inside (40 W per person per hour).

### 2.3.5 Heat from Vehicle Engine / Generator ($Q_{engine}$)
For engine-driven refrigeration units, the compressor is driven by a small auxiliary diesel engine (e.g., Kubota, Yanmar). Its radiant and convective heat into the cargo space must be added (typically 8–12 % of compressor work).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every KTU ESE question on refrigerated vans starts from one of these formulas.**

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $Q_{trans} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat gain through van body | **W (watts)** |
| 2 | $U = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{h_i} + \sum \frac{x_j}{k_j} + \frac{1}{h_o}}$ | Composite wall U-value (use 1/h ≈ 0 for still air) | W/m²·K |
| 3 | $Q_{sens} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Product sensible cooling | W |
| 4 | $Q_{latent} = m \cdot h_{fg}$ | Product latent heat removal | W |
| 5 | $Q_{resp} = m \cdot r$ | Respiration heat (produce) | W |
| 6 | $Q_{inf} = \frac{V \cdot \rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}{3600}$ | Infiltration heat per opening | W |
| 7 | $Q_{pull\text{-}down} = \frac{m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}{t_{pd}}$ | Required refrigeration for pull-down | W |
| 8 | $t_{pd} = \frac{m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}{Q_{ref} - Q_{leakage}}$ | Pull-down time | **hours (h)** |
| 9 | $R_{thermal} = \frac{1}{U \cdot A}$ | Total thermal resistance of van body | K/W |
| 10 | $\dot{V}_{air} = 0.05$ to $0.10$ m³/s per kW | Air circulation rate | m³/s |
| 11 | $P_{comp} = \frac{Q_e}{COP \cdot \eta_{mech}}$ | Compressor shaft power | kW |
| 12 | $Q_{engine} = (0.08$ to $0.12) \times P_{comp}$ | Heat dumped into van from engine drive | W |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The design of refrigerated vans directly impacts the **Indian agricultural economy** — according to NCCD (National Centre for Cold Chain Development), India loses nearly **₹92,000 crore worth of perishables annually** due to cold chain gaps. Engineers specializing in reefer van design contribute to:

- **Pharma logistics** (COVID-19 vaccine distribution at −20 °C, Covishield at +2 to +8 °C).
- **Marine exports** (shrimp, tuna frozen at −25 °C shipped from Kochi port).
- **Dairy cold chain** (Mother Dairy, Amul fleet uses eutectic plate vans).
- **Floriculture exports** (roses, orchids from Kerala, Karnataka).
- **E-commerce grocery** (BigBasket, Blinkit use last-mile reefer vans with multi-temperature zones).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Steady-State Heat Transmission Through Van Body

**Problem Statement:** A refrigerated van has four side walls, a roof, and a floor, all constructed of the same composite sandwich. Derive the rate of heat ingress from the outside ambient at $T_o$ to the inside cargo at $T_i$.

### Step 1 — Set up the composite wall resistance model
Each wall consists of (from outside to inside): outer steel skin, outer vapour barrier, PUF insulation, inner vapour barrier, inner steel skin. The one-dimensional steady-state Fourier heat conduction equation gives:

$$Q = \frac{T_o - T_i}{R_{total}}$$

where $R_{total}$ is the total thermal resistance.

### Step 2 — Express $R_{total}$ as sum of individual layer resistances
Applying the resistance-in-series model (neglecting the thin steel and vapour barrier layers, retaining only the dominant insulation layer):

$$R_{total} = \frac{1}{h_o \cdot A} + \frac{x_{ins}}{k_{ins} \cdot A} + \frac{1}{h_i \cdot A}$$

For still air inside the cargo space, $h_i \approx 5$ to $10 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$; for forced air over the outer skin during vehicle motion, $h_o \approx 10$ to $30 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$. However, KTU textbook convention (and ATP standard) is to lump these into an **overall heat transfer coefficient $U$**:

$$\frac{1}{U \cdot A} = R_{total} \quad \Rightarrow \quad U = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{h_o} + \frac{x_{ins}}{k_{ins}} + \frac{1}{h_i}}$$

### Step 3 — Generalize to all six faces of the van
For a van with floor, ceiling, four walls, and doors (each possibly of different $U$ and $A$):

$$Q_{trans} = \sum_{i} U_i \cdot A_i \cdot (T_o - T_i)$$

In KTU exam shorthand:

$$Q_{trans} = U \cdot A_{total} \cdot \Delta T$$

### Step 4 — Worked numerical example (typical KTU 14-mark setup)
**Given:**
- Van inner dimensions: $4.2 \text{ m (L)} \times 2.0 \text{ m (W)} \times 2.1 \text{ m (H)}$
- PUF thickness $x = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.10 \text{ m}$, $k = 0.022 \text{ W/m·K}$
- Outer skin $h_o = 12 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$, inner air $h_i = 8 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$
- $T_o = 45 \text{ °C}$ (Kerala summer), $T_i = 0 \text{ °C}$ (chilled dairy transport)

**Calculation:**

**Step 4.1 — Compute $U$:**

$$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{12} + \frac{0.10}{0.022} + \frac{1}{8} = 0.0833 + 4.5455 + 0.125 = 4.754 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$

$$U = \frac{1}{4.754} = 0.2104 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$$

**Step 4.2 — Compute total surface area:**

- Floor: $4.2 \times 2.0 = 8.40 \text{ m}^2$
- Ceiling: $4.2 \times 2.0 = 8.40 \text{ m}^2$
- Two long walls: $2 \times (4.2 \times 2.1) = 17.64 \text{ m}^2$
- Two short walls: $2 \times (2.0 \times 2.1) = 8.40 \text{ m}^2$
- Total $A = 8.40 + 8.40 + 17.64 + 8.40 = \mathbf{42.84 \text{ m}^2}$

**Step 4.3 — Apply transmission formula:**

$$Q_{trans} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 0.2104 \times 42.84 \times (45 - 0)$$

$$Q_{trans} = 0.2104 \times 42.84 \times 45 = 405.6 \text{ W}$$

**Result:** The transmission load is **≈ 406 W (≈ 1.16 kW ≈ 0.33 TR)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity check:** This is a small load — about 1/3 of a domestic window AC. Reefer van refrigeration units are typically rated **2 – 8 kW** because they must also handle product cooling, respiration, and door openings.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Pull-Down Time for Pre-Cooled Cargo

**Problem Statement:** A reefer van is loaded with **1500 kg of fresh fish** at an ambient loading temperature of **+25 °C**. The fish must be cooled to **0 °C** before departure. The refrigeration unit has a net capacity of **3.5 kW**, and the van's leakage load (transmission + infiltration + respiration at 0 °C) at this moment is **0.6 kW**. Calculate the **pull-down time**.

### Step 1 — Identify the energy balance
During pull-down, the available cooling capacity is partially consumed by ongoing heat leakage. The **net** capacity available to cool the product is:

$$Q_{net} = Q_{ref} - Q_{leakage} = 3.5 - 0.6 = 2.9 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the sensible heat equation
For fresh fish above freezing point, $c_p \approx 3.5 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ (high water content, plus oils).

$$Q_{sens} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 1500 \times 3.5 \times (25 - 0) = 131{,}250 \text{ kJ}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for pull-down time

$$t_{pd} = \frac{Q_{sens}}{Q_{net}} = \frac{131{,}250 \text{ kJ}}{2.9 \text{ kJ/s}} = 45{,}259 \text{ seconds}$$

Converting to hours:

$$t_{pd} = \frac{45{,}259}{3600} = 12.57 \text{ hours}$$

**Result:** The pull-down takes **≈ 12.6 hours**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why **pre-cooling at the depot** (before the van leaves) is critical. If the same fish is pre-cooled to 0 °C at the warehouse, the in-transit refrigeration unit only has to handle the **0.6 kW leakage** — a 5× reduction in required capacity and a vast improvement in fuel economy.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Respiration Heat for Fresh Produce

**Problem Statement:** A van is loaded with **5 tonnes of bananas** at **+13 °C** for transport at **+13 °C** (controlled ripening transport). Respiration rate of bananas at 13 °C is $r = 18 \text{ W/tonne}$ (from ASHRAE tables).

### Step 1 — Respiration load

$$Q_{resp} = m \cdot r = 5 \text{ tonnes} \times 18 \text{ W/tonne} = 90 \text{ W}$$

### Step 2 — Adjust for actual transit time and $\text{CO}_2$ build-up
If transit is 18 hours, total heat of respiration = $90 \text{ W} \times 18 \text{ h} = 1.62 \text{ kWh}$. This must be added to the refrigeration load sizing, **even though no temperature change is occurring in the product**, because respiration is a continuous metabolic exothermic process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Trick:** Students often *forget* respiration load because the product temperature is "constant." Respiration is heat generation, not heat change. It must always be added to $Q_{total}$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selecting Refrigeration Unit Capacity

| S. No. | Heat Load Source | Typical Magnitude (8 t reefer van) | Mitigation Strategy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Transmission through walls | 350 – 500 W | Increase PUF thickness to 120 mm |
| 2 | Product sensible cooling | 1500 – 3000 W (transient) | Pre-cool at depot |
| 3 | Product latent heat (freezing) | 5000 – 8000 W (transient) | Avoid in-transit freezing; pre-freeze |
| 4 | Respiration heat | 50 – 200 W | Ventilation, ethylene scrubbers |
| 5 | Infiltration (door openings) | 200 – 600 W | Strip curtains, air-curtain at door |
| 6 | Evaporator fan motors | 150 – 300 W | Use EC fans |
| 7 | Defrost heaters (cyclic) | 500 – 1200 W | Hot-gas defrost, demand-based |
| 8 | Engine-driven unit heat | 100 – 400 W | Insulate compressor compartment |
| **Total** | | **~ 3 – 8 kW (1 – 2.5 TR)** | |
