# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"List the various types of refrigeration systems used in refrigerated vans. Explain any one in brief."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The principal types of refrigeration systems used in refrigerated vans are:

1. **Ice-cooled system** — uses block ice as the cooling medium; simple but limited to ≥ 0 °C.
2. **Mechanical Vapour Compression Refrigeration (MVCR) system** — uses R-134a / R-404A in a compact compression cycle; most widely used.
3. **Cryogenic system** — uses liquid nitrogen (−196 °C) or liquid CO₂ (−78 °C) sprayed into the cargo.
4. **Eutectic plate system** — frozen saline plates act as cold accumulators.
5. **Hybrid multi-temperature system** — segregated compartments with independent temperature zones.

**Detailed explanation of MVCR (any one):** The MVCR system consists of a compressor (driven by an auxiliary diesel engine or by the vehicle engine via belt/PTO), a roof-mounted condenser with fan, a thermostatic expansion valve (TXV), and a ceiling-mounted evaporator with forced-air circulation fans. The refrigerant R-404A circulates, absorbs heat from the cargo at the evaporator, and rejects it to the ambient at the condenser. The system is controlled by a digital thermostat that cycles the compressor to maintain the setpoint temperature. **Advantages:** Wide temperature range (−25 °C to +10 °C), reliable, easy maintenance. **Disadvantages:** Higher cost, requires fuel for the auxiliary engine, more moving parts.

*(3 marks awarded: 1 mark for listing, 2 marks for explanation.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**"What is the significance of the ATP agreement in refrigerated transport? What is the maximum permissible $U$-value for an ATP Class-A insulated body?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **ATP (Accord Transports Périssables)** is an international agreement under the **United Nations Economic Commission for Europe (UNECE)** that lays down uniform standards for the international carriage of perishable foodstuffs and the technical specifications of the special equipment (refrigerated vehicles, insulated containers, etc.) used for such carriage. Its significance lies in:

1. **Standardization** of insulation performance across countries, enabling global trade in perishables.
2. **Quality assurance** — adherence to ATP confirms the cold chain integrity.
3. **Reduction of post-harvest losses** by enforcing minimum insulation and refrigeration performance.

The ATP classifies insulated bodies as **Class A, B, C, D** based on their mean overall heat transfer coefficient ($K$-value):

- **Class A:** $K \le 0.40 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$ — for fresh and chilled goods (0 °C to +7 °C).
- **Class B:** $K \le 0.40 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$ — for frozen goods.
- **Class C:** $K \le 0.70 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$ — for fruits and vegetables.
- **Class D:** $K \le 0.40 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$ — for deep-frozen goods (< −10 °C).

**The maximum permissible $U$-value (overall heat transfer coefficient) for an ATP Class-A insulated van body is $\mathbf{U \le 0.40 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}}$.**

*(3 marks awarded: 1 for ATP significance, 1 for class definition, 1 for U-value with units.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option (i) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]

**(a)** Explain the constructional features of a refrigerated van body. What are the functions of the outer skin, insulation, and vapour barrier? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 7-tonne capacity insulated van with internal dimensions **5.0 m × 2.2 m × 2.3 m** is used to transport fresh fish. The insulation is **120 mm thick PUF** with thermal conductivity **0.022 W/m·K**. The outer surface heat transfer coefficient is **12 W/m²·K** and the inner surface coefficient is **8 W/m²·K**. The van must maintain a cargo temperature of **0 °C** while the ambient temperature is **42 °C**. The fish enters at **+5 °C** and must be cooled to **0 °C** in **8 hours**. Additional loads include: door openings 6/hour, fan motor 250 W, defrost (cyclic) 800 W, engine waste heat 200 W. **Calculate the total refrigeration load and select a suitable refrigeration unit.** **(7 marks)**

Given data:
- Specific heat of fresh fish $c_p = 3.5 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$
- Latent heat of freezing $h_{fg} = 235 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (not applicable as fish is chilled, not frozen)
- Density of air $\rho_a = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Specific heat of air $c_{pa} = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$
- Assume 1 door opening = complete air change with $\Delta T = 42 \text{ °C}$

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Constructional Features (7 marks):**

A refrigerated van body is essentially a **monocoque insulated sandwich panel** comprising the following layers (from outside to inside):

1. **Outer Skin (1 mark):** Made of pre-coated galvanised iron (PCGI) or aluminium sheet of thickness 0.6 – 1.2 mm. It provides:
   - Mechanical rigidity and impact resistance.
   - Weather protection (rain, dust, UV).
   - Aesthetic finish.

2. **Vapour Barrier (Outer) (1 mark):** A continuous film of aluminium foil (0.05 mm) or polyethylene (0.1 mm) laminated to the inner face of the outer skin. **Function:** to prevent water vapour from the warm, humid ambient air from migrating into the PUF insulation. If moisture enters the foam, it condenses, the $k$-value rises, and thermal performance degrades (this is called *thermal bridging by moisture*).

3. **Insulation Core (2 marks):** Rigid **Polyurethane Foam (PUF)** of 80 – 150 mm thickness, in-situ foamed between the two skins. PUF is preferred because:
   - Lowest $k$ value among common insulations (0.020 – 0.024 W/m·K).
   - Self-adheres to skins (no air gaps).
   - Closed-cell structure (≥ 95 % closed cells) gives very low water absorption.
   - Fire-retardant grades (Class B2) available.

4. **Vapour Barrier (Inner) + Inner Skin (1 mark):** Inner vapour barrier protects against reverse vapour migration when the van is parked in a cold depot at night. Inner skin is food-grade PCGI/aluminium/FRP, often ribbed to allow free air circulation. The floor uses marine plywood or aluminium tread plate bonded to PUF and supported by Z-channels.

5. **Door Seals, Hinges, and Locking (1 mark):** Triple-lip rubber gaskets, stainless-steel cam-lock hinges, and a thermally broken door frame. These reduce $Q_{infiltration}$ through the largest single thermal bridge in any van.

6. **Drainage (1 mark):** A sloped floor with a drain plug and a T-baffle to allow melt-water and condensation to exit without letting warm air in.

---

**Part (b) — Heat Load Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Calculate U-value of the wall (1 mark):**

$$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_o} + \frac{x}{k} + \frac{1}{h_i} = \frac{1}{12} + \frac{0.120}{0.022} + \frac{1}{8}$$

$$\frac{1}{U} = 0.0833 + 5.4545 + 0.125 = 5.6628 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$

$$U = 0.1766 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Total surface area (0.5 marks):**

- Floor: $5.0 \times 2.2 = 11.0 \text{ m}^2$
- Ceiling: $5.0 \times 2.2 = 11.0 \text{ m}^2$
- Two long walls: $2 \times (5.0 \times 2.3) = 23.0 \text{ m}^2$
- Two short walls: $2 \times (2.2 \times 2.3) = 10.12 \text{ m}^2$
- **Total $A = 55.12 \text{ m}^2$**

**Step 3 — Transmission load (0.5 marks):**

$$Q_{trans} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 0.1766 \times 55.12 \times (42 - 0) = 408.7 \text{ W} \approx 0.409 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Product sensible cooling load (1 mark):**

$$Q_{sens} = \frac{m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}{t_{pd}} = \frac{7000 \times 3.5 \times 5}{8 \times 3600} = \frac{122{,}500}{28{,}800} = 4.253 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Infiltration load (1 mark):**
Volume of cargo $V = 5.0 \times 2.2 \times 2.3 = 25.3 \text{ m}^3$

$$Q_{inf} = \frac{n \cdot V \cdot \rho_a \cdot c_{pa} \cdot \Delta T}{3600} = \frac{6 \times 25.3 \times 1.2 \times 1.005 \times 42}{3600}$$

$$Q_{inf} = \frac{7686.2}{3600} = 2.135 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Internal loads (0.5 marks):**

$$Q_{internal} = 0.25 \text{ kW (fan)} + 0.80 \text{ kW (defrost, cyclic — use 50 percent duty)} = 0.25 + 0.40 = 0.65 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 7 — Engine waste heat (0.5 marks):**

$$Q_{engine} = 0.20 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 8 — Total refrigeration load (1 mark):**

$$Q_{total} = 0.409 + 4.253 + 2.135 + 0.65 + 0.20 = 7.647 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 9 — Apply 15 % safety factor and select unit (1 mark):**

$$Q_{selected} = 1.15 \times 7.647 = 8.79 \text{ kW}$$

Since 1 TR = 3.517 kW, this is equivalent to **2.5 TR**. A standard reefer van unit of **8.8 kW (2.5 TR)** capacity at 0 °C evaporating / 45 °C condensing should be selected. Examples in the Indian market: Thermo King V-200 / V-300 series, Carrier Supra series, Zanotti Z-series.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not forget the safety factor** — university answer scripts without the 10–20 % safety margin are penalized by 1 full mark.
> 2. **Watch the units:** W vs. kW — many students mix them and get astronomically wrong answers. Use kW consistently in the final sum.
> 3. **Infiltration is a *rate*, not a *total* energy:** Some students multiply the per-opening load by total transit time and forget the per-hour basis. Verify the unit conversion (per hour → per second requires division by 3600).
> 4. **Defrost is intermittent** — full defrost power is ON only for ~20–30 minutes per cycle. Use a 50 % duty factor unless otherwise stated.
> 5. **Respiration heat is often missed in fish-transport questions** because fish don't respire. But for fresh produce, it is mandatory.

---

### Question B — Option (ii) [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Modified]

**(a)** Discuss the different types of refrigeration systems used in road refrigerated transport. Compare mechanical refrigeration with cryogenic refrigeration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A reefer van is loaded with **4 tonnes of mangoes** at a field temperature of **30 °C** for transport at **+10 °C**. The transit time is **20 hours**, and the van has:
- Internal volume = **20 m³**, Total wall area = **40 m²**
- Overall $U$-value = **0.40 W/m²·K**
- Respiration rate of mangoes at 10 °C: **r = 25 W/tonne**
- Door openings: **4 per hour**, ambient = **38 °C**, inside = **10 °C**
- Internal loads: fan 200 W, defrost 600 W (50 % duty), engine heat 150 W
- Specific heat of mangoes $c_p = 3.6 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$

**Calculate the total refrigeration load and the pull-down time required to bring the mangoes from 30 °C to 10 °C, assuming the refrigeration unit is sized exactly equal to the total load.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Refrigeration Systems Comparison (7 marks):**

(2 marks for listing, 5 marks for comparison table + commentary)

The four major types of refrigeration systems for road transport are:

1. **Ice-cooled systems** — block ice in bunkers above the load; used for short-haul fish/vegetables in coastal Kerala.
2. **Mechanical Vapour Compression (MVCR)** — R-134a/R-404A systems driven by auxiliary diesel engine or vehicle engine; most versatile.
3. **Cryogenic (LN₂/LCO₂)** — liquid nitrogen or CO₂ sprayed into cargo; ultra-rapid chill, used in pharma/frozen food.
4. **Eutectic plate** — frozen salt solution plates; ideal for multi-stop milk and ice-cream delivery.

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Parameter | MVCR | Cryogenic (LN₂) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Temperature range | −25 °C to +10 °C | Down to −50 °C / −196 °C capability |
| Refrigerant cost | Moderate (R-404A) | High (LN₂ refilling) |
| Initial cost | High (₹ 3–6 lakh) | Moderate (₹ 2–4 lakh) |
| Running cost | Moderate (diesel) | High (cryogen consumption) |
| Pull-down speed | Slow (1–2 h) | Very fast (minutes) |
| Moving parts | Many (compressor, fans, valves) | Almost none |
| Maintenance | Periodic, skilled | Minimal, but cryogen logistics |
| Cargo suitability | Wide range | Frozen food, pharma |
| Environmental | GWP issue (R-404A) | Zero GWP (LN₂ from air) |
| ATP class achievable | A, B, C, D | A, B, C, D |

**Commentary (already in table):** MVCR is the workhorse of the industry — flexible, repairable, and cost-effective for multi-temperature fleets. Cryogenic vans excel in niche applications (vaccine distribution, premium frozen seafood exports from Kochi) where ultra-rapid chill and zero on-board emissions are valued over running cost.

---

**Part (b) — Heat Load Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Transmission load (1 mark):**

$$Q_{trans} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 0.40 \times 40 \times (38 - 10) = 0.40 \times 40 \times 28 = 448 \text{ W} = 0.448 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Product sensible cooling load (1 mark):**

$$Q_{sens} = \frac{m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}{t_{transit}} = \frac{4000 \times 3.6 \times (30 - 10)}{20 \times 3600} = \frac{288{,}000}{72{,}000} = 4.0 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Respiration load (0.5 marks):**

$$Q_{resp} = m \cdot r = 4 \text{ tonnes} \times 25 \text{ W/tonne} = 100 \text{ W} = 0.10 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Infiltration load (1 mark):**

$$Q_{inf} = \frac{n \cdot V \cdot \rho_a \cdot c_{pa} \cdot \Delta T}{3600} = \frac{4 \times 20 \times 1.2 \times 1.005 \times 28}{3600}$$

$$Q_{inf} = \frac{2701.4}{3600} = 0.750 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Internal loads (0.5 marks):**

$$Q_{internal} = 0.20 \text{ kW (fan)} + 0.60 \times 0.50 \text{ (defrost)} = 0.20 + 0.30 = 0.50 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Engine heat (0.25 marks):**

$$Q_{engine} = 0.15 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 7 — Total load (0.75 marks):**

$$Q_{total} = 0.448 + 4.0 + 0.10 + 0.750 + 0.50 + 0.15 = 5.948 \text{ kW} \approx 5.95 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 8 — Pull-down time for product from 30 °C to 10 °C (1 mark):**

Since the unit is sized *exactly* equal to the total load, the net cooling available for the product is *zero* during the steady-state condition. Therefore, the pull-down time is **theoretically infinite** under steady state. In practice, the unit is oversized by 10–20 %, and pull-down occurs during the initial hours when the system runs continuously and the leakage is still ramping up.

**Practical interpretation:** The student should note that the calculated $Q_{total}$ (5.95 kW) is the *running* load to maintain 10 °C. To achieve pull-down, the refrigeration capacity must be larger. For example, if the actual unit is 7 kW:

$$Q_{net,pd} = 7.0 - (Q_{leakage} \text{ at start}) = 7.0 - (0.448 + 0.10 + 0.50 + 0.15) = 7.0 - 1.198 = 5.802 \text{ kW}$$

$$t_{pd} = \frac{4000 \times 3.6 \times 20}{5.802 \times 3600} = \frac{288{,}000}{20{,}887} = 13.79 \text{ hours}$$

So pull-down takes ~13.8 hours; this is why **pre-cooling at the pack-house** is universally recommended.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout for Part B Option (ii):**
> 1. **Always include respiration heat** for fresh fruits/vegetables — this is a very common omission.
> 2. **Specific heat of food** varies — use the value provided in the question; do not assume water (4.18 kJ/kg·K).
> 3. **Convert W to kW** before summing — mixing units in the final addition is the #1 cause of wrong answers.
> 4. **Door openings are *events per hour*** — multiply by 3600 (seconds per hour) when computing W.
> 5. **Engine heat** is often forgotten; if the unit is engine-driven, add 8–12 % of compressor work or use the value given.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Refrigerated Vans**

- ✅ **Definition:** A thermally insulated road vehicle with an active/passive refrigeration unit, used to maintain perishable cargo at a controlled low temperature.
- ✅ **Five types of systems:** Ice-cooled, MVCR (mechanical), Cryogenic (LN₂/CO₂), Eutectic plate, Hybrid multi-temperature.
- ✅ **Five construction layers:** Outer skin → Vapour barrier (outer) → PUF insulation (80–150 mm) → Vapour barrier (inner) → Inner skin.
- ✅ **Why PUF?** Lowest $k$ (0.020–0.024 W/m·K), closed-cell, self-bonding, seamless.
- ✅ **ATP classes:** A (0 to +7 °C), B (−10 to 0 °C), C (+7 to +12 °C), D (< −10 °C); max $U$ = 0.40 W/m²·K (A, B, D), 0.70 (C).
- ✅ **Heat load components (must include ALL):** Transmission + Product (sensible + latent) + Respiration + Infiltration + Internal (fan/defrost/personnel) + Engine heat.
- ✅ **Master formulas:**
  - $Q_{trans} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$
  - $Q_{sens} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$
  - $Q_{latent} = m \cdot h_{fg}$
  - $Q_{resp} = m \cdot r$
  - $Q_{inf} = \dfrac{n \cdot V \cdot \rho_a \cdot c_{pa} \cdot \Delta T}{3600}$
  - $t_{pd} = \dfrac{m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}{Q_{ref} - Q_{leakage}}$
- ✅ **Typical design U-value:** 0.20 – 0.40 W/m²·K (Indian tropical conditions).
- ✅ **Sizing rule:** Total load + 10–20 % safety margin → refrigeration unit capacity in kW or TR.
- ✅ **Respiration is heat generation, not heat change** — must always be added even if product temperature is constant.
- ✅ **Pull-down time** can be 8–18 hours for fresh produce → pre-cooling at the pack-house saves fuel and time.
- ✅ **Defrost is intermittent** → use 50 % duty cycle unless stated.
- ✅ **Engine-driven units** dump 8–12 % of compressor work into the cargo space — must be added to $Q_{total}$.
- ✅ **Air infiltration** is the most variable load — use strip curtains, air curtains, and minimize door openings.
- ✅ **Real-world impact:** Cold chain integrity saves ₹ 92,000 crore/yr in India (NCCD data); reefer van engineers are central to pharma, dairy, and agri-exports.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:**
> In 14-mark problems, always present your answer in the **exact order** of the formula derivation:
> (1) State the given data in a box.
> (2) Compute $U$-value and surface area.
> (3) Compute each heat load term *separately* with units.
> (4) Sum all loads in kW.
> (5) Apply safety factor.
> (6) Select refrigeration unit and convert to TR if required.
> This structured approach alone can fetch 2–3 extra marks because KTU examiners award marks for *method* in addition to *correctness*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Refrigerated Display - Applications of Refrigeration and Air Conditioning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT411 - REFRIGERATION, AIR CONDITIONING AND COLD STORAGE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Refrigerated Display - Applications of Refrigeration and Air Conditioning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🧊 Refrigerated Display Cabinets — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

A **Refrigerated Display Cabinet (RDC)** is a thermally insulated, customer-accessible enclosure that maintains perishable food products (dairy, meat, fish, fruits, vegetables, beverages, frozen foods) at controlled temperatures in the range of **−25 °C to +12 °C** while simultaneously allowing visual merchandising and physical access by customers in a commercial retail environment such as supermarkets, hypermarkets, and convenience stores.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Refrigerated displays fall under the broader "Applications of Refrigeration" cluster. They are essentially *retail cold-chain terminals* that combine the science of **heat transfer**, **refrigeration**, and **human-factors ergonomics**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Cold Shop Window"

Imagine a shop window behind which a jeweller keeps diamonds at a comfortable viewing temperature. Replace the diamonds with ice-cream, and the comfortable room with a freezer — that is a **refrigerated display**. The unique engineering challenge is that:

- The "shopkeeper" (refrigeration system) must keep the goods cold.
- The "window" (transparent door or open front) must let customers *see* and *reach* the goods.
- The "outside" is a warm, humid supermarket aisle at roughly **24 °C / 55% RH**.

Every time a customer opens a door or reaches in, warm air **infiltrates**, raising the refrigeration load. The display cabinet is therefore a *compromise between visibility, accessibility, and thermal efficiency*.

## 1.3 Key Operational Parameters

| Parameter | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|
| Design store temperature | −25 °C to +12 °C (product class dependent) |
| Ambient (store) conditions | **24 °C DBT, 55% RH** (ASHRAE / ISHRAE standard) |
| Display life of product | Hours to several days |
| Energy consumption | **High** (RDCs account for 30–60% of supermarket refrigeration energy) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification by temperature (IS/ASHRAE convention):**
> - Frozen food: ≤ −18 °C
> - Ice cream: ≤ −25 °C
> - Meat/fish (chilled): 0 °C to +2 °C
> - Dairy/eggs: +2 °C to +6 °C
> - Fresh produce: +4 °C to +8 °C
> - Beverages: +6 °C to +12 °C

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ Refrigerated Display Cabinets — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Refrigerated Displays

RDCs are broadly classified along **three independent axes**:

### 2.1.1 By Geometry & Orientation
1. **Vertical (Multideck)** — Upright, multi-shelf, resembles a tall glass-door wardrobe. Used for dairy, beverages, fresh produce.
2. **Horizontal (Island / Bin)** — Low, open-top chest-style. Used for ice cream, frozen foods, vegetables (waterfall ends).
3. **Semi-vertical** — Hybrid with inclined shelves and partial front glass.

### 2.1.2 By Front Access
1. **Closed type** — Hinged or sliding glass doors. Lowest energy use.
2. **Open type (Open-front)** — No door, protected only by an **air curtain**. Higher energy use (~30–50% more) but superior merchandising.
3. **Hybrid (Night blind / Lid)** — Closed at night, open by day.

### 2.1.3 By Refrigeration Loop
1. **Integral (Self-contained)** — Hermetic compressor mounted within the case.
2. **Remote (Centralised)** — Connected to a **racks-based compressor plant** via DX or secondary-loop piping — the industry norm in large supermarkets.

> [!TIP]
> KTU frequently tests the difference between **Open and Closed** displays. The closed type is the **reference baseline**; the open type carries an *air-curtain penalty* in load calculations.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Subsystem

- **Insulated walls (PUF / VIP):** Minimize conduction heat gain $Q_{\text{cond}}$; thicker insulation → lower $U$-value → lower operating cost but higher cabinet cost.
- **Vapour-compression refrigeration:** Removes heat $Q_L$ from the product and the cabinet interior.
- **Forced-air circulation (fans):** Maintains temperature uniformity across shelves and supports the air curtain.
- **Air curtain (for open cases):** A high-velocity laminar sheet of cold air that forms a *thermal barrier* across the open front.
- **Defrost system:** Removes frost from the evaporator (electric, hot-gas, or reverse-cycle).
- **Lighting:** Low-heat LED strips to avoid adding to the refrigeration load.
- **Drainage & Heater strips:** Remove condensate and prevent door-frame icing.

## 2.3 The Air Curtain — The Heart of the Open Display

The **air curtain** is the most heavily examined KTU concept on this topic. It is a **rectangular sheet of cold air** discharged from a top grille across the opening, re-circulated through a bottom return grille.

### 2.3.1 Why the Air Curtain Exists
1. **Suppresses convective infiltration** of warm, humid store air into the cold interior (this is the dominant load).
2. **Suppresses diffusive mass transfer** (water vapour) into the cabinet, reducing frost formation on the evaporator.
3. **Suppresses radiative heat transfer** by acting as a partially opaque, low-emissivity layer.

### 2.3.2 Air Curtain Effectiveness ($\eta$)
The **effectiveness** of an air curtain is defined as the fraction of the open-front heat transfer it suppresses:

$$\eta = 1 - \frac{Q_{\text{with curtain}}}{Q_{\text{open front}}}$$

- $\eta = 0$ → No curtain (just an open hole — like leaving a window open in winter).
- $\eta = 1$ → Perfect insulation (like a closed door).
- Typical real values: $\eta \approx 0.6$ to $0.85$.

### 2.3.3 Mass Flow Rate of the Air Curtain
The required mass flow rate is set by balancing the curtain's momentum against buoyancy:

$$\dot{m}_c = \rho_{\text{air}} \, A_c \, v_c$$

where $A_c$ is the cross-sectional area of the curtain (height $H$ × depth $D$) and $v_c$ is the discharge velocity (typically **0.5 to 1.5 m/s** for chilled, higher for frozen).

## 2.4 Refrigeration Load Components

The total refrigeration load $Q_{\text{total}}$ for a display cabinet has **five** contributors, and KTU exam problems test all five.

| # | Load Component | Symbol | Physical Origin |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transmission (conduction) through walls | $Q_{\text{trans}}$ | $U A \Delta T$ through insulated panels |
| 2 | Infiltration via air curtain (open) or door openings (closed) | $Q_{\text{inf}}$ | Enthalpy difference of incoming warm air |
| 3 | Product load (sensible + latent) | $Q_{\text{prod}}$ | Pulling goods down from room temp to store temp |
| 4 | Internal loads (fans, lights, defrost) | $Q_{\text{int}}$ | Electrical → heat conversion |
| 5 | Radiation through glass | $Q_{\text{rad}}$ | Direct solar or lighting IR (minor) |

## 2.5 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_{\text{trans}} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Conduction through cabinet walls | W |
| 2 | $Q_{\text{inf}} = \dot{m}_{\text{inf}} \, (h_{\text{out}} - h_{\text{in}})$ | Enthalpy-based infiltration load | W |
| 3 | $Q_{\text{inf,open}} = (1 - \eta) \, \dot{m}_{\text{open}} \, \Delta h$ | Infiltration through open front with air curtain | W |
| 4 | $Q_{\text{prod}} = m \, c_p \, (T_{\text{in}} - T_{\text{store}}) + m \, h_{fg}$ | Sensible + latent product pull-down | W |
| 5 | $Q_{\text{int}} = P_{\text{fan}} + P_{\text{light}} + P_{\text{heater,defrost}}$ | Internal heat dissipation (electric) | W |
| 6 | $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{\text{with curtain}}}{Q_{\text{open}}}$ | Air curtain effectiveness | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\dot{m}_c = \rho \, A_c \, v_c$ | Curtain mass flow rate | kg/s |
| 8 | $A_c = H \cdot D$ | Curtain cross-section (height × depth) | $\text{m}^2$ |
| 9 | $Q_{\text{total}} = Q_{\text{trans}} + Q_{\text{inf}} + Q_{\text{prod}} + Q_{\text{int}} + Q_{\text{rad}}$ | Total display load | W |
| 10 | $\dot{V}_{\text{curtain}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_c}{\rho}$ | Volumetric curtain flow | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, $U$ is the **overall heat transfer coefficient** of the insulated wall, $A$ is the surface area, $\Delta T$ is the temperature difference between the store ambient and the cabinet internal air, $h$ is the specific enthalpy (J/kg), and $h_{fg}$ is the latent heat of fusion (for frozen goods).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Supermarket design**: Heat extracted by RDCs is often the **largest single load** in a supermarket. Designers sum per-cabinet $Q_{\text{total}}$ to size the central compressor rack.
- **Heat reclaim**: Waste heat from the condenser side of RDC refrigeration is reused to heat the store in winter, cutting HVAC costs.
- **Energy codes (BEE / ECBC / ASHRAE 90.1)** set maximum $Q_{\text{total}}$ per linear metre of display length — designers must demonstrate compliance.
- **Cold-chain integrity**: RDC temperature logging is legally required for food safety (FSSAI in India, HACCP globally).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Refrigerated Display Cabinets — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Refrigeration Load for an Open Multideck Chilled Display

**Given:** Vertical multideck open-front display, dimensions 2.0 m (L) × 2.2 m (H) × 0.9 m (D). Store at **24 °C DBT, 55% RH**. Cabinet internal air at **+4 °C**. Air curtain velocity $v_c = 1.0$ m/s, depth $D = 0.9$ m. Curtain effectiveness $\eta = 0.75$. Wall $U = 0.30 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$, total wall area (excl. front) $A = 14.8 \text{ m}^2$. Internal fan + light + defrost = **600 W**. Product pull-down 200 kg/day from 20 °C to 4 °C.

**Step 1 — Transmission (conduction) load**

$$Q_{\text{trans}} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$

Substitute $U = 0.30 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$, $A = 14.8 \text{ m}^2$, $\Delta T = 24 - 4 = 20 \text{ K}$:

$$Q_{\text{trans}} = 0.30 \times 14.8 \times 20$$

$$Q_{\text{trans}} = 0.30 \times 296 = 88.8 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — Infiltration load via open front (no curtain, hypothetical baseline)**

For the open hole of height $H = 2.2$ m and width $L = 2.0$ m, the discharge coefficient $C_d \approx 0.7$, and buoyancy drives flow. Empirical correlation for natural-discharge cold-air loss:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{open}} = C_d \, \rho_{\text{amb}} \, A_{\text{open}} \sqrt{2 g H \, \dfrac{\Delta T}{T_{\text{amb}}}}$$

$\rho_{\text{amb}} \approx 1.18 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $A_{\text{open}} = 2.0 \times 2.2 = 4.4 \text{ m}^2$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $H = 2.2$ m, $\Delta T = 20$ K, $T_{\text{amb}} = 297$ K.

$$\dot{m}_{\text{open}} = 0.7 \times 1.18 \times 4.4 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 2.2 \times \dfrac{20}{297}}$$

Inside the square root:

$$2 \times 9.81 \times 2.2 \times \dfrac{20}{297} = 43.164 \times 0.0673 = 2.905$$

$$\sqrt{2.905} = 1.704 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{open}} = 0.7 \times 1.18 \times 4.4 \times 1.704$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{open}} = 0.7 \times 1.18 \times 7.498 = 6.193 \text{ kg/s}$$

Enthalpy difference: $h_{\text{amb}}$ at 24 °C, 55% RH $\approx 49.0$ kJ/kg; $h_{\text{int}}$ at 4 °C, saturated $\approx 12.5$ kJ/kg. So:

$$\Delta h = 49.0 - 12.5 = 36.5 \text{ kJ/kg} = 36500 \text{ J/kg}$$

$$Q_{\text{inf,open}} = 6.193 \times 36500 = 226{,}045 \text{ W} \approx 226.0 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Actual infiltration load with the air curtain**

With $\eta = 0.75$, only 25% of the open-front load passes through:

$$Q_{\text{inf}} = (1 - \eta) \, Q_{\text{inf,open}} = 0.25 \times 226.0 \text{ kW}$$

$$Q_{\text{inf}} = 56.5 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Product pull-down load (averaged over 24 h)**

Using $c_p = 3.6$ kJ/(kg·K) for typical chilled food, $\dot{m} = 200/24 \times 3600 = 2.31 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/s:

$$Q_{\text{prod}} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 2.31 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 \times (20 - 4)$$

$$Q_{\text{prod}} = 8.32 \times 16 = 133.1 \text{ W}$$

(Latent term is zero because products are chilled, not frozen.)

**Step 5 — Internal heat loads**

$$Q_{\text{int}} = 600 \text{ W}$$

**Step 6 — Total refrigeration load**

$$Q_{\text{total}} = Q_{\text{trans}} + Q_{\text{inf}} + Q_{\text{prod}} + Q_{\text{int}}$$

$$Q_{\text{total}} = 88.8 + 56{,}500 + 133.1 + 600$$

$$Q_{\text{total}} \approx 57{,}322 \text{ W} \approx 57.3 \text{ kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Infiltration via the open front dominates the load** (≈ 98.5% of $Q_{\text{total}}$). This is precisely why **air curtain design is the single most important engineering decision** in supermarket refrigeration. A jump from $\eta = 0.75$ to $\eta = 0.85$ would cut $Q_{\text{inf}}$ from 56.5 kW to 33.9 kW — saving ~22.6 kW of refrigeration capacity, which is real money over a year of operation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Curtain Mass Flow Rate

Given: $H = 2.2$ m, $D = 0.9$ m, $v_c = 1.0$ m/s, $\rho_{\text{curtain}} \approx 1.27$ kg/m³ (cold air at 4 °C).

$$A_c = H \times D = 2.2 \times 0.9 = 1.98 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\dot{m}_c = \rho \, A_c \, v_c = 1.27 \times 1.98 \times 1.0$$

$$\dot{m}_c = 2.515 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\dot{V}_c = \dfrac{\dot{m}_c}{\rho} = \dfrac{2.515}{1.27} = 1.98 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

In volumetric terms, the curtain circulates the equivalent of a small room of air every second — yet the engineering value of doing so is to **save more than 22 kW of refrigeration load**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Open vs. Closed Multideck Cabinet

| Performance Metric | Closed Multideck (with glass doors) | Open Multideck (with air curtain) |
|---|---|---|
| Energy consumption (relative) | **1.0 (baseline)** | 1.4 – 2.0 |
| Infiltration share of $Q_{\text{total}}$ | 5–15% | 70–95% |
| Customer visibility | Slightly reduced (door frames) | Excellent (full frontal view) |
| Hygiene exposure | Low (door closed) | Higher (open air contact) |
| Product temperature stability | **Excellent** | Moderate |
| Typical application | Frozen foods, premium dairy | Beverages, fresh produce, grab-and-go |
| Capital cost | Higher (glass doors, gaskets, hinges) | Lower (no doors) |
| Operating cost | Lower | Higher |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Closed Multideck Door-Opening Load

**Given:** Glass-door multideck, $N = 30$ door openings per hour, each lasting $t = 6$ s. Effective opening area $A_{\text{do}} = 0.6$ m². Air exchange rate during opening $\approx C_d = 0.65$ with velocity $v \approx 1.5$ m/s due to door motion. Store 24 °C, 55% RH; cabinet +4 °C saturated.

Volumetric inflow per opening:

$$V_{\text{op}} = C_d \, A_{\text{do}} \, v \, t = 0.65 \times 0.6 \times 1.5 \times 6 = 3.51 \text{ m}^3$$

Mass per opening (warm air, $\rho \approx 1.18$ kg/m³):

$$m_{\text{op}} = 1.18 \times 3.51 = 4.14 \text{ kg}$$

Hourly mass: $\dot{m} = 30 \times 4.14 = 124.2$ kg/h $= 0.0345$ kg/s.

Infiltration enthalpy load:

$$Q_{\text{inf,closed}} = 0.0345 \times 36{,}500 = 1259.3 \text{ W} \approx 1.26 \text{ kW}$$

This is roughly **45× smaller** than the open-front case (56.5 kW), explaining why door-fitted closed cabinets dominate frozen-food retail.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Refrigerated Display Cabinets — Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Open Multideck Display

The diagram below shows the **block-level functional architecture** of a typical open-front vertical multideck display. It traces the cold-air path, the warm-air infiltration path, the air-curtain flow, the refrigerant loop, and the auxiliary subsystems.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Store Ambient Air 24 C 55 RH]:::warm
    A2[Display Cabinet Interior 4 C]:::cold
    A3[Air Curtain Discharge Grille Top]:::curtain
    A4[Air Curtain Return Grille Bottom]:::curtain
    A5[Evaporator Coil with Fans]:::cold
    A6[Product Load Zone Shelves]:::product
    A7[Insulated Wall Panels PUF]:::shell
    A8[LED Lighting Strip]:::aux
    A9[Defrost Heater and Drain Pan]:::aux
    A10[Compressor Rack Remote]:::refrig
    A11[Condenser Outdoor]:::refrig
    A12[Expansion Valve]:::refrig
    A13[Controller and Sensors T RH]:::ctrl

    A1 -- Warm Infiltration Buoyancy --> A2
    A2 -- Cold Plume Discharge --> A1
    A3 -- High Velocity Cold Sheet --> A4
    A4 -- Return to Evaporator --> A5
    A5 -- Chilled Air to Shelves --> A6
    A6 -- Heat Gain to Air --> A5
    A1 -- Conduction Through Wall --> A7
    A7 -- Reduced Heat Gain --> A2
    A8 -- Heat to Interior --> A2
    A9 -- Heat During Defrost --> A2
    A5 -- Low Pressure Vapour --> A10
    A10 -- High Pressure Vapour --> A11
    A11 -- Liquid Refrigerant --> A12
    A12 -- Low Pressure Liquid --> A5
    A13 -- Modulates Fan Speed and Valve --> A5
    A13 -- Switches Defrost --> A9

    classDef warm fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#c33,stroke-width:1px,color:#600
    classDef cold fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#36c,stroke-width:1px,color:#036
    classDef curtain fill:#e0ccff,stroke:#93c,stroke-width:1px,color:#309
    classDef product fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#c93,stroke-width:1px,color:#630
    classDef shell fill:#dddddd,stroke:#666,stroke-width:1px,color:#222
    classDef aux fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#c60,stroke-width:1px,color:#630
    classDef refrig fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#393,stroke-width:1px,color:#030
    classDef ctrl fill:#ffccff,stroke:#939,stroke-width:1px,color:#303
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to read this diagram for the exam:** Trace the **blue cold loop** (return grille → evaporator → shelves) and the **red warm loop** (store ambient → infiltration → cabinet) separately. The **purple curtain path** is what *intercepts* the warm path before it dumps its enthalpy into the cabinet. The **green refrigerant loop** is the standard vapour-compression cycle that lifts the absorbed heat to the condenser.

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Closed Multideck with Glass Doors

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    B1[Store Ambient]:::warm
    B2[Cold Interior -18 C Frozen or 4 C Chilled]:::cold
    B3[Glass Door with Hinge or Slider]:::shell
    B4[Door Gasket Triple Seal]:::shell
    B5[Anti Sweat Heater on Door Frame]:::aux
    B6[Evaporator with Low Fan Power]:::cold
    B7[LED Side Lighting]:::aux
    B8[Defrost Cycle Hot Gas]:::aux
    B9[Product Storage Zone]:::product
    B10[Night Blind Optional]:::shell
    B11[Compressor Plant Remote]:::refrig
```

# 📝 Refrigerated Display Cabinets — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between open-front and closed-front refrigerated display cabinets with respect to (i) air infiltration, (ii) energy consumption, and (iii) merchandising effectiveness.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Open-Front | Closed-Front (Glass Door) |
|---|---|---|
| Air infiltration | **High**; suppressed by an air-curtain only | **Low**; suppressed by gaskets and doors |
| Energy consumption | High (~1.4–2.0 × closed) | Low (baseline) |
| Merchandising effectiveness | Excellent frontal visibility | Slightly reduced by door frames |

Open cabinets trade energy for visibility, while closed cabinets trade visibility for efficiency.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define "air-curtain effectiveness" and state the typical values achieved in commercial refrigerated displays.**

**Model Answer:**

Air-curtain effectiveness $\eta$ is defined as the fraction of the open-front heat-transfer load that is suppressed by the air curtain:

$$\eta = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{\text{with curtain}}}{Q_{\text{open front}}}$$

Typical values for commercial refrigerated displays lie in the range **0.6 to 0.85**, with well-designed modern systems reaching **0.85–0.90** when discharge velocity and air-temperature differential are carefully tuned.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### 📌 Question A — 14 Marks (Unit-aligned on Heat-Load Calculation for an Open Multideck) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

A supermarket plans to install an open-front vertical multideck display for chilled dairy products. The cabinet has the following specifications:

- Dimensions: 2.5 m (length) × 2.0 m (height) × 0.9 m (depth)
- Store ambient: 26 °C DBT, 60% RH
- Cabinet internal: +4 °C, saturated
- Air curtain: discharge velocity 1.1 m/s, effectiveness $\eta = 0.78$
- Wall: $U = 0.28 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$, total envelope area (excluding the open front) = 18 m²
- Internal loads (fans + LED + intermittent defrost) = 750 W
- Product load: 250 kg/day pulled down from 22 °C to 4 °C; $c_p = 3.6$ kJ/(kg·K), no latent component

Compute:  
**(a) The total refrigeration load on the cabinet, listing each load component. (7 marks)**  
**(b) The volumetric flow rate of the air curtain and comment on how a 10% increase in curtain velocity affects the total load. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Total Refrigeration Load**

*Step 1: Transmission load*

$$Q_{\text{trans}} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 0.28 \times 18 \times (26 - 4)$$

$$Q_{\text{trans}} = 0.28 \times 18 \times 22 = 110.88 \text{ W}$$

*Step 2: Open-front baseline infiltration (no curtain)*

$A_{\text{open}} = L \times H = 2.5 \times 2.0 = 5.0 \text{ m}^2$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{open}} = C_d \cdot \rho_{\text{amb}} \cdot A_{\text{open}} \cdot \sqrt{2 g H \cdot \dfrac{\Delta T}{T_{\text{amb}}}}$$

With $C_d = 0.7$, $\rho_{\text{amb}} = 1.165$ kg/m³ (at 26 °C), $g = 9.81$ m/s², $H = 2.0$ m, $\Delta T = 22$ K, $T_{\text{amb}} = 299$ K:

$$\text{Term inside sqrt} = 2 \times 9.81 \times 2.0 \times \dfrac{22}{299} = 39.24 \times 0.0736 = 2.888$$

$$\sqrt{2.888} = 1.700 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{open}} = 0.7 \times 1.165 \times 5.0 \times 1.700 = 6.93 \text{ kg/s}$$

Enthalpy difference: $h_{\text{amb}}$ at 26 °C/60% RH $\approx 58.0$ kJ/kg; $h_{\text{int}}$ at 4 °C saturated $\approx 12.5$ kJ/kg. $\Delta h = 45.5$ kJ/kg = 45,500 J/kg.

$$Q_{\text{inf,open}} = 6.93 \times 45{,}500 = 315{,}315 \text{ W} \approx 315.3 \text{ kW}$$

*Step 3: Actual infiltration with curtain ($\eta = 0.78$)*

$$Q_{\text{inf}} = (1 - \eta) \times Q_{\text{inf,open}} = 0.22 \times 315.3 = 69.4 \text{ kW}$$

*Step 4: Product pull-down load*

$\dot{m} = 250/(24 \times 3600) = 2.89 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/s

$$Q_{\text{prod}} = 2.89 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 \times (22 - 4) = 187.4 \text{ W}$$

*Step 5: Internal loads*

$$Q_{\text{int}} = 750 \text{ W}$$

*Step 6: Total*

$$Q_{\text{total}} = 110.88 + 69{,}400 + 187.4 + 750 \approx 70{,}448 \text{ W} \approx 70.45 \text{ kW}$$

**Part (b) — Curtain Volumetric Flow & Sensitivity**

$$A_c = H \times D = 2.0 \times 0.9 = 1.8 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\dot{m}_c = \rho_{\text{curtain}} \cdot A_c \cdot v_c = 1.27 \times 1.8 \times 1.1 = 2.515 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\dot{V}_c = \dfrac{2.515}{1.27} = 1.98 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

If velocity rises by 10% ($v_c' = 1.21$ m/s), assuming effectiveness also rises to $\eta' = 0.82$ (a 4-point gain is realistic for a 10% velocity increase, with diminishing returns beyond a certain point):

$$Q_{\text{inf}}' = 0.18 \times 315.3 = 56.75 \text{ kW}$$

$$\text{Load reduction} = 69.4 - 56.75 = 12.65 \text{ kW (≈ 18.2% reduction in } Q_{\text{inf}}\text{)}$$

But: **fan power rises** (proportional to $v^3$). Original fan power estimate = ~150 W; new fan power = 150 × $1.1^3$ ≈ 200 W. Net benefit: ~12.6 kW saved in infiltration vs. ~50 W added in fan heat — **the trade-off is overwhelmingly favourable**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. Forgetting to apply the air-curtain effectiveness $(1-\eta)$ factor and reporting $Q_{\text{inf,open}}$ as the final $Q_{\text{inf}}$. This single error costs **3–4 marks**.
> 2. Mixing up $h_{\text{amb}}$ and $h_{\text{int}}$ (a common sign error). Always: $\Delta h = h_{\text{warm}} - h_{\text{cold}}$.
> 3. Computing product load per hour and forgetting to convert to seconds (W, not kJ/h).
> 4. Forgetting to add internal loads in the final sum (at least ½ mark).

---

### 📌 Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice: Closed Multideck with Door-Opening Analysis) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

A closed multideck cabinet for frozen foods has the following specifications:

- Cabinet internal temperature: −20 °C, saturated
- Store ambient: 24 °C DBT, 50% RH
- Glass-door area: 1.8 m (H) × 0.8 m (W) per door, 5 doors total
- Door openings: 20 per door per hour, each lasting 4 seconds
- Wall $U = 0.20$ W/m²K; envelope area (excl. doors) = 12 m²
- Internal fan + light + anti-sweat heater = 900 W
- Product load: 100 kg/day pulled from 18 °C to −20 °C; $c_p = 2.0$ kJ/(kg·K) frozen, $c_p = 3.6$ kJ/(kg·K) chilled, latent heat of freezing = 235 kJ/kg, freezing point 0 °C

Compute:  
**(a) The total refrigeration load. (7 marks)**  
**(b) Compare the door-opening infiltration load of this closed cabinet with an equivalent open-front cabinet and discuss design implications. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Total Load**

*Step 1: Transmission load*

$$Q_{\text{trans}} = 0.20 \times 12 \times (24 - (-20)) = 0.20 \times 12 \times 44 = 105.6 \text{ W}$$

*Step 2: Door-opening infiltration*

Volumetric inflow per opening (assume $C_d = 0.6$, $v = 1.2$ m/s during opening):

$$V_{\text{op}} = 0.6 \times (1.8 \times 0.8) \times 1.2 \times 4 = 4.147 \text{ m}^3$$

Mass per opening at $\rho_{\text{amb}} \approx 1.18$ kg/m³: $m_{\text{op}} = 1.18 \times 4.147 = 4.89$ kg.

Total openings per hour: $N = 5 \times 20 = 100$. Hourly mass: $100 \times 4.89 = 489$ kg/h $= 0.1358$ kg/s.

Enthalpy at 24 °C, 50% RH: $h_{\text{amb}} \approx 47.6$ kJ/kg. Enthalpy at −20 °C saturated: $h_{\text{int}} \approx -19.5$ kJ/kg. $\Delta h = 67.1$ kJ/kg = 67,100 J/kg.

$$Q_{\text{inf,closed}} = 0.1358 \times 67{,}100 = 9111 \text{ W} \approx 9.11 \text{ kW}$$

*Step 3: Product load*

Daily product mass = 100 kg. Three stages:
1. Chilled 18 °C → 0 °C: $Q_1 = 100 \times 3.6 \times 18 = 6480$ kJ
2. Freezing at 0 °C: $Q_2 = 100 \times 235 = 23{,}500$ kJ
3. Frozen 0 °C → −20 °C: $Q_3 = 100 \times 2.0 \times 20 = 4000$ kJ

Total daily = 33,980 kJ. Average power = 33,980 / 86,400 = 0.393 kW = 393 W.

*Step 4: Internal loads*

$$Q_{\text{int}} = 900 \text{ W}$$

*Step 5: Total*

$$Q_{\text{total}} = 105.6 + 9111 + 393 + 900 = 10{,}510 \text{ W} \approx 10.5 \text{ kW}$$

**Part (b) — Comparison and Design Implications**

If the same cabinet were converted to open-front (no doors) under the same ambient/cabinet temperatures, using $\eta = 0.75$ and an empirical open-front mass flow rate of ~7 kg/s (from prior analysis scaled to this area):

$$Q_{\text{inf,open}} \approx (1 - 0.75) \times 7 \times 67{,}100 = 0.25 \times 7 \times 67.1 = 117.4 \text{ kW}$$

The open-front infiltration would be **~13× higher** than the closed-cabinet door-opening infiltration (117.4 kW vs 9.11 kW).

**Design implications:**
1. **Closed cabinets are mandatory for frozen storage** — open fronts would cause excessive frost, compressor over-sizing, and product temperature rise.
2. **Anti-sweat door heaters** are essential to prevent condensation fogging the glass (otherwise visibility fails).
3. **Gasket integrity** is a maintenance critical item — a 1 mm gasket gap can double the infiltration rate.
4. **Door auto-closers** reduce $t_{\text{open}}$ from 4 s to < 2 s, cutting infiltration by ~50%.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question B version:**
> 1. Treating the product as a single $c_p$ across the entire cooling range. **Always split the product load at the freezing point** and use the *chilled* $c_p$ above it and the *frozen* $c_p$ below it.
> 2. Forgetting the **latent heat of freezing** term — for frozen food product loads it is the **single largest contributor** (~69% of total product load here).
> 3. Treating the glass-door area as part of the wall conduction area (it should be excluded or treated with a separate $U_{\text{glass}} \approx 1.0$ W/m²K, not $U_{\text{wall}} = 0.20$).
> 4. Comparing closed and open infiltration without using the same $\Delta h$ — both must use the same enthalpy difference for the comparison to be valid.

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam**

- **Definition:** An RDC is a customer-accessible, temperature-controlled merchandising cabinet. Range: **−25 °C to +12 °C**.
- **Three classification axes:** Geometry (vertical/horizontal/semi-vertical), Front access (closed/open/hybrid), Refrigeration loop (integral/remote).
- **Open-front cabinets** are dominated by **infiltration load** (70–95% of $Q_{\text{total}}$).
- **Closed-front cabinets** have infiltration only from intermittent door openings — much smaller.
- **Air curtain effectiveness $\eta = 1 - Q_{\text{with}} / Q_{\text{open}}$**, typical range 0.6–0.85.
- **Five load components (memorise the order):** $Q_{\text{trans}}$, $Q_{\text{inf}}$, $Q_{\text{prod}}$, $Q_{\text{int}}$, $Q_{\text{rad}}$.
- **Curtain mass flow:** $\dot{m}_c = \rho A_c v_c$ where $A_c = H \times D$ (not $H \times L$).
- **Product load for frozen goods** requires **three terms**: sensible above freezing point, latent at freezing point, sensible below freezing point.
- **Internal loads** always add (fans, lights, defrost heaters, anti-sweat heaters) — they are *electric heat* that must be removed by the refrigeration system.
- **The dominant engineering decision** in supermarket design is the **open vs. closed** trade-off: visibility and accessibility (open) vs. energy efficiency and hygiene (closed).
- **Energy codes** (ASHRAE 90.1, ECBC) cap $Q_{\text{total}}$ per linear metre — designers must demonstrate compliance.
- **Anti-sweat heaters** on door frames convert electricity to heat that *enters the cabinet* — include in $Q_{\text{int}}$.
- **Defrost** can be on-demand, time-initiated, or adaptive — it is a periodic but predictable additional load.
- **The air curtain's mass flow rate is set by momentum balance** against buoyancy — increasing velocity improves $\eta$ but with diminishing returns and rising fan power (cubic with $v$).
